query: What was the significance of Earl Spencer's eulogy during Princess Diana's funeral?

documents.text: Earl Spencer, Princess Diana's brother, her two sons Prince Harry and Prince William, and her former husband Prince Charles arrive into Westminster Abbey for her funeral, Sept. 6 1997. (Photo: Associated Press) It may be the most memorable funeral oration of all time: Heartbroken, loving and defiant, it was by turns elegiac and fiery, elegant and over-the-top. When Princess Diana's younger brother Charles, the ninth Earl Spencer, delivered his eulogy at her funeral in Westminster Abbey on Sept. 6, 1997, no one among the 1,900 sitting inside, nor the million listening outside in parks and on The Mall, expected what poured forth. Least of all the royal family sitting right by him. And when he finished, voice trembling with emotion, no one expected the surging applause that erupted outside and soon spread inside the abbey. It was just one more unforgettable moment when the world said goodbye to the Princess of Wales. Here is the text of Spencer's eulogy: "I stand before you today the representative of a family in grief, in a country in mourning before a world in shock. "We are all united not only in our desire to pay our respects to Diana but rather in our need to do so. For such was her extraordinary appeal that the tens of millions of people taking part in this service all over the world via television and radio who never actually met her, feel that they too lost someone close to them in the early hours of Sunday morning. It is a more remarkable tribute to Diana than I can ever hope to offer her today. No Need for Royal Title "Diana was the very essence of compassion, of duty, of style, of beauty. All over the world she was a symbol of selfless humanity. All over the world, a standard bearer for the rights of the truly downtrodden, a very British girl who transcended nationality. Someone with a natural nobility who was classless and who proved in the last year that she needed no royal title to continue to generate her particular brand of magic. "Today is our chance to say thank you for the way you brightened our lives, even though God granted you but half a life. We will all feel cheated always that you were taken from us so young and yet we must learn to be grateful that you came along at all. Only now that you are gone do we truly appreciate what we are now without and we want you to know that life without you is very, very difficult. "We have all despaired at our loss over the past week and only the strength of the message you gave us through your years of giving has afforded us the strength to move forward. Earl Spencer, Princess Diana's brother addresses the congregation in London's Westminster Abbey, Saturday Sept. 6 1997 during the funeral service for his sister, Diana, Princess of Wales. Earl Spencer, scathingly condemned the newspapers and the paparazzi which had followed Diana's every step and pledged himself to protect Diana's son's, Princes' William and Harry from such intrusion. Princess Diana was killed in a car crash in Paris on Aug. 31. (Photo: John Stillwell, pool photo) Great Gift of Intuition "There is a temptation to rush to canonize your memory, there is no need to do so. You stand tall enough as a human being of unique qualities not to need to be seen as a saint. Indeed to sanctify your memory would be to miss out on the very core of your being, your wonderfully mischievous sense of humor with a laugh that bent you double. "Your joy for life transmitted where ever you took your smile and the sparkle in those unforgettable eyes. Your boundless energy which you could barely contain. "But your greatest gift was your intuition and it was a gift you used wisely. This is what underpinned all your other wonderful attributes and if we look to analyze what it was about you that had such a wide appeal we find it in your instinctive feel for what was really important in all our lives. "Without your God-given sensitivity we would be immersed in greater ignorance at the anguish of AIDS and HIV sufferers, the plight of the homeless, the isolation of lepers, the random destruction of landmines. Prince William, right, and Earl Spencer at Westminster Abbey for funeral ceremony of Princess Diana, Sept. 6, 1997. (Photo: JOEL ROBINE, Associated Press) Innermost Feelings of Suffering "Diana explained to me once that it was her innermost feelings of suffering that made it possible for her to connect with her constituency of the rejected. "And here we come to another truth about her. For all the status, the glamour, the applause, Diana remained throughout a very insecure person at heart, almost childlike in her desire to do good for others so she could release herself from deep feelings of unworthiness of which her eating disorders were merely a symptom. "The world sensed this part of her character and cherished her for her vulnerability whilst admiring her for her honesty. "The last time I saw Diana was on July 1, her birthday in London, when typically she was not taking time to celebrate her special day with friends but was guest of honor at a special charity fundraising evening. She sparkled of course, but I would rather cherish the days I spent with her in March when she came to visit me and my children in our home in South Africa. I am proud of the fact apart from when she was on display meeting President Mandela we managed to contrive to stop the ever-present paparazzi from getting a single picture of her — that meant a lot to her. "These were days I will always treasure. It was as if we had been transported back to our childhood when we spent such an enormous amount of time together — the two youngest in the family. "Fundamentally she had not changed at all from the big sister who mothered me as a baby, fought with me at school and endured those long train journeys between our parents' homes with me at weekends. "It is a tribute to her level-headedness and strength that despite the most bizarre-like life imaginable after her childhood, she remained intact, true to herself. Charles, Earl Spencer, the brother of Princess Diana, arrives by car at St James's Palace Sept. 3, 1997. (Photo: JOHN STILLWELL, Associated Press) Diana's Goodness Threatened the Media "There is no doubt that she was looking for a new direction in her life at this time. She talked endlessly of getting away from England, mainly because of the treatment that she received at the hands of the newspapers. I don't think she ever understood why her genuinely good intentions were sneered at by the media, why there appeared to be a permanent quest on their behalf to bring her down. It is baffling. "My own and only explanation is that genuine goodness is threatening to those at the opposite end of the moral spectrum. It is a point to remember that of all the ironies about Diana, perhaps the greatest was this — a girl given the name of the ancient goddess of hunting was, in the end, the most hunted person of the modern age. "She would want us today to pledge ourselves to protecting her beloved boys William and Harry from a similar fate and I do this here Diana on your behalf. We will not allow them to suffer the anguish that used regularly to drive you to tearful despair. Blood Family Will Protect Sons "And beyond that, on behalf of your mother and sisters, I pledge that we, your blood family, will do all we can to continue the imaginative way in which you were steering these two exceptional young men so that their souls are not simply immersed by duty and tradition but can sing openly as you planned. "We fully respect the heritage into which they have both been born and will always respect and encourage them in their royal role but we, like you, recognize the need for them to experience as many different aspects of life as possible to arm them spiritually and emotionally for the years ahead. I know you would have expected nothing less from us. More Timeline of Princess Diana's life: Timeline: Princess Diana's life and the events that made her who she was "William and Harry, we all cared desperately for you today. We are all chewed up with the sadness at the loss of a woman who was not even our mother. How great your suffering is, we cannot even imagine. "I would like to end by thanking God for the small mercies he has shown us at this dreadful time. For taking Diana at her most beautiful and radiant and when she had joy in her private life. Above all we give thanks for the life of a woman I am so proud to be able to call my sister, the unique, the complex, the extraordinary and irreplaceable Diana whose beauty, both internal and external, will never be extinguished from our minds." Source: The BBC Read or Share this story: https://usat.ly/2wTSLTd

## Preserving the Web: How the Internet Archive Combats Broken Links

The Internet Archive is dedicated to building a comprehensive digital library of the internet. A core part of this mission involves actively conducting web crawls to meticulously archive online content. This process goes beyond simply capturing the pages users directly visit; it also includes gathering all the pages those initial pages reference. This proactive approach ensures that even if a linked page is later modified or disappears from the live web, a preserved record of its original state, as it existed when the initial page was created, remains accessible.

### The "No More 404" Initiative: Restoring Lost Connections

The Internet Archive's vision is to leverage these archived versions to actively mend broken links across the internet. By replacing defunct links with pointers to the archived content, they aim to restore the intended user experience and provide crucial context for original authorial intent. This initiative, often referred to as "No More 404," specifically targets supported websites to systematically address and rectify broken links.

### Building a More Robust and Accessible Web

The ultimate aspiration behind this effort is to create a more robust and reliable web. By ensuring that information remains discoverable and accessible, even as the digital landscape constantly evolves, the Internet Archive contributes to a more stable and enduring online information ecosystem. This comprehensive archiving strategy is vital for the long-term health and usability of the internet.

## Princess Diana's Enduring Legacy: A Tribute to Compassion and Global Impact

Twenty years after her tragic passing, the world continues to **deeply honor Princess Diana**, affectionately known as "the People's Princess." Her life, **devastatingly cut short at 36** in a Paris car crash, left an **indelible mark on global consciousness**. Diana's legacy is defined not only by her **profound humanitarian efforts** but also by the **significant impact she had on her sons**, Prince William and Prince Harry, who were mere boys when they lost their mother.

### A Private Anniversary Marked by Public Tributes

Princes William and Harry, now adults, are marking this anniversary privately. They recently visited the **Kensington Palace sunken garden**, now a beautiful display of Diana's favorite white flowers. This **poignant visit** was shared with William's wife, Princess Kate, and representatives from charities **deeply connected to Diana's advocacy**. These included Great Ormond Street Hospital, the English National Ballet, the Leprosy Mission, Centrepoint, the Royal Marsden Hospital, and the National Aids Trust.

The princes also made an **impromptu stop at Kensington Palace** to connect with well-wishers and acknowledge the heartfelt tributes left at the palace gates. Kensington Palace shared on Twitter, "The Duke and Prince Harry are **grateful for the many flowers, letters, and messages** they have received about their mother. They wanted to say thank you to those who made the journey to Kensington Palace."

### Reflecting on Loss and Her Unwavering Influence

For the first time, William and Harry have spoken publicly about the **profound grief of their mother's sudden death**. In a documentary, Harry shared, "There's not a day that William and I don't wish that she was still around, and we wonder what kind of a mother she would be now, and what kind of a public role she would have, and what a difference she would be making." William echoed this sentiment, expressing sadness that Diana would never meet his wife, Princess Kate, or their children, Prince George and Princess Charlotte. He told GQ magazine, "I would like to have had her advice. I would love her to have met Catherine and to have seen the children grow up. It makes me sad that she won’t, that they will never know her."

Elton John, a close friend, poignantly captured the enduring nature of her spirit during Diana's 1997 funeral, singing, "**Your candle's burned out long before your legend ever will.**"

### A Legacy of Compassion and Fearless Advocacy

Diana's enduring appeal stems from her **unique blend of style, glamour, and unwavering humanitarianism**. Her tireless work for those affected by HIV/AIDS and leprosy, communities often facing marginalization, **significantly shifted public perception**. Her courageous act of publicly embracing a young boy with AIDS remains a **powerful symbol of her compassion**. Furthermore, her walk through an Angolan minefield shortly before her death brought **critical global attention to the devastating impact of landmines**, a cause that contributed to the eventual signing of the Ottawa Treaty by Britain and 120 other nations.

Harry recounted at an awards ceremony, "One of the things our mother taught William and I was the **value of doing good when no one is watching**. She visited hospitals late at night to comfort patients. She spent hours writing letters to privately support the work of others. She achieved a lot by shining a spotlight, but she worked just as hard when the cameras were gone."

### Preserving Her Memory for Future Generations

To honor their mother, Princes William and Harry have recreated her study at Buckingham Palace, featuring her desk where she meticulously organized her charitable endeavors. They have also commissioned a statue to be erected on the Kensington Palace grounds. In a BBC documentary, Harry expressed his deep desire to carry on her legacy: "Now all I want to do is try and fill the holes that my mother has left, and that's what it's about for us, is trying to make a difference and in making a difference, making her proud. She was the Princess of Wales, and she stood for so many things, but deep down inside for us, she was a mother. And we will miss our mother, and I wonder every single day what it would be like having her around."

Kensington Palace has hosted several commemorative events, including the creation of a memorial garden adorned with white roses, tulips, hyacinths, and forget-me-nots. An exhibition, "Diana: Her Fashion Story," celebrating her evolving style, opened earlier this year.

Documentaries continue to explore Diana's final days and her lasting impact. You felt as though she was genuinely interested in what you did and what you had to say." Mrs. Conn concluded, "She was lovely. I feel very fortunate to have met her. ### The Royal Family's Initial Response to Diana's Death: A Public Relations Crisis

The initial reaction of Britain's royal family to the death of Diana, Princess of Wales, in August 1997 is widely considered a significant misstep that eroded public trust. While the world was plunged into mourning, the royals, then on their annual summer break at Balmoral Castle, issued a brief statement expressing shock and distress. This measured response, however, failed to resonate with a public deeply affected by Diana's life and tragic end. The subsequent handling of the situation, marked by a perceived emotional distance and adherence to protocol, further exacerbated the disconnect.

#### A World in Mourning, a Family in Seclusion

As news of Diana's death spread, an unprecedented wave of grief enveloped the globe. Politicians struggled to articulate the profound sense of loss, and Prime Minister Tony Blair famously dubbed her the "People's Princess." Her brother, Earl Spencer, delivered a powerful tribute, directly implicating the press in her demise. In stark contrast, the royal family remained largely silent, sequestered at Balmoral. Their rationale, as understood by many, was that Diana, having divorced Prince Charles and relinquished her royal highness title, was no longer considered a senior member of the family whose death would necessitate the usual protocols. This interpretation, however, proved to be a critical misjudgment.

#### The Queen's Strategy Fails to Connect

The Queen's characteristic "keep calm and carry on" approach, a strategy that had served her well through numerous personal and national crises, proved inadequate in this unprecedented situation. The public perceived the royal family's reserved reaction as a sign of disrespect towards Diana, prompting widespread criticism, even from traditionally royalist newspapers. The Daily Express, for instance, ran headlines demanding the royals "Show us You Care." To the public, Diana was not just a former royal but a beloved mother to the future king, and many felt she had been mistreated by both the royals and Prince Charles. The initial response to her death only amplified these sentiments.

#### A Shift Towards Public Engagement

Sensing the growing public resentment, courtiers and politicians urged the royal family to take more visible action. This led to the planning of a ceremonial funeral. On the eve of the funeral, the Queen delivered a televised address, a departure from her usual stoicism. In a deeply emotional tone, she spoke of the family's grief and their focus on supporting the princes, addressing the nation "as a grandmother." This was a rare display of vulnerability from the monarch. However, the most poignant words of that period were not the Queen's, but those of Earl Spencer during the funeral oration. He eloquently described Diana as a "symbol of selfless humanity" and a "standard bearer for the suffering." He highlighted the tragic irony of her life, noting how the woman named after the Roman goddess of hunting became "the most hunted person of the modern age." He concluded by asserting that her "beauty, both internal and external, will never be extinguished in our minds."

#### Funeral Arrangements and Public Perception

The funeral arrangements themselves drew comparisons to plans for the Queen Mother's funeral, though Diana did not lie in state. This honor, typically reserved for monarchs and their consorts, was denied to Diana, despite many believing she would have eventually become queen consort. The absence of a lying in state, even for a single day, was seen by many as a missed opportunity to appease public anger. Furthermore, the princes were instructed to walk behind their mother's coffin, a decision Earl Spencer vehemently opposed, stating Diana would not have wanted it. He later revealed he was misled about the princes' wishes. Prince Harry himself has since expressed that no child should be subjected to such an ordeal.

#### The Long Road to Restoring Trust

In the immediate aftermath of Diana's death, the royal family committed a significant error in judgment. While the Queen's televised address and the funeral itself helped to mend some of the damage, a full restoration of public favor took considerable time. Twenty years later, Diana's legacy continues through her sons. This year, a private ceremony at her family estate, Althorp, led by her brother and attended by the princes, commemorated her life. While the royal family has regained some popularity, it is figures like the Queen, the Duke and Duchess of Cambridge, and Prince Harry who command widespread admiration. Prince Charles, in contrast, struggles to connect with the public, with significantly less media attention compared to his sons. A recent poll indicated that only a third of Britons believe he has been beneficial to the monarchy.

#### An Enduring Legacy and Future Considerations

Ultimately, the affection of the British people is not guaranteed, and public opinion can shift rapidly. The hope within Charles's circle is that his eventual ascension to the throne will see the mistakes of the 1990s forgotten. However, Diana's enduring presence in the public consciousness means the royal family will likely continue to be viewed, and perhaps judged, through the lens of her life and untimely death.

## The Tragic Paris Tunnel Crash of August 31, 1997: A Night of Speed, Pursuit, and Devastation

The Paris tunnel crash on August 31, 1997, remains a deeply impactful event in public consciousness. The immediate aftermath was marked by an eerie silence, a stark contrast to the violent chaos that had just occurred. This quiet was broken only by the faint, mournful wail of the mangled Mercedes-Benz's horn, a sound that seemed to emanate from both ends of the tunnel on Paris' Place de l’Alma. The luxury vehicle, a black Mercedes with its signature silver trim, had entered the tunnel at a speed estimated to be at least double the legal limit of 35 miles per hour. The tunnel itself, a passage beneath the city, opened onto a striking vista of the Seine's left bank at its western terminus. Above, on the bustling streets, the iconic Eiffel Tower continued its nightly illumination, and the distant, isolated sound of a car horn might have easily been dismissed as just another urban noise.

### The Moment of Impact

Mere moments before this unsettling quiet descended, a deafening roar had shattered the night's tranquility. Tom Richardson and Joanna Luz, visiting from San Diego, California, were walking near the tunnel's entrance when they witnessed the black Mercedes hurtle into the passage, closely pursued by a relentless swarm of motorcycles and scooters. They then heard a sound akin to a violent explosion. Just inside the 660-foot tunnel, the car made catastrophic contact with the concrete divider that separates the eastbound and westbound lanes. The impact was so severe that the vehicle appeared to cartwheel, executing a full 360-degree roll before spinning nearly an additional 180 degrees.

### The Grim Discovery

Upon cautiously entering the tunnel after the initial shock, Richardson and Luz discovered the Mercedes facing back towards its point of entry, a disorienting and grim tableau. The car's roof was grotesquely crushed, the windshield a spiderweb of shattered glass, and the airbags had deployed in a futile attempt to protect its occupants. The chauffeur, Henri Paul, who was tragically killed instantly, was slumped lifelessly over the steering wheel, his body's weight inadvertently depressing the car's horn, contributing to the unsettling sound. Standing just in front of the wreckage, a paparazzo, later identified as the last individual to photograph Diana, Princess of Wales, was already in position, his sophisticated camera raised and actively capturing images of the devastation. "When I ran into the tunnel, he was already there," Richardson recounted, his voice still tinged with disbelief. "I could see that his equipment was far too sophisticated for a tourist."

### Police Response and Apprehension

Parisian police responded with remarkable speed, swiftly cordoning off the entire area with the stark red-and-white tape of a crime scene. Their cruiser lights cast an urgent glow as they entered the tunnel. The officers quickly organized themselves into two distinct units: one group immediately converged on the wrecked vehicle, while the other fanned out with the objective of apprehending the photographers believed to have been instrumental in causing the high-speed chase and subsequent accident. It was later estimated that over seven paparazzi were involved in the reckless pursuit, with at least five still present within the tunnel at the time of the police arrival. All were swiftly apprehended and led out of the tunnel in handcuffs. As they emerged into the growing crowd of onlookers, jeers and shouts erupted, and one cameraman was even subjected to an assault and beaten before police intervention restored order.

### The Devastation Within

Inside the tunnel, the scene was one of utter devastation. It became tragically apparent almost immediately to the second group of responding officers that the chauffeur and Dodi Al Fayed, both seated on the vehicle's left side, had not survived the catastrophic impact. Diana and her bodyguard, Trevor Rees-Jones, on the right side of the car, however, appeared to be clinging precariously to life. "We knew it was somebody messed up bad," stated Michael Walker, another American tourist whose taxi had stopped near the wreckage, offering a firsthand account of the scene. "It was a bad accident. The car was crushed and tilted up against the wall." The taxi driver, observing the scene, recalled seeing a blonde woman in the backseat, her breathing labored, gasping and crying out in distress.

### Rescue Efforts and Critical Condition

As a growing crowd of concerned onlookers watched from a safe distance, the specialized rescue team meticulously worked to cut through the mangled roof and doors of the Mercedes using advanced hydraulic tools. They carefully extracted the two survivors, Diana and Rees-Jones, and swiftly transported them by ambulance to the Pitie-Salpetriere hospital, a highly regarded medical institution within the city. En route to the hospital, paramedics diligently assessed the injured princess, noting that her condition was extremely grave. She was suffering from severe chest injuries, a significant wound to her left lung, and multiple fractures throughout her body. Her blood pressure had dropped to alarmingly low levels, indicating profound internal trauma.

### The Final Hours

Upon her arrival at the hospital, the emergency room physicians confirmed that Diana was indeed alive, though critically injured, with extensive internal bleeding. For more than two agonizing hours, dedicated medical teams worked tirelessly in a desperate effort to stabilize her condition. Their efforts included a thoracotomy and direct cardiac massage, invasive procedures aimed at reviving her failing heart. Despite their profound expertise and unwavering commitment, the overwhelming blood loss and the systemic trauma inflicted by the crash proved insurmountable. At 4 a.m. Paris time, after two hours of continuous attempts to restart her heart, the attending doctors were forced to pronounce the princess dead, marking the tragic end of a life that had captivated the world.

query: What immediate political responses followed the shooting of Rep. Steve Scalise and four others during the baseball practice?

documents.text: (CNN) The shooting of Rep. Steve Scalise and four others at a baseball practice for Republican members of Congress on Wednesday morning in Alexandria, Virginia, was always going to quickly turn to politics. Rep. Chris Collins, one of President Donald Trump's most prominent congressional supporters, insisted that the shooting was directly tied to anti-Trump rhetoric from the left. "I can only hope that the Democrats do tone down the rhetoric," Collins said on a local radio station in upstate New York. "The rhetoric has been outrageous -- the finger-pointing, just the tone and the angst and the anger directed at Donald Trump, his supporters. Really, then, you know, some people react to things like that. They get angry as well. And then you fuel the fires." Collins also said in the same interview he would have his gun "in my pocket from this day forward." That pivot from police incident to politics happened rapidly around 11:15 a.m., when CNN confirmed that the alleged shooter was James T. Hodgkinson of Illinois. A quick scan of his social media presence -- Facebook and Twitter -- suggested that he was strongly opposed to Trump and was a supporter of the 2016 presidential candidacy of Vermont Sen. Bernie Sanders, an independent who ran as a Democrat. Hodgkinson also apparently volunteered for Sanders campaign in Iowa during the 2016 campaign. Sanders condemned the shooting in a statement issued Wednesday afternoon. "I have just been informed that the alleged shooter at the Republican baseball practice is someone who apparently volunteered on my presidential campaign," Sanders said in a statement. "I am sickened by this despicable act. Let me be as clear as I can be. Violence of any kind is unacceptable in our society and I condemn this action in the strongest possible terms." The newspaper described Hodgkinson this way "The shooter was James T. Hodgkinson of Belleville, who belonged to a number of anti-Republican groups, including one called 'Terminate the Republican Party.'" Police officials would not comment on any motive for the shooting or whether Hodgkinson was targeting Republicans. But CNN's Dana Bash reported that the shooting was deliberate and not a random act. Trump made no mention of politics in a brief statement just before noon eastern time. "We are strongest when we are unified and we work together for the common good," Trump said. JUST WATCHED President Trump reacts to attack (full remarks) Replay More Videos ... "An attack on one of us is an attack on all of us," Ryan said. None of that stopped some conservatives from concluding that Hodgkinson was aiming to injure Republicans specifically, and that he was driven by a liberal culture that glorifies violence against GOPers. MUST WATCH Lawmaker on shooting: Political hate to blame 01:07 Former House Speaker Newt Gingrich, appearing on Fox News , called the shootings "part of a pattern" and blamed "an increasing intensity of hostility on the left." He said conservative college students are afraid they'll be beaten on campus. "The intensity is very real, whether it's a so-called comedian holding up the President's head covered in blood, or right here in New York City, a play that shows the President being assassinated, or it's Democratic leading national politicians using vulgarity because they can't find any common language to talk," he said. And Michael Caputo, a former Trump adviser, was even more blunt in an interview with a Buffalo radio station. "For nine months, Democratic Party leaders have lied, regularly calling me and my friends traitors, so forgive me if I'm not more tender with their karma in Alexandria," Caputo said Others were less direct in tying Hodgkinson's apparent politics to this incident but did suggest that that the partisan political atmosphere clearly had something to do with the shooting. JUST WATCHED Virginia governor: Too many guns on the street Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH Virginia governor: Too many guns on the street 00:47 Virginia Gov. Terry McAuliffe, a Democrat, echoed Davis' sentiment. "I do think that things have become very partisan and very hardened in the country today," he said. "We have to work together to get things done and we're the greatest nation in the world and there has been too much raw discourse today that is pulling people apart." But McAuliffe also mentioned that "there are too many guns on the streets," a common theme for gun control advocates in the wake of attacks like these involving guns. Rep. Mo Brooks, the Alabama Republican and another one of the people at the baseball field when the shooting happened, dismissed the idea that he might rethink his staunch support of the 2nd Amendment in the wake of this shooting. "The Second Amendment right to bear arms is to ensure that we always have a Republic," Brooks said . "What we just saw here is one of the bad side effects of someone not exercising those rights properly." Georgia Rep. Barry Loudermilk, a Republican from Georgia who was on the field when the shooting happened, suggested that members of Congress should be allowed to carry guns. "I think we need to look at some reciprocity for members here but we also need to look at security details," Loudermilk told CNN's Ashley Killough. Until we know more about Hodgkinson's motive -- assuming we can find it out since he has now died from wounds he suffered -- it's difficult to reach hard and fast conclusions about why, allegedly, he did what he did. But a man with a gun shooting at members of Congress will always be political. That Hodgkinson was an outspoken critic of Trump makes it even harder to keep away from politics. The question now is where the political debate goes from here. Does the focus land on better protecting members of Congress in public? Tamping down the viciousness of political rhetoric? Gun control? Something else?

The individual identified as the suspect in the shooting at a congressional baseball practice, **James T. Hodgkinson, 66**, harbored **intense animosity towards President Donald Trump**. Evidence from his social media activity **strongly indicates a deep-seated hatred for Trump**.

Hodgkinson's Facebook profile was **replete with anti-Trump messages**. He was also a **fervent supporter of Bernie Sanders**. Furthermore, Hodgkinson expressed **negative sentiments towards Hillary Clinton**, with multiple posts dedicated to her.

One particular post on Hodgkinson's page, dated March, explicitly stated: "**Trump is Traitor. Trump Has Destroyed Our Democracy. It's Time to Destroy Trump & Co.**" This statement clearly articulates a **desire to eliminate Trump and his associates**.

Later, at 9:30 AM PT, Bernie Sanders issued a statement regarding Hodgkinson. Sanders stated, "I have just been informed that the alleged shooter at the Republican baseball practice is someone who apparently volunteered on my presidential campaign. I am sickened by this despicable act. Let me be as clear as I can be. Violence of any kind is unacceptable in our society and I condemn this action in the strongest possible terms." This statement confirmed Hodgkinson's connection to his campaign while **unequivocally denouncing the violence**.

The recent shooting at a congressional baseball practice in Alexandria, Virginia, which resulted in multiple injuries, underscores the critical role of the U.S. Capitol Police. Witnesses reported that the swift intervention of Capitol Police officers, part of House Majority Whip Steve Scalise’s security detail, likely prevented a far graver outcome. Senator Rand Paul stated, "Without the Capitol Hill police, it would have been a massacre." This incident has prompted discussions about the motivations behind the attack, including speculation about partisan politics, given the gunman's alleged inquiry about the political affiliation of the players.

Historically, the establishment of the U.S. Capitol Police force, tasked with safeguarding Congress, was a direct response to a violent incident that occurred within a highly polarized political environment in 1828. During this period, "Jacksonian" politicians, aligned with Andrew Jackson, had achieved a congressional majority following the midterm elections. The incumbent President John Quincy Adams anticipated a contentious presidential election against Jackson in 1828, but an unexpected physical altercation involving his son, John Adams Jr., and a Jacksonian supporter predated the election.

The incident involved Russell Jarvis, a journalist for the pro-Jackson newspaper *The Washington Telegraph*. At a White House New Year's Eve celebration, Jarvis and his family were present when John Adams Jr., serving as the President's son and personal secretary, allegedly uttered a "grievous insult to the ladies of my family," as Jarvis later recounted. According to *Perley’s Reminiscences of 60 Years in the National Metropolis*, a chronicle of mid-19th century Washington D.C., the exchange unfolded as follows: Mr. Stetson inquired about a lady, to which Mr. John Adams responded loudly, "That is the wife of one Russell Jarvis, and if he knew how contemptibly he is viewed in this house they would not be here." The Bostonians, including Jarvis's wife, departed after paying their respects to Mrs. Adams, with Jarvis confirming Mr. John Adams as the source of the insult.

Subsequently, Jarvis dispatched a note to Mr. John Adams via his friend, Mr. McLean, seeking an explanation. Mr. Adams conveyed to Mr. McLean that he offered no apology and desired no further correspondence. Jarvis reportedly awaited an opportune moment for retribution. In April 1828, encountering Adams in the Capitol Rotunda, Jarvis inquired about an apology. Upon Adams's continued refusal, Jarvis recounted, "I was excited by his continued refusal, and by a recollection of the offense, to commit an assault upon his person, which consisted merely in pulling his nose and slapping one side of his face, with my open hand."

Jarvis's actions were characterized as "all standard and approved provocations for a duel" in *America’s Royalty: All the Presidents’ Children*. However, President John Quincy Adams's strong opposition to dueling was evident in his response. He submitted a message to Congress requesting appropriations to secure the passageway between the presidential office and Congress, aiming to preempt similar future incidents. Consequently, Congress enacted legislation establishing the U.S. Capitol Police on May 2, 1828.

Steve Scalise, the Republican congressman for Louisiana's District 1, enjoys strong backing from his constituents. This was clear in November when he won reelection with 75% of the vote. His district, located in the "toe" of Louisiana's boot shape, covers parts of the New Orleans suburbs and is known as one of the country's most reliably Republican areas.

Even with debates over President Trump's early term and efforts to change the Affordable Care Act, Scalise's local support stayed firm, as reported by The Times-Picayune. His fundraising also showed strength in early 2017, when he raised a record $1.6 million.

Scalise is a regular participant in the yearly Congressional Baseball Game. This charity event features Democrats and Republicans playing against each other to support charities in the Washington D.C. area, usually held at Nationals Park.

During a practice for the game at Eugene Simpson Stadium Park in Alexandria, Virginia, a shooter opened fire. Scalise, a seasoned player, was at second base when the attack began. Alabama Representative Mo Brooks told CNN that Scalise was one of five people hit by bullets. Brooks recalled hearing a "bam" and then Scalise crying out after being shot near second base.

Scalise's presence, as a member of House leadership, helped reduce the number of victims. His security detail, present at the practice, quickly confronted the shooter. CBS News’s Nancy Cordes noted that these officers bravely moved toward Scalise through the gunfire, eventually stopping the attacker with their weapons.

This brave action, however, led to injuries for two officers. The shooter later died at the hospital. Ohio Representative Brad Wenstrup emphasized the security detail's vital role, telling CBS News, "If they had not been here it probably would have been far worse." Scalise's fellow lawmakers feel his attendance at the practice led directly to his security detail's quick action, possibly saving more lives.

(CNN) Louisiana's Steve Scalise is the 9th member of Congress to be shot while in office and one of two dozen to be targeted by attackers since 1789, according to a 2011 Congressional Research Service report . Scalise, the third-ranking Republican in the House, was shot in the hip by a man wielding a rifle at an early-morning practice for the GOP baseball team in advance of the scheduled Congressional Baseball Game tomorrow. He was one of five people injured -- including two members of the US Capitol Police force -- in the shooting. The Louisiana congressman was, according to a statement released by his office , undergoing surgery Wednesday morning but in stable condition. He is the first member of Congress to be shot since January 2011 when then Arizona Rep. Gabrielle Giffords, a Democrat, was shot in the head at a congressional event in Tucson. "My heart is with my former colleagues, their families & staff, and the US Capitol Police - public servants and heroes today and every day," Giffords tweeted in the immediate aftermath of the shooting. My heart is with my former colleagues, their families & staff, and the US Capitol Police- public servants and heroes today and every day. Six people were killed in the Arizona shooting that targeted Giffords. She returned to Congress in August 2011, but resigned from the chamber in January 2012. Other attacks were not included in the report, such as the 1998 shooting inside the Capitol when a gunman stormed into the building and shot and killed two Capitol Hill police officers. That gunman had a history of mental illness. Tourists leave the Capitol on a stretcher after the violence and chaos caused by the shootings that claimed the lives of US Capitol Police officers John Gibson and Jacob J. Chestnut in 1998. Prior to the attack on Giffords, it had been three decades since a member of Congress had been attacked. In November 1979, a woman with a knife got into Ted Kennedy's Senate office. She was stopped by the US Secret Service prior to reaching Kennedy; a Secret Service officer was "slightly wounded" according to CRS. Almost a year to the day prior, California Rep. Leo Ryan was shot and killed at an airstrip in Guyana after he and two dozen others traveled to investigate the Jonestown cult headed by Jim Jones, which was based there. Ryan, along with four others -- including three journalists -- were murdered. Jackie Speier, who now holds Ryan's seat, was on that trip as a staffer to the Congressman. She was shot five times but lived . (Jones as well as 908 of his followers committed mass suicide by poisoning on the same day Ryan was killed.) Jackie Speier, an aide to Congressman Leo Ryan, being taken from a plane at Georgetown on November 19, 1978, after its arrival from Jonestown where Speier was shot five times and Ryan and four others were ambushed and killed by members of the People's Temple. JUST WATCHED Jonestown survivor: 'I was shot five times' Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH Jonestown survivor: 'I was shot five times' 02:31 A decade earlier, New York Sen. Robert Kennedy, then a leading candidate for the Democratic presidential nomination, was assassinated in Los Angeles following his victory in the June California primary. Photos: Behind the Picture: RFK's Assassination, 1968 Behind the Picture: RFK's Assassination, 1968 – A wounded Paul Schrade, a regional director of the United Auto Workers Union, labor chair of Robert Kennedy's campaign and one of five other people shot by Sirhan Sirhan, on the floor of the kitchen at the Ambassador Hotel, June 5, 1968. Hide Caption 1 of 6 Photos: Behind the Picture: RFK's Assassination, 1968 Behind the Picture: RFK's Assassination, 1968 – Senator Robert Kennedy gives a speech at the Ambassador Hotel in Los Angeles before his assassination, June 1968. Hide Caption 2 of 6 Photos: Behind the Picture: RFK's Assassination, 1968 Behind the Picture: RFK's Assassination, 1968 – Sen. Kennedy gives a speech at the Ambassador Hotel in Los Angeles before his assassination, June 1968. Hide Caption 3 of 6 Photos: Behind the Picture: RFK's Assassination, 1968 Behind the Picture: RFK's Assassination, 1968 – "Heading for his victory speech in the Ambassador Hotel ballroom, Robert Kennedy stops in the kitchen to shake hands. A few minutes later the gunman was waiting for him in the corridor just outside the kitchen." Hide Caption 4 of 6 Photos: Behind the Picture: RFK's Assassination, 1968 Behind the Picture: RFK's Assassination, 1968 – A less-famous image of Sen. Robert Kennedy and Ambassador Hotel employee Juan Romero moments after RFK was shot by Sirhan Sirhan, June 1968. Hide Caption 5 of 6 Photos: Behind the Picture: RFK's Assassination, 1968 Behind the Picture: RFK's Assassination, 1968 – A young Robert Kennedy supporter registers disbelief after his shooting by Sirhan Sirhan, June 1968. Hide Caption 6 of 6 Rep. Kenneth Roberts shown here being carried down the Capitol steps after Puerto Rican nationalists opened fire in the Capitol Building, shouting "Free Puerto Rico." The incident involving the most members of Congress happened on March 1, 1954 when a group of Puerto Rican nationalists entered the House press gallery and opened fired. Reps. Clifford Davis (Tennessee), Alvin Bentley (Michigan), Ben Jensen (Iowa), George Hyde Fallon (Maryland) and Kenneth Roberts (Alabama) were injured -- although all five recovered. At issue was the ongoing US control of Puerto Rico

Charles Orear, 50, a restaurant manager from St. Louis, said in an interview Wednesday that he became friendly with James T. Hodgkinson, whom law enforcement officials identified as the shooter, during their work in Iowa on Sen. Bernie Sanders’s presidential campaign. Orear said Hodgkinson was a passionate progressive and showed no signs of violence or malice toward others. “You’ve got to be kidding me,” Orear said when told by phone. “I met him on the Bernie trail in Iowa, worked with him in the Quad Cities area.” Orear described Hodgkinson as a “quiet guy” who was “very mellow, very reserved” when they stayed overnight at a Sanders’s supporter home in Rock Island, Ill., after canvassing for the senator. “He was this union tradesman, pretty stocky, and we stayed up talking politics,” he said. “He was more on the really progressive side of things.” The Post reached out to Orear after seeing that he liked one of Hodgkinson’s Facebook posts.

Former Speaker Newt Gingrich (R-Ga.) on Wednesday linked a growing trend of "hostility" on the left to violent incidents like the shooting that morning at congressional Republicans' baseball practice. "It's part of a pattern," Gingrich said on Fox News. "An increasing intensity of hostility on the left." Gingrich pointed to the violence and intimidation toward conservatives on college campuses as another sign of the left's hostility toward Republicans and President Trump. ADVERTISEMENT "Look, I talk to college students regularly," Gingrich continued, "who say that if they are openly for Trump, they get threatened." "I've had college students tell me they get threatened with being beaten up, some of them get death threats," he said. Gingrich then targeted comedian Kathy Griffin as an example of a member of the left advocating violence against Trump and other Republicans. Griffin was fired from CNN's New Year's Eve program after she posted a photo of her holding up a dummy head of Trump covered in ketchup. "The intensity is very real, whether it's a so-called comedian holding up the president's head covered in blood, or right here in New York City, a play that shows the president being assassinated, or it's Democratic leading national politicians using vulgarity because they can't find any common language to talk," Gingrich argued. He likely was referring to Central Park’s summer Shakespeare production of “Julius Caesar” that appears to portray the slain Roman dictator as Trump and to Sen. Kirsten Gillibrand Kirsten Elizabeth GillibrandChris Murphy’s profile rises with gun tragedies Overnight Energy: Dems take on Trump's chemical safety pick Dems lambaste Trump’s ‘outrageous’ EPA chemical safety pick MORE (D-N.Y.), who recently slammed the president for not keeping his promises using the expression "f--- no."

Following a shooting at a baseball field in Alexandria during a practice for an annual charity game, Virginia Governor Terry McAuliffe spoke out about gun control. The incident injured five people, including House Majority Whip Steve Scalise.

### Governor's Plea for Enhanced Safety

At a news conference, Governor McAuliffe stressed the necessity of stronger measures to safeguard the public. "I think we need to do more to protect all of our citizens," he declared. While recognizing that immediate policy discussions were not the priority, he voiced his ongoing worry regarding the widespread availability of firearms. "There are too many guns on the street," the Democratic governor observed. He specifically highlighted the importance of background checks and closing loopholes at gun shows, emphasizing that these concerns affect the safety of everyone, not just elected officials.

### Political Fallout and Legislative Impact

The shooting promptly spurred conversations about gun rights and gun control. Although lawmakers from both parties denounced the violence and called for solidarity, the event also impacted legislative schedules. A House subcommittee hearing planned for Wednesday to debate a proposal to relax rules on gun silencers was postponed due to the shooting.

### Upholding Second Amendment Rights

Alabama Republican Rep. Mo Brooks, who was present and unharmed at the baseball practice, reaffirmed his commitment to Second Amendment protections. He noted that while constitutional rights can have potential downsides, the recent event demonstrated an improper use of those rights. "I'm not changing my position on any of the rights we enjoy as Americans," Brooks stated. He also expressed a desire to gather more information about the shooter's history, such as any prior felony convictions, before reaching a final conclusion.

query: What was the significance of the 'happy hour' debate held before the main Republican primary debate in Cleveland?

documents.text: It's been called the "happy hour" debate -- the 5 p.m. face-off that precedes the prime-time showdown among the top-polling Republican presidential candidates in Cleveland. But the stakes for those invited to "happy hour" could be even higher. Seven lower-polling candidates were invited to the 5 p.m. ET Fox News/Facebook debate stage. The smaller pond, it's an opportunity for at least one of the candidates to make a big splash -- and start the buzz that builds a fledgling bid into a serious campaign. "An underdog is going to emerge from this first debate," Craig Shirley, a conservative strategist and Reagan biographer, said. Texas Gov. Rick Perry, who flamed out of the 2012 race after an infamous debate gaffe, is looking for redemption. Former New York Gov. George Pataki, even though he won't be on the big stage with Donald Trump, appears poised to hammer the billionaire front-runner. Former HP exec Carly Fiorina has been blunt in saying that, as the only woman in the field, she would be a potent adversary against Democratic front-runner Hillary Clinton. More on this... Santorum: My record of accomplishment in DC sets me apart Any of the seven could have a break-out moment Thursday afternoon that catapults them into the upper mix of the 17 candidates now on the field. Those invited to the 5 p.m. debate are: Perry; Pataki; Fiorina; former Pennsylvania Sen. Rick Santorum; Louisiana Gov. Bobby Jindal; South Carolina Sen. Lindsey Graham; and former Virginia Gov. Jim Gilmore. JOIN THE DEBATE DISCUSSION Pataki told Fox News the race right now is "all about name ID," with a "reality show host, a brother and son two of presidents" leading the field. He said he'll distinguish himself by his record as governor. "I think I changed New York with sweeping conservative change more than any other state in America," he said. "Enough of the sound bites, enough of the posturing. Bring Americans together, governor successfully, solve our problems and move forward with confidence." Like Trump, Fiorina also comes from a business background. But she told Fox News, "I've had a very different set of experiences than Donald Trump. I started out as a secretary, I went on eventually to lead the largest technology company in the world. So it's a very different experience set of working my way up." GET THE ELECTION HQ APP She said she knows more world leaders than anyone running save for maybe Clinton, "Only I didn't do photo ops, I wrestled with bureaucracies." As for his debate plans, Santorum told Fox News on Thursday he plans to just be "authentic." "I did this 22 times the last time around," said the former presidential candidate. He said he'll be able to stand out by pointing to his legislative record as a former senator, fighting to ban partial-birth abortions, enact welfare reform and impose Iran sanctions. "They're really isn't anybody else up there on the stage who has a record of accomplishment in Washington, D.C.," he said. WATCH THE DEBATE ON YOUR MOBILE DEVICE Syndicated columnist and Washington Examiner chief political correspondent Byron York wrote that the 5 p.m. debate provides opportunities for candidates for several reasons. Namely, Trump won't be on the stage dominating attention, it will be less personality-driven, and the winner of the debate will get momentum. Shirley said he thinks the media want to essentially pick someone from the first debate to advance. "I think the first debate is not going to get as much attention but it might surprise everybody and be the most issue-oriented because of the distracting fight opening up between [Jeb] Bush and Trump in the second debate," he said. Both debates, hosted by Fox News and Facebook in conjunction with the Ohio Republican Party, will be held at the Quicken Loans Arena in Cleveland, Ohio. The 9 p.m. ET debate will include the top 10 candidates in an average of recent national polls. They are Trump; former Florida Gov. Jeb Bush; Wisconsin Gov. Scott Walker; former Arkansas Gov. Mike Huckabee; retired neurosurgeon Ben Carson; Texas Sen. Ted Cruz; Florida Sen. Marco Rubio; Kentucky Sen. Rand Paul; New Jersey Gov. Chris Christie; and Ohio Gov. John Kasich.

Republican presidential candidates from left, Jim Gilmore, Lindsey Graham, Bobby Jindal, Rick Perry, Rick Santorum, Carly Fiorina, and George Pataki take the stage for a pre-debate forum at the Quicken Loans Arena, Thursday, Aug. 6, 2015, in Cleveland. Seven of the candidates have not qualified for the primetime debate. (AP Photo/Andrew Harnik) This is a full transcript of the Republican party’s first debate of Thursday night--an undercard featuring seven lower-performing candidates. HEMMER: This is first official event in the campaign for the Republican nomination for president. Welcome to Cleveland Ohio. It is debate night. HEMMER: I'm Bill Hemmer. MACCALLUM: And I'm Martha MacCallum. It all starts here. We are ready, the candidates are ready. We're live at the Quicken Loans Arena, where we have partnered with Facebook to bring you, the voter, into today's debate. HEMMER: So you will hear from all 17 candidates tonight, and you'll meet seven of them right now, starting with three-time governor in the state of Texas, Rick Perry. (APPLAUSE) MACCALLUM: Also, two-time senator from Pennsylvania, Rick Santorum. (APPLAUSE) HEMMER: Two-time. Two-time governor of the State of Louisiana, Acting Governor Bobby Jindal. HEMMER: So you will hear from all 17 candidates tonight, and you'll meet seven of them right now, starting with three-time governor in the state of Texas, Rick Perry. (APPLAUSE) MACCALLUM: Also, two-time senator from Pennsylvania, Rick Santorum. (APPLAUSE) HEMMER: Two-time. Two-time governor of the State of Louisiana, Acting Governor Bobby Jindal. (APPLAUSE) MACCALLUM: Businesswoman and former CEO of Hewlett-Packard, Carly Fiorina. (APPLAUSE) HEMMER: The senior senator from South Carolina, Lindsey Graham. (APPLAUSE) MACCALLUM: Former three-term governor of New York, George Pataki. (APPLAUSE) [Live updates: The Republican presidential debate] HEMMER: And former Virginia governor, Jim Gilmore. MACCALLUM: Now, this debate will last one hour. We're going to have four commercial breaks. MACCALLUM: Each candidate will have one minute to anwer each question and 30 seconds for rebuttal. If you run out of time, you're going to hear this. OK? HEMMER: Gentle. MACCALLUM: Everybody got the bell? HEMMER: Wait til you hear what the others are going to get later, huh? MACCALLUM: Exactly. (LAUGHTER) HEMMER: One year from now, a Republican nominee will be standing on this stage in this very same arena. That person is in Cleveland today. So let's get started. First topic, electability. First question to Governor Perry from Texas. Welcome, Governor. PERRY: It's good to be with you. HEMMER: You were in charge of the 12th largest economy in the world, and you recently said that four years ago, you weren't ready for this job. HEMMER: Why should someone vote for you now? PERRY: After those four years of looking back and being prepared, the preparation to be the most powerful individual in the world requires an extraordinary amount of work: not just having been the governor of the 12th largest economy in the world, which I might add, we added 1.5 million jobs during that period of time over that 2007 through 2014 period, a period when America was going through the most deep recession it had been through since the Great Depression. I think Americans want someone to have a track record of showing them how to get this country back on record, someone who will stand up and every day project that best days of America are in front of us. And I will assure you, as the governor of the state of Texas, and as those last four years have shown me, the preparation to be ready to stand on this stage and talk about those monetary policies, those domestic policies, and those foreign policies, Americans are going to see that I am ready to be that individual. HEMMER: Thank you, Governor. MACCALLUM: Now we go to Carly Fiorina. Carly, you were CEO of Hewlett-Packard. You ran for Senate and lost in California in 2010. This week, you said "Margaret Thatcher was not content to manage a great nation in decline, and neither am I." Given your current standings in the polls, is the Iron Lady comparison a stretch? FIORINA: Well, I would begin by reminding people that at this point in previous presidential elections, Jimmy Carter couldn't win, Ronald Reagan couldn't win, Bill Clinton couldn't win, and neither could've Barack Obama. I started as a secretary and became ultimately the chief executive of the largest technology company in the world, almost $90 billion in over 150 countries. I know personally how extraordinary and unique this nation is. I think to be commander in chief in the 21st century requires someone who understands how the economy works, someone who understands how the world works and who's in it; I know more world leaders on the stage today than anyone running, with the possible exception of Hillary Clinton; understands bureaucracies, how to cut them down to size and hold them accountable; and understands technology, which is a tool, but it's also a weapon that's being used against us. Most importantly, I think I understand leadership, which sometimes requires a tough call in a tough time. But mostly, the highest calling of leadership is to challenge the status-quo and unlock the potential of others. We need a leader who will lead the resurgence of this great nation and unlock its potential once again. Thank you. HEMMER: Senator Santorum, you won the Iowa caucus four years ago and 10 other states. But you failed to beat Mitt Romney for the nomination. And no one here tonight is going to question your conviction or your love for country. But has your moment passed, Senator? SANTORUM: I would say the message that got us the win in Iowa and 10 other states against pretty overwhelming odds, is the message that's going to deliver us in this election. SANTORUM: You know, we didn't start out four years ago at the top of the heap. We were behind where we were today. But we stuck to our message. We stuck to the fact that Americans are tired of Washington corporate interests and Democrats who are interested in just politics and power and they're looking for someone who's going to fight for them; looking for someone who's going to grow manufacturing sector of our economy, so those 74 percent of Americans who don't have a college degree have a chance to rise again. Someone who's going to stand up, and be very clear with our enemies as to the lines their going to draw and stand with them. I've got a track record. The reason I did so well last time is not just because of the vision, it's because I have a track record in Washington, D.C. of getting things done. Iran sanctions -- the Iran sanctions that brought them to the table, those are sanctions that we put in place when I was in the United States Senate, and a whole host of other things that put me in a position of saying, I not only have a great vision, but I can govern effectively in Washington. HEMMER: Thank you, Senator. MACCALLUM: Governor Jindal, you're one of two sitting governors on the stage tonight. But your approval numbers at home are in the mid 30s at this point. In a recent poll that showed you in a head-to- head against Hillary Clinton in Louisiana, she beat you by several points. So if the people of Louisiana are not satisfied, what makes you think that the people of this nation would be? JINDAL: Well, first of all, thank you all for having us. You know, I won two record elections. Last time I was elected governor, won a record margin in my state. Martha, we got a lot of politicians that will kiss babies, cut ribbons, do whatever it takes to be popular. That's not why I ran for office. I ran for office to make the generational changes in Louisiana. We've cut 26 percent of our budget. We have 30,000 fewer state bureaucrats than the day I took office. I don't think anybody has cut that much government anywhere, at any time. As a result, eight credit upgrades; as a result, a top ten state for private sector job creation. And we fought for statewide school choice, where the dollars follow the child, instead of the child following the dollars. We've been the most pro-life state six years in a row. My point is this: I won two landslide elections, I made big changes. I think our country is tired of the politicians who simply read the polls and fail to lead. Both Democrats and Republicans have gotten us in the mess we're in -- $18 trillion of debt, a bad deal with Iran, we're not staying with Israel. I think the American people are look for real leadership. That's what I've done in Louisiana, that's what I'll do in America. HEMMER: Senator Lindsey Graham, you worked with Democrats and President Obama when it came to climate change, something you know is extremely unpopular with conservative Republicans. How can they trust you based on that record? GRAHAM: You can trust me to do the following: that when I get on change with Hillary Clinton, we won't be debating about the science, we'll be debating about the solutions. In her world, cap- and-trade would dominate, that we will destroy the economy in the name of helping the environment. In my world, we'll focus on energy independence and a clean environment. When it comes to fossil fuels, we're going to find more here and use less. Over time, we're going to become energy independent. I am tired of sending $300 billion overseas to buy oil from people who hate our guts. The choice between a weak economy and a strong environment is a false choice, that is not the choice I'll offer America. A healthy environment, a strong economy and energy independent America -- that would be the purpose of my presidency, is break the strangle hold that people enjoy on fossil fuels who hate our guts. HEMMER: Thank you, Senator. MACCALLUM: Governor Pataki, four years ago this month, you called it quits in a race for the presidency in 2012, but now you're back. Mitt Romney declined to run this time, because he believed that the party needed new blood. Does he have a point? PATAKI: I think he means somebody who hasn't been a career politician, and who's been out of office for awhile. I think the last eight years in the private sector have allowed me to see government from the outside, and I think that is a positive thing. Yes, I thought about running four years ago. I was ready to lead, but I wasn't ready to run. But I look at this country today, and I look at how divided we are, I look at how politicians are always posturing and issuing sound bites but never solving problems. What I did in New York was bring people together, an overwhelmingly Democratic state. But I was able to get Democrats to support the most conservative sweeping policy changes in any state in America. And when I look at Washington today, we need to bring us together. We need to solve problems, we need to rebuild our military so we can stand up to radical Islam, we need to get our economy growing much faster by throwing out the corrupt tax code and lowering the rates. We have to end crony capitalism in Washington, where the lobbyists and the powerful can get tax breaks and tax credits, and the American people don't get laws in their interest. I can do that. And I can do it regardless of what the makeup of Congress is because I did it in New York state. So we need new leadership -- yes. I will be that new leader. MACCALLUM: Thank you, Governor. ## Republican Presidential Hopefuls Face Off in Inaugural 2016 Debate

The 2016 Republican presidential campaign officially kicked off with its first debate, held at Quicken Loans Arena in Cleveland. Ten candidates took the stage to articulate their visions and policy stances, marking a crucial moment in the race for the party's nomination.

### A Diverse Field of Contenders

The lineup featured a mix of established political figures and newcomers. Among those participating in the prime-time event were Senator Ted Cruz of Texas, Senator Rand Paul of Kentucky, and former Florida Governor Jeb Bush. Real estate mogul Donald Trump also commanded attention on the stage. Other notable candidates included former Pennsylvania Senator Rick Santorum, Ohio Governor John Kasich, Wisconsin Governor Scott Walker, and former Hewlett-Packard CEO Carly Fiorina. A separate pre-debate forum was held for seven lower-polling candidates, including former Governors Jim Gilmore, Lindsey Graham, Bobby Jindal, Rick Perry, and George Pataki.

### Pre-Debate Buzz and Key Issues

The day began with a forum for candidates not selected for the main event, which was determined by national poll standings. Rick Perry, addressing his presidential aspirations, highlighted the demanding nature of global leadership and asserted his preparedness, referencing his previous campaign.

### NRA's Bold Ad Campaign

In a significant development, the National Rifle Association announced it would air its first political advertisement of the 2016 campaign during the prime-time debate. The 30-second spot targets former New York City Mayor Michael Bloomberg, a vocal advocate for gun control. The advertisement claims Bloomberg's agenda includes restricting food choices, increasing energy costs, and curtailing Second Amendment rights. The NRA's substantial investment of approximately $1 million in this ad underscores its commitment to influencing public opinion, with placements planned across major cable news networks and in crucial early-voting states like Iowa, New Hampshire, Nevada, and South Carolina.

### Campaign Trail Developments

Senator Rand Paul addressed a recent indictment of a former political aide, Jesse Benton, calling it a "distraction" during the campaign's opening debate. Federal prosecutors have charged Benton and two others with conspiracy to secure an Iowa state senator's support before the 2012 Iowa caucuses. Senator Paul expressed suspicion about the timing of these charges, suggesting a potential political motive by the Obama administration. Benton has temporarily stepped down from his role with a super PAC supporting Paul, stating his intention to return after being exonerated.

### White House and Congressional Reactions

From the White House, Press Secretary Josh Earnest indicated that President Barack Obama anticipates significant discussion of his administration during the Republican debate. While not planning to watch the entire event, Obama is expected to follow news coverage. Earnest predicted the debate would largely focus on Obama's policies and influence, though the White House is not preparing immediate responses to potential criticisms. Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell confirmed his intention to watch the debate, anticipating an engaging two-hour broadcast.

### A Call for Unity and Solutions

South Carolina Governor Nikki Haley offered advice to the Republican candidates, urging them to demonstrate respect and a commitment to finding solutions. Speaking at a Republican National Committee meeting, Haley referenced the unifying response in South Carolina following a racially motivated massacre, which ultimately led to the removal of the Confederate battle flag. She expressed optimism that similar consensus-building could be achieved nationwide. Her message to the candidates was a call for substantive proposals to address national challenges.

### Walker's Campaign Trail Presence

Supporters of Scott Walker provided a spirited send-off as the Wisconsin Governor departed for Cleveland. Walker, who visited the Wisconsin State Fair prior to his departure, drew a parallel between his attendance at the fair and Abraham Lincoln's visit in 1859, the year before his presidential election. Walker committed to returning to the fair for various events, including a meat auction and a potato-sack slide with his nieces.

### Democratic Debate Schedule Announced

Meanwhile, the Democratic National Committee announced its schedule for presidential debates, with the first of six planned events set for October 13 in Nevada. Four debates are slated for early primary states preceding the Iowa caucuses. This decision has drawn criticism from some candidates, including former Maryland Governor Martin O'Malley, who voiced concerns about limiting debate opportunities. The dates for the final two Democratic debates are yet to be determined but are expected to occur in February or March.

Republican candidates have **strongly opposed** the nuclear agreement with Iran, a key concern being the **financial benefits** Iran would gain from lifted sanctions. While Donald J. Trump claimed, "We’re giving them $150 billion plus," this **misrepresents** how the deal works. World powers aren't directly sending money to Iran. Instead, by **easing sanctions**, they are allowing Iran to **access its own frozen capital** held overseas.

This frozen capital comes from Iran's **oil and commodity exports**, with significant amounts immobilized in countries like China, India, and South Korea. While exact figures are debated, the U.S. Treasury Department estimated Iran's global foreign exchange assets at **$100 billion to $125 billion**. However, the Treasury also noted that Iran's **readily accessible liquid assets** after sanctions relief would be much lower, possibly around **$50 billion**.

Treasury Secretary Jacob J. Lew informed Congress that after accounting for Iran's existing financial commitments, the nation would likely have about **$56 billion remaining**. Iran's central bank governor recently stated that out of roughly $77 billion held offshore, only about **$29 billion would be usable**. The rest is already designated for petrochemical investments or pledged as collateral for Chinese-financed projects. Despite these figures, critics argue they are **deliberately understated** to influence the political debate.

Carly Fiorina **emerged as a standout** in the Republican presidential debate for candidates not yet in the top tier, **captivating viewers** and **dominating online conversations**. Despite facing questions about her long-shot status, Fiorina projected an image of a **composed and experienced executive**. She directly challenged Hillary Rodham Clinton, labeling her a **liar**, and offered **sharp criticism** of Donald Trump, the current front-runner in the GOP field. Notably, Fiorina spoke for a **longer duration** than most of her fellow candidates on the crowded stage, injecting **humor and wit** into her responses. Her performance quickly garnered **widespread praise** on social media, with a significant spike in Google searches and endorsements from political commentators who declared her the **winner of the undercard debate**. Former House Speaker Newt Gingrich even tweeted, suggesting Fiorina deserved a spot in the prime-time event.

This debate season offers valuable lessons from past presidential races. Revisiting the 2012 Republican debates, we can recall some of the most memorable "oops" moments, highlighting the **high stakes** and **intense scrutiny** faced by candidates. For undercard GOP candidates, the bar for success can sometimes feel low, but Fiorina's performance demonstrated a **clear strategy to rise above the noise**.

Fiorina's **commanding presence** on stage was characteristic of her public persona. As the former CEO of Hewlett-Packard, a position she held without prior elected office experience, she has consistently received positive reactions, including applause, laughter, and standing ovations, particularly from conservative audiences in Iowa and New Hampshire. However, these strong showings have not yet translated into a **significant boost in early polling numbers**. A considerable portion of the electorate remains **unfamiliar with her**, with many even mispronouncing her last name. This lack of widespread recognition is precisely why she found herself participating in the second-tier debate, rather than the main event featuring the top ten candidates. Her closest competitor for attention during this undercard debate was former Texas Governor Rick Perry, who, despite a notable "oops" moment in a previous debate four years prior, generally performed well on this occasion.

Fiorina's impactful performance once again ignited the crucial question: Will this be the moment that **propels her out of the lower ranks** of candidates, even if only slightly? The very first question of the evening directly addressed the significant hurdles Fiorina faces. She was asked to reconcile her past as CEO of Hewlett-Packard and her unsuccessful Senate run in California in 2010 with her recent comparison of herself to Margaret Thatcher, who, as Fiorina stated, "was not content to manage a great nation in decline, and neither am I." Given her current poll standings, the moderator questioned if the Iron Lady comparison was a stretch.

Unfazed, Fiorina skillfully deflected the challenge by drawing parallels to four former U.S. presidents. She reminded the audience that at similar stages in their respective presidential campaigns, figures like Jimmy Carter, Ronald Reagan, Bill Clinton, and even Barack Obama faced **significant doubts about their electability**. This historical context served to normalize her own underdog status and underscore the **unpredictable nature of presidential politics**.

Fiorina took the opportunity to introduce herself to a broader audience by highlighting her professional journey. She emphasized her rise to become the CEO of one of the world's largest technology companies, a role that she argued provided her with a **deep understanding of economic principles, global markets, and the importance of streamlining bureaucracy**. "I understand leadership, which sometimes requires a tough call in a tough time," Fiorina stated, referencing her departure from Hewlett-Packard following a contentious and publicly scrutinized dispute with her board. She further elaborated on her leadership philosophy, asserting that "**the highest calling of leadership is to challenge the status quo and unlock the potential of others**."

Perhaps the most memorable and impactful exchange of the debate occurred when Fiorina was asked to explain Donald Trump's surprising success in the polls. Her response was a masterclass in political wit and pointed commentary. "Well, I don’t know, I didn’t get a phone call from Bill Clinton before I jumped in the race," she quipped, alluding to a reported conversation between Clinton and Trump prior to Trump's entry into the 2016 race. She continued, "Did any of you get a phone call from Bill Clinton? I didn’t. Maybe it’s because I hadn’t given money to the foundation or donated to his wife’s Senate campaign." This jab cleverly implied a potential quid pro quo or preferential treatment for Trump, while simultaneously highlighting her own lack of such connections. It's worth noting that Fiorina had previously shared a stage with former President Clinton at a Clinton Global Initiative event the previous year in Denver, a detail she omitted in her debate response. Shifting to a more serious tone, Fiorina then suggested that Trump had effectively "**tapped into an anger that people feel. They’re sick of politics as usual**."

Fiorina's most pointed criticisms were, as has been her consistent approach, directed at Hillary Clinton. Fiorina has frequently argued that as a woman, she is uniquely positioned to challenge Clinton aggressively without the risk of being perceived as sexist. "**Hillary Clinton lies about Benghazi. She lies about e-mails**," Fiorina declared. "She is still defending Planned Parenthood, and she is still her party’s front-runner." This direct accusation aimed to undermine Clinton's credibility on key issues. Furthermore, Fiorina subtly criticized former Florida Governor Jeb Bush, a candidate who had experienced some stumbles on the campaign trail. "**We need a nominee who is going to throw every punch, not pull punches, and someone who cannot stumble before he even gets into the ring**," Fiorina asserted, drawing a contrast between her own perceived readiness and Bush's perceived vulnerability. Philip Rucker from Cleveland also contributed to this report.

The Republican candidates excluded from the prime-time GOP presidential debate faced a stark reminder of their underdog status on Thursday evening. Moderators repeatedly posed questions designed to highlight their long-shot campaigns, essentially asking, "Why are you even here?" Amidst this challenging environment, Carly Fiorina emerged as a potential breakout star of the undercard debate. Pundits and instant polls widely recognized her as the clear winner, attributing this to a polished and commanding performance that contrasted with the struggles of other participants to gain traction.

This secondary forum, held at 5 p.m. and broadcast before the main 9 p.m. Fox News event, was for candidates who did not meet the polling thresholds for prime time. This group included former governors, senators, and a business executive, many of whom had already expressed frustration with the debate qualification metrics. Their exclusion was partly attributed to the prominence of Donald Trump, whose vocal presence dominated discussions. Fox News moderators Martha MacCallum and Bill Hemmer amplified the candidates' underdog positions by initiating the debate with questions underscoring their faltering poll numbers. For instance, Fiorina was questioned about the validity of comparing herself to Margaret Thatcher, Rick Santorum was asked if his political moment had passed, and Bobby Jindal was confronted with his declining poll numbers in Louisiana. These overtly skeptical inquiries were met with varying degrees of good humor by the candidates.

The participants in the earlier forum included former Governors Rick Perry, George Pataki, and Jim Gilmore; Louisiana Governor Bobby Jindal; South Carolina Senator Lindsey Graham; former Senator Rick Santorum; and former Hewlett-Packard CEO Carly Fiorina, the sole female candidate in the Republican field. Senator Graham had notably referred to this event as the "happy hour debate." They presented their platforms in a sparsely attended auditorium, enduring remarks that some perceived as patronizing. Bill Hemmer, for example, commended their adherence to time limits, stating, "You’re all very well-behaved so far."
