Inside was an Oracle putter with my name engraved on the back of its face. Dr. V had spent an hour on the phone getting my specifications — the length of my fingers, the distance between my wrist and the ground, which of my eyes was dominant. She then spent another half-hour talking me through drills to show me how to use the club. The concept of zero MOI had remained abstract until the moment I first swung the Oracle. While other putters twisted when you pulled them back, Dr. V’s didn’t — a reflection, perhaps, of the stability needed to design wings for the stealth bomber, which she often said was her inspiration when building the putter. It seemed as if all I had to do was hold the club, pick a line, and hit the ball, then watch it roll smoothly in that direction. The club didn’t fight me. I then went to a public course to try the Oracle on some actual greens. I didn’t make every putt — far from it. But I did seem to sink more than usual. And like McCord, the more I used the putter the more I became its unofficial pitchman. I began to look forward to the “oohs” and “ahhs” from strangers when they would first see me use the club to pick the ball out of the hole. I enjoyed telling the wild story behind the putter’s invention. It turned a normal round of golf into an act of seduction. And it was all because Dr. V’s club had me putting with a lot more confidence. I was ready to proclaim her an unknown genius with an idea that could revolutionize golf. All that was left to do was make sure the stories about engineering accolades and top-secret defense projects were legit. It was, I thought, just a formality. I started with Dr. V’s biggest accomplishment — her work on the stealth bomber. The Department of Defense could not confirm her employment without a Social Security number, and I figured that Dr. V wouldn’t want to share hers. So I contacted Aviation Week senior international defense editor Bill Sweetman, who had written a book on the plane. Sweetman said there was no way to confirm Dr. V’s work without forcing her into a compromising position, since stealth workers signed lifetime nondisclosure agreements. “It would not be surprising if she worked on the B-2,” he wrote in an email, “and that she would not want to talk about it if she did.” He was certainly right about that. I emailed Dr. V to tell her how much I loved her putter. I also told her that an equation-heavy document she had sent me called “The Inertia Matrix,” which further explained how to use the Oracle, had looked too confusing for me to follow. Finally, I asked if she could help me confirm a few facts about her past life. When I heard back, the patient woman I had spoken to on the phone had been replaced by an angry, mocking scientist. She wrote: As I clearly stated at the onset of your unsolicited probing, your focus must be on the benefits of the Science for the Golfer not the scientist, however, at this juncture you are in reversal of your word, as well as neophytic in your modus operandi of understanding the science of Yar. If you were observant or should I state; had the mental aptitude of ratiocination you would have gleaned or inquired about the advantages of the Inertia Matrix … If you are what you presented yourself to [Gary McCord], as a golf nut, then you should be in shock and awe that someone has given the golfing milieu a scientific breakthrough as revolutionary as the two-piece rubber core golf ball was a hundred years ago!!! The email was a surprise. Dr. V’s initial requests for privacy had seemed reasonable. Now, however, they felt like an attempt to stop me from writing about her or the company she’d founded. But why? It didn’t take long to uncover some serious discrepancies in her story. I contacted the registrar’s office at MIT. It had no record of anyone named Essay Anne Vanderbilt attending. The registrar at the University of Pennsylvania confirmed the same thing. Whatever Dr. V’s actual credentials, they didn’t include a business degree from Wharton, where she had supposedly gotten her MBA. This was significant but inconclusive. After all, Dr. V could have attended the schools under a different name. But why wouldn’t she have mentioned that? The deeper I looked, the stranger things got. It seemed as if there was no record of Dr. V’s existence prior to the early 2000s. And what little I managed to find didn’t exactly align with the image she projected of a world-class scientist. I couldn’t find any record of her ever living in Boston. The same went for Washington, D.C. And when I contacted Walter Reed, I was told the hospital had no way to prove she had ever worked there. I also found a lawsuit filed against the town of Gilbert, Arizona, in July 2007. The plaintiff’s name: Essay Vanderbilt, who had accused the town and three of its employees of sexual discrimination. The suit alleged that the previous year Vanderbilt was working as a “vehicle service writer” in Gilbert’s Fleet Management Division. In other words, at the same time that Dr. V claimed to have been working on top-secret government projects in D.C., she was actually coordinating car repairs for a Phoenix suburb. Vanderbilt didn’t win her case. And in 2011, a civil court in Maricopa County, Arizona, ordered her to pay nearly $800,000 to a commercial developer. That judgment may have been the reason why, later that year, Vanderbilt filed for bankruptcy, listing assets of less than $50,000 and liabilities of more than $1 million. At this point I was still hoping everything I’d found was all a big misunderstanding. I wanted to believe Dr. V’s story. After all, the putter worked. People who knew a lot more than me about golf swore by the club. There were even logical explanations for much of what I had uncovered: Dr. V could have gone to school under a different name; she could have mixed up the dates while telling the story of when she founded Yar; she could have taken the job in Gilbert as an extra source of income to pay her bills; and she may have filed for bankruptcy simply because the golf club business can be cutthroat, and Yar had struggled financially before catching a hot streak in the past year. I was still clinging to these threads when Leland Frische came along and snipped them all. ♦♦♦ Frische is the risk manager for Gilbert, and he had been there when Vanderbilt first came to work for the town. He said she was hired in April 2006 and there were problems almost immediately. Vanderbilt had applied to be the manager of the fleet services division, but she lost out on that job to someone else. She believed she was more qualified, however, and others complained that she did not try to hide that. “She would confront her boss in open meetings,” Frische told me. “She would talk down to people. She really didn’t give us many options.” The town eventually fired Vanderbilt. Not long after, she filed her lawsuit. And that’s when something weird happened, Frische told me. The town’s lawyers began investigating her background. Like me, they found some big holes, namely an education history she claimed to have but didn’t. The town’s lawyers also suspected that at one point she might have been known by a different name, and they asked her to reveal it. When she refused, the judge asked her to sign an affidavit saying she had always gone by Essay Anne Vanderbilt. She refused that request, too, and with it forfeited the right to continue her lawsuit. Frische said Gilbert’s search had ended there. But while we spoke on the phone, he started saying things that sounded odd to me. “Have you ever seen her in person?” he asked. “What I really hope for you is that you could meet her someday,” he said at another point in the conversation, from what seemed out of nowhere. He was clearly trying to tell me something, which is why he began emphasizing certain words. Every time he said “she” or “her” I could practically see him making air quotes. Finally it hit me. Cliché or not, a chill actually ran up my spine. “Are you trying to tell me that Essay Anne Vanderbilt was once a man?” It took a moment for him to respond. “I cannot confirm or deny anything on that,” he said, sounding once again like a risk manager. “But let me ask you a question. How far have you looked into her background?” ♦♦♦ Here is what I now know about Dr. Essay Anne Vanderbilt, inventor of the Oracle GX1 putter. She was born a boy on July 12, 1953, in Philadelphia. She was given the name Stephen Krol, a person who has not received degrees from MIT or the University of Pennsylvania. She has been married at least twice, and the brother of one of Krol’s ex-wives says Dr. V has two children, possibly more. She was once a mechanic at a Sunoco station that she also may have run in Bucks County, Pennsylvania. She filed and subsequently dropped a lawsuit against Sunoco. She moved to Arizona at some point after marrying her second wife in 1997. She ended up in Bonney Lake, Washington, a short time later. She filed a “petition for change of name” on October 14, 2003, in the Pierce County, Washington, District Court. She scratched out an unsuccessful first attempt at writing “Essay” on that petition. She wrote “OLD NAME DOES NOT MATCH ME” where the court paperwork asked why she no longer wanted to be known as Stephen Krol. She worked as general manager at Trax Bar and Grill, an LGBT bar in Kent, Washington. She was the subject of three separate harassment claims from her time there, including one from a male coworker who said she made “inappropriate comments about her breasts and genitalia.” She moved to Arizona again sometime later. She met Gerri Jordan. She built a putter. She met Gary McCord. She told me the focus should be on the science and not the scientist. What little else I know about Stephen Krol in the years before and after he changed his name comes from people who knew him, but didn’t know him well. My attempts to get in touch with members of his family and his ex-wives were unsuccessful. Some people didn’t pick up or return my calls. Others, like Ewa Kroll, whose name showed up alongside his in searches and whose relationship to Stephen I still haven’t been able to parse, hustled me off the phone as quickly as possible. “I have not talked to him for years,” she said. “I’m just going to have to say ‘good-bye’ now.” The darkest discovery was something that occurred after Krol had decided to live as Dr. V. In 2008, she tried to kill herself with an overdose of prescription drugs and carbon monoxide poisoning from closing herself in a garage with her car running. A police report offered some explanations for why she might have tried to take her own life — Yar’s business was slow and Dr. V’s romantic relationship was on the ropes. She had recently fought with her girlfriend, Gerri Jordan, president of Yar Golf. Jordan told police that she and Dr. V were in a monogamous relationship and that they had gotten into an argument two days before. She had found Dr. V in the passenger seat of her car after the suicide attempt and tried to keep her awake. Jordan had also presumably been the first person to read the suicide note Dr. V had taped to the window of the car door, which read in part, “Tell Gorgeous Gerri that I love her.” ♦♦♦ What began as a story about a brilliant woman with a new invention had turned into the tale of a troubled man who had invented a new life for himself. Yet the biggest question remained unanswered: Had Dr. V created a great golf club or merely a great story? She had faked the credentials that made the science behind her club seem legitimate. But the more I talked to people in the world of club design, the more I came to understand that many believed the physics behind the Oracle putter were solid, even if the “scientist” was not. I found Kelvin Miyahira, a golf instructor in Hawaii with no ties to Yar who nonetheless had become one of its biggest fans. Miyahira had used a high-speed camera to compare the Oracle with other, more popular putters. In slow-motion videos he posted to YouTube, he showed that when he used the Oracle, it was more stable and rolled the ball more smoothly and with less sidespin than any of the other clubs he tried. Champions Tour player David Frost had once received an hour-long putting lesson from Dr. V and four days later had won a tournament by tying the lowest score ever recorded on that course. The information Dr. V had imparted to him was so valuable, Frost told me, that he wasn’t even willing to share it. Maybe if I’d had the same access, the Oracle would have remained as effective for me. But positive contagion, at least in my case, only seemed to work when I believed I was still infected. When I was under the impression that Dr. V was a brilliant engineer, my putting improved dramatically. As soon as I learned she had simply been a struggling mechanic, the magic was lost. Today, Dr. V’s Oracle is collecting dust in my garage. The other question to consider was if any of the lies actually mattered. Yes, Dr. V had fabricated a résumé that helped sell the Oracle putter under false pretenses. But she was far from the first clubmaker to attach questionable scientific value to a piece of equipment just to make it more marketable. Sure, her lies were more audacious than the embellishments found in late-night infomercials. But her ultimate intent — to make a few bucks, or, maybe, to be known as a genius — remained the same. Whatever the answers, Gary McCord would not be able to help me find them. The man who had once been so willing to talk stopped responding to my emails. Finally, a spokesperson at CBS told me that McCord had “nothing more to add to the story.” That left Jordan and Dr. V. I called them both, and realized that they had given me the same phone number. Dr. V had said the number was for her lab with the “switchboard personnel.” This time, though, no one answered and I heard the outgoing message. What sounded like a young girl’s voice filled the receiver: “Thank you for calling Essay Vanderbilt and family …” The next day I tried again. No answer still, but the recording had changed. Instead of a young girl, the voice was Jordan’s: “Hello, you’ve reached the offices of Yar Golf …” ♦♦♦ I was under the belief that what had transpired at Yar was ultimately harmless until I heard from a mysterious “silent investor” whom both Jordan and Dr. V had alluded to in our previous talks. His name was Phil Kinney. He was a retiree from Pittsburgh and he said he wasn’t the only one who had put money into the company. He had invested $60,000 — money that he believed he’d never see again. It wasn’t that Kinney didn’t love Yar’s putter or have high hopes for its future. He had loved it from the moment he met Dr. V at a convention four years ago. (Before I told him about her past, he told me that because of her height and vivid red hair, it was hard to miss the “pretty woman walking toward me in a miniskirt.”) He still loved the club enough to sell it to friends and clients, too. But he had also come to know the frustrations of working with Dr. V. Kinney had heard his own share of incredible claims. Dr. V had told him that she was a $1,000-an-hour consultant. She said she was one of the original designers of Bluetooth technology. She even suggested that her status as a Vanderbilt provided access to some exclusive company who could help Yar’s business. Kinney said Dr. V told him she was good friends with the Hilton family, and that the relationship would pay off in the form of putters sold at their hotels. Kinney also recalled a trip he had taken to Arizona where, in Dr. V’s house, she had shown him a computer that she said mirrored the one in Phoenix’s airport traffic control tower. For all her wild stories, though, what Dr. V was most, Kinney said, was a difficult person to deal with. “She would just explode. If you’re disagreeing with her while she had one of her headaches, you were in trouble.” And Kinney often disagreed with Dr. V. He tried to get her to change the design of the putter. She wouldn’t budge. He tried to get her to change Yar’s confusing website. She had the same reaction. He even tried to convince Dr. V to let well-known club designers like Bobby Grace, whom Kinney said wanted to invest, buy into the company. “She just told me, ‘We don’t need him.'” It seemed unlikely that Yar would ever deliver a return on Kinney’s investment. Maybe the most surprising thing about my conversation with Kinney was how calmly he took the news that the woman he thought was an aerospace engineer had once been a man, and a mechanic. “I’m pretty dang gullible, I guess,” he said. For all the hassle that came with his partnership with Dr. V, what had kept him going was the putter. That was what Kinney couldn’t understand. If Yar had simply been a scam, the story would have been much simpler. But the Oracle worked. And Dr. V seemed more interested in achieving fame as a club designer than in getting rich. “She could have took my money and ran,” he said. “But she didn’t. She took it and built a great product.” Kinney said he was worried that the putter’s excellence would be lost in the strange tale of Dr. V. He genuinely believed the Oracle was a superior product. But at one point near the end of our conversation, he had a thought that seemed to trouble him. “Maybe I liked it because she convinced me before I even hit it,” he said. “Maybe it’s not as good as I think. Someone tells me a story, I believe it.” ♦♦♦ The last time I heard from Dr. V she warned me that I was about to commit a hate crime. But before that, I received a voice mail from Jordan. Neither of them had contacted me in months, since I had sent an email trying to confirm what I had discovered, and Jordan wrote back to deny everything. “Your attack tale should be published in the National Enquirer,” Jordan wrote, “right next to the article on Martians … If I am to believe your diatribe, what you are telling golfers is that the most scientifically advanced Near Zero MOI putter, and the science of the Inertia Matrix was invented by a lesbian auto mechanic.” Now, Jordan’s message said she was calling to propose a deal. When I phoned her back, Jordan explained the offer. I could fly to Arizona and meet with Dr. V at her attorney’s office, where she would show me proof of her degrees from both MIT and the University of Pennsylvania. Dr. V then got on the phone and added another detail. Once I saw the documents I would have to sign a nondisclosure agreement barring me from revealing any of the details I’d learned about Dr. V’s past. The “deal” was one I could not accept, and when I explained this Dr. V got upset. “What is your intention?” she asked. “Are you being paid by someone to destroy Yar?” Dr. V’s anger made it so that what she said came out fast and with almost no interruption. I tried to record everything she said and ask the occasional question, but it was like yelling into a wind tunnel. When she finally had said her piece, she handed the phone back to Jordan. “Well, I guess you’re just going to print what you’re going to print,” Jordan said. “Try to lead a decent life. Have a good one.” Then she hung up. A few days later, Dr. V sent one final email. It had her signature mix of scattered punctuation and randomly capitalized words. Once upon a time I had brushed off these grammatical quirks, but now they seemed like outward expressions of the inner chaos she struggled to contain. “To whom this may concern,” it read. “I spoke with Caleb Hannan last Saturday his deportment is reminiscent to schoolyard bullies, his sole intention is to injure or bring harm to me … Because of a computer glitch, some documents that are germane only to me, were visible to web-viewers, government officials have now rectified this egregious condition … Caleb Hannan came into possession of documents that were clearly marked: MADE NON-PUBLIC (Restricted) … Exposing NON-PUBLIC Documents is a Crime, and prosecution of such are under the auspices of many State and Federal Laws, including Hate Crimes Legislation signed into Law by President Obama.” Over the course of what was now eight months of reporting, Dr. V had accused me of being everything from a corporate spy to a liar and a fraud. She had also threatened me. One of the quotes I was able to type down during our last conversation was this: “You have no idea what I have done and what I can do.” It’s not all that menacing when transcribed, but her tone made it clear she believed she could harm me. Yet despite all that, the main emotion I felt while reading her desperate, last-ditch email was sadness. Although there were times when I had been genuinely thrilled with the revelation that Dr. V’s official narrative didn’t line up with reality, there was nothing satisfying about where the story had ended up. People had been hurt by Dr. V’s lies, but she was the person who seemed to be suffering most. Not long after she sent her email, I got a call from a Pennsylvania phone number that I didn’t recognize. It was Dr. V’s ex-brother-in-law, who represented the closest I had gotten to finding someone who could tell me what she’d been like in her previous life. “Well, there’s one less con man in the world now,” he said. Even though he hated his former family member, this seemed like an especially cruel way to tell me that Dr. V had died. All he could tell me was what he knew — that it had been a suicide. A few weeks later a police report filled in the details. Around 11 a.m. on October 18, Jordan walked into the home office she shared with Dr. V and found pieces from her business partner’s jewelry collection laid out on a desk next to some handwritten letters. Each letter explained which friend or family member was to get which piece of jewelry in case of Dr. V’s death. Jordan then noticed that Dr. V’s car was missing. At first, Jordan explained to the police, she didn’t think much of the missing vehicle. Jordan prepared some breakfast and then drove to her nearby apartment. When Jordan arrived and reached her bedroom, she found Dr. V lying on the floor curled in a fetal position with a white plastic bag over her head; an empty bottle of pills sat on the kitchen counter. Writing a eulogy for a person who by all accounts despised you is an odd experience. What makes it that much harder is that Dr. V left so few details — on purpose, of course. Those who knew her in her past life refused to talk about her. Those who knew her in the life she had created were helpful right up to the point where that new life began to look like a lie. The only person who can provide this strange story with its proper ending is the person who started it. The words she spoke came during our last conversation, when she was frantically trying to convince me of things I knew couldn’t possibly be true. Yet though they may have been spoken by a desperate person at one of the most desperate times in a life that had apparently seen many, it’s hard to argue with Dr. V’s conclusions. “Nobody knows my life but me,” she said. “You don’t know what the truth is.”

The tragic suicide of Essay Anne Vanderbilt, a transgender woman whose story was the subject of the Grantland article "Dr. V's Magical Putter," has ignited a crucial conversation about journalistic ethics, particularly concerning transgender individuals. This incident highlights the profound impact reporting can have and raises critical questions about how sensitive stories are handled.

### Ethical Breaches in Reporting

The Grantland piece, initially lauded for its unusual scientific focus on Vanderbilt and her seemingly magical golf putter, later faced intense scrutiny. Following a critical examination by Melissa McEwan, significant and avoidable errors by writer Caleb Hannan and Grantland came to light. These missteps are widely believed to have contributed to Vanderbilt's decision to end her life, transforming the article from a piece about a golf club into a complex case study of reporting, writing, and editing failures.

### The Unfolding Narrative and its Controversies

Discussions surrounding the article have been extensive, spanning various platforms and forums dedicated to trans* issues and journalism. While some have defended Hannan, many hold him responsible for Vanderbilt's death. Amidst this debate, several key points of consensus have emerged regarding the ethical boundaries crossed.

Initially, Hannan's reporting itself was not the primary point of contention. Vanderbilt had misrepresented her credentials, claiming degrees from MIT and Penn and involvement in classified military projects. A journalist investigating such claims would naturally seek to verify them. Hannan's discovery of Vanderbilt's transgender identity was incidental to this verification process.

However, Hannan's decision to reveal Vanderbilt's transgender status to a Yar Golf investor crossed a significant ethical boundary. This action stripped Vanderbilt of her autonomy in disclosing deeply personal information. As Christina Kahrl observed, "revealing her gender identity was ultimately as dangerous as it was thoughtless."

### Dehumanizing Narrative Choices

Furthermore, specific narrative choices, such as detailing Vanderbilt's past as a man and her suicide, coupled with insensitive language, were dehumanizing. The article framed her death as a mere consequence of a sensational fraud narrative, rather than acknowledging the profound tragedy of a woman's suicide.

### Ambiguity and Editorial Failures

Beyond these points, numerous critiques highlight the article's failure to adequately explain its own actions. For instance, the text provides no evidence of Hannan aggressively harassing Vanderbilt or threatening to out her. Instead, it suggests he uncovered information, offered her a chance to respond, and continued his investigation. Conversely, the article also fails to demonstrate humane reporting by emphasizing his intent to verify facts without controlling her personal choices. Kahrl noted that, based on the published text, it's reasonable to assume Hannan did not "reassure her that her gender identity wasn't germane to the broader problems he'd uncovered with her story."

This ambiguity allows for subjective interpretations. Journalists and editors might empathize with the writer, while trans* individuals and allies may view the events through a different lens, a divergence stemming from writing and editing flaws.

### The Core of the Problem: Writing and Editing

Journalism encompasses reporting, writing, and editing. Hannan's primary reporting error appears to be outing Vanderbilt to an investor. While he gathered and sought to verify information, and at times retreated, any omissions regarding his intentions about her transgender status are his to bear, but cannot be definitively linked to her death without further insight. Attributing suicide directly to journalistic mistakes is an oversimplification of a deeply complex issue.

Clear writing and editing errors are evident. By structuring the story as a chronological mystery, Hannan felt compelled to reveal Vanderbilt's transgender identity to fill narrative gaps, making it the story's emotional core. Steve Silberman noted that Grantland's editor-in-chief, Bill Simmons, indicated the story was largely complete before Vanderbilt's suicide, suggesting Hannan believed outing her was acceptable to bolster his claims and discredit her invention.

Ultimately, poor writing is published with editorial approval, with the most serious issues lying in editing. Line editing problems include misgendering Vanderbilt in references to her past and emphasizing Hannan's "chill" upon learning she was transgender. Critics interpreted this as malicious ignorance, though it could also represent the moment the story's inconsistencies became clear to Hannan. While not malicious, the narrative's overemphasis on her gender identity created this perception.

### A Flawed Conception and Missed Opportunities

More critically, the article's fundamental conception is flawed. Published three months after Vanderbilt's death, "Dr. V's Magic Putter" presented a writer confronting the potential impact of his work in the most tragic circumstances. The final article shows little curiosity about Vanderbilt's death itself. At some point, the focus should have shifted from a golf putter to a woman's death. If Hannan couldn't reframe the narrative, someone else should have. A story beginning with her death and exploring the events leading up to it, including her gender identity, would have been profoundly different. Gerri Jordan's suggestion of focusing on "the most scientifically advanced Near Zero MOI putter, and the science of the Inertia Matrix... invented by a lesbian auto mechanic" highlights a potentially compelling narrative: an auto mechanic who fabricated credentials to gain recognition for her innovative golf invention. How Grantland's editorial team missed these opportunities remains unclear, with sources at ESPN reportedly questioning the absence of respected journalist John Walsh.

### The "Grantland Problem" and Editorial Priorities

Bill Simmons's apology, while seemingly earnest, is characteristically self-centered, dedicating more space to his ambitions for the site than to Vanderbilt. This reflects Grantland's premise that the appeal of sports lies in the writers' narratives, often overshadowing the subjects themselves. This fixation leaves a void in Grantland's apology, mirroring the original article's unanswered questions.

As a prestige publication, Grantland prioritized perceived quality over actual quality, serving as a vehicle for Bill Simmons's vision, gifted by ESPN to showcase his value. Such editorial failures can occur at any publication where editors lack sufficient attunement to trans* issues. However, this particular breakdown exemplifies the broader "Grantland problem," intrinsically linked to the "Bill Simmons problem." It reflects a philosophy where form trumps function, perception outweighs substance, and large-scale success justifies itself. ESPN president John Skipper reportedly desires awards and recognition for Grantland, aiming for the site to compete with publications known for sensational, unbelievable stories. Last week, Grantland found such a story, but it revealed more about Grantland's shortcomings and the troubles they can cause than about Dr. V and her magical putter.

query: What reasons are given for Kim Kardashian and Kanye West's desire to have another child soon after the birth of North West?

documents.text:
### Kim Kardashian and Kanye West's Family Planning

Just a few months after welcoming their daughter, North West, into the world, Kim Kardashian and Kanye West are reportedly already considering expanding their family. Sources close to the couple reveal that discussions about having another child are serious and happening "right away."

#### Reasons for a Swift Second Pregnancy

The impetus behind this quick decision appears to stem from several factors. At 32, Kardashian is reportedly mindful of her age and the desire to build a larger family. Additionally, she's expressed a pragmatic approach to pregnancy, suggesting it might be more efficient to endure a single, extended period of pregnancy and weight gain, followed by a definitive weight loss journey, rather than experiencing multiple cycles of gaining and losing weight.

#### Kanye's Perspective and Parental Bliss

While Kardashian's physical concerns are a consideration, Kanye West's feelings are also a driving force. A source indicates that West is supportive of Kardashian at any size but is enthusiastic about the prospect of another child soon. The couple has been immersed in the joys of parenthood since North's birth, and reports suggest West is proving to be a surprisingly devoted father. Despite no immediate wedding plans, their focus remains on their growing family.

#### The Possibility of Rapid Succession

While a definitive decision hasn't been made, the ease with which Kardashian conceived their first child suggests a swift second pregnancy is a distinct possibility. This approach mirrors that of other celebrities who have welcomed children in close succession. For instance, Jessica Simpson became pregnant with her second child less than six months after her first. Britney Spears also had children spaced only a few months apart. Tori Spelling holds a notable record, discovering her third pregnancy just one month after giving birth to her second child.

**Kim Kardashian and Kanye West are reportedly planning for another baby soon after welcoming daughter North West.**

Just four months after the birth of their daughter, North West, **Kim Kardashian and Kanye West are already discussing expanding their family.** A source close to the couple revealed to RadarOnline.com, "**They are discussing having another baby right away. It’s serious.**"

The apparent rush stems from Kardashian's desire to have a larger family. "**She is well aware that she’s getting older, and that if she wants to have a bigger family, then she needs to get it done,**" the source explained. Additionally, Kardashian is reportedly considering the logistics of multiple pregnancies. "**Kim is telling Kanye that she doesn’t think it makes sense to gain pregnancy weight, lose it, and then gain it all back again. She’s starting to think she might want to just suck it up and be big and pregnant in one long go and then lose the weight once and for all.**"

While Kardashian's appearance is not a concern for West, he is enthusiastic about the prospect of another child. "**Kanye likes Kim at any size, so that part doesn’t matter to him. But he loves the idea of having another baby soon,**" the source stated. The couple has been enjoying parenthood since North's arrival, despite having no immediate plans for marriage. "**Both of them are so blown away with her. And Kanye is a better father than anyone ever imagined he would be,**" the source added.

Although a definitive decision hasn't been made, the source believes it's only a matter of time before North has siblings. "**Kim and Kanye believe what will be will be, but since she got pregnant so easily the first time around, Kim could be pregnant again soon!**"

This rapid succession of children is not uncommon among celebrities. For instance, Jessica Simpson became pregnant with her son Ace less than six months after her daughter Maxwell was born. Britney Spears welcomed her son Sean in November 2005 and was pregnant with her son Jayden about three months later. **Tori Spelling holds the record, discovering she was pregnant with her son Finn just one month after giving birth to her daughter, Hattie.**

query: How does the episode 'The Queen's Justice' reflect the evolving dynamics between Jon Snow and Daenerys Targaryen as leaders?

documents.text:
"The Queen's Justice," the third episode of the season, delivered **long-awaited character reunions and plot developments**. While some encounters resonated more than others, the episode's primary goal was to **establish new obstacles for Daenerys Targaryen**. Her arrival with dragons does not guarantee an effortless conquest. This installment focused on **weaving together the diverse narrative threads** established over the previous seven seasons.

A **pivotal moment** was the reunion of Jon Snow and Tyrion Lannister. They exchanged greetings marked by **mutual respect**, a rare and hard-won commodity for both men in Westeros. Their initial meeting in Season 1 found Jon as the Bastard of Winterfell and Tyrion as the ostracized son of House Lannister. Now, Jon stands as King in the North, and Tyrion serves as Hand to the invading Queen. The episode underscored the symbolic convergence of "Ice and Fire," a concept even referenced by Melisandre. However, their interactions, both in the throne room and in private, were **strictly formal, reflecting their roles as heads of state**. The narrative strongly suggests a need for **greater personal chemistry between Jon and Daenerys**, hinting at future scenes where they might interact as ordinary individuals.

Cersei Lannister's arc showcased her **capacity for both cruelty and vulnerability**. While she grieved Myrcella's death, her method of murdering Tyene Sand inflicted a **far more brutal torment on Ellaria Sand** than Ellaria's actions had on Cersei. Cersei was spared the agonizing sight of Myrcella's body decaying, though the thought clearly haunted her. She had previously spoken of picturing her mother and daughter's faces. In this episode, she inflicted a **real-life torture on Ellaria**, mirroring her own psychological anguish. Cersei also displayed a **newfound flagrancy**, a stark departure from past seasons where she would have concealed her relationship with Jaime. The episode also touched upon the **influence of the Iron Bank**, a modern power in a world steeped in magic. Cersei's **manipulative prowess and inherent evil** were evident, drawing a parallel to how businessmen accept the slave trade as long as it doesn't incite revolution.

The reunion of Bran and Sansa Stark, though a genuine sibling encounter, felt **underwhelming**. Bran's **impassive reaction** to his sister's embrace and his preoccupation with his "Three-Eyed Raven" identity left Sansa yearning for more practical engagement. His experiences beyond the Wall have **estranged him from typical human interaction**, suggesting his destiny is tied to something beyond the castle's confines. Sansa, however, is demonstrating a **growing capability to manage castle logistics independently**.

Meanwhile, Samwell Tarly was **not expelled from the Citadel**. The episode hinted at the **significant value of the ancient scrolls and books** he is transcribing, suggesting the Maesters recognized his **aptitude for learning**. A **note of caution** was sounded regarding Jorah Mormont, with Sam advised against shaking his hand due to the potential for lingering infection.

The Unsullied's successful capture of Casterly Rock, achieved by entering through the drains, was a **seemingly brilliant but perhaps overly convenient victory**. The whereabouts of the remaining Lannisters were left **deliberately unclear**. The scene featuring Bronn riding with the Lannisters, despite his mercenary nature, offered a **somber reflection**.

Lady Olenna Tyrell's final moments were characterized by a **calm acceptance of her fate**. Her ability to speak so serenely to Jaime, knowing her life's purpose, including her quest for revenge, was extinguished, served as a **testament to her formidable character**. The **respect for Diana Rigg's performance** was palpable in the dignified portrayal of her death, with her final frame evoking a Flemish Renaissance painting.

The episode explored the concept of "Queen's Justice" as **inherently flawed when enacted by and towards powerful women**. Cersei's revenge was excessive, Ellaria's vengeance was turned against her, Yara received none (yet), Daenerys's justice appeared to be faltering, and Olenna would not achieve hers. The reactions of these women highlighted Cersei's extreme actions, yet her **unvarnished villainy conveyed more raw emotion** than Daenerys's meticulously crafted imperial persona. Daenerys's consistent regal pronouncements over many episodes left viewers longing for a scene where she might act like a **normal young woman**. However, the preview for the subsequent episode, "The Spoils of War," suggested a **potential crack in her composed facade**.

## Olenna Tyrell: A Queen's Final Gambit and a Legacy of Wit

This analysis delves into Season 7, Episode 3 of Game of Thrones, "The Queen's Justice." Viewer discretion is advised for those not yet privy to the episode's significant plot developments.

### A Poisoned Chalice, A Dignified Departure

Lady Olenna Tyrell's demise in "The Queen's Justice" was, by Westerosi standards, remarkably dignified. Her final moments, granted by Jaime Lannister, underscored her indomitable spirit and her unwavering control over her own fate. With her passing, the venerable House Tyrell, as depicted in the series, effectively ceases to exist, the narrative having focused solely on prominent members like Olenna and Margaery. The iconic rose has indeed withered. This poignant end casts a stark light on Olenna's earlier assertion in Episode 2, where she claimed to have outlasted wiser counsel by disregarding it. While her sharp wit was undeniably captivating, Daenerys Targaryen might find continued prudence in heeding Tyrion Lannister's strategic advice.

### The Enduring Echo of the Queen of Thorns

Though the Queen of Thorns has departed the stage, her indelible legacy persists. As the geopolitical landscape of Westeros has intensified, the HBO adaptation has afforded less space for the biting, acid-laced repartee that Olenna wielded with such masterful precision. This tribute serves as a moment to recall the more spirited, epigrammatic exchanges that defined Olenna's tenure, celebrating Dame Diana Rigg's unforgettable portrayal.

#### Olenna's Disdain for the Iron Throne

Long before it became a popular sentiment, Olenna Tyrell had already dismissed the Iron Throne as a mere bauble, as early as Season 3. Her focus consistently transcended the superficial allure of the seat of power.

#### Olenna's Critique of the Tyrell Motto

Lady Olenna, originally of House Redwyne, harbored immense pride in her family's considerable influence and affluence. However, this did not equate to an uncritical endorsement of all its tenets. Her assessment of the Tyrell house words, "Growing Strong," as rather pedestrian, proved prescient. Olenna was unafraid to employ even a well-timed jest, much to Sansa Stark's mortification, to underscore her disdain for the ostentatious floral motif. She was even prepared to make significant sacrifices within House Tyrell, sparing only her beloved Margaery, to emphasize her point. One can only speculate on the more potent house words she might have conceived.

#### Olenna's Pragmatic Alliance with Sansa Stark

Olenna exhibited a surprising degree of leniency towards Sansa Stark, a rarity within the machinations of King's Landing. This benevolence, however, was largely pragmatic, with Sansa serving as a crucial intelligence conduit regarding Joffrey Baratheon and later as a potential political pawn for Loras Tyrell. Nevertheless, Olenna's decision to entrust Loras, the supposed future of House Tyrell, to Sansa's care was a significant gesture. Yet, in Sansa's absence, Olenna's true sentiments were often more candidly expressed.

#### Olenna's Selective Tolerance of Service Staff

Olenna's casual dismissal of a serving boy for his cheese was, in retrospect, a minor transgression. Her sharp tongue, however, became less amusing when directed at more vulnerable individuals like Podrick Payne, suggesting a need for greater deference.

#### Olenna's Masterful Kingslaying

It is difficult to dispute that Olenna Tyrell aimed to surpass all previous acts of regicide in Westeros with the audacious and ingenious manner in which she orchestrated King Joffrey Baratheon's demise at his own wedding, an event she generously financed. Her pre-nuptial conversation with Cersei Lannister, lamenting mothers' futile efforts to shield their sons, hinted at a chilling foresight. "We shower them with good sense," she remarked, referring as much to her son Mace as to Joffrey, "and it slides right off like rain off a wing." Her intentions became unequivocally clear when she subtly procured a poisoned gemstone from Sansa Stark's necklace, all while feigning sympathy. The juxtaposition of lamenting "monsters" at weddings and simultaneously pocketing her instrument of death was utterly frigid. She maintained an unnerving composure as Tyrion Lannister administered the poisoned goblet and even summoned aid for the dying king she had just murdered, demonstrating unparalleled panache in her kingslaying endeavors.

#### Olenna's Weariness with Gardens

The Queen of Thorns frequently cited the King's Landing gardens as her preferred venue for clandestine meetings, citing concerns about internal surveillance. However, this preference did not equate to genuine enjoyment. After a lifetime spent within the confines of Highgarden, a certain weariness with mere horticultural displays is understandable.

#### Olenna's Strategic Alliance with Varys

The purported meeting of minds between Olenna Tyrell and Varys, the Master of Whisperers, was a fascinating dynamic. They shared a surprising number of traits: a predilection for opulent attire, a mastery of secrets, and a talent for clandestine operations. When Olenna's initial overtures to Varys proved unsuccessful, primarily due to their competing interests regarding Sansa's betrothal to Loras, she aligned herself with the more unpredictable Petyr Baelish. It is arguable that House Tyrell's fortunes have since experienced a decline following this alliance.

#### Olenna's Verbal Sparring with Tyrion Lannister

The only other character in Game of Thrones possessing a verbal dexterity to rival Olenna's was Tyrion Lannister. Despite their limited shared screen time, Olenna emerged as the clear rhetorical victor in their sole significant exchange.

#### Olenna's Dismissal of Artisanal Efforts

The meticulous craftsmanship of King's Landing jewelers held little sway with the Queen of Thorns, who seemed unconcerned with the labor invested in their creations.

#### Olenna's Efficient Neutralization of the Sand Snakes

The Season 6 confrontation with the Sand Snakes, while perhaps catering to fan service, showcased Olenna's remarkable efficiency in neutralizing three Dornish warriors with decisive action.

#### Olenna's Ultimate Defeat by the High Sparrow

In the final analysis, one must concede that the High Sparrow ultimately bested Olenna Tyrell. While not in direct confrontation, his actions indirectly precipitated the decimation of her family. Nevertheless, the epithet "little fellow" remains a brilliantly dismissive characterization of the High Septon.

#### Olenna's Respectful Rivalry with Tywin Lannister

Few individuals managed to challenge Tywin Lannister effectively, but Olenna consistently held her own against the Lannister patriarch. She a) deemed his prized daughter, Cersei, too aged, b) insinuated his potential youthful dalliances with other boys, and c) brazenly alluded to the Lannisters' incestuous lineage. Tywin matched her every barb, a testament to their formidable intellects. It is plausible that, had circumstances differed, these two formidable figures might have forged a powerful ruling partnership.

#### Olenna's Fatal Confrontation with Cersei Lannister

Ultimately, Olenna's most formidable adversary proved to be Cersei Lannister, the Queen Regent. Their conflict culminated in the tragic loss of each other's children. While one might be tempted to grant Olenna the advantage in this grim exchange, it is crucial to acknowledge that she initially planted the seed for the wildfire plot in Cersei's mind. By taunting Cersei about her isolation and surrounded by enemies, Olenna questioned her ability to prevail alone. As it transpired, Cersei proved capable of doing just that.

#### Olenna's Unwavering Esteem for Brienne of Tarth

This tribute concludes not on a note of failure, but on a high one. Throughout her extensive tenure on Game of Thrones, Olenna Tyrell consistently expressed praise for only one individual, aside from her granddaughter Margaery: Brienne of Tarth. The Queen of Thorns held Brienne in exceptionally high regard, a testament to Olenna's own discerning judgment and character assessment.

In the seven seasons of *Game of Thrones*, Tyrion Lannister has consistently demonstrated strategic acumen and resilience, often in defiance of his father Tywin's profound disapproval. While incapacitated during the Season 1 Battle of the Green Fork, Tyrion's pivotal contributions were instrumental in the victories at the Battle of Blackwater Bay and the Battle of Fire. However, the narrative arc of Season 7, Episode 3, "The Queen's Justice," positions Tyrion to confront a spectral echo of his paternal legacy. Trailer footage and strategic discussions between Tyrion and Grey Worm in Episode 2 strongly foreshadow Tyrion's calculated attempt to reclaim his ancestral seat, Casterly Rock, leveraging an Unsullied contingent.

Although Tyrion's current objective is to secure Casterly Rock for Queen Daenerys, a significant prior attempt to claim the stronghold occurred after his triumph at Blackwater in Season 2. As Hand of the King to Joffrey Baratheon, Tyrion formally petitioned his father, Tywin, for possession of Casterly Rock. Given Jaime Lannister's status as a Kingsguard knight, precluding inheritance, Tyrion was the legitimate heir. Tywin's response was unequivocally dismissive. When pressed, Tywin delivered a scathing indictment in a memorable Season 3 scene: "You are an ill-made, spiteful little creature full of envy, lust, and low cunning. Men’s laws give you the right to bear my name and display my colors since I cannot prove that you are not mine. And to teach me humility, the gods have condemned me to watch you waddle about wearing that proud lion that was my father’s sigil and his father’s before him. But neither gods nor men will ever compel me to let you turn Casterly Rock into your whorehouse. Go, now. Speak no more of your rights to Casterly Rock. Go!"

This deeply personal rejection undeniably informs Tyrion's present campaign. For aficionados of George R.R. Martin's *A Song of Ice and Fire* series, Casterly Rock transcends mere patrimony; it is the dynastic seat of House Lannister, imbued with the same profound significance for Tyrion as Winterfell is for the Starks. Despite a childhood likely devoid of positive recollections, Tyrion, within the literary canon, perceives Casterly Rock as an integral component of his identity. In *A Clash of Kings*, he articulates this sentiment: "Stone, I must be stone. I must be Casterly Rock, hard and unmovable." The Rock's prominence is consistently underscored throughout both the novels and the television adaptation, with Cersei Lannister's inaugural line in the HBO series referencing it.

Despite its narrative importance, Casterly Rock has not achieved the same cultural resonance as Winterfell or other ancestral strongholds. This is largely attributable to the absence of any depicted scenes within its walls in either the books or the show, though Martin has indicated its eventual appearance before the saga's conclusion. Tywin could scarcely have conceived of his younger son breaching Casterly Rock, a fortress renowned for its impregnability, having never been successfully besieged by a standing army. While it was previously subjugated, the dragonriders of Aegon's Conquest compelled Lannister submission only after a decisive military engagement, a relief to the Targaryens who feared the castle might even withstand dragonfire.

In Season 3, Robb Stark elucidated the formidable strategic challenges associated with assaulting Casterly Rock. The cliffside citadel is geographically secured by water to the west and allied territories to the east, rendering a direct overland advance from King's Landing infeasible. Furthermore, the maritime approaches to Lannisport are highly susceptible to incursions by Ironborn remnants loyal to Euron Greyjoy, whose primary base is Pyke, situated north of the Rock. As Jaime Lannister noted in Season 7, Episode 1, Euron systematically annihilated all vessels in Lannisport during the Greyjoy Rebellion. Consequently, Tyrion's forces would necessitate an extensive circumnavigation of Westeros' southern coastline, substantially diminishing the logistical support derivable from Dragonstone.

Tyrion, however, possesses a unique strategic advantage. His operational plan circumvents the necessity of a direct land assault or a conventional naval confrontation. As he confided to Varys in Season 2, Tyrion was once entrusted with the oversight of Casterly Rock's subterranean infrastructure during his youth, a network familiar to the Unsullied. The sole historical figure credited with the capture of Casterly Rock is Lann the Clever. According to apocryphal accounts, Lann gained ingress through a concealed, narrow passage. Martin's narrative in *The World of Ice & Fire* details how Lann, once inside, instigated psychological warfare against the slumbering Casterlys by whispering threats, emulating demonic vocalizations, and orchestrating the clandestine appropriation and redistribution of valuables. This calculated manipulation fostered an atmosphere of spectral dread, compelling the Casterlys to relinquish control of their stronghold.

This legendary feat of subterfuge and psychological manipulation resonates with Tyrion's own intellectual prowess and strategic acumen. Even if his current stratagem is less overtly theatrical, any perceived parallel between Lann the Clever and Tyrion would undoubtedly exacerbate the spectral torment of Tywin Lannister. Lann the Clever is the progenitor of House Lannister, and Tywin's paramount source of pride was his lineage, while his most profound source of shame was Tyrion's perceived lack of belonging. Nevertheless, Tyrion the Clever is unequivocally a Lannister.

Although Tywin Lannister met his demise in Season 4 (or Book 3), Casterly Rock endures as a potent symbol of the legacy he assiduously cultivated. In *A Dance with Dragons*, Nymeria Sand posits its conquest as the ultimate denouement for the Lannisters. "It ends," she declares, "when Casterly Rock is cracked open, so the sun can shine on the maggots and the worms within. It ends with the utter ruin of Tywin Lannister and all his works." Tywin could never have anticipated that his own son, the individual he consistently underestimated, would represent the most significant existential threat to the Lannister legacy and hold the key to breaching Casterly Rock from within.

In "The Queen's Justice," the latest Game of Thrones episode, Daenerys Targaryen faces further strategic hurdles. Following the significant blow in "Stormborn," the Mother of Dragons loses another key ally: Olenna Tyrell, the renowned "Queen of Thorns." Daenerys and her advisor, Tyrion Lannister, had planned to recover from Yara Greyjoy's fleet's defeat. Their strategy involved Grey Worm and the Unsullied launching a surprise attack on Casterly Rock, House Lannister's ancestral home.

However, Jaime and Cersei Lannister outmaneuvered their younger brother and the dragon queen. Driven by their debt to the Iron Bank of Braavos, the Lannisters strategically abandoned Casterly Rock. Instead, they focused their forces on Highgarden, the wealthy House Tyrell's stronghold. This house, led by Olenna Tyrell, had pledged loyalty to Daenerys after Cersei's devastating destruction of the remaining Tyrells in the previous season's finale. With crucial help from Randyll Tarly, a bannerman who chose fealty to the crown over Olenna, Jaime's army successfully took the castle.

Upon entering Highgarden, Jaime Lannister offered Olenna Tyrell a swift death by poisoned wine. This poignant moment served as a final, impactful scene for Diana Rigg before her departure from the series. In a shocking revelation, Olenna confessed to Jaime that she had poisoned King Joffrey in season four, a fact known to viewers but hidden from the Lannisters. "Tell Cersei. I want her to know it was me," Olenna stated before Jaime left her to face her end.

Olenna's significant impact on the game of thrones was evident from her season three introduction. Her sharp wit and biting remarks immediately set her apart. Unlike many self-serving characters, Olenna showed little interest in power or the "ugly iron chair." She had initially advised her family against involvement in the war for the throne. However, her granddaughter, Margaery, desired to be queen. This led the Tyrells, first with Margaery and Loras aligning with Renly Baratheon, and later with the Lannisters and King Joffrey after Renly's death.

These events forced Olenna into the capital's complex politics, primarily to protect her family. She recognized Joffrey's cruel nature, deeming a marriage to Margaery unsuitable. Her focus then shifted to Tommen, Joffrey's younger brother, whose gentle nature offered a contrast. Olenna's solution was cunning: she conspired with Petyr "Littlefinger" Baelish to poison Joffrey at his wedding, intending to frame Tyrion Lannister and Sansa Stark. Her plan was for Margaery to then marry Tommen, thus securing their position.

While much of this plan succeeded, it ultimately led to the destruction of Olenna's family. Her decision to frame Tyrion for Joffrey's murder triggered a chain of events, including Tyrion killing his father, Tywin Lannister, during his escape. Tywin had ruled King's Landing with an iron will. Though ruthless, he was a figure Olenna could negotiate with, and with whom she seemed to enjoy sparring. However, Tywin's death created a power vacuum, allowing more volatile forces like Cersei Lannister and the Faith Militant to rise.

Without her father's influence, Cersei targeted the Tyrells by empowering a zealous religious movement. This group, led by the High Sparrow, seized power, arresting Margaery and Loras. The Faith Militant then turned on Cersei, forcing King Tommen and Queen Margaery to submit. Olenna could not counter the Faith Militant and left the capital after Margaery warned her of danger. Before departing, Olenna challenged Cersei's ability to handle her enemies. Cersei's response was to destroy the Sept of Baelor, eliminating the Faith Militant, Margaery, Loras, and Olenna's son, Mace.

With her descendants gone and House Tyrell seemingly extinguished, Olenna focused her remaining energy on revenge against Cersei, aligning with Daenerys Targaryen. Tragically, she would not see this revenge enacted, having died from the poisoned wine given by Jaime Lannister, mirroring the method she used against his son.

Showrunners David Benioff and D.B. Weiss have intentionally created setbacks for Daenerys to rebalance the narrative. At the end of the previous season and the start of the current one, Daenerys and her powerful forces—three dragons, the Unsullied, and the Dothraki—seemed overwhelmingly dominant. She had also secured alliances with three major Westerosi powers: Dorne, the Tyrells, and the Greyjoy faction led by Theon and Yara. However, the Lannisters have systematically dismantled these alliances. Euron Greyjoy destroyed Yara's fleet, captured her, and eliminated or imprisoned Dorne's leaders. Olenna's death and Randyll Tarly's betrayal effectively neutralized the Tyrells. While Grey Worm and the Unsullied achieved a tactical victory by capturing Casterly Rock, they are now strategically isolated. Euron Greyjoy's attack on their ships left them stranded on Westeros' west coast, forcing a difficult overland journey east.

These early, dragon-less conflicts, including the battle at Highgarden, serve to remove secondary characters and build anticipation for major confrontations between the main players. Daenerys and Tyrion have expressed hesitation about using the dragons or Dothraki, fearing excessive civilian casualties and alienating the Westerosi population. However, without a strong reason for Jon Snow to intervene in southern conflicts, Daenerys may be forced to use her full power. This action would align with the late Lady Olenna's memorable advice to Daenerys in a previous episode: "You're a dragon. Be a dragon."

A Game of Thrones, the first book in author George R. R. Martin's epic fantasy series A Song of Ice and Fire, was published on August 6, 1996. So if longtime fans' hopes that Daenerys Targaryen and Jon Snow would one day meet were a person, they'd be about a week away from the legal drinking age. "The Queen's Justice" – the Game of Thrones episode that served up this long-awaited meeting before Martin himself could get to it – displayed a surprising lack of romantic or interpersonal fireworks between the two monarchs. God knows it's pleasant to see Emilia Clarke and Kit Harington in the same shot, but their characters spent most of their time bickering over loyalties and priorities. Still, the point of this meeting of ice and fire made was more important, if less fan-servicey, than romance. As befits its title, this week's installment asks us to consider what it means to be a ruler ... and what it means to seek justice. Related 'Game of Thrones': Emilia Clarke, the Queen of Dragons, Tells All How HBO's insanely popular hit show turned a young British actress into a feminist, a fantasy icon and a royal fan favorite Both Snow and his hand, Davos Seaworth, make their argument to Dany on these matters in no uncertain terms. The Northern regent notes that he felt he could do business with her because instead of simply taking her dragons and sacking King's Landing, innocent lives be damned, she devised an elaborate plan to defeat the mad queen Cersei Lannister by less lethal means. While the Mother of Dragons may talk tough about her right to the realm, she's clearly out to do right by the realm as well. For his part, Davos sells the Targaryen heir to the throne on a possible alliance by talking up his leader's revolutionary bonafides. Snow formed an unprecedented alliance with the hated wildlings for their common good. He was voted both Lord Commander of the Night's Watch and the King in the North despite his bastard status, because his people believed in him. Change the proper nouns and you've got an echo of the Khaleesi's story, in which she recognized her moral obligation to help unite and liberate disparate groups who unite with her in turn, simply because they feel she's the best woman for the job. Even if, as she claims, her belief in herself alone was what kept her going through the seven hells she walked through to get here, she kept her eyes on the real prize – a better life for the many. "It doesn't matter whose skeleton sits on the Iron throne," Davos concludes, attempting to draw Daenerys away from conquest and toward their mutual existential threat. If anyone can see the wisdom in that, it's her. Now compare this idea of justice to the one espoused by Cersei, as the Lion Queen goes on what Kill Bill's Bride might refer to as a roaring rampage of revenge. First, her pirate-king suitor Euron Greyjoy brings her human trophies — not just his own rebel niece Yara, but also the Dornish ruler Ellaria Sand and her daughter Tyene — to a rapturous heroes' welcome. (The inversion of Cersei's own walk of shame is hard to miss, even before her brother/lover Jaime points it out.) After an excruciatingly long dungeon conversation in which Cersei teases a number of gruesome fates for her captives, she settles on the same poisonous kiss of death Ellaria bestowed upon Cersei's own daughter. The Queen's next target is another rival matriarch. As Grey Worm and his Unsullied supersoldiers seize the Lannister stronghold Casterly Rock, Jaime and his generals — our old pal Bronn, along with Samwell Tarly's father Randyll and brother Dickon — maneuver south to Highgarden, home of House Tyrell. There, they soundly defeat the forces of Lady Olenna, to whom the Kingslayer offers a gentle death instead of the gruesome methods his sister preferred. But the so-called Queen of Thorns can still draw emotional blood. After she swallows her painless poison, she tells him that she was responsible for the death of his son, King Joffrey. In the end, it's Jaime who winds up storming out of the room in horror, as his victim simply poker-stares at him until he departs. (P.S.: Anyone wanna bet that the servant who spots the post-coital Lannister siblings in that earlier scene is Arya in disguise?) But there's an even deeper contrast at work than the one between mercy and the cycle of violence. The parts of this episode that aren't concerned with the two queens' power plays are almost entirely devoted to matters of faith rather than flesh and blood. Jon tries desperately to convince Dany that the army of the dead is real. She, in turn, delights in showing off her dragons – animals believed extinct for centuries until she helped bring them back to life. (And by the way, when did these beasts first appear to the King in the North? When he said "I'm not a Stark," which is technically true given he's actual heritage, i.e. he's a secret Targaryen!) She also notes that before he stopped himself, Davos started to mention something about Jon once coming back to life. Related 25 Best 'Game of Thrones' Episodes – Updated From Blackwater to the Battle of the Bastards, we count down the HBO fantasy epic's most memorable hours Up north, Bran Stark returns unexpectedly to Winterfell, bringing his sister Sansa to tears of joy — then to shudders of fear as he reveals his creepy psychic powers as the new Three-Eyed Raven. ("It's difficult to explain," he deadpans, in one of actor Isaac Hempstead Wright's finest scenes in years.) In the Citadel, Sam Tarly cures Jorah Mormont's greyscale out of sheer determination, despite the lengthy track record of failures in this area that his boss Archmaester Ebrose rattles off. Even disgraced mystic Melisandre seems to insist she has a role to play, psychically predicting both she and Lord Varys will ultimately die in Westeros' defense. In each case, Game of Thrones sends a message. You can focus on worldly, bloody matters like revenge. Or you can make the leap of faith and focus on the lives of your fellow human beings. "People's minds aren't made for problems that large," Tyrion frets. Almost in response, Bran Stark tells his sister "I can see everything that's ever happened to everyone" — a mystical callback to the far more self-interested seven-dimensional-chess advice Sansa's advisor Littlefinger gave her. Seek triumph, and you're merely a killer. Seek solidarity, and … well, that's not quite clear yet. But if winter is here, which of the two would you count on to turn back the cold?
