**About the National Science Foundation (NSF):**

The NSF is an independent federal agency dedicated to advancing fundamental research and education across all scientific and engineering disciplines. In FY 2017, its budget is $7.5 billion, supporting nearly 2,000 institutions nationwide. The NSF receives over 48,000 proposals annually, making approximately 12,000 new funding awards.

The **direct detection of gravitational waves** is a **monumental achievement in physics**. These **elusive ripples in spacetime**, first theorized by Albert Einstein over a century ago, have now been **observed for the first time** by the LIGO collaboration. This groundbreaking discovery **confirms a key prediction of Einstein's general theory of relativity**.

On Thursday, February 11th, at 10:30 a.m. ET, the National Science Foundation held a press conference in Washington D.C. Scientists from Caltech, MIT, and the LIGO Scientific Collaboration shared this **historic news**. The Laser Interferometer Gravitational-wave Observatory (LIGO) has been **instrumental in this endeavor**.

For years, rumors had circulated within the scientific community about the potential discovery of these spacetime distortions. The international LIGO collaboration's announcement finally **put an end to the speculation**, confirming the existence of gravitational waves.

But what exactly are gravitational waves, and why is their detection so significant? At their core, gravitational waves are **disturbances or ripples in the fabric of spacetime**. Imagine dropping a pebble into a still pond; the resulting ripples spread outward across the water's surface. Gravitational waves function similarly, but instead of water, they are **ripples in spacetime itself**.

These waves are generated by **accelerating massive objects in the universe**. When such objects move, they disturb the surrounding spacetime, creating these waves. These ripples then **carry gravitational energy away from their source**.

A prime example of a phenomenon that could produce gravitational waves is the **collision of two black holes**. Such a cataclysmic event would send powerful "ripples" through spacetime, propagating outward at the speed of light. While indirect evidence for gravitational waves has existed for some time, their **direct detection has remained an incredibly challenging feat**. Until now, this has been an almost impossible task.

The artistic rendering accompanying this text depicts spacetime being warped by the immense mass of a galaxy. Another artistic visualization illustrates the potential generation of gravitational waves during a galactic merger.

To understand which astrophysical phenomena are capable of producing these powerful gravitational waves, further exploration is recommended. This discovery opens a **new window into observing the universe**, allowing scientists to study cosmic events that were previously invisible. The direct detection of gravitational waves promises to **revolutionize our understanding of the cosmos** and the fundamental forces that govern it.

Rumors are swirling that the **Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory (LIGO)** may have detected **gravitational waves**, the elusive ripples in spacetime predicted by Einstein. While initially just whispers on the internet, these rumors have gained traction due to an email that surfaced on Twitter, offering **specific details** about a potential discovery.

This email, reportedly from someone who has seen the LIGO paper detailing the findings, suggests that researchers have observed **two black holes merging**. Clifford Burgess, a theoretical physicist at McMaster University and the Perimeter Institute, expressed cautious optimism. "I've been around a long time, so I've seen rumors come and go. This one seems more credible," he stated, emphasizing that he had not seen the paper himself but heard from those who had.

The leaked information indicates that the detected signal originates from the merger of two black holes, one **29 solar masses** and the other **36 solar masses**. Crucially, the signal's **statistical significance reportedly exceeds the "five-sigma" standard**, the gold bar for scientific discovery. LIGO, which uses two massive interferometers to detect the minuscule stretching of space caused by gravitational waves, is said to have registered this event with the appropriate time delay between its detectors.

While LIGO's primary focus has been on the merger of neutron stars, the email suggests that the signal from merging black holes of this magnitude would be **stronger and detectable from further away**, as noted by Marc Kamionkowski, a theoretical physicist at Johns Hopkins University. Other unconfirmed reports hint at LIGO having observed **multiple gravitational wave sources**.

The scientific community awaits an official announcement from the LIGO team, which Burgess speculates could happen as early as next week. He believes that **if this discovery is confirmed, it has a high probability of earning a Nobel Prize in Physics**. Burgess originally sent the email to his department to inform his colleagues and students about a potentially groundbreaking event that could ignite their scientific curiosity.

Gravitational waves are 'ripples' in the fabric of space-time caused by some of the most violent and energetic processes in the Universe. Albert Einstein predicted the existence of gravitational waves in 1916 in his general theory of relativity. Einstein's mathematics showed that massive accelerating objects (such as neutron stars or black holes orbiting each other) would disrupt space-time in such a way that 'waves' of distorted space would radiate from the source (like the movement of waves away from a stone thrown into a pond). Furthermore, these ripples would travel at the speed of light through the Universe, carrying with them information about their cataclysmic origins, as well as invaluable clues to the nature of gravity itself. The strongest gravitational waves are produced by catastrophic events such as colliding black holes, the collapse of stellar cores (supernovae), coalescing neutron stars or white dwarf stars, the slightly wobbly rotation of neutron stars that are not perfect spheres, and possibly even the remnants of gravitational radiation created by the birth of the Universe. The animation below illustrates how gravitational waves are emitted by two neutron stars as they orbit each other and then coalesce. (Credit: NASA/Goddard Space Flight Center) Your browser does not support this video tag. Though gravitational waves were predicted to exist in 1916, actual proof of their existence wouldn't arrive until 1974, 20 years after Einstein's death. In that year, two astronomers working at the Arecibo Radio Observatory in Puerto Rico discovered a binary pulsar--two extremely dense and heavy stars in orbit around each other. This was exactly the type of system that, according to general relativity, should radiate gravitational waves. Knowing that this discovery could be used to test Einstein's audacious prediction, astronomers began measuring how the period of the stars' orbit changed over time. After eight years of observations, they determined that the stars were getting closer to each other at precisely the rate predicted by general relativity if they were emitting gravitational waves (which would remove energy from the system and cause the stars to get closer and closer together). This system has been monitored for over 40 years and the observed changes in the orbit agree so well with general relativity there is no doubt that it is emitting gravitational waves. For a more detailed discussion of this discovery and work, see Look Deeper. Since then, many astronomers have studied the timing of pulsar radio-emissions and found similar effects, further confirming the existence of gravitational waves. But these confirmations had always come indirectly or mathematically and not through actual 'physical' contact. All of this changed on September 14, 2015, when LIGO physically sensed the distortions in spacetime caused by passing gravitational waves generated by two colliding black holes nearly 1.3 billion light years away! LIGO's discovery will go down in history as one of humanity's greatest scientific achievements. Lucky for us here on Earth, while the processes that generate gravitational waves can be extremely violent and destructive, by the time the waves reach Earth they are billions of times smaller. In fact, by the time gravitational waves from LIGO's first detection reached us, the amount of space-time wobbling they generated was thousands of times smaller than the nucleus of an atom! Such inconceivably small measurements are what LIGO was designed to make. To find out how LIGO can achieve this task, visit LIGO's Interferometer.

Growing Old With Einstein: The Long Wait For Detection Of Gravitational Waves Enlarge this image toggle caption Matt Heintze/Caltech/MIT/LIGO Lab Matt Heintze/Caltech/MIT/LIGO Lab I wrote this with the expectation that today, Thursday, Feb. 11, 2016, the biggest science story since the discovery of the Higgs particle would be all over the news. With that in mind, please allow me to recount my own personal history that led to this moment: 1988 : I'm a young physics graduate student at the University of Washington. Some guy comes and gives a talk about Einstein's Theory of Relativity and its prediction of gravitational waves: traveling ripples in the fabric of space and time. Sounds cool to me. Someone asks when we'll be able to see these waves. The guy says soon, if they can get the money to build a big detector. : I'm a young physics graduate student at the University of Washington. Some guy comes and gives a talk about Einstein's Theory of Relativity and its prediction of gravitational waves: traveling ripples in the fabric of space and time. Sounds cool to me. Someone asks when we'll be able to see these waves. The guy says soon, if they can get the money to build a big detector. 1994: I'm a post-doctoral astrophysics researcher at the University of Minnesota with two small kids. Some guy comes and gives a talk about the two big gravitational wave detectors they got money to build. The project is called LIGO (Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory). Sounds cool to me. Someone asks when we'll be able to see these waves. The guy says soon, but first they have to construct the miles-long laser "telescopes." The project is called LIGO (Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory). Sounds cool to me. Someone asks when we'll be able to see these waves. The guy says soon, but first they have to construct the miles-long laser "telescopes." 2002: I'm an assistant professor of astrophysics at the University of Rochester. Some guy comes and gives a talk about the two big gravitational wave telescopes now up-and-running in Washington and Louisiana. Sounds cool to me. Someone asks when we'll be able to see these waves. The guy says soon, if they're lucky. But there is a lot work that needs to be done calibrating the systems. I'm an assistant professor of astrophysics at the University of Rochester. Some guy comes and gives a talk about the two big gravitational wave telescopes now up-and-running in Washington and Louisiana. Sounds cool to me. Someone asks when we'll be able to see these waves. The guy says soon, if they're lucky. But there is a lot work that needs to be done calibrating the systems. 2007 : I'm an associate professor of astrophysics at the University of Rochester. Some guy comes and gives a talk about the status of LIGO. They are starting to upgrade the systems to become more sensitive to gravitational waves. Sounds cool to me. Someone asks when we'll be able to see the waves. The guy says soon, once the upgrade is done. : I'm an associate professor of astrophysics at the University of Rochester. Some guy comes and gives a talk about the status of LIGO. They are starting to upgrade the systems to become more sensitive to gravitational waves. Sounds cool to me. Someone asks when we'll be able to see the waves. The guy says soon, once the upgrade is done. 2012: I'm a full professor of astrophysics at the University of Rochester. My kids are in college, my hair is showing a lot of grey and I need to back way off on the big bump runs when I ski 'cause my knees hurt. Some guy comes and gives a talk about the status of LIGO... I think you get the picture. All my professional life I have been sitting in scientific talks about detecting — directly "seeing" — the vibrations of space and time called gravitational waves. At some point, after all those years, I began to think: "Hey, maybe this isn't going to work. Maybe it's just too hard or too subtle." It is a beautiful thing to find out I was wrong. The long, long wait to get to a direct detection tells us a lot about why gravitational waves are so important and why the announcement will be such a triumph. Einstein's vision that space and time are really a single flexible, stretchable fabric is just as stunning an idea today as it was when he first proposed it 100 years ago. Soon after he first announced his theory, he published another paper showing that any movement of matter — even wiggling your hands back and forth — would produce traveling ripples in this space-time fabric. Just like water waves disturb the surface of a pond after a rock is dropped into it, these gravitational waves distort space-time, causing distances to shorten and lengthen as they pass. But these changes in space and time are so utterly tiny that they're extremely hard to detect. Only the most violent and cataclysmic events, like two black holes eating other, will produce gravitational waves strong enough that we could even hope to see them. The reason for this is that space-time may be a fabric — but it's a pretty stiff one. That is why it took so long to make a working gravitational wave detector. Only by using crossed lasers running through miles-long tubes could we get the ultra-high sensitivity yardsticks needed to see waves squeezing and stretching space. The effort required an army of smart, dedicated women and men working tirelessly for decades to refine LIGO and develop the understanding of Einstein's grand theory required to make it usable. It took that long and required that much effort to show us something remarkable — reality itself is ringing like a bell. But now, we are told, we can finally see gravitational waves directly. This is like someone who is blind to the color red — and all things red — suddenly waking up to a world with apples and roses and Valentine's Day cards. This would mean that, using LIGO as a gravitational wave telescope, we've just opened up an entirely new window on a universe of black holes, neutron stars and, perhaps, even the Big Bang itself. That is why today's announcement matters. It's about patience and effort in the service of that most precious of human experiences: wonder. Today we can be proud to be part of a species that has gained such an understanding of the world. We can also be proud to be part of a nation that is willing to spend some of its hard earned treasure just to gain that understanding. But, most of all, we can look out at the night sky and just be filled with wonder. Adam Frank is a co-founder of the 13.7 blog, an astrophysics professor at the University of Rochester, a book author and a self-described "evangelist of science." You can keep up with more of what Adam is thinking on Facebook and Twitter: @adamfrank4

**Listening to the Cosmos: The Hunt for Gravitational Waves**

The scientific world is buzzing with anticipation, driven by the prospect of a monumental discovery: the potential detection of gravitational waves. For over a decade, the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory (LIGO) has been diligently listening for these elusive cosmic ripples. Its recent upgrade to Advanced-LIGO, a more sensitive system with enhanced noise filtering, has significantly boosted its chances of capturing concrete evidence of these phenomena – if it hasn't already. While physicists are understandably excited, the concept of gravitational waves often leaves the general public pondering: what exactly are these cosmic vibrations, and why do they command such attention in the scientific world?

**What are Gravitational Waves?**

Gravitational waves are minute distortions that propagate through the very fabric of spacetime. Imagine dropping a pebble into a perfectly still pond; the resulting ripples spread outwards, distorting the water's surface. Albert Einstein's groundbreaking general theory of relativity posits that a similar phenomenon occurs when massive objects move through spacetime. Far from being an empty void, spacetime, according to Einstein, is a dynamic, four-dimensional "fabric" that can be warped and distorted by the presence and motion of mass. These distortions are, in fact, the fundamental cause of gravitational attraction.

A common and helpful analogy to visualize this is a taut rubber sheet. When a heavy object, like a bowling ball, is placed on the sheet, it creates a noticeable sag. If a smaller marble is then rolled nearby, it will naturally curve towards the heavier object, mimicking the gravitational pull between celestial bodies. While this analogy isn't a perfect representation of the complex, four-dimensional nature of spacetime, it effectively illustrates how what we perceive as empty space can behave like a tangible, dynamic medium. Any object undergoing acceleration, especially massive ones, should theoretically generate ripples in this cosmic fabric. However, the ripples produced by smaller, less massive objects tend to dissipate quickly. It is only the cataclysmic events involving incredibly massive celestial bodies, such as the collision of neutron stars or the merger of black holes, that generate gravitational waves powerful enough to travel across vast cosmic distances and reach Earth.

**The Ingenious Methods of Detection**

The quest to detect these faint cosmic whispers involves several sophisticated experiments, with LIGO currently at the forefront of the search. LIGO operates by meticulously tracking how gravitational waves subtly alter spacetime itself. As a gravitational wave passes through, it causes a minute stretching of space in one direction and a corresponding compression in a perpendicular direction. LIGO's primary instrument, an interferometer, is designed to detect these minuscule changes.

This remarkable device works by splitting a single laser beam into two. These beams are then directed along two perpendicular arms of equal length. The beams travel to mirrors at the ends of these arms, reflect back, and recombine at their source. In the absence of a gravitational wave, the beams travel identical distances, and their waves remain perfectly in sync upon their return. However, the passage of a gravitational wave can minutely alter the length of one or both of the interferometer's arms. This change in arm length means the laser beams will travel slightly different distances. When the beams recombine, scientists can detect this discrepancy, indicating the presence of a gravitational wave.

The challenge lies in the incredibly small scale of these distortions. Gravitational waves alter the length of LIGO's arms by an astonishingly tiny amount – approximately one ten-thousandth the width of an atom's nucleus. To isolate such a minuscule signal, LIGO must employ rigorous methods to filter out all other sources of interference, including seismic activity from earthquakes and even the vibrations caused by nearby traffic. Despite nearly a decade of operation without a confirmed detection, the recent upgrade to Advanced-LIGO has significantly enhanced its sensitivity and its potential to finally capture these elusive signals.

LIGO is not alone in this endeavor. It faces competition from the European Space Agency's (ESA) Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (LISA). LISA is envisioned as a colossal, space-based version of LIGO, designed to detect even lower-frequency gravitational waves. The ESA has already launched LISA Pathfinder, a precursor mission that spent several months in space testing the crucial technologies that will be deployed in future LISA missions.

Beyond laser interferometry, other innovative approaches are being employed. The North American Nanohertz Observatory for Gravitational Waves (NANOGrav) utilizes a different strategy. NANOGrav monitors the precisely timed radio wave pulses emitted by rapidly rotating neutron stars known as pulsars. These pulses are incredibly regular, acting like cosmic clocks. If a gravitational wave were to pass between Earth and a pulsar, it could subtly alter the travel time of these radio pulses, causing them to arrive slightly earlier or later than expected. By observing these timing deviations across an array of pulsars, NANOGrav aims to infer the presence of gravitational waves.

Furthermore, scientists are also searching for a specific type of gravitational wave believed to have been generated during the immediate aftermath of the Big Bang. Experiments like the Background Imaging of Cosmic Extragalactic Polarization (BICEP) program, conducted at the South Pole, analyze the cosmic microwave background radiation – the faint afterglow of the Big Bang. The theory suggests that if gravitational waves were produced during this primordial epoch, they would have left a distinctive imprint on the polarization patterns of this ancient radiation, appearing as subtle swirls. By meticulously observing these patterns, researchers hope to find evidence of these earliest gravitational waves.

**The Profound Significance of Detecting Gravitational Waves**

The successful detection of gravitational waves would represent a paradigm shift in our understanding of the universe. It would not only confirm a key prediction of Einstein's theory of general relativity but also open an entirely new window onto the cosmos. Unlike electromagnetic radiation (like light), which can be blocked or scattered by intervening matter, gravitational waves travel unimpeded through the universe. This means they can carry information from the most extreme and enigmatic events, such as the mergers of black holes and neutron stars, events that are otherwise invisible to conventional telescopes.

By studying the characteristics of these waves – their frequency, amplitude, and polarization – scientists can glean unprecedented insights into the nature of gravity, the properties of black holes, and the dynamics of the early universe. It would allow us to "hear" the universe's most violent events, providing a complementary perspective to the visual information we currently gather. This new form of cosmic messenger promises to revolutionize astrophysics and cosmology, enabling us to probe the universe in ways we could only dream of before. The ongoing quest to detect gravitational waves is not just a scientific endeavor; it is a journey to unlock the deepest secrets of the cosmos and to truly understand the fundamental forces that shape our reality.

query: What are the key elements featured in Newsweek's cover story imagining Princess Diana at 50?

documents.text: The image of Princess Diana walking alongside Catherine, Duchess of Cambridge, in the shadow of the Newsweek logo is an attention-getter. Surely, that was the intention of Newsweek's editor Tina Brown, who's penned the latest issue's cover story on what Diana would be like if she'd lived to see her 50th birthday on Friday. But the Photoshopped, looking-you-straight-in-the-eye picture of Diana at 50 seems about half as controversial as the entire cover package which includes not only Tina's brief article, the obvious side-by-side slideshow of Diana and Kate's fashion sense, an update on Diana's causes, a fake Facebook page for Diana, and one doctored photograph of Diana clutching a white iPhone. It's all alarmingly difficult to look away from, bloggers agree. Tina Brown sure loves princesses, says Daniel D'Addario at the New York Observer. Glazing over the cover image, D'Addario quantifies Tina's love for Kate Middleton--she's been on four out of the 15 covers since Brown took over as editor--and calls Newsweek's latest indulgent round-up of princess news "this week's installment of Ms. Brown's beautiful, dark, regal fantasy." About that fantasy, though, D'Addario noticed something funny: A follow-up to our item on Newsweek's creepy alive-Diana cover: the “plot” of Ms. Brown’s article, in which an aging Diana gets Botox and moves to New York, is like-but-unlike the plot of Monica Ali’s new novel, Untold Story, in which an aging Diana dyes her hair and moves to the Midwest. It’s been getting a lot of press, including the cover of this week’sNew York Times Book Review–perhaps Ms. Brown had heard of it before the writing process began. Tina Brown's version of Princess Diana at 50 sounds kind of shallow, says Joe Coscarelli at The Village Voice. Furthermore, isn't it a little indulgent of Brown to frame the whole thing in the context of a socialite magazine editor's glamorous life hanging out in "the London über-swirl of fashion and society and media?" First of all, if she were turning 50 this month, Diana would still be "great-looking." That is literally the first piece of information in the cover story of this weekly news magazine, after a description of a party that the editor-in-chief of said magazine attended. Diana might've worn J.Crew and Galliano, Brown imagines, like Michelle Obama, and might've moved to New York, married a "super-rich hedge-fund" guy who would buy her "toys" and blah blah, France, charity, blah, Kate Middleton. Did you know Tina Brown wrote a book about Diana? We'll be watching for a sales spike. Is the cover image creepy? Let the readers decide! The Los Angeles Times and The Huffington Post ran brief posts and corresponding reader polls on the "jarring" cover image. Nearly half of the L.A. Times readers polled find the image "horribly offensive," and 60 percent of HuffPost readers think it's "a bit too much!" Twitter, the 21st-century polling device seems to agree. The top trending tweet comes from Janice Turner, a columnist for The Times of London. She tweeted, "Astonishing. Why didn't Newsweek just row to Althrop island, exhume & snap Diana's rotted corpse?" What do you think? Let us know in the comments! Want to add to this story? Let us know in comments. You can share ideas for stories on the Open Wire. Adam Clark Estes

Category: Newsweek has never been in the same league as the celeb weeklies US Weekly, OK!, or even People magazine. It's a news magazine, and you'd think they'd want to be taken seriously. But I guess not, given that the cover of the publication this week features a PhotoShop-ped image that's not only "imagined" (aka false), but also cringe-worthy and inappropriate. To promote editor-in-chief Tina Brown's story, "Diana at 50: If She Were Here Now," the cover features Princess Diana "walking beside" her new daughter-in-law Kate Middleton, aka the Duchess of Cambridge. Inside, the story speculates about Diana's would-be life in 2011. Brown muses that the Princess would be hangin' with the Middletons, tweeting on her iPhone from Davos (there's even a creepy, unnerving photo of Diana with a superimposed white iPhone 4), and using Botox "strategically." She would have also been jealous of Kate (because I guess Tina figured throwing something in that was a wee bit Oedipal would get tongues wagging). How on Earth is this dream of a maniac in any way worthy of being in a publication that aims to print serious journalism? Yes, during the royal wedding, we all thought, either fleetingly or at length, about how it was sad that Diana wasn't there to witness Will's and Kate's "I do"s. Sure, when the anniversary of her death passes every August, we wonder what it would have been like if she hadn't been killed. There's even a controversial documentary about her death called Unlawful Killing, in which the filmmaker asserts there was a conspiracy to hide important facts about Diana and boyfriend Dodi Al-Fayed's deaths in 1997. But to take Diana's memory and dumb it down to a fictionalized glossy, celeb tabloid-esque cover and story -- befitting of the types of tabloids that hunted Diana throughout her entire public life -- is nothing short of cheap and tacky. Brown may summarize the article by writing, "Diana still lives and is vibrant in his memory. And in ours." But the "sentimental" article accomplishes exactly the opposite of what it intends to -- it's a dishonor and a disservice to Diana, her sons, and anyone who cherished "The People's Princess." If Brown and Newsweek actually respected Diana, they simply wouldn't have "gone there" with this exploitative cover, photos, and story. Do you think the cover and story are tacky and exploitative? The initial seed list for this extensive data acquisition was derived from Alexa's top 1 million websites, obtained shortly before the crawl's commencement on March 9, 2011.

The crawling infrastructure employed Heritrix version 3.1.1-SNAPSHOT, with strict adherence to robots.txt protocols. The crawl's scope was broadly defined, with minimal manual exclusions. Notably, the rapid expansion of URL queues beyond their intended capacity may have resulted in incomplete crawling of embedded and linked objects within pages, as these resources were not always processed. Furthermore, the inclusion of repeated crawls of specific Argentinian government websites introduces a potential skew in country-specific data analysis.

While significant advancements have been implemented in subsequent wide-crawl methodologies, this dataset is being made available for research and experimentation in its entirety. In an era of disaster fatigue, her generosity of spirit and the potent impact of her star power are profoundly missed. "I will give it back to you one day when I am king." In many respects, he has already done so. His deliberate efforts to incorporate his mother’s memory into the most significant day of his life are evident. The engagement ring he presented to Kate belonged to Diana. The opening hymn at the wedding, "Guide Me, O Thou Redeemer," a favorite of Diana’s, was chosen by William and Harry to conclude both her funeral service and the memorial service marking the tenth anniversary of her passing. Perhaps most significantly, in the days preceding the wedding ceremony, William took Kate on a deeply personal pilgrimage to Althorp to visit Diana’s grave on the island in the lake. He had waited years for this moment, signifying to his future wife that Diana’s presence remains vibrant in his memory, and in ours.

Newsweek's recent cover story, "Diana at 50: If She Were Here Now," by Tina Brown, offers a hypothetical look at the late Princess of Wales. The piece speculates on what Diana's life might be like today, suggesting she could favor J. Crew clothing, similar to Michelle Obama, undergo "strategic Botox treatments," and maintain a friendly relationship with her ex-husband, Prince Charles.

Beyond this imagined scenario, the magazine's feature includes a fashion comparison between Diana and the current Princess Catherine, a mock Facebook profile, an update on Diana's charitable work, and a digitally altered image of her with an iPhone. The cover itself displays digital art showing an older Diana walking with Middleton. Adam Clark Estes of The Atlantic Wire described this ghostly portrayal as "creepy."

Responses to the cover and article have been mixed. Dodai Stewart of Jezebel called the cover "ridiculous" and Brown's story "shudder-inducing fanfic." In contrast, Matt Donnelly of The Los Angeles Times advised a calmer reaction, admitting the cover was "jarring" but finding Brown's speculative idea "possible, if not entirely believable." Lizzie Manning of Mediaite proposed that Brown chose Diana because she was a "beloved public figure" who was "idolized and worshipped," and whose "sudden death" profoundly impacted many. Manning warned against a story that "acts as if it didn't happen," finding it "disrespectful in many ways."

For transparency, Sir Harold Evans, editor-at-large of The Week, is married to Tina Brown, who is the editor-in-chief of Newsweek and The Daily Beast.

## Reimagining Diana: Ethical Questions in Posthumous Portrayals

Tina Brown's recent Newsweek feature, "Diana at 50: Chilling with the Middletons. Tweeting from Davos. And still the people’s princess," prompts a **critical look at how Princess Diana's legacy is being reshaped after her death**. It's natural to wonder about her life had she not died so tragically, given her immense popularity and the profound grief her passing caused. However, this article raises **significant ethical questions about posthumous portrayals**.

### The Unsettling Nature of Speculative Imagery

The article's **speculative narrative**, which imagines Diana engaging in modern activities like attending Davos or interacting with the Middleton family, is amplified by its visual elements. The **digitally manipulated images**, particularly the cover photo placing Diana alongside Kate Middleton and depictions of Diana with a smartphone, are **unsettling**. While technically impressive, this visual strategy creates a disquieting sense of **artificial resurrection**, bordering on the macabre. Presenting such simulations, rather than simply acknowledging her lasting impact on our collective memory, feels particularly problematic.

### Projections of a Hypothetical Future

Brown's projections for Diana's imagined future include a continued focus on fashion, blending casual and glamorous styles reminiscent of Michelle Obama, alongside cosmetic enhancements and a commitment to physical fitness. More significantly, the article speculates that Diana would have remarried twice, actively pursued her philanthropic work, and developed a complex relationship with the Middleton family, characterized by both acceptance and a degree of envy towards Kate.

### A Dissonant Conclusion

The piece concludes by discussing Prince William's pre-wedding visit to Diana's grave at Althorp, framing it as a gesture to his fiancée that symbolizes Diana's enduring presence in his memory. However, this poignant reflection is starkly contrasted by the article's earlier visual and textual assertions of Diana's continued, active participation in contemporary life. This internal dissonance suggests a fundamental misjudgment in the article's overall approach.

### The Disrespectful Creation of an Alternate Reality

Ultimately, while Princess Diana undeniably remains a vibrant presence in our collective consciousness, the attempt to materialize her in a simulated, contemporary existence through speculative narratives and digitally fabricated imagery is not only disingenuous but also potentially hurtful, especially to her surviving family. The article's premise, which seems to grapple with the paradox of Diana's living memory versus her actual absence, falters in its execution by creating a disorienting and ultimately disrespectful alternate reality.

documents.text: Warning: This post is dark and full of spoilers for all of "Game of Thrones" Season 6! Arya serious, HBO? After a week of crazy theories and speculation, "Game of Thrones" decided to give us a reality check in its latest episode, "No One." There may have never been an episode that killed so much hype, but the Many-Faced God's demands must be met. Here are the theories that were added to the wall: R.I.P. "It Wasn't Arya." Perhaps the most popular theory floating around this week was that the girl who got stabbed in the previous Sunday's episode, "The Broken Man," wasn't Arya. It made a lot of sense, too. Arya was basically broadcasting her plans to sail to Westeros to the world. She strolled about the city without her sword, Needle, she made a show of booking passage to Westeros, and she didn't seem to care much that a guild of face-changing assassins was out to get her. (New face. Who dis?) Well, it turns out that that was Arya after all. A girl was acting super dumb, and Lady Crane ended up dying after helping her with her tummy boo-boo, but in the end she killed the Waif and will soon be on her way back to Westeros. R.I.P. Cleganebowl. After we were informed of the Hound's miraculous recovery and glorious comeback to the show, the Septon that saved him said he had survived for a reason. The hype said "Cleganebowl" -- fans thought perhaps the Hound would fight against his brother, the Mountain, in Cersei's trial-by-combat. Tommen thought differently. The king declared there would be no more Cleganebowl trial-by-combats, and, just in case you still hoped for one, the Hound is now apparently going to head north to fight White Walkers, so, yeah, that's basically done, too. R.I.P. Lady Stoneheart. We've been holding out hope that Lady Stoneheart might make an appearance in Season 6, but the return of Beric Dondarrion this week seems to have finally ended that. In the books, Dondarrion gives his life to bring back Catelyn Stark, who basically becomes a zombie out for revenge for the Red Wedding. Meh. That doesn't look like it's going to happen. But don't get too down, people. Some theories did survive the purge. As for our favorite ... Still holding out hope: Arya and the Waif were the same person. This theory states Arya and the Waif had some sort of "Fight Club" connection going on, and, technically, it hasn't been disproven. At the end of "Fight Club," Edward Norton's character kills Brad Pitt's, thereby ending his split personality problems. Arya may have done the same exact thing. Yeah, it seems a little far-fetched, but how else would you explain a girl's extremely bizarre behavior? There is only one thing we say to the death of this theory ...

Game of Thrones' sixth season has navigated a narrative that has frequently oscillated between glacial pacing and precipitous plot acceleration. However, the eighth episode of the season achieved a commendable equilibrium. Significant events transpired: a fortress capitulated, a naval armada materialized, and a monarch traversed vast distances with improbable celerity. Crucially, pivotal legislation was enacted, a long-standing narrative impasse was resolved, and a cherished protagonist may be poised to re-engage in the overarching conflict. Despite these developments, the episode maintained a remarkably measured tempo. There was ample opportunity for character interactions, such as Bronn's jests with Podrick Payne, and for Ser Jaime Lannister and Lord Edmure Tully to articulate their divergent worldviews. Tyrion Lannister even attempted to persuade Missandei and Grey Worm that liberation encompasses the indulgence of libations. The successful execution of this compelling hour of television was facilitated by the strategic marginalization of the Jon/Sansa/Davos narrative in the North, which had become the show's emotional fulcrum, and a near-complete sidelining of Daenerys Targaryen.

**Winner 1: Arya Stark**
While few genuinely believed Arya was deceased following her grievous stabbing in the previous episode, her survival defied the narrative logic that would have rendered her extensive training at the House of Black and White a futile endeavor, culminating in an ignominious demise. The series has historically employed the unexpected elimination of central figures—Ned Stark, Robb Stark, Stannis Baratheon—a hallmark of George R.R. Martin's "A Song of Ice and Fire." Arya's act of compassion towards Lady Crane was reciprocated with medical attention, enabling her to gain the upper hand against the Waif. Subsequently, through a somewhat implausible and unsatisfying resolution, she received absolution from Jaqen H'ghar and was granted permission to return to Westeros. The entire subplot involving the acting troupe and Arya's subsequent defection strikes this reviewer as a narrative misstep. Nevertheless, Arya emerges as an unequivocal victor: she is alive, possesses formidable assassin skills, and has resisted assimilation into a peculiar death cult. This represents a remarkably positive outcome for a character who has endured considerable hardship.

**Winner 2: Jaime Lannister**
Jaime Lannister successfully reclaimed Riverrun, achieving this objective with minimal bloodshed and in a manner entirely consistent with his evolution into the series' most compelling character. While audiences may have harbored a desire for Lord Edmure Tully to steadfastly uphold his principles against the Lannisters and Freys, and for the Blackfish to lead the Tully forces northward in a defiant stand, the reality of surrender, as orchestrated by Jaime, presents a more pragmatic and humane resolution. This outcome not only preserved the lives of numerous combatants on both sides but also mitigated the potential for extensive pillaging of the local populace that a protracted siege would have entailed. In Meereen, Tyrion's jest about the Starks' excessive adherence to honor, to the point of admonishing a fly for pilfering ale, contextualizes Stark honor as a source of absurdity. However, within the broader tapestry of the Game of Thrones, this same honor has demonstrably precipitated immense bloodshed and devastation. Conversely, Jaime's decision to betray King Aerys II, thereby forfeiting his honor, likely averted countless casualties. This underscores the complex moral lessons embedded within the narrative.

**Winner 3: The Rule of Law in Westeros**
Despite any reservations regarding King Tommen Baratheon's reign, his pronouncement against trial by combat is demonstrably sound. This archaic ritual, while occasionally serving as an engaging narrative device, fundamentally undermines the principles of governance by permitting affluent and influential individuals to circumvent legal processes through the employment of mercenaries. While this edict directly curtails Queen Cersei's reliance on "Ser Robert Strong" as her enforcer, its broader implication lies in Tommen's potential success in reforming the Westerosi judicial system to preclude such arbitrary, might-makes-right adjudications. This would represent a significant service to the realm. Francis Fukuyama, in his seminal 2011 work, "The Origins of Political Order," compellingly argues that the establishment of the rule of law is intrinsically linked to the emergence of robust religious movements that exert authority beyond familial structures. In this context, the High Sparrow fulfills a crucial macrohistorical role analogous to organized religion. With the cessation of hostilities, the territories under effective Lannister dominion—the Westerlands, Crownlands, Stormlands, Riverlands, and the Reach—appear poised for a period of constructive political development. This optimistic outlook is, however, juxtaposed against the looming existential threats of encroaching ice zombies and/or dragons.

**Winner 4: The Hound**
Sandor Clegane's pursuit of tranquility and a new existence was demonstrably unattainable, as evidenced by his inability to passively accept adversity. His temperament is fundamentally incompatible with turning the other cheek. Furthermore, the Faith of the Seven lacks the appeal of the Brotherhood Without Banners, an organization with which Clegane appears destined to align. This group embraces combat and violence—Clegane's principal proficiencies—while also fostering a sense of camaraderie and the moral satisfaction derived from engaging in conflict for a righteous cause. While the prospect of a felicitous conclusion for the Hound remains uncertain, he is likely to experience a period of relative contentment.

**Loser 1: The Blackfish**
The reintroduction of a formidable character who had previously received limited narrative focus presented an opportunity for a compelling character arc for one of the last surviving commanders of the Stark/Tully forces from the War of the Five Kings. Instead, he was abandoned by his men and compelled to surrender Riverrun. His departure from the series was as abrupt and arbitrary as his reappearance. His refusal to join forces with Brienne and Podrick, followed by his offscreen demise, relegated him to the status of a minor player.

**Loser 2: Fans of Lady Stoneheart**
Numerous book readers had ardently anticipated the televised depiction of this particularly macabre character, and episode seven seemed to foreshadow her imminent arrival. However, this did not materialize. In HBO's adaptation, Beric Dondarrion remains extant, and the Brotherhood Without Banners continues to uphold its honorable tenets. The marauders encountered in the preceding episode were identified as rogue elements. The potential for Lady Stoneheart's eventual appearance persists, as the series has demonstrated a willingness to adapt book elements out of chronological order, as exemplified by the Siege of Riverrun. Nevertheless, a strong premonition suggests this particular narrative thread will remain unfulfilled.

**Loser 3: Fans of the Dorne Plot**
It is a widely held sentiment that the Dorne plotline has garnered minimal audience appreciation. However, any hypothetical adherents to this narrative would undoubtedly be perplexed by its complete omission from the current season's discourse. The last known developments in Dorne involved Ellaria Sand and her daughters orchestrating a successful coup, resulting in the assassinations of Prince Doran Martell, his son Trystan, and Princess Myrcella Baratheon. This event unequivocally portends open warfare with House Lannister. Notwithstanding this critical development, Cersei and Jaime dispatched their forces northward to Riverrun, seemingly disregarding the impending conflict with Dorne. This momentous event has also failed to elicit discussion among other characters, including those in Meereen or the Riverlands. It is conceivable that the ravens tasked with disseminating information found this particular plotline too tedious and incoherent to transmit.

Watch: Game of Thrones' time travel, explained

On a recent episode of "Jimmy Kimmel Live," the host introduced viewers to a unique perspective on the immensely popular fantasy series, "Game of Thrones." Kimmel enlisted the assistance of two centenarian guests, 101-year-old Gramma and 96-year-old Ginga, to offer their candid reactions to the latest installment of the acclaimed television program. Their feedback, however, proved decidedly unenthusiastic.

When questioned about their viewing preferences, Ginga expressed a strong affinity for procedural dramas, specifically citing "Law & Order" as a favored program. Gramma, on the other hand, indicated a penchant for what she termed "the dancers," a description that strongly suggests an appreciation for the competitive ballroom dancing show, "Dancing with the Stars."

A significant point of contention for the elder reviewers involved the show's visual presentation. Both women found certain scenes to be insufficiently illuminated, rendering them difficult to discern. When Kimmel posed the hypothetical question of whether improved brightness would enhance their viewing experience, their responses diverged. Ginga offered a noncommittal "Well, maybe," while Gramma emphatically declared, "No, hell no!" This suggests that the show's current visual aesthetic and narrative complexity may not resonate with audiences accustomed to different genres and eras of television.

Game of Thrones doesn’t give us a whole lot in the way of optimism, but at least it’s shown us that people can change. Theon Greyjoy can go from a shallow cad to a stammering shell to a loyal kid brother. Jon Snow can go from brooding teen to brooding teen in charge of the Night’s Watch. Sansa Stark can go from wide-eyed romantic to hollowed tactician. Unless, as “No One” suggests, they’re the exact same people they’ve always been. When Edmure Tully asks Jaime Lannister how he lives with himself, we know the answer’s gonna be good. What we don’t know is that Jaime will take us all the way back to the pilot, when a beaming golden boy pushed a curious kid right out the window. “The things we do for love,” Jaime says, and with that, he’s back in full, unrepentant soldier mode. Over the past five years, Jaime has arguably undergone the most complex evolution of any character on the show. The series’ ability to take a standard villain and mold him into a man with a definite, though peculiar, code is part of what makes Game of Thrones so much more nuanced than the rest of high fantasy, and even the rest of television. But “No One” sees him come full circle, with the person who both revealed and catalyzed his do-gooder streak fittingly there to witness it. Jaime tried committing to his role in the Kingsguard; Jaime tried sending Brienne to do something decent, if not necessarily in his best interest. By taking back Riverrun, and Sansa’s potential army along with it, the unlikely alliance that represented Jaime’s hard-earned ambiguity is over. Once again, Brienne and her onetime captive are on opposite sides of a war — and we know which side we’re meant to root for. Jaime is only the most dispiriting example of characters going back to what they know best, an appropriate theme for an episode setting up the season’s climax (and, in two short years, the end of the series). Sandor Clegane has taken up his sword again, because he’s literally a Hound in need of a master. Daenerys has returned to Meereen, because it’s time to shut this quagmire down and take her khalasar to Westeros. And on a much more triumphant note, Arya’s time in Braavos is finally coming to an end, though it’s still unclear what the point of that year-plus of commitment and decommitment and recommitment was. Whatever the answer, her subplot had slowly become one of the show’s most tiresome, largely because its outcome was obvious. A major character wasn’t going to suddenly become someone, or rather no one, else. She has a kill list to get back to! But the symbolism of defeating the Waif with her one remaining relic from home has an undeniable power to it. So does the prospect of Arya finally getting back into the mix, with opponents we care about more than a shapeshifting schoolmarm. Which brings us to the main advantage of walking back a few seasons’ worth of character growth. Depressing as Jaime’s backslide may be, it redraws some clear battle lines: Once again, a Lannister’s goals are at odds with a Stark’s. Ditto for Dany, who’s finally back on track to take what is hers with fire and a whole lot of blood. And while we may not know what role Sandor has to play — though “No One” directly acknowledges more fan theories than “Jaime and Brienne are gonna bone!” — we know it’ll involve some bloodshed; it’s what he does. Everybody has a true nature. If it vanishes, it’s only a matter of time until it reemerges.

Sunday was a profoundly somber day of mourning in America, so it was perhaps for the best that the nation's entertainment obsession — Game of Thrones — was in somber and reflective mood too. Season 6 Episode 8, "No One," could have just been one of those setting-the-table episodes where characters are moved into place for wars to come. But along with the maneuvering, we got some of the show's best disquisitions on life, love and loyalty, with a side helping of honor and dignity. Better yet, the episode saw cracks of enlightenment open up in the bloody medieval hellscapes of war-torn Westeros and Essos. Even in a world as dark as this, it turns out, unlikely friendships can blossom. Old acquaintances can be reunited. The hangman's form of justice can prevail over the butcher's form of justice. All-but-inevitable battles can be avoided. Dragons and their mistresses can return in the nick of time. And a girl can learn to be someone. Indeed, the episode was largely about women coming to know themselves, and learning who they can and can't rely on. The show has been tilting in this direction all season, of course — see also Sansa Stark, Gilly and Lady Lyanna Mormont — but it was never clearer than in "No One," in which no characters displayed more agency than Arya, Brienne, Daenerys and Cersei. You might quibble with including Cersei in this list, given that the Lannister Queen had to suffer two major indignities. First, to be relegated by her own uncle, Hand of the King Kevan Lannister, to "the gallery with the other ladies of the court." Moments later came the gut punch of seeing her own son declare from the Iron Throne that trial by combat, Cersei's only hope in her forthcoming court date with the Sparrows, is now "forbidden through the seven kingdoms." But Cersei is smart enough to always keep at least two allies to hand. She has the brawn of Ser Robert Strong, a.k.a. The FrankenMountain, whose crushing of a Sparrow right in front of Lancel and his mob prevented Cersei from being taken captive. (And she didn't forget her manners: "Please tell the High Sparrow he's welcome to visit.") And she has the brains of Qyburn, who has taken over Varys' "little birds" network and apparently been investigating an "old rumor" that is "much more" than they'd hoped. Could this perhaps have something to do with the vast stores of wildfire under the city? Or something even more nefarious? All we know is: Never underestimate Cersei when she's hatching a plot, and someone should probably alert whatever rudimentary Fire Department they may have in King's Landing. Image: HBo You might say Jaime Lannister has agency in this episode; after all, it is he, not Brienne, who ends the siege of Riverrun. He talks Edmure Tully into walking into the castle and demanding the inhabitants, his liege servants, lay down their arms. But why does he do this? In a surprisingly unguarded moment, he tells Edmure that none of the battle to come will matter to Jaime, because he loves Cersei and is merely doing this to return to her. He would launch Edmure's baby over the battlements in a trebuchet, because nothing matters. "The things we do for love," Jaime said, echoing what he said in the very first episode before throwing Bran out of a window for Cersei and setting the entire disastrous tale in motion. Then as now, he is very much her creature. The only good in Jaime is brought out, then and now, by Brienne — perhaps the only other woman he loves, and not in the crude way Bronn enjoyed joking with Podrick about. (Bronn also grudgingly conceded that Jaime simply has more game than he: "The way all women look at him is frankly irritating.") Image: HBO The long-awaited reunion of the Kingslayer and his former captor was much more sublime. Brienne reported that she'd found Sansa, and tried to broker a deal for the Blackfish's life in order to bring him and his army to her. She tried to return his sword, appropriately called Oathkeeper, even as she warned him that an attack on the Blackfish was an attack on her. Jaime bade her keep the sword — a huge deal, this is Valryian steel we're talking about — and the worrying foreshadowing of Jaime and Brienne taking up arms against each other did not pass, at least not in this episode. She escaped, he saw it, chose to do nothing, and the two frenemies shared a final meaningful wave. Brienne also received possibly the greatest compliment of the Blackfish's life as he assisted her escape. She had previously informed him in no uncertain terms that she would serve Sansa until the day she died, so he had the measure of her. She insisted he come with her. "You'll serve Sansa better than I could," said the gruff old soldier, before laughing off his death by grumbling about how long it had been since he'd had a sword fight. Arya hadn't had a sword fight in a long time either, or a proper ally — and in this episode, she found both. She also has renewed purpose, and even hinted at where she might end up at the end of the series. "The edge of the world, maybe," said the actress Lady Crane as she nursed Arya back to life from the stab wounds inflicted by the no-name waif, in response to Arya's question about what was west of Westeros. Arya's face lit up: "I'd like to see that." Foreshadowing alert! Lady Crane was killed by the waif, and the chase was on again. As Arya fled and jumped off very tall ledges in the backstreets of Braavos, it was even harder to believe she'd survive her injuries than it was last week, let alone make it out without breaking a bone. A girl has become very good at parkour. But the waif was a genuinely scary threat (and a very corporeal one — sorry, everyone who believed that "Arya and the waif are the same person" fan theory). Slicing the candle with Needle was a neat trick given that she's learned how to fight in the dark. Back at the House of Black and White, Jaquen gave his charge another ultimate compliment: "A girl has become no one." The look on his face when Arya insisted on who she was, and that she was going home, was a nicely economical way of telling the story too. He acknowledged her agency. Elsewhere in Essos, the lack of female leadership — specifically, Daenerys Targaryen — was being keenly felt. "She'll come back," insisted Varys to Tyrion as he left on a secret mission to aid Mereen, a sweet and sorrowful parting for one of our favorite buddy comedy pairings. "She has to. My heart's been broken too many times already." And return she did, atop Drogon, though not before the rest of Slavers' Bay launched a sneak attack by sea and Tyrion tried to get Misandre and Grey Worm to drink wine and tell jokes. After Tyrion's killer Stark joke, we can only hope he gets a standup routine — if only to hear the end of that "honeycomb and the jackass" gag. Though through an odd editing choice, we barely lingered on Daenerys for a second before cutting away. She didn't get to say a word to her people. A perfect opportunity for the mother of dragons to drop the mic went begging. The best line of the episode, meanwhile, went to the Hound. Newly nihilistic after witnessing a massacre of many good people last week, he went on a rampage against the perpetrators, rogue members of the Brotherhood Without Banners. He didn't have agency; his blind need for revenge did. The hunt led him to his old frenemies, Beric Dondarrion, the Brotherhood's undead leader, and Thoros of Myr, the Red priest who brought Beric back to life. Beric and Thoros convinced the Hound to execute the remaining guilty men by rope rather than butcher them by axe. But they can't convince him that he has returned to their midst for some godly purpose. Replies the Hound: "A lot of horrible shit gets done in this world for 'something larger than ourselves.'" On a day like this, we couldn't agree more. And as book readers know, the return of the Brotherhood, and the many discussions of Catelyn Stark in this episode, may be the greatest foreshadowing of all. The show may be about to reveal an angry, vengeful and powerful female figure to put all the rest to shame. Have something to add to this story? Share it in the comments.

**"No One" - Arya's Fate in Game of Thrones Season 6, Episode 8**

This week's episode of "Game of Thrones," Season 6, Episode 8, titled "No One," largely confirmed our suspicions about Arya Stark's storyline. While the enigmatic Faceless Men always leave room for doubt, the attack on the bridge by the Waif appears to have been precisely as it seemed. Arya was indeed wounded, and the Waif was genuinely hunting her.

Arya's desperate escape was a **thrilling sequence**, though the episode itself felt somewhat anticlimactic. The **unusual behavior** Arya displayed last week – walking through the city seemingly unconcerned by assassins – was, it turns out, simply that: odd behavior, not a clever ploy. The promising character of Lady Crane, who hinted at a larger role, was **swiftly dispatched** by the Waif.

Ultimately, Arya **lured her relentless pursuer** to the hidden chamber we saw two weeks prior. This was the location where she extinguished the candle, signaling her **ingenious plan of attack**. She intended to use the very **sightless fighting skills** honed through the Waif's brutal training to defeat her former tormentor. Her victory was sealed by **mounting the Waif's head** on the wall of the House of Black and White.

Jaqen H'ghar's pronouncement, "Um, you passed!", suggests either that this entire ordeal was his elaborate scheme, or simply that Arya had proven her formidable capabilities by holding a sword to his chest. A girl is **finally no one**. Congratulations, Arya!

"The things we do for love." When Jaime Lannister says this to Edmure Tully, his prisoner and bargaining chip, he's quoting no less an authority than himself. These were the same words he uttered just before he tossed Bran Stark out the window to cover up his sexual relationship with his own sister, Cersei. Now he's using them to describe the intensity of his love for her — confident that his prisoner's feelings for his own family (especially the young son he's never seen) will lead him to surrender the castle of Riverrun to save them. His gamble pays off, of course. If there's one thing that tonight's episode of Game of Thrones — "No One" — gets right, it's how much our desire to see the people we care about one more time can motivate us. That, and how much leaving them behind can hurt us. Related 30 Best 'Game of Thrones' Villains From the Hound to the High Sparrow, our picks of the worst people in Westeros and beyond Indeed, Jaime learns his own lesson bitterly. As he stands alone on the battlements of Riverrun at dawn, triumphant but despondent after the castle's surrender, he sees Brienne of Tarth — the closest thing he has to an actual friend — escaping in a boat down the river. He raises his golden hand to wave goodbye, a wave she returns with evident sorrow. As she slowly floats away from him, the cold gray light of the early morning makes his face look carved from stone, like a statue in a crypt. Yes, he loves Cersei enough to threaten to murder Edmure's baby. But the promises he made to Brienne, and vice versa, are the best part of himself. Now he's watching that part sail away, likely for good. "Honor compels me to fight for Sansa's kin — to fight you," Brienne had told him during their earlier meeting. "Let's hope it doesn't come to that," he replied. Both are equally upset by the notion of having to battle each other. That their farewell comes in this fashion rather than at swordpoint is a mercy they'll likely both think about for a long time to come. If the Kingslayer and the Maid of Tarth's goodbye was the episode's most moving, Tyrion and Varys' was a surprisingly close second. As they walk to the docks of Meereen, where the spymaster plans to depart for Westeros to drum up support for Daenerys, the pair banters about politics and their physical deficits; doing anything else would be too painful to contemplate. The Spider is a slippery character, and the Imp a cynical one, but Conleth Hill and Peter Dinklage invest their unlikely friendship with a warmth that easily transcends whatever doubts you might have about their sincerity. These guys like each other, and that affection has saved countless lives across the world. Like Jaime and Brienne, they bring out the best in each other, understanding one another like only outcasts (self-made or otherwise) can. Some departures, however, are more symbolic than spatial. Take Cersei Lannister and her son Tommen, for example. When the boy king calls an unexpected meeting in the throne room, the Queen Mother is banished … to the gallery, with the other ladies of the court. It's hardly being expelled from King's Landing, or being thrown back into prison, both of which seemed possible. Yet the distance, small as it is, proves emotionally insurmountable when the young royal issues a ban on trial by combat. This was to be the Lannister lioness' ace in the hole to prove her innocence, since her towering guardian Gregor "The Mountain" Clegane can literally tear his opponents' heads off with one hand. By cutting her off from this option, Tommen's essentially sentencing her to death. Parallel tracking shots, one following the child as he walks from the throne, the other following the mother as she races alongside in disbelief, emphasize how far apart they really are. Yet there are reunions in this episode that aren't engineered to break your heart. Jaime and Brienne's may have been a colossal bummer, but that can't be said for Bronn and Podrick Payne: Tyrion's two former cronies, somehow among the most lovable characters on the whole show, meet again with all the dick jokes and horseplay you'd expect. The Hound's run-in with his former enemies Beric Dondarrion and Thoros of Myr, leaders of the Brotherhood guerillas, also goes much better than expected: After gruesomely slaughtering his way to them in search of vengeance for the massacre of his religious community last week, he finds that they too are equally outraged by the violence. He turns their execution of the perpetrators into black comedy — he's the Hound, that's kind of his thing — and literally pisses as they warn him of the coming war against the White Walkers. But he's clearly considering taking up arms for a cause bigger and better than mere bloodlust. Related 'Game of Thrones' Season Premiere: No Country for Old Women A shocking revelation marks the smash hit's return — but it's not the one you think His old partner Arya fares just as well. Her rescue by Lady Crane, the talented actor whose life she saved, ends in tragedy when her rival, the Waif, returns to finish the job she refused to do. (The strength of Maisie Williams's sad, scared performance throughout the sequence makes it all that much more upsetting.) But the Stark child defeats her enemy and returns to the assassins' temple, where she tells her mentor Jaqen H'ghar of her plans to return to Winterfell. Instead of stopping her, he smiles: The Many-Faced God has received his due, and the girl he clearly cares about has discovered who she really is. But the most important reunion of all, geopolitically speaking, happens in the Great Pyramid of Meereen. After a drinking scene with Tyrion, Missandei, and Grey Worm, followed by a spectacular naval attack on the city by the slave masters, Daenerys returns, dragon in tow. We wouldn't want to be the slavers now, that's for sure — and we're glad that scene after scene has shown that good men and women still exist in Westeros, each ready to take up her fight. Previously: Dog Day Afternoon See who made our list of the 30 best 'Game of Thrones' villains.

query: What led to Liu Xiaobo's extended imprisonment and eventual diagnosis with liver cancer?

documents.text:
The world mourns the loss of **Liu Xiaobo**, a Nobel Peace Prize laureate whose life was dedicated to the **unwavering pursuit of democracy, freedom, and human rights in China**. Liu passed away at the age of 61, succumbing to advanced liver cancer after a courageous battle. He spent his final days receiving treatment for the aggressive illness in a Shenyang hospital.

Authorities in Shenyang officially confirmed Liu's death, stating he passed away at 5:35 PM. His wife, Liu Xia, and brothers, Liu Xiaoguang and Liu Xiaoxuan, were by his side. His last words to his wife were a poignant, "Live on well." Medical professionals described his cancer as a **rare and aggressive malignancy** that progressed rapidly.

News of Liu Xiaobo's death sparked global grief and numerous tributes. The Norwegian Nobel Committee issued a statement, and governments of the **United States, Germany, and France** urgently appealed to Beijing to ease travel restrictions on his wife and family.

Liu Xiaobo, known for his **resolute voice speaking inconvenient truths**, was a central figure in China's human rights movement. Shenyang authorities announced his death shortly after 9 PM, with state news agency Xinhua reporting it around 10:37 PM. Xinhua also noted the involvement of **distinguished Chinese physicians and international specialists** in his care.

In Hong Kong, over 100 people gathered outside Beijing's liaison office, transforming a protest for Liu's release into a solemn vigil. Law Yuk-kai of the Hong Kong Human Rights Monitor observed, "Liu’s case shows that Beijing will hold its grip very tight despite international pressure. And we can foresee that Hong Kong’s road to democracy will get more difficult." Despite fervent appeals, Liu died before he could receive the specialized treatment in Germany he and his wife had hoped for.

Liu Xiaobo's death is particularly somber as he is the **second Nobel laureate to die in state custody**. The first was German pacifist Carl von Ossietzky, who died in 1938 under Nazi rule.

Liu's activism led to his arrest in late 2008 for participating in drafting **"Charter 08,"** a manifesto advocating for political reform and expanded freedoms. He was sentenced to 11 years in prison in 2009 and awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 2010 for his commitment to human rights. The Shenyang Justice Bureau disclosed Liu was diagnosed with late-stage liver cancer in late May, but critical information about his deteriorating condition was not publicly shared until late June. Beijing consistently denied international requests for Liu to seek treatment abroad, claiming he was receiving optimal care in China.

According to close associates, Liu fell into a coma early on Thursday, briefly regained consciousness, and then lapsed back into a coma around 11 AM. Exiled Tiananmen student leader Wang Dan remembered Liu as "a great man who left us, a light extinguished." Wang called Liu's death "the second June 4. This is a naked political murder," and vowed to continue the struggle for China's democratization in his honor.

A collective statement from ten of Liu's close friends hailed him as **"one of the most important voices of the contemporary freedom and human rights movement in China"** and a symbol of the Chinese people's relentless struggle for freedom. Bao Tong, a former aide to Zhao Ziyang, stated, "The state may have convicted him of subversion, but it must be pointed out that he was a great patriot."

Berit Reiss-Andersen, head of the Norwegian Nobel Committee, held the Chinese government accountable for Liu's "premature death," expressing concern he was not transferred for adequate medical treatment. United Nations Secretary General Antonio Guterres joined the global mourning.

German Chancellor Angela Merkel mourned Liu Xiaobo as "the courageous fighter for human rights and freedom of expression." Both Germany and the U.S. reiterated calls for Chinese authorities to allow Liu's relatives to leave the country. German Foreign Minister Sigmar Gabriel urged China to allow Liu Xia and her brother to travel freely and questioned whether Liu Xiaobo's cancer could have been detected earlier.

U.S. Secretary of State Rex Tillerson acknowledged the "tragic passing of 2010 Nobel Peace Prize Laureate Liu Xiaobo, who died while serving a lengthy prison sentence in China for promoting peaceful democratic reform." Tillerson also implored China to release Liu Xia from house arrest and permit her to leave the country.

Dissidents Wang Dan and Hu Jia echoed these sentiments, urging the lifting of travel restrictions on Liu Xia, who had been under de facto house arrest since 2010. Hu Jia emphasized the need to **"continue to push for Liu Xia’s freedom, and the unfulfilled dreams in Charter 08, in order to sustain the spirit of Liu Xiaobo."**

BEIJING — In his final days, as China’s most prominent political dissident, Liu Xiaobo, succumbed to liver cancer under state surveillance, he dedicated his efforts to completing a poignant, perhaps final, written testament. This work transcended overt political discourse, instead offering a deeply personal and often enigmatic homage to his wife, Liu Xia. An artist and poet herself, she endured prolonged house arrest during his eleven-year incarceration. “Love as intense as ice, love as remote as blackness,” one of the handwritten fragments penned in a Shenyang hospital reveals, a testament to his profound affection. “My praise is perhaps an unforgivable poison,” he further articulated in this brief, sometimes fragmented, tribute to his wife and her artistic endeavors. These poignant notes were intended as the preface for an as-yet-unpublished compilation of Liu Xia’s photographs, tentatively titled “Accompanying Liu Xiaobo.” The manuscript and photographic collection were disseminated by a trusted Chinese editor, a confidant of the couple who had assisted in the book’s compilation. This editor disclosed that Liu Xiaobo initiated contact late last month, with subsequent transmissions of hospital-bound photographic evidence of his notes facilitated by individuals close to him. The editor, citing apprehension regarding potential reprisal, requested anonymity. Liu Xiaobo will undoubtedly be remembered primarily as an unyielding dissenter, recognized with the Nobel Peace Prize in 2010 while imprisoned. His conviction in 2009 followed his involvement in advocating for democratic reforms, a petition that precipitated his arrest.

China’s most famous dissident, Liu Xiaobo, has died in custody at age 61. The cause was liver cancer. His death, confirmed Thursday evening local time on July 13 by Chinese justice authorities (link in Chinese) in the northeastern city of Shenyang, comes more than two weeks after prison authorities announced he was being moved to a hospital for treatment, news that was followed by pleas from around the world to allow him to travel abroad for care. Thursday’s brief statement didn’t mention the Nobel Prize awarded to Liu for a life dedicated to the memory of the protesters killed in Beijing’s Tiananmen Square in 1989. In April 1989, Liu, a literary critic and comparative literature professor, was in New York City as a visiting scholar at Columbia University when he heard of the protests in Tiananmen Square. He hurried back to Beijing and spent weeks among the student protesters. Two days after the Communist Party’s deadly crackdown on June 4, Liu was detained and jailed. When he was freed in 1991, he resumed his public demands for greater freedoms in China over the next two decades. In 2008, along with other dissidents, he drafted Charter 08, a document that called on the Chinese state to fundamentally change its character and abandon one-party rule. Soon after he was tried for subversion. According to freedom-of-speech advocacy group PEN America, he was convicted in 2009 for writing seven sentences, a total of 224 Chinese characters. He was given an 11-year sentence. On June 26, 2017, after serving more than seven years of his sentence, the prison holding Liu announced that he was being transferred to a nearby hospital to be treated for late-stage liver cancer—the same disease that killed his father. Here is a timeline of the life of a man who has been deeply admired, who is being deeply mourned, and whose last days have become for many a symbol for many of the darkness that clouds the story of China’s economic rise. 1955 Liu Xiaobo is born on Dec. 28 in northeastern China’s Jilin province, the third of five brothers. 1966 The Cultural Revolution begins. Millions of urban youth head to the countryside to be “re-educated” by peasants, following leader Mao Zedong’s exhortations to “go up into the mountains and down to the countryside (上山下乡运动)” (paywall). Liu’s grandmother, whom he is very close to, is designated a “landlord” and sent away. Meanwhile, his father, a Communist Party member, is busy, and his older brothers go away to become Red Guards. Liu remembers this time of turmoil as one of unexpected freedom according to his biography, Steel Gate to Freedom: “My parents were off ‘revolutionizing,’ schools stopped, and I was for a time able to be rid of the constraints of an ‘education.'” 1977 Liu is admitted to Jilin University and studies Chinese literature. During his university life, he organizes The Innocent Hearts (赤子心), a poetry group with his friends. 1982 Liu starts a two-year master’s program in Chinese literature at Beijing Normal University. After receiving his master’s, Liu stays at Beijing Normal University to teach and marries Tao Li, with whom he has a son, Liu Tao. 1987 Liu begins his doctoral studies and publishes literary criticism in dozens of magazines. In 1987 he publishes his first book, Criticism of the Choice: Dialogues with Li Zehou, which critiques a prominent intellectual. It becomes a bestseller. 1988 Liu works abroad at universities in Oslo and Hawaii and at Columbia University as a visiting scholar. 1989 While Liu is still in America, Chinese students break into massive pro-democracy demonstrations in Tiananmen Square. He flies back to stand staunchly on the side of students. Liu, as well as others, including Zhou Duo and Hou Dejian (a Taiwan-born singer), play pivotal roles, including organizing a hunger strike. Thanks to Liu’s efforts to persuade students to leave the square, many of them escape death. However, because of his leadership role in the protest, Liu is detained, fired from his job as a teacher, and his books are banned. He later divorces his wife Tao in order to protect his family, according to the Guardian newspaper. His son, then about six years old, goes to live with his grandparents; Liu will see little of him for the rest of his life, according to his biography. 1992 Liu doesn’t stop writing. His book The Monologues of a Doomsday’s Survivor, about his memories and evaluation of the Tiananmen Square protest, is published in Taiwan. In the book, he points out what he sees as the misjudgments of the movement. He says that it delayed China’s democratization, and that students and intellectuals had become “conceited—just as if we had reverted to the time of the Cultural Revolution.” Will Burgess/Reuters Pro-democracy activist Liu Xiaobo makes a point during a conversation in March 1995. 1996 Liu is sentenced to three years behind bars for a letter criticizing party rule. While still in the labor camp, he marries his current wife, fellow poet Liu Xia. According to Ian Johnson, writing for the New York Review of Books: They had met in the 1980s but became close only after Tiananmen. In 1995, Liu Xiaobo was detained for writing an open letter calling for basic rights and then formally sentenced in 1996 to three years in prison. Liu Xia traveled regularly up to the labor camp and—in a phrase that is now legendary—told the guards that “I want to marry that enemy of the state!” Eventually she did, while he was still in prison; their wedding banquet was in the prison cafeteria. David Gray/Reuters Liu Xia, wife of Liu Xiaobo, was placed under house arrest in 2010. 1999 Liu Xiaobo is released in October. 2001 Liu Xiaobo helps establish the Independent Chinese PEN Center, a “non-political, non-profit organization of writers that fights for the protection of freedom of expression and publication,” and serves as its president from 2003 to 2007. During this time he writes the essay “The internet is God’s present to China,” about his belief that the internet is making China freer (republished later in the Times of London). 2008 Liu Xiaobo co-authors Charter 08 with other dissidents, a political manifesto. The document appeals for a fundamental political transformation including a new constitution, separation of powers, and guarantee of human rights. In December 2008, he is detained. 2009 He is put on trial.
