**New Evidence Points to "Life Is Beautiful" Festival as Potential Paddock Target**

Investigators are exploring a significant new lead in the Las Vegas mass shooting, suggesting Stephen Paddock may have initially planned to attack a different music festival held the week before the Route 91 Harvest Festival. A senior law enforcement official, privy to the ongoing inquiry, indicated that Paddock had rented multiple condominium units offering a clear view of the annual Life Is Beautiful Festival. This event, which occurred the week prior to the fatal shooting, showcased notable performers like Lorde and Chance the Rapper.

**The Ogden Building Examined**

In an effort to corroborate these assertions, The Daily Beast investigated The Ogden, a prominent high-rise residential building that provides unobstructed sightlines to the Life Is Beautiful festival grounds. A representative for Fais Foley Warren, the property management company for The Ogden, released a statement: "We cannot confirm or deny any information concerning Mr. Paddock's actions. We recommend directing all questions to the Metropolitan Police Department, as this investigation is still active."

**Change of Plans and the Route 91 Harvest Festival**

The source indicated that Paddock might have changed his intentions, possibly due to a change of mind, before checking into the Mandalay Bay Hotel on September 28. It was from his Mandalay Bay suite, overlooking the Route 91 Harvest Festival, that Paddock unleashed gunfire on approximately 20,000 attendees. Authorities discovered 23 firearms within the hotel suite, many of them rifles modified for automatic firing. Sheriff Joseph Lombardo of the Las Vegas Metropolitan Police Department verified that Paddock had transported this extensive collection of weapons in 10 suitcases. Further searches at his Mesquite, Nevada, home revealed an additional 19 firearms, a substantial quantity of ammunition, and materials for constructing explosives, including ammonium nitrate found in his car.

Investigators trying to determine what sparked the deadliest mass shooting in modern U.S. history have found the massacre to be the work of a sophisticated planner with the means and desire to inflict unprecedented carnage. But on the second full day after Stephen Paddock smashed out the windows of a high-floor suite on the Las Vegas Strip and opened fire on a crowd of unsuspecting concertgoers, authorities still were trying to understand what drove him to such evil. Several new details emerged Tuesday about Paddock — a 64-year-old retired accountant — and how he worked methodically to thwart law enforcement as he killed scores of people and injured hundreds more. [ Two strangers bond over country music and beer. Then the gunshots started. ] As he fired round after round during an 11-minute stretch from a suite on the 32nd floor of the Mandalay Bay Resort and Casino, Paddock used video cameras to keep an eye out for police storming his hotel room, according to Clark County Sheriff Joe Lombardo. Paddock hid one camera in the peephole of his suite and two more in the hall, at least one of them disguised on a service cart, authorities said. At one point, he shot numerous rounds through the door, wounding a security guard. Paddock eventually put a gun in his own mouth and pulled the trigger as SWAT officers closed in. They found him with blood pooling behind his head and around the empty shell casings that littered the carpet, a handgun near his body. “It was preplanned, extensively, and I’m pretty sure that he evaluated everything that he did in his actions, which is troublesome,�? Lombardo said. The sheriff said investigators were “making progress�? on determining a motive, but complete answers remained elusive. There were precious few clues in Paddock’s background. An investigator works in the room at the Mandalay Bay Resort and Casino on Tuesday, in Las Vegas. A gunman opened fire from the room, killing at least 58 people and injuring hundreds. (AP Photo/Marcio Jose Sanchez) Neighbors in several states where Paddock owned homes in retirement communities describe him as surly, unfriendly and standoffish. Paddock was the son of a bank robber who was once on the FBI’s most-wanted list and who authorities described at the time as a “psychopath,�? but Paddock’s brother said their father was not involved in their lives when they were children. Relatives say the roots of Paddock’s loner lifestyle might have been planted on July 28, 1960. On that day, when Paddock was 7, a neighbor from across the street took him swimming. The neighbor told a local newspaper at the time that she knew authorities were coming for his father, and she wanted to spare the young boy from the trauma of seeing his father taken away. From that point on, Paddock’s family was never the same. Until carrying out the massacre Sunday night, Paddock had no criminal history himself. Despite repeated claims by the Islamic State to the contrary, he also had no ties to international terror groups, authorities said. He had done some government work during his career, as a letter carrier for the Postal Service, an agent for the Internal Revenue Service and an auditor for the federal government’s Defense Contract Audit Agency in the late 1970s and 1980s. He was divorced twice and recently had been dating a woman from the Philippines who has Australian citizenship. He was known to gamble routinely and extensively. Some public officials seemed to suggest Paddock’s mind was troubled, though there were no immediate indications that he had been diagnosed with a mental illness or was anything other than fully aware of what he was doing. “A normal person would not cause this type of harm to innocent people,�? said Rep. Ruben Kihuen (D-Nev.). “Clearly, there was something wrong with this man.�? People close to the investigation also said that in the weeks before the attack, Paddock transferred a large amount of money — close to $100,000 — to someone in the Philippines, possibly his girlfriend. The significance of that development was not immediately clear, though investigators said they were interested in probing Paddock’s finances and his avid interest in high-stakes gambling. The girlfriend, Marilou Danley, returned to the United States from the Philippines Tuesday night and was met at Los Angeles International Airport by FBI agents, according to wire reports. Investigators considered her a “person of interest,�? Lombardo said. Sen. Dean Heller (R-Nev.) said that police are eager to talk to her, as Paddock “doesn’t meet any profiles�? and the “best lead is through this girlfriend.�? “They don’t know a lot about who the girlfriend is and why she left the country a week prior to the shooting,�? said Heller, who has been briefed by authorities. “She is someone they need to have this discussion with to better understand the shooting and what his thought process was.�? Douglas and Sylvia Vick from Texas pray at a makeshift memorial on the Las Vegas Strip on Tuesday. (Mark Ralston/AFP/Getty Images) At his home in Orlando, Eric Paddock, Stephen Paddock’s brother, said he doubts Danley had any prior knowledge of the incident and speculated that Stephen might have been trying to quietly ensure her financial stability. Stephen Paddock loved and doted on his girlfriend, whom he had met when she was a hostess at a casino, Eric Paddock said. The couple often gambled side by side. “He manipulated her to be as far away from here and safe when he committed this,�? Eric Paddock said. “The people he loved he took care of, and as he was descending into hell he took care of her.�? The shooting — which left 59 people dead and injured more than 500 others — reignited the debate on Capitol Hill and across the country about whether lawmakers should impose new restrictions on firearms. Clark County Coroner John Fudenberg on Tuesday evening clarified that Paddock was among the 59 slain in the incident; previously, authorities had said he wasn’t. Undersheriff Kevin C. McMahill, speaking after Fudenberg, warned that the number of dead and injured could fluctuate as the investigation progresses. “The answer that the coroner provided you is the most recent, relevant number that we have,�? McMahill said. Caution tape lines the doors of the gunman’s suite on the 32nd floor of the Mandalay Bay hotel. (Bild Exclusive/Polaris.) Several Democrats used the massacre to push for new restrictions, and House Speaker Paul D. Ryan (R-Wis.) asserted Tuesday that Republican leaders have no plans to advance a bill, which passed a House committee last month, that would make it easier to buy gun silencers. Leaving the White House to visit hurricane-ravaged Puerto Rico on Tuesday morning, President Trump said that “we’ll be talking about gun laws as time goes by.�? Paddock brought 23 guns to his Mandalay Bay suite, which overlooked the concert grounds on the southern end of the Strip. Included in that cache was an AR-15-type rifle with a high-capacity magazine and 12 “bump stocks,�? a modification that would allow a gun to fire as if it were an automatic weapon. He also brought another AR-15-type rifle with a magnification scope commonly used for hunting and a bipod stand to help steady it, according to law enforcement officials and experts who reviewed images of the weapons posted online. “For this individual to take it upon himself to create this chaos and harm is unspeakable,�? Lombardo said. [ The lives lost in the Las Vegas massacre ] The guns appeared to be but a piece of the arsenal Paddock had acquired during a period of years, at least some of it legally obtained from major retailers such as Cabela’s, national retailer of guns and outdoor gear, and Guns & Guitars, a store in Mesquite, Nev., according to people familiar with the case. ATF Special Agent in Charge Jill A. Snyder said Tuesday Paddock had purchased shotguns, handguns and rifles in Nevada, Utah, California and Texas. At Paddock’s home in Mesquite, Nev., investigators recovered 19 more guns and an explosive, and in his car, they found ammonium nitrate, a chemical that can be used in bomb making. At another property in Reno, Nev., investigators recovered five more handguns, two shotguns and a “plethora�? of ammunition, Lombardo said Tuesday. Paddock probably secreted the weapons to his Mandalay Bay suite using more than 10 suitcases he brought with him. Lombardo said hotel housekeeping had been there since Paddock checked in on Sept. 28 and spotted nothing nefarious. Lombardo said investigators had scoured Paddock’s suite, his car and the homes in Nevada, and they were examining surveillance and body camera video evidence and other electronic items they had seized in their searches. Separately, Lombardo said the department had opened an investigation into the unauthorized release of images that show the crime scene. Another sheriff’s official confirmed the authenticity of those images. Police also continued documenting the crime scene and performing the grim task of identifying those slain, some of whom died blocks away from the concert as they tried to flee or seek help. As of late Tuesday afternoon, authorities had tentatively identified all of the victims. [ How the shooting unfolded ] Since the shooting, horrific stories have emerged of lives cut short, some taking bullets as they sought to protect their loved ones. Bobby and Jenny Parks, a married couple from California who had been high school sweethearts, were swaying to a love song when a bullet struck her. He held her as she died. After Cameron Robinson, a 27-year-old from Utah, was shot in the neck, his boyfriend, Robert Eardley, carried him to a vehicle. Robinson died before they made it to a hospital. Two people who met at the festival — Kody Robertson and Michelle Vo — were watching the final act together when a bullet struck her chest. Robertson threw his body over hers to protect her. Later, they were separated; he tracked her down to a hospital and was told late Monday morning that she was dead. Paddock’s weaponry would have allowed him to fire both rapidly and precisely at the crowd that was several hundred yards away and below him. The shots — fired from two windows of Mandalay Bay’s golden facade, which Paddock had smashed out — sent thousands of people scrambling for safety under Vegas’s neon glow. Police on Tuesday showed body camera footage of officers scrambling to determine where the shots came from as they yelled for people to leave the area. “They’re shooting right at us, guys,�? an officer can be heard saying. “Stay down! Stay down!�? Hospitals across the region continued to treat patients from the scene, many of them seriously injured. Sunrise Hospital and Medical Center says as of Tuesday night, 59 patients remain in the hospital and 31 of them are still in critical condition. University Medical Center said it had 64 patients from the attack, 12 of them critical. Roads inside the Mandalay Bay Resort are closed for investigation on Tuesday in Las Vegas. Davis, William Wan and Sari Horwitz in Washington contributed to this report.

### Diazepam Prescription for Las Vegas Shooter Stephen Paddock Revealed

A prescription for the anti-anxiety drug diazepam was issued to Stephen Paddock, the 64-year-old Mesquite resident responsible for the Las Vegas massacre, on June 21, 2017. This information comes from a state Prescription Monitoring Program record obtained by the Review-Journal. Paddock filled this prescription for 50 10-milligram tablets on the same day it was written, purchasing the medication, known by its brand name Valium, without insurance at a Walgreens in Reno.

#### Potential Link to Aggressive Behavior

Diazepam belongs to the benzodiazepine class of drugs, which are sedative-hypnotics. Studies have indicated a potential link between benzodiazepine use and aggressive behavior. Dr. Mel Pohl, chief medical officer of the Las Vegas Recovery Center, explained that for individuals with an underlying predisposition to aggression, sedatives like diazepam can exacerbate these tendencies, acting similarly to alcohol in disinhibiting emotional states. He noted that the drug's effects can be amplified by alcohol consumption.

#### Research on Benzodiazepines and Violence

Further research has explored the association between benzodiazepine use and violent acts. A 2015 study published in *World Psychiatry*, involving 960 Finnish adults and adolescents convicted of homicide, found a 45 percent higher likelihood of killing during periods when they were taking benzodiazepines. Additionally, a study in the *Australian and New Zealand Journal of Psychiatry* from 2014 concluded that benzodiazepine use is moderately associated with subsequent aggressive behavior.

#### Expert Opinions on Paddock's Actions

Dr. Michael First, a clinical psychiatry professor at Columbia University and an expert on benzodiazepines, acknowledged the findings of the Finnish study but suggested that the drugs are more likely to fuel impulsive aggression rather than premeditated actions. He pointed out that Paddock's attack, which involved meticulously planning and bringing an arsenal of weapons to the Mandalay Bay hotel, appeared to be highly premeditated. Dr. First emphasized the importance of understanding the reasons behind Paddock's prescription, suggesting it could offer insight into his actions.

#### Previous Prescription and Broader Concerns

The Nevada state monitoring report also indicated that Dr. Steven Winkler, the Henderson physician who prescribed the diazepam in June 2017, had previously prescribed 50 10-milligram tablets of the same drug to Paddock in 2016. That prescription was filled the same day it was written at Evergreen Drugs in Henderson, with instructions for two tablets per day. The revelation of this prescription adds to ongoing discussions and concerns about the potential link between psychiatric medications and mass shootings, although definitive connections remain elusive and researchers caution against stigmatizing individuals who rely on these medications for their well-being.

**Unsung Heroes: Off-Duty Firefighters' Courageous Response to the Las Vegas Mass Shooting**

The Las Vegas country music festival shooting, a horrific event that unfolded on October 1, 2017, revealed the extraordinary bravery and swift action of off-duty firefighters who found themselves thrust into the role of critical first responders. What began as a night of entertainment for thousands quickly devolved into a scene of unimaginable terror when a lone gunman opened fire from the 32nd floor of the Mandalay Bay hotel. Amidst the ensuing chaos and panic, individuals who were enjoying the concert as civilians, including off-duty firefighters Benjamin Kole, Jesse Gomez, and Anthony Robone, were among the first to react. Unprepared and lacking their standard emergency equipment, such as radios and advanced medical kits, these trained professionals were compelled to act, transforming into indispensable lifesavers in the critical moments following the initial volleys of gunfire. For eleven harrowing minutes, their extensive training, ingrained sense of duty, and inherent drive to assist propelled them into action, making them vital components of the immediate response.

Benjamin Kole, a firefighter by profession, found himself amidst the fleeing crowd. His immediate, decisive command, "Not one (vehicle) leaves without a patient, a victim," underscored his unwavering commitment to ensuring that every available resource was directed towards aiding the injured. His daughter, Rachel Kole, who was employed by an ambulance company and present at the event, was also caught in the unfolding tragedy. After a brief, emotional reunion amidst the pandemonium, Benjamin’s focus shifted from personal safety to his professional obligation. "Time to get to work," he declared, and together, father and daughter began providing immediate medical assistance to those around them. Kole was deeply struck by the remarkable composure and effectiveness of civilian bystanders who, without hesitation, followed instructions and contributed significantly to the rescue efforts. Their willingness to step forward and assist, even in the face of extreme danger, was a testament to the human spirit's capacity for compassion and courage.

Jesse Gomez, attending the festival with his family, initially mistook the sustained gunfire for fireworks. However, the escalating panic and the frantic exodus of performers from the stage quickly dispelled any illusions, revealing the grim reality of a mass shooting. As his family sought safety, Gomez’s attention was drawn to a woman suffering from a severe head wound. He made the difficult decision to direct his family to continue to safety while he and other bystanders attended to her. He then made what he described as "probably one of the hardest decisions" he has ever made: calling his wife to inform her that he was remaining to help. His wife, upon meeting him in the parking lot, implored him not to return to the active threat zone. Gomez recounted the terrifying proximity of perceived bullet impacts, suggesting a close-quarters firefight was underway. Despite the escalating danger and the palpable fear, he and another individual "went back to work," demonstrating an unwavering dedication to their duty. Gomez was profoundly moved by the significant number of civilians who chose to remain and offer aid, improvising with available materials like tarps, trash cans, and even fences to transport the wounded. He also described the somber and emotionally taxing duty of relocating deceased individuals, a necessary action that required informing grieving relatives they had to evacuate the immediate vicinity due to ongoing risks. This task, performed under immense duress, highlighted the multifaceted and often heartbreaking responsibilities faced by those on the ground.

Anthony Robone’s experience was equally traumatic and deeply personal. He initially attempted to reassure his girlfriend that the sounds were innocuous, but as the situation escalated and he suspected gunshots, he discovered his brother had been struck and was bleeding profusely from the mouth. While a friend escorted his girlfriend to safety, Robone and another companion worked to transport his injured brother towards what appeared to be medical triage areas. The relentless gunfire was overwhelming, with Robone noting, "The rounds seemed like they’d never stop." After administering immediate care to his brother using improvised bandages, Robone’s professional training took precedence. He witnessed a substantial number of fatalities, acknowledging the painful reality: "Unfortunately we couldn’t help at that time." However, amidst the devastation, he also witnessed an inspiring demonstration of collective resilience and selflessness. Civilians were actively applying direct pressure to wounds, utilizing belts as improvised tourniquets, and exhibiting profound altruism. Robone recounted instances where patients with survivable injuries, despite their own pain and suffering, urged first responders to prioritize those with more critical conditions. "I was shot in the arm, I can wait … hey that person is shot in the head or that person is shot in the neck, let them go (to the hospital)," they reportedly conveyed to responders, a testament to an extraordinary level of altruism amidst the crisis. This selflessness, even in the face of their own life-threatening injuries, was a powerful display of humanity.

These three firefighters, along with twelve other off-duty firefighters who sustained gunshot wounds themselves – two of whom were injured while performing cardiopulmonary resuscitation on victims – were among the many who responded when their community faced an unprecedented crisis. The International Association of Fire Fighters union corroborated these harrowing details. Less than 48 hours after the deadliest mass shooting in modern U.S. history, these courageous individuals convened at the Las Vegas Fire Fighters Union Hall to share their experiences and process the profound trauma they had endured. They discussed the agonizing choices they were compelled to make, including the difficult task of informing critically injured individuals that, despite their utmost efforts and the limited resources available, further intervention was not possible. This profound sense of duty, even when off-duty and without their standard operational equipment, underscores the exceptional character of these first responders and the remarkable resilience of the human spirit in the face of unimaginable tragedy. Their actions serve as a powerful testament to the dedication and bravery inherent in the firefighting profession, extending far beyond the confines of their official duties and highlighting the profound impact of their commitment to saving lives. The events of that night serve as a stark reminder of the vital role that off-duty first responders play in moments of crisis, often stepping into the breach with nothing but their training and their courage.

A federal law enforcement official earlier said two rifles were outfitted with scopes and set up on tripods in front of two big windows. Another official said that among the weapons were AR-15-style assault rifles. Both officials spoke on the condition of anonymity because they were not authorized to divulge details of the investigation. Sheriff Lombardo said that Mr. Paddock brought at least 10 suitcases into his hotel room over a period of time. The sheriff said that Mr. Paddock fired through his hotel room door at security guards, striking one in the leg. The guard is still alive, he said. SWAT officers went in after the guard was shot. In addition to the weapons at the hotel, the sheriff said the police retrieved 19 firearms, as well as explosives, several thousand rounds of ammunition and “electronic devices” from Mr. Paddock’s home in Mesquite, Nev. Paddock raised no suspicions at a store where he bought firearms. The investigation into Mr. Paddock’s gun arsenal led investigators to New Frontier Armory, a large North Las Vegas dealer, distributor and manufacturer of firearms. David M. Famiglietti, the company’s president, issued a statement on Tuesday confirming that Mr. Paddock purchased “several” rifles and shotguns from the business last spring — all at the same time. It’s not uncommon for customers to do this to save money on background check fees, Mr. Famiglietti said. “The firearms he purchased did not leave our store capable of what we’ve seen and heard in the video without modification,” he said. “They were not fully automatic firearms, nor were they modified in any way — legally or illegally — when they were purchased from us.” Mr. Famiglietti said that nothing about Mr. Paddock raised red flags with the store’s sales clerk. On the contrary, according to Mr. Famiglietti, Mr. Paddock seemed to be interested in competitive shooting, asking the clerk a lot of questions about three-gun shooting matches — an increasingly popular sport in which players use a rifle, a shotgun and a pistol. “He just seemed like a normal guy,” said Mr. Famiglietti, whose store has been the target of hate mail, threatening phone calls and fake reviews since the shooting. “We obviously did not sell him these firearms with the intent that he would use them to hurt anyone in any way.” On Monday, a Mesquite, Nev., gun dealer, Guns & Guitars, acknowledged having sold two rifles and a handgun to Mr. Paddock. Like the purchases at Frontier, those were cleared by state and federal background checks. The gunman was ‘not an avid gun guy at all,’ said one of his brothers. Mr. Paddock, 64, was described as a high-flying gambler who lived in a quiet retirement community and played golf. Officials said he had no significant criminal history and drew little attention to himself. Investigators are trying to piece together his financial history to search for clues that could help determine what set him off. Image Stephen Paddock in a photo provided by his brother Eric Paddock. Credit via Associated Press Mr. Paddock often bet heavily at the major casino hotels, and his girlfriend, Ms. Danley, 62, who was out of the country at the time of the shooting, had worked for some of those hotels. They lived in Mesquite, Nev., 80 miles northeast of Las Vegas, and he also owned a home in Reno. Ms. Danley boarded a flight from Manila to Los Angeles on Tuesday, according to Antonette Mangrobang, a spokeswoman for the Philippine Immigration Bureau. The authorities, who met Ms. Danley at the Los Angeles airport, have called her a “person of interest” in the shooting investigation, which does not necessarily mean that she is suspected of committing a crime. ‘I’ve never been that scared in my life,’ said one witness. The police estimated that when the shooting began, there were 22,000 people at the Harvest Festival, listening to Jason Aldean, the final act of the three-day event. Video of the shooting captured nine seconds of continuous, rapid fire, followed by 37 seconds of silence from the weapon and panicked screaming from the crowd. Gunfire then erupted again and again in extended bursts. Some concertgoers thought the noise was fireworks, but as it became clear what was happening, people fled, many of them unsure where the shots were coming from, or where they should go. “Everyone was running, you could see people getting shot,” said Gail Davis, one of the witnesses. “I’ve never been that scared in my life,” she added. “To have this happen, I can’t wrap my mind around it.” Video from the shooting showed Mr. Aldean running off the stage as the gunfire erupted.

## Bump Stocks: From Novelty to National Debate

Following the deadliest mass shooting in U.S. history, the spotlight has intensified on "bump stocks," controversial devices that can dramatically increase a semi-automatic rifle's rate of fire, mimicking automatic weapon capabilities. These largely unregulated accessories, once a niche item popular among YouTube gun enthusiasts seeking to simulate machine gun fire, have now become a focal point for gun control advocates demanding a ban.

### The Mechanics of Rapid Fire

Bump stocks attach to semi-automatic firearms and leverage the weapon's recoil. As the rifle recoils, the trigger mechanism is propelled forward, bumping against the shooter's finger. This allows for a continuous firing sequence without the shooter needing to repeatedly pull the trigger, significantly accelerating the rate of fire. One company, Bump Fire Systems, advertised these devices on its website, highlighting the ability to achieve "simulated full-auto firing" legally for $99.99.

### A "Toy" for Enthusiasts, Not a Serious Tool

Gun experts have historically characterized bump stocks as a "toy" or something primarily of interest to "gun geeks," rather than a tool for serious shooters prioritizing accuracy. Prior to the Las Vegas attack, these devices were frequently featured in online videos showcasing gun stunts and enthusiast modifications.

### Calls for a Ban and Legal Loopholes

The potential use of bump stocks by the Las Vegas shooter has galvanized calls for their prohibition. Senator Dianne Feinstein, a long-time advocate for stricter gun laws, stated that banning bump stocks should be a top priority in the wake of the tragedy. Shannon Watts, founder of Moms Demand Action, emphasized that hardware capable of transforming legal firearms into automatic weapons should be prohibited, arguing that such devices circumvent federal law that heavily regulates automatic weapons.

David Chipman, a former ATF agent and now a policy advisor for Americans for Responsible Solutions, explained that bump stocks represent a "masterful creation of a technical workaround creating a loophole to circumvent the intention of the law." He noted that the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives (ATF) has repeatedly confirmed that bump stocks and similar devices are not strictly regulated under existing federal firearms legislation.

### Personal Experiences and Concerns

One owner of a bump stock, who requested anonymity, purchased a device for approximately $100 two years prior to the shooting. He explained that it was the closest he could get to simulating a machine gun, particularly in his home state of Delaware, which bans machine gun ownership. He noted that purchasing the bump stock did not require a background check, likening the process to ordering ammunition online.

While he found the device to be a fun "range toy," he also acknowledged its difficulty to master and its inaccuracy, stating, "This thing requires practice." He suggested that many gun stores might avoid selling bump stocks to prevent attracting "irresponsible gun owners" who might be influenced by online videos without considering the consequences. Similarly, many shooting ranges prohibit their use for safety and liability reasons.

Ultimately, the novelty of the bump stock wore off for him, and he found it unreliable for self-defense. He expressed concern that the actions of one individual might lead to a ban on the devices, which he believes would be regrettable and contrary to his principles of freedom.

query: What evidence does the article provide to refute the Daily Mail's claim of a mini-ice age due to decreased solar activity?

documents.text: The Daily Mail in Britain tends to be terrific for news of the weird — who can forget its exposé on the girl who ate nothing but chicken McNuggets for 15 years? — and terrible for science reporting. Case in point: Their latest story on how the world is about to enter a mini-ice age. The piece cites recent research from the UK Met Office on how solar activity is set to decrease in the coming years — which, in turn, will cool the planet. That’s technically true, but the decrease in solar activity won’t be enough to counteract the warming effects of all the greenhouse-gas pollution we’re putting into the air. How do we know? Just ask the UK Met Office. In a post refuting the Daily Mail, the climate research center notes that greenhouse gases are on pace to warm the planet 2.5°C over the next 90 years. At most, the decrease in solar activity is expected to cool the planet by about 0.13°C. The math here is simple: 2.5°C minus 0.13°C equals 2.37°C. That’s not an ice age. That’s the Earth getting hotter. As a bonus, the Daily Mail also repeats the canard that global warming has somehow “stopped” since 1998. The trick here is to cherry-pick numbers and start from the exceptionally hot El Niño year. Taking a longer view, the world is clearly warming. Here’s a nice animated chart from Skeptical Science demonstrating that it’s possible to pick plenty of periods in the past 100 years to imply that the world is cooling — even though the overall trend is clearly upward: Meanwhile, as the Met Office notes, the 2000s were clearly the hottest decade in the instrumental record, beating the previous record set in the 1990s, which in turn beat the previous record set in the 1980s. Despite what the Daily Mail says, the world is warming and the Thames isn’t about to freeze over. The paper does, however, raise excellent questions about the wisdom of eating nothing but Chicken McNuggets.

When considering **global warming**, public office candidates must understand that the widely promoted idea of **near-universal scientific consensus demanding drastic action is not entirely accurate**. A significant and growing group of respected scientists and engineers **hold differing views**, questioning the necessity of extreme measures.

Nobel Prize-winning physicist **Ivar Giaever** publicly resigned from the American Physical Society (APS) in September. His resignation letter stated his dissent: "I did not renew [my membership] because I cannot live with the [APS policy] statement: 'The evidence is incontrovertible: Global warming is occurring... We must reduce emissions of greenhouse gases beginning now.'" Giaever found it perplexing that the APS would allow discussions on topics like proton mass changes or the multi-universe, yet label the evidence for global warming as "**incontrovertible**."

Despite a decades-long global campaign promoting the idea that rising carbon dioxide (CO2) levels will devastate civilization, many prominent scientists echo Dr. Giaever's perspective. The number of scientific "**heretics**" is steadily increasing, supported by factual evidence. A crucial, often overlooked, fact is the **absence of significant global warming for over a decade**. Climate scientist Kevin Trenberth acknowledged this in a 2009 "Climategate" email, stating: "The fact is that we can't account for the lack of warming at the moment and it is a travesty that we can't."

This observed plateau in warming becomes evident when comparing **real-world data with computer models**. These models frequently amplify the minor warming effect of CO2 through feedback mechanisms involving water vapor and clouds. The lack of warming over the past decade, and the slower-than-predicted warming trend over the 22 years since the U.N.'s Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) began issuing projections, strongly suggest these models **significantly overestimate CO2's warming potential**. In response to this discrepancy, proponents of alarm have shifted their focus from warming itself to weather extremes, attributing any unusual climate event to CO2.

It is important to understand that **CO2 is not a pollutant**. It is a colorless, odorless gas that is **fundamental to life**, exhaled by humans and a vital component of the biosphere. Greenhouse operators routinely increase CO2 concentrations by three to four times to promote plant growth, as plants flourish with elevated CO2 levels. This is unsurprising, considering that plant and animal life evolved when CO2 concentrations were approximately ten times higher than they are today. While advancements in plant varieties, chemical fertilizers, and agricultural management have undeniably boosted crop yields, **increased atmospheric CO2 has also likely played a contributing role** in this growth.

Although the number of scientists publicly voicing dissent is growing, many younger scientists privately harbor serious doubts about the prevailing global warming narrative. However, they often **fear professional repercussions**, such as delayed promotions or more severe consequences. These anxieties are not unfounded. In 2003, Dr. Chris de Freitas, then editor of the journal Climate Research, published a peer-reviewed article concluding that recent warming was not unusual within the context of the past thousand years. The global warming establishment initiated a campaign to have him removed from his editorial position and dismissed from his university. Fortunately, Dr. de Freitas managed to retain his university employment. This suppression of scientific inquiry bears a resemblance to historical instances, such as Trofim Lysenko's manipulation of biology in the Soviet Union, where scientists who adhered to genetics, a field Lysenko dismissed as bourgeois fiction, faced persecution.

The intense fervor surrounding global warming and the reluctance of organizations like the American Physical Society to remove the word "incontrovertible" from their climate statements can be attributed to several factors, most notably **financial incentives**. Alarmism regarding climate change benefits numerous entities by facilitating government funding for academic research and expanding governmental bureaucracies. It also provides governments with a rationale for tax increases, creates avenues for taxpayer-funded subsidies for politically connected businesses, and attracts substantial donations to foundations dedicated to environmental causes.

Speaking on behalf of many scientists and engineers who have independently scrutinized climate science, we advise any candidate for public office that there is **no compelling scientific basis for drastic measures aimed at "decarbonizing" the global economy**. Even when accepting inflated climate forecasts from the IPCC, aggressive greenhouse gas control policies are **not economically justifiable**.

A recent study conducted by Yale economist William Nordhaus, examining various policy options, indicated that allowing 50 more years of economic growth without greenhouse gas controls offers the **highest benefit-to-cost ratio**. This approach would particularly benefit developing nations striving to achieve the material well-being, health, and life expectancy enjoyed by developed countries. Many other proposed policies, conversely, would yield negative returns. Furthermore, **increased CO2 and the modest warming it might induce are likely to benefit the planet overall**.

If elected officials feel compelled to take action on climate, we recommend supporting scientists who are dedicated to **enhancing our understanding of climate through well-designed instruments and rigorous data analysis**. A more profound understanding of climate will better equip us to adapt to its perpetually changing nature, which has historically presented significant challenges to human civilization. However, a substantial portion of the private and government investment in climate research warrants **critical review**. Candidates should champion sensible environmental protection measures, but investing in costly programs predicated on alarming, unsubstantiated claims of "incontrovertible" evidence is **ill-advised**.

*Claude Allegre, former director of the Institute for the Study of the Earth, University of Paris; J. Scott Armstrong, cofounder of the Journal of Forecasting and the International Journal of Forecasting; Jan Breslow, head of the Laboratory of Biochemical Genetics and Metabolism, Rockefeller University; Roger Cohen, fellow, American Physical Society; Edward David, member, National Academy of Engineering and National Academy of Sciences; William Happer, professor of physics, Princeton; Michael Kelly, professor of technology, University of Cambridge, U.K.; William Kininmonth, former head of climate research at the Australian Bureau of Meteorology; Richard Lindzen, professor of atmospheric sciences, MIT; James McGrath, professor of chemistry, Virginia Technical University; Rodney Nichols, former president and CEO of the New York Academy of Sciences; Burt Rutan, aerospace engineer, designer of Voyager and SpaceShipOne; Harrison H. Schmitt, Apollo 17 astronaut and former U.S. senator; Nir Shaviv, professor of astrophysics, Hebrew University, Jerusalem; Henk Tennekes, former director, Royal Dutch Meteorological Service; Antonio Zichichi, president of the World Federation of Scientists, Geneva.*

A recent Mail on Sunday article, "Forget global warming – it’s Cycle 25 we need to worry about," by David Rose, presents a misleading view of scientific findings from the Met Office Hadley Centre. Rose claims recent global temperature data shows no warming for 15 years, a statement contradicted by the Met Office's own research. Despite the Met Office providing detailed explanations about decadal projections and warming trends since 1997, these clarifications were not fully included in the published article.

A Met Office representative explained, "Our ten-year projection is still cutting-edge. The full period for the initial projection hasn't ended, and these forecasts are updated with the latest data. However, it's clear that warming continues. The 2000s were the hottest decade on record since 1850. Depending on the data used, 2010 was the warmest year for NOAA NCDC and NASA GISS, and the second warmest for HadCRUT3."

The article also critiques a Met Office study without seeking comment on potential misunderstandings. This research, supported by many other studies, examines how climate reacts to changes in solar activity. It found that while solar output is expected to drop over the next 90 years, this decrease is unlikely to significantly slow the global warming caused by greenhouse gases. The study estimated that reduced solar activity would likely lower global temperatures by only 0.08°C. This is a small amount compared to the projected warming of about 2.5°C over the same period, driven by greenhouse gas emissions under the IPCC's B2 scenario, which assumes no action to reduce emissions. The research also suggested that even if solar output fell below levels seen during the Maunder Minimum (1645-1715), a time of very low solar activity, global temperatures would only drop by 0.13°C.

« Will Coral Reefs be Gone by the End of the Century? | Main | Global Warming Is Ruining The Minnesota Winter » Category: Climate • Climate Change • Global Warming • LIAR!!!! Posted on: January 29, 2012 11:21 AM, by Greg Laden Since the Daily Mail is a British thing and the latest form of entertainment in Britain is Libel Tourism, I won't say to you that the Daily Mail is a rag full of lies and deceit. Instead, I'll let you be the judge. These studies: Decline in solar output unlikely to offset global warming 23 January 2012 - New research has found that solar output is likely to reduce over the next 90 years but that will not substantially delay expected increases in global temperatures caused by greenhouse gases. Carried out by the Met Office and the University of Reading, the study establishes the most likely changes in the Sun's activity and looks at how this could affect near-surface temperatures on Earth. It found that the most likely outcome was that the Sun's output would decrease up to 2100, but this would only cause a reduction in global temperatures of 0.08 °C. This compares to an expected warming of about 2.5 °C over the same period due to greenhouse gases (according to the IPCC's B2 scenario for greenhouse gas emissions that does not involve efforts to mitigate emissions). Gareth Jones, a climate change detection scientist with the Met Office, said: "This research shows that the most likely change in the Sun's output will not have a big impact on global temperatures or do much to slow the warming we expect from greenhouse gases.
