The Internet Archive's "No More 404" initiative is a crucial effort to **preserve the web's history** by archiving pages as they are created and the content they link to. This proactive approach ensures that even if linked pages are later changed or removed from the live web, a **permanent record of the original content** remains accessible.

The core objective is to **combat broken links**, a pervasive issue that degrades the user experience and fragments online information. By archiving referenced pages, the Internet Archive aims to provide a **fallback for broken links**, allowing users to see the content that was originally intended by a page's author. This creates a more **robust and reliable web**, where historical context is maintained and the integrity of online information is safeguarded. The initiative specifically targets supported "No More 404" sites for these comprehensive crawls.

Astronaut Scott Kelly is set to conclude his extended mission aboard the International Space Station (ISS) on March 1, 2016, marking a total of 520 days in orbit. Throughout his tenure, Kelly has generously shared a remarkable collection of photographic documentation of his experiences via Twitter.

Kelly commenced his year-long sojourn on the ISS approximately one year prior to his scheduled return, which is slated for late Tuesday night. On Saturday morning, he presented what he characterized as one of his most breathtaking celestial vistas. He observed that the ISS's orbital velocity around Earth is so pronounced that a sunrise or sunset transpires every 92 minutes. In September, Kelly tweeted, "Of all the sunrises I've seen on my #YearInSpace, this was one of the best! One of the last too. Headed home soon."

Kelly has meticulously chronicled his year in space, frequently posting multiple updates daily on social media platforms. Among his most striking visual records are images captured two months into his mission, depicting an approaching storm system over Texas. Shortly thereafter, he immortalized the 25th anniversary of the Hubble Space Telescope with a stunning photograph of the Milky Way.

The aurora borealis was a notable spectacle, rendering Chicago particularly captivating. From orbit, the Sahara Desert exhibited an astonishing palette of colors, which Kelly has likened to watercolors in his posts. On November 22, 2015, he tweeted, "Pictures like this make me really regret that my watercolors didn't make it up here. #EarthArt #YearInSpace."

The French Riviera was rendered with its characteristic allure in one of his photographs. Another image showcased a frozen lake in the Himalayas, captured mere weeks before his departure. Central America presented a particularly striking tableau under the early morning illumination.

Through his "Colors of Earth" series, Kelly consistently highlights the planet's dynamic chromatic splendor. The sands of Africa, with their intricate patterns and vivid hues, are especially evocative. On February 22, 2016, he shared, "Will be missing these sands too, but looking forward to sandy beaches up close. #EarthArt #Africa #YearInSpace."

### A Landmark Year in Space Exploration

The past twelve months have been a period of extraordinary achievement in space exploration. While no missions ventured to the Moon, humanity made significant strides, including a close encounter with Pluto, successful cultivation of crops in extraterrestrial environments, and the initiation of recruitment for the first crewed mission to Mars. During this time, astronaut Scott Kelly has been continuously orbiting aboard the International Space Station (ISS), providing remarkable imagery and setting a new American record for cumulative time spent in space.

### The "Year in Space" Mission Concludes

All journeys, however, must eventually end. In anticipation of his return to Earth on March 1st, Kelly handed over command of the ISS to NASA astronaut Tim Kopra. For those who have closely followed the nearly year-long mission of Kelly and cosmonaut Mikhail Kornienko, this recent transfer of authority ceremony is deeply moving. Even for those unfamiliar with the record-breaking "Year in Space" mission, the accomplishment of spending 340 consecutive days aboard a spacecraft roughly the size of a football field is undeniably impressive. Furthermore, Kelly consumed an estimated 730 liters of recycled bodily fluids, a testament to the rigorous scientific protocols designed to understand the physiological effects of extended space habitation.

### Reflections on Teamwork and Future Endeavors

During the ceremony, Kelly expressed his gratitude to Kornienko, affectionately known as "Misha," acknowledging that farewells are always "bittersweet when you're leaving this incredible place." Kornienko is scheduled to return to Earth on March 1st alongside Kelly and cosmonaut Sergey Volkov, who has been a crewmate for the past six months. Kelly remarked, "Obviously, Misha and I have been up here for a really, really long time and recently we've been joking with each other that we'll say something like 'We did it!' or 'We made it!'" He further elaborated, "But we both recognize that this is a lot more about teamwork and all the people that it takes to put these missions together and be successful than it is about us." He emphasized the collaborative nature of spaceflight, stating, "Spaceflight is the biggest team sport there is, and it's incredibly important that we all work together to make what is seemingly impossible possible."

### Scientific Impact and the Road Ahead

Upon Kelly's return to Earth, NASA scientists will begin a comprehensive assessment of the physiological impact of his extended mission. This evaluation will involve a detailed comparison with his identical twin brother, Mark Kelly, who remained on Earth throughout the duration of the "Year in Space" experiment. The insights gained from studying the effects of microgravity and prolonged space travel on human physiology are crucial for developing strategies to ensure astronaut health during future interplanetary voyages, including the ambitious endeavor to reach Mars and potentially explore further reaches of the Solar System. As NASA Mission Control conveyed to Kelly and Kornienko at the conclusion of the handover: "What you have provided for our human spaceflight endeavors has been monumental... We look forward to seeing you back safe on the ground and back in Houston." This marks a poignant sign-off from a true pioneer.

**NASA's groundbreaking astronaut twin experiment, designed to unlock the secrets of long-duration spaceflight, is nearing a critical milestone.** One year ago, NASA launched an ambitious study involving identical twins Scott and Mark Kelly. Scott embarked on a 340-day mission aboard the International Space Station (ISS), while his twin brother, retired astronaut Mark Kelly, remained on Earth. As Scott prepares to return on March 1st, the scientific community eagerly anticipates the insights gained from this unique endeavor.

The core of the experiment lies in **comparing the physiological changes** Scott experienced in space against Mark's baseline health on Earth. While Scott was conducting vital astronaut duties, he also meticulously collected biological samples – including blood, urine, saliva, and stool – for scientific analysis. Simultaneously, Mark provided comparable samples on Earth. This **dual-pronged approach** allows scientists to pinpoint the specific effects of space on the human body.

The duration of Scott's mission, a full year, is **twice the typical ISS stay** and mirrors the time astronauts would need for a round trip to Mars. This extended exposure to the space environment, characterized by **low gravity and high radiation**, presents significant challenges.

**Key physiological effects observed include:**

*   **Musculoskeletal Deterioration:** In the absence of gravity, bones and muscles weaken. Bones thin, and muscles atrophy. To combat this, ISS astronauts dedicate approximately two hours daily to rigorous exercise, utilizing treadmills with resistance bands and weight training. Vitamin D supplements also play a role. Despite these measures, astronauts can lose an average of **1.5% of their bone mass per month** in space.
*   **Fluid Shift and Cardiovascular Changes:** Microgravity significantly impacts the distribution of bodily fluids. Without gravity pulling them downwards, fluids shift towards the head, leading to a phenomenon known as the "Charlie Brown effect," where astronauts develop rounder faces. This fluid shift also causes the heart to shrink, as it expends less effort pumping blood. Furthermore, increased pressure in the head can affect vision and the sense of smell. NASA employs devices like the CHABIS (a specialized garment) to help redistribute fluids back to the legs.
*   **Cosmic Radiation Exposure:** Beyond the protective shield of Earth's atmosphere and magnetic field, astronauts face increased exposure to **dangerous cosmic radiation**. The ISS orbits at a relatively safe altitude, but missions to Mars would expose astronauts to significantly higher radiation doses. Understanding the precise dangers of this radiation is a critical objective for future interplanetary travel.

While Scott's return marks the end of the data collection phase for the twin study, the **in-depth analysis of the collected data is just beginning**, promising to revolutionize our understanding of human resilience in the cosmos.

As astronaut Scott Kelly nears the end of his historic 340-day mission on the International Space Station (ISS), NASA is preparing for post-flight tests designed to simulate the harsh conditions of Mars exploration. Upon his return to Earth, Kelly will undergo rigorous evaluations, including the ability to stand unassisted for three minutes and navigate a special obstacle course. These assessments are crucial for measuring his immediate physical resilience and understanding how the body adapts to long periods in microgravity, especially for future Mars missions where immediate help from Earth will be unavailable.

Kelly's current mission, which began in March, is a key step toward sending humans to Mars. During his final orbital press conference, Kelly highlighted the mission's importance, stating, "I believe we will glean substantial insights into long-duration spaceflight and its implications for future Mars endeavors." He added, "This mission represents a significant stepping stone toward achieving a human landing on Mars." His 340-day stay, exceeding NASA's previous record by 125 days, will conclude on the Kazakh steppes with his descent via a Soyuz spacecraft, alongside cosmonaut Mikhail Kornienko.

Immediately after exiting the capsule, both astronauts will participate in extensive field tests, overseen by lead flight surgeon Dr. Stevan Gilmore. These initial tests aim to answer critical questions about immediate post-landing capabilities on Mars. Specifically, they will assess the astronauts' ability to stand up from a lying position, handle essential equipment, and potentially conduct critical spacewalks (EVAs). Comprehensive medical exams will continue for weeks, including blood tests, continuous heart monitoring, and other vital physiological checks. These evaluations will occur at the landing site and later at their home facilities in Houston and Star City, Russia, ensuring a thorough understanding of their recovery and adaptation.

A particularly unique and scientifically valuable aspect of this mission is the participation of Scott Kelly's identical twin, retired astronaut Mark Kelly. The brothers' involvement provides NASA with an exceptional opportunity for comparative physiological research. Scott, in space, offers direct data on the effects of prolonged spaceflight, while Mark, on Earth, acts as a control, allowing researchers to isolate changes caused solely by the space environment. This dual-subject approach promises to yield rich data on genetic and physiological responses to extended space durations, offering insights not possible with a single subject. While Russian cosmonauts have logged longer cumulative time in space, notably a 438-day flight on the Mir space station, those missions occurred when medical monitoring and data collection were far less advanced than current ISS capabilities.

As of Day 335, Kelly reported feeling strong, a testament to his training and the ISS environment. Dr. Gilmore expects no major issues upon landing, though he acknowledges the anticipated physiological changes from such a long mission. Kelly's vision has slightly worsened, a common effect of increased pressure in the brain due to weightlessness. Reduced bone and muscle mass is also expected, despite rigorous daily exercise. A primary concern for both Kelly and NASA, however, is the potential long-term impact of cumulative space radiation exposure, a significant challenge for Mars missions due to higher radiation levels beyond Earth's protective magnetic field.

Beyond radiation, physiologist John Charles of the Johnson Space Center points to other major challenges for Mars expeditions. These include the psychological strain of long spaceflights, difficulties in providing effective in-flight medical care, and the need for advanced food preservation. Planned Mars missions in the 2030s, expected to last about 2.5 years with crews of four to six, will require unprecedented self-sufficiency. This is demonstrated by the experimental greenhouse on the ISS, where Kelly and his crew successfully grew romaine lettuce and zinnias, proving the feasibility of growing food on-site for long missions. Kelly's successful cultivation of zinnias, a task he humorously compared to "channeling my inner Mark Watney," underscores the need for astronaut independence and adaptive problem-solving.

Charles further distinguishes Mars travel from routine ISS stays, emphasizing the lack of real-time communication with loved ones and reduced direct oversight from Mission Control. Support will be primarily asynchronous, sent via email, demanding high astronaut autonomy. "They will be highly autonomous," Charles stated, "and that is something we are actively working to cultivate on the space station now, by gradually reducing Mission Control's direct involvement in astronaut operations." This gradual shift is vital for preparing crews for the isolation of a Mars mission.

Kelly also offers a practical view on the cramped living quarters expected on Mars-bound spacecraft, noting they will be much smaller than the ISS. He estimates spending nearly half his time in his personal cubicle, about the size of a phone booth. This highlights the urgent need for better privacy solutions to reduce stress and fatigue on future long missions. The profound physical distance from Earth, 250 miles during his ISS mission, will be vastly amplified on a journey to Mars, where Earth will eventually be invisible to the naked eye.

Despite these significant challenges, Kelly remains optimistic. "Obviously going to Mars, there are a lot of other challenges, but none of these we can't overcome," he stated. NASA is exploring additional yearlong missions, often with international partners, to gather more data on the complex issues of extended spaceflight. As Kelly recently reflected, "a year now seems longer than I thought it would be," a sentiment that captures the profound human experience of prolonged extraterrestrial living and the unique perspective it provides.

This project actively safeguards the federal government's digital legacy. Building on the foundational 2016 End of Term initiative, it focuses on the systematic archiving of government websites and associated data. This continuous preservation process involves regular web crawling of Uniform Resource Locators (URLs). These URLs are sourced from collaborating institutions and public nominations. This systematic crawling offers an ongoing perspective on the web and social media engagement of federal agencies.

Key collaborators in this endeavor include the Environmental Data & Governance Initiative and the Data Refuge project. This curated collection represents a direct extension of the 2016 End of Term web archiving efforts. Consequently, it undergoes deduplication against the prior collection. This process ensures that publicly nominated websites are preserved beyond the initial "official" conclusion of the End of Term project.

Public engagement is actively solicited. Specifically, government information specialists and interested parties can propose selected websites for inclusion. A dedicated public nomination tool facilitates this process. For comprehensive information regarding partner institutions, the web crawling methodology, and prior End of Term projects, please consult the End of Term Archive.

U.S. astronaut Scott Kelly has retired from NASA, after 20 years of service and more than 520 days in space—more time in space than any other American. He also departs with a record as a social media maven and renowned photographer from space. Kelly made four trips to space, the most prominent being a 340-day journey that ended last month. During that flight, Kelly made more than 5,000 trips around the Earth, and drank nearly 200 gallons of drinking water made from recycled sweat and urine. From his perch 400 kilometers (249 miles) above the earth’s surface, he snapped hundreds of beautifully abstract photographs of our planet’s landforms and waterways. He spotted hurricanes ominously swirling on sapphire-blue oceans. He gazed out at the aurora’s glittering fog. He consistently turned Earth’s lands and waters into an abstract artist’s dream, with shots of beaches, deserts, forests, and everything in between. And he did it all with equipment accessible to earthlings, taking most of his images with a Nikon D4 digital camera with lenses more commonly used to photograph football games. Kelly’s photographs gained him a massive following. From Twitter to Tumblr, hundreds of thousands of social media users expressed their wonderment at the photos—and a devoted band of mapping experts tagged along, helpfully noting where Kelly snapped his pics. “It’s fun to be a part of the public’s reaction,” says David MacLean of Nova Scotia Community College’s Centre of Geographic Sciences, who’s among the most influential of Kelly’s hobbyist geotaggers. MacLean has been mapping astronauts’ photos since 2013, but says Kelly’s penchant for abstraction sets his pictures apart. “Other astronauts have more of a city and regional view: ‘Here’s Indianapolis from space,’” MacLean says. Kelly, on the other hand, “has concentrated on details of little pieces of the world.” “He’s caught the interest of quite a lot of people, [who] see these [places] in a whole new way.” And Kelly’s eye for composition hasn’t gone unnoticed at National Geographic. “Kelly’s photographs are striking and incredibly artistic," wrote Patrick Witty, National Geographic’s deputy director of photography. “[It’s] hard to believe they were made by an astronaut and not a working professional photographer.” “I’m sure every photographer on Earth is incredibly jealous of his angle.” To celebrate Kelly’s #YearInSpace, we’ve selected our favorite Scott Kelly space pictures. We hope you enjoy the photos—and check out National Geographic's photographic coverage of Kelly’s final return to Earth.

query: What is the primary goal of the United Noshes dinner project started by Jesse Friedman and Laura Hadden?

documents.text:
United Noshes: A Culinary Journey Through Global Flavors, One Dinner Party at a Time

Imagine a dinner party where the menu isn't just a selection of dishes, but a passport to the world. That's precisely the ambitious and delicious concept behind United Noshes, a unique project dedicated to exploring the vast tapestry of global cuisine. The brainchild of Jesse Friedman and his wife, Laura Hadden, United Noshes embarked on a remarkable three-year culinary adventure with a singular goal: to recreate and share meals from every single one of the United Nations' 193 member states, presented in alphabetical order. This endeavor is far more than just a series of dinner parties; it's a profound exploration of culture, connection, and the universal language of food.

The genesis of United Noshes was rooted in a desire to delve into the rich culinary landscape of New York City, a melting pot of global flavors. As Friedman and Hadden began their alphabetical journey, starting with countries like Algeria and Djibouti, and moving on to Guyana, their dinner parties evolved organically. What began as intimate gatherings of a few close friends around a living room table soon blossomed into larger events, accommodating dozens of guests in more formal banquet hall settings. The ingredients themselves became a testament to the diversity of their undertaking, ranging from the exotic cashew juice to the familiar yet refined French charcuterie, and even the complex, earthy notes of fermented corn flour.

Sourcing ingredients for such a diverse range of cuisines presented its own set of challenges, particularly for those flavors that hadn't yet gained widespread recognition or availability in America. Friedman recounts a humorous anecdote from an African market, where his inquiry about a specific ingredient was met with a knowing smile and the question, "So where did you do Peace Corps?" This question, he explains, highlights the often-insular nature of specialty food markets, where patrons are typically those with direct cultural ties or extensive international experience. Interestingly, Friedman discovered that Peace Corps cookbooks often serve as an invaluable resource, offering authentic recipes, especially for cuisines from smaller, less-represented nations.

Beyond the culinary discoveries, a significant aspect of United Noshes has been the joy of cooking for and connecting with people. In the bustling environment of New York City, where time can be a precious commodity, the act of sharing a home-cooked meal holds particular significance. The initial dinners were a warm affair, shared with friends and family. As articles about their project began to surface and their blog readership and mailing list expanded, the guest list naturally grew to include curious strangers, some of whom were even expatriates from the very countries whose cuisine was being featured. This organic growth, Friedman explains, led to a natural and meaningful evolution of the project: transforming it into a charitable endeavor.

"We felt we had to acknowledge the fact that many people couldn't even enjoy the sorts of foods we were celebrating from their own country," Friedman states, articulating the heartfelt motivation behind their fundraising efforts. This realization underscored the privilege inherent in their culinary exploration and fueled their desire to give back. Diners are invited to contribute a small donation, and over the years, United Noshes has successfully raised over $20,000. Initially, these funds supported the U.N.'s World Food Programme, and more recently, the project has directed its contributions to Mercy Corps, a respected international relief and development organization based in Friedman's current home of Portland, Oregon.

The project has offered a fascinating education, not only in the culinary legacies of colonialism and the myriad ways rice can be prepared across cultures but also in uncovering surprising food traditions in unexpected places. The African islands of Comoros, for instance, proved to be a revelation. Hadden, who admitted to experiencing a touch of fatigue from a succession of meals featuring similar staples like fufu and collard greens, particularly from Central African countries that began with the letter "C," found herself delighted by the unique flavors of Comorian cuisine. "There's a lot of central African countries that start with the letter c," she laughs. "And so we were like OK, another one of these meals. And then [we] discovered this really great cuisine." Comoros, with its rich tapestry of Portuguese, French, and Arabic influences, boasts a culinary heritage distinct from its continental neighbors. The standout dish from Comoros, a delectable lobster in vanilla crème fraîche sauce, quickly became an all-time favorite among guests.

Of course, not every culinary experiment has been a resounding success. The project has also encountered its share of less-than-ideal dishes, including the challenging combination of yak butter and chiles from Bhutan, the acquired taste of sheep's head from Iceland, and a memorable "red palm oil fry disaster" from Cameroon. These occasional missteps, however, are an integral part of the learning process and add to the authenticity of their global gastronomic quest.

Ultimately, the impact of United Noshes extends far beyond the monetary contributions it generates. Friedman and Hadden emphasize that the true value lies in the profound connections forged through the shared experience of a meal. These connections ripple outwards, extending from the dinner table to the wider world. Hadden shares a poignant anecdote about a cab driver from Burkina Faso. When she mentioned having tried food from his home country, he initially expressed disbelief. However, upon her describing a specific type of small doughnut, his skepticism turned to astonishment. "Oh, my god! I used to sell them in the market with my mom when I was a kid!" he exclaimed, a powerful testament to the personal and cultural resonance of food.

As United Noshes approaches its halfway point with an upcoming meal from Libya, Friedman and Hadden anticipate concluding their ambitious project in approximately four more years. Their journey is a vibrant reminder that food is not merely sustenance; it is a powerful vehicle for understanding, empathy, and building bridges across the diverse landscapes of our world.

We're hosting a series of 194 dinner parties for the members of the United Nations, in alphabetical order. We invite guests to our home in Portland to share what they know about that country or to learn through the food. The dinner parties are also a fundraiser supporting Mercy Corps.

In times of **crisis**, humanitarian organizations work together using a system called **clusters**. These are specialized groups, organized by sector, and managed by one or more agencies. The **World Food Programme (WFP)** plays a key role, leading the **Logistics** and **Emergency Telecommunications** clusters. WFP also co-leads the **Food Security** cluster. Furthermore, WFP manages the **United Nations Humanitarian Air Service** and the **United Nations Humanitarian Response Depots**, making these vital resources available to all humanitarian efforts. Discover more about how humanitarian aid is delivered.

United Noshes is a culinary project dedicated to exploring global cuisine by hosting 194 dinner parties, one for each member of the United Nations, in alphabetical order. This ambitious undertaking began in July 2011 in Brooklyn, initially in a modest 500-square-foot apartment, and later relocated to Portland, Oregon, in August 2014. The core mission is to recreate authentic meals, meticulously researching recipes and employing traditional preparation methods to the best of their ability and resources.

The project thrives on community involvement. They actively seek out individuals with connections to the featured countries—whether through birth, residency, family ties, or deep familiarity—to guide their culinary choices and preparation. The experience is further enriched when these individuals join the dinner parties, sharing their insights at the table and in the kitchen. The founders express gratitude for the opportunity to find joy in food and share it with friends through United Noshes.

Recognizing global food insecurity, United Noshes incorporates a charitable element. Guests are invited to bring a donation to support Mercy Corps, an international development and relief non-profit organization. These contributions are then matched, amplifying their impact.

**Key Facts & Figures:**

*   **Total Meals:** 194 dinners, covering all 193 UN member states plus the two permanently observing non-member states (Holy See and Honduras).
*   **Timeline:** The project is projected to conclude around 2019, spanning eight years. They reached the halfway point with Meal 97 (Libya) after nearly four years.
*   **Fundraising:** As of the end of 2014, over $22,800 was raised, including a dollar-for-dollar match from Google. Through their first 88 meals, they raised over $21,300 for World Food Program USA. Post-Portland move, fundraising benefits Mercy Corps.
*   **Pace:** Meals are typically held on an every-other-week schedule.
*   **Locations:** Primarily hosted at their home in Portland, Oregon, with occasional events in other locations like the Bay Area, New York, Seattle, and Washington D.C.

**Culinary Approach:**

The selection of dishes is guided by several strategies:
*   **Holiday Focus:** Utilizing significant national holidays and their associated foods, such as Day of the Dead in Haiti.
*   **Regional Diversity:** For countries with extensive culinary variations (e.g., China), efforts are made to present a cohesive and representative selection of regional dishes.
*   **Occasion Feasts:** Aiming to create a celebratory meal that highlights the distinctiveness of a country's cuisine, particularly for nations where culinary distinctions can be subtle (e.g., parts of Central Africa and the Caribbean).

The research process involves:
1.  **Cuisine Summaries:** Starting with an overview, often from Wikipedia.
2.  **Dish Identification:** Searching for distinctive and representative dishes, cross-referencing multiple recipes and descriptions to identify consistent ingredients and techniques.
3.  **Authenticity:** Prioritizing recipes that avoid Westernized adaptations and maintain traditional methods.
4.  **Expert Collaboration:** Actively working with individuals familiar with the cuisine to refine menus.

**Ingredient Sourcing:**

In New York, diverse immigrant communities provided access to specialized markets for ingredients like bitter gourds, Balkan smoked meats, frozen cassava greens, and breadfruit. While Portland offers a broader range of ingredients, sourcing can still require improvisation, especially for fish, where substitutions are sometimes necessary. When specific sauces or spice mixes are unavailable, they are often made from scratch if a recipe can be found.

**Origins of the Project:**

The idea stemmed from a desire to move beyond typical bar and restaurant meetups, reconnecting with the intimacy of home-based gatherings. Jesse missed cooking for larger groups, and they sought a way to explore the rich diversity of nations represented in New York City while acknowledging their good fortune. This spirit of global exploration through shared meals has continued in Portland, revealing the diversity present even in a city often perceived as more homogeneous.

**Future Plans:**

The United States will be the final meal, planned as a large potluck inviting a representative from each of the 50 states. While not planning a traditional cookbook due to the complexity of representing entire national cuisines from limited experience, Jesse is documenting his learning journey, exploring topics like the global variations of the word "turkey" and the diverse ways rice is prepared worldwide.

**Team:**

*   **Jesse Friedman:** Chef, researcher, shopper, and blogger. Hailing from Oakland, CA, he learned to cook from his father and bake from his mother, who ran a microwave oven store. He studied linguistics at the University of Chicago, lived abroad in France and Switzerland, and began cooking for crowds at a folk festival. Professionally, he is a product marketing manager at Google, working with the Crisis Response team and organizing online advocacy campaigns. His favorite cuisine is Eastern Mediterranean, and he has a particular fondness for fresh mozzarella.
*   **Laura Hadden:** Dishwasher, photographer, and webmaster. Originally from Tacoma, WA, by way of Vancouver, Canada, she is a documentary media artist and multimedia producer. Her work has been showcased at various film festivals and on broadcast channels. She has received awards for her short films and has been an artist-in-residence focusing on income inequality. For three years, she managed media and communications for The Moth, producing their podcast and assisting with their Peabody Award-winning radio show. She has facilitated storytelling workshops and is pursuing an MFA in Integrated Media Arts. She identifies as a reluctant foodie with a favorite cuisine of Thai.

**Press Mentions:**

*   Think Out Loud | Oregon Public Broadcasting | January 19, 2015
*   United Noshes: Dinner Party Aims To Eat Its Way Through Global Cuisine | NPR | January 17, 2015
*   The Food Show (approx. 43 minutes in) | KBOO | October 15, 2014
*   United Noshes: The Alphabet Foodies (article video) | Jewish Daily Forward | August 5, 2014
*   United Noshes: Tasting One Meal From Each United Nations Member State | Denizen | October 14, 2013
*   Solving World Hunger with Dinner Parties: Meet United Noshes | EcoSalon | January 26, 2013
*   United Noshes: 170 Dinner Parties from Afghanistan to Zimbabwe (sic) | America’s Test Kitchen Radio | October 19, 2012
*   Celebrate World Food Day With Meals From Every Country (VIDEO) | Buzz60 | October 11, 2012
*   United We Nosh | Scoutmob | October 10, 2012
*   United Noshes: Meals Around the World | Food 52 | July 13, 2012
*   From Afghanistan to Zimbabwe: Cooking through the United Nations | CNN Eatocracy | June 7, 2012
*   United Noshes Supper Club Has Served Meals From 34 U.N. Countries (and Counting) | Grub Street New York | May 15, 2012
*   Journey Through World’s Cuisine Teaches About Community | State Department IIP Digital | April 24, 2012
*   United Noshes in Washington D.C. | World Food Program USA Blog | April 24, 2012

Jesse Friedman and Laura Hadden, a married couple, are embarking on an ambitious culinary journey from their Brooklyn kitchen. Their project, "United Noshes," aims to explore the diverse world of cuisine by hosting dinner parties that feature the culinary traditions of all 193 United Nations member states, plus two permanent non-member observers. This endeavor, spanning three years and counting, involves meticulously researching recipes, sourcing authentic ingredients, and preparing elaborate feasts to represent each nation, progressing in a roughly alphabetical sequence.

Friedman primarily handles the cooking, while Hadden expertly documents each event and engages with the guests. They actively seek input from individuals knowledgeable about a country's culture and cuisine to enhance the authenticity of their meals. This project not only broadens their palates and culinary skills but also cultivates a vibrant community of individuals passionate about gastronomic exploration. Attendees include a mix of old friends, new acquaintances, and food enthusiasts who discover the project through their newsletter. The admission fee for these unique gatherings is a donation to the World Food Program, a vital organization dedicated to combating global hunger.

To date, approximately 500 individuals have subscribed to the "United Noshes" newsletter, and the couple has hosted events in various cities, including Boston, D.C., Seattle, and the San Francisco Bay Area. At their 83rd event, a dual celebration of Israeli and Palestinian culinary heritage, guests sampled dishes such as succulent lamb kebabs, freshly prepared hummus, and traditional rugelach. Friedman explained the project's genesis: "We hit upon the idea because it combined two things we’re interested in: exploring international dishes and meeting new people. We’ve met hundreds of people who are adventurous enough to try what we’re cooking. In New York, people tend not to visit each other in their homes. We wanted to open ours up."

Not every culinary experiment is a resounding success. Friedman candidly admits, "There are disasters of technique and some things that are just bad. Sometimes it’s like, ‘All right, we tried that, never again.’" He recounts a particularly challenging experience with headcheese for Iceland, which they humorously refer to as a "milkshake meal" due to its unpalatability.

The couple strives to recreate a quintessential home-cooked Sunday meal, akin to what a grandmother might prepare for a family gathering. Representing countries with diverse regional cuisines, like India, presents a significant challenge. Friedman and Hadden have learned to eschew Westernized interpretations of non-Western dishes and to employ spices with greater creativity. Their ingredient hunts have led them to explore the far reaches of New York City, from Harlem to Ozone Park, Astoria, and Brighton Beach. Friedman has also refined his cooking methods, stating, "I don’t fry things any more. It gets too messy. And we try not to do things to order, anything that keeps you stuck in the kitchen and unable to interact with guests."

This summer marks a new chapter as they relocate to Portland, Oregon, where they intend to continue "United Noshes." Hadden estimates the project will take another six to eight years to complete, but emphasizes their intrinsic motivation: "We’re not doing it for anyone else. We’ve always said that if it gets tedious than we can just stop. But it hasn’t gotten boring yet." This story, "United Noshes: The Alphabet Foodies," was originally written by Margaret Eby.

Week 1: Afghanistan Posted by Jesse Friedman on Monday, July 11, 2011 By virtue of the alphabet, first up in the culinary romp around the world is Afghanistan! I had it easy with the research for what to cook, since my good friend Oliver lived in Kabul for two years and is very into food. I took his advice on what to cook just about exactly, merely turning to the internet for the recipes. (Don’t think I know anyone with deep experience on Albania, so next week will require some more planning.) Afghan food turned out to be super good. It’s got a lot of spices, especially cardamom, which has a mysteriously floral aroma. But none of the dishes were at all spicy, at least none of the dishes I made. Dough | Yogurt-cucumber drink | Recipe “Say it like a Klingon and you’ll have the pronunciation,” says Oliver. And to our Westernized taste, a drink of salty, watered-down yogurt with crunchy bits of cucumber and flakes of mint may as well come from another planet. Then again, Afghans probably find it bizarre that we mix malt and yeast and let it spoil into something bitter. Anyway, everyone gave it the old college try, and nobody hated it, so that’s a good thing. (I actually like it and will probably have some of the remainder for breakfast.) Qabuli Pilau | Kabul-style lamb and rice | Recipe From all accounts, it’s Afghanistan’s national dish. A big mess of basmati rice cooked in the juices of the lamb it covers on the platter, with sauteed carrots and raisins (yes, sauteed raisins) mixed in. And of course, those spices. I wish I could have found a shoulder or shank or other sort of bone-in lamb, but Fairway let me down on this one. I think I overcooked the rice, but it was all pretty yummy. Borani Banjan | Eggplant and tomato casserole | Recipe Eggplant gets a bum rap from many people who find it rubbery and bitter, but you couldn’t accuse this dish of either. I grilled the eggplant slices first (traditionally you fry, but what the heck), and then baked it with tomato, cilantro, and a thin, garlicky, spiced tomato-y sauce. It was the first time I’d fired up the oven in this new place of ours, and I was terrified when it didn’t heat quickly, but thankfully when I cranked the dial it fired up and the dish turned out to be shockingly well balanced and flavorful. (Note to self: buy an oven thermometer, this was probably sheer luck.) Nan | Flatbread | Recipe Quoth Oliver, “When Afghan naan is fresh it’s reminiscent of a mediocre but tasty pizza dough. Think Dominos, not Delfina.” The dough, with a healthy amount of yogurt, was somewhat reminiscent of a spongy Domino crust, but it was actually super good right off the grill. I did kind of screw up the shapes: you’ll see the one on bottom is puffy (I didn’t slit the top well enough to vent it), and the one I’m holding looks more like a baguette, since I made the freshman mistake of rolling them all out and then stacking them without enough flour to keep them separated. With all the countries in the world that grilled breads, I’ll get the hang of it yet! Firni | Cardamom-scented pudding | Recipe As the gelatinous blobs of cornstarch-thickened sweetened milk flopped off the serving spoon, everyone politely requested small portions — this is not a good-looking dessert. But lo! The cardamom and saffron made for a delicate aroma, with a bit of crunch from the pistachios, and the cool milk base made for a pleasant end of meal. They asked for seconds. The weather was lovely, so we ate outside with our inaugural guests Jessica, Natasja, and Mike. Once the sun set and with the firni mostly gone, we watched Afghan Star, an excellent documentary about the equivalent of American Idol, but in a country where singing was not too long ago a grave offense, and dancing on TV a national scandal. For more shots by Laura, check out the Picasa album, including live-action grilling snaps!

Posted by Jesse Friedman on March 25, 2018 · Leave A Comment

Peru, a land of remarkable geographic diversity, stretches from its dramatic coastline to its majestic towering mountains and lush, verdant jungles. This rich tapestry of landscapes is mirrored in its population, a vibrant convergence of Inca, Spanish, and Asian influences. This cultural fusion, as one might expect, has profoundly shaped its culinary traditions. Crafting a menu that could encapsulate this extensive variety within a single, semi-coherent meal presented a delightful challenge. Fortunately, our guest, Dan, proved invaluable in this endeavor, assisting significantly in pulling together a selection that truly represented the breadth of Peruvian cuisine. We also extend our heartfelt gratitude to my aunt Marcia, whose generous contributions were instrumental.

Posted by Jesse Friedman on March 25, 2018 · Leave A Comment

Paraguay, a nation nestled in the heart of South America, holds the distinction of being one of only two landlocked countries in the Western Hemisphere. A fascinating linguistic footnote is that it is the sole country where the indigenous Guaraní language is spoken by a larger population than the language of its former colonizers, Spanish. This linguistic dominance naturally translates into a strong Guaraní legacy within both the ingredients and the cooking techniques that define Paraguayan food. While Western staples like cheese and beef have certainly integrated into the local diet, the Guaraní influence remains a prominent and defining characteristic. It's worth noting that Paraguay is a frequent and significant destination for Peace Corps volunteers, contributing to its unique cultural landscape.

Posted by Jesse Friedman on March 25, 2018 · Leave A Comment

Papua New Guinea, situated north of Australia and to the east of Indonesia, is widely recognized as one of the most linguistically diverse countries on Earth, boasting over 800 distinct languages and even pockets of uncontacted tribes. Despite sharing the large island of Papua with Indonesia, a nation firmly within Southeast Asia, Papua New Guinea is culturally classified as part of Melanesia, representing the southwestern edge of the vast Pacific island archipelago. This distinct cultural grouping is clearly reflected in the unique ingredients and traditional cooking methods employed across the country. Our exploration has revealed a fascinating culinary heritage.

Posted by Jesse Friedman on March 25, 2018 · Leave A Comment

For the second consecutive year, we organized a late-summer gathering, affectionately termed a "Nosh," inviting everyone on our block to join us. Laura secured the necessary permit to temporarily close down the street, and our neighbors generously contributed tables and chairs, creating a communal dining space. Just as we were all settling in, the first rain of the season began to fall! Given that much of the global perception of Panama is tied to its pivotal role in international transportation, largely due to its famous canal, it felt remarkably fitting to be enjoying our meal together in a long, communal line down the street. This setting added a unique charm to the occasion.

Posted by Jesse Friedman on February 24, 2018 · Leave A Comment

Palestine, while not a full member of the United Nations, has held the status of a "non-member observer state" since 2012, a designation that suffices for our culinary explorations. Setting aside political considerations, Palestinian cuisine stands out as distinctly flavorful and unique in its own right. This is particularly noteworthy in a region where many culinary traditions are characterized by subtle regional variations on common themes – it's often the case that a restaurant might be broadly categorized as "Middle Eastern." However, the West Bank proudly claims a distinctive layered dish as the cornerstone of its culinary identity, a testament to its rich gastronomic heritage.

Posted by Jesse Friedman on February 10, 2018 · Leave A Comment

Palau, another gem among the low-population island nations scattered across the vast Pacific Ocean, presents a unique culinary landscape. We've observed a consistent pattern: traditional foods in these island communities tend to be quite straightforward in their preparation. Furthermore, finding precise recipes can often be a challenge, as the cooking process is frequently defined more by a general approach to combining ingredients rather than by a strict set of instructions and measurements. Once again, the task of determining what to cook, and when, led me to scroll through various Facebook groups for inspiration. Our gathering was enhanced by the presence of new friends.

Posted by Jesse Friedman on February 4, 2018 · Leave A Comment

Pakistan, prior to its independence in 1947, was an integral part of India. The partition, intended to establish a new nation for Muslims, was in many respects an arbitrary and ill-conceived division, particularly given the extensive intermingling of religious communities. A significant example of this is the division of the vast state of Punjab into two. Subsequently, millions of people undertook perilous journeys across these newly drawn lines, either relocating to the country that aligned with their religious identity or tragically perishing in the attempt. Both of these historical factors profoundly explain the deep culinary connections and similarities that Pakistani food shares with its Indian counterpart, a testament to shared history and migration.

Posted by Jesse Friedman on January 26, 2018 · Leave A Comment

The Midsummer festival, celebrated across Scandinavia and including Norway, is perhaps a bit of a misnomer, as it actually takes place around the summer solstice, the first day of summer. Regardless of the precise timing, it undeniably marks the peak of the year, characterized by exceptionally long days and gardens bursting with the bounty of fruits and vegetables. We opted to hold our celebration a few weeks earlier, a decision that proved fortuitous for Portland's local seasons. This timing allowed us to enjoy new potatoes, strawberries, and rhubarb at their absolute peak of freshness and flavor. The abundance was certainly not lacking.

Posted by Jesse Friedman on September 29, 2017 · Leave A Comment

Among the 193 member nations of the United Nations, Oman is the sole country whose name begins with the letter "O." Geopolitically, it also occupies a somewhat solitary position. Oman maintains a considerably lower profile compared to its neighbors in the Middle East, exhibiting neither the ostentatious displays of wealth seen in some other sultanates nor the regional conflicts that have affected certain neighboring countries. However, Oman possesses a rich and extensive history, largely due to its strategic location at the mouth of the Persian Gulf. For centuries, its traders were active participants in the maritime commerce of the Indian Ocean, and it wasn't until the mid-20th century that its influence began to shift.

Posted by Jesse Friedman on July 8, 2017 · Leave A Comment

Niger, a nation in West Africa, serves as the much lesser-known northern neighbor to Nigeria. To help mitigate the inevitable confusion between the two countries, its English pronunciation deliberately imitates the French: "nee-ZHEHR." The demonym for its people is "Nigerien," with the subtle addition of that second "E" providing the necessary phonetic distinction. Niger is classified as a Sahel nation, meaning it resides in the transitional zone situated between the vast Sahara Desert to the north and the tropical West African coast to the south. Mercy Corps, our designated fundraising beneficiary, undertakes significant and impactful work in Niger, encompassing both emergency food relief efforts and initiatives aimed at enhancing the long-term resilience of local farmers. Our gathering was enriched by the participation of dedicated individuals.

query: What event did Jessica Biel and Justin Timberlake host in Puglia, Italy, prior to their wedding?

documents.text: Jessica Biel and Justin Timberlake attend the 2011 Vanity Fair Oscar Party Hosted by Graydon Carter at the Sunset Tower Hotel on February 27, 2011 in West Hollywood, California. This can include renting out nearby properties to keep paparazzi away, a cost that can add up to an extra million dollars for very private locations. Another example is arranging helicopter tours for guests to see nearby islands. These elaborate plans significantly increase the total cost. query: What specific elements of Rand Paul's op-ed appear to be plagiarized from Dan Stewart's article in The Week?

documents.text: Share On more Share On more Share On more Share On more Sections of an op-ed Kentucky Sen. Rand Paul wrote on mandatory minimums in The Washington Times in September appear nearly identical to an article by Dan Stewart of The Week that ran a week earlier. The discovery comes amid reports from BuzzFeed that Paul plagiarized in his book and in several speeches. Paul also delivered testimony before the Senate Judiciary Committee on Sept. 16, 2013, that included the copied sections. Here's how Stewart wrote his introduction to his article on "Rethinking mandatory sentencing": It's the automatic imposition of a minimum number of years in prison for specific crimes — usually related to drugs. By design, mandatory sentencing laws take discretion away from prosecutors and judges so as to impose harsh sentences, regardless of circumstances. Mandatory sentencing began in the 1970s as a response to a growing drug-and-crime epidemic, and over the decades has put hundreds of thousands of people behind bars for drug possession and sale, and other non-violent crimes. Since mandatory sentencing began, America's prison population has quadrupled, to 2.4 million. America now jails a higher percentage of its citizens than any other country, including China and Iran, at the staggering cost of $80 billion a year. Is that a good thing? Most public officials — including liberals, conservatives, and libertarians — have decided that it's not. At least 20 states, both red and blue, have reformed their mandatory sentencing laws in some way, and Congress is considering a bipartisan bill that would do the same for federal crimes. And here's how Paul wrote it a week later with the text bolded that appears copied: Mandatory-minimum sentences automatically impose a minimum number of years in prison for specific crimes — usually related to drugs. By design, mandatory-sentencing laws take discretion away from prosecutors and judges so as to impose harsh sentences, regardless of circumstances. Since mandatory sentencing began in the 1970s in response to a growing drug-and-crime epidemic, America's prison population has quadrupled, to 2.4 million. America now jails a higher percentage of its citizens than any other country, including China and Iran, at the staggering cost of $80 billion a year. Drug offenders in the United States spend more time under the criminal justice system's formal control than drug offenders anywhere else in the world. Most public officials — liberals, conservatives and libertarians — have decided that mandatory-minimum sentencing is unnecessary. At least 20 states, both red and blue, have reformed their mandatory-sentencing laws in some way, and Congress is considering a bipartisan bill that would do the same for federal crimes. Another section of the article related to John Horner, a father of three who sold some pain pills to a police informant. Here's how The Week wrote it, again with bolded text on the copied sections: When a friend asked John Horner if he could buy some painkillers, the 46-year-old father of three didn't see a problem. The Osceola County, Fla., resident had been taking prescribed painkillers for years after losing his eye in an accident, and agreed to sell his friend, "Matt," four unused bottles. After the pills exchanged hands, Horner discovered that "Matt" was in fact a police informant, and he was charged with dealing drugs. At the advice of his public defender, Horner pleaded guilty, and was later sentenced to the mandatory minimum of 25 years in jail. He will be 72 by the time he is released, and his three young children will have grown up without him. "Matt," who turned out to have a long history of drug offenses, was more fortunate — he received a reduced sentence of just 18 months after informing on Horner, and is now free. And here's how Paul wrote it: John Horner was a 46-year-old father of three when he sold some of his prescription painkillers to a friend. His friend turned out to be a police informant, and he was charged with dealing drugs. Horner pleaded guilty and was later sentenced to the mandatory minimum of 25 years in prison. John will be 72 years old by the time he is released, and his three young children will have grown up without him. The informant, who had a long history of drug offenses, was more fortunate — he received a reduced sentence of just 18 months, and is now free. "We've always known that the audience of The Week consists of smart, busy people who want to feel even smarter, including a lot of people on Capitol Hill," said Bill Falk, the editor in chief of The Week. "We'd like to thank Sen. Paul for his endorsement.'' Paul's office did not return a request for comment.

This is an update of a Street Sweeper article originally published on March 16, 2011. On March 14, 2011, a federal Indictment filed in the Southern District of Ohio charged Steve T. Kinder , 51, and Kinder Caviar Inc . with illegally harvesting paddlefish from Ohio waters and falsely reporting to the Kentucky Department of Fish & Wildlife Resources that he caught the fish in Kentucky; and , 51, and . with illegally harvesting paddlefish from Ohio waters and falsely reporting to the Kentucky Department of Fish & Wildlife Resources that he caught the fish in Kentucky; and Cornelia Joyce Kinder , 53, as well as Kinder Caviar Inc. and Black Star Caviar Company with providing false information about the paddlefish eggs to the U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service in order to obtain permits to export the paddlefish eggs to foreign customers, including the amount of paddlefish eggs to be exported, the names of the fishermen that harvested the paddlefish, and the location where the paddlefish were harvested. , 53, as well as Kinder Caviar Inc. and with providing false information about the paddlefish eggs to the U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service in order to obtain permits to export the paddlefish eggs to foreign customers, including The Kinders, husband and wife, both owned and operated Kinder Caviar and Black Star Caviar. Those companies were in the business of exporting paddlefish eggs as caviar to customers in foreign countries. The alleged violations occurred between March 2006 and December 2010. If convicted, the Kinders face a maximum penalty of five years in prison, a $250,000 fine or both on each count. The companies could be fined up to $500,000 per count. NOTE: The charges contained in the indictments are merely accusations and defendants are presumed innocent unless and until proven guilty at trial beyond a reasonable doubt. SIDE BAR: The American paddlefish (Polydon spathula), also called the Mississippi paddlefish or the spoonbill is a freshwater fish that is primarily found in the Mississippi River drainage system. Paddlefish eggs are marketed as caviar. Paddlefish were once common in waters throughout the Midwest. However, the global decline in other caviar sources, such as sturgeon, has led to an increased demand for paddlefish caviar, which has resulted in over-fishing and, as a consequence, the decline of the paddlefish population. Paddlefish are protected by both federal and Ohio law. It is illegal to harvest paddlefish in Ohio waters, but they can be harvested legally in Kentucky waters. Notwithstanding the above criminal allegations, the State of Kentucky's Department of Agriculture maintains a webpage touting Kinder Caviar. We are advised that: Caspian Sea style caviar made right here in the Bluegrass! American caviar now comparable to the caviars of the Caspian Sea! [A]s the catch and quotas of Caspian Sea caviar continue to decline, prices will steadily increase. Kinder Caviar is fast becoming the choice for the caviar savvy connoisseur. . . Also, if you visit http://Kentucky.gov, you will learn that in October 2008, Kentucky Governor Steven L. Beshear (D)appointed five members to the state's Aquaculture Task Force. The official announcement lists among those appointees: Joyce Kinder, of Perry Park, is the owner of Kinder Caviar, Inc. She represents wholesalers of aquaculture products. In the October 2008 edition of the Legislative Record the following is noted under the heading: INTERIM JOINT COMMITTEE ON AGRICULTURE AND NATURAL RESOURCES / Minutes of the 4th Meeting of the 2008 Interim /September 12, 2008: Finally, Representative McKee noted that Ms. Joyce Kinder, of Kinder Caviar, Inc.; had signed up to speak to the committee to address concerns regarding some paddlefish administrative regulations. Ms. Kinder agreed that regulations are necessary but would like to see some provisions amended. She specifically noted the revocation of a fisherman’s license or permit could occur even though water boundaries were inaccurate because of GPS positioning. She stated they feel that license revocation is extremely harsh. She also noted that they could not agree upon snagging methods. She said that under the current regulation all snagging methods must stop. Finally, Ms. Kinder noted disagreement with the size limit, noting that they do not want to hold up the process but would like the committee to take a further look at the regulation. . . Ain't it grand? The world is best by terrorism, civil war, earthquakes, tsunamis, and nuclear catastrophe but we still have time to go after alleged caviar bandits in the murky waters of Ohio and Kentucky. Where's Neil Diamond when we need him? Perhaps he could pen Kentucky Caviar as his next hit? Neil, call me. Let's chat. Do lunch. I'll bring the caviar. UPDATE On January 17, 2012, Steven and Cornelia Joyce Kinder, and their caviar companies pleaded guilty in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Ohio to trafficking in and falsely labeling illegally harvested paddlefish. According to the plea agreement Cornelia Joyce Kinder admitted to making false statements in CITES Export Registration Forms for paddlefish eggs on behalf of: Kinder Caviar : on or about March 15, 2007, she misrepresented the amount of legally-harvested paddlefish eggs that she could provide documentation for, as well as misidentified the fishermen who harvested the paddlefish and the location of harvest; and : on or about March 15, 2007, she misrepresented the amount of legally-harvested paddlefish eggs that she could provide documentation for, as well as misidentified the fishermen who harvested the paddlefish and the location of harvest; and Black Star Caviar Company: on or about Dec. 18, 2010m she completed the form using the name of a subordinate employee and forged that employee’s signature on the form in order to give the impression that she was not the applicant. According to the plea agreement, both Steve Kinder and Cornelia Joyce Kinder admitted to aiding and abetting one another in harvesting paddlefish that they should have known were harvested in Ohio waters in violation of Ohio law through the use of gill nets attached to the Ohio shoreline, on or about May 5, 2007, and, thereafter, transporting the paddlefish to Kentucky with the intent to sell them when. As part of a plea agreement, both Kinder Caviar and Black Star Caviar Company have each agreed to pay a $5,000 fine and serve a three-year term of probation, during which time those companies will be prohibited from applying for or receiving a CITES Export Permit. Additionally, Steve and Cornelia Joyce Kinder have agreed to serve a three-year term of probation, during which time they will each perform 100 hours of community service, be prohibited from fishing anywhere in the Ohio River where that river forms the border between Ohio and Kentucky, and be prohibited from applying for or receiving a CITES Export Permit, either on behalf of themselves or anyone else. In accordance with Kentucky law, the Kinders face possible suspension of their Kentucky commercial fishing licenses. Finally, as part of the plea agreement, the boat and truck that were used in furtherance of the Lacey Act crimes have been forfeited.

ANALYSIS/OPINION: [Editor’s Note: Portions of the following article should have been attributed to “Rethinking mandatory sentencing,” an article written by Dan Stewart that appeared in The Week on Sept. 14, 2013.] If I told you that in America almost 1 million black Americans were forever forbidden from voting, you might think I was talking about Jim Crow 50 years ago, but you would be wrong. According to the Sentencing Project, a staggering number of nonviolent individuals who have been released from prison, are not on probation or parole and who have committed no further crimes, are forever prohibited from voting. Many black Americans are prevented from ever voting because of the war on drugs. On Wednesday, I testified before the Senate Judiciary Committee and described the injustice and effects of mandatory-minimum sentences. These sentences are disproportionally affecting minorities and low-income communities. A recent report from the American Civil Liberties Union reports that blacks are four to five times more likely to be convicted for drug possession. Some dismiss this because they think blacks are committing more drug crimes, but in June, The New York Times reported that although black Americans were four times more likely to be arrested than whites for marijuana possession, both groups used the drug at similar rates. Why are the arrest rates so lopsided? One widely cited study by The San Jose Mercury News reviewed 700,000 criminal cases that were matched by crime and criminal history of the defendant. The analysis revealed that similarly situated whites were far more successful than blacks and Hispanics in the plea-bargaining process. Today, the United States incarcerates more people than any other country in the world, and the racial disparity in arrest rates has been absolutely devastating to the black community. Professor Michelle Alexander has even called the war on drugs “the New Jim Crow.” It’s not just black Americans. Regardless of the color of your skin, the war on drugs has ruined the lives of thousands of young people. I know a man about my age in Kentucky who grew marijuana plants in his apartment closet in college. Thirty years later, he still can’t vote, can’t own a gun, and when he looks for work, he must check the box that basically says: “I’m a convicted felon, and I guess I’ll always be one.” Getting a job is nearly impossible for him. John Horner was a 46-year-old father of three when he sold some of his prescription painkillers to a friend. His friend turned out to be a police informant, and he was charged with dealing drugs. Horner pleaded guilty and was later sentenced to the mandatory minimum of 25 years in prison. John will be 72 years old by the time he is released, and his three young children will have grown up without him. The informant, who had a long history of drug offenses, was more fortunate — he received a reduced sentence of just 18 months, and is now free. So many judges oppose mandatory-minimum sentencing precisely because such an arbitrary law does not take into account that each case is different. I want to be clear: I am not advocating for any type of get-out-of-jail-free passes for individuals who break the law. I am simply arguing that the federal government should get out of the way, and allow local and state judges to do their jobs. Mandatory-minimum sentences automatically impose a minimum number of years in prison for specific crimes — usually related to drugs. By design, mandatory-sentencing laws take discretion away from prosecutors and judges so as to impose harsh sentences, regardless of circumstances. Since mandatory sentencing began in the 1970s in response to a growing drug-and-crime epidemic, America’s prison population has quadrupled, to 2.4 million. America now jails a higher percentage of its citizens than any other country, including China and Iran, at the staggering cost of $80 billion a year. Drug offenders in the United States spend more time under the criminal justice system’s formal control than drug offenders anywhere else in the world. Most public officials — liberals, conservatives and libertarians — have decided that mandatory-minimum sentencing is unnecessary. At least 20 states, both red and blue, have reformed their mandatory-sentencing laws in some way, and Congress is considering a bipartisan bill that would do the same for federal crimes. About 1.3 million people — more than half the total prison population — are behind bars for nonviolent crimes, and federal prisons are 40 percent over capacity. “It’s a waste of tax dollars and human lives,” said Anthony Papa of the Drug Policy Alliance. It’s time for these unjust laws to end. On March 20, I introduced the Justice Safety Valve Act of 2013 with the Sen. Patrick J. Leahy, Vermont Democrat. We have been joined by Sens. Carl Levin, Michigan Democrat; Angus King, Maine independent; and Kristen Gillibrand, New York Democrat, in the Senate, and Rep. Robert C. “Bobby” Scott, Virginia Democrat, joined by 11 others, introduced similar legislation in the House. The legislation is short and simple. It amends current law to provide “authority to impose a sentence below a statutory mandatory minimum” if certain requirements are met. Mandatory-minimum sentencing has done little to address the very real problem of drug abuse, while also doing great damage by destroying so many lives. Each case should be judged on its own merits, yet mandatory minimums prevent this from happening. The Justice Safety Valve Act will be an important step in improving justice in our nation’s courtrooms. Sen. Rand Paul, Kentucky Republican, is a member of the Senate Foreign Relations and Homeland Security committees.

A section of Rand Paul's 2012 book Government Bullies appears to be plagiarized from a Forbes article from earlier in that year. BuzzFeed had previously reported that more than three pages plagiarized from The Heritage Foundation and Cato Institute were the only instances of copying in the book. As was the case with cut-and-pasted sections from The Heritage Foundation and a Cato scholar, Paul included a link to the Forbes article in the book's footnotes, but made no effort to indicate that not just the source, but the words themselves, had been taken from Forbes. The article related to the case of Steven and Cornelia Joyce Kinder, who owned a Kentucky-based caviar company and pled guilty to harvesting paddlefish in Ohio waters with intent to sell in Kentucky, in violation of the Lacey Act. Here's how the Forbes article reads: According to the plea agreement, both Steve Kinder and Cornelia Joyce Kinder admitted to aiding and abetting one another in harvesting paddlefish that they should have known were harvested in Ohio waters in violation of Ohio law through the use of gill nets attached to the Ohio shoreline, on or about May 5, 2007, and, thereafter, transporting the paddlefish to Kentucky with the intent to sell them when. As part of a plea agreement, both Kinder Caviar and Black Star Caviar Company have each agreed to pay a $5,000 fine and serve a three-year term of probation, during which time those companies will be prohibited from applying for or receiving a CITES Export Permit. Additionally, Steve and Cornelia Joyce Kinder have agreed to serve a three-year term of probation, during which time they will each perform 100 hours of community service, be prohibited from fishing anywhere in the Ohio River where that river forms the border between Ohio and Kentucky, and be prohibited from applying for or receiving a CITES Export Permit, either on behalf of themselves or anyone else. In accordance with Kentucky law, the Kinders face possible suspension of their Kentucky commercial fishing licenses. Finally, as part of the plea agreement, the boat and truck that were used in furtherance of the Lacey Act crimes have been forfeited. And here's now Paul wrote it, with the identical sections copied in bold: The Justice Department's plea agreement stated that both Steve Kinder and Cornelia Joyce Kinder admitted to aiding and abetting one another in harvesting the fish. The agreement stated that they should have known these paddlefish were harvested in Ohio waters in violation of state law, through the use of gill nets attached to the Ohio shoreline. This happened on or about May 5, 2007, and thereafter the Kinders had transported the paddlefish to Kentucky with intent to sell them. Now, if you're like me, obviously we are getting down on our hands and knees to thank our federal government for addressing this pressing matter. But it gets even more ridiculous. As part of a plea agreement, both Kinder Caviar and Black Star Caviar Company have each agreed to pay a $ 5,000 fine and serve a three-year term of probation, during which time those companies will be prohibited from applying for or receiving a CITES Export Permit. Additionally, Steve and Cornelia Joyce Kinder have agreed to serve a three-year term of probation, during which time they will each perform one hundred hours of community service, be prohibited from fishing anywhere in the Ohio River, where that river forms the border between Ohio and Kentucky, and be prohibited from applying for or receiving a CITES Export Permit, either on behalf of themselves or anyone else. In accordance with Kentucky law, the Kinders also face possible suspension of their Kentucky commercial fishing licenses. Finally, as part of the plea agreement, the boat and truck that were used in furtherance of the Lacey Act crimes have been forfeited. Paul's office did not immediately return a request for comment on this instance but said Tuesday that "there have also been occasions where quotations or typesetting indentations have been left out through errors in our approval process." The Kentucky senator's office said that "a new approval process implemented by Sen. Paul will ensure proper citation and accountability in all collaborative works going forward." Bill Singer, the author of the Forbes article, said "no, absolutely not," when asked if he knew the Senator used his work. He also noted that he relied heavily on a press release from the Justice Department. "It would appear whoever wrote the senator's book copied my language not the press release. Plagiarism is a very sincere form of flattery," he said. Mr. Singer pointed out even the snide section of his article appeared similar Paul's book, but both men used different examples for their sarcasm about the government prosecution. "Ain't it grand? The world is best by terrorism, civil war, earthquakes, tsunamis, and nuclear catastrophe but we still have time to go after alleged caviar bandits in the murky waters of Ohio and Kentucky," Singer wrote. "We have a trillion-dollar deficit hole in our budget. We have bridges literally falling down and an infrastructure that is crumbling. We have a military that is stretched entirely too thin. We have a broken entitlement system, and a bunch of Washington "leaders" too scared or inept to do anything about it.
