In February 2017 Ming Chi Kuo predicted that the (5.15in) iPhone 8 will have a resolution of 2,436 x 1,125 and a pixel density of 521ppi, far higher than the iPhone 7's 326ppi. And the 5.8in version of the iPhone 8 - the iPhone 8 Plus, presumably - will have a resolution of 2,800 x 1,242 and a pixel density of 528ppi. Processor iPhones contain proprietary processor chips that you won't find in other smartphones: there's an A9 in the iPhone 6s and an A10 Fusion chip in the iPhone 7, for example. So it doesn't take a genius to predict that the iPhone 8 will get an A11 (or maybe an A11 Fusion). But while the A chips are made to Apple's own design and specifications, several other companies are involved; they contain technology licensed from ARM, and are manufactured, at present, by Samsung and TSMC. And that could change in the nearish future. Intel has declared its intention of expanding its smartphone business, and is hot favourite to displace TSMC from the iPhone contract, according to the Nikkei Asian Review. Economic Daily News, however, thinks TSMC still has the gig, and will begin production of the A11 in April 2017. The site has little detail of the A11 design, but does say it will be fabricated with a 10 nanometre FinFET process, a significant upgrade from the A10 Fusion's 16 nanometres. RAM The iPhone 7 comes with 2GB RAM, while the iPhone 7 Plus offers 3GB. In February 2017 research firm TrendForce published a report predicting that the iPhone 8 will have 3GB RAM. Storage The iPhone 7 handsets come in three storage configurations: 32GB, 128GB and 256GB. The TrendForce report linked above suggests that Apple may phase out the 32GB model for the iPhone 8 generation. The late-2017 iPhones will come in 64GB and 256GB configurations, the firm says. A poster on Weibo, meanwhile, has posted a screen from an unknown retailer's stock system, which refers to the "iPhone X Edition" coming in 128GB and 256GB flavours. In 2017, Apple has launched new iPad Pro models that offer the most storage of any iOS device yet, going up to 512GB. This seems like overkill for phones but one (usually reliable) poster on Chinese social media now claims to have leaks confirming the iPhone 8 will also be offered with half a terabyte of storage. When the first-gen iPhone was launched it came with a maximum of 8GB of storage (although a 16GB option was added later). That means that, assuming this prediction is correct, storage will have increased by a factor of 64 in ten years. Camera The notorious and far-reaching HomePod firmware leak at the end of July 2017 contained numerous clues about the iPhone 8, including a hint (spotted by the Portuguese-language site iHelp BR) that it may offer 4K video at 60fps on both its front and back cameras - which would be a major step forward. The iPhone 7 and 7 Plus do offer 4K video capture but this is capped at 30fps (you can capture at 60fps, but only at a resolution of 1080p) and is possible on the rear-facing camera only. The front-facing camera has a video limit of 1080p. The same firmware leak also suggests that front and back cameras will support slo-mo video at 1080p and 240fps. (The iPhone 7 and 7 Plus offer a less impressive 1080p at 120 fps or 720p at 240 fps on the rear camera, while the front camera doesn't offer slow-mo at all.) A MIC report suggests that all models of the new iPhone will feature the dual camera that's currently only available on the iPhone 7 Plus. Unverified schematics posted on Slashleaks suggest this will be aligned vertically, with the twin lenses stacked on top of one another, rather than the horizontal setup on the 7 Plus; this was backed up by photos that BGR got hold of in May 2017. This leaked image shared on Twitter also suggest that the dual camera layout might change: This is a tipped leak what means I can't confirm if legit or not but there you have it... #iPhone8 pic.twitter.com/6OgASNUDNb — OnLeaks (@OnLeaks) 26 April 2017 Battery Ming Chi Kuo has predicted that the iPhone 8 will squeeze a battery with a capacity to match the iPhone 7 Plus - 2,700mAH - into a chassis comparable to the iPhone 7. So we could see a significant battery life increase in the next generation of iPhones. There's also the possibility that the new iPhone could feature two batteries. A leak of the alleged schematics for the iPhone 8, posted on Slashleaks, shows that the main board is smaller and that there are two batteries rather than just one. And IDC analyst Sean Kao claims the new iPhones will use new, smaller printed circuit boards that would allow for a more powerful battery. Smartphone battery life is one of those things that everyone says is important, but you do wonder how much of a compromise the average Apple fan would be willing to make in return. As Jony Ive put it in an interview with the Financial Times, "With a bigger battery [the iPhone] would be heavier, more cumbersome, less 'compelling'." UK price Here are the prices of the iPhone 7 and iPhone 7 Plus. There's a fair chance that the iPhone 8 will be pitched at a similar point, since Apple generally tries to keep these things consistent. iPhone 7 (32GB): £599 iPhone 7 (128GB): £699 iPhone 7 (256GB): £799 iPhone 7 Plus (32GB): £719 iPhone 7 Plus (128GB): £819 iPhone 7 Plus (256GB): £919 However, there's a growing sense that the 8 will cost rather more, as a reflection of the design changes, new features, spec improvements and so on. Prolific leaker Benjamin Geskin has published what he claims are the prices for the iPhone 8 in the US: #iPhone8 64 GB: 999$ 256 GB: 1099$ 512 GB: 1199$ — Benjamin Geskin (@VenyaGeskin1) September 4, 2017 That's a big increase on current pricing, which starts at $649 (for only 32GB, admittedly) and tops out at $969. UBS analysts, however, think this is pushing it. In an investor note to investors, Steven Milunovich and Benjamin Wilson "questioned the logic" of a $1,200 iPhone 8, arguing that Apple generally prices its flagship models slightly below Samsung's Galaxy Notes. The Note 8 costs $929.99. Instead, the analysts predict prices ranging from $900 (64GB) to $1,000 (256GB). ## iPhone 8 Packaging Leak Reveals Near Bezel-Less Display and Top Notch

A leaked packaging diagram, reportedly originating from Weibo and shared via Slashleaks, offers an early look at the rumored iPhone 8's display. The diagram specifically details the SIM card ejection process and highlights the device's nearly bezel-less screen, featuring a prominent notch at the top. This notch is designed to accommodate the earpiece and front-facing camera. If authentic, this leak represents an unusually early packaging disclosure for an iPhone, as such information typically emerges closer to the official launch.

### Design Consistency and Speculation Reinforcement

The diagram's visual style closely resembles schematics found in iPhone 7 packaging, using a similar white and gray color scheme and a green arrow for SIM tray operation. This consistency adds weight to the leak's credibility.

This schematic significantly strengthens existing speculation about the iPhone 8. The display appears to have an 18:9 aspect ratio, and the power button is shown as an elongated element. Notably, the diagram shows no visible bottom bezel. The top notch clearly indicates the housing for the earpiece and front camera. While more sensors are expected in this area, Apple might have omitted them for diagram simplicity.

### Skepticism and Potential for Tampering

Despite these details, caution is warranted. The image's origin is unverified, and its early appearance is unusual for packaging leaks. It's possible an existing iPhone 7 SIM tray diagram was altered to resemble the iPhone 8, a tactic seen before. However, no clear signs of digital manipulation are immediately visible. A similar iPhone 7 diagram is available for comparison.

### Expected Launch and Production Issues

Industry analysts expect Apple to unveil the iPhone 8 at a September event, alongside two new "iPhone 7s" models expected to retain the current iPhone 7 design. Reports indicate the iPhone 8 is facing manufacturing challenges, potentially delaying shipments until October or November. For more Apple product insights, visit 9to5Mac on YouTube.

The Internet Archive is on a mission to **combat link rot** by actively crawling and archiving the web. This isn't just about saving individual pages; it's about **preserving the entire web ecosystem**. When a page is archived, the Internet Archive also captures the pages it links to. This is incredibly important because, as the live web changes, those linked pages might disappear or be altered.

Thanks to this proactive approach, even if a referenced page is later deleted or modified, **a stable link to its archived version remains available**. This ensures that the original context and intent of a webpage are not lost to time. The Internet Archive's ultimate goal is to **systematically fix broken links across the internet**. They envision these archived links becoming **reliable companions to live web content**, allowing users to see what authors originally intended, regardless of subsequent changes online. To achieve this, they conduct special crawls for initiatives like the "No More 404" program, specifically targeting content to **prevent the dreaded 404 error**.

Mizuho Securities analyst Abhey Lamba has **downgraded Apple (AAPL) shares to Neutral from Buy**, citing concerns that the stock's recent surge has **already priced in the excitement for the upcoming iPhone**. He also **reduced his price target to $150 from $160**.

Lamba acknowledges that the **new iPhone cycle is expected to drive strong holiday sales and continue into early next year**. However, he believes this strength is **already anticipated by the market**, leaving **limited room for upside surprises**.

While Lamba doesn't doubt the new "iPhone 8" will be a **"strong product cycle,"** he anticipates that **most sales will come from existing Apple users upgrading their current devices**. He argues this **shouldn't fundamentally alter Apple's valuation**.

The **primary concern for Lamba is pricing**. He suggests that a **premium model priced above $1,000**, featuring an OLED screen, might cause some consumers to **delay their purchase until the following year**. This is because the **OLED technology is expected to become available in lower-priced models later**.

Lamba's Japan research team also believes that **broader adoption of OLED screens could lead to their inclusion in less expensive iPhones next year**. Consequently, he predicts that **adoption of the new devices might be more gradual**, as customers may **wait for future launches offering advanced technology at lower price points**.

In Lamba's view, **Apple's pricing strategy is likely to be the main obstacle to significantly expanding its user base**. While some immediate upgrades may occur due to aging hardware, **customers with the flexibility to extend their current device's lifespan might opt to do so** until OLED panels and enhanced features are more widely accessible. This could result in a **more prolonged product cycle compared to previous launches**.

Lamba believes that **current analyst estimates already account for most of the potential from this cycle**. He notes that consensus estimates for fiscal year 2018 include iPhone shipments of 242 million units, an average selling price (ASP) of $680, operating margins of 27%, and earnings per share (EPS) of $10.43. Even in his most optimistic scenario, assuming 252 million units shipped at a $700 ASP, the EPS would be around $11. Applying a 15x multiple to this suggests a stock value in the mid-$160s, indicating **limited upside from current levels**.

Similarly, analyzing from an enterprise value perspective, assuming a 25% free cash flow margin for FY18 (slightly above the consensus 24%), 12% sales growth (in line with consensus), and an EV/FCF multiple of 11x (matching the peak multiple before the iPhone 6 cycle), he also arrives at a stock value in the mid-$160s.

Regarding reports of **iPhone 8 production delays**, Lamba acknowledges that his colleagues in Japan believe a **"few weeks' production delay on the OLED model may not be unreasonable,"** with initial supplies of the higher-end model being **"severely limited."** However, he downplays the significance of these delays, drawing parallels to the **iPhone 6+ launch in 2014 and the iPhone 7+ Jet Black SKU last year**. Both of those launches experienced limited availability initially but achieved supply-demand balance within a few months.

Lamba concludes that **consumer demand is expected to remain strong due to Apple's powerful brand loyalty**. He believes **investors will likely overlook a potential few-week delay**. Apple's stock closed Friday's session at $148.98.

### iPhone 8 Design Leaked: Apple's HomePod Firmware Reveals Key Features

Apple's upcoming iPhone 8 is set to redefine smartphone design, moving away from previous models with a dramatically different look. While numerous leaks, including CAD drawings and renders, have long suggested a device without a physical home button and with significantly reduced screen borders, these rumors now appear to be officially confirmed. The most substantial evidence has emerged from an unexpected source: Apple's own HomePod smart speaker firmware.

#### A Significant Firmware Revelation

In a surprising development, Apple, a company renowned for its tight security, has inadvertently provided an unprecedented look at the iPhone 8's design. While developers were examining the firmware for the new Siri-enabled speaker, they discovered a hidden schematic detailing the new iPhone's form factor.

#### The Bezel-Less Display Unveiled

This critical schematic, found within the `PassKitUIFoundation.framework` file as `Payment_glyph_phone-D22.caar`, clearly illustrates a bezel-less iPhone. The image, initially shared by developer Steve Troughton-Smith and later verified by others like Guilherme Rambo, aligns precisely with earlier design predictions.

#### Confirmed iPhone 8 Design Elements

The leaked schematic confirms several anticipated changes for the iPhone 8:

*   **Elimination of the Physical Home Button:** The front of the device no longer features a traditional home button.
*   **Ultra-Thin Bezels:** The borders around the display are remarkably slim, promising a more immersive visual experience.
*   **Display Notch:** A distinct cutout at the top of the screen is visible, housing the earpiece, camera, and sensors.

Although the leaked images do not show the rear of the phone, this discovery is still a major breakthrough. The absence of a rear-mounted Touch ID sensor is also strongly implied, as its presence would likely have been indicated in the front-facing schematic if it were a primary feature.

#### The Mystery of the Leak's Origin

The exact reasons behind the HomePod firmware's public release remain unclear. However, it's evident that this was not intended for public access, as Steve Troughton-Smith noted that the firmware contained comprehensive details because it was not meant for widespread distribution. The fact that the firmware is still accessible for download from Apple's servers only deepens the mystery and raises questions about potential consequences for those involved.

The possibility of **Apple launching an iPhone priced at $1,000 or more** first surfaced in February, following a report from Fast Company. This report, citing anonymous sources, suggested that a **special 10th-anniversary edition** of the iconic smartphone would boast **exclusive features and a price tag "very likely north of $1,000."** While initially met with some doubt, the idea of a **premium-priced iPhone** has since gained significant traction.

This market speculation aligns with predictions from **Ming-Chi Kuo**, a KGI Securities analyst known for his accurate Apple forecasts. As far back as November, Kuo had anticipated Apple developing **three new iPhone models for 2017**, including one with an advanced **OLED display**. With the launch of these devices drawing near, discussions about the **high-cost special edition** have intensified.

Some industry analysts believe this pricing strategy could be **detrimental**. Abhey Lamba, a Mizuho Securities analyst, expressed concern that by exclusively equipping the **flagship 2017 model with premium features like the OLED screen**, Apple might alienate consumers who cannot afford it. He suggests these customers might **delay their purchases until these sought-after features are available in more affordable models in 2018**. Lamba noted, "While some near-term upgrade activity may be associated with failing hardware of aged devices, customers with the flexibility to extend the life of their devices may choose to do so until OLED panel and incremental functionality are more widely available. As a result, the product cycle could end up being more prolonged this time around versus prior launches."

Conversely, others see this as a **strategic move for Apple to boost revenue** by targeting a higher market segment. Tech commentator John Gruber of Daringfireball.net argues that introducing a **deluxe "Pro" tier to the iPhone lineup is a logical progression**, mirroring Apple's established strategy with its iPad and Mac product lines. He believes this approach is particularly smart, given Apple's likely **limited supply of OLED panels this year** to equip all new iPhone models. Gruber explained, "In the same way it made sense for Honda and Toyota to create their Acura and Lexus divisions to sell higher-end cars without eroding the value or popularity of their best-selling Accords and Camrys, it makes sense for Apple to create a premium tier for the iPhone, the best-selling product the company has ever made and likely will ever make."

This situation raises questions about consumer buying habits. Will you choose the **premium iPhone**? Will you **wait until next year** to upgrade? Or might you consider **advanced Google Android devices** currently on the market?

query: What medical complications led to Manuel Noriega's intensive care on March 7?

documents.text: Mr. Noriega had been in intensive care since March 7 after complications developed from surgery to remove what his lawyer described as a benign brain tumor. His daughters told reporters in March that he had had a brain hemorrhage after the procedure. He had been granted house arrest in January to prepare for the operation. His medical problems came on the heels of a legal odyssey that had begun with the invasion and led to prison terms in the United States, France and finally Panama. While imprisoned abroad he suffered strokes, hypertension and other ailments, his lawyers said. After returning to Panama on Dec. 11, 2011, he began serving long sentences for murder, embezzlement and corruption in connection with his rule during the 1980s. It was an inglorious homecoming for a man who had been known for brandishing a machete while making defiant nationalist speeches and living a lavish, libertine life off drug-trade riches, complete with luxurious mansions, cocaine-fueled parties and voluminous collections of antique guns. It was a quirky life as well: He liked to display his teddy bears dressed as paratroopers.

Manuel Noriega, the **former Panamanian dictator**, has died. His life concluded after spending his final three decades in **correctional facilities and entangled in legal proceedings** across the United States, France, and Panama. Noriega's **authoritarian six-year tenure** ended abruptly in December 1989 with the **U.S. invasion of Panama**, codenamed **Operation Just Cause**. The subsequent capture of Noriega was a **remarkably drawn-out and unconventional operation**.

### A Unique Siege Tactic

In a maneuver that remains one of military history's most peculiar operations, U.S. forces employed an unusual tactic: **psychological warfare through amplified music**. Instead of relying solely on conventional ordnance, the military opted for a strategic eccentricity, broadcasting **loud volumes of Guns N' Roses music**. This sonic assault began on December 20th.

### The Hunt for Noriega

Over a 13-hour period, American forces deployed over 400 bombs. During this time, Noriega was engaged in a **desperate flight across Panama City**. He utilized a Hyundai vehicle and attempted to evade capture by moving between various **untenable sanctuaries**. His evasive maneuvers included seeking refuge in a school, a medical facility, and even a Dairy Queen establishment.

In January 1990, shortly after his surrender to U.S. forces following the military invasion of Panama, former Panamanian dictator Manuel Antonio Noriega received a distinctive package in his federal prison cell near Miami. Inside was a leather-bound copy of the *Soul Winner’s New Testament*, an evangelical text aimed at converting individuals to Christianity. This marked the beginning of a profound spiritual transformation for Noriega, who died Monday at 83.

The New Testament was a gift from Rev. Clift Brannon, an evangelical minister from Longview, Texas. Brannon claimed to have witnessed Noriega carrying a bible as he turned himself in to U.S. authorities. Over the subsequent two years, while awaiting trial on federal drug trafficking charges, Noriega, a nominal Roman Catholic, underwent a significant conversion to evangelical Christianity. This spiritual journey was reportedly guided by Brannon and another minister, culminating in his baptism within a portable fiberglass tub in the atrium of a federal courthouse, a ceremony witnessed by a dozen guards.

This conversion became a notable achievement for the two ministers, both now deceased, who subsequently shared their experiences in media interviews. Noriega's spiritual shift was even mentioned in Brannon's obituary, highlighting its significance to the minister.

The authenticity of Noriega's conversion remains a subject of speculation. Once a U.S. ally and CIA asset, he had become a notoriously repressive dictator, accused of ordering political assassinations and transforming Panama into a hub for drug cartels. Some theorized his embrace of Christianity was a strategic move to garner media sympathy or leniency from the court. The *Los Angeles Times* questioned whether it represented "sincere remorse or cynical spin control," noting the "baffling juxtaposition of good and evil" often seen in prison religious practices.

It is not uncommon for incarcerated individuals to adopt new religious beliefs. Faith groups actively engage within the U.S. prison system, where religious proselytizing is permitted. A 2011 Pew survey indicated that three-quarters of prison chaplains across all 50 states reported significant instances of religious switching among inmates. Research suggests prisoners convert for various reasons, often as a coping mechanism and a tool for "shame management." A 2006 study by British social scientists found that conversion can help prisoners create a new social identity, imbue their imprisonment with purpose, empower them by framing them as agents of God, provide a framework for forgiveness, and offer a sense of control over an uncertain future.

Noriega, incarcerated in a maximum-security cell in January 1990, appeared to be a prime candidate for Brannon, who also served as the editor of the *Soul Winner’s New Testament*. According to American Rehabilitation Ministries (A.R.M.), a Christian prison outreach program, Noriega responded to Brannon's gift with a letter on January 10th, expressing gratitude for the Scriptures. Brannon promptly applied for a permit to visit Noriega, leading to weekly religious instruction sessions conducted by Brannon and Rev. Rudy Hernandez of San Antonio, as reported in a 1991 *New York Times* article.

Brannon led these sessions, with Hernandez providing Spanish translation. Noriega, who had previously dabbled in the occult, embraced evangelical Christianity and reportedly prayed for forgiveness in a three-hour session in May 1990. He described his conversion in letters, stating, "I received Jesus Christ as my Saviour the 15th May of 1990 at 11 a.m." In some correspondence, he acknowledged suffering from "illusions of grandeur and a heart hardened to the Gospel." His attorney confirmed the conversion at the time, dismissing suggestions that it was a tactic to influence the judge, stating, "There are people who are just cynical about everything."

Brannon and Hernandez expressed triumph over Noriega's spiritual awakening. In a 1991 interview with the *Chicago Tribune*, they praised Noriega's "generous heart" and his purported intentions to assist drug addicts. The ministers emphasized their motivation was not personal gain, with Hernandez stating, "We could tell he needed God, that the Lord had chosen him. We didn’t say, ‘he’s a very influential man, let’s get him.’" He likened the opportunity to visiting Saddam Hussein, stating he would "pay my way to Iraq."

Prosecutors remained unimpressed by Noriega's conversion. Diane Cossin, a spokeswoman for the U.S. attorney’s office, told the Associated Press that Noriega's religion was a "personal business" and would not affect his trial. She added, "Sin is endemic in the trafficking of illegal narcotics, and our criminal laws are built on the belief that people are responsible for their acts."

In the fall of 1992, Noriega was sentenced to 40 years for drug trafficking and conspiracy. Shortly thereafter, Brannon and Hernandez petitioned the judge to allow Noriega's baptism. They requested a portable fiberglass baptistery be brought into the courtroom for Noriega to be immersed "in accord with the Holy Scriptures and in obedience to the command of our Lord Jesus Christ." A.R.M. reported that the judge consented and supplied the baptistery.

The ceremony on October 24, 1992, was described by A.R.M. as a significant event. The baptistery was placed in an atrium outside a courtroom and filled with water. A Deputy Marshal brought Noriega, dressed in civilian clothes, into the courtroom. Surrounded by 12 guards, Noriega removed his jacket and shoes before entering the baptistery. Brannon delivered a sermon on the meaning of baptism, interpreted by Hernandez, followed by prayer and Scripture reading. Brannon then baptized Noriega. Brannon recounted a palpable sense of the Holy Spirit in the room as Noriega emerged from the water, with Hernandez playing "Amazing Grace" on an accordion, the music echoing off the marble floor. Brannon described it as a "sacred moment," feeling God's approval.

Both Brannon and Hernandez passed away in 2005. While Hernandez's obituary made no mention of Noriega, Brannon's obituary in his hometown newspaper referred to Noriega's conversion as a "crowning moment" for the minister.

The head of the Panamanian military, called the Panama Defense Force, is widely viewed as the politically dominant force in the country. Noriega Visiting U.S. General Noriega is on a visit to the United States this week and presented a Panamanian medal of honor today at a private ceremony at the Inter-American Defense Board. Requests to interview the general in Washington received no response. In Panama last weekend, General Noriega could not be interviewed to discuss the Americans' assertions despite three days of telephone calls to the army press office and attempts to reach him through other senior army officers. The general, said Capt. Eduardo E. Lim Yueng, the second in command of the Panama Defense Force press office, was too busy with other important business. Two days of telephone calls, including several written messages left with secretaries, brought no reply from the presidential press office. Spokesman Denies Assertions Captain Lim Yueng, however, denied the assertions as a spokesman for the ''institution of the armed forces and for General Noriega.'' He said General Noriega and the military were the victims of a campaign of slander that had no basis in fact. ''These are political attacks,'' he said. ''General Noriega would answer the same way. This campaign is trying to damage our institution.'' A White House official said the intelligence information about General Noriega's activities had been made available to senior officials of the White House. But this official and others said they did not know whether President Reagan was aware of the reports. A senior Reagan Administration official would not discuss the assertions against General Noriega, who was previously head of military intelligence and became army commander when Brig. Gen. Omar Torrijos Herrera was killed in a helicopter crash in 1981. The Administration official expressed concern that the intelligence information would damage relations with Panama if it was seen as reflecting the views of the White House. Advertisement Continue reading the main story Officials in the Reagan Administration and past Administrations said in interviews that they had overlooked General Noriega's illegal activities because of his cooperation with American intelligence and his willingness to permit the American military extensive leeway to operate in Panama. They said, for example, that General Noriega had been a valuable asset to Washington in countering insurgencies in Central America and was now cooperating with the Central Intelligence Agency in providing sensitive information from Nicaragua. But many Reagan Administration officials made clear in interviews that the extent of General Noriega's activities was seen as a potential national security threat because of the strategic importance of Panama and the Panama Canal. Under treaties negotiated with General Torrijos in the 1970's, the United States has agreed to turn the canal over to Panama in the year 2000. ''It's precisely because we have long-term strategic interests in Panama, with the canal, that it's important to have reliable people we can deal with,'' a senior American diplomat said. In addition, Panama has become increasingly important for the United States and its monitoring of insurgencies in Central America. General's Activities: 'Legal and Illegal' A recent classified report by the Defense Intelligence Agency concludes that General Noriega, operating through a small band of top associates in the military, maintains tight control of drug and money-laundering activities by his associates in the Panama Defense Force, according to one American official. The study said the general was ''deeply involved in legal and illegal business.'' ''Nothing moves in Panama without the instructions, order and consent of Noriega,'' the official said. According to the State Department, White House, Pentagon and intelligence sources, there has been longstanding evidence among intelligence officials of General Noriega's activities, including his relations with the Cuban Government and his willingness to sell arms to the M-19 rebel group in Colombia. The goal of M-19, which is pro-Cuban, is to overthrow the democratically elected Government. Over the years, the guerrilla group has been responsible for violent attacks that have led to hundreds of deaths. Advertisement Continue reading the main story Of the assertion on the M-19 guerrillas, Captain Lim Yueng, the Panamanian Army spokesman, said: ''We have no information on M-19. We do all we can to avoid Panama being used as a trampoline for terrorism.'' The captain also denied any Cuban intelligence efforts in Panama or that General Noriega was involved in any shady activities with Cuba. He also denied any export of embargoed goods to Cuba. ''Cuba has an embassy here and normal relations with us like many countries,'' the captain said. He added, ''We've captured drugs here, and are doing our best to collaborate with the United States to fight narcotraffic in Panama.'' 'A Critical Misjudgment' In Killing of a Critic What has come to be seen within the United States Government as the Noriega problem was heightened by recent intelligence directly tying the general and the top leadership of the Panama Defense Force to the slaying last September of Dr. Hugo Spadafora, one of the army's leading critics. In his statement, Captain Lim Yueng said: ''There is absolutely nothing in this case involving the army. Spadafora had many enemies. The institution of the armed forces absolutely denies any ties to the death of Spadafora. We criticize this crime.'' A classified Defense Intelligence Agency report on General Noriega described his involvement in the killing as ''a critical misjudgment'' on his part. The D.I.A. is also known to have intelligence demonstrating that General Noriega ordered the killing, according to an official with first-hand information. Dr. Spadafora's decapitated body was found stuffed in a United States mailbag in Costa Rica just across the Panamanian-Costa Rican border. The killing occurred a few weeks before General Noriega ousted the civilian President, Nicolas Ardito Barletta, who was about to name an investigating commission. Mr. Barletta was replaced by Eric Arturo Delvalle, who is viewed by American officials as another nominal leader, with the army commander actually in control of the country. Advertisement Continue reading the main story Some senior White House officials have privately been concerned about General Noriega's activities. Late last year Vice Adm. John M. Poindexter, then the newly appointed national security adviser, visited the general and privately told him to ''cut it out'' - alluding to the drug and money laundering activities and his close relations with Cuba, according to a Government official. Admiral Poindexter was later quoted as having raised questions about an alternative to the Panamanian general. The issue is a chronic one for American policy makers: how far to overlook corruption and a lack of democratic principles in allies in order to protect secret intelligence installations. Senior civilian officials in the Pentagon, headed by Nestor D. Sanchez, a former C.I.A. and White House aide for Latin American issues who is a Deputy Assistant Secretary of Defense for Inter-American Affairs, are known to be concerned that any successor to General Noriega might not be willing to tolerate the American military activities that now exist in Panama. In Panama, a Web Of U.S. Intelligence Since the early 1980's the National Security Agency, operating through its military components in the Army, the Navy and the Air Force, has vastly increased its intelligence-gathering activities in Panama. It is now capable of monitoring all of Central America and most of South America from its Panamanian installations. The Central Intelligence Agency has also used military bases in Panama, especially Howard Air Base near Panama City, as a jumping-off point for intelligence gathering and for agents sent to Nicaragua, according to intelligence officials. In interviews, Reagan Administration officials emphasized the nature of the evidence tying General Noriega and the top leadership of the Panama Defense Force to money-laundering and drug trafficking activities. One official who said he had extensively reviewed the most sensitive intelligence available to the American Government on General Noriega, including reports from agents and intercepts, described most of the specifics as ''having to do with gun and drug running.'' He said General Noriega's name appeared ''over and over'' in connection with specific dates, places and contacts in money-laundering and drug activities. Advertisement Continue reading the main story Much of the information, the sources acknowledged, has been gleaned from National Security Agency intercepts, among the most highly classified information in the Government. In interviews, intelligence officials repeatedly described General Noriega as brilliant in masking much of his direct involvement, preferring to operate through cutouts or as a secret partner in Panamanian trading companies and banks. An American official with long experience in Panamanian affairs noted that the general seemed to have become more circumspect in his pattern of activity in the early 1980's, moderating overt support for M-19 and direct financial involvement in drug activities.
