In the Bill Cosby sex assault trial, his lawyer, Brian McMonagle, delivered a lengthy and impassioned closing argument. He directly addressed the jury, stating, "I pray to God you’ll do your job better than I did mine." McMonagle acknowledged his own emotional delivery, saying, "If during the course of this trial I have said or done anything that might have offended one of you, hold it against me, not him." He emphasized his personal approach, noting, "I wear my emotions on my sleeve and that’s not good for a lawyer."

The defense's closing arguments followed a brief witness testimony, and the defense rested without Cosby taking the stand. McMonagle reminded the jury that the case was not prosecuted in 2005 when Andrea Constand first reported the assault. He argued this was because the initial case was too weak. "This is their evidence, that they gave you," he stated. "This is why this case went into a trash can because Mr. Cosby told them everything they needed to know." He then quoted Cosby's account: "'We laid down, we did what we normally do, I went to bed. I got up, she was up. I made her a muffin.'"

McMonagle also highlighted inconsistencies in Constand's timeline of the alleged assault. He told the jury, "If that doesn’t make you hesitate, I don’t know what will." Constand had initially told police the assault occurred in March 2004 after she was drugged. However, she later changed this to sometime in January. Furthermore, Constand had previously told police she had never been alone with Cosby before the incident, a claim she later admitted was untrue. "Be careful, be careful, a man’s life hangs on it," McMonagle warned the jury.

The defense's strategy throughout the trial was to portray Constand as pursuing a romantic relationship with Cosby. McMonagle argued that Constand called Cosby 53 times after the alleged assault. "Why are we running from the truth of this case, of the relationship? Why?" he questioned.

McMonagle also addressed Cosby's prior deposition testimony about obtaining Quaaludes decades ago. He downplayed this admission, exclaiming, "He’s talking about things that happened in 1976! That’s what was going on back in the day." The lawyer also directly attacked the credibility of both Constand and accuser Kelly Johnson, who testified that Cosby also allegedly drugged and assaulted her. McMonagle called them liars, stating, "When you do things this way, it all blows up. Are you kidding me? It’s not right." He concluded his argument by emphasizing the importance of truth, saying, "I don’t care where you’re from, who you like, if you think he’s the worst comedian in the world. This isn’t right. The truth becomes a lie and a lie becomes a truth. And who’s the worst off for it? He is." Prosecutors were scheduled to deliver their closing statements after the lunch break.

## Bill Cosby's Defense: A Consensual Relationship Amidst Sexual Assault Allegations

As Bill Cosby's sexual assault trial neared its end on Monday, June 12, 2017, his defense team presented a **critical argument** to the jury: the relationship between the comedian and accuser Andrea Constand was **consensual**, marked by "romantic interludes." Cosby himself **chose not to testify**, leaving his attorney, Brian McMonagle, to **shape the defense's narrative**. McMonagle asserted that while Cosby, then 79, had been unfaithful to his wife, Camille, his 2004 encounter with Constand was **not a criminal act**.

### Examining Phone Calls and Discrepancies in Testimony

A **key element** of the defense's strategy involved scrutinizing Constand's numerous phone calls to Cosby after the alleged incident. McMonagle presented these calls as evidence of an **ongoing relationship**, not the actions of a victim. Constand, who was the director of women's basketball operations at Temple University while Cosby was a trustee, maintained these calls were solely for university business. However, McMonagle **emphasized the significant length** of one call, lasting 49 minutes, suggesting it indicated a personal connection. "This isn't talking to a trustee. This is talking to a lover," he argued, **challenging the prosecution's portrayal** of their relationship.

McMonagle also aimed to highlight **perceived inconsistencies** in Constand's account. He presented evidence from three interviews she gave to police after coming forward, noting discrepancies in her timeline of the alleged assault, with initial accounts suggesting it occurred in either March or January 2004. Furthermore, he pointed out that Constand initially told police she had **never been alone with Cosby** prior to the incident, only to later admit to spending time with him privately. Cosby's own account to police indicated three prior romantic encounters, which he described as instances where he brushed her leg or held her face. Constand, however, maintained that she had **rebuffed his advances** on two occasions. McMonagle questioned the jury, "Why on earth would you go to the Foxwoods casino in Connecticut, into a man's bedroom, after a man unbuttons your pants and puts his hands down your pants?"

### The High Stakes and Cosby's Deposition Testimony

The stakes were **undeniably high** for Bill Cosby, who faced the possibility of spending the remainder of his life in prison if convicted. Camille Cosby, married to Bill for 53 years, was present in the courtroom for the first time during the six-day trial, maintaining a **stoic demeanor**. Constand, in contrast, remained impassive during McMonagle's two-hour presentation.

Although Cosby declined to testify, the prosecution was expected to reference his deposition statements. In his 2005 sworn testimony, Cosby admitted to obtaining prescriptions for quaaludes in the 1970s and offering the sedatives to women he intended to have sexual relations with. He also stated that he gave Constand three half-tablets of Benadryl before the "petting" began. Prosecutors, however, suggested that he had administered a **more potent substance** to incapacitate her.

Andrea Constand, 44, had testified earlier in the week, detailing her account of the alleged assault. She claimed that Cosby administered three blue pills to her before penetrating her while she was in a state of paralysis and semi-consciousness. Following this event, Constand initiated a lawsuit against Cosby after prosecutors initially declined to press charges in 2005. Portions of Cosby's deposition testimony in her civil suit were unsealed in 2015, prompting a **renewed investigation** and Cosby's formal charges approximately 18 months before the trial.

The legal proceedings and a wave of similar accusations from approximately 60 other women have **significantly tarnished** Cosby's carefully cultivated public image. McMonagle stressed to the jury that the case was not merely about financial repercussions but about Cosby's **future freedom**. "This is not a civil case about money, money, money. We're talking about all the man's tomorrows," he declared.

For ongoing updates, historical context, visual materials, and an in-depth audio exploration of the Bill Cosby trial, please visit www.apnews.com/tag/CosbyonTrial.

query: What sport did actress Christine Taylor play during her childhood in Pennsylvania?

documents.text: Before They Were Stars HOT GALLERY: Who Are These Sporty Stars? It’s a rite of passage: playing sports in high school. Even some of the biggest Hollywood celebs did it, back before they were stars—though some might prefer to forget the uniforms. Can you guess who these sporty stars are? Nina Hämmerling Smith By

Al Bello/Getty Images Christine Taylor, husband Ben Stiller and friend Owen Wilson watch the U.S. Open in September. Christine Taylor, husband Ben Stiller and friend Owen Wilson watch the U.S. Open in September. Actress Christine Taylor always loved tennis, playing as a child growing up in Pennsylvania. When she married actor/director Ben Stiller in 2000, she wanted him to love the sport also. "It started out for him loving tennis by default because it was always on our television," said Taylor, who started acting in 1989 and is known for roles in "The Brady Bunch Movie" and "Dodgeball: A True Underdog Story," among others. "I begged him to take me to Wimbledon for my birthday a few years ago and we did. And this past U.S. Open, he was screaming at the finals harder than me!" Now with two children -- Ella, 9, and Quinlin, 7, and living in New York City, Taylor is hoping to spread the word about the sport. She recently was named the spokeswoman for the USTA's 10 and Under Tennis program as the organization launches the March into Tennis, a monthlong drive to get more children to play the sport. "I started playing when I was young, and now that I'm a mom, I want them to play," said Taylor, who met her husband while making a TV pilot in 1999. "It was so inspiring for me to watch tennis growing up. I thought I was really good playing, until my brother told me I wasn't!" March Into Tennis coincides with Monday's "Tennis Night in America," which will feature Roger Federer, Andy Roddick, Maria Sharapova and Caroline Wozniacki squaring off in a battle of former world No. 1s in Madison Square Garden. Live coverage of the matches begins at 7 p.m. on ESPN3. A tape-delayed broadcast of the event will air at 11:30 p.m. on ESPN2. Taylor will be there to show her support for her 10 and Under venture and then watch some tennis. "We want to encourage the young ones to learn and get some confidence in sports," Taylor said. "It's fun and keeps you active and moving." Taylor said it has worked for her children. "They are out there having fun, even if they're just hitting the ball in the driveway," Taylor said. "You've never met my kids. But if they weren't having fun, my life would be miserable!"

Although Anna Wintour told R.J. Cutler that she wanted a better backhand, apparently she could improve her "spin," too, according to her instructor at the Midtown Tennis Club, the subject of a piece in today's Times. Apparently THE place to go for tennis in Manhattan — despite the fact that "the space between the baseline and the wall is infamously cramped," ugh — Wintour plays there regularly, along with other fashion people like Sean Avery, Hilary Rhoda, and Vanity Fair's Jessica Diehl. Anna is quite skilled, of course, says her wonderfully named instructor, Byron Spence: "She’s like a retriever," he explains. "She’s so fast. She’ll chase down everything. She doesn’t hit with a lot of spin, but she has an amazing cross-court forehand." Meanwhile, he says that her colleague Jillian Demling "is pretty good" but "could be great, if she was able to cut down on her hours at Vogue." #Excuses.

Prince William and the Duchess of Cambridge, née Kate Middleton, lingered after the intense final to engage in conversation with the Serbian athlete.

The esteemed sportsman initiated the exchange by expressing his gratitude to the royal couple for their presence throughout the match. The Duchess of Cambridge, a known aficionado of the sport, responded with genuine warmth, stating, "How could we leave?"

"Very keen, I'm slightly out of practice since having George," she admitted, noting that she and Prince William maintain a "quite competitive game."

When Prince William playfully inquired about their competitive dynamic, Kate indicated that their matches are "even." Djokovic then turned his attention to the Duchess' sister, Pippa Middleton, also a keen sportswoman and a regular attendee at Wimbledon, a family tradition. The conversation also touched upon Djokovic's personal life, with the expectant father being asked about his fiancée, who was not present for the gripping men's final. "She's six months pregnant," he shared, adding that she was watching the emotionally charged match from home and that he would be joining her shortly.

Later, the Wimbledon champion shared a heartwarming photograph on social media featuring himself with his expectant wife and their two pets, accompanied by the caption, "My precious little family :) #WimbledonChampion #Love."

## Matthew Perry on "Go On": Finding Humor and Humanity in Grief

Matthew Perry, star of the new NBC sitcom *Go On*, believes that great comedy often stems from "lovable losers sitting in a room together." This sentiment, he explains, is central to his new role as Ryan King, a sportscaster grappling with the sudden loss of his wife. The show, Perry suggests, adds a crucial element to the classic adage "Comedy equals tragedy plus time": the cathartic power of group therapy. This blend, he feels, creates a narrative that is both deeply moving and unexpectedly funny.

Perry, who plays the 42-year-old King, notes that audiences connect with characters who are relatable in their struggles, rather than idealized figures. He draws parallels to beloved sitcoms like *Taxi*, *Cheers*, and *Friends*, where the shared vulnerabilities of the characters fostered a strong audience connection. *Go On* premieres tonight at 9 E.T. on NBC, and in anticipation, Perry sat down to discuss authentic expression, redefining success in midlife, and the societal pressures men face to maintain a stoic facade.

### The Reluctant Journey to Connection

**Men's Health:** Your character, Ryan King, in *Go On*, faces significant emotional adversity and appears resistant to confronting it.

**Matthew Perry:** His journey is undeniably one of reluctance, a sentiment many of us can identify with. The pilot episode derives much of its comedic tension from his pretense of well-being, his assertion that all is copacetic. However, by the conclusion of the inaugural episode, he acknowledges his imperative need for a support network, a realization that is seldom easily embraced. The fundamental human requirement for interpersonal connection is underscored.

### Humor as a Shield: Masculinity and Vulnerability

**Men's Health:** Ryan exhibits a defense mechanism of humor, a reluctance towards vulnerability, and a performative demeanor that masks his pain. This resonates with a significant demographic of men. What contributes to the perceived difficulty in contemporary masculinity?

**Matthew Perry:** The precise etiology remains elusive, yet the challenge is palpable. I am, however, gratified that the archetypal "John Wayne" stoicism is largely relegated to the past; it's a paradigm I could never authentically embody. Were that the prevailing expectation, my professional prospects would be considerably diminished.

### The Power of Transparency

**Men's Health:** Your performances consistently elicit a strong audience affinity. What do you believe accounts for this widespread support for you and the characters you portray?

**Matthew Perry:** I tend to be transparent with my emotions, a trait that I believe permeates my characterizations. There's an inherent yearning or vulnerability that emanates from me, which audiences find relatable. This was particularly evident in my portrayal of Chandler Bing on *Friends*; he articulated sentiments that individuals often harbor but hesitate to vocalize. This tendency to externalize internal thoughts and feelings, even when others might refrain, is a characteristic that transcends my personal identity and informs the roles I undertake.

**Men's Health:** That approach to life can be perceived as precarious.

**Matthew Perry:** Indeed. [Laughs] It is, I concede. I am, by most accounts, an open book.

### From Class Clown to Professional Comedian

**Men's Health:** Having been in the industry for an extended period, when did you first recognize the utility of humor as a form of social currency?

**Matthew Perry:** This realization dawned during my formative school years. I was prone to jesting and engaging in physical comedy, seeking positive reinforcement. The act of eliciting laughter, particularly from my peers, was profoundly gratifying. By the age of fifteen, I began to apprehend the potential for a professional trajectory in comedy, the prospect of remuneration for making people laugh struck me as extraordinary.

### The Shift from Competition to Collective Success

**Men's Health:** You were a proficient tennis player in your youth before dedicating yourself to acting. What parallels, if any, exist between the disciplines of tennis and the entertainment industry?

**Matthew Perry:** The transition from tennis was not a deliberate choice. I achieved a considerable level of proficiency in tennis in Ottawa, Canada, attaining national rankings by thirteen. Upon relocating to Los Angeles at fifteen, I encountered a significantly higher caliber of competition, which rendered my previous achievements less consequential. My standing in Canada was substantial; in Los Angeles, it was considerably less so. This disparity led me to conclude that a professional tennis career was improbable, prompting my pivot to acting. The correlation between tennis and acting is debatable, though both are intensely cerebral pursuits demanding significant mental fortitude. I aspire to have shed the intense competitiveness I possessed as a tennis player as I've matured. In my twenties, my focus was on outperforming others; now, my aspiration is for collective success.

**Men's Health:** How does this shift in perspective occur?

**Matthew Perry:** It is an evolution intrinsically linked to aging. Youthful aspirations often center on external achievements as panaceas for life's challenges. The belief that specific accomplishments will usher in universal contentment is a common misconception. As one matures, the realization dawns that such external markers do not inherently resolve all difficulties. While success is a desirable and fortunate outcome, with advancing years, a desire for the well-being and success of others often emerges.

**Men's Health:** So, what is the ultimate "fix" for life's complexities?

**Matthew Perry:** [Laughs] As Jack Palance sagely remarked in *City Slickers*, "That's something you've gotta figure out for yourself."

query: What happened to Vera Smith's body after Hurricane Katrina, and who took action to ensure she was buried?

documents.text: For five days after Hurricane Katrina struck, the body of Vera Smith lay where it fell, alone on the roadside, partly covered by a tarpaulin. Eventually – with the authorities apparently having neither the resources or inclination to remove the corpse – a local man buried her under a makeshift grave. He said prayers, fashioned a cross and placed a plastic sheet over the soil on to which someone painted the words: “Here Lies Vera. God Help Us”. Ten years after the storm that killed 1,833 people and whose destruction topped $100bn, the death of 66-year Vera Smith and the utter break-down of authority symbolised by her abandonment, continue to haunt the city. There are some in New Orleans – many of whose residents have an intense relationship with the dead – who literally believe they have felt her spirit. Vera Smith, in an undated photograph provided by her family Visiting the junction of Magazine Street and Jackson Avenue in the Garden District today, it is hard to imagine the scenes that played out ten years ago in the chaotic aftermath of the storm, when Ms Smith had gone to a local shop to try and find supplies for her and her partner, Max Keene. Across the US and around the world, people watched stunned and outraged as the media carried images of the seemingly countless corpses left stranded by the storm. They lay rotting on the roadside, floating in floodwater, hanging from wires. The US government appeared both hapless and heartless, failing to come to the aid of some of its most most vulnerable people, a large percentage of whom were black. Television viewers could not believe that what they were watching was happening in their country, rather than somewhere in the distant developing world. President Barack Obama, who will this week visit the city to mark the anniversary, said there had been a “shameful breakdown in government.” More than 1,800 were killed by Katrina and the flooding it triggered Ms Smith did not die in the actual storm but was killed in the lawless aftermath, apparently the victim of a hit and run accident, the perpetrator of which was never found. Her frail partner – who passed away the following year - did the little he could to provide some dignity to her body. After five days of intense heat, someone decided more serious was required. That person was John Lee, an engineer and businessman who has a home in the Garden District. Over a bottle of root beer in Stein’s Deli, located on Magazine Street, Mr Lee said he had gone to the local police station and asked them to remove her body. Mr Lee said they not only declined to do so, but also refused him permission to move it. Eventually, they said he could bury it but could not move it. As a result, he dug the grave on the actual pavement. Mr Lee has written a book of his experiences, Our Sleepless Nights: Surviving Katrina and Burying Miss Vera. In it he details the break-down of authority and the looming fear that permeated – some of it exacerbated by inaccurate reports about looting – in the ten days after Katrina. But his book is also testimony to the acts of quiet heroism that took place, the coming together of people, often strangers, to try and help one another. John Lee led efforts to build the makeshift grave for Vera Smith “I think of her often,” Mr Lee said of Ms Smith, whom he used to see around the neighbourhood. “I do have faith. I do wish she is at rest.” He said he remembered the prayers that he and two black women – people he had met that day – spoke as they buried her. “We were praying for her soul.” Ms Smith, whose full name was Elvira Briones Smith, was born in Mexico and grew in Texas before she moved to New Orleans. She was married four time has two adult daughters. For 20 years or more she lived with CN Keene, better known as Max. She liked clothes and shoes, shopping and drinking, and was a regular at the local Catholic church, St Mary’s Assumption. They had met when she was working as a waitress in a bar and he was working off-shore for an oil company. “She was married, her old man left her. I had a different girlfriend then, she left me. It was the right time. We just got together. Every now and then it happens that way,” Mr Keene told The Independent ten years ago. “We used to lie in bed. I’d drink bourbon, she’d read books.” Large parts of New Orleans were inundated by flooding Ms Smith’s family learned of her death from the front page of a Texas newspaper. Three months after Katrina, Ms Smith’s body was eventually collected, taken to a mortuary and her ashes returned to her family in Santa Rosa. In November 2005, they were interred in a family plot. Ms Smith’s daughter, Cindy Briones, said her mother had returned to visit the family every year for Thanksgiving Day. She said she was also delighted to be among her relatives and spend weeks in advance of her visit buying special gifts for each of them. Speaking from Santa Rosa, she said she was grateful to Mr Lee and the others who had built the makeshift grave, but said it had been important for the family to have her returned to Texas. She said she and her sister remained traumatised by what happened to their mother and the their emotions had been stretched every year on the anniversary of her death. “I still have flash-backs. I have seen her body on the internet, pictures of before it was buried,” she said. This image - believed to be of Vera Smith - was left at the grave In some limited respects, Ms Smith’s family was fortunate in that they were able to locate and recover her body. Even today, some of those killed by the storm remain unidentified and unclaimed. Asked what officials had used to identify the remains, the office of Jeffrey Rouse, the coroner for New Orleans, said their work made use of dental records, DNA testing, plane manifests, unique scars and tattoos, follow-ups on missing persons cases, jewellery, unusual hair, finger-prints, medical implants and government identification. In the City Park neighbourhood, a memorial to Katrina, designed in the shape that the Category Five storm assumed as it made landfall, was erected in the memory of those who died. Even today, the remains of 30 people who died during Katrina have not been identified Despite the effort of the coroner’s office, the remains of 30 people were not identified while a total of 43 were never claimed. “The memorial received the remains of the unidentified or unclaimed,” read the words on a black granite stone. Jayson Orlando, who works at the nearby Sacred Grinds coffee shop, said New Orleans was less care-free since Katrina hit. “Even cities feel trauma,” he said. “It’s not just people.” Since 2005, several memorials have also been erected in the memory of Ms Smith. The one that currently stands attached to a wall was built by an artist, Simon Hardeveld, who is originally French, and metal worker Scott Evert. Mr Hardeveld, whose memorial features a working fountain and bright colours, said he had been asked to make it two years ago by the owner of an adjoining burger restaurant, built on the site where Ms Smith’s grave had stood. He had contacted him amid reports that the restaurant was haunted. The reports were taken so seriously that a local television channel dispatched a film crew. “This is New Orleans. People believe these things,” he said with a shrug. Blaine Presenbach built a restaurant on the plot where the makeshift grave had stood Blaine Presenbach, 36, the owner of the Charcoal restaurant, said some of his workers had joked after the “ghost of Vera” when a piece of equipment broke down. But he said nobody had really believed it. Rather, he had commissioned the memorial out of respect for Ms Smith and what had befallen her at that location. “We don’t want to forget it,” he said. Mr Evert, the man who did the metal work for the memorial, heard the stories too and was unsure what to think. And then he set about the task of shaping the memorial. “It was weird. I don’t really believe this sort of thing,” he said. “But the job became really tedious and difficult.” He said that each point in the job, something went wrong. Each star – something that would normally attach simply – fell off. “It seemed to me she did not like what I was doing,” he said. Simon Hardeveld and Scott Evert at the memorial they built Mr Evert said he too felt the trauma of having lived through the storm, having used a boat to help rescue stranded people and remembering the terrible scenes. Even now it upset him to recall what happened to his city, to people such as Ms Smith. He likened the memorial built by himself to small shrines he had seen when he visited Thailand, a place where a spirit might sit. “The point we wanted to make was that we wanted to remember Vera,” he added. “It was just to say ‘this was someone who lived here and something terrible happened to her and people think she was worth remembering’.”

Photo by Willie Allen Jr./St. Petersburg Times via ZUMA Wire On the fifth anniversary of Hurricane Katrina, in 2010, New Orleans Mayor Mitch Landrieu preached unity. “With the rising water, differences and divisions were washed away,” he said, asking the audience to listen to each other, and embrace their common aspirations. “We will hear and we will learn the beautiful truth that Katrina taught us all,” he declared, “We are all the same.” Jamelle Bouie Jamelle Bouie is Slate’s chief political correspondent. With this, Landrieu invoked our national memory of the hurricane—a catastrophe that devastated New Orleans for all of its residents. In his own address on the fifth anniversary, President Obama struck a similar tone, with a message of rebuilding and harmony. “Five years ago we saw men and women risking their own safety to save strangers. We saw nurses staying behind to care for the sick and the injured. We saw families coming home to clean up and rebuild—not just their own homes, but their neighbors’ homes, as well.” Advertisement With the 10-year anniversary this week—Katrina’s storm surge breached the levees a decade ago on Saturday—we’ll soon see similar rhetoric from politicians and those seeking to pay respect to the storm’s victims. Hurricane Katrina was one of the worst disasters in American history: It killed more than 1,800 Americans, displaced tens of thousands more, and destroyed huge swaths of New Orleans. While the government couldn’t stop the storm, it could have prepared for the damage. But it didn’t. The days and weeks after Katrina were marked with scandalous mismanagement, as the federal government made history with its incompetence and failure. Thousands of New Orleans residents who weren’t evacuated and couldn’t escape the city were left with inadequate aid and shelter, all but abandoned by officials who couldn’t, or in some cases wouldn’t, help them. In our current remembrance, Katrina is a synonym for dysfunction and disaster. In our current remembrance, Katrina is a synonym for dysfunction and disaster, a prime example of when government fails in the worst way possible. It’s also a symbol of political collapse. George Bush never recovered from its failure, and “Brownie, you’re doing a heck of a job” stands with “Mission Accomplished” as one of the defining lines of the administration and the era. But there’s a problem with this capsule summary of Katrina and its place in national memory. It assumes a singular public of “Americans” who understand events in broadly similar ways. This public doesn’t exist. Instead, in the United States, we have multiple publics defined by a constellation of different boundaries: Geographic, religious, economic, ethnic, and racial. With regards to race, we have two dominant publics: A white one and a black one. Each of them saw Katrina in competing, mutually exclusive ways. And the disaster still haunts black political consciousness in ways that most white Americans have never been able to acknowledge. White Americans saw the storm and its aftermath as a case of bad luck and unprecedented incompetence that spread its pain across the Gulf Coast regardless of race. This is the narrative you see in Landrieu’s words and, to some extent, Obama’s as well. To black Americans, however, this wasn’t an equal opportunity disaster. To them, it was confirmation of America’s indifference to black life. “We have an amazing tolerance for black pain,” said Rev. Jesse Jackson in an interview after the storm. Rev. Al Sharpton, also echoed the mood among many black Americans: “I feel that, if it was in another area, with another economic strata and racial makeup, that President Bush would have run out of Crawford a lot quicker and FEMA would have found its way in a lot sooner.” Even more blunt was rapper Kanye West, who famously told a live national television audience that “George Bush doesn’t care about black people.” Photo by STR/Reuters Advertisement In his book Is This America? Katrina as Cultural Trauma, sociologist Ron Eyerman writes that Katrina “left an indelible mark on American collective memory, not merely as a severe storm but as a social disaster, a catastrophe which revealed the nation at its worst.” For blacks, this was especially true. In barbershops, at church, and around dinner tables, black people discussed the awful images from New Orleans, where black survivors begged for help from rooftops, and where black bodies floated through streets. When we look at the first 15 years of the 21st century, the most defining moment in black America’s relationship to its country isn’t Election Day 2008; it’s Hurricane Katrina. The events of the storm and its aftermath sparked a profound shift among black Americans toward racial pessimism that persists to today, even with Barack Obama in the White House. Black collective memory of Hurricane Katrina, as much as anything else, informs the present movement against police violence, “Black Lives Matter.” Among the first images of New Orleans after the storm were shots of low-income black Americans, stranded and desperate to escape the floods and debris. In the narrow sense, they were there because the city’s evacuation plan—which didn’t account for massive traffic out of the region—fell apart. Rather than bring remaining New Orleansians out, officials sent them to the Superdome and the convention center, which were quickly overcrowded and undersupplied. In a much broader sense, however, they were there because in a city defined by decades of poverty, segregation, and deep disenfranchisement, poor and working-class blacks (including the elderly, and children) would largely shoulder the burden of the storm. To black Americans around the country, this looked like neglect. In an ABC News and Washington Post poll taken shortly after the hurricane, 71 percent of blacks said that New Orleans would have been “better prepared” if it were a “wealthier city with more whites,” and 76 percent said the federal government would have “responded faster.” A Newsweek poll confirmed this sense among black Americans that the government responded slowly because most of the affected people were black. “I, to this day, believe that if that would have happened in Orange County, California, if that would have happened in South Beach, Miami, it would have been a different response,” said then Mayor Ray Nagin in a speech to the National Association of Black Journalists, a year after the storm. Photo by Mario Tama/Getty Images Advertisement One study—“Feeling the Pain of My People: Hurricane Katrina, Racial Inequality, and the Psyche of Black America”—tests the claim that blacks had a heightened and racialized response to Katrina. The authors conclude that “Despite geographic and socioeconomic differences, black Americans throughout the country … identified with Hurricane Katrina’s primarily African American victims.” More so than white Americans, they write, “blacks were deeply angered and depressed by the events surrounding the hurricane.” The disaster of Hurricane Katrina, and its impact on the collective experience of black America, sowed the ground for a reckoning. Support for the racialized view of Katrina can be seen in the way news outlets and commentators talked about the victims. One study, published a year after the storm, found a connection between mentions of race in news stories and references to survivors as “refugees,” a description opposed by many black commentators at the time. Analyzing stories from the period, researchers found exaggerated claims of violence among Katrina victims, as well as grossly inaccurate reports of crime and disorder. In one infamous example of skewed coverage, an Associated Press photo shows a young black man wading through water that has risen to his chest. He’s holding a case of soda and pulling a bag. The caption says he had just been “looting a grocery store.” A second photo, this time from AFP/Getty Images, shows a white couple in the same situation. The caption says they are shown “after finding bread and soda from a local grocery store.” Even worse than this were comments from conservative commentators like Bill O’Reilly of Fox News, who denounced the “poor in New Orleans” as “drug addicted” and “thugs”—with the inescapable conclusion being that they deserved their fates. It should be said that black alienation wasn’t inevitable. Even with the polls and the coverage, views may have changed if white Americans also perceived black treatment during and after the storm as unfair and racially biased. But that’s not what happened. Photo by Mario Tama/Getty Images Instead, white Americans discounted claims of racial bias. According to the Pew Research Center, 77 percent of whites said the government response would have been the same if the victims had been white, and only 32 percent said the event showed that racial inequality was still a major problem (for blacks, 66 percent believed the response would have been faster had the victims been mainly white and 71 percent said it demonstrated racial inequality). Other polls confirmed these findings. Advertisement In addition to disbelief that Katrina was a racial story, research and polling also showed a white public that held survivors in contempt. In a 2006 study that examined white and black attitudes toward Katrina victims, political scientists Leonie Huddy and Stanley Feldman found that 65 percent of white respondents blamed residents and the mayor for being trapped in New Orleans. In a CNN/USA Today survey, half of all whites said that people who broke into stores and took things were “mostly criminals,” compared to 77 percent of blacks who said they were “mostly desperate people” trying to find a way to survive. (Pew had similar findings.) If you turned to right-wing media, you’d find unvarnished disdain for those left behind in the city. The idea that black Americans had a legitimate grievance was dismissed. The result was a collapse in black racial optimism. The year before Katrina, according to Gallup, 68 percent of blacks said race relations were either “somewhat good” or “very good.” The year after Katrina, that declined to 62 percent. The next year, it declined to 55 percent, the lowest point of the decade. In broader surveys from the Pew Research Center, the period after Katrina is an inflection point, where the percentage of blacks who say they are worse off finally overtakes the percentage who say their lives have improved. Black optimism stayed on a downward trajectory for the three years after Katrina. In another Gallup trend-line, black satisfaction with society dips from a steady 41 percent in 2005, to 37 percent in 2006, to 30 percent in 2007. With the recession, there was every sign these trends would continue. But then, Barack Obama made his historic run for the White House, a tangible sign of racial progress. Public opinion tells the story. In June 2007, just 11 percent of black Americans said they were satisfied with the state of the nation and 30 percent said they were satisfied with the way blacks were treated. Just four months before the election, 78 percent of blacks said racism was widespread. A year later, blacks were far rosier about the future. “Despite the bad economy, blacks’ assessments about the state of black progress in America have improved more dramatically during the past two years than at any time in the past quarter century,” noted Pew, in a survey that showed black optimism at a high of 39 percent, nearly double what it was two years earlier. The black/white gap in how race relations are viewed also narrowed considerably in the immediate aftermath of Obama’s election. There is a natural question in all of this. If Barack Obama sparked such a significant shift in black opinion, isn’t his election the key point in black political consciousness and not Katrina? The answer is straightforward: No, because the euphoria didn’t last. Photo by Carlos Barria/Reuters Since 2012, but especially since last year, a rapid and steady succession of high profile shootings has moved black opinion to its pre-Obama, post-Katrina state of pessimism. Between the incidents and the outcomes—where shooters escape with little punishment or sanction—blacks have become more pessimistic about their place in American life. From 2009 to 2013, notes a Pew survey taken on the 50th anniversary of the March on Washington, the percentage of blacks who felt a sense of progress dropped from 39 percent to 26 percent. As of this summer, reports the New York Times, just 28 percent of black Americans say race relations are generally good, which is in line with where they were in the months before Obama’s election.

Hurricane Katrina's devastating arrival on August 29, 2005, serves as a potent and enduring symbol of nature's immense power and its capacity to reshape not only landscapes but also societal structures. The storm's relentless fury culminated in catastrophic flooding across New Orleans, a direct consequence of widespread levee failures. This natural disaster inflicted over $100 billion in damages and tragically claimed the lives of nearly 2,000 individuals. To this day, Katrina is indelibly marked in historical records as the costliest and the third-deadliest hurricane to ever make landfall in the United States. This week, President Obama's visit to New Orleans marks a profoundly significant moment: the 10th anniversary of Hurricane Katrina, a solemn occasion that also highlights the city's extraordinary and inspiring journey of recovery and rebuilding.

However, beyond the tangible reconstruction of roads, bridges, and buildings, an equally profound, though often less visible, transformation has taken root within the city's educational landscape. It is a poignant and almost ironic twist of fate that such widespread destruction became the unexpected catalyst for an educational renaissance. From the very ashes of Katrina's devastation has emerged one of the most significant and far-reaching overhauls witnessed in American public education in recent history. This phenomenon has even garnered recognition among education reformers, who now refer to the transformative power of such crises in fostering innovation and change as the "Katrina effect."

In the immediate and chaotic aftermath of Hurricane Katrina, the physical fabric of New Orleans was irrevocably altered. A vast majority of the city's schools were literally submerged beneath floodwaters, rendering them inoperable and forcing them to remain closed for many months. This profound disruption to the educational infrastructure compelled countless families to flee their homes, seeking safety, refuge, and a fresh start in new communities, with many never ultimately returning to their beloved city. The students who remained in New Orleans faced extended and prolonged absences from their formal education, missing crucial months of learning and academic development.

Yet, it is important to acknowledge that even before Katrina's catastrophic arrival, the inherent shortcomings and systemic failures of New Orleans' public school system were widely recognized and discussed. As far back as 2003, a full two years prior to the hurricane's landfall, the Louisiana Legislature had proactively established the statewide Recovery School District (RSD). This district was created with the explicit and ambitious mission of intervening in and revitalizing the state's most underperforming schools. By the time Katrina unleashed its fury, five struggling New Orleans schools had already been transitioned into charter schools under the nascent guidance and oversight of the RSD.

The unprecedented scale of the natural disaster presented state leaders with a critical and defining juncture. Rather than simply focusing their efforts on the monumental task of rebuilding what had been physically destroyed, they made a bold and visionary choice to fundamentally reimagine the future of New Orleans' entire educational ecosystem. Driven by an acute sense of urgency and a commitment to a new vision, they swiftly enacted a transformative piece of legislation. This measure led to the transfer of over 100 chronically failing New Orleans schools to the jurisdiction of the RSD. This decisive action resulted in the closure of many existing city schools and, controversially, the dismissal of all teachers from their positions. In lieu of reopening these same underperforming institutions, a comprehensive and innovative network of entirely new charter schools was authorized and subsequently established. This ambitious initiative successfully attracted a new cohort of dedicated and forward-thinking educators, ultimately paving the way for the development of the innovative "portfolio" system of schools that now defines the educational landscape of New Orleans. Today, an impressive 92 percent of students in the city attend independent, charter schools, a testament to the scale of this transformation.

The RSD's strategic and decisive response to the profound crisis precipitated by Hurricane Katrina has undeniably become a landmark achievement in the broader narrative of American education reform. The academic outcomes for public school students in New Orleans have experienced a dramatic and measurable improvement in the decade that has elapsed since the hurricane's passage. In 2005, New Orleans was infamously ranked as the second-lowest-performing school district within one of the nation's lowest-ranked states for educational achievement. Today, students in New Orleans are actively and successfully closing the persistent achievement gap with their peers across the country. Graduation rates have soared to unprecedented levels, accompanied by significant and sustained gains in standardized test scores, and a growing proportion of students are now pursuing higher education opportunities. The progress is not merely anecdotal; it is quantifiable. In 2008, a mere 30 percent of RSD schools were performing above the failing status; by 2014, this figure had surged dramatically to over 80 percent, representing a truly remarkable and rapid turnaround. It is widely acknowledged that no other school district in the United States has achieved such substantial and widespread improvements in academic performance within such a compressed and challenging timeframe. This profound educational revolution, born from unimaginable tragedy, has provided a powerful beacon of hope and a compelling model for transforming chronically failing schools across the nation.

The devastating storm and its far-reaching aftermath accomplished what no amount of political will or dedicated education advocacy had previously managed to undertake. It presented the nation with a clear, albeit tragic, direction for fundamentally transforming struggling schools. However, this potent transformative potential has not been universally embraced or replicated. For instance, leaders in California have yet to implement a similar statewide initiative, such as a hypothetical "Golden State Recovery School District," to address the thousands of schools in their state that are, in a metaphorical sense, still "underwater" due to persistent underperformance. In a striking example of grassroots activism, parents at Anaheim's Palm Lane School, leveraging California's controversial "parent trigger" law, attempted to convert their struggling school into a charter. Their determined efforts have been met with fierce and organized opposition from entrenched school district leaders who appear determined to maintain the status quo, even in the face of demonstrably failing schools. This situation underscores a critical and often overlooked point: it should not require a catastrophic natural disaster to catalyze meaningful and necessary change in underperforming educational institutions. This week's solemn commemoration of Hurricane Katrina serves as a powerful and timely reminder that such profound transformations are not only entirely possible but have, in fact, been demonstrably achieved, offering a blueprint for a brighter educational future.

*Gloria Romero is a staff opinion columnist, a dedicated education reformer, and a former Democratic state senator representing Los Angeles.*

Ten years after Hurricane Katrina, Houston, Texas, became a significant refuge for those displaced by the storm. A large number of the over 250,000 evacuees who fled to Houston have made it their permanent home, transforming the city into a new center for a displaced population. This climate-driven migration is one of the most profound population shifts in modern U.S. history, comparable to the Dust Bowl migrations. While many never returned to their original homes, and some settled in other cities, Houston absorbed the largest and most lasting influx of evacuees. This sprawling Texas metropolis, just 350 miles west of New Orleans, became a sanctuary and a permanent home for many uprooted by Katrina.

As New Orleans marks the 10th anniversary of Katrina, many former residents are still absent, their lives permanently changed. Tens of thousands traded the historic city for Houston's vast, car-dependent suburbs, seeking stability and opportunity. Houston's metropolitan area, now home to six million, is significantly larger than greater New Orleans. Current estimates suggest between 75,000 and 100,000 of the 250,000 evacuees have permanently settled in Houston, becoming an integral part of the city.

Mtangulizi Sanyika, an academic and Katrina survivor who relocated to Houston, now leads the New Orleans Association of Houston. He aptly calls Houston "New Orleans West," highlighting the strong cultural ties and the significant presence of former New Orleanians. New Orleans Mayor Mitch Landrieu has actively engaged with displaced residents, visiting Houston and Atlanta. These visits express gratitude, showcase New Orleans' recovery, and invite former residents to return, emphasizing available housing. New Orleans experienced a population decline exceeding 50% between 2000 and 2006. Its current population of approximately 384,000 is 79% of its pre-Katrina numbers. The African American community saw a notable reduction of about 30%, with approximately 97,000 individuals not returning.

Despite New Orleans' revitalization, economic incentives for many to return remain limited, posing a challenge to full recovery. Data from the Texas Organizing Project shows a persistent 9.5% unemployment rate among Black Texans, reflecting economic realities in their new environment. A 2011 Loyola University study revealed that 52% of working-age Black men in New Orleans were unemployed, underscoring deep economic disparities that may deter return. Additionally, rising housing costs in New Orleans make downtown living unaffordable for many low-income individuals, creating a barrier to re-establishing roots.

Professor Carl Lindahl of the University of Houston notes that parents of young children perceived Houston as offering greater safety and better educational institutions. The elderly, conversely, felt New Orleans lacked adequate social services. In the immediate aftermath, Houston's Astrodome served as a critical shelter for thousands evacuated from the Superdome. This period saw an extraordinary outpouring of community support from Houstonians. Lindahl observed this initial phase, which lasted about two months, "brought out the best in the city." However, within a year, negative narratives emerged, falsely linking New Orleanians to increased crime. Lindahl also documented job discrimination based on New Orleans area codes. At a Houston church gathering a year after the disaster, some residents demanded the mayor expel "Katrina illegal immigrants," fueled by sensationalized early police reports. These misconceptions took years to overcome with accurate data.

In 2005, Lindahl co-founded a project for survivors to share their stories. Some evacuees found therapeutic benefits in recounting their experiences, while others received compensation. However, the most common motivation was, "We want people to know who we are," a powerful testament to their resilience.

Despite dispersal across Houston, evacuees maintained vital cultural connections, as noted by photographer Dallas McNamara. Music played a key role in fostering community, leading to new ensembles and the establishment of New Orleans-based churches and restaurants in Houston. McNamara observed that many evacuees were pleasantly surprised by their positive experiences in Houston, appreciating improved housing and education, despite increased car reliance. She does, however, miss New Orleans' ingrained politeness and relaxed social customs.

Sanyika identifies transportation as the biggest drawback of Houston for many New Orleanians, a stark contrast to their former city's walkability. Distinct culinary variations are also a point of contention, with Sanyika wryly noting, "a Texas gumbo doesn't taste quite the same." He misses the organic, grassroots cultural expressions of New Orleans' working-class communities, which he finds less prevalent in Houston. Nevertheless, he is content, believing cultural heritage is integrated and transformed, not lost. Sanyika, now 73, visits New Orleans regularly and feels a palpable melancholy upon leaving, a reminder of his enduring connection to his homeland. He emphasizes that New Orleans remains deeply embedded in one's soul, providing an enduring anchor to identity.

But stark problems remain. A recent report by the Education Research Alliance confirmed that principals engage in widespread “creaming” — selecting, or counseling out, students based on their expected performance on standardized tests. In a forthcoming study, the alliance expects to show that lowest-scoring students are less likely to move to higher-performing schools. The rhetoric of reform often fails to match the reality. For example, Paul G. Vallas, the superintendent of the Recovery School District from 2007 to 2011, boasted recently that only 7 percent of the city’s students attend failing schools today, down from 62 percent before Katrina, a feat accomplished “with no displacement of children.” This was simply false. Consider Joseph S. Clark Preparatory High School, one of the city’s last traditional public schools to be “taken over.” Most of its 366 students declined to re-enroll when it reopened under new management in the fall of 2011. During its first year under FirstLine, a charter management organization, Clark had only 117 “persisters,” or returning students, according to a study by Stanford University’s Center for Research on Education Outcomes, known as Credo. FirstLine could not account for where the students went after they left Clark. However, Jay Altman, its chief executive, told me in an email that before FirstLine took over, a similarly low proportion of students, about 35 percent, were returning. (The school district did not respond to my queries about Clark.) One problem is that in the decentralized charter system, no agency is responsible for keeping track of all kids. Two years ago the Recovery School District, acknowledging that it was “worried” about high school attrition, began assigning counselors to help relocate students from schools it was closing. Louisiana’s official dropout rates are unreliable, but a new report by Measure of America, a project of the Social Science Research Council, using Census Bureau survey data from 2013, found that over 26,000 people in the metropolitan area between the ages of 16 and 24 are counted as “disconnected,” because they are neither working nor in school. Ironically, schools like Clark actually feed the New Orleans success narrative because when bad schools are taken over their “F” grades automatically convert to a “T” — for a turnaround. Thus, in the 2013-14 school year, the four schools with “T” grades wouldn’t be counted as “failing” schools, nor would the 16 schools that received a “D” grade. About 40 percent of Recovery School District schools were graded “D,” “T” or “F” that year. Adding to the difficulty of assessing the New Orleans experiment is the fact that Louisiana education data has been doled out selectively, mostly to pro-charter researchers, and much of the research has been flawed. Last fall, the Cowen Institute for Public Education Initiatives was forced to retract a study that concluded that most New Orleans schools were posting higher-than-expected graduation rates and test scores. Last spring, Credo produced a study of 41 urban charter districts, including New Orleans, that purported to show that charters outperformed urban public schools on standardized test scores; but this study was also highly flawed. The methodology was based on comparing each charter student to a virtual “twin,” a composite of as many as seven public-school kids who attend “feeder” schools and who match the charter students on demographics and test scores. The problem in New Orleans was that there are virtually no local feeders left from which to draw comparisons.
