## A Decade of Scars: Documenting Katrina's Enduring Impact

Ten years after Hurricane Katrina's devastating landfall, the persistent tendrils of cat's claw vine cascade from the dilapidated rooftop of an abandoned dwelling, aggressively colonizing every available surface of this once-proud Victorian cottage in the Tremé neighborhood. This skeletal ruin stands as a stark testament to the storm's destructive fury. The oppressive, humid atmosphere, pregnant with the threat of an impending thunderstorm, signifies not a few months or even years post-disaster, but a full decade. In July, I accompanied David Spielman, a wiry, 65-year-old freelance photographer distinguished by his piercing blue eyes, through urban landscapes still bearing the savage imprints of the storm. Our vehicle navigated roads so profoundly fractured and potholed they posed a tangible threat to vehicular integrity, jeopardizing axles and severely taxing shock absorbers.

Spielman has meticulously documented the city's post-Katrina milieu since the storm's landfall, amassing an archive exceeding 10,000 images. "This is a protracted struggle that numerous sectors of the city will continue to confront for an extended duration," he observes. His photographic oeuvre vividly portrays the devastation wrought by Katrina and the enduring inability of the city, despite substantial financial aid, insurance disbursements, numerous success narratives, and abundant well-intentioned efforts, to fully rectify the damage. Flanking these ravaged thoroughfares are expansive tracts of untamed vegetation, punctuated only by fractured concrete driveways or foundational slabs, serving as spectral reminders of the residences that once occupied these sites.

### Press Park: A Microcosm of Struggle

Within the Press Park district, remnants of brick and concrete townhomes, originally erected by municipal authorities, persist. Katrina's rendering of these structures uninhabitable constituted a secondary catastrophe for their occupants, given their construction atop a toxic landfill. These residents have received no compensatory recourse from the city. Chain-link fences now cordon off the vestiges of these communities, while pervasive graffiti mars every available surface. Stray canines seek refuge from the oppressive heat. An elementary school stands in desolate abandonment. Nevertheless, interspersed among the desolation, clusters of meticulously rebuilt and maintained single-story homes are occasionally encountered along Press Park's periphery. These residences are clustered together, featuring manicured lawns and predominantly late-model automobiles. Notably, these houses are not elevated, rendering them susceptible to future inundation. This is not a neighborhood in the throes of recovery; it is a profoundly impacted casualty struggling to endure.

### Spielman's Mission: Capturing the Unvarnished Truth

Spielman has undertaken a personal mission to ensure this facet of the hurricane's legacy remains unrecorded, even as the city orchestrates a narrative of recovery around the tenth anniversary. His photographic collection, spanning from 2005 to 2015, meticulously captures scenes of widespread destruction across virtually every neighborhood. Image after image presents vacant, derelict edifices: residences, educational institutions, commercial establishments, places of worship, and healthcare facilities. This comprehensive visual chronicle of the storm's aftermath is now compiled in Spielman's recently published monograph, *The Katrina Decade: Story of an Altered City*. "One of the paramount objectives I aimed to achieve was to demonstrate the pervasive nature of the disaster. I possess only a limited number of photographs from the Lower 9th Ward, as a widespread misconception took hold that the damage was geographically confined to that area. The sheer magnitude of the event is something that overwhelms individuals once they venture beyond the confines of the French Quarter. This was an indiscriminate catastrophe."

### The Eerie Silence: Katrina's Immediate Aftermath

In the latter part of August 2005, Katrina, an immense and foreboding meteorological phenomenon, advanced inexorably toward the Gulf Coast. Shortly after 10 a.m. on the 28th, the National Weather Service disseminated a dire warning: a significant portion of the region would be rendered uninhabitable, even lethal, for an extended period. The majority of New Orleans' populace who possessed the means to evacuate did so. Spielman, however, elected to remain and weather the storm. He had previously experienced numerous hurricanes during his three decades of residency in New Orleans. A native of Tulsa, Oklahoma, he was initially drawn to the city's rich cultural tapestry in the early 1970s, a period marked by a significant surge in urban development following years of stagnation. New Orleans was undergoing a renaissance, rediscovering its unique heritage of music, gastronomy, ritual, art, and architecture. The economic impetus for this boom was fueled by oil revenues, but by the mid-1980s, a precipitous decline in fuel prices led to an economic downturn. The city languished in a prolonged period of stagnation for approximately two decades, until the arrival of Katrina dramatically altered its trajectory.

On that fateful August day, Spielman assisted a small contingent of Poor Clare Sisters in their preparations for the impending storm. These nuns were old acquaintances and neighbors residing in the Uptown district of New Orleans. Uptown, typically spared the most severe flooding during hurricanes due to its elevation of two feet above sea level—a considerable height for the city—was not immune to Katrina's impact. He meticulously boarded up the third-story windows of the century-old monastery, a robust brick edifice fortified with eighteen-inch-thick walls and substantial cypress shutters. When the eight nuns extended an invitation to shelter within the monastery, he accepted, perceiving it as a secure sanctuary. Late that night, at the hurricane's zenith and amidst a complete power outage, the building vibrated violently, and the shutters rattled in the profound darkness. At one juncture, the occupants heard a deafening crack—a sixty-foot pine tree had fractured with the ease of a pencil. Upon opening the convent doors the subsequent morning, the scene was one of utter devastation: debris was strewn everywhere—shingles, shattered glass, tree limbs, and an overwhelming proliferation of power lines resembling tangled string. His initial assessment was grim: "I anticipated a ten-day ordeal of extreme hardship, characterized by a lack of electricity, oppressive heat, and extensive physical exertion assisting others with cleanup."

Spielman commenced his reconnaissance on foot. The silence was unnerving: an absolute absence of sound, not even the chirping of birds or the buzzing of insects. "I was canvassing the neighborhood, checking on friends' residences," he recounts, "attempting to ascertain the extent of the damage." A small group of two or three individuals approached him, seeking guidance on departing the city. They reported witnessing acts of looting, and when a photographer present attempted to document the scene, gunfire erupted. This was his initial premonition of the unfolding crisis. Communication networks, including cellular services, had collapsed, leaving residents unaware that a critical levee along the Industrial Canal, which connects the Mississippi River to the adjacent Lake Pontchartrain, had been breached. Subsequently, at least fifty additional levees were compromised.

### Whisked Away to the Morgue: The Scale of Loss

Later that day, Spielman observed law enforcement officers stationed in patrol cars, passively witnessing the ransacking of commercial establishments and, in some instances, participating in the appropriation of goods. It appeared to Spielman that the police had effectively surrendered their authority. "It became apparent to me that this would be an exceptionally arduous period, prompting me to devise a strategy for evacuating the nuns. They agreed to relocate to a convent in Texas. One of my final directives to them was to refrain from assisting anyone en route. They assured me they would exercise extreme caution and departed, uncertain if they would ever return."

Spielman remained, isolated and disoriented, within the deserted monastery, contemplating how to effectively document the unfolding events. "It was at this juncture that I formulated my approach—to emulate the methodology employed by the WPA photographers during the Dust Bowl and the Great Depression." He resolved to adopt a long-term perspective, remaining engaged with the narrative long after the national media's departure. "It was imperative for me to discover images that would be profoundly evocative and distinctive," he reflects. His objective was to maintain neutrality, "seeking solely the most impactful and comprehensive images that required no accompanying caption or explanation."

Spielman endured forty days without electrical power amidst sweltering conditions. The scarcity of gasoline rendered individuals possessing it vulnerable to attack. The pervasive wreckage had created a labyrinthine urban environment, obstructing numerous thoroughfares and necessitating extensive detours and backtracking for vehicular navigation. Armed with his Leica camera, he traversed the residential districts of New Orleans, including Central City, Tremé, Press Park, Bywater, and the Seventh Ward. These areas were largely deserted, their inhabitants having been evacuated to Houston, Atlanta, or other locales, or, in nearly 700 instances, to the morgue. "Immediately following the storm, I felt profoundly inadequate as a photographer," Spielman admits. "Standing in any given location, I was confronted with an overwhelming abundance of visual narratives—images directly before me, to my left and right, and dozens more behind me. What constituted a worthy subject for photographic capture? How could a single photographer adequately address an event of such unprecedented magnitude?"

It was only when Spielman accompanied a rescue helicopter that the staggering scale of the catastrophe truly became apparent. "It is an experience that even I still struggle to fully comprehend. We were surveying sections of the city that I had visited hundreds of times, yet I was unable to recognize them. Stores, landmarks, entire neighborhoods had vanished—submerged." Entire sectors of the city had been obliterated. Within a mere few days, New Orleans' population plummeted from over 485,000 to approximately 230,000, as victims caught in the destruction were evacuated. The more established, affluent, and historically

Ten years after Hurricane Katrina, James Gray returned to his New Orleans home, finding only a handbag's worth of possessions. This **immense loss** deeply affected his wife. Yet, the Grays have since **meticulously restored** their New Orleans East residence, a home they've lived in for over two decades. Many neighbors have also returned, contributing to the district's **palpable resurgence**. Gray, now a City Council representative, points to **tangible recovery indicators**: a new state-of-the-art hospital, educational facilities under construction, and the return of national retailers like Wal-Mart and CVS. He notes, "**These new amenities surpass their pre-flood predecessors** in scale, aesthetics, and functionality. While we endured immense hardship, our recovery has demonstrably exceeded expert projections."

However, these localized improvements **don't tell the whole story** of New Orleans a decade after the levee failures. Economic indicators reveal a more complex picture, especially for the **African-American middle class**, a vital part of the city's identity. While New Orleans's economy is strong, its remarkable recovery has **disproportionately bypassed many African-Americans**, who still form the majority of the population. Post-Katrina, Black New Orleanians face **lower employment rates and higher poverty levels** compared to before the storm. Inflation-adjusted black household incomes have declined, and the **wage gap between black and white residents has widened**. Erika McConduit-Diggs of the Urban League of Greater New Orleans observes, "While the visible progress—new infrastructure and revitalized public spaces—is undeniable, a deeper examination reveals **persistent challenges for the black community**."

New Orleans has **transformed significantly** over the past decade. It's now a more compact, affluent, and demographically diverse city, but with **exacerbated socioeconomic inequalities**. The revitalized tourism sector, an ambitious school system overhaul, and an influx of educated young professionals have fostered a vibrant entrepreneurial scene. However, concerns about **rising housing costs, gentrification, and the dilution of the city's unique cultural fabric** are widespread. These shifts are deeply tied to racial dynamics. Over 175,000 black residents left New Orleans after the storm, with more than 75,000 not returning. In contrast, the non-Hispanic white population has largely recovered, and the Hispanic population has grown by over 30 percent. Consequently, the **African-American share of the population dropped from 66% in 2005 to 59% in 2013**.

Beyond demographic changes, the **economic standing of African-Americans has fundamentally altered**. Historically, African-Americans were the majority of the city's poor but also a significant part of the middle and professional classes. Post-Katrina, while the poor remain predominantly black, the affluent and middle classes are increasingly white. Furthermore, the existing black middle class is aging. Many returning middle-class African-Americans are retirees, while younger black professionals have sought opportunities elsewhere. This contrasts sharply with the influx of **young, educated, predominantly white professionals** who have reshaped the city. Between 2011 and 2013, New Orleans welcomed nearly 10,000 college graduates under 40, many drawn by a burgeoning startup scene. Amidst a national decline in entrepreneurship, New Orleans boasts one of the highest business formation rates, particularly attracting "social entrepreneurs." Propeller, a business accelerator for socially oriented startups, exemplifies this trend, originating from post-Katrina volunteerism. Andrea Chen, Propeller's co-founder, highlights their focus on projects addressing community needs, inspired by the resilience witnessed after the storm.

However, this influx of new residents has **accelerated gentrification**, intensifying pressure on the already strained black middle class. Housing costs in some New Orleans areas now rival those in major coastal cities, despite significantly lower average incomes. New Orleans ranks poorly for housing affordability, with **37% of renters dedicating over half their pre-tax income to rent and utilities**. Councilwoman LaToya Cantrell notes the significant financial strain, concerned that rising rents may displace artists and musicians vital to the city's culture and tourism, while also disrupting the tradition of multi-generational households within the African-American community.

While many new entrepreneurs are local, some, like Larry Irvin, CEO of Brothers Empowered to Teach, are native New Orleanians who initially considered leaving due to violence and limited opportunities. Irvin's experience during Katrina instilled a sense of responsibility to contribute to New Orleans's rebuilding. Brothers Empowered to Teach aims to increase the representation of black men in teaching, providing positive role models for black boys. With only 3% of New Orleans teachers being black men, Irvin and co-founder Kristyna Jones are dedicated to combating the "school-to-prison pipeline" by showing children that individuals who look like them can achieve positions of influence and success.

Despite these initiatives, Irvin observes **little fundamental change** since the storm, citing persistent high murder rates and scarce employment opportunities. A stark statistic reveals that **35% of black men under 25 in New Orleans are neither employed nor enrolled in education**, compared to 5% of their white counterparts. For many, upward mobility still necessitates relocation. Irvin concludes, "The aspirations and prospects for young people today are not significantly different from the pre-Katrina era; many still aim to leave for cities like Atlanta and New York."

New Orleans leadership acknowledges that Katrina **exacerbated pre-existing issues**. High crime, a dysfunctional school system, and a lack of viable employment opportunities had been driving middle-class residents away long before the storm. Council member Gray suggests the black population might be smaller today had Katrina not occurred, though New Orleans lost over 20,000 black residents between 2000 and 2005, indicating a pre-existing trend of decline. While the post-Katrina economic resurgence has benefited the white middle class, the **erosion of the black middle class has continued or even accelerated**. In 2013, the median black household income was just over $25,000, a $5,000 decrease from 2000. In contrast, the median white household income exceeded $60,000, nearly 2.5 times greater.

These diverging economic trajectories are partly due to **decades of systemic inequality and segregation**. Traditionally white neighborhoods, on higher ground, experienced less storm damage. Black neighborhoods, even those with established middle-class populations, were often in low-lying areas that sustained greater devastation. Furthermore, affluent African-Americans generally possessed less accumulated wealth to facilitate rapid post-disaster recovery compared to their white counterparts. The 2008-09 recession disproportionately impacted African-American families nationwide, exacerbating these challenges.

**Policy decisions also played a critical role** in hindering the black middle class's recovery. Gary Rivlin's research highlights how the federal Road Home rebuilding program, by basing payments on appraised property values (often lower in black neighborhoods) rather than repair costs, effectively discriminated against black families. Local governmental actions also contributed. The Louisiana State Legislature's decision to assume control of the New Orleans school district and dismiss all teachers and staff had a profound impact. While the long-term educational consequences are debated, the immediate effect was the displacement of thousands of predominantly black school employees, many of whom were not rehired. Beverly Wright, a sociologist at Dillard University, emphasizes that teaching was a key profession offering upward mobility for African-Americans, and its disruption significantly impacted the black middle class.

Critics like Wright argue that recovery efforts have **prioritized the tourism industry and gentrifying entrepreneurs** over the needs of the black majority, citing a deficit in affordable housing and the continued neglect of infrastructure outside revitalized tourist zones. Wright contends, "**The city is being whitewashed. The black middle class has been entirely disregarded** throughout this recovery process, left to languish."

Despite these criticisms, Wright acknowledges **increased attention to these issues** under Mayor Mitch Landrieu, who initiated the NOLA for Life initiative. This program aims to reduce the city's murder rate through law enforcement and enhanced economic opportunities for young African-Americans. At a conference for the initiative, Landrieu spoke about the stark disparities faced by children, stating, "From day one, for many children the deck is stacked against them." McConduit-Diggs notes that while the storm reduced the black population, it **galvanized civic engagement** among those who remained. She also points to the significant advantage of a diaspora eager to return, provided they are assured of safety and adequate prospects for their children. McConduit-Diggs, who evacuated nine months pregnant, returned three years later, observing that her sister and brother-in-law recently moved back. "There is an extraordinary and deeply personal connection to this city that compels people to return, despite its challenges," she stated.

In the wake of catastrophic levee failures and the subsequent inundation of New Orleans, a profound commitment has emerged to prevent a recurrence of the 2005 disaster. Witnessing the harrowing scenes of residents stranded on rooftops, within the Superdome and Convention Center, and along the interstate, underscores the urgent necessity for robust preparedness. Evacuteer.org represents a vital initiative, synergizing the inherent compassion of New Orleans' citizenry with strategic governmental planning. This collaborative effort aims to ensure that every individual has a secure and timely evacuation pathway prior to the advent of another significant hurricane. Projections indicate that as many as 40,000 individuals may require assistance during such an event. Our organization comprises over 500 dedicated New Orleanians, united by an unwavering resolve to guarantee that no resident is again subjected to the perils of being left behind. We invite you to contribute to this critical mission.

query: What specific concerns do the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe have regarding the potential impact of the Dakota Access Pipeline on their water supply?

documents.text: Actresses Shailene Woodley and Susan Sarandon participate in a rally outside the US District Court in Washington, Wednesday, Aug. 24, 2016, in solidarity with the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe.ap It is a major issue that has a disproportionately negative impact on the Latino community. It is playing out on the national stage – and it has nothing to do with building a wall. It has to do with the construction of a pipeline. The Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL) would transport oil across four Midwestern states, starting in North Dakota, then running through South Dakota and Iowa before ending in Illinois. The project – specifically the potentially catastrophic impact it could have on water – has been the subject of months-long protests by the Standing Rock Sioux and their supporters. (The DAPL should not be confused with the heavily protested Keystone XL Pipeline, although the public outrage surrounding – including within the Native community – and ultimate failing of the Keystone XL project should have served as forewarning.) The protests in North Dakota have swelled in recent weeks as supporters from across the country have joined the efforts. The situation came to a head this past weekend as site security guards pepper-sprayed and set dogs on a crowd of protesters that included children. On Tuesday, a federal judge ordered a temporary stop to construction ahead of a ruling on the overall case by Sept. 9. The case against the nearly $4 billion pipeline is – at the most basic level – about water. Water is considered a sacred resource to not only the Sioux, but by many Native American tribes whose members have journeyed to the North Dakota-South Dakota border to stand in solidarity. The construction of the pipeline would place it under the Missouri River, just a mile from Stand Rock’s water intake – which supplies water to the entire reservation. Any damage to the pipeline at that location in the near or distant future would almost certainly contaminate the entire reservation’s water. In a state that has already had to combat water contamination from fracking and the area’s oil boom, the potential for devastation is not hypothetical, but something North Dakota has been dealing with for the better part of a decade. Of course, with less than 15,000 Latinos in all of North Dakota (ranking it 49th in the nation in that regard), the state isn’t exactly a hotbed of all things Hispanic. Neighboring South Dakota doesn’t fare much better with 23,000 total Latinos (46th), so why should the Latino community be paying such close attention? Because on a larger level, safe water is an issue that plagues a disproportionate number of minority communities. The Flint water crisis, which affected a predominately African-American community, is only one recent example. A report earlier this year highlighted ongoing water safety issues in California’s Central Valley, where Latinos make up 40 percent of the population. In fact, a 2011 study published in the American Journal of Public Health showed numerous reports of serious water quality issues appearing most prevalent in tribal communities, communities along the U.S.-Mexico border and in other predominately Latino communities. That same study noted that “In California, the Environmental Justice Coalition for Water found that counties with the highest number of drinking water violations had a higher proportion of people of Latino ethnicity than counties with the lowest number of violations (42 percent versus 16 percent).” It should be unacceptable that in a country like ours tens of thousands of individuals in Latino and tribal communities lack access to safe drinking water. Projects that would further jeopardize that access should be unthinkable. There is also the undeniable reality surrounding the situation’s further complexities — that as much as it is about water, it is also compounded by generations of systematic abuse and marginalization. To date over 150 tribes have submitted letters of support or resolutions backing the Standing Rock Sioux. And it is in no small part because many people in these communities see their histories and potentially their futures playing out on the North Dakota-South Dakota border. Communities of color are disproportionately negatively impacted when it comes to these type of projects. In fact, construction plans were revised to move the site closer to Standing Rock. And these projects too often benefit a microscopic clique of multi-millionaires and billionaires at the expense of affected communities – whether it’s the luxury property developers and inner-city gentrification or big oil corporations and the DAPL. At Standing Rock, the community is still dealing with the aftereffects of a dam project over a generation ago by the Army Corps of Engineers (the same planners who mapped out the DAPL) that annexed land by eminent domain and then flooded over 200,000 acres of reservation farmland. With the DAPL posing a legitimate threat the community’s water, it became a matter of enough is enough. The tribe has also asserted that the construction would destroy sacred and cultural sites. Now, what began as a trickle of protesters has swelled to become an international story gaining attention and perhaps evoking enough emotion to turn the tide. “When we stick together, we have a powerful voice,” said Dr. Erich Longie, a member of the Spirit Lake Dakota – one of the seven bands that along, with Standing Rock, comprise the Sioux Nation – and a tribal historic preservation officer. On multiple occasions, Longie has traveled from Spirit Lake to Standing Rock to participate in the protests. He believes that the collective efforts of the Standing Rock Sioux and their allies, both Native and non-Native, could ultimately make the difference as to whether the pipeline proceeds — and that the impact of this event could reach much further. “On a larger scale, minorities are tired of old white men running everything,” Longie said. “I think we’re seeing a seismic shift around what this protest could mean for a lot of communities.” Count the Latino community among them. Maria Burns Ortiz is a journalist and entrepreneur. A regular contributor to Fox News Latino, she is also the co-author of the New York Times best seller "My Fight/Your Fight" with Ronda Rousey and the co-founder of 7 Generation Games. Follow her on Twitter: @BurnsOrtiz Follow us on twitter.com/foxnewslatino Like us at facebook.com/foxnewslatino

The Sacred Stones Overflow Camp is experiencing significant growth in both size and attendee numbers. This expansion is occurring at the site situated along North Dakota Highway 1806, directly across the Cannonball River from the Standing Rock Sioux Indian Reservation. This burgeoning encampment is a focal point in the ongoing dispute over the Dakota Access pipeline.

A federal judge is expected to issue a ruling by Friday concerning the Standing Rock Sioux tribe's lawsuit. This legal challenge contests federal permits granted for the four-state, $3.8 billion Dakota Access pipeline. The tribe's lawsuit contends that the pipeline's proposed route, less than a mile upstream from the reservation, poses a substantial risk to the drinking water supply for over 8,000 tribal members and millions of others downstream. Furthermore, the situation has been marked by confrontations between protesters and private security personnel on private land, where the tribe asserts that construction activities have disturbed ancient sacred sites.

**Key aspects of the Dakota Access pipeline and the tribe's protest include:**

**What is the Dakota Access Pipeline?**
The Dakota Access pipeline is a $3.8 billion, 1,172-mile project owned by Dallas-based Energy Transfer Partners. It is designed to transport approximately half a million barrels of crude oil daily from North Dakota's oil fields. The pipeline's route extends through South Dakota and Iowa, terminating at an existing pipeline in Patoka, Illinois. From this point, shippers can access markets in the Midwest and along the Gulf Coast.

**What is the Lawsuit Targeting?**
The Standing Rock Sioux tribe has filed a lawsuit against federal regulators, specifically challenging the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers' decision to grant permits for over 200 water crossings. Filed by the environmental group Earthjustice on behalf of the tribe, the suit argues that the project violates multiple federal statutes, including the National Historic Preservation Act. A central claim is that the pipeline's construction will disturb sacred sites located outside the 2.3-million-acre reservation.

**Pipeline Safety Measures:**
Energy Transfer Partners asserts that the pipeline incorporates robust safety features. These include advanced leak detection equipment and remote monitoring capabilities. Personnel in Texas can reportedly close block valves on the pipeline within three minutes of detecting a breach.

**Description of the Protest:**
Since April, a nonviolent protest, primarily led by members of the Standing Rock Sioux tribe, has been established at a "spirit camp." This camp is located at the confluence of the Cannonball and Missouri rivers, directly in the pipeline's projected path. The protest has seen considerable growth, attracting participants from other American Indian tribes and non-Native individuals from across the nation. Notable figures, such as actress Shailene Woodley, have joined the demonstrations. The protest has, however, experienced heightened tensions, resulting in nearly 40 arrests, including that of Standing Rock Sioux Tribal Chairman David Archambault II. The confrontation on Saturday between protesters and construction workers did not lead to any arrests. Green Party presidential candidate Jill Stein and her running mate Ajamu Baraka participated in the protest on Monday. Stein's campaign spokeswoman stated that Stein spray-painted construction equipment on Tuesday. Both Stein and Baraka have been charged with misdemeanor counts of criminal trespass and criminal mischief. In anticipation of U.S. District Judge James Boasberg's federal ruling, North Dakota authorities are actively recruiting law enforcement officers statewide to secure the protest site.

**Concerns Regarding Cultural Sites:**
Tribal officials reported last weekend that construction crews bulldozed several sites of "significant cultural and historic value" on private land. Energy Transfer Partners has denied these allegations. This incident is believed to have precipitated Saturday's clash between protesters and private security guards employed by the pipeline company. Law enforcement officials indicated that four security guards and two guard dogs received medical attention. Conversely, a tribal spokesman stated that six individuals were bitten by guard dogs and at least 30 people were subjected to pepper spray. On Tuesday, Judge Boasberg partially granted the tribe's request for a temporary halt to work near Lake Oahe, aiming to prevent further destruction of sacred sites. However, this injunction did not extend to the private land that initially triggered the protest. North Dakota's chief archaeologist, Paul Picha, is scheduled to inspect the area, likely next week.

**Protests in Other Locations:**
While protests are occurring elsewhere, they have not reached the scale seen in North Dakota. In August, construction equipment at several sites in Iowa was set ablaze, causing over $1 million in damages.

**Rationale for Pipeline Construction:**
The Dakota Access pipeline project, announced in 2014, is supported by arguments that it will create new markets for crude oil. Proponents also contend that it will reduce reliance on truck and oil train transportation. The latter has become a significant concern following a series of fiery derailments involving trains carrying North Dakota crude oil.

## Indigenous Resistance and the Dakota Access Pipeline: A Multifaceted Struggle

Thousands of Native American activists, representing at least 200 tribes, have converged in North Dakota to protest the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL). This remarkable display of inter-tribal unity, which one elder likened to the Battle of the Little Bighorn, underscores the complex nature of this conflict. While often framed as a simple confrontation between "Big Oil" and Indigenous peoples, the motivations behind the protest are varied and, at times, appear contradictory.

### The Dakota Access Pipeline: Project Scope and Environmental Concerns

The DAPL, a $3.8 billion undertaking by Energy Transfer Products (ETP), is designed to transport 570,000 barrels of crude oil daily from the Dakotas to Illinois. Supporters maintain that the pipeline is crucial for energy independence and poses no significant environmental risks, a position echoed by various agencies and judicial rulings. However, opponents, notably the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe, voice grave concerns about the potential contamination of their water supply. The pipeline's route crosses the Missouri River just half a mile upstream from their reservation. Adding to these anxieties, activists have reported the discovery of tribal relics during construction on lands the pipeline traverses. In response, the tribe has initiated legal action against the Army Corps of Engineers, alleging violations of the Clean Water Act, the Rivers and Harbors Act, and the National Historic Preservation Act. They contend that the Corps failed to adequately consult with the tribe regarding historical impacts and should conduct a comprehensive environmental impact statement rather than issuing a Finding Of No Significant Impact (FONSI).

### Beyond Water Protection: The Climate Change Imperative

While public figures like Leonardo DiCaprio have lent their support to the Standing Rock Sioux Nation's efforts to safeguard their water and lands, the situation extends beyond immediate environmental threats. The DAPL is part of a larger $4.8 billion project involving 26 banks and nine partners. A significant driving force behind the opposition is the broader fight against climate change. Campaigns such as #keepitintheground, championed by climate activist Bill McKibben, aim to halt fossil fuel development and accelerate the transition to renewable energy. Activists are not only opposing the DAPL in North Dakota but are advocating for a nationwide halt to new fossil fuel infrastructure, seeking to steer America towards renewable energy sources and mitigate global climate change. Currently, fossil fuels account for 81 percent of American energy consumption, with renewables making up a mere 5 percent. This overarching agenda helps explain the opposition to pipelines like DAPL, as well as previous controversies surrounding the Keystone XL and Spectra Algonquin AIM pipelines.

### The Economic and Social Dimensions of Protest

Pipeline spills can have catastrophic consequences. In 2010, a rupture in an Enbridge Energy pipeline in Michigan released over a million gallons of crude oil into the Kalamazoo River, necessitating four years and $765 million for cleanup and resulting in the closure of 35 miles of the river. In 2014 alone, 119 pipeline accidents were documented.

The protests and legal challenges also serve to increase the financial burden of fossil fuel development, which benefits from substantial government subsidies. By incorporating legal and law enforcement expenditures into the cost of these projects, environmentalists aim to make fossil fuel ventures less economically viable, thereby favoring renewable energy alternatives.

However, this strategy is not without its complexities. Concerns have been raised about potential exploitation, questioning whether environmentalists are genuine allies of Native Americans or are leveraging their cause for their own objectives. If the alternative to pipelines involves increased oil transport via rail or truck—a scenario potentially worse for the climate but better for Native American lands—would environmentalists still support it? The current solidarity with the Sioux could be tested if their ultimate goals diverge.

Furthermore, protests can devolve into chaos, shifting the focus from the core issues to the conflict itself. At Standing Rock, reports of attack dogs and crowd dispersal tactics have overshadowed discussions about Native American relics and land rights. While the tactics employed bear resemblance to other controversial protests, the stakes are undeniably real, with Native Americans facing harm and potentially becoming pawns in a larger geopolitical struggle. To date, 29 arrests have been made.

Law enforcement faces the challenging task of maintaining order and enforcing court mandates, with local agencies like the Morton County Sheriff’s Department bearing a significant financial burden. While striving to de-escalate tensions, police are tasked with ensuring public safety and maintaining access to construction sites.

The slogan "Shut It Down," while emotionally powerful, represents a temporary form of activism. The ultimate objective should be to dismantle the systemic reliance on fossil fuels and address environmental concerns comprehensively, rather than achieving fleeting victories at construction sites.

Construction of the DAPL commenced in May 2016, with permits issued in July. Judicial requests to halt construction and remove protesters have been denied. The tribe's lawsuit remains ongoing, with a ruling anticipated from the D.C. Circuit Court of Appeals on September 9. The strength of the lawsuit may lie in the unique impacts of the DAPL on Native American lands and artifacts. Should the Corps be compelled to conduct more extensive reviews, the process could extend for years, potentially allowing environmentalists to exert similar political pressure that stalled Keystone XL. This outcome could protect Native American lands and increase the costs associated with climate-changing fossil fuels, depending on which priority is ultimately emphasized.

Continental Resources' **2016 Investor Update** prominently featured the **Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL)** as a cornerstone of its marketing strategy for Bakken shale oil. The company's presentation emphasized how enhanced pipeline capacity, specifically through DAPL and the ETCOP line, would lead to **reduced transportation expenses and improved profit margins** for their Bakken output.

A substantial part of the update was dedicated to **"Bakken Takeaway Capacity,"** visually demonstrating with a bar graph how the **launch of Dakota Access would facilitate greater pipeline transport of Continental's oil**. This strategic pivot is noteworthy considering the past stance of Continental's CEO, Harold Hamm. Previously, Hamm was a **staunch proponent of TransCanada's Keystone XL pipeline**, even utilizing his lobbying organization, the Domestic Energy Producers Alliance, to champion it. However, upon recognizing the political obstacles facing Keystone XL, Hamm redirected his focus. In **November 2014**, he informed Politico, "We’re supporting other pipelines out there, we’re not waiting on Keystone. Nobody is." He conveyed that the window for Keystone XL had closed and it was time to **"move on."**

Dakota Access appears to be among those "other pipelines" that captured Hamm's attention. While neither Hamm nor President Trump have publicly addressed DAPL, Continental Resources chose not to comment on specific pipeline agreements.

Questions have arisen regarding DAPL's ultimate destination. Despite Energy Transfer's assertions that the pipeline would transport "100% Domestic produced crude" for "100% domestic consumption," **some oil originating from Dakota Access could potentially be directed to export markets**. This development aligns with the objectives of Hamm's Domestic Energy Producers Alliance, which, as reported by DeSmog in **December 2015**, **led the successful campaign to repeal the crude oil export ban**.

The Dakota Access Pipeline has become a central point of **significant opposition**, creating a conflict between Native American tribes, environmental advocates, and private property rights defenders on one side, and Energy Transfer Partners and government bodies on the other. The Standing Rock Sioux Tribe is presently awaiting a judicial decision on its lawsuit against the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers. Angie Carter of the Women, Food and Agriculture Network criticized Hamm, stating, **"Hamm is an oil profiteer exploiting the health of the water, farmland, and communities in the Dakotas and all downstream."** Organizations such as the Bakken Pipeline Resistance Coalition have implored political candidates to **voice their opposition to the pipeline**.

Thousands of people have joined the Standing Rock Sioux Nation's fight against construction of a contentious oil pipeline, a showdown Indigenous leaders in North Dakota warn won't end anytime soon. They're opposed to the Dakota Access Pipeline, a multimillion-dollar project that's supposed to transport light sweet crude oil from the Bakken oilfield near the Canadian border to Illinois. An area cleared for the Dakota Access Pipeline can be seen from the side of Highway 6, south of Bismarck, N.D. (Trevor Brine/CBC) Tribal leaders and their supporters fear a potential leak in that pipeline would poison the Missouri River, which borders the entire western edge of the reservation. For weeks, people from across North America have been gathering at camps that have sprung up in and around Cannon Ball, N.D., a town within the Standing Rock Reservation, just south of Bismarck, N.D. "It's overwhelming," Standing Rock Sioux Tribal Council chair David Archambault said. "I never expected it to be this big, but I'm thankful and proud to be Native American, because I know that with unity, there's a lot of things we can overcome." 'Good hosts' The largest camp, called the Red Warrior Camp, has swelled even more since a recent confrontation involving private security guards armed with dogs and pepper spray, sparked after construction crews allegedly bulldozed an area believed to be a tribal burial ground. The camp was already populated by Indigenous people from across the U.S. and Canada, but even more have arrived since video and photos of that incident were shared widely on social media. Thousands of people are now living in what has become a small town of teepees, lodges, tents and RVs, where people on horseback are a common sight — and where even more people seem to arrive by the hour. Those entering the massive camp are greeted by a road lined with flags from dozens of Indigenous nations that have offered support to Standing Rock. Flags from dozens of Indigenous nations that support Standing Rock's fight against the pipeline greet people arriving at the camp. (Tim Fontaine/CBC) Those who stay in the camp are fed from a huge kitchen that seems to operate round the clock, offering hot meals to an army of people. Clothing, camping supplies and toiletries are also distributed from a tent to anyone who needs them. Most of what's offered is donated; the rest is provided by the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe. "We want to be good hosts," said Archambault . 'Uplifting' experience for youth Layha Spoonhunter, 26, is from the Wind River Indian Reservation in Wyoming. Already a youth advocate in his own community, Spoonhunter said living in the camp for over two weeks now has given him something of a spiritual reawakening. "I'm not looking at a television each day. I'm getting to hear from elders, I'm getting to hear our stories, and hearing the different songs from all the different tribes that have come here, it's really uplifting." Spoonhunter is among a group that has organized a two-day youth gathering at the Red Warrior Camp that kicks off on Thursday. While most of the people who have arrived to support Standing Rock are Indigenous people from the United States, many people living north of the border are travelling here. Kevin Hart, a regional chief for the Assembly of First Nations in Manitoba, recently spent a few days in the area. More from Manitoba and other parts of Canada are expected to arrive in the coming days. Emergency declaration But depending on where people are travelling from, the trip to this area can take a little longer than normal. The quickest way to get to Standing Rock from Bismarck is usually south on Highway 1806, which winds along the western bank of the Missouri River. The trip normally takes under an hour. But for weeks, North Dakota Highway Patrol have been redirecting many people onto a detour that can double the normal travel time, depending on traffic. As we leave Standing Rock earlier, a police checkpoint south of Bismarck pic.twitter.com/invPuXnltw — @anishinaboy It's the result of an emergency declaration signed by North Dakota Gov. Jack Dalrymple on Aug. 19, in response to the growing protest. The governor's office has said there have been complaints about the increase in traffic on that route since the protests began, and the detour is for safety reasons. "The governor's executive order does not include activation of the North Dakota National Guard, but makes available other state resources for the purpose of protecting the health, safety and well-being of the general public and those involved in the protest," reads a statement from the governor's office. 'Not going to end anytime soon' On Tuesday, a federal judge ordered work must stop on portions of the Dakota Access Pipeline, but many here are waiting for a separate ruling expected this Friday — an injunction that could potentially halt all work in and around the reservation. Regardless of the outcome on Friday, Archambault believes the losing side will file appeals. "This didn't just begin two weeks ago, and it's not going to end any time soon," he said.

The Dakota Access Pipeline, a $3.7 billion project spanning four states, has ignited a fierce debate about its potential to reshape the U.S. crude oil supply. Proponents herald it as an economic boon and a path to greater energy self-sufficiency, while opponents decry it as an environmental disaster that threatens sacred Native American sites. Here's a comprehensive look at this contentious project.

**What is the Dakota Access Pipeline?**

The Dakota Access Pipeline is an ambitious 1,172-mile infrastructure project designed to transport crude oil from the prolific Bakken Formation, a vast underground oil deposit straddling Montana, North Dakota, and the Canadian border. From its origin, the pipeline snakes southeast through South Dakota, Iowa, and ultimately terminates in Illinois. The Bakken Formation holds immense oil potential, with the U.S. Geological Survey estimating approximately 7.4 billion barrels of undiscovered oil in its U.S. portion alone. Once operational, the pipeline is projected to shuttle 470,000 barrels of crude oil daily, according to developer Energy Access Partners. This volume is equivalent to producing roughly 374.3 million gallons of gasoline per day. From Illinois, the oil can be distributed to markets and refineries across the Midwest, East Coast, and Gulf Coast, significantly altering the nation's energy logistics.

**Who Approved the Project?**

The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers granted the Dakota Access Pipeline its final permits in July, marking a significant step in its approval process. This decision, however, has been met with strong opposition. The Standing Rock Sioux Tribe has filed a lawsuit against the Corps, asserting that the pipeline poses a direct threat to the tribe's environmental and economic well-being. Crucially, the tribe contends that the pipeline's route would damage and destroy sites of profound historic, religious, and cultural significance. While the Army Corps of Engineers has declined to comment on the ongoing litigation, an advocacy group has countered the tribe's claims, stating that the pipeline "does not cross into the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe's reservation."

**Key Statistics:**

*   **Length:** 1,172 miles
*   **Pipeline Diameter:** 30 inches
*   **Daily Crude Oil Capacity:** 470,000 barrels
*   **Equivalent Daily Gasoline Production:** 374.3 million gallons

*Sources: Energy Access Partners, U.S. Energy Information Administration*

**The Argument For and Against:**

The debate surrounding the Dakota Access Pipeline is multifaceted, encompassing economic, environmental, and cultural considerations.

**Arguments in Favor:**

Energy Transfer Partners, the developer, argues that the pipeline will not only stimulate economic growth but also substantially reduce the United States' reliance on foreign oil. Furthermore, they contend that by shifting crude oil transport from railways to pipelines, it will free up vital rail capacity for other commodities, such as agricultural products, which are currently facing transportation constraints.

**Arguments Against:**

The Standing Rock Sioux Tribe has voiced grave concerns, stating that the pipeline's construction will lead to the destruction of sacred burial sites, prayer sites, and culturally significant artifacts. Beyond these cultural impacts, opponents highlight significant environmental risks. These include the potential for oil spills and contamination due to pipeline breaches, as well as the eventual greenhouse gas emissions associated with the transportation and processing of the crude oil.

**Environmental Impact: A Point of Contention**

The environmental implications of the Dakota Access Pipeline are a central focus of the controversy, with starkly different perspectives presented by stakeholders.

The developer asserts that pipelines offer a safer and more environmentally responsible method for transporting crude oil compared to alternatives like rail or truck transport. Supporters point to past incidents, such as the 2013 Quebec train derailment that devastated the town of Lac-Mégantic, as evidence of the inherent risks associated with other transportation modes.

However, Standing Rock Sioux Chairman David Archambault II has expressed strong opposition to increasing crude oil transport from North Dakota, advocating instead for a transition to alternative and renewable energy sources. This sentiment is echoed by over 274,000 online petitioners who argue that the pipeline will exacerbate climate change, inflict severe environmental damage, and deeply disrupt sacred lands and the traditional way of life for Native Americans in the upper Midwest. A significant concern for opponents is the potential for the pipeline to rupture and contaminate the Missouri River, a vital source of drinking water for downstream communities.

Conversely, the Midwest Alliance for Infrastructure Now supports the developer's claims regarding pipeline safety. They emphasize that numerous pipelines already cross the Missouri River, safely transporting vast quantities of energy products daily, suggesting that the Dakota Access Pipeline would operate within established safety protocols.

**Economic Impact: Promises and Perils**

The economic arguments surrounding the Dakota Access Pipeline are equally divided.

Energy Transfer Partners estimates that the project will generate approximately $156 million in sales and income taxes for state and local governments. Additionally, the developer anticipates the creation of 8,000 to 12,000 construction jobs during the pipeline's development phase.

However, Chairman Archambault has articulated a different perspective, stating that his tribe's ultimate goal is to halt the pipeline's construction. He clarifies that the opposition is not to energy independence or economic development in principle, but rather to a historical pattern where federal governments and corporations exploit indigenous lands and rights. He argues that indigenous communities are often burdened with the costs and negative consequences of projects that benefit the broader nation, such as energy independence and economic growth, while receiving little to no benefit themselves. This sentiment is shared by tribes across the country who are increasingly vocal about the need to protect indigenous people and their lands.

**The Ongoing Protests**

Months of protests have taken place in North Dakota, highlighting the deep-seated opposition to the pipeline. Law enforcement has made numerous arrests, with one day seeing at least 141 protesters taken into custody. In one instance, law enforcement officers used bean bag rounds and pepper spray to disperse approximately 200 protesters who were being moved from an area back to their main encampment. In response, protesters reportedly set debris on fire and threw Molotov cocktails at law enforcement. The situation has been volatile, with vehicles being towed and some vandalized or burned.

**Landowner Compensation and Indigenous Rights**

Energy Transfer Partners has stated its efforts to route the pipeline away from residential areas and to secure voluntary agreements with property owners at fair prices. However, Chairman Archambault believes that Native Americans are once again being disadvantaged. He argues that indigenous communities are being made to bear the costs of national benefits, such as energy independence and economic development, without commensurate gains. He emphasizes a long history of such exploitation and a growing collective voice among tribes demanding an end to the mistreatment of indigenous people and their ancestral lands.

query: What concerns did Rep. Mike Rogers express regarding the cybersecurity of the new Obamacare website?

documents.text: 'I'm even more concerned today than I was even last week,' Rogers said. | Reuters Rep. Mike Rogers on Sunday decried the state of cyber security of the new Obamacare website, warning the private information of applicants is at risk. "It was very clear to me in the hearing that (the websites) do not have an overarching solid cyber security plan to prevent the loss of private information," the chairman of the House Intelligence Committee said on CNN's "State of the Union." "I'm even more concerned today than I was even last week." Even though government officials have claimed no private information is stored, the Michigan Republican said the system has to store an application at some point and "and that's a lot of your very personal information." (PHOTOS: Obamacare online glitches: 25 great quotes) All the healthcare exchanges websites may need to be redesigned because of a weakness in the "boundary," when one agency goes to another agency with a piece of information, Rogers said. "I know that they've called in another private entity to try to help with the security of it," Rogers said. "The problem is they may have to redesign the entire system. The way the system is designed, it is not secure." Read more about: Mike Rogers, Obamacare, Affordable Care Act

Senator Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) forcefully advocated for affordable housing objectives on Tuesday, articulating a comprehensive strategy to reform the nation's housing finance system. In her inaugural public address concerning housing finance reform, Senator Warren detailed her plan to restructure the market in the wake of the subprime mortgage crisis. She underscored the imperative for Congress to guarantee that mortgage accessibility remains within reach for the middle class, deeming it the "centerpiece of the American Dream." Addressing the Mortgage Bankers Association, Warren asserted that affordable housing mandates, frequently cited by conservative critics as a primary catalyst for the proliferation of high-risk mortgages that precipitated the financial collapse, bore no causal relationship to the crisis.

Louisiana Governor Bobby Jindal is urging a delay in key aspects of the Affordable Care Act (ACA), commonly known as Obamacare. The growing technical difficulties and the broader concerns about government efficiency fuel the arguments for a pause in the ACA's implementation.

The Internet Archive is dedicated to **preserving the web's history** by actively archiving web pages as they are created, along with the content they reference. This proactive strategy guarantees that even if linked pages are subsequently modified or deleted, a **permanent record** of the original material remains available.

The **"No More 404" initiative** is a key effort to address the pervasive issue of broken links online. By capturing and storing linked content, the Internet Archive aims to offer a **reliable alternative**. When a hyperlink directs users to content that has vanished, a link to its archived counterpart can be substituted. This archived version can also function as a **supplementary resource**, providing readers with context about the initial purpose of a page's creators. The overarching **objective is to mend the web's fractured connections** through systematic crawling and archiving of participating websites.

**Obamacare Implementation Challenges Spark Calls for Secretary Sebelius's Resignation**

On CBS's "Face the Nation," Issa remarked, "The president has been poorly served in the implementation of his own signature legislation." He also questioned Sebelius's awareness of the problems, asking, **"Why didn’t she know that the president’s signature legislation was in trouble?"**

**Lawmakers Demand Accountability for Website Failures**

Issa's criticism is shared by other lawmakers. **House Budget Committee Chairman Paul Ryan (R-Wis.) has specifically called for Sebelius's resignation**, citing her handling of the Obamacare website's technical issues. In a letter to Sebelius, Ryan highlighted her lack of response to congressional inquiries, stating, "Your continued silence on these important inquiries after refusing to testify raises serious questions about the administration’s commitment to transparency and accountability."

**Democrats Prioritize Fixing the System**

Conversely, **Democratic Senator Jeanne Shaheen of New Hampshire is focusing on functionality rather than assigning blame**. When asked about Sebelius, Shaheen stressed that the **primary objective is to make the healthcare marketplaces and their websites operational**. "Our No. 1 goal is to get the market places working, get the websites working," she said. Shaheen believes that **assigning responsibility can wait until the technical problems are resolved**.

query: What was Rep. Nancy Pelosi's response to the calls from some Democrats for her to step down as House Minority Leader?

Nancy Pelosi is trying to tamp down any intra - caucus criticism of her leadership. | Getty Congress Pelosi defiant as restless Democrats consider options ‘I am a master legislator,’ Pelosi told reporters. Later that day, some of her Democratic colleagues met to discuss replacing her. A defiant House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi on Thursday said she has no plans to step down anytime soon, despite calls from some rank-and-file Democrats for new leadership and at least one meeting to plot to replace her. Pelosi spent a good chunk of her weekly news conference defending her longtime perch atop the Democratic Caucus, at times even mocking colleagues within her party who have in recent days said it’s time for her to step aside. Story Continued Below “I respect any opinion that my members have, but my decision about how long I stay is not up to them,” Pelosi told reporters. “My caucus is overwhelmingly supportive of me.” But as Pelosi was defending her status in the caucus, of group of Democrats were already planning a secret afternoon meeting to talk about potentially trying to replace her. A dozen Democrats attended the meeting out of the nearly 20 members invited. Afterward, leaders of the group said no decisions have been made about what to do next, but that support is growing within the caucus to topple Pelosi. “There are many more people today, even from November, who are much more vocal, who have approached us and said, ‘I’m done. We need to move forward and we need to get a new leadership team in place,’” Rep. Kathleen Rice (D-N.Y.) said after the hour-long meeting. Rice and another group leader, Rep. Seth Moulton (D-Mass.), said the ultimate goal would be to have a new leadership team in place before the 2018 campaign heats up. “The takeaway from the meeting is that we want to win again in 2018,” Moulton said, noting he’s seen a shift in the caucus since Pelosi won her leadership race last year. “I think people see the stakes more clearly.” Among the Democrats who attended the confab were Congressional Black Caucus Chairman Cedric Richmond (La.), Tony Cardenas (Calif.), a member of House Democratic leadership, and Reps. Ami Bera (Calif.), Filemon Vela (Texas), Ruben Gallego (Ariz.) and Del. Stacey Plaskett (V.I.). But despite the renewed noise surrounding Pelosi’s leadership, no one within the caucus has stepped forward to challenge her and an actual coup against the long-time leader is still a long shot. During the news conference earlier in the day, Pelosi boasted about her effectiveness as a leader, even though Democrats lost their majority in 2010 under her watch and have never come close to winning it back. Sign up here for POLITICO Huddle A daily play-by-play of congressional news in your inbox. Email Sign Up By signing up you agree to receive email newsletters or alerts from POLITICO. You can unsubscribe at any time. “I am a master legislator,” Pelosi declared. “I am a strategic, politically astute leader. My leadership is recognized by many around the country. That is why I am able to attract the [financial] support that I do, which is essential to our election, I am sad to say.” Pelosi also pointed out that she led Democrats to the majority in the first place back in 2006 after a dozen years out of power. One other reason Pelosi has held on to her leadership mantle: It’s unclear who could replace her and keep the conference’s disparate factions united. Questions about Pelosi’s longevity as Democratic leader have dogged her for a long time, but demands for new blood in leadership were given fresh life after Democrats’ disappointing loss in the Georgia special election Tuesday. Republicans constantly painted Democratic candidate Jon Ossoff as a Pelosi puppet representative of her “liberal San Francisco values” in a barrage of campaign ads and mailers and during appearances by Republican winner Karen Handel. Pelosi tells critics she's 'proud' of her leadership poster="http://v.politico.com/images/1155968404/201706/418/1155968404_5480204835001_5480140275001-vs.jpg?pubId=1155968404" true Pelosi is trying to tamp down any intracaucus criticism of her leadership and said the fact that Democrats came so close in a district that’s been a Republican stronghold for nearly four decades should count as a win. But critics of Pelosi, including some members of her own caucus, say that kind of mentality is exactly the reason why she should step down and let someone else lead the party before they get too deep into the midterm campaign. Pelosi shrugged off her colleagues’ criticism, at one point even daring them to keep up the drumbeat demanding her ouster. “When it comes to personal ambition and having fun on TV, have your fun. I love the arena, I thrive on competition, and I welcome the discussion,” Pelosi said. Rep. Tim Ryan, the Ohio Democrat who ran against Pelosi in November, said on MSNBC on Thursday morning that “it’ll be very hard” to win back the House with Pelosi at the helm. Rice, also on MSNBC Thursday morning, said Pelosi's time has "come and gone." Republicans — including President Donald Trump — have also engaged in the chatter over whether Pelosi should step down, taunting her leadership struggles and saying that as long as she’s leader, they’ll keep winning. “I certainly hope the Democrats do not force Nancy P out. That would be very bad for the Republican party – and please let Cryin’ Chuck stay!” Trump tweeted Thursday about Pelosi and Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.). In maybe the most concise response describing her reaction to the GOP attacks on her, Pelosi asserted, “I think that I am worth it.”

Representative Debbie Dingell of Michigan called for Democrats to go “on offense” and attack the president’s perceived strength on economic matters with working-class voters. Photo “We need to show working men and women we understand their anxieties and fears,” she said, “and show that Trump is treating them like just another politician.” By fiercely contesting a congressional race in the conservative Atlanta suburbs, Democrats had hoped to make an emphatic statement about the weakness of the Republican Party under Mr. Trump. Their candidate, Jon Ossoff, raised about $25 million, mostly in small donations, and assertively courted right-of-center voters with promises of economic development and fiscal restraint. That vague message, Democrats said Wednesday, was plainly not powerful enough to counter an onslaught of Republican advertising that cast Mr. Ossoff as a puppet of liberal national Democrats, led by Ms. Pelosi, an intensely unpopular figure on the right and a longstanding target of Republican attacks. While Mr. Ossoff made inroads by exploiting Mr. Trump’s unpopularity and a backlash against health care legislation approved in the House, Democrats said they would have to do more to actually win. Representative Eric Swalwell of California, who is close to party leaders, said Democrats would “crystallize our message on jobs, on health care” in the coming months. The results in Georgia and other special elections, he said, should encourage Democrats to campaign across a huge map of districts. “We need to compete everywhere,” he said. Representative Ben Ray Luján of New Mexico, the chairman of the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee, met Wednesday morning with a group of lawmakers who have been conferring about economic messaging, according to several people present who spoke on the condition of anonymity. Mr. Luján told the group that his committee would examine the Georgia results for lessons, but he urged the lawmakers to portray the race in positive terms in their public comments, stressing that Democrats have consistently exceeded their historical performance in a series of special elections fought in solidly Republican territory. Advertisement Continue reading the main story It was in the meeting with Mr. Luján that Mr. Cárdenas, a member of the Democratic leadership, brought up Ms. Pelosi’s role in the Georgia race, calling it “the elephant in the room.” Ms. Pelosi was not present. Photo A spokeswoman for Mr. Cárdenas, while acknowledging his comment, said he had invoked the leader in the context of “what can be done to stand up to those attacks in the future.” Ms. Pelosi has consistently rejected calls to step down, and there was little indication that her leadership post was at risk. She responded to the election results in a “Dear Colleague” letter to Democratic lawmakers late Wednesday, underscoring the party’s improving performance in conservative areas and saying that “every effort was made to win” in Georgia. But Ms. Pelosi also said it was time for Democrats to “put forth our message,” and promised an economic one that “we can all embrace and utilize in our districts.” She did not directly address the sometimes caustic criticism of her leadership from skeptics within the party. Several lawmakers who have opposed her in the past argued that Ms. Pelosi would undermine the party’s candidates for as long as she holds her post. Representative Seth Moulton of Massachusetts, an open critic of Ms. Pelosi, called the Georgia result “frustrating” and urged a shake-up at the top of the party.
