Sign Up You agree to receive occasional updates and special offers for The New York Times's products and services. Thank you for subscribing. An error has occurred. Please try again later. View all New York Times newsletters. Representative Kathleen Rice of New York told CNN the entire Democratic leadership team should go. Representative Tim Ryan of Ohio, who tried to unseat Ms. Pelosi as House minority leader late last fall, said she remained a political millstone for Democrats. But Mr. Ryan said the Democratic brand had also become “toxic” in much of the country because voters saw Democrats as “not being able to connect with the issues they care about.” “Our brand is worse than Trump,” he said. A top aide to Ms. Pelosi dismissed the idea that her lightning-rod status might have hurt the Democratic effort in Georgia, and pointed out that in some polls the Republican speaker, Paul D. Ryan, is viewed even more dismally. Any Democratic leader would become a target for the right, said the aide, Drew Hammill, Ms. Pelosi’s deputy chief of staff. Advertisement Continue reading the main story “Republicans blew through millions to keep a ruby red seat and in their desperate rush to stop the hemorrhaging, they’ve returned to demonizing the party’s strongest fund-raiser and consensus builder,” he said. “They don’t have Clinton or Obama, so this is what they do.” But in a possible omen, the first Democratic candidate to announce his campaign after the Georgia defeat immediately vowed not to support Ms. Pelosi for leader. Joe Cunningham, a South Carolina lawyer challenging Representative Mark Sanford, said Democrats needed “new leadership now.” Even Democrats who are not openly antagonistic toward Ms. Pelosi acknowledged that a decade of Republican attacks had taken a toll: “It’s pretty difficult to undo the demonization of anyone,” said Representative Bill Pascrell Jr. of New Jersey. In some respects, the sniping over the Democrats’ campaign message mirrors a larger divide in the Democratic Party, dating to the 2016 presidential primary contest and earlier. Senator Bernie Sanders of Vermont and his supporters have pressed Democrats to embrace a more bluntly populist message, assailing wealthy special interests and endorsing the expansion of social welfare programs, while more moderate Democrats in the party leadership have favored an approach closer to Mr. Ossoff’s. But in four contested special elections in Republican districts — including two, in Kansas and Montana, featuring Sanders-style insurgents — neither method provided the party with a breakthrough victory. In the absence of a smashing win that might have settled the left-versus-center debate, Democrats may face a longer process of internal deliberation before they settle on an approach that is broadly acceptable in the party. Photo Part of the Democrats’ challenge now is that the jobless rate is low, and many of the districts they are targeting are a lot like the Georgia seat: thriving suburbs filled with voters who have only watched their portfolios grow since Mr. Trump took office. Even as they smarted from their defeat on Wednesday, Democrats signaled that they intend to compete across a vast area of the country in 2018. Mr. Luján, moving to calm the party, circulated a memo to lawmakers and staff members that declared there was “no doubt that Democrats can take back the House next fall” in the midterm elections. He wrote that six to eight dozen seats held by Republican lawmakers would be easier for Democrats to capture than Georgia’s Sixth. Advertisement Continue reading the main story Citing snippets of private polling, Mr. Luján said there were Republican seats in southern Arizona and Florida, northern New Jersey and the Kansas City, Kan., suburbs, where Democratic challengers were already ahead of Republican incumbents. Democrats need to win 24 Republican-held seats to win control of the House. On the Republican side, jubilation over the victory in Georgia mixed with lingering unease about the overall political environment. While Ms. Handel defeated Mr. Ossoff by about 10,000 votes and nearly four percentage points, Republican outside groups had to spend $18 million defending a district where the party’s candidates had won easily for decades. And on the same night, a little-watched special election in South Carolina gave Republicans another scare, as an obscure Democrat, Archie Parnell, came within 3,000 votes of capturing a solidly Republican congressional district, with voter turnout far behind the Georgia race. Nick Everhart, a Republican strategist in Ohio, said the party should not allow its relief at having kept Democrats at bay to turn into complacency. Up to this point, he said, Republicans have been beating Democrats only on solidly red turf. “To pretend that there are not serious enthusiasm-gap issues with the G.O.P. base and, more crucially, independents fleeing, is missing the lessons that need to be learned before truly competitive seats are on the board,” Mr. Everhart said. Still, the immediate aftermath of the Georgia election was plainly tougher on the Democratic side, as the party endured a fourth special election that ended with a better-than-usual showing by a defeated Democrat. That pattern may put Democrats on track to gain power in the 2018 elections, but 17 months is a long wait for a party so hungry to win.

The recent Democratic defeat in Georgia's 6th Congressional District special election has amplified internal party discord. Liberal activists are scrutinizing the party's strategic direction, while frustrated lawmakers are questioning its leadership. Disappointed officials are grappling to identify the root causes of this electoral setback. This outcome has intensified pre-existing divisions within the Democratic Party, which have been evident since President Trump's election, and has escalated the ongoing discourse regarding the party's future trajectory and its leadership cadre.

"A comprehensive post-mortem analysis will necessitate considerable time," stated Rep. Linda Sánchez (Calif.), vice chairwoman of the House Democratic Caucus. "It is presently too early to definitively articulate the reasons for our lack of success and to prescribe the requisite adjustments."

Some Democrats have rapidly assigned blame to party leadership, contending that the failure to internalize lessons from the 2016 election—where campaigning against Trump without a robust positive platform proved insufficient—contributed to Jon Ossoff's loss to Republican Karen Handel. "We must engage in serious introspection and acknowledge that the fundamental issue resides within the party itself," declared Rep. Tim Ryan (Ohio) on Wednesday morning, exiting a closed-door Democratic Caucus meeting. He characterized Ossoff as a strong candidate who was ultimately encumbered by the "national baggage of the Democratic Party."

Rep. Seth Moulton (D-Mass.) concurred with Ryan's assessment, underscoring the imperative for a clearly defined vision. "We require a strategic plan, a compelling vision, and a message that articulates why Democrats offer a superior alternative, not merely why Trump is detrimental," Moulton asserted. "I believe our efforts in this regard are insufficient, and this Georgia race starkly highlights that deficiency."

Republicans effectively deployed a strategy of associating Ossoff with House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi (D-Calif.), whose public image remains unpopular in numerous conservative districts. Ryan, Moulton, and other younger Democrats, who had previously challenged Pelosi for leadership precisely for this reason, resurrected this tactic. "Those advertisements continue to be potent and damaging to our candidates," Ryan, 43, commented, referencing his prior challenge to Pelosi for leadership. "My position on this matter remains unwavering."

Moulton, who unseated a Democratic incumbent to secure his seat, expressed skepticism regarding the party's capacity for resurgence under its current leadership. "It is not an impossibility, but it certainly presents a more formidable challenge," he remarked. Rep. Kathleen Rice, a second-term Democrat from New York, advocated for a complete restructuring of the Democratic leadership. "We necessitate a change in leadership," Rice told CNN. "It is time for Nancy Pelosi and the entire leadership team to relinquish their positions."

This critique extended beyond elected officials. Filmmaker and liberal activist Michael Moore decried the party as lacking "a message, a plan, and leaders." David Wasserman, a House race analyst at the Cook Political Report, questioned whether Pelosi's renowned fundraising acumen justifies the negative repercussions of her association in Republican campaign advertisements. "It is exceedingly difficult for Democrats to argue that the benefits of Nancy Pelosi's fundraising skills still outweigh the cost of her appearance in GOP advertisements," Wasserman tweeted.

Pelosi's office responded by emphasizing her track record as a successful fundraiser, a skilled strategist, and an effective whip. "Republican voters do not dictate the leadership of the Democratic Party," stated spokesman Drew Hammill via email. He noted that Pelosi's approval ratings are comparable to those of Speaker Paul Ryan (R-Wis.). Hammill added, "Since Newt Gingrich, the politics of personal destruction has been a hallmark of the GOP. They will target any and every Democratic leader because their majority is sustained by desperation."

Many Democrats, including senior leaders, countered the internal criticism by highlighting that Georgia's 6th District has historically been a Republican stronghold. Despite the disappointment of Tuesday's loss, they expressed optimism regarding the message this closely contested race conveys heading into the 2018 midterm elections. "In a typical year, we wouldn't even have had a chance. We have dramatically closed the gap," said Rep. Keith Ellison (D-Minn.), deputy chairman of the Democratic National Committee (DNC). "They are the ones who should be worried."

Nevertheless, the palpable frustration among rank-and-file Democrats was evident. Many lawmakers expressed exasperation over the party's four consecutive losses in competitive special elections during the Trump administration. "Close only counts in horseshoes," stated Rep. Bill Pascrell (D-N.J.). "A loss is a loss, and there are no excuses."

Other Democrats observed that Handel's victory occurred in a district that has been Republican-held for decades. Former Rep. Tom Price, who departed Congress to lead the Department of Health and Human Services, had secured reelection there by 23 points the preceding year. However, Trump had only carried the district by a narrow margin. With the president's approval rating significantly low, Democrats believed an upset was attainable, and outside donors contributed nearly $24 million to Ossoff's campaign, establishing it as the most expensive House race in history.

While many Democrats cautioned against drawing definitive conclusions from this historic contest, some questioned how the party could invest such substantial resources and still fall short. "I believe it certainly shows that momentum is on our side, but there are no moral victories here. It's 218 seats or nothing," remarked Rep. Tim Walz (D-Minn.), referring to the number of seats required for a House majority. "If you are going to invest that magnitude of capital, you must secure victory in these contests."

## Democratic Party's Post-Georgia Election Reckoning: Leadership and Direction Under Scrutiny

The Democratic Party is grappling with significant internal debate following a costly loss in a Georgia congressional election, a result that has amplified concerns about the party's ability to connect with voters and its future direction. The outcome has intensified discussions surrounding leadership and the party's overall strategy.

### Internal Divisions on Party Strategy

Within the Democratic Party, differing perspectives have emerged regarding the reasons for electoral challenges. Some supporters of progressive ideals, like those aligned with Bernie Sanders, suggest that the nomination of moderate candidates, exemplified by Jon Ossoff's four-point loss to Republican Karen Handel in Georgia, is a primary issue. Conversely, others identify House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi as a significant liability, arguing that her public image alienates crucial voter segments. Republican advertising effectively leveraged this sentiment, linking Ossoff to Pelosi, whose approval ratings are notably low outside of California. Despite substantial financial backing from California Democrats, this support was strategically used against Ossoff.

### Scrutiny of Nancy Pelosi's Leadership

The Georgia election outcome has placed Nancy Pelosi's leadership under renewed scrutiny. Democratic strategist Sean Clegg remarked, "It was a very rough night for Nancy Pelosi." He observed that Republican attacks targeting Pelosi appeared to resonate more strongly with voters than Democratic efforts to criticize President Trump, presenting a considerable hurdle for future House races. Following the defeat, several Democratic members of Congress publicly voiced their concerns. Representative Filemon Vela of Texas stated to Politico, "I think you’d have to be an idiot to think we could win the House with Pelosi at the top." While acknowledging Pelosi was not the sole factor in Ossoff's loss, Vela conceded she was a contributing element. This sentiment marks a shift for Vela, who had previously supported Pelosi's leadership. Her most recent bid for leadership saw her secure the support of only about two-thirds of the Democratic caucus, her weakest performance in years.

During a private meeting, Representative Tony Cárdenas of California reportedly urged House Democrats to directly address the impact of Pelosi's standing on their candidates. A spokesperson for Pelosi declined to comment. However, Pelosi herself advised her caucus against overreacting to the loss in a traditionally Republican district, characterizing it as a setback rather than a definitive victory for Republicans.

Pelosi faces potential opposition from various factions within the party. Moderate Democrats view her as a detractor from the party's centrist appeal, while progressive Democrats perceive her as a symbol of the party establishment. Joey Aszterbaum, a former Sanders delegate, described Pelosi as a "liability" in competitive districts due to her perceived association with the party's perceived shortcomings.

Despite these criticisms, Pelosi remains a formidable fundraiser. Since assuming leadership in 2003, she has raised approximately $560 million for House Democrats, a figure unmatched by any other non-presidential candidate in history.

### The Georgia Race as a Referendum and a Sign of Hope

Nationally, the Georgia race was widely interpreted as a referendum on President Trump. Clegg suggested that the intense focus on the election may have galvanized Republican turnout. However, he also highlighted Ossoff's relatively strong performance in a district held by Republicans since 1978 as a positive indicator for Democrats heading into the 2018 elections. He pointed to Democrats outperforming expectations in several special elections throughout the year, stating, "While it would have been nice to have that trophy of Newt Gingrich’s former district, we have an energized, mobilized Democratic base that’s turned up and turned out again in four very red districts and very red states."

Both Ossoff and Handel largely avoided direct engagement with the Trump issue. Ossoff positioned himself as nonpartisan, opposing tax increases for the wealthy and a single-payer healthcare system. Liberals, such as Aszterbaum, viewed this approach as a misstep, expressing limited optimism for 2018 unless Democrats nominate more liberal candidates.

The Georgia race represented a significant financial commitment from California donors, becoming the most expensive House race in U.S. history. In April and May alone, the Ossoff campaign garnered more funding from the San Francisco Bay Area than from the entirety of Georgia. This record-breaking fundraising is unlikely to be replicated in the following year, as Democratic donors are expected to distribute their contributions across a wider array of competitive races nationwide. Clegg characterized the race as a "chew toy" for Democrats to channel their frustrations.

Donors who contributed to Ossoff with the explicit aim of opposing Trump expressed disappointment. Rosaria Haugland, a Palo Alto philanthropist who donated $2,167, lamented, "It feels like we don’t live in a democracy anymore. The whole thing is crazy." She voiced concerns that the outcome would embolden Republicans to repeal the Affordable Care Act.

Ironically, the substantial California support may have inadvertently backfired. Television advertisements highlighted Ossoff's Bay Area donations, often featuring stereotypical San Francisco caricatures. However, Aram Fischer of Indivisible San Francisco, an anti-Trump organization, stated that his members were not discouraged by the loss. He emphasized the party's growing capacity to compete in Republican-held districts and expressed confidence in securing victories in these areas in 2018.

### Looking Ahead to 2018: California's Crucial Role

As the Democratic Party shifts its focus to the 2018 elections, California is expected to be a key battleground. Seven Republican House members represent districts that voted for Hillary Clinton, making these seats critical for Democrats aiming to regain a House majority. Clegg's firm is already experiencing a surge in inquiries from potential candidates interested in running in these targeted races, even though official filing deadlines are not until March. He anticipates a competitive and potentially contentious landscape of Democratic primaries in these districts.

President Trump offered his perspective on the situation via Twitter, advising, "Democrats would do much better as a party if they got together with Republicans on Healthcare,Tax Cuts, Security. Obstruction doesn’t work!"

### Pelosi's Leadership Under Scrutiny as Democrats Eye House Majority

As Democrats **look toward the upcoming election cycle with a strong desire to regain control of the House majority**, House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi **offers an optimistic outlook**. She views the coming year as a **key moment** for their resurgence. However, this hopeful stance is **tempered by a clear sense of urgency** among some Democrats, reflected in the sentiment, "**It better be.**" This feeling has **grown stronger after a disappointing loss** in a closely watched special House election in Georgia. The race's outcome has **increased focus on Pelosi's leadership**, especially given the **extensive and pointed negative ads** that targeted her. These ads **portrayed her as an out-of-touch liberal from San Francisco**, aiming to **closely tie her image** to the Democratic candidate.

The **clear success of this messaging** has led some within the party to question if the 77-year-old Californian might actually **be a disadvantage** for Democrats in their goal of regaining House control. Adding to these worries is Pelosi's past, **incorrect prediction** that Democrats were close to taking back the House in the previous election. This past misjudgment has **raised the stakes for her to achieve real results** this time. In a message to her fellow House Democrats, Pelosi acknowledged the ongoing competitive nature of the House, stating, "**The House was in play before the Georgia race. The House remains in play now.**"

#### Electoral Success and Leadership's Connection

The **future of Pelosi's leadership is directly tied to the party's election results**. "**If we take back the House in 2018 then I think she'd stay leader**," said Rep. Ruben Gallego, D-Ariz. "**If we don't, then I think it's incumbent upon her and all of us to reassess who our leadership should be.**" Pelosi has, for over a decade, **led House Democrats through periods of both majority and minority status**, showing a **consistent ability to handle political challenges and resist internal opposition**. This includes a significant challenge last fall from Democratic Rep. Tim Ryan of Ohio. While Ryan's bid failed, it received a **large number of votes**, indicating some discontent with Pelosi and the **perceived aging of her leadership team**. This situation has reportedly **hindered promising young Democrats**, limiting their career growth within the party.

#### Renewed Divisions and the Call for New Leadership

The period right after Donald Trump's inauguration and the Republican effort to repeal former President Barack Obama's healthcare law, the Affordable Care Act, saw Democrats unite in opposition. This shared goal temporarily hid the **deep internal divisions and generational differences** that have long existed in the party. However, the recent loss in Georgia, along with three other special House elections where Democrats did not win unexpectedly, has caused these **divisions to reappear**. For many Democrats, these election losses are prompting **important questions about the effectiveness of their current leadership**, particularly Pelosi's ability to create and communicate an economic message that can **effectively challenge the Trump administration's agenda and lead to election wins**.

"**We as Democrats have to come to terms with the fact that we lost again**," stated Rep. Seth Moulton, D-Mass., voicing a common feeling. "**We are the party that stands up for working families and the middle class, yet many of them are not voting for us. So it's time for change. Personally I think it's time for a new generation of leadership in the party.**" Ryan agreed, noting that Pelosi's continued presence as a Republican target makes it "**much harder**" for Democrats to win in the key Republican-leaning districts they need to capture to regain power. To win a House majority, Democrats need to gain a net of 24 seats. "**One of the disappointing things from the last couple days is that that approach has a little bit of punch to it, it still moves voters**," Ryan added, referring to how well the anti-Pelosi ads worked.

#### Pelosi's Lasting Strengths and Strategic Value

Despite these growing concerns and the clear calls for change, the current political climate does not suggest an **immediate leadership challenge for Pelosi**. Her **significant strengths as a highly effective fundraiser** for the Democratic party are well-known. Additionally, she is seen as a **legislative strategist with few rivals on Capitol Hill** when it comes to the complex negotiations needed to keep the government running. Even in the minority, her support is often **essential for passing key spending bills and other important legislation** that may face opposition from House conservatives, often leading Republican Speakers to seek her help. Pelosi continues to command **strong loyalty from a large group of House Democrats**, and her supporters have consistently dismissed any claims that her leadership is unstable.

"**I'm in my 10th term and I certainly have never seen someone who is smarter in the kind of backroom maneuvering that Nancy Pelosi does**," said Rep. Jan Schakowsky, D-Ill., a strong supporter. "**We've seen her run circles around (former House Speaker) John Boehner and get things done that nobody thought Democrats could do in the minority. That's Nancy Pelosi.**" Others have tried to minimize Pelosi's direct impact on the Georgia outcome, where Democrat Jon Ossoff ultimately lost to Republican Karen Handel by about five percent. The race, held in Atlanta's wealthy suburbs, was notably the **most expensive House race ever**, with over $50 million spent by both sides. "**She's easy to demonize. Do you think people went to the polls and said, 'Oh my God, we've got to stop Nancy Pelosi?' I don't think so**," commented Rep. John Larson, D-Conn. Instead, Larson stressed the **critical need for Democrats to focus on creating a strong message that truly connects with voters** in Republican-leaning districts. "**What was the Democratic message?**" he asked, pointing out a perceived lack. "**It's not enough to dislike Trump.**" The way forward for Democrats, therefore, involves not only smart election strategies but also a **fundamental rethinking of their core messages and leadership** to effectively reach a wider range of voters.

Associated Press writers Matthew Daly, Kevin Freking, Stephen Ohlemacher, and Andrew Taylor contributed to this report.

The Georgia special election loss has intensified scrutiny on House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi within the Democratic Party. While an immediate leadership challenge isn't expected, a growing frustration among rank-and-file members stems from the party's significant electoral defeats over the past five years. The effectiveness of Republican campaign ads, which consistently linked Democratic candidate Jon Ossoff to Pelosi and progressive policies, underscores her persistent role as a liability for Democratic contenders in competitive districts.

Some Democrats contend that reclaiming the House majority is unattainable with Pelosi at the party's helm. This sentiment has prompted a change in perspective even among her former supporters. Representative Filemon Vela (D-Texas) articulated this view, stating, "I think you’d have to be an idiot to think we could win the House with Pelosi at the top." He further clarified, "Nancy Pelosi is not the only reason that Ossoff lost. But she certainly is one of the reasons."

Representative Kathleen Rice (D-N.Y.), who previously supported a challenge to Pelosi, believes the Ossoff race results underscore the necessity for new leadership. "There comes a time when every leader has to say, ‘For the good of the order and for the betterment of the party, it’s time for me to step aside.’ And I wish that that would happen right now," Rice remarked. She stressed that her stance is not personal but driven by the imperative to restore the party to majority status.

Representative Tim Ryan (D-Ohio), who previously challenged Pelosi for minority leader, declined to comment directly on her potential resignation, stating his position remains unchanged. He expressed concern over the continued potency of tactics that associate Democrats with Pelosi. "I think it’s very concerning that that tactic still has some punch," Ryan commented. "Again, it’s part of the broader national brand that average people don’t feel connected to the Democratic Party. Walk up the street and ask 10 people what the Democrats stand for, you’ll get 10 different answers. That’s no way to build a national party."

During a private caucus meeting, Pelosi characterized Ossoff as a "young and enthusiastic candidate who attracted national support." She pointed out that Ossoff's narrow 3.8 percent loss margin was considerably smaller than the previous Democratic candidate's defeat in November. While acknowledging the loss as a setback, Pelosi cautioned against overreacting, noting that Democrats have historically experienced special election victories followed by substantial House seat losses, and vice versa. She also suggested that the recent shooting of Representative Steve Scalise (R-La.) might have influenced the outcome, as Republican groups attempted to connect the incident to anti-Trump rhetoric.

Drew Hammill, a spokesperson for Pelosi, asserted, "Republican voters don’t get to select the leaders of the Democratic Party." He described "politics of personal destruction" as a Republican hallmark since Newt Gingrich, arguing that Republicans target any Democratic leader out of desperation to maintain their majority. Some Democrats expressed surprise and disappointment that Pelosi's attack ads were not directly addressed during the caucus meeting. Rice described the meeting as "one of the more disturbing caucus meetings that I’ve ever been in," noting the silence and shock among members regarding the party's interpretation of the loss.

With former President Barack Obama no longer in office, Pelosi has become the primary Democratic figure targeted by Republicans. Her extensive tenure on the national stage has seen "San Francisco values" frequently used as a pejorative against Democratic challengers. Despite strong enthusiasm among the Democratic base, the party confronts the reality that an anti-Trump message alone may prove insufficient to secure a majority in 2018.

Pelosi's fundraising capabilities remain a significant advantage. She is the most successful non-presidential political fundraiser in U.S. history, having amassed over $560 million for House Democrats since assuming leadership in 2003. However, critics argue that fundraising success is undermined by persistent electoral losses. Nevertheless, Pelosi maintains considerable support among many House Democrats. Representative Keith Ellison (D-Minnesota) praised her, stating, "I think she’s one of the best speakers ever, and I am glad to support her."

Democratic Caucus Chairman Joe Crowley (D-N.Y.) dismissed the GOP's attacks on Pelosi as "nothing new," suggesting that a comprehensive review of all campaign advertisements and issues would be necessary. He cautioned against drawing premature conclusions immediately following an election.

Some Democrats running for statewide office advocate for a departure from nationalizing every race, emphasizing the importance of focusing on local issues to connect with constituents. They argue that while the Trump-Russia probe is significant, it is not a daily concern for average voters. Representative Tim Walz (D-Minn.), campaigning for governor, stated, "I think what people are looking for is that they want to believe someone knows where they are coming from. They want to believe that you’re a decent person." He also noted that his district, while potentially winnable for Democrats, would still be a closely contested race and that the outcome would largely be attributable to President Trump himself, rather than the party's national platform.

**Democrats Grapple with Leadership Questions After Georgia Special Election**

The recent Georgia special election, where Jon Ossoff narrowly lost a highly publicized race, has reignited internal Democratic debates about leadership, particularly concerning House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi. The contest, the most expensive in House history, saw Pelosi frequently targeted in Republican advertising, intensifying discussions within the party about the long-serving leadership trio.

President Donald Trump commented on the situation, suggesting that Pelosi's potential departure would benefit the GOP. Joe Cunningham, a Democrat running for Congress in South Carolina, stated he would not support Pelosi for Speaker if elected, advocating for a fresh start to achieve electoral success.

Echoing these sentiments, Rep. Kathleen Rice (D-NY) told MSNBC that Pelosi's era of influence has passed, arguing that the party's consistent losses since 2010 necessitate new leadership. Rice, along with others expressing similar concerns, previously supported Rep. Tim Ryan's (D-OH) challenge to Pelosi. Ryan indicated to MSNBC that Pelosi's continued leadership would make regaining the House difficult, citing the "toxic Democratic brand" that candidates are forced to contend with, and the persistent effectiveness of attack ads linking them to her.

Ryan's challenge last year was the most significant to Pelosi's leadership, though she ultimately retained the support of a majority of the caucus. Pelosi is acknowledged as a formidable Democratic fundraiser and a skilled legislative strategist with a strong base of support. Her office reports significant fundraising achievements, including $568 million for Democrats since she joined leadership in 2002, with $142 million raised in the most recent election cycle. Despite these recent calls for change, a recent private meeting indicated no open challenges to the current leadership.

Pelosi's spokesman, Drew Hamill, asserted that Republicans will continue to use the Democratic leader as a political target, drawing parallels to past Republican attacks on figures like Tip O'Neill. He noted that Republicans invested heavily in the Georgia race and resorted to demonizing Pelosi, the party's top fundraiser and a unifying figure, due to a lack of other compelling messaging.

Conversely, other Democrats are emphasizing party unity. Rep. Eric Swalwell (D-CA) expressed his disappointment with electoral losses but stressed the need to focus on opposing Republicans rather than internal party conflicts. While some officials have publicly called for new leadership, many privately share similar views. Rep. Bill Pascrell (D-NJ) acknowledged the necessity of discussing leadership transitions, and Rep. Seth Moulton (D-MA) agreed that the caucus needs to engage in this conversation.

Pelosi's association has proven to be a significant liability for Democrats in swing districts. Republican Karen Handel, who defeated Ossoff, even utilized a Spanish-language advertisement to criticize Pelosi. An independent group also ran an ad featuring San Franciscans thanking Ossoff. Unlike Speaker Paul Ryan, who actively campaigned for Republican candidates, Pelosi's connection is viewed as detrimental in Republican-leaning areas. The Congressional Leadership Fund, a GOP super PAC, identified Pelosi as an effective attack message in the Georgia race, undermining Ossoff's moderate image. Former GOP House Speaker Newt Gingrich remarked that Republicans "know exactly how to run against a Nancy Pelosi led party" and expressed a desire for her to remain leader for another decade.

query: What key promises did President Trump make during his address to Congress?

documents.text: President Trump promised to lower taxes, combat terrorism and replace the Affordable Care Act in a speech to a joint session of Congress, Feb. 28. Here are key moments from that speech. (Sarah Parnass/The Washington Post) President Trump delivered a strong speech to a joint session of Congress on Tuesday night, an address sure to embolden both the chief executive and congressional Republicans who support him. I watched, tweeted and took some notes about the best and the worst of the night. My picks are below. Winners * Donald Trump: This was the best “big” speech he has given as president. It may well have been the best speech Trump has given since he entered politics way back in June 2015. Trump didn't walk away from his decidedly dark vision of the current state of the country, but his overall tone was more conciliatory and optimistic than I've ever heard him. Trump hit a few very nice notes: His condemnation of threats against Jewish community centers at the start of the speech was a very nice grace note, and his honoring of the widow of the Navy SEAL killed in the recent Yemen raid was a remarkably powerful moment. Carryn Owens, the widow of William "Ryan" Owens, a Navy SEAL killed in the Trump administration's first counterterrorism operation, was honored by President Trump during his address to a joint session of Congress. Trump called Owens a "warrior and a hero." (The Washington Post) Critics will rightly point out that several of Trump's claims — about the rising violence in America, for example — missed the factual mark by a wide margin. And, at times, Trump seemed to be on the verge of returning to his confrontational self — particularly when discussing immigration and the border wall. But, top to bottom, Trump delivered both a forceful defense of his nationalist worldview — “My job is not to represent the world. My job is to represent the United States of America,” he said at one point — and a proof point that he can be, dare I say it, presidential when the moment demands it. 1 of 45 Full Screen Autoplay Close Skip Ad × The scene as President Trump delivers his first address to a joint session of Congress View Photos The president returned to familiar themes from his campaign in his speech to Congress, promising to reduce regulations and taxes, combat terrorism, crack down on illegal immigration and replace the Affordable Care Act. Caption The president returned to familiar themes from his campaign in his speech to Congress, promising to reduce regulations and taxes, combat terrorism, crack down on illegal immigration and replace the Affordable Care Act. Feb. 28, 2017 Vice President Pence and Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell (R-Ky.), behind Pence, shake hands with attendees as they walk into the House Chamber for the president’s speech to a joint session of Congress in Washington. Melina Mara/The Washington Post Buy Photo Wait 1 second to continue. * Congressional Republicans: Anyone who tells you House Speaker Paul D. Ryan (R-Wis.) and Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell (R-Ky.) weren't a little bit worried about how Trump would do on Tuesday night — and what that would mean for his ability to rally Republicans going forward — is lying to you. Trump's speech will go a long way to quieting the nerves of congressional Republicans and convincing them that Trump might just be capable of being the president they desperately hope he can be. His address will serve as a validation for the likes of Ryan and McConnell, who have steadfastly supported Trump throughout the rough seas of his first month in office. *Polarization: If you needed to understand just how polarized our politics are, the repeated shots of Republicans rising to cheer Trump while Democrats sat on their hands would do it. The theater of the State of the Union is always a bit overdone but what was clear from the start on Tuesday night was that Republicans were bound and determined to cheer for virtually everything Trump said and that Democrats were bound and determined to do the exact opposite. Just watch this clip. * Stephen K. Bannon and Ivanka Trump: If you are looking for the two biggest influencers in terms of what Trump said and how he said it, look no further than Bannon, the president's chief strategist, and Ivanka Trump, the president's elder daughter. The unapologetic nationalism and indictment of the political system was pure Bannon. The push for paid family leave and focus on education was all Ivanka Trump. If you polished and repackaged the "deconstruction of the admin. state" for a nat'l TV audience, you'd get this speech. Bannonism. — Robert Costa (@costareports) March 1, 2017 Another clear influence on this speech: Ivanka. I've seen her talk through these issues with Trump. She pushes him. He listens. — Robert Costa (@costareports) March 1, 2017 * Joe Scarborough and Elijah E. Cummings: Symbolism can be empty. But it can be important too. Kudos to Scarborough, a conservative Republican House member turned cable news host, and Cummings, a prominent Democratic Maryland congressman, for sitting together to show that just because we disagree doesn't mean we have to be disagreeable. * Joe Biden: Sometimes you don't know what you've got until it's gone. And the State of the Union speech — which this was in all but name — was always a moment for Biden (and his face of a thousand expressions) to shine. Without Biden to watch as the president delivered his speech, the night felt slightly less momentous. Here's one for the road, Diamond Joe: * C-SPAN's Howard Mortman: I loved Howard before this. And my love for C-SPAN is also well known. But, this image, captured by Howard, of Trump en route to the speech, made me love him even more. Losers * The devilish details of replacing the Affordable Care Act: In the run-up to the Trump speech, Republican members of Congress voiced hopes that Trump might give them guidance about how he'd like to replace the Affordable Care Act. What Trump did talk about — lowering the price of prescription drugs, keeping the preexisting conditions provision — are widely popular idea. The issue, of course, is how to pay for all of this if you strip out the rest of the elements of the health law. * People rooting for Trump's imminent demise: He's not going anywhere, folks. And that speech suggests he might have more upside than almost anyone thought. This speech is clearest example Trump is beginning to own and understand the powers of the office — Zeke Miller (@ZekeJMiller) March 1, 2017 * “Aisle hogs”: I H-A-T-E the annual stories of the people — Eliot L. Engel (D) of New York and Sheila Jackson Lee (D) of Texas are two of the most prominent — who stake out spots on the aisle to ensure they shake hands with the various luminaries, including the president. There was a noticeable lack of crowding along the aisles this time around. Possible we can drop this as a story in the future? Okay, it's agreed! * Georgetown basketball: I needed a win — just one — to keep me going through a long night ahead. My alma mater couldn't get one — again. Three turnovers in the last two minutes of the game against Seton Hall and a terrible final attempt to tie the game. I have but one question to ask: Come on, man. What are we even doing out here, man?

President Trump's address to Congress, **aimed at projecting bipartisan unity**, **largely sidestepped concrete policy details** needed to turn his ambitious campaign promises into reality. The hour-long speech marked a shift from his usual online pronouncements and rally rhetoric, adopting a **more measured and aspirational tone**. While he primarily read from a teleprompter, Trump's stated goal was to "deliver a message of unity and strength." However, these **conciliatory sentiments were not backed by specific, bipartisan policy proposals**.

### Policy Ambiguities and Contrasts

Trump reaffirmed his commitment to **repealing the Affordable Care Act** and **significantly reducing regulatory oversight** at agencies like the Food and Drug Administration. Despite optimistically declaring that "dying industries will come roaring back to life," he **failed to outline specific strategies** to support the blue-collar workers who had shifted their political allegiance to him. Furthermore, Trump pledged to "promote clean air and clear water," a statement that **sharply contrasted with his administration's proposal to cut the Environmental Protection Agency's budget by 25%**. He also proclaimed, "the time for trivial fights is behind us," a sentiment that **clashed with his presidency's early tendency for numerous online disputes**.

### The Commencement of Governance

Despite the **overall lack of specific policy blueprints**, this address effectively signaled the **true commencement of the Trump presidency**. It marked the beginning of a critical period requiring **direct engagement with Congress**, moving beyond reliance on executive orders that bypass legislative input and oversight. The president now faces the **imperative to forge consensus** on complex societal challenges, such as establishing a fair, affordable, and equitable healthcare system for over 320 million Americans, rather than continuing to rely on reheated campaign slogans and the belittling of rivals to enthusiastic supporters.

Sarah Anderson, a political science professor at UC Santa Barbara, observed, "What I heard was more of a recognition that talking to Congress was really different from talking at a rally. A recognition that he’s bargaining with Congress and the courts. That’s what he’s finding out right now — that this isn’t an authoritarian regime. There are checks and balances." This sentiment underscores the **shift from campaign rhetoric to the realities of governance**.

### The Demands of Governing

The time has arrived for President Trump to **demonstrate his capacity for achieving tangible results**. By this juncture in his tenure, his predecessor, Barack Obama, had already successfully enacted the substantial $831 billion stimulus package and signed the landmark Lily Ledbetter Fair Pay Act into law. These were **significant legislative achievements** that directly impacted the nation's economy and workers' rights.

President Trump is now exhibiting early signs of acknowledging that the act of governing is **considerably more demanding and intricate than campaigning**. This week, he candidly informed a gathering of the nation's governors that healthcare is "an unbelievably complex subject. Nobody knew that healthcare could be so complicated." In reality, the complexity of healthcare has been **widely understood by most informed individuals for a considerable time**. Since the era of the Great Depression, only two presidents, Lyndon B. Johnson and Barack Obama, have managed to broadly expand healthcare coverage across the nation. Similar to them, President Trump benefits from the advantage of a supportive Congress. However, despite seven years of consistent advocacy for the repeal of the Affordable Care Act, the Republican party remains **notably divided on a viable replacement strategy**, highlighting the inherent difficulties in achieving consensus on such a critical issue.

Chris Hoene, executive director of the nonpartisan California Budget and Policy Center in Sacramento, commented, "The speech was mellow and optimistic, without a lot of specifics. The rhetoric about helping low- and middle-income people is optimistic, but the policy proposals we expect to come down are not aimed at helping them." Hoene referenced the potential adverse impact on "the 5 million Californians who would lose their health care (should the Affordable Care Act be repealed) or all the safety net programs that would be cut" to fund Trump's proposed $54 billion increase in defense spending. This perspective highlights a **significant concern regarding the disconnect between optimistic rhetoric and potentially detrimental policy outcomes for vulnerable populations**.

### Navigating a New Arena

Navigating the intricate dynamics of a co-equal branch of government represents **unfamiliar territory for President Trump**. He spent the majority of his adult life operating within a family business that he inherited, a professional environment where he was accustomed to functioning without the direct scrutiny of adversarial boards or the constant threat of termination. His established business negotiation style, as famously outlined in his book "The Art of the Deal," often involved employing **aggressive opening offers to gain strategic leverage**. This particular approach may not translate effectively or yield the desired results within the complex and nuanced political arena. "Once he got into office he’s facing things that a CEO of a company like his wouldn’t face," Anderson noted, emphasizing the **fundamental differences in accountability and operational constraints between the corporate world and public service**.

### Avenues for Collaboration: Infrastructure

Certain aspects of his address did, however, present **potential avenues for bipartisan collaboration and progress**. President Trump announced his administration's plans to formally request a substantial $1 trillion infrastructure bill from Congress, with the stated goal of revitalizing the nation's aging roads and bridges. Democrats have generally expressed an openness to such infrastructure initiatives, although they may voice objections to his proposed funding mechanisms, particularly those relying on "both public and private capital." Trump articulated that this ambitious effort "will be guided by two core principles: Buy American, and hire American"—a distinctly populist message that is **likely to resonate with a broad spectrum of Democrats**, especially those representing constituencies in the historically industrial Rust Belt states.

Carah Ong Whaley, co-author of "American Government: Roots and Reform" and a political science lecturer at the University of Virginia, stated, "The clearest outreach was on infrastructure. For the Democrats who do want to address infrastructure, will this be an area will they work on with him or will they prevent them from working across lines?" This question highlights the **critical juncture at which bipartisan cooperation on infrastructure could either flourish or falter**, depending on the political will and strategic decisions made by both parties.

### Persistent Divides: Immigration

While President Trump declared on Tuesday that "every problem can be solved. And every hurting family can find healing, and hope," his administration's rhetoric and actions continued to **target immigrants**. He dedicated a significant portion of his speech, notably without providing specific statistical evidence, to **insinuating a propensity for crime among immigrant populations**. In furtherance of this agenda, he announced the creation of a new office within the Department of Homeland Security, to be known as Voice (Victims of Immigration Crime Engagement), with the stated purpose of assisting "American victims." On a night where President Trump ostensibly attempted to adopt a more conciliatory and unifying tone towards certain segments of the American populace who felt alienated, **immigrants did not receive the same consideration or a similar gesture of goodwill**. This selective approach underscores a **persistent division in his administration's messaging and policy priorities**.

Missing from the congressional address Tuesday were staples of a President Donald Trump speech. | Getty Six things Trump didn’t say in his address to Congress Missing were many staples of a Trump speech along with many of the things we have come to expect from the president's annual speech to Congress. President Donald Trump delivered a largely conventional address Tuesday night before a joint session of Congress that was a toned down version of his blustering, unpredictable self. Missing were staples of a Trump speech: talk of crowd size, alleged voter fraud and and his own unfair treatment by the dishonest media. In their place, adjustments to some of his more controversial positions: "we strongly support NATO," declared the president who last month called the alliance "obsolete." Story Continued Below Here is more of what Trump didn’t say: I love Russia. Bogged down by investigations into campaign operatives’ ties to Russian officials, Trump steered clear of what is typically a staple of his foreign policy pitch -- that fostering a good relationship with Russia is good for America. In fact, he didn’t even mention the country by name once all night. At one point, Trump vaguely alluded to the special relationship he wants to have when he said America is willing to “find new friends, and to forge new partnerships, where shared interests align.” But overall, Trump appeared to be in distancing mode from the country that has most defined his presidency so far. The press is evil. Trump’s favorite target in recent weeks has been the “dishonest media” -- particularly CNN, which he likes to call the “Clinton News Network,” and the “failing New York Times” -- which he on Friday he called the “enemy of the people.” The vitriol with which he speaks about the press seems to have grown with the influence of his chief strategist, Steve Bannon, who has branded the media as "the opposition party." But on Tuesday night, the aggrieved Trump, who complains that he and his top officials are treated “very, very unfairly” by the press, was nowhere to be seen. Trump’s ire seemed directed inward earlier in the day, when he blamed his own team for not properly communicating his message. Trump knows how to read a room, and the politically savvy president must have recognized that those attacks -- criticized this week by former President George W. Bush -- wouldn't play, even with his base, in a formal address before Congress. Iraq or Afghanistan Trump did not mention either of the countries where the United States has fought wars in this century, the first time they have been absent from a presidential address to a joint session since George W. Bush’s first in 2001. President Obama even worked both conflicts into his 2009 address pushing his health care overhaul. Also absent was any mention of Syria, whose ongoing civil war and fight against ISIS was a focus of Trump’s campaign. Trump did say he would “demolish and destroy ISIS,” but the mention was fleeting. The most emotionally charged moment of the night -- when Trump honored Carryn Owens, the widow of William "Ryan" Owens, a Navy SEAL who died last month in a raid in Yemen -- was the only mention of American troops on the ground and in harm’s way. We should broaden access to health care. Candidate Trump promised to “broaden health care access” once he eventually repealed and replaced Obamacare. Though he embraced Speaker Paul Ryan’s five point plan in detail -- to the glee of House Republicans who were praying for specifics -- he dropped from his plans any mention of actually broadening healthcare access for Americans. Deport the 11 million undocumented immigrants. During a lunch with cable television anchors on Tuesday ahead of his speech, Trump hinted at a softening of his hard-line immigration stance. “The time is right for an immigration bill as long as there is compromise on both sides,” the president reportedly told the anchors, saying he wanted to allow undocumented immigrants who are not violent criminals to live in America without fearing deportation. That was a shift from his position on the campaign, when he said he wanted to deport the country’s 11 million undocumented immigrants. Though the president didn’t mention a compromise in his speech, he did say he believed “that real and positive immigration reform is possible,” and projected a more optimistic vision based on an effort “to improve jobs and wages for Americans, to strengthen our nation’s security, and to restore respect for our laws.” Overturn Roe v. Wade and return abortion laws to the states. Trump didn’t mention abortion on Tuesday night. He steered clear of same-sex marriage. Even when praising the conservative Justice Antonin Scalia, or the man Trump chose to replace him on the Supreme Court, Neil Gorsuch, the president avoided wading into the culture wars. As he ticked through the executive orders he has signed since taking office, he skipped over the one that rolled back protections for transgender students, and he made no mention of plans to defund Planned Parenthood as part of his budget, which has become Republican orthodoxy. In largely ignoring those issues, which for years have been among the most divisive for members of Congress and the American public at large, Trump seemed to stick to his promise from the opening of the speech, to “deliver a message of unity.”

President Trump's address to a joint session of Congress on Tuesday evening marked his most significant public appearance since his inauguration. The speech offered several key takeaways for observers.

**A Conventional Approach for the GOP**

While President Trump won his election by defying political norms, his Tuesday address was notably restrained. He avoided attacks on the media, flamboyant claims, and personal insults, largely sticking to his prepared remarks. This conventional approach was a welcome relief for Republican lawmakers who have urged Trump to temper his self-defeating impulses and focus on a clear message. Although the speech offered few specific policy details, it outlined clear objectives that could energize the Republican base. This moment echoed his nomination of Neil Gorsuch to the Supreme Court, another instance where he adhered to traditional political conduct.

Trump's tribute to Carryn Owens, the widow of a Navy SEAL killed in Yemen, was particularly impactful. This emotional high point, often sought by presidents in such addresses, drew praise across the political spectrum. Commentator Van Jones suggested that if Trump can replicate such moments, he could remain in office for eight years.

**From "Carnage" to a "New Chapter"**

In contrast to his inaugural address, which described "American carnage" and a landscape of "rusted out factories," Trump's Tuesday speech presented a more optimistic outlook. He declared that "a new chapter of American greatness is now beginning" and encouraged the audience to "think of the marvels we can achieve if we simply set free the dreams of our people."

While Trump did not solely rely on unalloyed optimism, addressing concerns about crime rates and immigration enforcement, his overarching message was one of hope and resolution: "Everything that is broken in our country can be fixed. Every problem can be solved. And every hurting family can find healing, and hope." This more harmonious tone, even if temporary, could make him more appealing to centrist and independent voters.

**An American Nationalist Perspective**

Unlike recent presidents who have positioned themselves as global leaders, Trump emphasized his role as the representative of the United States. "My job is not to represent the world," he stated. "My job is to represent the United States of America." This sentiment, reflecting the influence of his chief strategist Stephen Bannon, highlights a belief in the nation-state as a defense against globalism and corporatism. Trump affirmed respect for international institutions like NATO and the UN, but also for "the sovereign rights of nations."

Regarding the fight against ISIS, Trump pledged to work with allies in the Muslim world. He also reiterated his commitment to national security through "improved vetting procedures" to "keep our nation safe — and to keep out those who would do us harm," a stance consistent with his executive order on immigration and refugees.

Despite criticisms that he has exacerbated racial and religious divisions, Trump made a clear effort to counter these perceptions. He condemned anti-Semitic threats and a hate crime in Kansas City that resulted in the death of one Indian man and injuries to another.

**A Vision for Healthcare**

Trump reiterated his goal to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act (ACA), drawing some negative reactions from Democratic lawmakers. He expressed a desire for new laws that would "expand choice, increase access, lower costs and, at the same time, provide better healthcare." He criticized the ACA's "mandate" but assured that individuals with pre-existing conditions would retain coverage and that no one would be excluded from Medicaid.

However, most experts believe achieving such an ideal outcome is improbable, especially in a divided Congress.

**Improved Fact-Checking**

Critics have often pointed to President Trump's tendency to misrepresent facts. For instance, he recently claimed the largest Electoral College victory since Ronald Reagan, a statement later challenged. On Tuesday, however, there was a noticeable improvement in his factual accuracy. His comments on the 2015 murder rate and violent crime in Chicago were accurate, unlike previous statements. He also cited a 116 percent premium increase in Arizona for ACA plans, a figure supported by a Department of Health and Human Services report.

While some interpretations of his remarks on immigration's economic impact or his credit for job announcements may be debated, Trump largely avoided factual missteps that could be used against him. As Glenn Thrush of The New York Times noted, the speech appeared to have undergone nominal fact-checking.

Washington (CNN) President Donald Trump reached for poetry and conjured a vision of common national purpose Tuesday during his first address to Congress, shifting his tone from the dark, searing approach of his previous big speeches to the nation. Trump adopted a statesmanlike cadence, hitting notes of inspiration. For once, this most unorthodox of politicians struck a conventional presidential posture as he sought to stabilize his administration after a tumultuous five weeks in office. Though his language was more lofty and unifying than normal, Trump gave little quarter on the substance of his policies on issues ranging from trade, defense, immigration and counterterrorism. The result was a populist, nationalistic prescription that he said would yield "a new chapter of American greatness." "From now on, America will be empowered by our aspirations, not burdened by our fears, inspired by the future, not bound by failures of the past, and guided by a vision, not blinded by our doubts," Trump said, from the Speaker's rostrum in the House of Representatives. During a vitriolic campaign and a raucous start to his term, Trump has done little to reach beyond his base of deeply committed voters who revile the kind of political elites that the President was staring down as he spoke on Tuesday. But, beset by the lowest approval ratings of any new commander-in-chief of modern times, Trump made a palpable effort to court voters who didn't support him with an offer to lay down the battles of the past. In fact, his address ticked almost all the boxes of a traditional State of the Union style appearance. 'Embrace this renewal' "I am asking all citizens to embrace this renewal of the American spirit. I am asking all members of Congress to join me in dreaming big, and bold, and daring things for our country," Trump said. "I am asking everyone watching tonight to seize this moment. Believe in yourselves. Believe in your future. And believe, once more, in America." Behind him, from left, are Vice President Mike Pence and House Speaker Paul Ryan.
