documents.text: The Senate flipped, John Boehner expanded his majority in the House and now Republicans want to show they can govern. Before the election, top-level Republican aides and lawmakers described the strategy they hoped would let them notch some early victories before the presidential election season is in full swing in the second half of 2015 — a play by Boehner and other leaders to quickly shift away from crisis governing and toward a positive agenda. Text Size - reset At the top of the list is to get a funding bill passed to keep the government open through most of next year, freeing up the calendar to take on other issues like a GOP budget and a long-term highway bill. (Senate results by state) Legislating won’t begin until after Thanksgiving. Both parties intend to spend the weeks immediately after election organizing for the new partisan makeup of Washington. Capitol Hill will burst back into action by Dec. 1, and that’s when serious lawmaking could break out. Republicans are desperate to avoid fiscal fights in the first half of 2015, which is why they will try to use the first few weeks in December to pass a long-term spending bill to keep the government open through most of the next year. Funding runs dry Dec. 11, as does the authority for President Barack Obama’s administration to train and equip moderate Syrian rebels in their fight against the Islamic State. That provision could complicate the calculus for lawmakers’ hopes for a drama-free spending bill. Congress will also try to clear a host of lapsed tailored business tax provisions and renew a government-backed program that helps insurance companies protect against terrorist attacks. And depending on the result of the Senate, Democrats may push to confirm a new attorney general in the waning months of 2014 with Eric Holder’s impending retirement. (POLITICO's 2014 race ratings) The hope is that this spate of legislating will clear the calendar for the first half of 2015, so Congress can pass a long-term highway and infrastructure bill, lift the debt limit and pass a budget. With two chambers of Congress under GOP control, the budget process could give the party its best chance to alter Obamacare, particularly if Senate Republicans try to use budget reconciliation, which requires just 51 votes. Congress will also try to fix the Sustainable Growth Rate, a pricey formula by which the government reimburses doctors who treat Medicare patients. One wild card is GOP Sens. Ted Cruz of Texas and Mike Lee of Utah, who have together vowed to stymie any legislation considered non-essential during the lame duck period. Senior aides caution that anything – or everything – can change. But it’s certain that this lame-duck session will not include action on big items like immigration reform, tax reform or a large-scale fiscal deal. (PLAYBACK: Best of 2014) Just as important as the year-end legislating is the internal meetings that both parties will hold. A Republican majority in the Senate would need to devise the structure of their committees and the ratio of Democrats to Republicans. Perhaps the most drastic change will be a new slate of GOP committee chairmen that will attack the administration’s agenda. No fan of the Obama’s energy regulatory agenda, Sen. Jim Inhofe of Oklahoma is expected to ascend to be chairman of the Environment and Public Works Committee. Sen. John McCain of Arizona is likely to end up the chairman of the Senate Armed Services Committee — a prime perch for his continued broadsides on Obama’s foreign policy. And Sen. Jeff Sessions of Alabama, one of the most conservative senators, is in line to chair the Senate Budget Committee. An expanded majority in the House means there will be a lot of housekeeping on Capitol Hill. New members will arrive in Washington Nov. 12 for their orientation and leadership elections for both the House and the Senate, including new Democratic and Republican chairmen for the Senate’s campaign arms. The House, meanwhile, will also need to elect new chairmen for key committees. (Driving the Day: Top House races to watch) The Ways and Means Committee, which is gearing up to rewrite the tax code, will get a new leader in Wisconsin Rep. Paul Ryan. Texas Rep. Mac Thornberry, a 20-year veteran of Congress, will now lead the Armed Services Committee. The Oversight and Government Reform Committee, Congress’s top investigatory panel, will get new leadership, as Rep. Darrell Issa (R-Calif.) will reach his term limit. Reps. Jason Chaffetz of Utah and Mike Turner of Ohio are seen as front-runners for that slot. There’s one race that won’t be competitive: the chairmanship of the National Republican Congressional Committee. Some House Republicans spent the fall grumbling about Walden’s leadership. But Tuesday night will quiet those concerns.

Following the Republican Senate victory on Tuesday night, Texas Senator Ted Cruz proclaimed the "era of Obama lawlessness is over." Addressing supporters in Austin, Cruz highlighted the duty of Republicans to govern effectively should they secure a national majority. "If and when we retake the majority nationally, it will be incumbent on Republicans to lead," he stated. Cruz articulated a Republican vision focused on presenting a "bold positive, optimistic agenda to turn this country around, to bring back jobs and growth and opportunity." He further remarked, "The American people have risen up and frankly, at the national level, they have given Republicans another chance."

Cruz, frequently mentioned as a 2016 presidential contender, detailed a broad conservative platform for the incoming Congress. His proposals included substantial tax and regulatory changes. He also called for an end to what he described as "amnesty" for unauthorized immigrants. Additionally, Cruz underscored the necessity of defending religious freedom and preserving gun ownership rights. A central aim for the senator was the determined repeal of the Affordable Care Act, widely referred to as ObamaCare. Cruz strongly encouraged Republicans to "do everything humanly possible to repeal ObamaCare."

The 2014 U.S. midterm elections marked a significant turning point in American politics, most notably culminating in the Republican Party securing a majority in the Senate. While the final vote counts in a few states, such as Alaska, were still being finalized, the overall outcome was clear: the GOP had achieved its primary objective. To gain control of the Senate, the Republican Party needed to achieve a net gain of six seats, building upon the three seats they already held. An early victory for Cory Gardner in Colorado hinted at a close race, but as more results came in, the extent of the Republican triumph became increasingly apparent.

Several crucial wins solidified the Republican Party's hold on the Senate. In Kansas, Senator Pat Roberts successfully defended his seat against a strong challenge from independent candidate Greg Orman. Similarly, Thom Tillis, the speaker of the North Carolina statehouse, managed to unseat the incumbent Democratic Senator Kay Hagan. The political landscape, and indeed many observers including myself, were taken by surprise by how unexpectedly close the contest was in Virginia. It was a race where, had it not been for the presence of a third-party libertarian candidate, Ed Gillespie might have successfully ousted the incumbent Democrat, Mark Warner.

The Democratic Party faced a particularly challenging electoral map in 2014. A significant number of Senate elections were held in states that had historically been part of the Confederacy, with November 4th being the election day for all but one of these states (Florida being the exception). Adding to their difficulties was the well-documented historical trend of the party holding the presidency typically performing poorly in midterm elections. Despite these external headwinds, Democrats also suffered defeats in states they had previously considered strongholds. Maryland, for instance, elected a Republican governor, a rare occurrence not witnessed since Spiro Agnew left the office in 1969. The gubernatorial race in Florida, which garnered considerable national attention, also resulted in a Republican victory, with Rick Scott securing the governorship.

With the Republican Party now in control of Congress for the first time in a decade, a pivotal question arises: how will the party effectively leverage its newfound legislative power? Mitch McConnell, poised to assume the role of Senate Majority Leader and marking his thirtieth year in the Senate, struck a conciliatory tone during his victory celebration in Kentucky. He expressed a desire for cooperation, stating, "Just because we have a two-party system doesn't mean we have to be in perpetual conflict." However, the entrenched realities of Washington D.C. politics suggest that perpetual conflict might be an inherent characteristic for certain factions within the party.

Kentucky's other Senator, Rand Paul, offered a contrasting vision, asserting that a Republican-controlled Senate would actively pursue legislation aimed at countering the President's agenda, including renewed efforts to repeal the Affordable Care Act. Despite being recognized as a highly influential figure in national politics, McConnell will face considerable limitations. The projected Republican majority in the Senate is expected to be around 53 or 54 seats, which falls short of the 60 votes necessary to overcome a filibuster by the minority party. This procedural reality means that Democrats will retain the ability to obstruct or significantly influence the Republican legislative agenda.

Furthermore, the dynamics within the incoming Republican Senate cohort may not always align seamlessly with McConnell's leadership. Tom Cotton, who defeated Mark Pryor in Arkansas, had previously supported the government shutdown initiated by the House of Representatives the year before, while McConnell was actively engaged in efforts to resolve that very shutdown in the Senate. Similarly, newly elected Senators Joni Ernst from Iowa and Thom Tillis from North Carolina are unlikely to be easily swayed or consistently adhere to party directives without careful persuasion.

An initial assessment of the new Senate's internal dynamics will likely take shape even before the officially sworn-in members formally take their seats in January. A substantial portion of the federal government is currently operating under a continuing resolution, which is set to expire on December 11th. McConnell will undoubtedly be motivated to prevent a repeat of the governmental shutdown experienced in the previous year. Looking ahead, potential areas for bipartisan agreement with the President might emerge concerning trade policy, corporate taxation, and possibly an increase in visa allocations for highly skilled professionals. However, for those anticipating that the resolution of America's most pressing challenges will be an immediate consequence of the Senate's partisan realignment, a more nuanced understanding of divided government is essential. The inherent complexities of a government where different parties control distinct branches frequently lead to protracted negotiations and incremental progress, rather than swift and comprehensive solutions. The Republican majority's capacity to implement its agenda will necessitate a delicate equilibrium, influenced by internal party dynamics, the Democratic minority's procedural leverage, and the President's willingness to engage. The forthcoming months will undoubtedly offer a clearer picture of the Republican Party's true influence and their aptitude for navigating the intricate mechanisms of Washington.

**Harry Reid Congratulates Mitch McConnell on Senate Leadership Amidst Shifting Political Tides**

Following a significant Republican victory in the Senate, Senator Harry Reid, a Democrat from Nevada, has congratulated Senator Mitch McConnell, a Republican from Kentucky, on his forthcoming role as the new Senate Majority Leader. This transition follows the Republican party's success in securing a majority of crucial Senate races, signaling a notable change in the chamber's political direction.

In a statement released via email, Senator Reid recognized the decisive mandate from the electorate. "The message from voters is clear: they want us to work together," he remarked. He conveyed his readiness to cooperate with Senator McConnell, stating, "I look forward to working with Senator McConnell to get things done for the middle class."

Senator Reid's brief remarks underscored a moment of reflection for the Democratic party. Many within the party are anticipated to scrutinize the election outcomes, with some already privately attributing the results to the President and the White House. Senator Reid's strategic approach, characterized as a "prevent defense," did not achieve the intended success.

The statement also confirmed Senator Reid's intention to retain his leadership role rather than resign. Despite some internal discontent, no formal challenge to his leadership has emerged.

Focus now shifts to the upcoming lame-duck session, the period preceding Senator McConnell's official assumption of Senate leadership. Critical matters slated for discussion and action during this time include nominations and the approval of a comprehensive spending bill. These items represent the immediate priorities for the Senate as it navigates this leadership transition.

The recent Republican electoral success has profoundly altered the nation's political scene, heralding a period of increased legislative deadlock and governmental inertia for President Obama's final two years. This outcome, which granted Republicans full control of Congress for the first time since 2006, prompts an analysis of how the party intends to use its new power and what this means for American governance. The GOP successfully capitalized on broad public discontent with Washington D.C.'s perceived ineffectiveness, achieving a complete congressional takeover. This victory not only strengthened their House majority but also secured vital wins in several key governor's races.

### A New Senate Leader and Legislative Outlook

Senator Mitch McConnell of Kentucky, a seasoned politician, is poised to become the Senate Majority Leader following his successful re-election. After his win, McConnell voiced a desire for bipartisan collaboration, stating, "We have a duty to work together on issues where we can agree. I think we have a duty to do that." However, this sentiment contrasts with the Republican Party's legislative record since regaining the House in 2010, a period marked by a general unwillingness to advance significant legislation. Therefore, even with McConnell's stated aims, substantial legislative achievements in the coming years seem limited.

### Unresolved National Issues and Presidential Authority

This political climate suggests that critical national issues, such as comprehensive immigration reform, decisive action on climate change, addressing income inequality, and modernizing infrastructure, will likely remain stalled. In this context, President Obama may increasingly resort to his veto power to block legislation from the Republican-controlled Congress. The future of the Affordable Care Act remains uncertain, as it was not a primary campaign focus for many Republican candidates.

### Shifting Party Dynamics and Future Elections

The Republican Party's rise, particularly their Senate takeover, could empower more conservative wings, potentially shifting the party's platform further right. For example, Senator Ted Cruz initially expressed doubts about endorsing McConnell for Majority Leader. House Speaker John Boehner did not explicitly prioritize immigration reform for the incoming Congress but acknowledged the significant responsibility entrusted to them by voters, remarking, "We are humbled by the responsibility the American people have placed with us, but this is not a time for celebration. It’s time for government to start getting results and implementing solutions to the challenges facing our country, starting with our still-struggling economy."

The loss of the Senate also significantly curtails a key presidential power Obama had largely maintained after Republicans took the House in 2010: his ability to confirm judicial nominees. This development signals prolonged political disputes over any potential Supreme Court vacancies. The implications for the 2016 presidential election are complex. While Republicans' increased ability to obstruct Obama's agenda could further fuel voter dissatisfaction, potentially boosting their desire for a White House change, this strategy might also backfire. If Republicans are seen as overreaching or excessively catering to their base, they could alienate a broader electorate, appearing to be part of the problem they campaigned against.

### Election Night Trends and Voter Mood

This election night outcome echoed the significant Republican gains of 2010, a result President Obama himself called a decisive "thumping" of the Democrats. Exit poll data indicated that the results were largely driven by the President's declining approval ratings and widespread dissatisfaction with an economic recovery that, despite statistical improvements, many Americans felt did not translate into tangible benefits. On a positive note for Republicans, they achieved notable successes by electing two women to the Senate, along with one Black senator and one Black female representative. Key Democratic Senate incumbents lost in Arkansas, Colorado, and North Carolina, while Republicans also secured Senate seats in Montana, West Virginia, Iowa, and South Dakota. Many of these Senate races were expected to be highly competitive. Even on election night, there was a possibility that Senate control would remain undecided for weeks, with potential runoffs and an independent senator yet to declare his party affiliation for caucus purposes. A panel discussion highlighted the Republicans' historic House majority and potential challenges Speaker John Boehner might face in managing more ideologically driven colleagues.

However, states like Kansas, Arkansas, Georgia, Kentucky, Iowa, and Colorado appeared to shift decisively towards the GOP in the campaign's final days. Republican candidates effectively targeted the President in their messaging, a tactic that proved highly successful. The NBC News national exit poll revealed the President's approval rating at 45%, a significant nine-point drop from his re-election two years prior. Concurrently, 70% of voters expressed dissatisfaction with economic conditions, with only 29% believing the economy was performing well.

### Gubernatorial Races and Emerging Presidential Candidates

In closely watched gubernatorial contests, Wisconsin Governor Scott Walker defeated Democrat Mary Burke, while Ohio's John Kasich secured re-election. These victories position both Walker and Kasich as potential 2016 presidential contenders. In Florida, Republican Governor Rick Scott narrowly won against Democrat Charlie Crist. Texas saw Republican Attorney General Greg Abbott achieve a decisive victory against Democratic State Senator Wendy Davis. Illinois elected Republican Bruce Rauner, unseating incumbent Democrat Pat Quinn. In a notable exception for Democrats, businessman Tom Wolf ousted the unpopular Republican Governor of Pennsylvania, Tom Corbett.

The Fix’s Chris Cillizza breaks down how close the battle for the Senate really is. (The Washington Post) The Fix’s Chris Cillizza breaks down how close the battle for the Senate really is. (The Washington Post) Sen. Ted Cruz spent the final weekend of the midterms on the far edge of the country trying to help fellow Republican Dan Sullivan win a race the GOP is counting on in its effort to retake the Senate. It’s a team-player role the tea party firebrand from Texas has filled a handful of times this fall — but one he plans to abandon if Republicans win control of both congressional chambers. In an interview at the Hotel Captain Cook here between campaign stops for Sullivan, Cruz made it clear he would push hard for a Republican-led Senate to be as conservative and confron­tational as the Republican-led House. Piggybacking on what House leaders have done, Cruz said the first order of business should be a series of hearings on President Obama, “looking at the abuse of power, the executive abuse, the regulatory abuse, the lawlessness that sadly has pervaded this administration.” (Live coverage: On the campaign trail) 1 of 13 Full Screen Autoplay Close Skip Ad × New faces to Washington? Or bigger roles? View Photos Hopes among these politicians are high that they’ll be headed to the nation’s capital – or upgrading from House digs to the Senate side of the Capitol. Caption An eclectic group is coming to Congress — or moving across the Capitol from the House to the Senate. Here are a few. Mia Love — Utah Love, a former small-town Utah mayor and fitness instructor from Brooklyn, became the nation’s first black, female, Republican member of Congress. She lost a House race in 2012 to incumbent Democrat Jim Matheson, who is retiring. Rick Bowmer/AP Buy Photo Wait 1 second to continue. Cruz also would like the Senate to be as aggressive in trying to repeal the Affordable Care Act as the House, which has voted more than 50 times to get rid of the law. Republicans should “pursue every means possible to repeal Obamacare,” Cruz said, including forcing a vote through parliamentary procedures that would get around a possible filibuster by Democrats. If that leads to a veto by Obama, Cruz said, Republicans should then vote on provisions of the health law “one at a time.” And when asked whether he would back Sen. Mitch McConnell of Kentucky for Republican leader, Cruz would not pledge his support — an indication that there are limits to how much of a partner he’s willing to be. At the heart of Cruz’s shift from the insular approach that defined his first year in office is a belief that he can use his popularity with conservatives to expand his influence in the Senate and improve his standing as he considers a 2016 presidential campaign. Cruz’s desire to turn his party further right in the coming months is one of the challenges already facing McConnell should Republicans regain the Senate, with tea party leaders inside and outside the Capitol spoiling for a number of hard-line moves. “Senator Cruz has been rather quiet over the past few months,” said Ron Bonjean, a spokesman for Trent Lott when the Mississippian was the Senate Republican leader. “That time seems to be coming to an end. I understand why he’s eager to go after Obamacare. But the reality is that it’ll take 60 votes to repeal it and Republicans will have nowhere near that amount. If Obamacare remains the focus, he will certainly get the base jazzed up about what he’s doing, but he won’t get rid of the law.” Cruz has gained some traction in terms of shaping the contours of what a Republican Senate would do, in part because McConnell and House Speaker John A. Boehner (R-Ohio) have not offered their own definitive vision of what a Republican-led Congress would look like. View Graphic Election Lab: See our current forecast for every congressional race in 2014 Two weeks ago, Cruz wrote an opinion piece in USA Today laying out 10 conservative priorities he thinks Republicans should pursue, including moving toward a flat tax and drawing a hard line on illegal immigrants. In the interview here, Cruz reiterated some of those points, such as approving the Keystone XL pipeline. McConnell has mostly been coy about what he would like to accomplish other than adding amendments to curb federal regulations to spending bills as a means of putting pressure on the president. “It’s never a good idea to tell the other side what the first play is going to be,” McConnell said at an event last month. Cruz is not interested in adding amendments that may put indirect pressure on Obama. He favors direct political combat. That way, either the president gives in, or, Cruz said, “you have clear accountability. It becomes transparent to everyone that it is the Democrats blocking meaningful progress.” Rep. Peter King (R-N.Y.), a Long Island moderate, said Republicans should be wary of Cruz’s guidance. “He is the last one we should listen to,” King said in an interview Sunday. “Don’t forget — a year ago he brought Republicans over the cliff.” Still, Cruz’s help on the trail has improved his image among some Republicans, while easing a measure of the anger aimed at him from some of the donors and colleagues he infuriated in October 2013 when he led a shutdown of the government. Cruz should be able to count on a handful of new friends, if not allies, when the Senate convenes next year. In recent weeks, he has campaigned for Senate contenders who beat Cruz-admiring insurgents in Republican primaries, from businessman David Perdue in Georgia and state Sen. Joni Ernst in Iowa to Sullivan and Sen. Pat Roberts, Kansas’s embattled incumbent. If she wins, Ernst is poised to be a powerful player in the run-up to the Iowa caucuses, the first nominating contest in the 2016 race for the GOP presidential nomination. Perdue, who has weak ties to his red state’s GOP base, could hew close to Cruz on some votes to keep conservatives in Georgia at bay. Sullivan, for similar reasons, could do the same. Cruz is not the only conservative Republican with national ambitions who is working to show skeptical Republicans that they can do more than squabble. Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.) has cut ads for the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, one of the groups that opposed tea party candidates in Senate primaries, and stumped for moderate former Massachusetts senator Scott Brown, who is running for Senate in New Hampshire. Cruz declined to say whether he’s going to run for president, but was dismissive of moderates in his party, particularly those who may challenge him for the 2016 Republican nomination. Of Jeb Bush, for instance, Cruz said he likes and respects him, “but I think we have seen election after election that when Republicans fail to draw a clear distinction with the Democrats, when we run to the mushy middle, we lose.” “At some point,” Cruz continued, “after Gerald Ford and Bob Dole and John McCain and Mitt Romney . . . we shouldn’t keep making the same mistakes over and over again. “One of the reasons Republicans have lost elections recently is that we have failed to engage in a meaningful way on the great issues of the day,” Cruz said. “We’ve played a prevent defense. You don’t win elections that way.”

query: What significant developments are expected to emerge from the Paris climate talks next month?

documents.text:
The upcoming Paris climate talks are poised to culminate in a landmark international accord aimed at curbing greenhouse gas emissions from both developed and developing nations. This potential achievement could be hailed as a significant diplomatic triumph for President Barack Obama and his contemporaries, showcasing their efforts to address global warming. However, a notable contributor to the progress being made in Paris is George W. Bush.

This assertion may be surprising, given the former Republican president's skepticism towards global warming science and his administration's legal challenges to Environmental Protection Agency climate policies. Yet, Bush played a pivotal role in initiating the very negotiations now nearing fruition. This might seem counterintuitive, especially considering the current Republican stance, which often seeks to impede the Paris negotiations and dismantle Obama's environmental initiatives. Nevertheless, Bush's presidency left a substantial mark, particularly during late 2007 negotiations held in Bali, Indonesia.

Although President Bush himself was not present, his administration's delegation became a focal point during the two-week talks. Al Gore, fresh from receiving his Nobel Peace Prize, delivered a speech criticizing Bush and urging negotiators to reserve space in their agreements for the next U.S. president. The atmosphere at the conference was tense, with Bush's officials facing vocal disapproval during the closing sessions.

Despite the contentious environment, the outcome of the Bali conference represented a significant breakthrough in international climate discussions. For the first time, nations of all economic capacities committed to addressing global warming. The resulting "Bali Action Plan" provided a foundational framework for future negotiations. Crucially, it established a precedent by securing commitments from rapidly developing countries like China, Brazil, India, and South Africa to submit their own emission reduction plans. This inclusion was instrumental in dismantling a major political obstacle to international climate action.

The momentum generated in Bali has faced numerous challenges. In 2009, ambitious expectations surrounding President Obama's leadership at the Copenhagen summit, where he and over 100 world leaders aimed to finalize a binding treaty, ultimately fell short. It took two more years, at the Durban climate conference, to re-establish the trajectory set by the Bali Action Plan.

While the Paris talks will address intricate details, such as providing financial and technical assistance to developing nations for clean energy adoption and climate adaptation, the core principle established in Bali—collective global engagement in combating climate change—underscores the strong likelihood of a successful outcome in Paris. Yvo de Boer, former top UN climate official, described Bali as a "turning point" that is often overlooked.

Navigating the complexities of UN climate conferences can be challenging, with their technical jargon and political maneuvering. These annual summits, held since 1995, often aim for tangible outcomes to justify the extensive efforts. While host countries may highlight similar achievements year after year, each conference builds upon previous agreements, supported by ongoing work in side meetings and at UN climate headquarters in Bonn.

The foundational treaty for climate action was negotiated at the 1992 Rio Earth Summit, signed by George H.W. Bush. In contrast, the subsequent "Berlin Mandate" in 1995, under President Bill Clinton, stipulated that only developed nations were legally bound to reduce emissions, while developing countries received exemptions. The 1997 Kyoto Protocol, negotiated with the assistance of Al Gore, formalized this distinction. Despite U.S. efforts to introduce flexibility, including broader coverage of greenhouse gases and a cap-and-trade system, the Kyoto Protocol faced significant opposition in the U.S. Senate, which had previously passed a resolution against ratifying any treaty that would harm the U.S. economy or exempt developing nations.

Reflecting on Kyoto, some former Clinton negotiators acknowledged the difficulty in securing commitments from developing countries, who often cited historical Western emissions as the cause of climate change. Stuart Eizenstat, who led the U.S. delegation, described the hostility in the room, stating, "We completely struck out with developing countries." Daniel Bodansky, a former Clinton-era climate coordinator, characterized Kyoto as a "false start." Although Clinton signed the protocol, he did not submit it for Senate ratification.

George W. Bush subsequently withdrew the U.S. from all talks related to the Kyoto agreement. International criticism intensified amid reports of the Bush administration censoring climate science and his reversal of a campaign pledge to cap power plant emissions. Bush's substantive engagement on climate issues didn't resume until 2007, a period marked by Democratic control of Congress and growing international pressure for an alternative to Kyoto.

Harlan Watson, a lead Bush administration climate negotiator, noted the persistent question: "When Bush came in and said we’re not going to do Kyoto, the obvious question was: What’s your alternative?" The Bali negotiations, following all-night sessions, resulted in an agreement where developed nations committed to assisting developing countries with climate adaptation and clean energy infrastructure. This agreement also significantly advanced the concept of breaking down the barriers between developed and developing nations.

George David Banks, a former senior Bush White House environmental aide, recalled the skepticism from environmentalists and Democrats, who viewed the Bali agreement as less ambitious than Kyoto. However, he emphasized the pragmatic necessity of a new approach, stating, "If you were concerned about the climate issue, just because of the math and the increase in emission levels [counting the world’s major economies], you had to do something different."

The post-Bali negotiations have been complex. The Copenhagen summit in 2009, while not yielding a legally binding treaty, resulted in an accord where nations pledged to continue developing environmental plans, deferring the establishment of a formal legal framework until Paris. Banks credits the Obama administration for building upon the 2007 goals, acknowledging that their success would not have been possible without Bush's groundwork. Kalee Kreider, an environmental group veteran who was present at the Bali talks, agreed that Bush's team "deserve credit, coming from a terrible place, undermining the system, to getting things done," calling Bali a "tipping point moment."

The irony of Bush's foundational role in setting the stage for Paris is lost on many current Republican lawmakers, who often reject climate science and oppose Obama's environmental agenda. Their recent actions, including resolutions to overturn emissions rules for power plants, stand in stark contrast to the architecture of the Paris agreement, which Nigel Purvis, a Clinton-era climate diplomat, notes is "the architecture that conservatives said we needed."

The images of more than 500 people are being projected onto the facade of France’s National Assembly building during the 2015 U.N. Climate Change Conference (COP21) in Paris to “remind leaders that the world is watching,” in the words of the artists behind the project. The Standing March is the collaborative effort of the French artist known as JR and the American filmmaker Darren Aronofsky. The “representation of humanity” is, the artists said, a message to the 25,000 world leaders who have gathered in Paris to negotiate a plan that will limit the rise of the global temperature to 2°C (3.6°F). “We expect decisions, we expect commitments, and we expect time frames,” Aronofsky, the controversial filmmaker behind Requiem for a Dream and Black Swan, said in a statement on the project’s website. “It’s about people, from Tuvalu to New Orleans, whose lives are threatened by climate change. The world depends on a solution.” The conference and the exhibition — which will travel around Paris for seven days after a weekend on the National Assembly building — come less than three weeks after the terrorist attacks that left 130 people dead. “For security reasons, marches are forbidden in Paris. But our art piece is a silent march,” JR said in a statement. “And we are marching, backed by the Assemblée Nationale, the heart of the French democracy. We must think about our future, the future of our environment and this is our answer to those who want to control our present.”

## The Environmental Cost of Climate Diplomacy: Examining COP21's Footprint

The **2015 Paris climate talks, COP21**, were a landmark event, drawing an estimated **50,000 participants** to discuss the urgent issue of climate change. This massive gathering included 22,000 official delegates from 195 nations, alongside thousands of representatives from NGOs, businesses, activists, and media.

### The Significant Carbon Footprint of Global Gatherings

The sheer scale of COP21 resulted in a substantial environmental impact, primarily driven by **travel**. With attendees traveling an average of 9,000 miles round trip, mostly by air, the carbon emissions were considerable. It's estimated that the travel for 50,000 people consumed around **27 million gallons of jet fuel**. Burning this fuel releases approximately 575 million pounds of CO2, equivalent to about **22 seconds of global CO2 output**. When factoring in accommodation and local transit for the two-week event, the total emissions might add another half-second to global totals. These figures highlight the **environmental cost of critical climate discussions**.

### The Ambitious 2°C Goal and Current Shortfalls

A **primary objective of COP21** was to limit global temperature rise to **2°C above pre-industrial levels**. However, the national pledges submitted at the time were insufficient to meet this target. Even with emissions reduced to under 1,000 gigatons annually by 2040, significant global challenges would remain. The year 2015 itself saw global average temperatures **exceed 1°C above the historical norm**, already linked to increased droughts, wildfires, and sea-level rise damages. A 2°C scenario would **substantially amplify these impacts**.

### Progress and Future Pathways for Climate Action

Despite the challenges, **progress in climate action is evident**. Aimee Barnes of the California EPA noted that 156 emissions commitments from 130 countries were achieved two years before COP21, a previously unthinkable accomplishment. She cited the 2010 Cancún talks as an example of how foundational work at earlier meetings can lead to significant outcomes. COP21 also aimed to **mobilize financial resources for developing nations** to transition away from fossil fuels, a crucial step for countries like India. Furthermore, sub-national entities, such as California with its successful cap-and-trade program, can implement effective emissions reduction strategies independently.

### The Crucial Need for Enforcement and Economic Incentives

However, **optimism alone is not enough**. Past climate negotiations, like those following the Kyoto Protocol, have been hindered by a **lack of robust enforcement**. Many experts advocate for global climate agreements to include shared incentives, such as a **price on carbon**. Economist Steve Stoft suggests a global framework where nations set a carbon price of $20-$30 per ton, allowing them to implement tailored programs like cap-and-trade or carbon taxes. While this approach is supported by economists and negotiators, its full implementation at Paris was unlikely. Consequently, the outcome was expected to be **incremental progress**, potentially just enough to offset the emissions generated by the event itself.

## Senate Seeks Control Over Paris Climate Summit Outcomes

A significant push is underway in the Senate to ensure congressional approval for any climate agreements emerging from the Paris summit. Led by Senator Jim Inhofe (R-Okla.), a "sense of the Senate resolution" has been introduced, aiming to grant the Senate a crucial voice in President Obama's potential commitments on greenhouse gas emissions.

### The Core Legal Debate: Treaty or Executive Agreement?

The central legal question for senators hinges on how the outcome of the Paris meeting will be classified. Will it be a treaty, requiring Senate ratification, or an executive agreement, which the President can enter into without congressional consent? If the summit's product is deemed a treaty, the Republican-led Senate is poised to reject it.

However, President Obama's administration is actively working to avoid this scenario. Their argument is that any agreement from Paris will not legally bind the United States to specific actions. They further contend that existing legislation, such as the Clean Air Act and the United Nations Framework on Climate Change, already empowers the President to implement climate commitments.

### Administration's Strategy: Potential Downsides

The administration's strategy of bypassing Senate treaty ratification carries potential drawbacks. A key concern is that a future president could easily reverse the Obama administration's climate policies. In contrast, a ratified treaty would legally obligate all subsequent presidents to adhere to its terms.

Despite this, the Obama administration is expected to proceed with its existing policies aimed at reducing carbon emissions by 26 to 28 percent below 2005 levels by 2025, even without formal Senate approval of a Paris agreement.

### Expert Views on Presidential Authority

Nigel Purvis, founding president of Climate Advisers and a former State Department treaty lawyer, stated, "The Senate has already had its say." He explained the general principle: "if an international agreement doesn’t require a change in U.S. law, if the president has the authority to implement the agreement with existing law, you don’t need to send it to Congress for approval." Purvis also highlighted that presidents throughout U.S. history have entered into "executive agreements" without seeking Senate approval.

### Senators' Counter-Argument and Inhofe's Position

However, senators challenging the President's authority remain unconvinced. Senators Jim Inhofe (R-Okla.), Roy Blunt (R-Mo.), and Joe Manchin (D-WestVa.) have introduced a resolution asserting that the President must seek the Senate's "advice and consent" on any agreement stemming from the Paris conference.

Senator Inhofe expressed his firm stance: "Should the negotiations produce a more substantive outcome as European delegates announced, then there is no way around the Senate." He cautioned against diplomatic promises that might suggest otherwise, implying the President might overstep his authority.

The involvement of Inhofe, a prominent climate change skeptic and chairman of the Senate's Environment and Public Works Committee, alongside Blunt and Manchin, who have supported the coal industry, is not unexpected. Their efforts are focused on scrutinizing the President's power to implement climate change commitments.

### The "Legally Binding" Debate Intensifies

A significant point of contention is the interpretation of "legally binding" in the context of any Paris agreement. David Sandalow, a fellow at Columbia University’s Center for Global Energy Policy, explained that the agreement might be legally binding in some areas but not in others, particularly concerning emission reduction commitments.

Secretary of State John F. Kerry has publicly stated that the Paris agreement "is definitely not going to be a treaty" and will not include "legally binding reduction targets like Kyoto." This stance has drawn criticism from European governments, who anticipate a legally binding international instrument from the summit.

### Administration's Definition of "Binding"

Administration officials clarify that the agreement would not impose top-down, binding emission targets. Instead, it would rely on "bottom-up country commitments" made voluntarily. The "binding" aspect, they argue, pertains to a process rather than specific outcomes. Countries would be obligated to submit climate action plans, ensure transparency, and consult with other nations if targets are missed. The absence of an international enforcement mechanism, such as an "international climate police," further supports the argument that penalties for non-compliance would be difficult to implement.

### Precedent and Future Ramifications

A similar model was employed in an earlier international accord on mercury emissions. Senior Obama administration officials involved in negotiating that accord reportedly saw it as a precedent for the Paris agreement.

Michael Glennon, a professor of international law at the Fletcher School at Tufts University, emphasizes that a state's intent determines whether it enters into a binding agreement. If the U.S. explicitly states its intent not to enter into a binding agreement, then no such obligation would legally exist.

Glennon also points to the Iran nuclear accord as an example of a president circumventing the Senate through executive authority. In that instance, Congress eventually established a special review process through legislation.

### Congressional Action and the Green Climate Fund

In response to the potential for executive agreements, a group of 30 Republican senators, led by Mike Lee of Utah, introduced a resolution urging the Obama administration to submit all climate agreements to the Senate, regardless of their perceived legal binding status, especially those with targets or timetables. This resolution also threatens to withhold funding for the Green Climate Fund until such agreements are presented to the Senate.

President Obama proposed contributing $3 billion annually to the Green Climate Fund, but many Republicans oppose this. Over 100 House lawmakers and Senators Inhofe and John Barrasso have sent letters to congressional leaders opposing the funding request, vowing to block taxpayer dollars from going to the fund until climate agreements are submitted for Senate review.

Ultimately, compliance with any agreement will depend on national will and international pressure. As Paul Bledsoe, an energy consultant, noted, "What does legally binding mean in an agreement that is not going to have financial penalties for non-compliance?" He concluded, "The bottom line is it’s not going to be a treaty. The United States is not going to agree to anything that requires Senate confirmation. And that’s that."

Photo by Steffen Kugler-Pool/Getty Images On Monday, more than 140 world leaders will gather in Paris to kick off tense two-week treaty negotiations over the fate of a planet in crisis. If this were about any topic other than climate change, it might even make the news. Granted, there’s been a lot of other news out of France recently—a major climate-themed march in Paris will be canceled for security concerns. And there is going to be a lot of coverage of the Paris climate talks. But it will be nothing compared to the attention that would be paid to a last-ditch meeting to avoid a nuclear standoff—even though climate change is no less dangerous. As Climate Home previews, “a treaty at this scale has never been accomplished before, and the one under construction will affect the way the entire global economy operates.” Advertisement Maybe climate change tends to take a back seat because the talks themselves are a jargon-filled monstrosity of diplomatic protocol, which means no one—not even the diplomats themselves!—understands what’s happening half of the time. "The most experienced lawyer on the Earth will not be able to understand this text" says Oleg Shamanov, Russia envoy #ADP2 — Edward King (@edking_CH) October 23, 2015 Here we are, closing out what’s quite possibly the warmest year since the invention of agriculture 10,000 years ago, with our atmosphere’s carbon dioxide at record levels and emissions still rising. But, alas, the most interesting drama and diplomatic wrangling are buried in a sea of legalese and acronyms. Case in point: Peru’s environment minister, Manuel Pulgar Vidal—a key figure in recent years at international climate negotiations—recently tweeted a link to a document designed to provide a more-or-less official guide to the Paris talks. It’s titled: “Scenario note on the twelfth part of the second session of the Ad Hoc Working Group on the Durban Platform for Enhanced Action.” Not exactly helpful or soul-stirring. So, here is my attempt to translate diplomat-speak to commoner language, focusing on why everyone’s in Paris, what the major sticking points are, and what it all means: Advertisement “Ad Hoc Working Group on the Durban Platform,” often abbreviated ADP2, especially for hashtagging purposes, is the official title of the international climate talks. Over the last four years or so, representatives from nearly every nation on Earth have gathered about once every three months, primarily in Bonn, Germany. At these preliminary talks, ADP2 laid the framework for a draft agreement—an unwieldy 54-page document. It’s called the “Durban Platform” because back in 2011 in Durban, South Africa, world leaders agreed that the first global climate treaty would be agreed upon in 2015—which brings us to today, in Paris. This mega-gathering is officially the 21st Conference of the Parties to the Kyoto Protocol, or #COP21 for short. The text of the Durban Platform contains 1,300 square brackets that provide different options for wording. For example, here’s what the section on the global temperature target currently looks like: In Paris, it will be the delegates’ job to eliminate the square brackets in the text. Among the major sticking points: Advertisement How much and how fast should countries reduce their emissions?: The world’s first climate treaty, the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change, was signed by 165 countries in 1992. Enshrined the UNFCCC, is the idea of “common but differentiated responsibility” that although all countries should reduce their emissions, developed countries with historically high emissions—like the United States, Japan, and Germany—should make steeper cuts. Negotiations leading up to Paris produced a series of voluntary pledges, or Intended Nationally Determined Contributions (INDCs) designed to allow countries to set their own plans, and then be shamed by the international community into ramping them up. It’s worked, sort of, but since the INDCs alone are not enough, a major topic at Paris will be coming up with a method to keep increasing the rate at which countries cut emissions. Finance: One way to encourage climate-friendly development that is for rich countries to give poor countries lots of money. Developed countries have already committed to contributions of $100 billion per year by 2020, but there’s still no clear idea on where that kind of money would come from. Many poor countries, to their credit, have hard-coded their finance requirements into their INDC pledges, noting they’d be able to transition to renewable energy much more quickly with help from the international community. Loss and damage: Even with rapid emissions reductions, there’s still a lot of warming in the pipeline thanks to thermal inertia in the ocean and the inherent lag in the global climate system. Basically, if we stopped all emissions now, the amount of CO2 in the atmosphere would quickly stabilize and then slowly decline—but elevated levels would remain for centuries, barring the widespread adoption of some sort of carbon-sucking geoengineering. That means that climate-linked disasters will continue to escalate over the coming decades, and like most disasters, they’ll hit poor and vulnerable countries the hardest. Understandably, these poor and vulnerable countries want a mechanism in place to appeal for aid and help in adapting to future weather extremes. Ratchet mechanism: Since the Paris talks on their own won’t fix climate change, a key negotiating point will be how often countries should announce bolder targets. Rich countries are generally advocating a ramp-up in targets once per decade, while poor countries say new targets should be agreed upon once every five years. Advertisement Since the U.N. climate talks operate by consensus, the strategy this time around, to avoid the failures of Kyoto and Copenhagen, is to make everything voluntary. Still, the European Union—especially host country France—wants the Paris deal to be legally binding. That would mean it would need to be approved by the Republican-controlled U.S. Congress, which is about as likely as a snowball’s chance in the Oklahoma summer. So, the U.S. is forcefully opposing strict legal language in Paris. Some other key players to watch: The G77 China, which now contains 134 members (with China playing an increasingly minor role) is a major force for the interests of developing countries. China and India increasingly operate like heavyweight developed countries; they act as a sort of intermediary between the EU/U.S. and the truly threatened countries like Bangladesh, Burkina Faso, and the Maldives. Among the groups advocating for the strongest possible climate deal are the Alliance of Small Island States (AOSIS), the Vulnerable Countries Forum (V20), and the Least Developed Countries (LDC). Major fossil fuel producing countries like Russia, Venezuela, and Saudi Arabia will advocate for the weakest deal possible. If you’d like to follow along with the negotiations on Twitter, I’ve put together a list that includes the best climate journalists, activists, and diplomats from the talks.

#### The Stakes: Our Planet's Future

At the heart of the COP21 discussions lies the critical objective of securing a legally binding plan to limit global temperature rise to no more than 2 degrees Celsius (3.6 Fahrenheit) above pre-industrial levels. Climate scientists widely agree that failing to achieve this benchmark will lead to increasingly severe droughts, storms, and floods, posing a significant threat to the planet's future.

The conference will bring together representatives from 196 nations. Notably, numerous world leaders, including President Obama and China's President Xi Jinping, will be in attendance. Their participation is particularly significant, given that the United States and China are the largest emitters of greenhouse gases contributing to global warming. A central focus of the COP21 conference will be the review of greenhouse gas emission reduction pledges submitted by each country, targeting achievements by 2020. She was hot because she was so f—ing talented and talented and funny.” While Stonestreet remains single for now, he continues to try and find his dream girl by any means necessary.

Lily Allen, the acclaimed 30-year-old British singer, offers a unique perspective on using dating apps like Tinder. While many seek romantic connections, Allen reportedly utilizes the platform to cultivate friendships during her extensive touring schedule. Married for four years to Sam Cooper and a mother to two young children, Allen's Tinder profile candidly states her intentions: "I'm a touring artist wanting to meet people abroad who don't want to talk about the industry formally known as entertainment." She further clarifies her marital status and family life, noting she is "married, [has] two beautiful girls, a massive mortgage and ridiculous schedule." This transparent approach certainly sets expectations clearly from the outset.

## Hilary Duff's Tinder Takeover: **Lessons from the Queen of Swiping Right**

It's official: **Hilary Duff is on Tinder!** What started as a Reddit user's discovery of a seemingly real profile, complete with a screenshot and a plea about not getting a match, has been confirmed by the star herself. While many initially suspected a clever fake, the profile's link to Hilary's verified Instagram account hinted at its legitimacy. Now, Duff has confirmed her presence on the popular dating app during an appearance on the Valentine In The Morning radio show.

Duff revealed she's been actively using Tinder for a few days and is **currently "talking to probably about nine guys."** This rapid engagement suggests she's already found success on the platform, proving that even a busy celebrity can navigate the world of online dating. Given her fame and undeniable charm, it's no surprise she's making waves. But beyond her celebrity status, what can we learn from her approach to online dating? After a closer look at her profile, here are some **key takeaways for anyone looking to improve their own Tinder game.**

### **Authenticity is Key: Look Like Yourself**

Hilary's Instagram often showcases a more natural beauty, and her Tinder profile picture reflects this. With **minimal makeup – just a touch of mascara and lip gloss – she appears genuinely herself.** In a digital landscape often filled with heavily filtered or misleading photos, presenting an authentic image is crucial for building trust and attracting genuine connections.

### **Master Your Lighting: The "Smize" Effect**

Just like aspiring models on "America's Next Top Model" are coached to "find their light," Hilary's profile picture radiates a **flattering glow.** This attention to lighting can significantly enhance your appearance and make your photo more inviting. A well-lit picture can make a world of difference in how you're perceived.

### **Be Direct About Your Desires: Honesty Sells**

Hilary's profile includes a refreshingly straightforward intention: **"I want to eat pizza."** This simple, honest statement has apparently led to numerous conversations, demonstrating that clarity about your interests can be a conversation starter. Being upfront about what you're looking for, even in a lighthearted way, can attract like-minded individuals.

### **Avoid the Humblebrag: Keep it Low-Key**

When it comes to her fame, Hilary is playing it cool. She mentioned that she's unsure how many of the guys she's talking to actually know she's famous, as her profile only displays her first name. This strategy of **not overtly flaunting celebrity status is a smart move.** It allows for more genuine interactions and avoids potential matches who might be more interested in her fame than in her as a person.

### **Leverage Instagram for Insight: Syncing Your Profiles**

For celebrities, syncing their Instagram to their Tinder profile can lend credibility and provide a more comprehensive view of their personality. For the average user, **linking your Instagram can offer potential matches a deeper glimpse into your life, hobbies, and interests,** potentially leading to a stronger connection and a better understanding of compatibility.

Hilary Duff's foray into Tinder offers valuable lessons for us all. Her approach emphasizes **authenticity, clear communication, and a touch of strategic discretion.** What are your thoughts on her online dating strategy? Have you ever encountered a celebrity on a dating app, or do you have your own set of online dating rules? Share your experiences and insights in the comments below!
