To celebrate **Her Majesty The Queen's 90th birthday**, a series of special engagements will take place in Windsor. These events highlight significant anniversaries and the enduring legacy of The Queen's reign.

On **Wednesday, April 20th**, The Queen's first engagement is a visit to the **Royal Mail Windsor delivery office** on William Street. This visit commemorates the **500th Anniversary of the Postal Service**. Her Majesty, accompanied by **The Duke of Edinburgh**, will meet **Mrs Moya Greene, the Chief Executive Officer of Royal Mail**, and **Mr Peter Long, the Chairman of the Royal Mail Group**. They will observe the operations of the **Caller's Office**, where customers collect undelivered items, and learn about modern tracking technology. The royal couple will also witness how **postmen and women sort mail** and meet dedicated staff. A highlight will be viewing an exhibition detailing the **history of Royal Mail over the last 500 years** and unveiling a commemorative plaque. The **Royal Mail choir**, comprised of frontline staff and formed for a BBC television series, will perform.

Following this, The Queen and The Duke of Edinburgh will visit **Alexandra Gardens** for the official opening of the new **Bandstand**. They will be welcomed by **Mr James Puxley, Lord Lieutenant of Berkshire**. An exhibition will showcase the Bandstand's design, influenced by workshops with local schoolchildren. The Queen will meet **students from six participating schools** and the bandstand's designers and builders. A **children's choir** will perform, followed by a Shakespearean performance as part of the School's Shakespeare Festival. Her Majesty will then unveil a plaque marking the occasion.

On **Thursday, April 21st**, The Queen and The Duke of Edinburgh will embark on a **walkabout in Windsor**. They will proceed from the **Henry VIII Gate of Windsor Castle** towards the **Statute of Queen Victoria**. Here, The Queen will unveil a plaque marking **The Queen's Walkway**. This **6.3km self-guided trail** connects 63 significant points in Windsor, symbolizing the moment Her Majesty became Britain's longest-reigning monarch. The Walkway draws inspiration from London's **Jubilee Walkway**, opened by The Queen in 1977. The **bronze EIIR crested markers, embossed panel, and promotional app** have now inspired a **parallel network of Walkways across the Commonwealth**, recognizing The Queen's service as Head of the Commonwealth. Several Walkways are in development, with the first complete route launched in Malta in November 2015. By 2018, the aim is to have **100 Commonwealth Walkways**, connecting 5,000 points of significance.

In the evening, The Queen, The Duke of Edinburgh, **The Prince of Wales**, and **The Duchess of Cornwall** will light a beacon. This principal beacon will initiate a chain reaction of **over 900 beacons lit across the country and worldwide** to celebrate The Queen's 90th Birthday.

**June events** are also scheduled to mark Her Majesty's official birthday.

LONDON — Queen Elizabeth II, a figure of remarkable endurance, marks her 90th birthday on Thursday, a milestone celebrated by a nation that has witnessed her reign through profound societal shifts. Having served under 12 prime ministers, commencing with Winston Churchill, she has navigated the twilight of the British Empire, endured personal and national tragedies, and, as of September 9th, surpassed Queen Victoria as the longest-reigning monarch in British history, a tenure now extending to 64 years. Renowned for her composure, often described as the "stiffest upper lip in the realm," she is lauded by biographer Douglas Hurd, a former foreign minister, as "The Steadfast." For seven decades, she has maintained an enigmatic presence, invariably attired in her signature pastel ensembles and vibrant hats, a deliberate choice, according to royal observers, to ensure she is easily identifiable in public gatherings.

In true British fashion, the occasion is being commemorated with characteristic pageantry, including extended pub hours, equestrian displays, and an appearance by Oscar-winning actress Helen Mirren, who previously portrayed the monarch. Demonstrating her characteristic dedication, Elizabeth commenced her birthday festivities on Wednesday with a visit to a Royal Mail delivery center. There, accompanied by her husband, the Duke of Edinburgh, she observed postal workers and was serenaded by a choir. On Thursday, she is scheduled to ignite the first of over 900 celebratory beacons. The tributes continue on Friday, with President Obama set to extend birthday wishes at Windsor Castle. The celebratory period extends beyond the week, with pub opening times in England and Wales extended until 1 a.m. on June 10th and 11th, coinciding with the official June celebrations for ceremonial purposes.

A unique tribute comes from a British artist who, honoring her service as an Army mechanic during World War II, has crafted a monumental sculpture of the Queen's head from 800 car parts, including a crown fashioned from spark plugs. A new series of first-class stamps, featuring four generations of the royal family, including young Prince George, also commemorates the birthday. The commercial sector is capitalizing on the event, with retailers offering a range of commemorative items. While a bespoke 22-karat gold music box retails for $1,425, more accessible souvenirs like a $50 waistcoat emblazoned with the Queen's image are available. Reflecting the multicultural evolution of Britain during her reign, Nadiya Hussain, a Muslim baker and winner of the "Great British Bake Off," will prepare an orange drizzle birthday cake for the Queen. This culinary nod is particularly poignant, given that a 68-year-old slice of the Queen's wedding cake previously fetched $719 at auction.

The Queen's renowned self-restraint, a trait honed during wartime, is frequently cited as a cornerstone of her success in unifying the nation. Constitutional scholar Vernon Bogdanor commends her ability to maintain her views privately, suggesting this enigmatic persona has been crucial to her symbolic role. He contrasts her upbringing with contemporary expectations of emotional expression. Bogdanor recalls only two notable missteps: a 1994 comment about St. Petersburg being "not such a nice place" and, more significantly, the public's perception of her delayed response following Princess Diana's death in 1997, when she initially remained at Balmoral.

Maintaining the relevance of a venerable institution presents ongoing challenges. An ITV documentary, "Our Queen at 90," offered a rare glimpse into royal life, revealing that her great-grandson, Prince George, affectionately calls her "Gan-Gan." Prince William noted that some individuals are so overcome by meeting his grandmother that they faint.

Born on April 21, 1926, Elizabeth was initially third in line to the throne. Her marriage to Prince Philip in 1947, a union she pursued against her parents' wishes, followed a childhood infatuation. She ascended to the throne in 1952 upon the death of her father, King George VI, whose elder brother, King Edward VIII, had abdicated in 1936 to marry Wallis Simpson. Cultural commentator Peter York posits that the Queen's perceived "unbridled blandness" represents a form of "human bondage," yet Britons reportedly appreciate her legendary frugality, including anecdotes of her conserving energy by turning off lights and enjoying simple meals from Tupperware. In private, she is said to possess a sharp wit. York suggests her longevity provides Britons with a sense of continuity. He also notes instances of barely perceptible annoyance, such as her 2008 inquiry at the London School of Economics regarding the lack of foresight concerning the financial crisis.

While her mother lived to 101, and the Queen shows no signs of diminishing her duties, the author of "Royalty Inc.: Britain's Best-Known Brand," Stephen Bates, observes that the word "abdication" is considered taboo within Buckingham Palace. He expresses concern that the presumed successors – Charles, Prince of Wales; Prince William, Duke of Cambridge; and Prince George – may not possess the same charisma as the Queen. Bates describes Prince William as having become "just another middle-aged bald bloke in a suit." Despite these potential challenges, the Queen appears to have fulfilled the promise she made on her 21st birthday: "I declare before you all, that my whole life, whether it be long or short, shall be devoted to your service."

query: What key points did Director of National Intelligence James Clapper make regarding the intelligence community's findings on Russian involvement in the hacking during the 2016 election?

documents.text:
U.S. intelligence chiefs publicly defended their findings on Russian interference in the 2016 election, directly countering President-elect Donald Trump's skepticism. They asserted that Russia's highest officials authorized the email theft and disclosures.

Director of National Intelligence James Clapper stated to the Senate Armed Services Committee, "There is a difference between skepticism and disparagement." He emphasized, "Public trust and confidence in the intelligence community is crucial." Clapper, CIA Director John Brennan, and FBI Director James Comey were scheduled to brief Trump on the hacking and leaks from Democratic entities supporting Hillary Clinton. Trump had previously questioned the intelligence agencies' conclusions, stating, "hacking is a very hard thing to prove" and that they have been incorrect in the past.

However, Clapper informed the Senate panel that the intelligence agencies' confidence in their findings is now "very high." He added they are "even more resolute" about Russian involvement than when they first publicly stated their assessment on October 7th. Clapper also clarified that Russian hacking did not alter the election outcome. He testified alongside Undersecretary of Defense for Intelligence Marcel Lettre and NSA Director Admiral Michael Rogers.

The hearing, initiated by Senator John McCain, highlighted a rift between Trump and some Republican foreign policy leaders. While Trump has praised Russian President Vladimir Putin, McCain and others advocate for stricter sanctions against Russia, which has denied the hacking accusations. Senator Lindsey Graham declared, "Putin is up to no good" and that it's time for decisive action, not minor responses. He advised Trump, "Mr. President-elect, when you listen to these people you can be skeptical, but understand they’re the best among us and they’re trying to protect us."

Clapper noted that while finding areas of mutual interest with Russia is beneficial, "there’s a threshold of behavior that’s unacceptable." Trump, in a series of tweets, questioned the delay in his briefing and cited WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange, who suggested a 14-year-old could be responsible for the breaches and that Russians did not provide him with information. Clapper and Rogers dismissed Assange as a credible source, citing his past endangerment of U.S. lives by publishing classified material. Clapper stated the intelligence community has "a whole lot of respect for him" and that Trump's comments "hardly help" morale.

President Barack Obama, in a television interview, expressed hope that Trump would gain confidence in the intelligence agencies after receiving his briefings. Beau Woods, deputy director of the Atlantic Council’s Cyber Statecraft Initiative, commented that while skepticism is valuable, outright dismissal of intelligence is "unhelpful for finding the truth."

McCain described Russia's hacking as an "unprecedented attack" on U.S. democracy, urging Americans to be alarmed. The intelligence officials' prepared remarks stated Moscow "poses a major threat" to U.S. networks and has developed a "highly advanced offensive cyber program." They anticipate future Russian cyber operations will target the U.S. for intelligence gathering, supporting decision-making, conducting influence operations, and preparing for future contingencies.

Clapper announced plans to brief Congress privately and release an unclassified version of their review publicly early next week, aiming to be as transparent as possible. White House Press Secretary Josh Earnest confirmed the White House has the intelligence review, ordered by Obama. Earnest called intelligence community members "patriots" dedicated to factual accuracy.

Following the hearing, Trump tweeted that the intelligence report was politicized, questioning its source and timing after NBC News reported it detailed attacks beyond the DNC, including on the White House and Joint Chiefs of Staff. He also claimed the DNC prevented the FBI from accessing its computer information, referencing a BuzzFeed story that revealed the FBI relied on a third-party analysis by CrowdStrike because the DNC denied direct server access.

In their statement, intelligence officials noted the Kremlin's increasingly "aggressive cyber posture" in recent years, targeting various organizations through phishing and masquerading as third parties. They are working to "harden" U.S. government systems, but acknowledged that over 30 nations are developing offensive cyber capabilities, and terrorist groups like ISIS continue to seek sensitive information.

Washington (CNN) The leading US intelligence official said Thursday that his foreign counterparts have expressed concern that President-elect Donald Trump is disparaging the US intelligence community, potentially damaging public confidence. "I do think public trust and confidence in the Intelligence Community is crucial" both in the US and in other countries that rely on US intelligence, James Clapper, the Director of National Intelligence, told the Senate Armed Services Committee. "I've received many expressions of concern from foreign counterparts about the disparagement of the US Intelligence Community, or I should say, what has been interpreted as disparagement of the US Intelligence Community." Clapper was speaking at a hearing on global cyberthreats that focused almost exclusively on Moscow's alleged hacking during the presidential elections. The hearing gave lawmakers and senior US intelligence officials the chance to draw a line in the sand for Trump, presenting a united front on their conclusion that Russia is a major threat to the United States and was behind election-related hacking -- a conclusion the President-elect has refused to accept. "We assess that only Russia's senior-most officials could have authorized the recent election-focused data thefts and disclosures, based on the scope and sensitivity of the targets," Clapper, Undersecretary of Defense for Intelligence Marcel Lettre and the commander of US Cyber Command, Michael Rogers, wrote in a joint statement to the Senate Armed Services Committee. Clapper said at the hearing that there was a line between critical thinking about assessment and undermining those gathering the intelligence. "I think there is an important distinction here between healthy skepticism which policymakers -- to include policymaker No. 1 -- should always have on intelligence, but I think there's a difference between skepticism and disparagement," he said. **George Mitchell: Trump's Skepticism of U.S. Intelligence on Russian Election Interference is Misplaced**

Former Senate Majority Leader George Mitchell stated Thursday that President-elect Donald Trump's doubts regarding U.S. intelligence findings on Russian-led hacking aimed at disrupting the election are misdirected. Mitchell, a seasoned Democrat and former diplomat, advised Trump on CNBC's "Squawk Box" to prioritize American intelligence assessments over denials from Russian President Vladimir Putin and WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange.

A Senate Armed Services Committee hearing on Thursday is anticipated to focus on Russia's alleged involvement in influencing Trump's election. Mitchell cautioned that Trump's apparent alignment with Russia could lead to a "rude awakening," asserting that Putin harbors ill will towards the United States. Mitchell elaborated, "He sees the United States as the perpetrator of a great tragedy upon the Soviet Union and Russia. He has said that the greatest catastrophe in recent years was the demise of the Soviet Union."

President Barack Obama is expected to receive an update Thursday on an intelligence report he commissioned concerning potential Russian cyberattacks on elections dating back to 2008. Trump is scheduled to receive a presidential intelligence briefing on Friday in New York.

## McCain and Graham: Challenging Trump on Russian Interference

For eight years, Senators John McCain and Lindsey Graham consistently challenged President Barack Obama's foreign policy, often questioning his administration's approach to global affairs. Now, with Donald Trump preparing to enter the White House, the tables have turned, and the two Republican senators are poised to scrutinize his administration, particularly concerning cyberthreats and Russian interference in the U.S. election.

### A Stance on Foreign Policy

McCain and Graham, known for their independent streaks and bipartisan collaborations, are set to address the issue of cyberthreats to the United States, with a specific focus on Russian interference in the recent election. This comes despite President-elect Trump's repeated downplaying of Russian meddling in his campaign.

"You can expect I’ll do what I’ve been doing: I worked with Obama, though I didn’t agree with him, I certainly was a check," Graham stated in an interview. He elaborated on his alignment with Trump on domestic policy and certain foreign policy issues like Iran and China, but expressed uncertainty regarding Trump's stance on Russia.

### Investigating Cyberthreats

The Senate Armed Services Committee, chaired by McCain, is initiating an extensive investigation into cyberthreats posed by Russia and other hostile nations. While the committee's mandate is cybersecurity, the hearings are expected to heavily focus on Russian intrusion into the U.S. election. Key intelligence officials, including Director of National Intelligence James Clapper and NSA chief Adm. Mike Rogers, are slated to testify. These public hearings may be followed by classified sessions.

This investigation, which includes a new cybersecurity panel led by Graham, could represent a significant challenge to Trump's skepticism of the U.S. intelligence community within a Republican-controlled Congress.

### Navigating Trump's Skepticism

Despite a strong consensus within the intelligence community regarding Russia's efforts to influence the election, President-elect Trump has consistently expressed doubt, referring to the events as "so-called Russian hacking." McCain and Graham have acknowledged Trump's right to his own opinions but have also expressed a desire to understand the basis of his doubts.

While many Republicans in Congress have maintained a critical stance on Russia, Trump's admiration for Vladimir Putin and his desire to reset U.S.-Russia relations have created a complex dynamic. Other Senate committees are also examining Russian activities, but many Republican leaders have been hesitant to publicly criticize Trump's questioning of intelligence assessments.

### A Call for Scrutiny

In contrast to the more cautious approach of some colleagues, Graham and McCain have joined forces with Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer in advocating for a dedicated select committee to investigate Russian hacking and other cyberthreats. Both Republicans have voiced concerns about Trump's nominee for Secretary of State, Rex Tillerson, citing his perceived close ties to Putin and Russia. McCain has indicated that Tillerson's views on the Russia hacking will be a crucial factor in his decision to support his nomination.

### An Independent Path

McCain and Graham's push for a select committee was ultimately unsuccessful due to opposition from Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell. McCain, however, has maintained that his primary responsibility is to defend the nation, rather than to act as a direct check on Trump.

For Democrats seeking bipartisan support for their criticisms of Trump, the voices of McCain and Graham have been invaluable. Senator Martin Heinrich (D-N.M.) described them as "intellectually honest" and noted that their stance on Russia has remained consistent. Senator Claire McCaskill (D-Mo.) suggested that other Republican senators are hesitant to criticize Trump due to fear, contrasting them with the courage displayed by McCain and Graham.

While some Republicans, like Senator David Perdue (R-Ga.), have expressed hope for a unified congressional front, they have also acknowledged the independence and capability of McCain and Graham. Senator Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) described their questioning as "healthy."

However, few other Republican senators have publicly supported a select committee on cybersecurity, with Senator Marco Rubio (R-Fla.) being another notable exception in raising concerns about Tillerson's Russian ties. This leaves McCain and Graham as somewhat isolated figures within the Republican Party for their willingness to challenge Trump. McCain has humorously suggested that forming a new group of Senate contrarians might be a "kiss of death," implying a reluctance among others to join such a dissenting faction.

**Intelligence Chief Differentiates Skepticism from Disparagement Regarding Election Interference**

Washington D.C. – The nation's **Director of National Intelligence, James R. Clapper Jr.**, drew a clear distinction on Thursday between **"skepticism"** of intelligence findings and **"disparagement"** of the intelligence community. This statement was notably directed towards President-elect Donald J. Trump, who has publicly questioned the intelligence that **Russia interfered with the American election**.

Clapper made these remarks while testifying before the **Senate Armed Services Committee**. During the hearing, **both Republicans and Democrats rallied to defend Clapper and his colleagues**, a stark contrast to Mr. Trump's ongoing questioning of foreign involvement in the election.

**Key takeaways from the hearing included:**

*   Intelligence officials asserted that **Julian Assange, the founder of WikiLeaks, should not be afforded credibility**.
*   Senator Lindsey Graham criticized President Obama's approach to Russian interference, stating the President had thrown a **"pebble"** while Graham himself intended to use a **"rock"**.

Russia's alleged interference in the U.S. presidential election has been a significant concern. Senator John McCain stated last month that Russia's actions constituted "an act of war." While he reiterated these comments, he clarified that this does not necessarily mean initiating military conflict.

The intelligence community's assessment officially supports media reports from mid-December. These reports indicated that Russian President Vladimir Putin was "personally involved" in cyberattacks. The goal of these attacks was to interfere with the United States presidential election.

In an interview with NPR on December 15, U.S. President Barack Obama vowed that the U.S. would take action. He stated the response would be "at a time and place of our own choosing." President Obama also mentioned, "Mr. Putin is well aware of my feelings about this, because I spoke to him directly about it."

On December 29, President Obama took concrete steps. He sanctioned two Russian intelligence services believed to be involved in the hacks. These were the Russian military intelligence agency, the GRU, and the FSB, the successor to the KGB responsible for counterintelligence and internal security. Additionally, he expelled 35 Russian officials from the U.S. who were believed to be intelligence agents.

Following threats of retaliation from Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov, President Putin ultimately declined to retaliate.

The question arises: wasn't the hacking of the Democratic National Committee and other entities by Russia already known? The recent intelligence assessment aims to provide official details regarding both the actors involved and their intentions.

Prior to mid-December, Putin himself had not been directly blamed for hacks that resulted in damaging leaks to the Clinton campaign. However, in October, Director of National Intelligence James Clapper came close to this conclusion. He stated that "based on the scope and sensitivity of these efforts... only Russia’s senior-most officials could have authorized these activities."

Furthermore, separate intelligence leaks to The New York Times and The Washington Post on December 9 marked a significant development. For the first time, these leaks claimed the intent of the hacking was to sway the election in favor of Donald Trump. This was a shift from the previous understanding, which suggested the goal was simply to sow generalized distrust.

It is important to note that it has not yet been suggested that these cyberattacks successfully altered the actual vote tally for either presidential candidate. This intelligence assessment now represents the official position of the U.S. intelligence community.

Information regarding the specifics of these events has been released gradually. Often, this information was provided anonymously and unofficially over several months. As early as mid-June, the Democratic National Committee reported a breach of its computer network. The cybersecurity firm CrowdStrike publicly attributed the intrusion to Russian hackers after analyzing the incident.

In July, after emails stolen from the DNC appeared on WikiLeaks, Democratic members of Congress also pointed fingers at Russia. Clinton campaign manager Robby Mook alleged, "It was the Russians who perpetrated this leak for the purpose of helping Donald Trump and hurting Hillary Clinton."

It wasn't until September that anonymous federal officials confirmed to The New York Times the intelligence community's "high confidence" in Russian government involvement in the hack. This confirmation did not definitively include the subsequent leak. Doubts remained as to whether the hacks were merely "routine cyberespionage" or specifically intended to influence the election.

Then, in October, Director of National Intelligence James Clapper officially blamed Russia. He specified government actors, not independent Russian cybercriminals. He cited "the scope and sensitivity of these efforts" as evidence. He further declared that these actions "were intended to interfere with the U.S. election process." Days later, emails stolen from Clinton campaign chairman John Podesta were published on WikiLeaks.

FILE - In this Feb. 9, 2016, file photo, National Intelligence Director James Clapper speaks on Capitol Hill in Washington. Clapper is among top U.S. intelligence officials set to testify on Jan. 5, 2017, at a Senate hearing to be dominated by accusations Russia meddled in America’s presidential election... (Associated Press)

WASHINGTON (AP) — U.S. intelligence officials have delivered a classified report detailing Russian and other foreign interference in American elections to the White House. A U.S. official confirmed Thursday that President Barack Obama received the report. President-elect Donald Trump is scheduled for a briefing on the findings Friday. The official, who spoke anonymously, was not authorized to discuss the matter publicly.

The Director of National Intelligence indicated that an unclassified version of the report is tentatively slated for release early next week.

Top U.S. intelligence officials have stated that Russia presents a significant and escalating threat to American government, military, diplomatic, and commercial operations, as well as the nation's critical infrastructure. National Intelligence Director James Clapper and other intelligence leaders are testifying before a Senate Armed Services Committee hearing focused on Russia's alleged interference in the U.S. presidential election. They noted that Russia is among at least 30 nations developing cyberattack capabilities.

President-elect Trump has voiced skepticism regarding Russia's involvement in the election hacking. U.S. intelligence agencies, however, maintain their assessment. The officials are not expected to reveal specifics of the foreign influence report before President Obama's briefing on Thursday.

Earlier, top U.S. intelligence officials were scheduled to testify at a Senate hearing addressing accusations that Russia interfered in America's presidential election to aid Donald Trump's victory. The Armed Services Committee hearing occurred the day before the president-elect was to receive briefings from the CIA and FBI directors, alongside the director of national intelligence, concerning the investigation into Russia's alleged hacking activities. Trump has been highly critical of these findings, even appearing to support WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange's assertion that Russia did not supply him with hacked Democratic emails. Scheduled to appear before the Armed Services Committee were national intelligence director James Clapper, undersecretary of defense for intelligence Marcel Lettre, and National Security Agency chief Navy Adm. Michael Rogers.

query: What are the main arguments presented by French politicians in support of the burkini ban?

documents.text:
The Jerusalem Post is dedicated to providing accessible journalism, delivering essential news and thoughtful analysis from Israel, the Middle East, and the Jewish world, all without a paywall. This commitment is vital as traditional media revenue models face growing difficulties.

### The Flawed Logic Behind Burkini Bans

Several French mayors have controversially banned the "burkini," a full-body swimsuit worn by some Muslim women, citing a desire to combat Islamist terrorism. This approach is fundamentally misguided, akin to fighting communism by outlawing red flags. While the threat of Islamism and terrorism demands a serious response, punitive measures like these bans project weakness and a lack of strategic thinking.

Thierry Migoule, a Cannes official, stated the burkini conveys "an allegiance to the terrorist movements that are waging war against us." This rhetoric plays into the hands of those who label counterterrorism efforts as "Islamophobia." In reality, Muslim women wearing burkinis are exercising a protected religious practice. Addressing the root causes of Islamism and terrorism, rather than targeting religious attire, would be a more effective strategy. The burkini, at minimum, allows devout women to participate in public life and social integration.

### Societal Anxieties and Feminist Divisions

The justifications for the burkini ban reveal deeper societal anxieties. Prime Minister Manuel Valls linked the burkini to "the enslavement of women," and his minister for women's rights, Laurence Rossignol, argued it was an attempt to "hide women’s bodies in order to better control them." Even Marine Le Pen invoked a nostalgic vision of French beaches. These statements question whether France's current values align with its ideals of Enlightenment and Emancipation.

From a religious perspective, modest dress is a valid choice. While imposed modesty can be enslavement, freely chosen modesty can be liberating, shielding women from societal pressures and objectification. This shared value of modesty offers potential common ground between religious Jews and Muslims.

The discourse on modesty also highlights a division within feminism. Some feminists view sexual expression as freedom, while others are concerned about the objectification of women and the demeaning of their intellect. This debate echoes past collaborations between feminists and Evangelical Christians on issues like pornography.

### The Perils of Libertinism and Fundamentalism

The current climate on many American campuses, where traditional social restraints are often disregarded, serves as a cautionary example. A perceived lack of modesty and an overemphasis on sexuality are correlated with a rise in sexual assault. While perpetrators are solely responsible, increased modesty and self-control could contribute to a reduction in such incidents.

Modern fundamentalism thrives in the moral vacuum created by postmodern libertinism. Islamist ideology and other extremist movements have flourished in this permissive environment. The pervasive Western embrace of libertinism is disheartening, while Islamist extremism is abhorrent. Demagogues exploit Western moral relativism to advance dangerous ideologies.

### Towards a Nuanced Vision

Banning the burkini is not a solution. Efforts should focus on supporting women's freedom to choose their attire. This requires a broader discussion encompassing lifestyle, sexuality, cultural norms, and political realities. The West needs a new moral framework and a revitalized sense of nationalism, rejecting past legacies of racism and sexism while re-embracing values like modesty, community, and family.

We must move beyond simplistic dichotomies, rejecting both nihilism and rigid fundamentalism. Islamist extremism is repugnant for its violent imposition of a worldview. Combating violence requires strategic planning, military action, and international cooperation. European nations can learn from Israel's counterterrorism experience. Extremist ideologies, rooted in flawed reasoning, can only be refuted by reason and a compelling vision for the future.

The author accepts both the burkini and the bikini. Intolerable are those who fail to acknowledge the complexities of these issues. The silent, reasonable majority must develop a nuanced vision that synthesizes tradition and modernity, respecting diverse perspectives while taking firm stands.

*The author is a distinguished professor of history at McGill University and the author of "The Age of Clinton: America in the 1990s." His forthcoming book will update Arthur Hertzberg's "The Zionist Idea."*

French beaches have become a focal point for a contentious debate, with recent incidents highlighting the enforcement of bans on the burkini. On a Nice beach, a woman was reportedly asked by authorities to remove her burkini. Similarly, in Cannes, another individual was fined for wearing attire described as leggings, a tunic, and a headscarf. These events have amplified the ongoing discussion about the prohibition of the Islamic swimsuit, a policy implemented by various French towns and cities in the wake of recent terrorist attacks. The incident in Nice, which was filmed, took place on the Promenade des Anglais, a site with a somber connection to the Bastille Day lorry attack. The video shows several armed officers approaching the woman, who then removed her burkini. Following this, an officer seemed to be recording the scene or issuing a penalty.

When I **invented the burkini in early 2004**, my primary goal was to **empower women with freedom**, not to restrict it. The inspiration struck when my niece, a hijab-wearing girl, faced challenges joining a netball team. My sister had to **advocate for her daughter's right to play**, questioning why modesty should be a barrier. Once permitted, her attire was entirely unsuitable for sports – a skivvy, tracksuit pants, and hijab, leaving her overheated and uncomfortable.

This experience prompted me to search for **appropriate sportswear for Muslim girls**. Finding nothing suitable in Australia, I reflected on my own childhood, where missed sporting opportunities stemmed from a desire for modesty. I wanted to create something that would **bridge the gap between Australian lifestyle and Western clothing** while respecting the needs of Muslim girls.

On my lounge room floor, I began designing. I **modified the veil**, reducing excess fabric, a decision that initially made me **nervous about community acceptance**. While the veil traditionally covers hair and shape, my design focused on a neck-covering element, which I deemed acceptable. Before launching, I **developed a sample and a questionnaire** to gauge public opinion and its potential to encourage activity. Despite initial hesitation from some in my community, the burkini became a **successful commercial venture**.

The burkini gained wider attention when **Surf Lifesaving Australia introduced a program to integrate Muslim youth** following the Cronulla riots. A young Muslim girl, wanting to compete, wore a burkini. In the wake of events like September 11, the Cronulla riots, and the French veil ban, I felt a strong desire to **prevent judgment of girls wearing these garments**. I wanted to emphasize that it was simply about a girl choosing to be modest, about **integration, acceptance, equality, and freedom from judgment**.

The Muslim community faced significant fear at the time, hesitating to visit public pools and beaches. I aimed to provide girls with the **confidence to lead fulfilling lives**. Sport is vital, and we are Australians. My intention was to create something positive, a garment accessible to **anyone seeking modesty**, including those with skin cancer or new mothers preferring not to wear bikinis. It was **never intended as a symbol of Islam**.

The name "burkini" was a quick decision, a blend of "burqa" and "bikini." Having grown up in Australia, "burqa" was a word I associated with covering, but it doesn't hold the same significance for me as it might elsewhere. My design was about **combining two cultures: Australian identity and Muslim faith**. The burqa itself is not mentioned in the Quran, and covering the face is a personal choice, not a religious mandate. I researched the burqa, finding it described as a cover-all, and combined it with the concept of a bikini.

The current negativity surrounding the burkini, particularly in France, deeply saddens me. I hope it's not rooted in racism, but rather a **misunderstanding of a garment that symbolizes leisure, happiness, fitness, and health**. The demand for women to leave the beach and return to kitchens is a step backward, **stripping away the freedom the burkini offers**. The comparison between the Taliban and French politicians is stark; both, in their own ways, seek to control women's choices. **No man should dictate a woman's attire; it is a personal choice.**

While I might identify as a feminist, I believe in partnership. I am the driving force, the "engine," choosing to support my partner while being the "quiet achiever." I wish I could convey to France that they have **misunderstood the burkini's positive message**. There are far greater global issues to address than creating controversy around a garment that represents joy and well-being.

I question the notion that the burkini doesn't align with French values. What does "French values" truly mean? If liberty involves dictating what women wear and do, it risks driving them back into their homes, creating a **backlash and division**. Isolating and pushing people away is detrimental to any society.

My initial testing of the burkini was thorough. I first tested it in my bathtub to ensure its functionality. Then, I went to a public pool to test the headband's stability while diving. I remember the stares, the curiosity about my attire. But as I dived in, the headband stayed perfectly in place. That moment was **transformative**. It was my first time swimming in public with the burkini, and I felt an overwhelming sense of **freedom, empowerment, and ownership**. I walked with my shoulders back, feeling the pure joy of diving into water. And the best part? I wear a bikini underneath – **I truly have the best of both worlds**.

The legal battle over burkini bans in France is set to reach the nation's highest administrative court tomorrow. This pivotal ruling will address a ban imposed by the town of Villeneuve-Loubet, a decision that has been appealed by the Human Rights League. A lower court in Nice had previously upheld the ban, deeming it "necessary, appropriate and proportionate" in the wake of recent jihadist attacks, including the one in Nice on July 14th.

Villeneuve-Loubet was one of the initial towns to prohibit the burkini, sparking a widespread and intense debate across France and internationally. The discussion centers on the implications of such bans for women's rights, the principle of secularism, and the freedom to wear religious attire. The State Council's decision is expected to establish a legal precedent that other French towns may adopt.

The controversy has already impacted individuals directly. One woman, a 34-year-old mother of two who identified herself only as Siam, recounted being fined on a beach in Cannes. She was reportedly wearing leggings, a tunic, and a headscarf, an outfit she described as classic. Her citation stated she was not adhering to "good morals and secularism." Siam emphasized that she was simply with her family and had no intention of swimming at the time.

In a **controversial incident** that has intensified the debate on secularism and religious attire in France, a woman was reportedly fined on a beach in Cannes for her clothing, which included a headscarf. The 34-year-old woman, identified as Siam, stated she was merely **sitting with her family** and had no intention of swimming. Her fine, as reported by AFP, cited her outfit as not respecting "**good morals and secularism**."

This event mirrors a similar, highly publicized incident earlier in the week where police in Nice were seen reportedly ordering a woman wearing a headscarf to remove her clothing. Witnesses described the scene in Cannes as distressing, with onlookers shouting and applauding the police while the woman's daughter cried.

France is known for its **staunch commitment to secularism**, rigorously upholding the separation of religion and public life. This principle often leads to a strong stance against overt religious symbols or clothing perceived as incompatible with French values. The debate over Islamic dress in France is **not new**. France was the first European country to ban the Islamic face veil in public in 2010, following a ban on headscarves and other conspicuous religious symbols in state schools six years prior. More recently, the focus has shifted to the **burkini**, a full-body swimsuit, which has faced bans in several French municipalities, including Cannes.

The legal landscape surrounding these bans has been **complex**. The Human Rights League (LDH) has been actively challenging these prohibitions. In one instance, the LDH appealed a lower court decision that upheld a burkini ban in Villeneuve-Loubet, one of the initial towns to implement such a ban. This sparked widespread discussion about women's rights, secularity, and appropriate swimwear.

The ruling by the administrative court in Nice, which deemed the Villeneuve-Loubet ban "**necessary, appropriate and proportionate**," was made in the context of preventing public disorder following a series of terrorist attacks. The court suggested the burkini could "**offend the religious convictions or (religious) non-convictions of other users of the beach**" and be perceived as a "**defiance or a provocation exacerbating tensions**." The decision from France's highest administrative court was anticipated to set a legal precedent.

These legal challenges raise fundamental questions about **unacceptable beach attire**. The debate centers not only on the burkini but also on the broader principle of being fully clothed on the seashore. Rights groups argue that burkini bans represent a "**serious and illegal attack on numerous fundamental rights**," including freedom of religion.

The political discourse has also been fervent. French Prime Minister Manuel Valls characterized the burkini as an "**expression of a political project, a counter-society, based notably on the enslavement of women**." Despite these debates and bans, citizens are generally permitted to wear headscarves in public spaces.

The incident in Cannes adds another layer to an ongoing national conversation about identity, integration, and the interpretation of secularism in a diverse society. The tension between upholding republican values and respecting individual freedoms continues to define contemporary French public life. While fines and bans are framed within secularism, many view them as discriminatory and a violation of basic human rights. The legal battles and public outcry highlight profound divisions on how France should navigate religious expression in the public sphere. The very definition of "**respecting secularism**" and "**good morals**" in beachwear remains contentious, with legal interpretations and public opinion often at odds. This ongoing saga underscores France's challenge in balancing its commitment to secularism with the evolving realities of a multicultural population. The implications extend beyond beaches, touching upon social cohesion and the rights of minority communities. International attention further emphasizes the global significance of France's approach to secularism and religious freedom.

The burkini, a full-body swimsuit designed to offer modest swimwear options, has unexpectedly seen a surge in sales, particularly among non-Muslim women, following controversial bans implemented in several French towns. This phenomenon has been highlighted by Aheda Zanetti, the Australian designer credited with creating this innovative swimwear and holding the trademark for the names "burkini" and "burqini." The French bans, introduced in approximately 15 southeastern towns, were ostensibly justified by local governments citing "security" concerns in the wake of a series of deadly jihadist attacks. However, this intense public debate has inadvertently opened up a new and significant market for Zanetti's creations.

Zanetti, an Australian-Lebanese designer who developed her first swimwear for Muslim women over a decade ago, shared her astonishment at the recent sales trend. She reported that on a single Sunday, her online store received an unprecedented 60 orders, with every single one coming from non-Muslim customers. This is a stark contrast to her usual Sunday orders, which typically range from only 10 to 12. "It's just been so hectic," Zanetti remarked, emphasizing the dramatic increase in demand. While she had not yet compiled specific sales figures for the preceding week, Zanetti confirmed a deluge of supportive messages. She noted that since the French bans were enacted earlier this month, she has received numerous communications of encouragement, with only a single disparaging email.

The outpouring of support comes from a diverse group of individuals, including cancer survivors and other swimmers who are seeking effective protection from the sun. These customers have expressed their gratitude for Zanetti's lightweight, quick-drying garments. "A lot of the correspondence... was that they are survivors of skin cancer and they've always been looking for something like this, saying, 'Thank god we've found someone like this producing such a swimsuit'," Zanetti revealed. This highlights a broader need for sun protection that extends beyond religious or cultural considerations.

The burkini gained wider international attention in April 2011 when celebrity chef Nigella Lawson was photographed wearing one during a vacation in Australia. The images, which quickly circulated across British newspapers and websites, sparked considerable online discussion. As The Guardian wryly commented at the time, "Perhaps she thought what she wore on the beach was her own business. How wrong could she be." This incident underscored the public's fascination and, at times, their judgment regarding modest swimwear choices.

While other modest Islamic swimsuits exist, Zanetti emphasizes that her designs were the first to be streamlined into a two-piece swimwear set that includes a head covering, offering a more practical and fashionable option. The messages of support Zanetti has received underscore a sense of solidarity among women. "Women are standing together on this," she stated, highlighting that the support transcends racial and religious boundaries. In contrast to the supportive messages, the single critical email she received questioned her motives, stating, "We prefer our women to be naked," revealing a starkly different perspective on female attire.

The burkini has not generated the same level of controversy in Australia, Zanetti's home country. This is largely due to the cultural norm of covering up on beaches to shield skin from the intense southern sun, a practice widely accepted and understood. In a broader international context, Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau recently dismissed the notion of implementing similar bans. He advocated for Canadians to rise above such controversies, emphasizing the importance of respecting individual rights and choices. This stance from a national leader further contrasts with the restrictive measures seen in some parts of France, suggesting a divergence in how different societies approach issues of personal freedom and cultural expression in swimwear. The burkini, therefore, has become a symbol of a complex global conversation about identity, modesty, security, and individual liberty.

Rachid Nekkaz, a prominent Algerian entrepreneur and human rights advocate, is providing financial backing to Muslim women in France facing penalties for wearing the burkini. Nekkaz has committed to covering the fines for any woman penalized for wearing this full-body swimsuit, designed to cover all but the face, hands, and feet. "I have resolved to cover all burkini-related fines to uphold their sartorial autonomy and, crucially, to nullify the enforcement of this unjust and oppressive legislation," Nekkaz stated.

This initiative emerges amidst European political efforts to prohibit attire favored by some Muslim women. France, which implemented a ban on the public wearing of the burqa and niqab in April 2011, has since introduced further restrictions. Violators of the burqa and niqab prohibition face a €150 penalty (approximately $205) or community service. More recently, the municipality of Cannes enacted a provisional burkini ban from July 28 to August 31, imposing a €38 fine (around $42) on transgressors, as confirmed by the mayor's office. The burqa ban was originally instituted under the administration of former President Nicolas Sarkozy. Sarkozy, a leading figure in the center-right Republicans party, has declared his candidacy for the upcoming presidential election.

Sara Silvestri, a City University London professor specializing in religion and politics, posits that such prohibitions inadvertently bolster extremist factions. "Groups like Al Qaeda and the so-called Islamic State gain traction whenever Western nations furnish them with evidence of anti-Muslim discrimination or stigmatization," she explained. Silvestri further noted that these statutes can foster a sense of alienation among Muslims, impeding their societal integration and potentially driving them toward radical ideologies.

Nekkaz has decried politicians for leveraging post-attack anxieties to curtail liberties in France, labeling it "unacceptable, inadmissible, and intolerable." He highlighted analogous restrictive measures surfacing across Europe, including German Interior Minister Thomas de Maiziere's announcement of a ban on full-face veils in security-sensitive environments such as educational institutions and government offices, citing national security and cohesion.

Aheda Zanetti, the originator of the burkini, characterized the ban as "misconstrued." She remarked to CNN, "I find it astonishing that a politician would intervene in the choice of swimwear women make." Zanetti underscored the burkini as a positive garment selected by numerous women for diverse lifestyles. She believes Cannes Mayor David Lisnard has fundamentally misunderstood the garment's purpose. Zanetti stated that women she has encountered opt for Islamic-style attire like the niqab voluntarily, without coercion. She even cited instances where husbands discouraged their wives from wearing the niqab, asserting women's autonomy.

Ironically, Zanetti reported a substantial commercial upswing attributable to the ban, with sales escalating by 90% in the preceding week. Her company, Ahiida, based in western Sydney, has experienced robust online sales and a 200% surge in global support from women. "If only he understood the empowerment he's bestowing upon women by forbidding these garments. We persist," she commented.

Nekkaz views his role as a reminder to European democracies of their foundational commitment to fundamental freedoms, which he contends are being eroded for women who choose traditional Islamic dress. Although married to a woman who does not wear traditional Islamic coverings, Nekkaz, who relinquished his French nationality in 2013, acknowledges that the niqab or burkini might not represent the most advantageous choice for societal integration. Nevertheless, echoing the sentiments of Voltaire, he declared, "Even if I disagree with these women, I will defend their freedom of expression until my last breath."

Human rights advocates also offer their perspectives. Hervé Lavisse, president of the Cannes-Grasse chapter of the Human Rights League, predicted the burkini ban would prove counterproductive, potentially exacerbating tensions rather than fostering conciliation. Nekkaz reaffirmed his resolve: "I have decided to deploy my financial resources to cover these fines, thereby safeguarding these women's liberties." As of his declaration, 15 women had contacted him for fine remittance, with an estimated hundred penalties anticipated during the decree's summer application period.

LIFEGUARDS in Australia wear them. A mainstream British retailer sells a fashion version of them on the high street. But the “burkini”, a body-covering swimsuit (named with the portmanteau of “burqa” and “bikini”), has been banned this summer by the mayor of Cannes from his stretch of Mediterranean beach, as well as by a dozen other mayors of French seaside towns. In countries with a tradition of liberal multiculturalism, such a ban is greeted by incomprehension, if not ridicule. Within France, however, it enjoys widespread political backing, not just from the far-right National Front but also from the mainstream right and left. Manuel Valls, the Socialist prime minister, has argued that the burkini is “not a fashion item”, but represents the “enslavement of women”. Why are the French so offended by Islamic body covering? The government’s defence of the burkini ban rests on worries about religious tension and public order after recent terrorist attacks, coupled with two underlying principles. The first is laïcité, a strict form of secularism enshrined by law in 1905 after a struggle against authoritarian Catholicism. This principle is supposed to keep religion out of public life, and has been the basis of previous French bans: on the headscarf (and other “conspicuous” religious symbols, including the Jewish kippah and oversized crucifixes) in state schools (in 2004), and the face-covering niqab in all public places (in 2010). The other principle is women’s equality. It may appear bizarre, or frivolous, to argue that women should bare more flesh. But many on the French left in particular regard the need to protect women from a male-imposed doctrine as being at stake—and are willing to put it even before liberty, another founding value of republican France. The logic of the burkini, says Laurence Rossignol, the Socialist women’s minister, is to “hide women’s bodies in order better to control them”. Get our daily newsletter Upgrade your inbox and get our Daily Dispatch and Editor's Picks. Over the years, such efforts have long been met with dismay, if not derision, outside France. When the French began to debate a ban on the burqa in 2009, for instance, Barack Obama declared in Cairo that Western countries should avoid “dictating what clothes a Muslim woman should wear” under “the pretence of liberalism”. Some civil-liberties groups within France have tried—but so far failed—to get the burkini ban overturned in the courts. Yet French governments bristle at the notion that their various attempts to defend laïcité amount to intolerance or an infringement of the freedom of expression. They may note that in 2014 the European Court of Human Rights upheld France’s burqa ban. What outsiders fail to understand, the French argue, is that such body wear is not just a casual choice but part of an attempt by political Islamism to win recruits and test the resilience of the French republic. Mr Valls dismisses as naive those who see it as being no different than a wetsuit. The burkini, he says, is part of a “political project”, and complacency plays into the hands of Islamists. The difficulty is that, after a series of deadly terrorist attacks over the past 18 months, France is in a state of heightened tension. Perceived provocations on both sides are amplified. It is not just civil-liberty activists who consider the mayors’ ban excessive, or stigmatising. Some French scholars of Islam, such as Olivier Roy, consider it “absurd” to conflate the burkini with hard-line Islamism, not least because the latter would not permit women to bathe publicly in the first place. Politicians, though, are unlikely to cede ground. The nature of French identity is likely to feature prominently in next year’s presidential election. Some contenders, such as Nicolas Sarkozy, a centre-right former president, argue that the Muslim veil should be banned on the campuses of state universities. France looks set to defend, if not tighten, its strict approach to head-covering.

The burkini, a full-body Islamic swimsuit designed to cover the entire body except for the face, hands, and feet, has experienced a significant surge in sales following controversial bans implemented in France. Aheda Zanetti, the Australian designer credited with inventing the garment and holding the trademark for the name "burkini" and "burqini," reported a remarkable 200% increase in online sales. Zanetti, a 48-year-old Sydney resident, asserts that her creation embodies freedom and a healthy lifestyle, rather than oppression. She emphasizes her profound understanding of Islamic values, stating, "I'm an Aussie chick, I've been here all my life. I know what hijab means. I know what veil means. I know what Islam means. And I know who I am."

The controversy in France originated from decrees issued by several mayors, including the mayor of Cannes. His ruling declared, "Access to beaches and for swimming is banned to any person wearing improper clothes that are not respectful of good morals and secularism." The decree further elaborated that "Beachwear which ostentatiously displays religious affiliation, when France and places of worship are currently the target of terrorist attacks, is liable to create risks of disrupting public order." Individuals violating this ban faced a financial penalty of €38 (£33). The ban was slated to remain in effect until August 31, 2016.

Zanetti explained that her initial motivation was to empower Muslim women to fully participate in the Australian beach lifestyle. She aspired for her daughters to possess the autonomy to select their swimwear, stating, "I wanted my girls to grow up to have that freedom of choice. I don't care if they want to have a bikini. It's their choice." She firmly believes that no individual should dictate women's attire, asserting, "No man in this entire world can tell us what to wear or what not to wear."

The development of the burkini was partly inspired by reports of France's prohibition on the hijab in educational institutions, which Zanetti perceived as an effort to curtail the expansion of Islam. Authorities in various French municipalities have enacted bans on the burkini, citing its perceived contravention of secularism principles. This debate has become particularly contentious in France, amplified by a series of devastating attacks perpetrated by Islamic extremists. The burkini, a portmanteau of "burqa" and "bikini," has consequently become a central element in discussions surrounding religious expression, secularism, and public order within France.

query: What unique features distinguish Ohio's marijuana legalization initiative from previous campaigns in other states?

documents.text: Meet Buddie, one of many controversial figures in Ohio's marijuana legalization battle. (John Minchillo/AP) On Tuesday, Ohio could become the fifth state to legalize recreational marijuana. But the story line there is less about how a culturally conservative state feels about pot and more about how a group of savvy political and business operatives are forging a new path forward for the growing movement that could fundamentally change who gets in the game -- and why. Depending on what happens in Ohio, the next iteration of pro-legalization activists could be motivated by an entirely different kind of green: Cash. Here's what you need to know about Ohio's unusual ballot initiative to legalize pot and how it could change the marijuana legalization movement. It's not your average legalization campaign There are a lot of firsts in Ohio, actually. If it legalizes recreation marijuana, Ohio would be the first Midwestern state and the first privately organized legalization campaign, notes Denver-based journalist Josiah M. Hesse in a Politico magazine piece. It's also the first state to try to legalize recreational and medical marijuana at once. (States like Colorado and Washington already had a medical marijuana infrastructure in place when they expanded to recreational pot.) But it's what happens after legalization that really sets Ohio's initiative apart. Cultivating and selling pot would be limited to 10 pre-determined farms. Any pot distributor in Ohio would have no choice but to buy marijuana grown from one of these farms. In essence, it's a marijuana monopoly. Also unusual is the legalization campaign's blunt end game (see what we did there?). It's a constitutional amendment backed and financed almost exclusively by those who stand to benefit from its passage. [6 questions about the future of pot legalization] It's backed by Ohio pop culture stars Like this guy. (Ethan Miller/Getty Images) The owners of the 10 farms include a grab-bag group of 24 investors that include former 98 Degrees boy-band crooner Nick Lachey, as well as descendants of former president William Howard Taft and NBA star Oscar Robertson. If the amendment is approved, they'll be ground-floor investors in a business that advocates estimate will bring in $1 billion a year. Each ownership group was asked to invest roughly $2 to $4 million in the ResponsibleOhio campaign advocating for legalization. And that's precisely the problem, say a diverse group of opponents that includes marijuana legalization advocates, who are joining forces with the usual cast of opponents like law enforcement officers. Ohio's constitution could soon basically enshrine these people's rights to make money, explains The Washington Post's Jessica Contrera. The idea is so controversial that, in July, state lawmakers threw their own ballot initiative out there to counter this one. Known as the "anti-monopoly amendment," it would ban anyone from creating a constitutional amendment exclusively for financial gain. If both pass -- a possibility -- this whole drama could go to the courts. It's splitting the marijuana industry 1 of 14 Full Screen Autoplay Close Skip Ad × An insider’s look at pot farming around the world View Photos A few places where cannabis is farmed for medical or recreational use. Caption A few places where cannabis is farmed for medical or recreational use. July 1, 2014 Bob Leeds, a recreational pot grower in Seattle and owner of Sea of Green Farms, inspects “clone” plants growing under lights. The clones will develop into plants that produce the "flower" required to make potent recreational marijuana. Ted S. Warren/AP Buy Photo Wait 1 second to continue. Supporters say the private-sector-driven initiative is the only way they could get a conservative-leaning state to even consider legalizing pot. The brainchild of the initiative is veteran Ohio political operative Ian James, who has 30 years of experience in Ohio politics but little track record in the way of drug legalization. James has zeroed in on the traditional arguments for legalizing pot -- civil liberties, social justice, tax benefits -- and stayed away from the controversial layout of Ohio's potential pot industry. "Let's stop pretending either that Ohioans don't consume marijuana or that only bad people do," he wrote in one of several op-eds during the spring. [Marijuana could improve my teen son's life. But we won't know until it's legal] Opponents, unsurprisingly, are calling to attention to the unique set-up. "They are creating a constitutionally mandated oligopoly,” argues Ethan Nadelmann, executive director of the Drug Policy Alliance. Despite Nadelmann's statement, the New York Times reports that the Marijuana Policy Project and Drug Policy Alliance are officially neutral on the initiative, while the National Organization for the Reform of Marijuana Laws "gave it an uneasy endorsement." The public is also split on what to do These guys are fans. (AP Photo/John Minchillo) Ohioans appear as undecided as the pros. A recent Bowling Green University poll shows 44 percent were for it and 43 percent were against it -- well within the three-and-a-half-point margin of error -- and a relatively high 13 percent undecided. Other polls also show just about anything could happen on Tuesday. [Ohio voters support and oppose legalizing marijuana. Wait, what?] Ohio has arguably already changed the legalization game Ohioans aren't just voting on a new model for financing and regulating legal pot. They're potentially reforming marijuana from a libertarian/hippie activity relegated to states out West into a socially acceptable investment that can flourish in unlikely places. That's a big change for a country where marijuana is still classified as one of the most dangerous of drugs and where banks are technically prohibited from financing the industry. (The Justice Department has agreed to look the other way in states that legalize it.) As Denver-based journalist Hesse notes, the game is already shifting just by Ohioans considering this measure. No matter what happens Tuesday, the state has begun to reframe the legalization debate from marijuana's social impact to its financial one. "[W]ith recreational marijuana having already proved itself as an endeavor worthy of Big Business attention," he wrote, "it’s certain that we’re going to see large influxes of cash into future campaigns by investors looking for a stake in the coming green rush."

Houston's mayoral election, initially anticipated to be dominated by a seven-candidate race, has been overshadowed by the contentious City of Houston Proposition 1, commonly known as the Houston Equal Rights Ordinance (HERO). This ordinance, championed by Mayor Annise Parker in 2014, aims to shield individuals from discrimination across various aspects of life, including employment, housing, and public accommodations. It prohibits discrimination based on a wide array of characteristics, such as race, age, sexual orientation, and ethnicity. Beyond its protective measures, HERO was designed to provide a swift legal recourse for those who experience discrimination.

Despite its broad scope in combating various forms of prejudice, opposition to HERO quickly coalesced around a single, highly publicized concern: the perceived threat of transgender women using women's restrooms. Opponents, including former Astros star Lance Berkman, voiced fears that the ordinance would enable "troubled men" to disguise themselves as women to commit assaults in public restrooms. Berkman's public stance against HERO, particularly his anti-discrimination ad, prompted a sharp response from Mayor Parker on Twitter. She questioned Berkman's concerns, highlighting that St. Louis, where he played baseball, had a non-discrimination ordinance without apparent issue for his family. Parker also pointed out Berkman's subsequent move to Dallas, which had also amended its charter to include gender identity protections, suggesting hypocrisy in his opposition to Houston's ordinance.

Following HERO's passage by the city council, anti-HERO groups initiated a petition drive to repeal the ordinance or force it onto the ballot for public vote. After considerable legal challenges, the Texas Supreme Court mandated that the ordinance be put to a vote as City of Houston Proposition 1. This decision transformed HERO into the central issue of the election. It's crucial for voters to distinguish this proposition from others on the ballot; Texas Constitutional Amendment Proposition 1 pertains to homestead exemptions for public school taxes, while Harris County's Proposition 1 concerns road improvement bonds. Those voting on HERO should select City of Houston Proposition 1.

The outcome of the vote on HERO appears uncertain. While pre-election polls indicated a leaning towards approval, a significant portion of early and mail-in ballots have already been cast. According to the Houston Chronicle, approximately 133,000 residents voted early or by mail. While this high turnout is generally positive, especially in a non-presidential election year, it may signal a disadvantage for HERO. Texas Monthly observed that HERO's success hinges on the participation of younger, socially progressive voters. However, early voting trends suggest an increased turnout from more conservative areas like Kingwood, Memorial, and Clear Lake, with a demographic skewing older. Conversely, younger voters, who are more likely to support HERO, have shown lower participation rates at the polls thus far.

Consequently, Election Day carries significant weight. The mayoral race, featuring candidates Sylvester Turner, Adrian Garcia, Ben Hall, Bill King, Steve Costello, Chris Bell, and Marty McVey, is expected to be competitive and likely head to a runoff. Nevertheless, the most compelling narrative of the election will be the public's decision on HERO. A rejection of HERO would mark a stark reversal for Houston, transforming it from a city that elected its first openly gay mayor to the only major city in Texas without an equal rights ordinance. Regardless of the vote's outcome, the results are poised to garner national attention.

The fate of Proposition 1 has been a prominent political topic for months. Democratic presidential candidate Hillary Clinton publicly endorsed the ordinance, tweeting her support for equality efforts in Houston and beyond. Conversely, the day before the election, Governor Greg Abbott urged opposition to HERO, stating his belief that "no men in women's bathrooms." This stark contrast in endorsements from national figures underscores the ordinance's significance.

As Houston goes to the polls, the city stands at a crossroads. The election will determine whether Houston is recognized as a city that embraces equal rights protections or one that rejects them. The decision on Proposition 1 will ultimately define the city's stance on inclusivity and non-discrimination for years to come.

The Kentucky Republican Party faces internal strife over its gubernatorial nominee, Matt Bevin. Bevin, known for his often unpredictable political stances, is shown here with his opponent, Jack Conway, during a recent debate. His candidacy poses a significant challenge for the GOP in the state. | AP Photo

**Key Races and Ballot Measures Shaping the 2016 Political Scene**

As voters prepare to cast their ballots, several critical contests and ballot initiatives are set to significantly shape the 2016 political landscape. While this year lacks a presidential election, these races offer valuable insights into shifting voter sentiments and party strategies. Democrats are working to counter the prevailing Republican dominance at the state level, aiming to win a competitive governorship and regain control of a key legislative chamber. Conversely, Republicans aim to reinforce their electoral strength in off-year elections by weakening the Democratic bench, a move seen as vital for future success. Beyond these partisan battles, activists are using the ballot box to advance substantial policy changes, including the potential legalization of recreational marijuana in a key Midwestern swing state.

Here are 10 significant contests to watch closely:

1.  **Kentucky Governor:** Despite Kentucky's general Republican leanings in presidential elections, the GOP has historically struggled to win the governorship, holding the office for only one term since the early 1970s. The departure of term-limited Democratic Governor Steve Beshear seemed to present a clear opportunity for Republicans. However, the party is currently grappling with its chosen nominee, Matt Bevin. Bevin, who secured a remarkably narrow victory in a highly contentious primary by just 83 votes, is perceived by some within the Republican Party as mercurial. Nevertheless, Republican strategists believe Bevin can unseat the incumbent Democratic Attorney General, Jack Conway, who currently holds a slight polling advantage. The Republican Governors Association has shown its commitment by investing over $3 million to support Bevin's campaign since Labor Day, even after a three-week pause in their campaign activities last month.

2.  **Kentucky Auditor:** Republicans are strategically targeting races that could shape future Democratic contenders. State Auditor Adam Edelen is widely seen as the leading Democratic candidate likely to challenge Senator Rand Paul in the upcoming senatorial election. The **Governor of Kentucky** is the state's **chief executive**, holding **significant power** and authority. This office commands **Kentucky's military forces** and bears the vital responsibility of **ensuring state laws are faithfully executed**. The governor possesses the power to **approve or reject legislation** passed by the Kentucky General Assembly and can also **convene special legislative sessions**. Additionally, the governor can **issue pardons** (excluding cases of treason and impeachment) and has the authority to **reorganize or reduce the size of state government agencies**. Historically, the Kentucky governorship is regarded as one of the **most influential executive positions** in the United States.

To date, **fifty-eight individuals** have held the position of Kentucky's governor. A **1992 constitutional amendment** removed the restriction preventing governors from serving consecutive terms. Prior to this change, four governors—**Isaac Shelby, John L. Helm, James B. McCreary, and Happy Chandler**—served multiple non-consecutive terms. Following the 1992 amendment, **Paul E. Patton** became the first governor eligible for a second consecutive term, securing reelection in 1999. **James Garrard**, who succeeded himself in 1800, served before this term limit was in effect. **William Goebel**, elected in the contested 1899 election, remains the **only U.S. governor assassinated** while in office. **Martha Layne Collins**, governor from 1983 to 1987, was the **first woman** to serve as Kentucky's governor and the third woman nationally to hold such a position without being the spouse or widow of a former governor. The 62nd and current governor is Republican **Matt Bevin**, who was inaugurated on December 8, 2015.

Kentucky achieved statehood on June 1, 1792, having previously been part of Virginia as Kentucky County. The **original 1792 Kentucky Constitution** established the governor's election via an electoral college for a four-year term. The **second constitution in 1799** transitioned to a popular vote and imposed a seven-year prohibition on governors succeeding themselves. The **third constitution, adopted in 1850**, shortened this ban to four years. A **significant 1992 amendment** permitted a second consecutive term, followed by a four-year succession restriction.

During the Civil War, a secessionist faction established a **Confederate government for Kentucky** in Russellville. Although this entity did not entirely displace the Union government in Frankfort, **two governors served under the Confederate authority**. George W. Johnson served from November 20, 1861, until his death on April 8, 1862, at the Battle of Shiloh. Richard Hawes succeeded Johnson and held the governorship until the Confederate surrender on April 9, 1865. The Confederate government formally disbanded shortly after the war concluded.

These individuals require comprehensive support, encompassing both physical and psychological services, alongside effective societal reintegration.

This is precisely why she champions HERO.
