March 8 marks International Women’s Day, a day typically observed in the US with feel-good campaigns highlighting women’s rights. However, this year, **grassroots activists globally are orchestrating a more radical action: a women’s general strike.** This strike aims to both **protest President Donald Trump and significantly advance women’s equality.**

Feminist activists and scholars argued in a February Guardian op-ed that the "age of Trump" is the **ideal moment for a women's strike.** They pointed to the **immense success of the Women's March on Washington** as proof that "millions of women in the United States are finally fed up not only with the blatant misogyny of the Trump’s administration, but also with decades of continuous attacks on women’s lives and bodies."

In conjunction with the International Women’s Strike planned in over 30 countries, Women’s March organizers have designated March 8 as **“A Day Without a Woman.”** They are encouraging women in the US to **take the day off work if possible**, or to show support in alternative ways if they cannot.

The potential success of this women's strike remains uncertain, particularly in the US, where the labor movement is relatively weak and "general strikes" are a challenging tactic. Nevertheless, even if this specific event doesn't achieve widespread impact, it signals a **growing and persistent opposition from women against Trump.** The Women's March organizers have already demonstrated their ability to transform a single event into a sustained movement, and "A Day Without a Woman" is just one of many planned actions.

**Key Guidelines for Participating in "A Day Without a Woman":**

*   **Women should take the day off from both paid and unpaid labor.**
*   **Avoid shopping for the day**, with exceptions for small, women- and minority-owned businesses.
*   **Wear RED in solidarity** with "A Day Without A Woman."

Male allies are encouraged to support by taking on childcare and household duties, or by initiating workplace conversations about promoting family-friendly policies like paid leave or flexible scheduling. While these are individual actions, **collective participation could yield a significant impact.** Some businesses and organizations, such as NARAL Pro-Choice America, are closing on March 8 in solidarity. Even some cities and municipal organizations are shutting down, with at least three school districts canceling classes due to anticipated teacher absences.

The overall impact of the strike is difficult to quantify. Questions remain about the visibility of public events, the number of participants, and how success will be measured. Bob Bland, a Women's March organizer, explained to Vox that "A Day Without a Woman is a very different type of action than the march. The march was all about people getting out, coming together, and showing themselves very explicitly. A strike can be very different from that." She further elaborated that strikes are typically more **distributed and local** than large marches, with individuals taking action wherever they are, rather than necessarily gathering publicly.

However, outlets for public protest exist. The US branch of the International Women’s Strike offers a website for finding and organizing local meetings. The Nation has also published detailed suggestions for involvement. Bland anticipates that the protests will be visible through **social media and the sheer number of people wearing red.** US organizers chose red to symbolize "revolutionary love and sacrifice" and its historical connection to the labor movement. Organizers in other countries are wearing black, referencing Poland's "Black Monday" protest against abortion bans.

Bland emphasizes that the strike is as much about **women's absence as their presence**, aiming to illustrate society's functioning without their active participation. The choice of the term "strike" carries both practical and symbolic weight, even with the inherent difficulties. Strikes are designed to **force workplace change**, and in every sphere, from the home to the boardroom, women's labor is often undervalued. Women disproportionately handle chores and childcare, and often take on undesirable tasks at work, frequently for less pay or no pay at all. The core idea of a women's general strike is that by **refusing to perform their usual labor for a day, women will highlight the importance and underappreciation of their work.**

Elisabeth Clemens, a sociology professor at the University of Chicago, suggested to Vox that while the "International Women’s Strike" might function more as a protest or boycott than a true general strike, it can still be a **powerful tool for drawing attention to women's rights.** She noted that the strike's name projects a "sense of global solidarity, and that’s a really powerful move." When a **large number of people share grievances**, it can create a positive feedback loop, attracting more activists.

**The Goal: A "Feminism of the 99 Percent"**

Organizers of the International Women’s Strike advocate for a **"feminism of the 99 percent,"** arguing that true inclusivity requires fighting for both economic and social equality. They believe it's crucial to resist not only Trump but also the underlying social issues that contributed to his rise. As organizers and activists stated in their Guardian op-ed, "While Trump’s blatant misogyny was the immediate trigger for the huge response on 21 January, the attack on women (and all working people) long predates his administration." They contend that the "women’s conditions of life, especially those of women of color and of working, unemployed and migrant women, have steadily deteriorated over the last 30 years, thanks to financialization and corporate globalization."

They argue that many women find little benefit in a "lean-in" feminism focused on corporate success. Instead, **strong labor organizing** is seen as a vital strategy for improving the lives of women in poverty, those in low-paying jobs, and those struggling to balance paid work with family responsibilities. Organizers stated, "Women play an indispensable role in the daily functions of life in all of society, through paid and unpaid, seen and unseen labor." The strike aims to **"highlight the economic power and significance that women have in the US and global economies, while calling attention to the economic injustices women and gender nonconforming people continue to face."**

**The Challenge of Executing a Successful General Strike**

"A Day Without a Woman" is being labeled a "general strike," a tactic also employed by other recent protests, such as the "Day Without Immigrants" strike. However, a previous general strike on February 17 saw limited participation. Experts explain that the disruptive nature of a general strike is intended to halt daily life and force attention to grievances. While effective in smaller, more homogenous countries, **general strikes are significantly more difficult to execute in the vast and diverse United States**, especially with declining union membership.

Bryce Covert at ThinkProgress highlights the **lack of legal protections for striking in the US** unless it's related to specific workplace grievances. Participating in a "general" strike for political solidarity carries personal risk, potentially jeopardizing employment. This has raised concerns about **class and privilege** within the women's strike, as those with secure jobs and benefits may be more able to participate.

Writer Sady Doyle questioned in Elle what a strike truly means in 2017, given the advancements in women's access to education and high-paying jobs, while acknowledging that **inequalities persist**, making it harder for women to empathize with each other's struggles. She noted that in the past, a women's strike had a clear, tangible effect by making invisible work visible. Today, with women in diverse roles, defining a specific problem for female workers to address through striking is more complex.

Conversely, Magally A. Miranda Alcazar and Kate D. Griffiths argued at The Nation that it's **misguided to label a strike as "privileged,"** as strikes are historically a tool for the least privileged. They draw parallels to the dire conditions of 1908, when International Women's Day originated from a garment workers' strike. They argue that the current situation, with historically low union membership, is not dissimilar. They also suggest that while a strike might reflect elite concerns, it can also foster **connection and solidarity between elite and marginalized women.**

Bland offers another perspective: **"Those of us who are able to strike on March 8 are striking on behalf of those who can't. We have to be there to represent each other."**

**March 8 Strikes Continue an International Trend**

The concept of **global solidarity** is crucial, as women in other countries and vulnerable populations in the US have been staging strikes and walk-outs for months. Strikes like the Black Friday protest for abortion rights in Poland have shown surprising success, potentially influencing conservative lawmakers. In Ireland, women are striking on March 8 to demand a referendum on the country's abortion ban. While some strikes have specific political aims, women have also protested issues like violence against women, reproductive rights restrictions, and gender-based inequality in countries from Argentina to Iceland.

Clemens emphasizes the **effectiveness of organizing as "women,"** a broad category that encompasses diverse struggles. Organizers argue that combating "violence" against women should extend beyond domestic or sexual violence to include "the violence of the market, of debt, of capitalist property relations, and of the state; the violence of discriminatory policies against lesbian, trans and queer women; the violence of state criminalization of migratory movements; the violence of mass incarceration; and the institutional violence against women’s bodies through abortion bans and lack of access to free healthcare and free abortion."

This **intersectional approach**, which recognizes how different problems intersect and disproportionately harm various groups, proved successful at the Women's March. Sociologist Dana Fisher surveyed attendees and found a high number of first-time protesters motivated by a range of intersectional reasons, including the environment, racial justice, LGBTQ

Participating in "A Day Without a Woman" is a powerful statement. Understanding its purpose and significance is key to broader engagement. This guide offers talking points to address common questions and reactions you might encounter when discussing the women's strike.

### Understanding and Responding to Initial Reactions

When you mention participating in "A Day Without a Woman," you might encounter various responses.

*   **Enthusiastic Support:** Some individuals will express full support and offer to help spread the message. This is a clear sign of an ally.
*   **Uninformed Curiosity:** Others may ask, "A Day Without a Woman? What's that?" For these individuals, directing them to an FAQ page is efficient. If further clarification is needed, provide concise answers, but remember it's not solely your responsibility to educate everyone on readily available information.
*   **Skeptical Questioning:** You might hear, "I'm not against feminism, but I don't see how taking a day off work will fix anything." Similar to the uninformed, pointing to resources is helpful. Focus the conversation on the strike's specific goals and the issues it addresses, rather than getting drawn into a broad debate about feminism. Educating those genuinely curious is valuable, but staying focused on the strike's objectives is more effective.
*   **Hostile or Sexist Responses:** This category includes offensive language, dismissive attitudes, or attempts to provoke. In such cases, it's often best to disengage. Your time is better spent on constructive actions than debating someone unwilling to listen.

### Addressing Deeper Questions and Criticisms

If someone grasps the basic concept but wishes to challenge the approach, consider these points:

*   **The Privilege Argument:** A common critique is, "This initiative seems privileged. Many working women can't afford to take the day off. There are better ways to fight for equality that include economic justice." Acknowledge that the strike may be more accessible to some than others. Emphasize that participation can take many forms beyond not working, such as conscious consumerism (not buying, buying less, or supporting women-owned businesses). The media attention generated can highlight systemic discrimination affecting all women. While inclusivity is a goal, dismissing the strike entirely isn't productive. Use the event as a learning opportunity to make future actions more inclusive. Remember, this strike doesn't preclude other forms of activism. If someone is passionate about economic justice for disadvantaged women, they are encouraged to organize their own initiatives.
*   **The Demanding Inquirer:** Some may state, "I have many questions because I want to learn. However, if you can't provide perfect, smart, and acceptable answers to every single one, or if you show any impatience, I'll dismiss you as ignorant." Answering endless questions and enduring commentary is emotional labor – unpaid and often expected of women. You are not obligated to provide free education. Suggest they seek information through formal channels like community college courses or libraries. You have the right to end the conversation at any time.
*   **The "Ineffective Protest" Argument:** You might hear, "This won't significantly impact the economy, change laws, or remove sexist politicians. You're wasting your time." This perspective overlooks the power of collective action. Strikes, marches, and social media campaigns amplify voices that are often ignored in mainstream discourse. Engaging people in issues that affect them directly is crucial, especially between elections. Sustained civic engagement beyond voting is vital, and attention-grabbing actions like strikes draw more people into the movement.
*   **The "Not Feminist Enough" Question:** Some ask, "If feminism is about equality between men and women, why is a strike just for women considered feminist? Shouldn't we treat everyone the same?" This argument often misses the point. Women's specific struggles and systemic disadvantages necessitate targeted action. Addressing these unique challenges is a necessary step towards achieving broader equality.

### Answering Specific Queries

Sometimes, questions are more straightforward:

*   **The Color Question:** Regarding specific colors, the color for this strike is red.
*   **The Donation Alternative:** While donating to organizations like Planned Parenthood is valuable, it's distinct from direct action. Civic engagement involves more than financial contributions; it requires active participation. Supporting women's causes financially is commendable, but it's not the same as actively participating in the strike.
*   **The Unavoidable Conflict:** If you genuinely cannot participate on the day itself due to unavoidable plans, avoid making a spectacle of it. Refrain from publicizing your inability to join or seeking validation. Participate in future actions. Consider your priorities if such conflicts arise repeatedly.
*   **The Search for Specific Narratives:** For inspiration, you might find narratives in historical accounts like "Newsies."

**Migrant Activist Hugo Castro Found Safe After Disappearance in Mexico**

San Diego-area migrant activist Hugo Castro has been found alive after he went missing in Mexico last Thursday. His partner, Gaba Cortes, announced his safe return on Facebook Tuesday afternoon. "Communicating to our friends, family, that Hugo has been found alive. That’s what we can share. We thank those who intervened," Cortes stated.

**The Disappearance and Plea for Help**

Castro's disappearance on Thursday marked four days since he was last heard from. He had made a public plea for help and provided clues to his location via a Facebook Live broadcast. Mexican and U.S. authorities, along with Castro's friends and family, had joined the search. Concerns for the Border Angels volunteer's life grew after he stated on Facebook Live that a "group of criminals" intended to harm him and that he did not trust local law enforcement.

In a 20-minute recording of his Facebook Live broadcast on Thursday afternoon, Castro expressed his distress: "I was threatened. They want to kill me. I’ve been walking for a long time, they don’t want to sell me anything, and they’re looking at me as if I were a dog. I need help."

**Widespread Concern and Social Media Speculation**

Castro's mysterious disappearance generated significant concern, with a lack of official details fueling speculation and rumors on social media. Many turned to his Facebook Live broadcast for any potential clues. The situation began when Castro left his home in Tijuana on April 9, en route to the Mexican state of Chiapas. He was scheduled to meet a caravan of individuals assisting migrants traveling from Central America to seek asylum in the U.S., as reported by The San Diego Union-Tribune.

**The Facebook Live Broadcast**

During his journey, Castro reported being followed and cornered by men in trucks near Puebla. It was at this point that he initiated his Facebook Live broadcast to request assistance. The video begins with Castro standing on the shoulder of a busy highway between Puebla and Mexico City. He described visible landmarks, including a water storage tank and the highway marker Kilometer 37, where he pleaded for someone to pick him up. He mentioned that locals were refusing to accept his U.S. dollars and that he had encountered a "group of criminals" who had been pursuing him. "I really need help. I can’t move from here. My battery is going to die. I’m not going to move from here," he stated in the video, which was his last known contact before his disappearance on Thursday.

**Search Efforts and Community Support**

San Diego-based Border Angels activist Hugo Castro was officially declared missing on Thursday, April 13, 2017, following his desperate Facebook Live plea. An image distributed in a bulletin shared on social media depicted him. As of Monday morning, Castro remained unfound, but his friends and relatives continued to post appeals for his safe return on his Facebook profile. "Hugo Castro, we ask God that you soon return home," read one such post. His video and a missing person bulletin were widely shared across social media platforms. Over the weekend, supporters organized prayer vigils at Chicano Park in San Diego. Messages of support and pleas for help in both Spanish and English circulated extensively on Facebook.

**Partner's Efforts and Past Threats**

Following Castro's disappearance, his partner and fellow Border Angels activist, Gaba Cortes, used Facebook Live to solicit help and share details about his journey. She indicated that he was on a bus to Chiapas and appeared to be in good spirits the night before he went missing. "It’s very hard to talk about this," she expressed. In a separate video recorded on Friday, Cortes urged the public to avoid spreading rumors and speculation regarding Castro's fate. On Sunday, a woman identifying herself as Cortes' sister established a PayPal account and requested donations via Facebook to fund her trip to Mexico City to search for Castro. Enrique Morones, founder of the immigrant rights group Border Angels, informed KPBS that Castro had received death threats in recent months while assisting deported Haitian migrants in Tijuana. "These people that threatened him were trying to take advantage of the Haitians and Hugo was defending them," Morones told KPBS. It remains unclear whether these prior threats are connected to his disappearance.

Media playback is unsupported on your device Media caption WATCH: 'Our hearts go out to the family' Facebook's chief has paid his respects to the family of a man whose killing was filmed and posted onto its site. "Our hearts go out to the family and friends of Robert Godwin Sr," said Mark Zuckerberg near the start of Facebook's annual F8 developers conference. His social network had been criticised over the amount of time it had taken to take the clip offline. About an hour before the event got underway, police had revealed that the murder suspect had killed himself. Steve Stephens had been the subject of a national manhunt. He was believed to have uploaded a video to Facebook showing his killing of 74-year-old Mr Godwin in Cleveland on Sunday and then boasting on subsequent Facebook Live streams that he had killed others. Facebook subsequently acknowledged it had taken it more than two hours to remove the clips after the first video was posted, despite it having received complaints in the interim. "We have a lot of work and we will keep doing all we can to prevent tragedies like this from happening," added Mr Zuckerberg. What was reported when 11:09AM PDT (19:09 GMT) - first video, of intent to murder, uploaded. Not reported to Facebook. 11:11AM PDT - second video, of shooting, uploaded. 11:22AM PDT - suspect confesses to murder while using Live, is live for 5 minutes. 11:27AM PDT - Live ends, and Live video is first reported shortly after. 12:59PM PDT - video of shooting is first reported. 1:22PM PDT - suspect's account disabled; all videos no longer visible to public. Cleveland's police chief had referred to Facebook's role in a separate press conference. "I think the people on social media kind of know the power and I think they know the harm it can do," said Calvin Williams. "We've talked before about people not living their lives on social media and being truthful on social media and not harming people via social media. "And this is a prime example, this is something that should not have been shared around the world. Period." Media playback is unsupported on your device Media caption The suspect said he was looking for victims in a video posted on Facebook One analyst attending F8 said it was no surprise Mr Zuckerberg had felt compelled to discuss the matter. "Obviously this is something they have to get on top of with some urgency, but it's an extraordinarily difficult problem," commented Geoff Blaber from the CCS Insight tech consultancy. "What Facebook has at its disposal is a enormous amount of talent and a very big emphasis on artificial intelligence, and I think that will be how it deals with this in the long-term. "AI holds the key to shortening the time required to flag and remove offensive and inappropriate material amidst the endless growth of user content." Augmented reality future Mr Zuckerberg went on to introduce new plans to include augmented reality experiences in Facebook apps, such as Messenger. He suggested that, in the near future, it would be far more common to place digital objects in video and live streams viewed on mobile phones. Media playback is unsupported on your device Media caption WATCH: Mark Zuckerberg shows how animated art can be superimposed over real-world views Animated artworks could be made visible at a particular physical location, for example, via the camera view of an app. "Augmented reality is going to help us mix the digital and physical in all new ways," he said. "That's going to help us make our physical reality better." 3D filters that can be placed into real-life scenes, not unlike those unveiled by rival Snapchat, were also demoed. Image copyright Facebook Image caption Users will be able to take part in virtual reality calls via their smartphones "Facebook will need to tread a careful path as it rolls out its new augmented reality capabilities," commented Mr Blaber. "When used responsibly they can deliver extremely compelling experiences but it only takes a few creepy use cases to emerge and it could derail the whole project." New virtual reality experiences making use of the Oculus Rift headset were also detailed, including: Facebook Spaces - where friends can hang out together in VR avatars that are intelligently customised to suit users, based on their Facebook profile pictures the ability to "call in" to Facebook Spaces with Messenger video calls if users do not have a VR headset Bot discovery Facebook also announced improvements to its Messenger platform to encourage the use of bots - a facility first unveiled last year. The software tools can be deployed by businesses to interact with customers in an automated manner, potentially allowing them to employ fewer call centre staff. The firm said 100,000 bots had been developed for Messenger to date. Image copyright Facebook Image caption Facebook has added a "discover" page to help users find bots for its Messenger program The changes include: the ability for users to add a business' bot to a group chat, so several people can interact with it at once support for QR codes, which can be displayed at events and be photographed by phones to activate associated bots that can answer questions about what is on show the introduction of a "discover" tab to Messenger's home screen, which shows recently used bots and ones that are currently popular with others Messenger has also added the ability to include Spotify extensions, allowing music to be shared and played within chats, and said Apple Music would also be supported shortly.

A gunman took to Facebook on Sunday to share a video of himself killing a man on the streets of Cleveland. The video was initially thought to have been filmed using Facebook Live, the social network’s video broadcasting feature, though a company spokesperson later confirmed that the killer recorded the video and uploaded it after the fact. He did use Facebook Live later in the day, though, to talk about the killing. Live or not, the video was a shocking and troubling reminder that Facebook does not yet have the kind of technology necessary to automatically detect this kind of violence, though it is working on it. Instead, the company relies on users to flag these posts before they’re reviewed, which takes time and subjects its community to disturbing and violent videos. That gap between posting and detection has, in a small number of cases, made Facebook Live a perverted tool for some to broadcast violence and abuse. In other instances, it has been a way for people to capture horrific scenes by accident, including multiple killings. Facebook condemned the video in a statement, calling the act a “horrific crime,” and added that it’s working with local law enforcement. But these horrific crimes get a lot of attention, and it’s time to ask if Facebook Live is worth the pain of exposing users to murder and torture. It’s tough to imagine how Facebook Live will make any money for the company outside of publisher and celebrity videos. Advertisers won’t be keen to put their video ads before or alongside live videos created by random Facebook users, and most live video content isn’t great, even the stuff that Facebook pays its partners to produce.* Facebook’s most-watched live video, Chewbacca Mom, was great, but not necessarily because it was live. The majority of the video’s 166 million views came after the live broadcast was over. If Facebook limited the live broadcasting feature to selected partners, it wouldn’t need to worry about incidents where people accidentally broadcast a live murder, or talk about a murder they just committed. To be sure, in the Cleveland incident, limiting Live wouldn’t have prevented the video of the killing, but emphasizing vetted partners would set a more professional tone for Facebook video altogether. Is this a drastic proposal? Yes. The vast majority of Facebook livestreams may be dull, but they are free of hatred and violence. No one thinks we should shut down the internet because it has dark corners, and no one is clamoring for Twitter to shut down because people use it to bully each other online. There are times when Facebook Live and other streaming services offer an incredible glimpse into otherwise closed-off environments, like when Senator Elizabeth Warren was cut off while trying to criticize now Attorney General Jeff Sessions during debate before his confirmation. Warren took her message to Facebook instead. Facebook is building artificial intelligence to detect the content of user videos, but CEO Mark Zuckerberg said in February it was still “very early in development.” Detecting the content of live videos is likely even further in the future. Postponing Facebook Live until the technology is ready to keep up should be an option. * Vox Media, which owns this site, is a Facebook publishing partner.

Photo illustration by Slate. Photo by Cleveland Police/Handout via Reuters. On Sunday in Cleveland, a man appears to have shot and killed a 74-year-old stranger point-blank, recorded it on his phone, and uploaded the video to Facebook. He then broadcast himself on Facebook Live as he drove around the city telling viewers he was searching for his next victims in what he said would be an “Easter Day slaughter.” While there is no evidence the perpetrator has killed anyone else, he remained at large ‪Monday afternoon. Will Oremus Will Oremus is Slate’s senior technology writer. Email him at will.oremus@slate.com or follow him on Twitter. The brutal slaying was the latest in a string of violent acts that have found their way onto Facebook since the company launched its live video feature a year ago. But you can’t really blame a social media platform for the appalling uses to which disturbed people apply its tools—or can you? Advertisement In this case, Facebook has been criticized for failing to promptly take down the video, which remained on suspected shooter Steve Stephens’ page for several hours, according to the Verge. It has since removed both the video and Stephens’ account, offering the following statement: This is a horrific crime and we do not allow this kind of content on Facebook. We work hard to keep a safe environment on Facebook, and are in touch with law enforcement in emergencies when there are direct threats to physical safety. It’s well and good for Facebook to say it doesn’t “allow” people to broadcast cold-blooded killings on the site, in the sense that it’s against the rules. The fact is, its service allowed just that, at least for long enough for large numbers of people to watch the video and even interact with the suspect before the company took it down. Now that shooting people live on Facebook (or in this case, on a slight tape delay) is apparently a thing—along with assaulting people live on Facebook, getting shot by police live on Facebook, and committing suicide live on Facebook—it stands to reason that the company bears some responsibility. But it’s worth taking a closer look at exactly what that responsibility is and what actions Facebook could take to fulfill it. Advertisement One extreme view would be that Facebook bears full responsibility for what its users publish, and deserves to be criticized or potentially even punished for whatever role its product plays in incentivizing or amplifying heinous behavior. In this view, Facebook Live would be essentially untenable as a product, because there’s no way Facebook could possibly police every video around the world in real time with perfect accuracy. It’s hard to imagine a platform more ripe for this sort of abuse. At the other extreme would be the claim that Facebook Live and Facebook video are merely tools, and it’s not up to the company to control how people use them. Internet startups have a rich history of pleading ignorance, helplessness, or neutrality with respect to their users’ behavior. Stephens, in this view, was going to kill one way or another and find a way to publicize it. That he opted to post his misdeeds to Facebook instead of YouTube or Snapchat is not Facebook’s fault. That’s a little too convenient, however. Any number of different broadcast platforms can be used to amplify objectionable messages. But they lend themselves to different balances between editorial oversight and publisher autonomy. A TV station can exert relatively tight control of what it broadcasts, albeit not full control, as we saw when a pair of reporters were shot to death live on a local newscast in Virginia in 2015. (The shooter posted his own graphic video to Twitter soon afterward.)* By contrast, content moderation is a much thornier problem if you run, say, a real-time, global, anonymous microblogging service. Twitter says it took down some 377,000 terror-related accounts in just the second half of 2016, yet it is still routinely accused of failing to adequately police its network. Facebook Live is attracting scrutiny because it lies near the extreme vanguard of this spectrum. It's real-time; anyone can use it at any moment; the medium of personal video is as intimate as it gets; and the potential audience for a popular stream is tremendous thanks to Facebook's algorithms. Its one advantage over Twitter, when it comes to moderation, is that accounts aren’t anonymous, which means that you can’t use it with impunity. By broadcasting on Facebook, Stephens made it highly likely that he’ll be caught, and in the meantime Facebook can deny him his page. Advertisement That aside, it’s hard to imagine a platform more ripe for user abuse or more difficult to effectively monitor. So while it may be wrong to blame Facebook for the shooting itself, it’s fair to expect that the company would have thought hard about the ways its live video features could be misused and devoted itself to finding ways to mitigate the damage before releasing them. It’s safe to say at this point that Facebook did not do that. If anything, the company seems to have welcomed the potential for drama as a way to stay relevant with young people in the face of challenges from Snapchat and other rivals. In an exclusive interview with BuzzFeed’s Mat Honan to announce Facebook Live in April 2016, CEO Mark Zuckerberg repeatedly and excitedly used the words “raw” and “visceral” to describe the product's potential. His quotes from that piece take on a rather grisly dimension in light of Sunday’s killing: We built this big technology platform so we can go and support whatever the most personal and emotional and raw and visceral ways people want to communicate are as time goes on. … Because it’s live, there is no way it can be curated. And because of that it frees people up to be themselves. It’s live; it can’t possibly be perfectly planned out ahead of time. Somewhat counterintuitively, it’s a great medium for sharing raw and visceral content. Advertisement Nowhere in the interview does he express serious concern about the possibility that someone might broadcast a murder, a suicide, a police shooting, a sexual assault, or an armed standoff. It’s no surprise, then, that as each of these scenarios has transpired in just the first year of the product’s existence, Facebook has appeared woefully unprepared to deal with them. It isn’t just a matter of being slow to take down the offending content. As this rundown by Fortune’s Mathew Ingram makes clear, the company has appeared to adjust its policies an ad hoc basis, stumbling over each episode in new and different ways. Zuckerberg said in a recent Fast Company interview, “I believe more strongly than ever that giving the most voice to the most people will be this positive force in society." He evinced no awareness that the bloody abuses of Facebook Live have challenged that notion repeatedly and, yes, viscerally. That it would be extremely hard for Facebook to prevent people from abusing its hot new product is not an excuse. It’s a feature of the system that Facebook built. And if the company’s reputation suffers because it can’t find better ways to handle it, that’s no ones fault but its own.

The recent, disturbing incident involving an alleged murder broadcast on Facebook has **heightened advertiser concerns** about livestreaming. "Most advertisers remain wary of Facebook Live, and this recent news will only amplify that," stated **Mark DiMassimo, CEO of branding agency DiMassimo Goldstein**. He explained, "Facebook's primary strength is its precise targeting, and brands trust the platform for sophisticated solutions. However, many are still assessing Facebook Live and **require more thorough checks and stronger guarantees of brand safety**."

Authorities in Ohio are seeking Steve Stephens, accused of recording and uploading a video of an alleged murder of an elderly man on Sunday, as reported by NBC News. While police initially stated the content was live on Facebook, they later clarified it was pre-recorded. Nevertheless, **Stephens did use Facebook Live on Sunday**, and police indicated he made other posts and at least one other video claiming to have killed others. DiMassimo confirmed that while his agency still advises clients to advertise on Facebook, they are **holding off on recommending Facebook Live** until it can be proven a "brand safe environment." He stressed, "**Advertisers need assurances that their ads will never appear next to violent or criminal live broadcasts**."

Similarly, marketing agency Attention will still consider using Facebook Live for its clients, but acknowledges the **risk of appearing alongside criminal content**. Tom Buontempo, President of Attention, commented, "Brands expect their content to be presented appropriately, **free from unsuitable and potentially harmful material**."

Facebook allows advertising on its videos, including **in-stream ads (mid-roll)** that play during Facebook Live streams. This feature is available to users with 2,000 or more followers or those who have recently attracted at least 300 viewers during a live video. The platform also offers video ads through its Audience Network, which places ads from Facebook partner companies on external websites and apps. However, advertisers and agencies have expressed concerns to CNBC about ad placement, noting that Facebook's **interest-based targeting, rather than category-based, makes controlling ad environments difficult**. They also highlighted the **inherent unpredictability of live streams as a significant risk** for advertising.

Chris Allieri, founder and principal at public relations and branding firm Mulbery & Astor, observed, "As Facebook Live is an evolving platform, its full development and broader societal role, along with how brands can participate, remain to be seen." He painted a vivid picture: "**You might find children's birthday parties, live-streamed tech conferences, or your aunt's Caribbean cruise appearing alongside potentially very disturbing content.**" Allieri suggested that Facebook should implement features to **block ads on videos reported as potentially criminal or violent**, and to **alert authorities in real-time** during unfolding incidents. A Facebook spokesperson confirmed that the company currently allows advertisers to **opt out of all video ad placements**. Reported content is reviewed by a global team, typically within hours. Videos violating community standards are removed, and law enforcement is notified if content poses a threat to safety.

By Justin Osofsky, VP, Global Operations On Sunday morning, a man in Cleveland posted a video of himself announcing his intent to commit murder, then two minutes later posted another video of himself shooting and killing an elderly man. A few minutes after that, he went live, confessing to the murder. It was a horrific crime — one that has no place on Facebook, and goes against our policies and everything we stand for. As a result of this terrible series of events, we are reviewing our reporting flows to be sure people can report videos and other material that violates our standards as easily and quickly as possible. In this case, we did not receive a report about the first video, and we only received a report about the second video — containing the shooting — more than an hour and 45 minutes after it was posted. We received reports about the third video, containing the man’s live confession, only after it had ended. We disabled the suspect’s account within 23 minutes of receiving the first report about the murder video, and two hours after receiving a report of any kind. But we know we need to do better. In addition to improving our reporting flows, we are constantly exploring ways that new technologies can help us make sure Facebook is a safe environment. Artificial intelligence, for example, plays an important part in this work, helping us prevent the videos from being reshared in their entirety. (People are still able to share portions of the videos in order to condemn them or for public awareness, as many news outlets are doing in reporting the story online and on television). We are also working on improving our review processes. Currently, thousands of people around the world review the millions of items that are reported to us every week in more than 40 languages. We prioritize reports with serious safety implications for our community, and are working on making that review process go even faster. Keeping our global community safe is an important part of our mission. We are grateful to everyone who reported these videos and other offensive content to us, and to those who are helping us keep Facebook safe every day. Timeline of Events 11:09AM PDT — First video, of intent to murder, uploaded. Not reported to Facebook. 11:11AM PDT — Second video, of shooting, uploaded. 11:22AM PDT — Suspect confesses to murder while using Live, is live for 5 minutes. 11:27AM PDT — Live ends, and Live video is first reported shortly after. 12:59PM PDT — Video of shooting is first reported. 1:22PM PDT — Suspect’s account disabled; all videos no longer visible to public. Update on April 18, 2017: Mark Zuckerberg, speaking at F8, Facebook’s developer conference said today, “We have a lot more to do here. We’re reminded of this this week by the tragedy in Cleveland. Our hearts go out to the family and friends of Robert Godwin Sr. We have a lot of work and we will keep doing all we can to prevent tragedies like this from happening.” Update on April 21, 2017: We take this issue very seriously so are conducting an extensive review of what happened. As part of the process, we have discovered additional reports from users, including reports on the first video. None of these reports, however, came in before this tragic event took place.

Facebook Live, initially envisioned for sharing celebratory milestones, has regrettably become a conduit for broadcasting disturbing and violent content. This concerning trend has been starkly illuminated by recent incidents, highlighting the platform's vulnerabilities.

On a recent Easter or Passover Sunday, amidst family observances, an individual in Cleveland uploaded a video depicting the murder of a 74-year-old man. Post-homicide, the perpetrator utilized Facebook to articulate his actions. This event exemplifies a disturbing pattern that has cast a shadow over Facebook's live video streaming capabilities.

While the Cleveland murder itself was not live-streamed, the video remained accessible on the platform for several hours post-incident. This raises critical questions regarding Facebook's content moderation responsibilities. The social network issued a statement to CNN on Sunday, condemning the crime as "horrific" and asserting that such content violates their policies. Nevertheless, the impact of its prolonged presence was significant. The subsequent live-streaming of the perpetrator discussing the incident amplified the distress.

Facebook subsequently released a statement on Monday, acknowledging the "terrible series of events" and reiterating its policy violations. The company attributed the video's extended online presence to a lack of user flagging. "We know we need to do better," the statement concluded.

Since Facebook Live's inception approximately one year prior, numerous instances of users broadcasting deaths and violent acts have been documented. While Facebook has sometimes implemented swift content removal, other times it has retained videos with cautionary labels.

Last year, Antonio Perkins of Chicago was fatally shot during a drive-by attack while live-streaming with friends. The video garnered hundreds of thousands of views within hours. Despite the graphic violence and Perkins' death, Facebook initially informed CNN that the clip did not contravene their community standards.

In July, Philando Castile of Minneapolis was killed by police during a traffic stop. His girlfriend, Lavish Reynolds, live-streamed the event, with her young daughter present. Facebook eventually removed the footage, but its dissemination contributed to galvanizing protests against police brutality.

Beyond homicides, other deeply disturbing incidents have surfaced. In January, a 12-year-old girl live-streamed her own suicide on Facebook. The clip, detailing her preparation and execution of the act, remained online for over two weeks, drawing widespread criticism. Reports also emerged of an individual in India live-streaming their suicide via the feature.

More recently, in January, a group in Chicago live-streamed a brutal assault on a developmentally disabled young man. The victim was bound, gagged, and subjected to knife wounds before escaping. The assailants were subsequently apprehended. At its peak, this stream had over 16,000 concurrent viewers, with some Facebook users engaging with the attackers during the abuse.

Just last month, several teenage males in Chicago live-streamed the sexual assault of a 15-year-old girl. Police reported that over 40 individuals were observing the attack, yet none contacted emergency services.

Facebook is not unique in confronting these challenges; similar violent content appears on platforms like YouTube. However, Facebook's extensive promotion of its live feature has positioned it as a focal point for criticism. The association with terms like "Facebook murder" or "Facebook killing" is undeniably detrimental to the company's brand equity.

Facebook possesses options to mitigate these risks, such as restricting live-streaming to verified users or even discontinuing the feature. However, given its strategic emphasis on video content, these measures are improbable. Consequently, we can anticipate a continued emergence of Facebook Live's darker applications with increasing frequency.

Facebook’s dilemma is part of a debate that has pulled in other technology giants, including Twitter, Amazon and Google. As these companies have rushed to provide tools for people to widely share their intimate moments more frequently, they are dealing with a rising tide of calls to more proactively filter the type of content that appears. In recent weeks, Google’s YouTube has been scrutinized for posting advertising next to racist video content, while Twitter contends with hate speech almost daily. But the attention is often focused on Facebook because of its nearly two billion users and global influence. It is an issue that is bedeviling Mark Zuckerberg, the company’s chief executive. Facebook has encouraged users to post more — it has spent the last two years emphasizing its push into photographs and video, underpinned by a thesis that cameras have become more important in how people share moments of their lives with their friends. The company was not prepared for the consequences of that push. Last summer, the death of Philando Castile, a Minnesota man shot by the police during a traffic stop, was broadcast by his girlfriend live across Facebook. In January, three men in Sweden were arrested on suspicion of raping a woman and streaming the assault live to a private Facebook group. In February, two radio journalists in the Dominican Republic were fatally shot during a Facebook Live broadcast. Some groups have pressured Facebook to take a stronger role in reviewing content posted on its platform. In a letter this year to Joel Kaplan, Facebook’s director of global policy, the American Civil Liberties Union called for the social network to be more transparent in its censorship process and to agree to an external audit of its practices. Image Frame from a video posted on Facebook shows Robert Godwin Sr. moments before being fatally shot. Credit Facebook, via Associated Press The problems have been compounded by the issue of fake news — false stories across the social network that some Facebook users have taken as true and that may have swayed thinking in events like elections. Mr. Zuckerberg has had to address the fake news problem several times in recent months, including working more closely with fact-checking organizations and beginning efforts to teach Facebook users how to discern what is or is not a real news story. For Mr. Zuckerberg, the timing of the shooting video could not be worse. He is scheduled to appear on Tuesday in San Jose, Calif., at Facebook’s annual developer conference, where the company typically focuses its energy on its technology.

query: What was the main argument presented in the Xinhua op-ed regarding Tsai Ing-wen's suitability as Taiwan's president?

documents.text:
An op-ed in an official Chinese state-published newspaper asserted that Taiwan's newly inaugurated president, Tsai Ing-wen, is unfit for her position due to her unmarried and childless status. The analysis, published by Xinhua, linked her personal life to her political approach, stating, "As a single female politician, she has no emotional encumbrances of love, no family restraint, no children to worry about. Her political style and tactics are often emotional, personalized, and extreme." The article further claimed this led to a short-term focus, with Tsai prioritizing details over strategic direction and setting extreme, immediate goals without considering long-term implications.

The original Xinhua piece was subsequently removed, as were copies on other Chinese news portals. However, it ignited widespread online criticism. Commenters highlighted successful single Asian female politicians like South Korean President Park Geun-hye and former Chinese Vice Premier Wu Yi. Li Yunlong, a professor at the Central Party School of the Communist Party of China, expressed his outrage on Weibo, questioning the relevance of marital status to political views and calling it "naked discrimination." He also pointed out that married former Taiwan President Chen Shui-bian was arguably more extreme. Others decried the state media's commentary as overtly sexist, attributing it to "Straight Man Cancer," a Chinese term for chauvinistic attitudes that demean women.

This incident raises concerns about increasing discrimination against women in China. While Chairman Mao Zedong famously declared women "held up half the sky," a growing sentiment suggests contemporary Chinese women face more, not less, prejudice. Reports indicate women constitute less than 10% of board members in top Chinese companies. State-run media frequently appears more preoccupied with women's appearances and marital status than with equal rights. For instance, the People's Daily published a gallery titled "Beauty with brains" featuring female journalists, and a 2015 Lunar New Year gala included skits mocking overweight and unmarried women. Feminists responded with an online petition demanding an end to the televised event. Earlier, influential writer Zhou Guoping sparked anger by suggesting a man's ambition is manifold, while a woman's is singular: to give birth.

Guo Weiqing, a professor at Sun Yat-sen University, suggests that societal expectations of traditional gender roles are being challenged by more women, and recent "sexist incidents" reflect some men's anxieties about women deviating from perceived femininity. He posits a desire for women to revert to traditional roles. However, many online comments indicate a strong disagreement with this view, with many seeing figures like Tsai as empowering role models for women. The Taiwanese government had not immediately responded to CNN's request for comment.

Image copyright EPA Image caption Taiwan's new leader has been outspoken in declaring that she has never been married An article on Chinese state media that said Taiwan's new leader Tsai Ing-wen has an "extremist style" because she is unmarried has prompted outrage online. The opinion piece, published by the Xinhua news agency, said Ms Tsai did not have the "emotional burden" of a family, leading to an "erratic style". Chinese social media users largely rallied in support of Ms Tsai, who has been outspoken about her single status. She was sworn in on Friday as Taiwan's first female president Tsai Ing-wen, Taiwan's shy but steely leader Taiwan: the place to be a woman in politics Her Democratic Progressive Party (DPP), which leans towards independence from China, won January's elections, ending eight years under pro-Beijing President Ma Ying-jeou. 'More emotional' Ms Tsai's singleness was a central focus of a commentary by Wang Weixing, originally published by Xinhua. Image copyright AFP Image caption Ms Tsai took the presidential oath at a ceremony in Taipei on Friday The article has since been removed from Xinhua's website, but can still be found on other news portals and micro-blogging accounts. Mr Wang, a senior military officer, pointed to an "extremist political-style development" displayed by Ms Tsai. "As a single female politician, Tsai Ing-wen does not have the emotional burden of love, of 'family' or children so her political style and strategies are displayed to be more emotional, personal and extreme," the report in Chinese read. It continued that her "erratic behaviour" influenced her political style and advised that she should take that into consideration, rather than "focus on long-term goals" for Taiwan. Alex Huang, a spokesman for Ms Tsai, told BBC News that her office had "no comments" in response to the piece. 'Typical discriminatory behaviour' But thousands of Chinese-language social media users have expressed outrage and anger towards the article, which has been reposted by several accounts on China's popular micro-blogging site Sina Weibo. Image copyright Facebook Image caption The president once said on Facebook she was "happiest" playing with her cats "This was the stupidest and most offensive thing I have read in ages," said a post from a Weibo user from the Chinese capital, Beijing. "Many women abroad admire Ms Tsai's tenacity and drive, especially the fact she is strong and independent and does not need a man to rule." "If the tables were turned and Ms Tsai was a man, she would be celebrated for being single. This is typical discriminatory behaviour but it still disgusts me," said another user on the site. Su Mei Cho, a teacher from Shanghai, left a comment in response to a post that was being circulated on the site: "If Xinhua wanted to criticise Tsai Ing-wen, do it on fair grounds and look at her ability to lead Taiwan and revitalise areas like defence and the economy. "What does her private life have to do with the way she governs Taiwan? Taiwanese celebrate her and her strong achievements and I doubt they will care as much about who she decides to date, such trivial writing and opinions expressed in this report." Over in Taiwan, netizens on the popular PTT forum were discussing the "poor taste" of opinions expressed towards their leader. "Even a woman's singlehood can make the news?" "They are probably threatened that a cat lady like Ms Tsai could beat any men she sets her mind to - they are intimidated," said another. But not everyone disagreed with the article. A user from China's Yunnan province said it was not an attack, and the article was based on facts. He questioned whether it would also be an attack for a psychologist to point out a patient's personality faults. Another user from Henan province, central China said the article was all based on public information, and said it was right to use public information to study a politician. Reporting by the BBC's Grace Tsoi, Heather Chen and Yashan Zhao.

A Chinese state media column has **criticized President Tsai Ing-wen**, labeling her as **“two-faced”** and suggesting her single status might lead to **“extreme” political decisions**. The author, **Wang Weixing**, identified as the director of the Association for Relations Across the Taiwan Straits and a scholar at the Academy of Military Science, claimed Tsai is a **“complicated person”** shaped by Taiwan's **“twisted society and political environment.”**

Wang argued that as a **single female politician**, Tsai lacks the **“burden of feelings of love,”** and is unconstrained by **“family” or “kids.”** This, he posited, results in a political style and executive strategy that **“tends to be emotional and extreme.”** He further asserted that Tsai's character is **“clearly two-faced,”** with one approach to her political management and another in her private life, which he described as **“very deceiving.”**

The article, initially published by Xinhua with the title “Exposing Tsai Ing-wen,” has since been **deleted from the original platform**. However, it has been **reposted by other Chinese media outlets** under the title “Xinhua: Being single may cause Tsai Ing-wen’s political style to lean towards extremism.”

Tsai was elected after voters **rejected closer ties with China**. In her inauguration speech, she advocated for **“positive dialogue”** with Beijing. On her first day in office, her government **dropped charges against sunflower movement activists**. Beijing had previously warned Tsai that it would **sever critical contacts** if she did not affirm her support for the **“one China” principle**, which posits that the mainland and Taiwan are part of a single sovereign state.

**Beijing's Sexist Attacks on Taiwan's New President Tsai Ing-wen**

Taiwan's new president, Tsai Ing-wen, faces a **rocky start** with Beijing, and one Chinese official has warned of **further trouble**, attributing it to her being a **single woman**. In a now-deleted editorial published by Xinhua news agency, Wang Weixing, a Beijing-based Taiwan specialist, launched a **personal and sexist attack** on Tsai, who was sworn in as Taiwan's first female president.

Wang controversially claimed that as a single woman, Tsai lacks the "emotional burden of love" and "family restraints," leading her to adopt an "**emotional, personal, and extreme**" political style. He further theorized that her "erratic behavior," influenced by her father's polygamy, causes her to prioritize "extreme short-term satisfaction" over long-term goals. This pseudo-psychoanalysis appears to be a thinly veiled jab at Tsai's inauguration speech, where she replaced the term "1992 Consensus" with "historic fact." Beijing insists that Tsai's acceptance of the "1992 Consensus"—an agreement stating "one China" with differing interpretations—is a **non-negotiable prerequisite** for future diplomatic talks.

While Wang's editorial was quickly removed from official Chinese news sites, it has ignited a **firestorm of criticism** on Chinese social media. Netizens have widely condemned the **sexist attacks**, with one Weibo user calling it the "stupidest and most offensive thing I have read in ages." Many pointed out that women abroad admire Tsai's **tenacity and independence**, highlighting her strength without needing a man.

Comparisons were drawn to other successful single politicians, such as South Korean President Park Geun-hye and former Chinese Vice Premier Wu Yi, to underscore the **absurdity and offensiveness** of Wang's claims. Professor Li Yunlong of the Central Party School of the Communist Party of China questioned the relevance of marital status to political views, labeling it "**naked discrimination**." He argued that marital status doesn't dictate political extremism, citing the married former Taiwan president Chen Shui-bian as an example.

The editorial has also drawn **significant backlash and bewilderment** within Taiwan. Some netizens speculated that Beijing's reaction stems from intimidation, suggesting they are "threatened that a cat lady like Ms Tsai could beat any men she sets her mind to."

**Taiwan has made history by inaugurating its first female president, Tsai Ing-wen.** She faces **significant hurdles** in her new position. These include managing **increasingly strained relations with Beijing** and revitalizing Taiwan's **struggling economy**.

Tsai was sworn in on Friday at the Presidential Office Building in Taipei. The ceremony took place before the national flag and a portrait of Sun Yat-sen, the **founder of the Republic of China**. This government relocated to Taiwan in 1949 after the communists gained control of mainland China.

**China considers Taiwan its territory** and has reacted to Tsai's election by **increasing pressure**. This pressure includes military drills, diplomatic actions, and deportations across the border.

Domestically, Tsai must address an economy that has entered a **recession**. This downturn is due to a **drop in exports** caused by weak global demand.

### State Media Criticizes Taiwan's President Based on Marital Status

An op-ed published in an official Chinese state newspaper, Xinhua, has drawn significant criticism for suggesting that Taiwan's newly inaugurated president, Tsai Ing-wen, is unfit for her position due to her unmarried and childless status. The article, published on Tuesday, linked Tsai's personal life to her political approach and policies.

#### Allegations of Emotional and Extreme Politics

The Xinhua piece asserted that as a single female politician, Tsai is free from "emotional encumbrances of love, no family restraint, no children to worry about." This, the article claimed, led to her political style and tactics being "often emotional, personalized, and extreme." Consequently, the analysis concluded that Tsai's approach was more short-term, with a focus on details rather than long-term political strategy.

#### Backlash and Accusations of Discrimination

The original Xinhua story was subsequently removed, as were copies on other Chinese news portals. However, the piece ignited widespread online condemnation. Many users pointed to successful, single Asian female politicians like South Korean President Park Geun-hye and former Chinese Vice Premier Wu Yi as counterexamples.

Li Yunlong, a professor at the Central Party School of the Communist Party of China, expressed his outrage on Weibo, China's microblogging platform. He questioned the relevance of being single to political views, calling it "naked discrimination against singleton." He also contrasted Tsai's situation with that of former Taiwan president Chen Shui-bian, who is married but, in Li's view, was more extreme.

Others accused state media of sexism and exhibiting "Straight Man Cancer," a popular Chinese term for chauvinistic behavior that belittles women.

#### Growing Concerns About Women's Rights in China

While under Chairman Mao Zedong, women were famously stated to "hold up half the sky," there is a growing perception that Chinese women today face increasing discrimination. Reports indicate that women constitute less than 10% of board members at the country's top 300 companies. Furthermore, China's state-run media frequently appears to prioritize women's appearance and marital status over discussions of equal rights.

Examples include the People's Daily publishing an online gallery titled "Beauty with brains" featuring female journalists, and a 2015 Lunar New Year television gala that included comedy skits mocking overweight and unmarried women. These incidents have led to feminist calls for an end to such televised programs.

Earlier, influential writer Zhou Guoping sparked anger by suggesting that "a man can have thousands of ambitions but a woman only one" – to give birth.

#### Societal Expectations and Female Leadership

Guo Weiqing, a professor at Sun Yat-sen University, noted that an increasing number of women are dissatisfied with traditional gender roles. He believes that recent "sexist incidents" reflect some men's anxieties about women not adhering to perceived traditional femininity.

However, online commentary suggests a strong disagreement with these views, with many seeing strong female leaders like Tsai as role models. The Taiwanese government had not immediately responded to a request for comment from CNN.

A recent opinion piece from a Chinese state-run news agency has sparked widespread criticism for its commentary on Taiwan's President Tsai Ing-wen. The article, published by Xinhua's International Herald Leader, suggested that Tsai's leadership style is "extreme" and "personalized" due to her being unmarried and childless.

Tsai Ing-wen, who holds degrees from the London School of Economics and Cornell University, was inaugurated as Taiwan's president. In her inaugural address, she encouraged citizens to embrace the future and reject prejudice.

However, the Xinhua opinion piece presented a different perspective. It argued that as a single female politician, Tsai lacks the "emotional burden of love, of 'family' or children." This, the article claimed, leads to her political style and strategy being "emotional, personalised and extreme." The author, Wang Weixing, an official from a Beijing-controlled body managing Taiwan relations, advised that when dealing with Tsai, one should consider her "experience, personality and mindset."

The commentary quickly drew a strong backlash online. Many users on Weibo, China's microblogging platform, condemned the article. Reactions included accusations of attacking her personal life and labeling the author a "male chauvinist pig."

Zhang Hongzhong, a journalism professor at Beijing Normal University, criticized the piece as intellectually flawed and offensive to single women. He also noted that as a state-run agency, Xinhua's publication of such content could damage China's international image.

Following the outcry, the article was removed from the newspaper's website. It was replaced with a "page not found" message.

This incident contrasts with President Xi Jinping's stated commitment to gender equality. Xi has previously emphasized the importance of empowering women in society and the economy, and supporting their leadership roles. However, reports indicate that feminist activism in China has faced increased restrictions since Xi came to power in 2012. For instance, celebrations for the 20th anniversary of the 1995 UN Fourth Conference on Women in Beijing were reportedly banned. Furthermore, there are no women currently serving on the Communist party's top seven-member ruling council, and very few women hold high-ranking positions in Chinese politics.

Hillary Clinton's public image is often defined by her **extensive professional achievements**, leading to scrutiny of her personal life beyond politics. While close friends describe her as **warm and compassionate**, her public persona primarily emphasizes her **résumé and policy stances**. For instance, a biographical video titled "Fighter" used archival footage of her advocacy. However, a contemporary interview segment, with its **carefully controlled lighting, setting, and attire**, presented her as a polished brand representative rather than an accessible individual.

This strong focus on her professional identity can evoke negative perceptions associated with **workaholism**. Workaholism is essentially a form of **emotional self-alienation**. Individuals deeply immersed in their work may find their personal feelings don't influence their critical decisions. Their professional role can overshadow their inherent personality, intruding on the natural intimacies of the human spirit. As Martyn Lloyd-Jones observed, some lives become so defined by their occupation that their epitaph might read: "Born a person, died a [profession]."

In her public presentation, Clinton projects an **exclusively professional image**: industrious, strategic, goal-oriented, and seemingly guarded. This makes it **difficult for external observers to connect with her on a personal level**; she often appears as a role rather than an individual. This formal, career-centric persona clashes with the social media era, which thrives on **intimacy, personal disclosure, vulnerability, and trust**. It also differs from the lived experiences of most Americans, who typically find more **vitality and engagement outside their professional roles**. Consequently, many perceive her as **Machiavellian, cunning, power-seeking, and untrustworthy**.

This situation offers a broader insight, especially for individuals who have achieved fulfilling careers. Even a socially beneficial calling can become all-consuming, leading individuals to disconnect from their authentic selves. It is arguably more critical for those with demanding vocations to actively cultivate and visibly demonstrate **personal sanctuaries separate from their professional lives**. These sanctuaries can be found in pursuits like **play, solitude, family engagement, spiritual practice, hobbies, and leisure activities**.

Abraham Joshua Heschel described the Sabbath as "**a palace in time which we build**." He saw it as more than just rest before work, but a temporal elevation for which one prepares. Josef Pieper argued that leisure is not an activity but a **disposition of the mind**. It involves stepping away from strenuous effort and fostering a stillness conducive to contemplation and appreciation of existence. Even individuals with successful careers need these sanctuaries to remain fully realized persons, not just productive units. It seems the electorate may not fully trust candidates who **don't reveal these personal dimensions**.

query: What does the R+L=J theory claim about Jon Snow's parentage in Game of Thrones?

documents.text: Warning: This post contains spoilers that prove fan theories can sometimes turn out true. It's a weird world. Where were you when a fan theory actually came true? R+L=J is something most Game of Thrones fans have heard about for years, but on Season 6's very bloody and eventful finale, the show finally confirmed the truth about Jon Snow's lineage. Well, basically. If your GoT math is a little dusty, the R+L=J theory, lovingly explained here, basically posits that Jon Snow was not a bastard Ned Stark brought home from Robert Baratheon's rebellion. In fact, the theory claims, Jon is the son of Ned Stark's sister Lyanna and her captor Rhaegar Targaryen, older brother to Daenerys. Clues in the books, hints on the show and wild conversations have led many fans to this conclusion. In the finale, Bran Stark, through his three-eyed raven powers, observed Ned finding his sister in the terribly-named Tower of Joy and taking her new baby as she slowly expires. So, that's it right? We can all go home and pat ourselves on the back for being so smart right? Eh, not exactly. While now we do know that Jon is not a bastard and indeed does have Stark blood flowing through his know-nothing veins, Lyanna never explicitly tells who the father is. The show creators have her whisper it into Ned's ear because they love more rampant speculation. But that didn't stop a self-congratulatory internet from loving itself. Tbh I'm so happy about R L = J because we got there just by the small hints scattered here and there and it's so satisfying #GameOfThrones — drama queen (@CiaoHalsey) June 27, 2016 R L = J. My favorite theory finally come to life. #GameofThrones pic.twitter.com/pNvbIKvPCa — Haley Beth (@lil_holmie94) June 27, 2016 R+L=J. It's done. We did it. It is law. It is canon. We've done our job guys. Cheers to the old gods and the new. #GoTFinale — MsStark (@notWendySon) June 27, 2016 IT IS CONFIRMED GUYS, AFTER YEARS THE R L = J IS CONFIRMED #GameofThrones pic.twitter.com/paM3Cqg0wI — clary • (@skywalkerules) June 27, 2016 Well, done, internet. We're proud of you. Have something to add to this story? Share it in the comments.

Warning: Contains spoilers for the Game of Thrones Season 6 finale. LONDON — Back in Season 1 of Game of Thrones, you probably never thought you'd be crying over the death of a Lannister — but here we are. Take a bow, Tommen Baratheon. You were too damn good for the world of Westeros. Although a lot of us predicted that Tommen would be killed during the Season 6 finale — some thought he'd be killed by the Mountain, while others thought he'd burn in the fire at the Great Sept — his actual death was much more poignant than that. Tommen's face when he sees the Great Sept burning says it all. The kid is completely distraught. Image: hbo In that moment, Tommen knows that Margaery — who, it turns out, he really did love a hell of a lot — has likely died. He also knows, at least on some level, that his mother is the one responsible. The fact that he takes his crown off before jumping is significant. This could obviously mean a few things, but to us it seems to signify that Tommen doesn't want any part of the game — or any part of the brutal world of Westeros — any more. Essentially, it's his suicide note. The way the whole scene is done — the lingering shot of the open window and the complete lack of music — makes the whole thing even more upsetting. Damn Tommen really loved Margarey. Now Cersei's prophecy has finally come true in full. 💔 #GameofThrones — Zakiya Jamal (@ZakiyaJamal) June 27, 2016 At the end of the day, Tommen was the antithesis of Joffrey and Cersei — he was just too nice for the backstabbing harshness of the Game of Thrones world. RIP, Tommen. Image: hbo Have something to add to this story? Share it in the comments.
