Noem made history by being the first female governor of the state AP 10/20 Gabierla Martinez and Cesar Delgado cheer on Democratic gubernatorial candidate Jared Polis REUTERS 11/20 Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez became the youngest women ever elected to Congress, representing New York’s 14th congressional district AFP/Getty 12/20 Democratic congressional candidate Rashida Tlaib celebrates with family and friends at her midterm election night party in Detroit after won and also became the nation’s first Muslim woman to congress alongside Ilhan Omar who was also elected Reuters 13/20 Supporters of Democratic candidate for Kansas' 3rd Congressional District Sharice Davids react to election results Getty 14/20 Already having become the first Native American woman to chair a state political party, Deb Haaland has now become the joint-first Native American woman to be elected to congress, alongside Sharice Davids Reuters 15/20 Supporters of Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez cheer during her election night party in the Queens Borough AFP/Getty 16/20 Young Kim hugs her son Alvin after she won a seat in congress AP 17/20 Supporters of Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez cheer AFP/Getty Images 18/20 Asma Mohammed and Ashley Fairbanks celebrate as results come in at Democratic congressional candidate Ilhan Omar's election night headquarters AP 19/20 Democrat Deb Haaland hugs a voter REUTERS 20/20 Supporters of Democratic House candidate from Kansas Sharice Davids cheer and cry after learning she won her race EPA However, it has emerged that a top attorney at the Florida Department of State sent a letter last week asking federal prosecutors to investigate whether Democrats distributed false information that could have resulted in voters having mail-in ballots disqualified over mismatched signatures. Four county supervisors turned over information showing Democratic Party operatives had changed official forms to say voters had until two days after the election to fix any problems with mail-in ballot signatures. Under current law, a voter has until the day before Election Day to fix a problem. Both the midterm races, for senator and governor, were very close in Florida. A few tens of thousands of votes – or even less – separated Rick Scott and Bill Nelson in the former, and Ron DeSantis and Andrew Gillum in the latter. Republicans Scott and DeSantis held the lead going into the recount. But some of Florida’s ageing vote-counting machines overheated, forcing some 174,000 ballots to be re-tallied. Both Mr Scott and Mr Nelson have filed lawsuits in an acrimonious count process which had uncomfortable echoes of the 2000 presidential election contest between George W Bush and Al Gore. Additional reporting by agencies

query: How are the daily lives of South Koreans affected by the increasing tensions between the U.S. and North Korea?

documents.text: SEOUL—The possibility of a military confrontation between the U.S. and North Korea is dominating headlines across South Korea, but in the streets of Seoul, concerns about a North Korean missile or nuclear test—or even a pre-emptive U.S. military strike—are drowned out by the rhythms of daily life. “North Korea makes threats, but they will not do it. No way,” said Yu Jeom-soon, a 59-year-old shop worker in downtown Seoul, who sat on a...

North Korea showcased what appeared to be new long-range and submarine-based missiles during a massive military parade on Saturday, commemorating the 105th birth anniversary of its founder, Kim Il Sung. This display coincided with the approach of a U.S. nuclear-powered aircraft carrier group to the Korean Peninsula. The parade prominently featured missiles, with leader Kim Jong Un observed greeting the commander of the Strategic Forces, the unit responsible for the nation's missile arsenal.

The recent U.S. Navy strike on a Syrian airfield using Tomahawk missiles has intensified scrutiny of President Donald Trump's intentions toward North Korea. The reclusive nation has a history of conducting missile and nuclear tests in defiance of United Nations sanctions, frequently issuing threats against the United States. Kim Jong Un, appearing relaxed in a dark suit and engaging with aides, presided over the "Day of the Sun" festivities in Pyongyang's main Kim Il Sung Square. The square was filled with goose-stepping soldiers and marching bands, followed by tanks, multiple-launch rocket systems, and other weaponry, all set against the backdrop of the Taedonggang River under a hazy spring sky. Overhead, single-engine propeller planes flew in a formation representing the number 105. Notably, unlike some previous parades attended by Kim, no senior Chinese official was present. China, North Korea's sole major ally, has voiced opposition to its missile and nuclear tests and supports UN sanctions. On Friday, China reiterated its call for dialogue to de-escalate the escalating tensions.

Weapons analysts suggested that some of the displayed missiles might be new types of intercontinental ballistic missiles (ICBMs). While North Korea claims to have developed and be capable of launching a missile that can reach the U.S. mainland, experts believe it is still some time away from possessing all the necessary technological capabilities.

**"Early Stages" of Development**

Melissa Hanham, a senior research associate at the U.S.-based Middlebury Institute of International Studies at Monterey, California, noted that North Korea displayed two new types of ICBMs housed in canister launchers mounted on trucks. She interpreted this as an indication that Pyongyang is exploring a "new concept" for ICBMs. However, Hanham cautioned, "North Korea has a habit of showing off new concepts in parades before they ever test or launch them. It is still early days for these missile designs."

The Pukkuksong submarine-launched ballistic missiles (SLBMs) were also featured in the parade, marking the first time these missiles, with a range exceeding 1,000 km (600 miles), have been shown publicly at a military event. Joshua Pollack, editor of the Washington-based Nonproliferation Review, stated that the display of multiple SLBMs suggests North Korea is advancing its strategy to deploy missiles from submarines, which are inherently difficult to detect. "It suggests a commitment to this program," Pollack commented. "Multiple SLBMs seems like a declaration of intent to advance the program."

North Korea, which remains technically at war with South Korea following the 1950-53 armistice, has a pattern of conducting missile or nuclear tests to coincide with significant political events. The nation frequently issues threats against the United States, South Korea, and Japan. Choe Ryong Hae, a close confidant of Kim Jong Un, delivered a strongly worded warning to the United States during the parade. He declared, "If the United States wages reckless provocation against us, our revolutionary power will instantly counter with annihilating strike, and we will respond to full-out war with full-out war and to nuclear war with our style of nuclear strike warfare."

**Pence to Visit South Korea**

North Korea's state news agency KCNA criticized the Trump administration's "serious military hysteria," describing it as having reached a "dangerous phase which can no longer be overlooked." The United States has signaled an end to its policy of "strategic patience" with North Korea. U.S. Vice President Mike Pence is scheduled to visit South Korea on Sunday as part of a 10-day trip to Asia.

China has also intensified economic pressure on North Korea, banning all imports of North Korean coal on February 26th under UN sanctions, thereby cutting off the North's most crucial export commodity. Air China, the country's national airline, had previously canceled some flights to Pyongyang due to low demand, but it has not suspended all services, according to a statement on Friday, refuting a report by Chinese state broadcaster CCTV that all flights between the two cities were to be halted. China's Global Times newspaper, an affiliate of the Communist Party's official paper, the People's Daily, suggested that North Korea must have felt the impact of the 11-ton "mother of all bombs" deployed by U.S. forces against Islamic State-linked fighters in Afghanistan on Thursday. An editorial in the paper stated, "It would be nice if the bomb could frighten Pyongyang, but its actual impact may just be the opposite."

On Friday, North Korea condemned the United States for deploying "huge nuclear strategic assets" to the region as the USS Carl Vinson strike group, led by a nuclear-powered aircraft carrier, approached. In Dandong, China's primary border crossing with North Korea, hundreds of North Koreans gathered at a cultural center, carrying floral tributes. Men wore pins featuring images of Kim Il Sung, while women were dressed in vibrant traditional attire. Crowds lined up to pay their respects to portraits of their state founder before viewing an exhibition of photographs and North Korean artwork. KCNA effusively praised Kim Il Sung, recalling his 1994 meeting with former U.S. President Jimmy Carter. The agency quoted Carter as saying, "Former U.S. President Jimmy Carter was so fascinated by his personality as to say that Kim Il Sung is greater than that George Washington, Thomas Jefferson and Abraham Lincoln put together, eulogizing him as the great sun god of human destiny."

### U.S. Nuclear Detection Aircraft Deploys to Okinawa Amid Korean Peninsula Tensions

A specialized U.S. Air Force aircraft, the WC-135 Constant Phoenix, has arrived at Kadena Air Base in Okinawa, Japan. This plane, nicknamed a "nuke-sniffer," is designed to detect radioactive particles in the air, a key capability for monitoring nuclear activities.

#### Deployment and Detection Capabilities

The WC-135 Constant Phoenix's arrival was reported on Friday, April 7, 2017, by a senior Japan Self-Defense Forces official to the Nikkei newspaper. An observer of military aircraft at Kadena, Satoru Kuba, also confirmed the plane's presence. While official statements from the Air Force’s 18th Wing at Kadena and Japan’s Ministry of Defense were not immediately available, photos of the aircraft on the base's runway have since surfaced.

The Pentagon routinely sends one of its two WC-135 aircraft to the Asia-Pacific region. This practice increased after North Korea's underground nuclear test in 2006. The aircraft also flew over Japan in 2011 following the Fukushima Dai-ichi nuclear power plant disaster. The WC-135, a modified C-135B, features special external equipment to collect air samples and particulate matter. These samples are then sent for lab analysis to identify any radioactive traces.

#### Heightened Regional Concerns

This deployment comes as concerns grow over North Korea's ongoing nuclear and missile programs. The country has conducted several nuclear tests and numerous missile launches recently. South Korean officials have warned of possible "provocations" from North Korea, especially before important commemorative events, such as the 105th birthday of Kim Il Sung and the 85th anniversary of the Korean People’s Army. South Korea is also preparing for its presidential elections in May, adding to regional sensitivity.

Following recent U.S. military actions in Syria, North Korea has issued strong warnings of "catastrophic consequences" if the United States launches a pre-emptive strike. These rising tensions have led the U.S. Pacific Command to redirect the USS Carl Vinson strike group toward the Korean peninsula. This is the second time the strike group has been sent to the region in recent months; its original destination was Australia for planned port visits. The Trump administration has reaffirmed its firm stance against Pyongyang's nuclear ambitions and has urged China to exert more influence over its ally. Chinese President Xi Jinping has pledged to work with the U.S. toward denuclearizing the Korean peninsula peacefully.

#### Previous Operations and Incidents

The WC-135 Constant Phoenix that arrived in Okinawa had previously been based at RAF Mildenhall in England in March. At that time, Air Force officials described the mission as routine, disputing media speculation about investigating a Russian nuclear test. Earlier in March, the Constant Phoenix experienced engine problems while en route to Kadena, forcing an emergency landing in Banda Aceh, Indonesia. An Indonesian air force spokesperson confirmed that the aircraft, carrying 20 American military personnel, requested permission to land due to a single engine failure. The exact cause of the engine issue is still being investigated, and no injuries were reported.

## Navigating North Korea's Nuclear Challenge: The Trump Administration's Delicate Balance

The Trump administration confronts a **pressing and escalating threat**: North Korea's nuclear ambitions, with reports suggesting a potential sixth nuclear test. A central element of President Trump's strategy involves **urging China to curb Pyongyang's aggressive actions**. In a significant display of military readiness, the Pentagon positioned a strike group in the western Pacific. National Security Adviser H.R. McMaster deemed this deployment **"prudent"** due to its proximity to North Korea. North Korea's state media responded with defiance, proclaiming their ability to **"strike the U.S. first"** and asserting that **"pre-emptive strikes are not solely the prerogative of the United States."**

### Independent Action and Diplomatic Pressure

President Trump's decision to authorize an airstrike in Syria during his summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping may signal a **willingness to act independently** against North Korea, mirroring his Syrian approach. Trump himself tweeted, **"if China decides to help, that would be great. If not, we will solve the problem without them!"** This indicates a two-pronged strategy: seeking Chinese cooperation while preparing for unilateral intervention.

### The Strategic Rationale Behind North Korea's Provocations

Addressing the North Korea issue is a **complex, multifaceted task**, often compared to brokering peace in the Middle East. The primary objectives are to **ease North Korea's security anxieties** while simultaneously **reducing regional fears of a North Korean attack**. Trump's policy is expected to focus on **pressuring Beijing to restrain Pyongyang** and implementing **additional sanctions**.

A critical insight for the U.S. is to **avoid underestimating North Korean leader Kim Jong-un** and to recognize the **essential role of South Korea**. While some in the U.S. might perceive Kim as irrational, a more strategic interpretation of Pyongyang's behavior—one that Trump, a proponent of brinksmanship, should consider—is that it serves North Korea's **strategic and economic interests**.

Kim's persistent pursuit of weapons programs is largely driven by a desire for **deterrence**, aiming to avoid the fates of leaders like Saddam Hussein or Muammar Gaddafi. As one individual involved in dialogues with North Koreans noted, they have questioned whether actions taken against Gaddafi and Syria would have occurred if they possessed similar capabilities.

### Kim Jong-un's Calculated Strategy for Survival

Despite the inherent secrecy of North Korea's internal politics, Kim appears to be acting with **deliberate intelligence** to maintain his leadership over the nation's corrupt system. Beyond deterrence and projecting domestic strength, his provocations may be strategically designed to **secure concessions**.

### Leveraging Perceived Danger for Strategic Gains

One compelling theory suggests that by **portraying itself as a greater threat**, North Korea can obtain more concessions from key regional players like China and, particularly, South Korea. These nations have a **stronger motivation than the U.S. to ensure Pyongyang's stability**. China fears a destabilizing collapse, while South Korea has long endured threats of Seoul being turned into a **"sea of fire."** Given Seoul's extreme proximity to the border, North Korea demonstrably possesses the means to carry out such devastating threats.

### Financial Incentives and Past Demands

Kim may anticipate further **financial benefits from Seoul**, especially with South Koreans heading to the polls in May, likely electing a president who favors engagement with Pyongyang. In his 2015 memoir, former South Korean President Lee Myung-bak detailed negotiations in 2009 where North Korea demanded **substantial aid**, including vast quantities of corn and rice, chemical fertilizers, and significant financial contributions for road construction and an economic development bank. These demands, totaling billions, were likely intended to **appease the elite and solidify Kim's power**. When Lee rejected these demands, North Korea's approach shifted dramatically, culminating in the **torpedoing of the South Korean naval ship Cheonan in March 2010**, which tragically killed 46 sailors.

### Diversion of Funds and Economic Interdependence

South Korea has also channeled funds to North Korea through the jointly operated Kaesong industrial zone. A February 2016 statement from South Korea's Unification Ministry indicated that **70% of funds designated for wages and fees at Kaesong had been diverted to Pyongyang's weapons program and luxury goods for Kim**. Since its inception in the early 2000s, Seoul and South Korean companies reportedly invested over **$820 million in Kaesong**.

While China's aid to North Korea remains undisclosed, it is undeniably the nation's **most significant trading partner**. The U.S. provided over **$1.2 billion in foreign assistance from 1995**, largely ceasing in 2009. China offers North Korea a crucial market and access to the global financial system, while South Korea provides direct financial support.

### The Grave Risk of Undermining Sanctions

If Seoul again provides Kim or other elites with **hundreds of millions of dollars**, a distinct and concerning possibility, it would **significantly undermine international sanctions**. South Korea has the most to gain from Korean unification and the most to lose from a devastating war. Therefore, maintaining **close and constant communication** between Donald Trump and Rex Tillerson with South Korea is absolutely vital, as even **$450 million in cash can have a substantial impact** on North Korea's strategic calculus.

**Ex-CIA Chief: Trump's Provocations Fuel North Korean Nuclear Threat**

Former acting CIA Director Mike Morell contends that President Donald Trump's current strategy is **intensifying the nuclear threat** from North Korea. Morell advises that Trump should **disregard Kim Jong Un's provocations**, as a show of force merely **exacerbates tensions**.

### North Korea's Escalating Actions

North Korea, under Kim Jong Un's leadership, has become a central international concern for President Trump. This focus is driven by the Democratic People's Republic of Korea's (DPRK) recent ballistic missile tests and preparations indicating a potential nuclear weapon detonation. The DPRK was a significant topic during Trump's summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping. Following this meeting, Trump ordered the deployment of the USS Carl Vinson and its carrier strike group to waters off the Korean peninsula.

### Morell's Recommended Strategy

Speaking on "CBS This Morning," Morell explained that Kim Jong Un is attempting to **challenge the new U.S. president** and compel a return to negotiations. He contrasted this with the Obama administration's policy of disengagement from North Korea's aggressive posturing. "Kim Jong Un desires a return to a paradigm where we offer concessions in response to their aggressive actions," Morell stated. He added that "our own saber-rattling and the deployment of naval assets like aircraft carriers are contributing to the escalation of this crisis."

### The Optimal Path Forward

Morell, who also served as Deputy CIA Director, outlined his recommended strategy: "The optimal strategy is to **simply ignore this adversary**, deter them from deploying or proliferating these weapons, and bolster our defensive capabilities." It's worth noting Morell was a vocal critic of Trump during the 2016 election and endorsed Hillary Clinton.

### Diplomatic and Military Pressure

Beyond naval deployments, President Trump has intensified his rhetoric. In private discussions with President Xi and public statements, Trump has sought to pressure China, North Korea's main patron, to rein in the Kim regime. Trump declared that if China proves unwilling to act, "we will solve the problem without them." Following the U.S. deployment of a large non-nuclear munition in Afghanistan, Trump remarked to reporters, "I don't know if this sends a message" to North Korea, adding, "it doesn't make any difference if it does or not." He concluded, "North Korea is a problem. The problem will be taken care of."

### North Korea's Response

The DPRK has responded with its characteristic belligerent rhetoric. A statement issued to CNN warned, "We will hold the U.S. fully accountable for the catastrophic consequences that may be brought about by its high-handed and outrageous acts." In an interview with The Associated Press, North Korea's Vice Foreign Minister indicated his nation's readiness for conflict with the U.S. should it become unavoidable. "We possess a formidable nuclear deterrent already, and we will certainly not remain passive in the face of a U.S. preemptive strike," he stated. "Whatever the U.S. initiates, we will confront it. We are fully prepared to manage any eventuality."

Since the November election campaign, U.S. President Donald Trump and his foreign policy advisors have adopted a notably assertive stance toward North Korea. Key pronouncements include the declaration that "the policy of strategic patience has ended," a statement that "the United States has spoken enough about North Korea. We have no further comment," and the assertion that "all options are on the table." Among these options, a preemptive strike targeting North Korea's nuclear and missile infrastructure is reportedly a primary consideration for Washington.

These strong warnings were underscored by tangible actions. During the Foal Eagle–Key Resolve military exercises, advanced strategic bombers and an aircraft carrier strike group were deployed to the Korean Peninsula. Concurrently, international perceptions have intensified regarding a deteriorating security environment on the peninsula, with some observers suggesting it is on the precipice of conflict.

It was against this backdrop of heightened tensions that President Trump convened a summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping. Trump repeatedly voiced his dissatisfaction with China's efforts to curb North Korea's activities, indicating that the U.S. would take unilateral action if Beijing failed to exert control. This dynamic fueled significant international attention and high expectations for a potential accord on the North Korea issue.

However, the summit concluded without a joint press conference or communiqué. Details regarding the depth of discussions on North Korea remain undisclosed, beyond a mutual reaffirmation of opposition to nuclear weapons and a commitment to robust sanctions against Pyongyang.

The United States and China share a clear divergence on the issue of North Korea's weapons of mass destruction programs. Washington maintains a firm stance against Pyongyang's development of an intercontinental ballistic missile (ICBM) capable of reaching the U.S. mainland. From the U.S. perspective, North Korea would cross a critical threshold upon successfully completing an atmospheric reentry test for such a missile, a pivotal step in acquiring ICBM technology. In such a scenario, the Trump administration would seriously contemplate a preemptive strike.

Such a preemptive strike carries the grave risk of igniting a second Korean War. A subsequent U.S. administration would face the formidable challenge of either preventing a U.S.-initiated preemptive strike or deciding to participate if North Korea exhibits clear signs of provocation. South Korea cannot permit the United States to unilaterally initiate hostilities on the peninsula.

President Trump's approach, characterized by direct pressure on Xi to leverage trade for controlling North Korea, met with resistance from the experienced Chinese leader, who defended Beijing's established principles without making definitive commitments. This impasse signals potential future complications. Even with the deployment of advanced U.S. strategic assets to the peninsula, Kim Jong-un's behavior may not change. Should North Korea successfully launch a mid-range ballistic missile, thereby advancing its ICBM capabilities, and conduct a sixth nuclear test, President Trump would feel compelled to act to preserve the credibility of America's extended deterrence. South Korea must proactively articulate its position regarding any potential U.S.-led preemptive strike.

A speculative report suggesting an agreement between Trump and Xi to facilitate Kim Jong-un's exile abroad appears to stem from a fundamental misunderstanding of Kim's current confidence and North Korea's strategic importance to China. For China, which is engaged in strategic competition with the U.S. in the Western Pacific, including the South China Sea, North Korea serves as a crucial strategic buffer. This geopolitical reality explains Beijing's continued operation of the oil pipeline to Pyongyang, despite international pressure.

Within the United States, a proposal advocating for a "cap" on North Korea's nuclear program is gaining traction as a potential resolution to the crisis. This approach represents a compromise between a nuclear freeze and complete, verifiable, and irreversible dismantlement. A nuclear freeze, however, carries the inherent risk of reversal as circumstances change. Conversely, complete dismantlement is currently deemed unrealistic given North Korea's apparent unwillingness to relinquish its nuclear program. The "cap" method necessitates that the imposed limitations remain permanent, but there is no assurance of North Korea's adherence, mirroring the inherent weakness of a freeze. Consequently, proponents of the nuclear cap plan argue for the simultaneous pursuit of diplomatic relations between North Korea and the U.S., alongside peace negotiations.

The U.S. deployment of strategic assets to the Korean Peninsula could also be interpreted as an initial step toward initiating dialogue between North Korea and the United States. It is noteworthy that opposition to a preemptive strike exists within the U.S., with proponents recognizing its potential to precipitate conflict on the peninsula. The primary obstacle remains North Korea's insistence on direct bilateral talks with the U.S., coupled with its apparent distrust of China.

It is imperative that South Korea avoids a situation akin to the 1994 Agreed Framework, where it faced unfavorable outcomes following U.S.-North Korea bilateral dialogue. The nation must prioritize the establishment of a clear roadmap for inter-Korean talks, peace, and eventual unification. Ensuring that major powers integrate this roadmap into their North Korea policies should be the paramount objective of South Korea's next administration in its foreign and inter-Korean affairs.

## **Fighting the "404 Error": The Internet Archive's "No More 404" Initiative**

The Internet Archive is on a **critical mission** to tackle the widespread problem of broken web links. By **systematically crawling** the internet, they meticulously save web pages as they appear, along with the pages they point to. This **proactive strategy** ensures that even if original content changes or disappears from the live web, a **preserved copy** of that referenced version is still available.

### **Making Archived Links Work for You**

The main goal is to **restore trust in online information** by swapping broken links with reliable references to archived content. The Internet Archive imagines a web where these **saved links** act as essential replacements for dead URLs. They can also serve as **extra resources**, helping users grasp the original meaning intended by webpage creators. This initiative specifically focuses on supported "No More 404" websites, showing a **dedicated effort** to repair the internet's digital structure.

Seoul, South Korea - Recent US missile strikes on a Syrian airfield have sparked concerns in South Korea, with some interpreting them as a sign of **US President Donald Trump's willingness to take unilateral military action against North Korea**. This is particularly significant given North Korea's proximity and its status as a **persistent diplomatic challenge for US policymakers**.

US Secretary of State Rex Tillerson has stated that **"all of the options are on the table"** regarding North Korea. President Trump himself has tweeted that North Korea is "looking for trouble" and, if China (North Korea's sole major ally) doesn't address its growing nuclear and missile programs, **"we will solve the problem without them!"**. Adding to the tension, the US recently dispatched the **USS Carl Vinson aircraft carrier strike group** towards the Korean peninsula in a clear show of force.

While these assertive actions haven't fundamentally altered daily life in South Korea, **a palpable sense of unease has emerged among the public**. Social media has been abuzz with **rumors of an impending war**, prompting government officials to publicly reassure citizens and urge calm. South Korea's Ministry of Foreign Affairs spokesman, Cho June-hyuck, stated on Tuesday that the **US would not initiate military action without first consulting Seoul**.

For South Koreans, **tensions with the nuclear-armed and rhetorically hostile North Korea are a familiar reality**, especially in April. This month typically sees large-scale joint military exercises between South Korea and the US. North Korea consistently protests these drills, accusing the allies of practicing for an invasion and often **escalating its threatening rhetoric in response**. April is also a significant month for North Korea, marked by **major annual events that have historically been used as occasions for missile launches**.

### The Conversation in the South

A significant portion of the domestic discourse in South Korea centers on the **possibility of the new Trump administration launching military action against the North without South Korean government consultation**. Some South Korean media outlets have voiced concerns that Washington might proceed with actions that could **jeopardize the country's security**. Kim Young-hie, a columnist for the JoongAng Ilbo newspaper, warned on Thursday that **"A pre-emptive strike could trigger a second Korean War"** and emphasized that **"South Korea cannot allow the US to make the decision alone to start a war on the peninsula."**

Moon Jae-in, a leading liberal candidate in South Korea's upcoming presidential election, addressed the escalating tensions on Facebook, stating, **"South Korea's security is as important as the US' security. Therefore, there must be no pre-emptive strike without South Korea's consent."** Professor Chang Yong-seok of Seoul National University noted, "I think South Koreans are nervous, even though the US is unlikely to carry out a pre-emptive strike. The deployment of so much US military power, and neighbouring countries such as China and Japan expressing worries, inevitably makes Koreans anxious."

### The US Variable

Since taking office, Trump administration officials have signaled a departure from the Obama administration's **"strategic patience"** policy towards North Korea, which involved waiting for verifiable denuclearization steps before engaging in formal dialogue. While Trump had previously expressed a willingness to meet with North Korean leader Kim Jong-un, his recent summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping concluded without a firm commitment to increased cooperation on North Korea. The **deployment of military assets, notably the USS Carl Vinson**, has been a prominent feature of Trump's approach. During a recent phone call, Xi reportedly urged stability on the peninsula **"through peaceful means."**

Yang Moo-jin, a professor at the University of North Korean Studies in Seoul, suggests that **"By deploying the USS Carl Vinson, Trump is probably trying to show off US power to North Korea. But throughout his tenure, Trump is likely to put pressure on North Korea through rhetoric, rather than military action."**

Satellite imagery released on Wednesday by 38 North, a US-based North Korean affairs website, indicated **continued activity at a North Korean nuclear test site**, with personnel and vehicle movements suggesting potential preparations for a nuclear weapon test. North Korea also convened its legislature for a rare meeting on Tuesday, and Saturday marks the birthday of its late founding leader, Kim Il-sung. Analysts are closely watching to see if Pyongyang will use this occasion to conduct a missile or nuclear test to **demonstrate its growing strike capabilities and bolster domestic patriotism**. Professor Chang anticipates some form of provocation, stating, **"It's likely that they'll launch [a] medium-range missile, just to show off their power, not to actually provoke a war."**

### Life Goes On in Seoul

South Korea is also navigating its own political landscape, with the **impeachment of President Park Geun-hye** in March leading to an accelerated presidential election campaign. The upcoming election, scheduled for May 9th, is dominated by issues of **economic recovery and anti-corruption efforts**, rather than the North Korean threat. Allegations that Park misused her presidential authority to benefit a friend were the primary catalyst for her impeachment, and she is currently facing criminal charges.

Despite the geopolitical tensions, life in Seoul continues its usual rhythm. On a pleasant Thursday evening, the **Mapo district buzzed with activity**, with restaurants and coffee shops filled with patrons. When asked about the rising tensions, a middle-aged restaurant server remarked, "I'm maybe a little nervous. It's impossible to tell what North Korea might do. Anyway, it makes no difference to me."

query: What was President Obama's primary focus during his last press conference in 2016, and how did he address concerns regarding Vladimir Putin and Russian hacking?

documents.text:
In his final press conference of 2016, President Barack Obama delivered a comprehensive address, dedicating significant attention to the issue of Russian interference in the U.S. election and his administration's decisive response. While he deliberately avoided direct criticism of the incoming administration's cabinet selections or President-elect Donald Trump's controversial communication with Taiwan, Obama's statements conveyed a powerful and unambiguous message concerning Russia's disruptive actions. He reserved a substantial portion of his remarks for Russian President Vladimir Putin, directly implicating him in the coordinated cyberattacks that targeted the Democratic National Committee and the presidential campaign of Hillary Clinton.

When questioned about Putin's personal involvement in orchestrating the election interference, Obama unequivocally stated that the cyberattacks originated from the "highest level of the Russian government." He underscored the centralized nature of power in Russia, emphasizing that "not much happens" without Putin's explicit awareness, thereby implying direct and ultimate responsibility. Obama further revealed that he had personally confronted Putin regarding these malicious activities during a bilateral meeting at the G20 Summit held in September. During this encounter, he conveyed a stern and unequivocal message: "if you mess with America, we can do stuff to you." Following this direct presidential intervention, Obama noted a discernible cessation of further Russian hacking activities, stating with confidence, "We did not see further tampering of the election process."

Addressing the critical question of the impact of Russian interference on the ultimate election outcome, Obama assured the American public that the "voting process" itself remained uncompromised and was not tampered with in any manner that would cause legitimate concern. He clarified that his administration had not uncovered any evidence suggesting that voting machines had been compromised or manipulated. Furthermore, he pledged to release a comprehensive intelligence report detailing the findings of an extensive investigation into Russian interference prior to his departure from office, ensuring transparency and accountability.

Obama then proceeded to offer a stark and candid assessment of Russia's global standing and influence. He asserted with conviction, "The Russians can’t change us or significantly weaken us." He characterized Russia as a fundamentally "smaller country," a "weaker country," whose economic capabilities were largely confined to the production of "oil and gas and arms." He pointedly observed that Russia "doesn’t innovate" and that its economy does not generate products or services that are widely sought after on the global market. This assessment was particularly noteworthy and pointed, especially considering recent polling data that revealed a significant portion of Republican voters, specifically 37%, held a favorable view of Vladimir Putin. Obama expressed deep concern over this sentiment, suggesting that such an attitude would have profoundly dismayed former President Ronald Reagan, a staunch anti-communist leader.

Despite considerable public and media pressure to adopt a more alarmist tone regarding his successor and the implications of Russian interference, Obama largely maintained a measured and diplomatic demeanor. He described his initial meetings with President-elect Trump as "cordial" and indicated that he had offered "pretty specific suggestions" during these private discussions, aiming for a smooth transition. However, Obama also strategically utilized the press conference as a platform to articulate his frustrations with the media's extensive and, in his view, often misplaced coverage of the election. He sharply criticized the media's "obsession" with the leaked documents originating from Russian hackers, which were subsequently published by WikiLeaks. He characterized the media's relentless focus on Hillary Clinton's campaign as "troubling" and expressed his firm belief that Hillary Clinton "was not treated fairly during the election."

Obama further admonished reporters for their apparent surprise at the negative impact that the WikiLeaks dumps of Clinton's emails had on her campaign. He reminded them of their own extensive coverage of "every single leak about every little juicy tidbit of political gossip, including John Podesta’s risotto recipe," thereby highlighting the media's active role in amplifying the significance and impact of these leaks. This pointed critique underscored Obama's perspective that the media's editorial focus had been disproportionately skewed, contributing to a distorted public perception of the election's true dynamics and the overall fairness of the electoral process. His concluding remarks powerfully underscored the complex and interwoven nature of geopolitical concerns, domestic political strategies, and the critical role of media responsibility as he prepared to conclude his distinguished presidency.

### Obama Addresses Russian Election Interference and Threatens Retaliation

President Barack Obama, in his final scheduled news conference of 2016, addressed the escalating concerns surrounding Russian interference in the 2016 U.S. presidential election. He defended his administration's response to the suspected cyberattacks, emphasizing the need to prevent such actions from becoming a partisan issue. "Our goal continues to be to send a clear message to Russia, and others, to not do this," Obama stated, adding a veiled threat of reprisal: "Because we can do stuff to you."

### Direct Confrontation with Putin and Intelligence Assessments

Obama revealed that he directly confronted Russian President Vladimir Putin about the hacking attempts during a meeting in China in early September. "I felt that the most effective way to ensure that that didn't happen was to talk to him directly and tell him to cut it out and there were going to be serious consequences if he didn't," he explained. He noted that following this conversation, "we did not see further tampering of the election process." The President attributed the hacks to "the highest level" of the Russian government, suggesting that significant actions in Russia require Putin's approval. He clarified that neither he nor U.S. intelligence agencies initially focused on the motives behind the hacks, but rather on confirming their occurrence.

### Concerns Over Election Integrity and Partisan Divisiveness

Obama expressed his primary concern was safeguarding the integrity of the election process, particularly the vote count itself. He lamented the "hyperpartisan atmosphere," where any statement from his administration could be perceived through a partisan lens. "I wanted to make sure that everyone understood that we were trying to play this thing straight," he said. Despite urging that the hacks should not be a partisan issue, Obama pointed to recent polling showing an increase in Republicans' approval of Putin, remarking, "Ronald Reagan would roll over in his grave." He characterized Russia as a "weaker country" than the United States but cautioned that they could still exert influence by distracting America from its core values.

### Disagreement with President-Elect Trump and Intelligence Community

Obama's remarks came amidst a notable disagreement with his Republican successor, Donald Trump, regarding the extent of Russian interference. While the FBI confirmed it concurred with the CIA's assessment that Russia actively interfered to aid Trump's victory, Trump continued to reject these intelligence findings. He also questioned the timing of the Obama administration's accusations, asking why complaints were only voiced after Hillary Clinton's defeat. The Obama administration had formally accused the Russian government of hacking on October 7, a month prior to the election. In a prior interview with NPR, Obama affirmed that the U.S. would retaliate for the cyberattacks "at the time and place of our choosing."

### Obama on Election Fairness and the Electoral College

When questioned about whether the Russian hacks contributed to Hillary Clinton's loss, Obama declined to speculate but did criticize the media's framing of the presidential contest. "I don’t think she was treated fairly during the election," he stated, finding the coverage "troubling." Regarding the electoral college, Obama described it as "a vestige, or a carryover, from an earlier vision of how our federal government was going to work," stopping short of advocating for its abolition.

### Reflections on Syria and U.S. Foreign Policy

The President also offered candid reflections on the ongoing conflict in Syria, acknowledging the fall of Aleppo to government forces. He expressed that while he couldn't claim success, he believed the U.S. approach was correct. Obama detailed the difficult decisions made since 2011, noting that no option would have stopped the violence without jeopardizing U.S. security interests. He admitted that fully addressing the brutality would have required extensive U.S. military involvement, stating, "Unless we were all in and ready to take over Syria, we were going to have problems." Despite this, Obama accepted responsibility for the violence, saying, "I always feel responsible."

### Navigating a Shifting Global Landscape

Obama acknowledged that the incoming Trump administration might bring significant shifts in U.S. policy toward Syria and Russia, referencing Trump's comments about Assad and Russia's role in combating ISIS. While recognizing the value of a new president viewing foreign policy with "fresh eyes," Obama stressed the importance of a "systematic, deliberate" approach to alliances. He also commented on Trump's call with the Taiwanese president, which challenged the long-standing "One China" policy, advising that while not every past action must be adhered to, potential reactions must be carefully considered.

Throughout his Dec. 16 news conference, President Obama came back to Russia's suspected role in the 2016 election, how the U.S. became vulnerable to that country's influence and what should be focused on going forward. (Reuters) President Obama may hold additional availability events before leaving office on Jan. 20 and may deliver some sort of farewell address, but today's news conference offers him one of the last chances to lay out the case for what he's accomplished in office. Here's what he said: OBAMA: All right, everybody. Good afternoon. This is the most wonderful press conference of the year. I have got a list of who has been naughty and nice to call on. But let me first make a couple of quick points and then I will take your questions. Typically I use this year-end press conference to review how far we have come over the course of the year. Today, understandably, I'm going to talk a little bit about how far we have come over the past eight years. As I was preparing to take office, the unemployment rate was on its way to 10 percent. Today it is at 4.6 percent, the lowest in nearly a decade. We've seen the longest streak of job growth on record, and wages have grown faster over the past few years than at any time in the past 40. When I came into office, 44 million people were uninsured. Today we have covered more than 20 million of them. For the first time in our history, more than 90 percent of Americans are insured. In fact, yesterday was the biggest day ever for health care.gov, more than 670,000 Americans signed up to get covered, and more are signing up by the day. We've cut our dependence on foreign oil by more than half, doubled production of renewable energy, enacted the most sweeping reforms since FDR to protect consumers and prevent a crisis on Wall Street from punishing main street ever again. None of these actions stifled growth as critics are predicted. Instead, the stock market has nearly tripled. Since I signed Obamacare into law, our businesses have added more than 15 million new jobs, and the economy undoubtedly more durable than it was in the days when we relied on oil from unstable nations and banks took risky bets with your money. Add it all up, and last year the poverty rate fell at the fastest rate in almost 50 years, while the median household income grew at the fastest rate on record. In fact, income gains were actually larger for households at the bottom and the middle than for those at the top. And we have done all this while cutting our deficits by nearly two-thirds, and protecting vital investments that grow the middle class. In foreign policy, when I came into office we were in the midst of two wars. Now nearly 180,000 troops are down to 15,000. Bin Laden, rather than being at large, has been taken off the battlefield, along with thousands of other terrorists. Over the past eight years no foreign terrorist organization has successfully executed an attack on our homeland that was directed from overseas. Through diplomacy, we have ensured Iran cannot obtain a nuclear weapon without going to war with Iran. We opened up a new chapter with the people of Cuba. And we have brought nearly 200 nations together around a climate agreement that could very well save this planet for our kids. And almost every country on Earth sees America as stronger and more respected today than they did eight years ago. In other words, by so many measures our country is stronger and more prosperous than it was when we started. It is a situation that I'm proud to leave for my successor. And it's thanks to the American people, to the hard work that you have put in, the sacrifices you have made for your families and your communities, the businesses that you started or invested in, and the way you looked out for one another. And I could not be prouder to be your president. Of course, to tout this progress does not mean that we are not mindful of how much more there is to do. In this season in particular, we are reminded that there are people who are still hungry, people who are still homeless, people who still have trouble paying the bills or finding work after being laid off. There are communities that are still mourning those who have been stolen from us by senseless gun violence, and parents who still are wondering how to protect their kids. OBAMA: And after I leave office I intend to continue to work with organizations and citizens doing good across the country on these and other pressing issues to build on the progress that we have made. Around the world as well, there are hotspots where disputes have been intractable, conflicts have flared up, and people, innocent people are suffering as result, and nowhere is this more terribly true than in the city of Aleppo. For years, we've worked to stop the civil war in Syria and alleviate human suffering. It has been one of the hardest issues that I've faced as president. The world as we speak is united in horror at the savage assaults by the Syrian regime and its Russian and Iranian allies on the city of Aleppo. We have seen a deliberate strategy of surrounding, besieging and starving innocent civilians. We've seen relentless targeting of humanitarian workers and medical personnel, entire and neighbors reduced to rubble and dust. There are continuing reports of civilians being executed. These are all horrific violations of international law. Responsibility for this brutality lies in one place alone, with the Assad regime and its allies, Russia and Iran, and this blood and these atrocities are on their hands. We all know what needs to happen. There needs to be an impartial international observer force in Aleppo that can help coordinate an orderly evacuation through say corridors. There has to be full access for humanitarian aid, even as the United States continues to be the world's largest donor of humanitarian aid to the Syrian people. And beyond that, there needs to be a broader cease-fire that can serve as the basis for a political rather than a military solution. That's what the United States is gonna continue to push for, both with our partners and through multilateral institutions like the U.N. Regretfully, but unsurprisingly, Russia has repeatedly blocked the Security Council from taking action on these issues, so we're gonna keep pressing the Security Council to help improve the delivery of humanitarian aid to those who are in such desperate need and ensure accountability, including continuing to monitor any potential use of chemical weapons in Syria. And we're gonna work in the U.N. General Assembly as well, both on accountability and to advance a political settlement because it should be clear that although you may achieve tactical victories, over the long-term, the Assad regime cannot slaughter its way to legitimacy. That's why we'll continue to press for a transition to a more representative government, and that's why the world must not avert our eyes to the terrible events that are unfolding. The Syrian regime and its Russian and Iranian allies are trying to obfuscate the truth. The world should not be fooled and the world will not forget. So even in a season where the incredible blessings that we know as Americans are all around us, even as we enjoy family and friends and are reminded of how lucky we are, we should also be reminded that to be an American involves bearing burdens and meeting obligations to others. American values and American ideals are what will lead the way to a safer and more prosperous 2017, both here and abroad. And by the way, you (ph) embody those values and ideals like our brave men and women in uniform and their families. So I just want to close by wishing all of them a very merry Christmas and a happy new year. With that, I will take some questions, and I'm gonna start with Josh Lederman of A.P. QUESTION: Thank you, Mr. President. There's a perception that you're letting President Putin get away with interfering in the U.S. election and that a response that nobody knows about (inaudible) don't cut it. Are you prepared to call out President Putin by name for ordering (inaudible)? And do you agree with Hillary Clinton now says, that the hacking was actually partially responsible for her loss? And is your administration open to correlate with Trump and his team on this issues, tarnishing (ph) the smooth transition of power that you have promised? OBAMA: Well, first of all, with respect to the transition, I think they would be the first to acknowledge that we have done everything we can to make sure that they are successful, as I promised, and that will continue. And it's just been a few days since I last talked to the president-elect about a whole range of transition issues. That cooperation's gonna continue. OBAMA: There hasn't been a lot of squabbling. What we've simply said is the facts, which are that based on uniform intelligence assessments, the Russians were responsible for hacking the DNC and that as a consequence, it is important for us to review all elements of that and make sure that we are preventing that kind of interference through cyber attacks in the future. That should be a bipartisan issue, that shouldn't be a partisan issue. And my hope is that the president-elect is going to similarly be concerned with making sure that we don't have a potential foreign influence in our election process. I don't think any American wants that. And that shouldn't be a source of an argument. I think that part of the challenge is that it gets caught up in the carryover from election season. And I think it is very important for us to distinguish between the politics of the election and the need for us as a country, both from a national security perspective but also in terms of the integrity of our election system and our democracy to make sure that we don't create a political football here. Now, with respect to how this thing unfolded last year, let's just go through the facts pretty quickly. At the beginning of the summer, were alerted to the possibility that the DNC has been hacked. And I made (ph) an order, law enforcement, as well as our intelligence teams to find out everything about it, investigate it thoroughly to brief the potential victims of this hacking, to brief on a bipartisan basis the leaders of both the House and the Senate and the relevant intelligence committees. And once we had clarity and certainty around what in fact had happened, we publicly announced that in fact Russia had hacked into the DNC. And at that time, we did not attribute motives or you know any interpretations of why they had done so. We didn't discuss what the effects of it might be. We simply let people know -- the public know just as we had let members of Congress know that this had happened. And as a consequence, all of you wrote a lot of stories about both what had happened and then you interpreted why that might have happened and what effect it was going to have on the election outcomes. We did not -- and the reason we did not was because in this hyper-partisan atmosphere, at a time when my primary concern was making sure that the integrity of the election process was not in any way damaged, at a time when anything that was said by me or anybody in the White House would immediately be seen through a partisan lens. I wanted to make sure that everybody understood we were playing this thing straight, that we weren't trying to advantage one side or another. But what we were trying to do was let people know that this had taken place. And so if you started seeing effects on the election, if you were trying to measure why this was happening and how you should consume the information that was being leaked, that you might want to take this into account. And that's exactly how we should have handled it. Imagine if we had done the opposite, it would become immediately just one more political scrum. And part of the goal here was to make sure that we did not do the work of the leakers for them by raising more and more questions about the integrity of the election right before the election was taking place -- at a time, by the way, when the president-elect himself was raising questions about the integrity of the election. And finally, I think it's worth pointing out, that the information was already out. It was in the hands of Wikileaks, so that was going to come out no matter what. What I was concerned about in particular was making sure that that wasn't compounded by potential hacking that could hamper vote counting, affect the actual election process itself. And so in early September when I saw President Putin in China, I felt that the most effective way to ensure that that did not happen was to talk to him directly and tell him to cut it out, there were going to be some serious consequences if he did not. And in fact, we did not see further tampering of the election process. But the leaks through Wikileaks had already occurred. So when I look back in terms of how we handled it, I think we handled it the way it should have been handled. We allowed law enforcement and the intelligence community to do its job without political influence. We briefed all relevant parties involved in terms of what was taking place. When we had a consensus around what had happened, we announced it, not through the White House, not through me, but rather through the intelligence communities that had actually carried out these investigations. And then we allowed you and the American public to make an assessment as to how to weigh that going into the election. And the truth is, is that there was nobody here who did not have some sense of what kind of effect it might have. I am finding it a little curious that everybody is suddenly acting surprised that this looked like it was disadvantaging Hillary Clinton, because you guys wrote about it every day, every single leak about every little juicy tidbit of political gossip, including John Podesta's risotto recipe. This was an obsession that dominated the news coverage. So I do think it is worth us reflecting how it is that a presidential election of such importance, of such moment, with so many big issues at stake and such a contrast between the candidates came to be dominated by a bunch of these leaks. What is it about our political system that made us vulnerable to these kinds of potential manipulations which, as I've said publicly before, were not particularly sophisticated. This was not some elaborate, complicated espionage scheme. They hacked into some Democratic Party e-mails that contained pretty routine stuff, some of it embarrassing or uncomfortable because I suspect that if any of us got our emails hacked into there might be some things that we would not want suddenly appearing on the front page of a newspaper or a telecast, even if there was not anything particularly illegal or controversial about it. And then it just took off. And that concerns me, and it should concern all of us. But the truth of the matter is, is that everybody had the information. It was out there, and we handled it the way we should have. Now, moving forward, I think there are a couple of issues that this raises. Number one is just the constant challenge that we are going to have with cyber security throughout our economy and throughout our society. We are a digitalized culture. And there's hacking going on every single day. There is not a company, there is not a major organization, there is not a financial institution, there is not a branch of our government were somebody is not going to be fishing for something or trying to penetrate or put in a virus or malware. And this is why for the last eight years I have been obsessed with how do we continually upgrade our cyber security systems. And this particular concern around Russian hacking is part of a broader set of concerns about how do we deal with cyber issues being used in ways that can affect our infrastructure, affect the stability of our financial systems, and affect the integrity of our institutions like our election process. I just received, a couple of weeks back, it wasn't widely reported on, a report from our cyber-security commission that outlines a whole range of strategies to do a better job on this. But it's difficult because it's not all housed -- the target of cyberattacks is not one entity, but it's widely dispersed and a lot of it is private, like the DNC. You know, it's not a branch of government. We can't tell people what to do. What we can do is inform them, get best practices. What we can also do is to on a bilateral basis warn other countries against these kinds of attacks, and we've done that in the past. So just as I told Russia to stop it and indicated there will be consequences when they do it, the Chinese have in the past engaged in cyberattacks directed at our companies to steal trade secrets and proprietary technology, and I had to have the same conversation with President Xi. And what we've seen is some evidence that they have reduced but not completely eliminated these activities, partly because they can use cutouts. One of the problems with the internet and cyber issues is there's not always a return address, and by the time you catch up to it, you know, attributing what happened to a particular government can be difficult, not always provable in court, even tough our intelligence communities can make an assessment. What we've also tried to do is to start creating some international norms about this to prevent some sort of cyber arms race because we obviously have offensive capabilities as well as defensive capabilities, and my approach is not a situation which everybody's worse off because folks are constantly attacking each other back and forth, but putting some guardrails around behavior of nation states, including our adversaries, just so that they understand that whatever they do to us, we can potentially do to them. We do have some special challenges because oftentimes, our economy is more digitalized. It is more vulnerable partly because we're a wealthier nation and we're more wired than some of these other countries and we have a more open society and engage in less control and censorship over what happens over the internet, which is also part of what makes us special. Last point, and the reason I'm going on here is because I know that you guys have a lot of questions about this and I addressed all of you directly about this. With respect to response, my principal goal leading up to the election was making sure that the election itself went off without a hitch, that it was not tarnished and that it did not feed any sense in the public that somehow, tampering had taken place with the actual process of voting and we accomplished that. That does not mean that we are not going to respond, it simply meant that we had a set of priorities leading up to the election that were of the utmost importance. Our goal continues to be to send a clear message to Russia or others not to do this to us because we can do stuff to you, but it is also important for us to do that in a thoughtful, methodical way. Some of it, we do publicly. Some of it, we will do in a way that they know but not everybody will. And I know that there have been folks out there who suggests somehow that if we went out there and made big announcements and thumped our chests about a bunch of stuff, that somehow that would potentially spook the Russians. But keep in mind that we already have enormous numbers of sanctions against the Russians. The relationship between us and Russia has deteriorated, sadly, significantly over the last several years. And so how we approach an appropriate response that increases costs for them for behavior like this in the future but does not create problems for us is something that's worth taking the time to think through and figure out. And that's exactly what we've done. So, at a point in time where we've taken certain actions that we can divulge publicly, we will do so. There are times where the message will be directly received by the Russians and not publicized. And I should point out by the way, part of why the Russians have been effective on this is because they don't go around announcing what they're doing. It's not like Putin's gone around the world publicly saying, look what we did. Wasn't that clever? He denies it. So the idea that somehow public shaming is gonna be effective, I think doesn't read the -- the thought process in Russia very well. OK. QUESTION: Did Clinton lose because of the hacking? OBAMA: I'm gonna let all the political pundits in this town have a long discussion about what happened in the election. It was a fascinating election. So, you know, I'm sure there are gonna be a lot of books written about it. I've said what I think is important for the Democratic Party going forward, rather than try to parse every aspect of the election. And I -- I've said before, I couldn't be prouder of Secretary Clinton, her outstanding service and she's worked tirelessly on behalf of the American people and I don't think she was treated fairly during the election. I think the coverage of her and the issues was troubling, but having said that, what I've been most focused on -- appropriate for the fact I am not going to be a politician in about -- what is it, 32 days, 31, 34? (LAUGHTER) What I've said is that I can maybe give some counsel advice to the Democratic Party. And I think the -- the -- the thing we have to spend the most time on -- because it's the thing we have most control over -- is, how do we make sure that we're showing up in places where I think Democratic policies are needed, where they are helping, where they are making a difference, but where people feel as if they're not being heard? And where Democrats are characterized as coastal, liberal, latte- sipping, you know, politically correct, out-of-touch folks, we have to be in those communities. And I've seen that, when we are in those communities, it makes a difference. That's how I became president. I became a U.S. Senator not just because I had a strong base in Chicago, but because I was driving downstate Illinois and going to fish fries and sitting in V.F.W. Halls and talking to farmers. And I didn't win every one of their votes, but they got a sense of what I was talking about, what I cared about, that I was for working people, that I was for the middle class, that the reason I was interested in strengthening unions and raising the minimum wage and rebuilding our infrastructure and making sure that parents had decent childcare and family leave, was because my own family's history wasn't that different from theirs even if I looked a little bit different. Same thing in Iowa. And so the question is, how do we rebuild that party as a whole, so that there's not a county in any state -- I don't care how red -- where we don't have a presence and we're not making the argument, because I think we have a better argument. But that requires a lot of work. You know, it's been something that I've been able to do successfully in my own campaigns. OBAMA: It is not something I've been able to transfer to candidates in mid-terms and sort of build a sustaining organization around. That's something I would have liked to have done more of, but it's kind of hard to do when you're also dealing with a whole bunch of issues here in the White House. And that doesn't mean, though, that it can't be done, and I think there are gonna be a lot of talented folks out there, a lot of progressives who share my values, who are gonna be leading the charge in the years to come. Michelle Kosinski (ph) of CNN. QUESTION: Thank you. This week we heard Hillary Clinton talk about how she thinks that the FBI director's most recent announcement made a difference in the outcome of the election. And we also just heard in an op-ed her campaign chairman talk about something being deeply broken within the FBI. He talked about thinking that the investigation early on was lackadaisical, in his words. So what do you think about those comments? Do you think there's any truth to them? Do you think there's a danger there that they're calling into question the integrity of institutions in a similar way that Donald Trump's team has done? And the second part to that is that Donald Trump's team repeatedly -- I guess, given the indication that the investigation of the Russian hack as well as retaliation might not be such a priority once he's in office. So what do you think the risk is there? And are you going to talk to him directly about some of those comments he made? OBAMA: Well, on the latter point, as I said before, the transition from election season to governance season is not always smooth. You know, it's bumpy. There are still feelings that are raw out there. There are people who are still thinking how things unfolded. And I get all that. But when Donald Trump takes the oath of office and is sworn in as the 45th president of the United States, then he has got a different set of responsibilities and considerations. And I've said this before. I think there is a sobering process when you walk into the Oval Office. And, you know, I haven't shared previously private conversations I've had with the president-elect. I will say that they have been cordial and in some cases have involved me making some pretty specific suggestions about how to ensure that regardless of our obvious deep disagreements about policy, maybe I can transmit some thoughts about maintaining the effectiveness, integrity, cohesion of the office, our various democratic institutions, and he has listened. I can't say that he will end up implementing, but the conversations themselves have been cordial as opposed to defensive in any way. And I will always make myself available to him just as previous presidents have made themselves available to me as issues come up. With respect to the FBI, I will tell you, I've had a chance to know a lot of FBI agents. I know Director Comey. They take their job seriously. They work really hard. They help keep us safe and save a lot of lives. And it is always a challenge for law enforcement when there's an intersection between the work that they are doing and the political system. It's one of the difficulties of democracy generally. We have a system where we want our law enforcement investigators and our prosecutors to be free from politics, to be independent, to play it straight. But sometimes that involves investigations that touch on politics and particularly in this hyper-partisan environment that we've been in, everything is suspect, everything you do one way or the other. One thing that I have done is to be pretty scrupulous about not wading into investigation decisions or prosecution decisions or decisions not to prosecute. I have tried to be really strict in my own behavior about preserving the independence of law enforcement, free from my own judgments and political assessments, in some cases. And I don't know why it would stop now. Mike Dorney (ph) of Bloomberg. QUESTION: Thank you, Mr. President. On Aleppo, your views of (ph) what happens there, the responsibility of the Russian government, the Iranian government, the Assad regime (inaudible), but do you, as president of the United States, leader of the free world, feel any personal moral responsibility now at the end of your presidency for the carnage we're all watching in Aleppo, which I'm sure disturbs you (inaudible)? Secondly, also on Aleppo, you've again made clear your practical disagreements with (inaudible) and President-elect Trump has throughout his campaign, and he said again last night, that he wants to create safe zones in Syria. Do you feel like in this transition, you need to help him toward implementing that or is that not something you need to be doing? OBAMA: Mike, I always feel responsible. I felt responsible when kids were being shot by snipers. I felt responsible when millions of people had been displaced. I feel responsible for murder and slaughter that's taken place in South Sudan that's not being reported on, partly because there's not as much social media being generated from there. There are places around the world where horrible things are happening and because of my office, because I'm president of the United States, I feel responsible. I ask myself every single day, is there something I could do that would save lives and make a difference and spare some child who doesn't deserve to suffer. So that's a starting point. There's not a moment during the course of this presidency where I haven't felt some responsibility. That's true, by the way, for our own country. When I came into office and people were losing their jobs and losing their homes and losing their pensions, I felt responsible and I would go home at night and I would ask myself, was there something better that I could do or smarter that I could be that would make a difference in their lives, that would relieve their suffering and relieve their hardship. So with respect to Syria, what I have consistently done is taken the best course that I can to try to end the civil war while having also to take into account the long-term national security interests of the United States. And throughout this process, based on hours of meetings -- if you tallied it up, days and weeks of meetings -- where we went through every option in painful detail with maps and we had our military and we had our aid agencies and we had our diplomatic teams, and sometimes, we'd bring in outsiders who were critics of ours. OBAMA: Whenever we went through it, the challenge was that short of putting large numbers of U.S. troops on the ground uninvited, without any international law mandate, without sufficient support from Congress, at a time when we still had troops in Afghanistan and we still had troops in Iraq and we had just gone through over a decade of war and spent trillions of dollars and when the opposition on the ground was not cohesive enough to necessarily govern a country and you had a military superpower in Russia prepared to do whatever it took to keep its (inaudible) involved and you had a regional military power in Iran that saw their own vital strategic interests at stake and were willing to maintain that territory in the absence of consent from the Syrian government and now the Russians or the Iranians. So it may be that with Aleppo's tragic situation unfolding that in the short term, if we can get more of the tens of thousands who are still trapped there out , that so long as the world's eyes are on them and they are feeling pressure, the regime in Russia concludes that they are willing to find some arrangement, perhaps in coordination with Turkey, whereby those people can be safe. Even that will probably be temporary, but at least it solves a short-term issue that's going to arise. Unfortunately we are not there yet because right now we have Russians and Assad claiming that basically all the innocent civilians who were trapped in Aleppo are out when international organizations, humanitarian organizations who know better and who are on the ground, have said unequivocally that there are still tens of thousands who are trapped and prepared to leave under pretty much any conditions. And so right now our biggest priority is to continue to put pressure wherever we can to try to get them out. (CROSSTALK) OBAMA: Mike, I can't have too much... QUESTION: (OFF-MIKE) but do you see, responsibility notwithstanding, moving in that direction or help President-elect Trump move in that direction? OBAMA: I will help President Trump -- President-elect Trump with any advice, counsel, information that we can provide so that he, once he's sworn in, can make a decision. Between now and then, these are decisions that I have to make based on the consultations that I have with our military and the people who have been working this every single day. Peter Alexander (ph). QUESTION: Mr. President, thank you very much. Can you, given all the intelligence that we have now heard, assure the public this was once and for all a free and fair election? And specifically on Russia, do you feel any obligation now as they have been insisting that this isn't the case to show the proof, as it were? They say, put your money where your mouth is and declassify some of the intelligence and the evidence that exists. And more broadly, as it relates to Donald Trump on this very topic, are you concerned about his relationship with Vladimir Putin, especially given some of the recent cabinet picks, including his selection for secretary of state, Rex Tillerson, who toasted Putin with champagne over oil deals together? Thank you. OBAMA: I may be getting older because these multipart questions, I start losing track. (LAUGHTER) OBAMA: I can assure the public that there was not the kind of tampering with the voting process that was our concern and will continue to be of concern going forward, that the votes that were cast were counted, they were counted appropriately. We have not seen evidence of machines being tampered with, so that assurance I can provide. That doesn't mean that we find every single, you know, potential probe of every single voting machine all across the country, but we paid a lot of attention to it. We worked with state officials, et cetera, and we feel confident that that didn't occur and that the votes were cast and they were counted. And so that's on that point. What was the second one? QUESTION: Say more about declassification. OBAMA: Declassification. Look, we will provide evidence that we can safely provide, that doesn't mean, though, that it can't be done, and I think there are going to be a lot of talented folks out there, a lot of progressives who share my values who are going to be leading the charge in the years to come. Michelle Kosinski (ph) of CNN. QUESTION: Thank you. This week we heard Hillary Clinton talk about how she thinks that the FBI director's most recent announcement made a difference in the outcome of the election. And we also just heard in an op-ed her campaign chairman talk about something being deeply broken within the FBI. He talked about thinking that the investigation early on was lackadaisical, in his words. So what do you think about those comments? Do you think there's any truth to them? Do you think there's a danger there that they're calling into question the integrity of institutions in a similar way that Donald Trump's team has done? And the second part to that is that Donald Trump's team repeatedly -- I guess, given the indication that the investigation of the Russian hack as well as retaliation might not be such a priority once he's in office. So what do you think the risk is there? And are you going to talk to him directly about some of those comments he made? OBAMA: Well, on the latter point, as I said before, the transition from election season to governance season is not always smooth. You know, it's bumpy. There are still feelings that are raw out there. There are people who are still thinking how things unfolded. And I get all that. But when Donald Trump takes the oath of office and is sworn in as the 45th president of the United States, then he has got a different set of responsibilities and considerations.
