On Tuesday night, one of the reptiles grabbed a 2-year-old Nebraska boy playing at the water's edge at the Grand Floridian Resort & Spa. The boy's body was found Wednesday, said Orange County Sheriff Jerry Demings, adding he was sure the cause of death was drowning. Other tourists reported alarming experiences with Disney alligators, especially at the Seven Seas Lagoon, a man-made lake between Disney's luxury hotels and the Magic Kingdom. San Diego attorney David Hiden told the Orlando Sentinel on Wednesday that last year he whisked his son to safety at Disney's Coronado Springs after a gator approached the boy playing in calf-deep water. Then Hiden saw a second gator nearby. Hiden said a hotel manager called one of them a "resident pet" and seemed unconcerned. Red Huber / Orlando Sentinel In the shadow of the Magic Kingdom Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Officers search for a young boy Wednesday, June 15, 2016 after the boy was grabbed Tuesday night by an alligator at Grand Floridian Resort at Disney World. (Red Huber/Staff Photographer) In the shadow of the Magic Kingdom Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Officers search for a young boy Wednesday, June 15, 2016 after the boy was grabbed Tuesday night by an alligator at Grand Floridian Resort at Disney World. (Red Huber/Staff Photographer) (Red Huber / Orlando Sentinel) "If I hadn't gone down there in another two seconds, my kid would have been killed," Hiden said. Alfred Smith of Charleston, S.C., said he alerted a Grand Floridian employee Tuesday night after seeing a gator in the lagoon. He thinks it's the same one that attacked the boy less an hour later. "I did warn another family of three that had small kids too close to the water and they along with another family took their children and left," Smith said via email. A British couple told the Mirror newspaper that in April, a gator "lurched" out of the Seven Seas Lagoon in front of them at Polynesian Village Resort. Current and former Disney employees say the resort does its best to manage the alligator population, but that's not always easy when so much wilderness lies nearby. Disney World consists of more than 40 square miles, much of it undeveloped land. "They're surrounded by quintessential alligator habitat," said conservationist and television personality Jeff Corwin, who hosts "Ocean Mysteries" on ABC, owned by The Walt Disney Co. "The entire property is interconnected via canals so it is difficult to keep them out of the lakes," former Disney executive Duncan Dickson said in an email. "Gators are on all of the golf courses. The team attempts to relocate the gators to the uninhabited natural areas as best they can, but the gators don't understand the boundaries." Disney said it works closely with the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission on a comprehensive approach to alligator management, including reducing the potential for interaction with humans. Disney said its employees call in sightings from guests, and technicians from the pest management team are dispatched. Technicians encourage gators back into the water when possible and determine if they need to be caught. Disney relocates alligators considered a nuisance, meaning they continually show up on the banks of waterways sunning themselves, don't fear humans or have done damage. Disney can catch and release alligators 4 feet or smaller. The state handles larger ones. "They have people constantly monitoring" the area on the lookout for gators, Corwin said. One employee at the Disney property who did not want to be identified said in an email "there is such a problem on property with guests feeding the alligators thinking it's cool." Visitors at Buena Vista Palace regularly feed two of them from the balconies, he said. In 1986, an alligator bit a boy at a pond at Disney's Fort Wilderness campground. The boy suffered knee and thigh injuries. The parents later sued the resort, alleging Disney failed to warn visitors. Alligators have been spotted in Magic Kingdom. Robert Niles, editor of ThemeParkInsider.com and a former Disney World raft operator, remembers having to delay the opening of Tom Sawyer Island one morning because a family of gators was parked on the dock. Floridians know that alligators lurk in many places, he said. but "if you're living up north there's just no thought" about any danger. "They really do need to be explicitly warned about it," he said. He expects Disney and other tourist spots with water will do more to notify visitors, he said, because "just saying no swimming really doesn't cut it." spedicini@orlandosentinel.com or 407-420-5240

query: What specific criticisms did Matt Lauer face for his moderation during the NBC News forum with Hillary Clinton and Donald Trump?

documents.text: It’s tough to be a moderator during a high-stakes presidential election. One misstep, and accusations of bias are bound to immediately rain down on your head. But even assuming that critics will be quick to cry foul, the early reviews of Matt Lauer’s performance on Wednesday night were brutal. The Today Show host was almost instantly pilloried for failing to fact-check the candidates and for posing superficial questions to both Hillary Clinton and Donald Trump during the NBC News forum. Clinton got tougher questions, but also got visibly irritated and defensive. Trump got mostly softballs. But he rode over them. — Maggie Haberman (@maggieNYT) September 8, 2016 Matt Lauer is clearly more comfortable asking Rita Wilson about her favorite healthy summer recipes — Bobby Big Wheel (@BobbyBigWheel) September 8, 2016 One thing we can all agree on is that Matt Lauer is terrible. Bad format, I know, but still terrible — John Bresnahan (@BresPolitico) September 8, 2016 Could we get questions of true importance regarding future of country's engagement w world. Matt Lauer is Today show host, wrong for this-- — Katrina vandenHeuvel (@KatrinaNation) September 8, 2016 Others noted that despite extensive questions about the FBI investigation into her private email server, Clinton got away without follow-up questions after promising to never send ground troops to Iraq "ever again": Lauer getting rightly whacked for Trump interview, but Clinton half also lame: Follow up on email but not Syria? Not ground troops in Iraq? — Alex Burns (@alexburnsNYT) September 8, 2016 But the most ferocious complaints against Lauer came from Clinton supporters and liberals who were amazed that Lauer failed to contradict Trump when the Republican candidate brazenly lied about opposing the Iraq War. (Trump supported the war, and has simply and persistently lied about his record throughout the campaign.) Lauer follows up by completely ignoring Trump lying about opposing the Iraq war from the beginning — Judd Legum (@JuddLegum) September 8, 2016 US media's failure to hold Trump to account in 2016 now almost on a par with US media's failure to hold Bush to account on Iraq/WMDs in 2003 — Mehdi Hasan (@mehdirhasan) September 8, 2016 TRUMP ADVOCATED FOR QUICK WITHDRAWAL OUT OF IRAQ. MATT LAUER, PLEASE POINT THIS OUT. — igorvolsky (@igorvolsky) September 8, 2016 Sure Trump is lying about the Iraq War up, but at least he didn't use a private server. Eh, Lauer? — Jamelle Bouie (@jbouie) September 8, 2016 Two of Trump's biggest critiques of Clinton are Iraq and Libya, both of which he supported. — Christopher Hayes (@chrislhayes) September 8, 2016 Seriously -- everyone, and I mean everyone, knew this would happen. And Matt Lauer didn't have a followup planned? https://t.co/mm5ufZKjEx — Paul Krugman (@paulkrugman) September 8, 2016 You can’t just let Trump tell obvious, widely-debunked lies in interviews https://t.co/I1bnTbMN3i — Matthew Yglesias (@mattyglesias) September 8, 2016 Trump lies about opposing Iraq war. Lauer lets it go. No follow up. Unreal. — Jonathan Chait (@jonathanchait) September 8, 2016 OK great @Mlauer call, quoting a Trump tweet about how rape is the natural consequence of having women in the military. No followup — Joan Walsh (@joanwalsh) September 8, 2016 As Vox’s Matt Yglesias explains, the criticisms reflect a real frustration over Trump’s ability to run roughshod over facts and his own record. What’s even more fascinating is NBC News’ own team of political reporters were on hand to do a live fact-check of the event. Fact-checking Donald Trump on his support for the Iraq War https://t.co/G8RcufJtgv #NBCNewsForum pic.twitter.com/niZvVM0ftp — NBC News (@NBCNews) September 8, 2016 Lauer’s review shows just how difficult of a job lies ahead for the moderator of the first presidential debate on September 26. Watch: Trump tells Lauer he was against the war in Iraq

The first national security debate between Hillary Clinton and Donald Trump was a striking demonstration of their significant candidate deficiencies, rather than a clear articulation of their commander-in-chief strategies. Both contenders revealed considerable vulnerabilities, failing to present a robust vision for U.S. defense in a complex global landscape. Instead of outlining concrete plans, the debate devolved into a display of mutual criticism, highlighting a missed opportunity for substantive discussion on critical national security issues.

Clinton adopted a defensive, lawyerly approach, often getting bogged down in technicalities and evading crucial details. At times, her responses contained demonstrably false information. She frequently pivoted to Trump's perceived missteps, a tactic that, while factually grounded, deviated from the agreed-upon focus on policy. Her explanations regarding secure communication protocols overseas, for instance, became mired in unnecessary jargon. When pressed by a veteran about her handling of classified information, she maintained, "I did exactly what I should have done and I take it very seriously, always have, always will." Her pledge to defeat ISIS, relying solely on "air power" and increased support for "Arabs and Kurds who will fight on the ground," lacked specificity and a comprehensive strategy.

Trump, conversely, embodied the persona of a demagogue, offering simplistic explanations for complex geopolitical phenomena. His stance on the Iraq War was inconsistent; despite claiming opposition from its inception, his reasoning was often convoluted. He attributed the rise of ISIS to the U.S. withdrawal from Iraq under President Obama, stating, "And really, ISIS was formed. This was a terrible decision. And frankly, we never even got a shot." He further suggested a controversial strategy: "I’ve always said, shouldn’t be there, but if we’re going to get out, take the oil. If we would have taken the oil, you wouldn’t have ISIS." This statement, among others, underscored a lack of nuanced understanding of the region's dynamics.

Both candidates committed significant factual inaccuracies, though Trump exhibited a far greater propensity for falsehoods. Clinton’s assertion that there would "never" be ground troops in Iraq and Syria again directly contradicted the presence of approximately 6,000 U.S. troops in Iraq and the deployment of at least 500 Special Forces in Syria. Trump, however, was a prolific purveyor of misinformation. He erroneously claimed the absence of a military judiciary system for prosecuting rape, overlooking Article 120 of the Uniformed Code of Military Justice, which explicitly criminalizes rape. He also disputed a veteran's statistic on daily veteran suicides, citing an outdated figure. Furthermore, his claim that ISIS's primary revenue source was stolen oil overlooked its diverse income streams, including bank heists and taxation of occupied populations.

Trump also introduced several perplexing and controversial propositions. Most notably, he suggested that sexual assault in the military was partly attributable to gender integration, rather than the actions of perpetrators. This statement was widely interpreted as an insult to women serving in combat roles. He defended this assertion by stating, "There are many people that think that that’s absolutely correct." Trump also welcomed a purported compliment from Vladimir Putin, despite uncertainty about its veracity, and claimed U.S. military leadership had been "reduced to rubble." He drew a parallel between President Obama's actions and Putin's, stating, "When he calls me brilliant, I’ll take the compliment, OK?" He further indicated a willingness to purge generals and admirals who disagreed with his foreign policy, a notion likely to alarm the Pentagon, where commanders are expected to provide objective counsel irrespective of political considerations. Trump's juxtaposition of praise for Putin with criticism of U.S. military leaders was particularly ill-timed, given the ongoing Russian military campaign in Syria. He also characterized Clinton's foreign policy as having a "happy trigger" and asserted a strong relationship with Mexico, despite his visit there being met with significant opposition. Additionally, he suggested potential amnesty for undocumented immigrants serving in the military, a stance incongruent with his campaign's prior declarations.

The televised forum, moderated by Matt Lauer, represented a significant missed opportunity for a campaign that has seldom engaged in substantive discussions about the future of national security or the requisite military capabilities for U.S. defense. Critical questions regarding the defeat of ISIS, enhanced veteran support, and paramount national security threats remained largely unanswered. The compressed, 30-minute format, characterized by rapid-fire questioning, exacerbated these shortcomings. Clinton, compelled to address past policy decisions, appeared rushed and defensive. Trump’s responses, often a jumble of unsubstantiated assertions, lacked depth and coherence. The evening concluded as a profound disappointment, offering voters a stark contrast between two deeply flawed presidential candidates, rather than a clear path forward for American security.

## Contrasting National Security Visions Emerge at Commander-in-Chief Forum

At the September 7th "Commander-in-Chief Forum" on NBC News, Democratic nominee Hillary Clinton and Republican opponent Donald Trump presented starkly contrasting visions for national security and foreign policy. The event, designed to scrutinize their leadership, highlighted Trump's admiration for Russian President Vladimir Putin and his proposed military buildup, while Clinton emphasized her experience and steady approach.

### Trump's "Strong Leader" Admiration and Military Expansion

Donald Trump defended his praise for Russian President Vladimir Putin, suggesting the Russian leader demonstrated superior leadership compared to President Obama. "Certainly, in that system, he’s been a leader, far more than our president has been a leader," Trump stated, attributing this to perceived U.S. division. He posited a U.S.-Russia alliance could be key to defeating ISIS. When questioned about Putin's international actions, Trump deflected, asking, "Do you want me to start naming some of the things Obama does at the same time?" He also acknowledged appreciating Putin's positive remarks, saying, "Well, I think when he calls me brilliant, I think I’ll take the compliment, okay?" Earlier that day, Trump reiterated his desire for a "good relationship" with Putin during an MSNBC town hall.

Prior to the forum, Trump outlined a plan in Philadelphia to significantly expand U.S. military capabilities and eliminate defense spending sequestration. "As soon as I take office, I will ask Congress to fully eliminate the defense sequester and will submit a new budget to rebuild our military. It is so depleted. We will rebuild our military," Trump declared. He stressed this would provide funding certainty and allow for future strategic planning. This address was Trump's most detailed national security proposal, aiming to bolster his policy image.

Trump also addressed a past tweet about military sexual assaults, questioning, "What did these geniuses expect when they put men & women together?" He defended the tweet, saying, "Well, it is, it is a correct tweet. There are many people that think that that’s absolutely correct." He expressed surprise that Obama and other officials hadn't followed expert recommendations in intelligence briefings, noting, "I could tell, they were not happy. Our leaders did not follow what they were recommending." Trump also stated he wasn't a proponent of the Russian system but anticipated strong relations with Putin, predicting, "I think I would have a very, very good relationship with Putin, and I think I would have a very, very good relationship with Russia." He denied any mutual admiration would benefit Russia, asserting, "It’s not going to get him anything. I’m a negotiator."

Trump accused Clinton of having a "happy trigger," implying a readiness for conflict. He argued seizing Iraq's oil would have prevented ISIS's rise, stating, "If we would have taken the oil, you wouldn’t have ISIS, because ISIS formed with the power and the wealth of that oil."

### Clinton's "Rock Steadiness" and Accountability

Hillary Clinton positioned herself as a figure of "absolute rock steadiness" in foreign policy. She navigated questions about her private email server and her Iraq War vote, while Trump made several controversial statements. Clinton criticized Trump's proposed Muslim ban as counterproductive to defeating ISIS.

Clinton acknowledged her 2002 Senate vote to authorize the Iraq War as a mistake, but emphasized her learning from it. She criticized Trump for claiming to have opposed the war from the start, stating, "I have taken responsibility for my decision. He refuses to take responsible for his support." Clinton reiterated her belief that the war was a mistake and her vote for war authority was an error. "I think that the decision to go to war in Iraq was a mistake, and I have said that my voting to give President Bush that authority was, from my perspective, my mistake," Clinton asserted. "My opponent was for the war in Iraq. He says he wasn’t; you can go back and look at the record. He supported it." She pledged no U.S. ground troops in Iraq if elected.

Clinton defended her email server practices, insisting no documents were marked classified. "Classified material has a header that says ‘top secret,’ ‘secret,’ ‘confidential.’ None of the emails sent or received by me had such a header," she responded. When a veteran noted military personnel would be prosecuted for similar actions, Clinton countered, "I communicated about classified material on a wholly separate system. I took it very seriously." Clinton, who also supports ending sequestration, focused on defending her email server practices, asserting responsible handling of classified information.

### Forum Dynamics and Voter Sentiment

The forum, hosted by Matt Lauer at the Intrepid Sea, Air & Space Museum in New York, featured candidates appearing separately before an audience of military personnel and veterans. Organized by the Iraq and Afghanistan Veterans of America, it preceded the presidential debates. Lauer had to reiterate a request for candidates to refrain from personal attacks.

A poll released that Wednesday showed Trump leading among active-duty personnel and veterans, 55 percent to Clinton's 36 percent. This demographic alignment with Trump's base mirrored past election trends. The lead-up to the forum saw intense campaigning over military leadership. Clinton called Trump "unfit to be our Commander-in-Chief." Trump announced an endorsement from 88 retired military officials, while Clinton released a list of 95 retired generals and admirals, a number her campaign claimed was higher than for any recent nonincumbent Democrat.

The Commander-in-Chief Forum, a pivotal event where Donald Trump and Hillary Clinton shared a stage, offered a stark contrast in their approaches to national security and foreign policy. This NBC News and MSNBC forum, serving as a critical preview to the presidential debates, highlighted Trump's controversial praise for Vladimir Putin over American military leadership, which he deemed "embarrassing." Clinton, while appearing more prepared, found herself defending her handling of classified information and her support for the Libyan intervention under the Obama administration.

A significant portion of the forum focused on Trump's pronouncements. He declined to reveal a specific strategy for defeating ISIS, characterized the U.S. military as "reduced to rubble," and asserted that Putin was a "far more" capable leader than President Obama. Trump also initially defended, then retracted, a controversial tweet regarding women in the military, and articulated a nuanced position on undocumented immigrants serving in the armed forces.

When questioned about his assertion of possessing superior knowledge of ISIS compared to American generals, Trump indicated a willingness to replace high-ranking officers based on recent performance before finalizing any strategic decisions. "Well, they'll probably be different generals, to be honest with you," Trump stated, implying a critical assessment of current leadership. He attributed the perceived weakness in military command to the current administration, suggesting that historical figures like General George Patton would be dismayed. "Under the leadership of Barack Obama and Hillary Clinton the generals have been reduced to rubble," Trump declared, adding, "They’ve been reduced to a point where it’s embarrassing to our country."

Trump's admiration for Putin was also a prominent theme. He defended Putin against criticism regarding the annexation of Crimea and support for Syrian dictator Bashar al-Assad. "Do you want me to start naming some of the things President Obama does at the same time?" Trump retorted. While claiming he wouldn't be swayed by Putin's positive remarks about him, Trump indicated a reciprocal personal relationship. "If he says great things about me, I’m going to say great things about him," he affirmed. Trump cited Putin's high approval rating and predicted a strong rapport, favorably comparing Putin's leadership to President Obama's. "The man has very strong control over a country," Trump observed of Putin. "It’s a very different system and I don’t happen to like the system, but certainly, in that system, he’s been a leader, far more than our president has been a leader."

Trump reiterated past claims about his evolving stance on the Iraq War, the withdrawal from Iraq, and the 2011 Libyan intervention, positions he had previously supported but criticized during the forum. Clinton directly challenged these discrepancies, stating, "There's no difference between my opponent and myself... And my opponent was for the war in Iraq. He says he wasn't. You can go back and look at the record. He supported it."

Regarding a 2013 tweet suggesting sexual assault in the military stemmed from mixing men and women, Trump initially defended it as a "correct tweet" that "many people think that’s absolutely correct." He later clarified that women would not be removed from the armed forces, emphasizing that aggressive prosecution of offenders was the solution to sexual assault.

Clinton, positioned on familiar ground as a former Secretary of State, aimed to underscore her trustworthiness with nuclear weapons and her extensive experience. She highlighted her involvement in the Osama bin Laden raid and her tenure on the Senate Foreign Relations Committee. However, she faced pointed questions from moderator Matt Lauer and a former Naval officer, John Lester, who questioned her confidence in her leadership given her handling of classified information. Clinton responded by explaining that none of the emails she handled were marked as classified and that she adhered to proper procedures for classified information. Republicans seized on her remarks, and her defense of the Libyan intervention, particularly her claim of "not a single American" casualty, drew criticism for omitting the deaths at the Benghazi diplomatic compound.

On other matters, Clinton pledged weekly Oval Office meetings to improve the Veterans Administration and identified defeating ISIS as a top counter-terrorism priority, ruling out the deployment of ground troops beyond special forces and trainers. "We are not putting ground troops into Iraq ever again. And we're not putting ground troops into Syria," she asserted. "We're going to defeat ISIS without committing American ground troops." Trump indicated a potential shift in his immigration policy, suggesting that undocumented immigrants serving in the military might be allowed to remain in the country under "very special circumstances," a departure from his previous stance advocating for the deportation of all undocumented immigrants.

Hillary Clinton and Donald Trump engaged in a sharp exchange Wednesday night on critical national security matters, highlighting starkly different approaches to military management and foreign policy in a world fraught with complexity. This "Commander in Chief" forum, hosted by NBC News, served as a potent preview of their upcoming presidential debate on September 26th. The chasm between their viewpoints was particularly evident when discussions turned to Russia and the ongoing fight against ISIS.

Clinton has consistently criticized Trump on the campaign trail for his apparent admiration of Russian President Vladimir Putin. During the forum, Trump once again drew a comparison, stating, "Certainly, in that system, he's been a leader, far more than our president has been a leader." He further elaborated, "If (Putin) says great things about me, I'm gonna say great things about him. I've already said he is really very much of a leader."

Regarding plans to combat ISIS, Trump offered a somewhat elusive strategy. When pressed by moderator Matt Lauer, Trump stated, "I have a plan... Look, I have a very substantial chance of winning. If I win, I don't want to broadcast to the enemy exactly what my plan is." He did, however, indicate a willingness to consult with US generals, while simultaneously criticizing Pentagon leadership, alleging they have not adequately challenged President Obama. "I think under the leadership Barack Obama and Hillary Clinton, the generals have been reduced to rubble," Trump asserted.

With the first presidential debate looming, Trump appeared comfortable with his characteristic freewheeling style. He notably defended a tweet suggesting that women serving in the military were a cause of military sexual assault. In contrast, Clinton skillfully navigated questions about her private email server, redirecting the conversation to her vision for a robust national security policy.

Addressing criticisms of her foreign policy record as "hawkish," Clinton clarified her stance: "We are not putting ground troops into Iraq ever again and we are not putting ground troops into Syria." She emphasized, "I view force as a last resort, not a first choice. I will do everything in my power to make sure our men and women in the military are fully prepared for any challenge they may have to face on our behalf, but I will also be as careful as I can in making the most significant decisions any president and commander and chief can make about sending our men and women into harm's way."

Clinton identified defeating ISIS as her "highest terrorism goal" and outlined a strategy involving air power and regional alliances. "We've gotta do it with much more support from the Arabs and the Kurds who will fight on the ground against ISIS," she stated. "We have to squeeze them by continuing to support the Iraqi military. They've taken back Ramadi, Fallujah. They've gotta hold them. They've gotta now get into Mosul."

In a press conference the following morning, Clinton characterized the forum as "yet another test and Donald Trump failed yet again." She added, "We saw more evidence that he is temperamentally unfit and totally unqualified to be commander in chief. He trash talked American generals."

On the Iraq War, Trump reiterated his claim of being "against" the conflict, a statement that has been frequently challenged. He did, however, praise the initial military operations in Iraq. Clinton, when questioned about her vote to authorize the war, acknowledged, "I have said that my voting to give President Bush that authority was, from my perspective, my mistake. I am asking to be judged on the totality of my record."

A notable divergence emerged regarding undocumented immigrants serving in the military. Trump expressed openness to "working" on a pathway for them to serve, a shift from his previous stance focused on deportation. "I think that when you serve in the armed forces, that's a very special situation," Trump remarked. "I could see myself working that. Absolutely. Now we have to vet very carefully, everybody would agree with that."

Concerning veterans' health, when asked about the daily suicide rate among veterans, Trump corrected the figure to 22, stating, "It's almost impossible to conceive that this is happening in this country. We're going to speed up the process, we're going to create a great mental health division. They need help." Clinton highlighted her recently introduced mental health policy agenda, emphasizing the need to "remove the stigma" and address addiction. "I have a whole section devoted to veterans' mental health. And we've gotta remove the stigma. We've gotta help people currently serving not to feel that if they report their sense of unease, their depression, that somehow it's gonna be a mark against them. We have to do more about addiction. Not only drugs but also alcohol," she explained.

Regarding her use of a personal email account and server while Secretary of State, Clinton reiterated, "it was a mistake" and that she would "certainly not do it again." She maintained, "I make no excuses for it." However, she defended certain emails, including those pertaining to the nation's covert drone program, asserting they did not reveal classified information. "There were no discussions about any of the covert actions in process being determined about whether or not to go forward," she stated. "But every part of our government had to deal with questions, secretary of state's office was first and foremost, so there are ways of talking about the drone program."

Questioned at a TV forum in New York, the candidates reveal clear faultlines on defence issues from the Middle East to women in the military Donald Trump and Hillary Clinton have struck a pointed contrast on deploying ground troops to Iraq in the first televised forum featuring the two presidential nominees. During the town hall held by NBC News and Iraq and Afghanistan Veterans of America in New York on Wednesday, Clinton pledged that the US was “not putting ground troops into Iraq ever again, and we are not putting ground troops into Syria”, while Trump disagreed. Isis, Putin, Iraq: key quotes from Trump and Clinton's foreign policy forum Read more The Republican nominee, who has long talked about the importance of “taking the oil” in the Middle East, said of Iraq: “We would leave a certain group behind and they would take the various sections where they have the oil.” The forum, which featured the two candidates speaking separately but back-to-back, came just hours after Trump laid into Clinton as “trigger happy”. During an address on defense spending in Philadelphia earlier in the day, Trump suggested there wasn’t a country in the Middle East that Clinton did not want to invade – an assertion he repeated on Wednesday evening. Navy and air force pilots also participate daily in air attacks on the terrorist group. The NBC News discussion, moderated by Matt Lauer at the Intrepid Sea, Air and Space Museum in Manhattan, centered on national security and foreign policy. While both candidates had fielded many of the same questions before, the evening marked the first opportunity of the general election for both candidates to tee up their arguments before the American public. For Clinton, a skirmish with history. From Trump, an ambush of the facts Read more It also arrived a little under three weeks before the first presidential debate on 26 September, serving as a test run for the first time Clinton and Trump will share the same stage with millions of voters watching at home. While Clinton was more controlled in her responses, reiterating the same talking points on issues ranging from the controversy over her use of a private email server as secretary of state to her support for the Iraq war, Trump maintained his signature freewheeling style that skirted specifics on policy. Facing a question from an audience member on military sexual assault, Trump stood by a tweet from 2013, in which he suggested the epidemic was a consequence of allowing women to serve in the military. “26,000 unreported sexual assults [sic] in the military-only 238 convictions. What did these geniuses expect when they put men & women together?” Trump had tweeted at the time. When confronted about the tweet, Trump defended his statement by saying “it is a correct tweet”. He then demurred, however, from saying women should no longer serve in the military and insisted firmly if incoherently that “something has to be happened”. Facebook Twitter Pinterest Matt Lauer looks on as Hillary Clinton speaks during the NBC forum in Manhattan on Wednesday. Photograph: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images Although he was specifically asked what he would do, if elected president, to curb military sexual assault, Trump remained vague. “We have to come down very hard on that and do something about that,” he offered. Trump also touched on his bumpy relationship with various foreign leaders. In the aftermath of his surprise trip to Mexico last week, he contended that it had been a success because Luis Videgaray, the country’s finance minister, had resigned on Wednesday. Videgaray reportedly played a key role in facilitating the visit, prompting Trump to boast that “the people who arranged the trip have been forced out of government”. Trump also furthered his so-called “bromance” with the Russian president, Vladimir Putin, whom he repeatedly praised and cast as a better leader than Barack Obama. In particular Trump cited Putin’s 82% approval rating as a sign of the Russian leader’s acumen. Trump went on to characterize his relationship with Putin as mutually beneficial. “If he says great things about me, I’m gonna say great things about him,” Trump said. “I think when he calls me brilliant, I’ll take the compliment. OK? “I think I would have a very, very good relationship with Putin and a very good relationship with Russia,” he added. Clinton, by contrast, sought to re-emphasize her foreign policy expertise while defending her legacy while serving as Obama’s secretary of state. The Democratic nominee repeated once more that it was “a mistake” for her to use a private email while at the helm of the State Department. “I have made no excuses for it. It was something that should not have been done,” Clinton said. How the ‘Great Paradox’ of American politics holds the secret to Trump’s success | Arlie Hochschild Read more She went on to reaffirm that there was neither evidence of her system being hacked nor that she had exchanged classified information on her private server. “Nothing, and I will repeat this … none of the emails sent or received by me had such a [top secret] header,” Clinton said, while referring to an investigation by the justice department that ultimately brought no charges upon her. The Department of Justice investigation did find a handful of classified markers in Clinton’s emails, some of which FBI director James Comey said she should have known about, but concluded there was no evidence she had intentionally mishandled classified information. Moments before Clinton appeared at the forum, Democrats on the House oversight committee released an email exchange between her and former secretary of state Colin Powell, in which he advised her on how to bypass state department servers. Asked by Clinton about restrictions on his use of a personal Blackberry, Powell responded that he had a personal computer hooked up to a private phone line “without it going through the state department servers”. “Be very careful,” he wrote. “I got around it all by not saying much and not using systems that captured the data.” Clinton did not reference Powell in her remarks at the forum, choosing instead to express regret for her actions. She struck a similar tone when discussing her support for the Iraq war, although not without pointing out that Trump also backed the invasion but has refused to acknowledge his support. “I took responsibility for my decision,” Clinton said of her vote for the Iraq war. “My opponent has refused to take responsibility for his support.” Indeed, Trump falsely stated that he had been against the war all along – a claim that went unchallenged by Lauer. Clinton also pointed out there was “no difference” between her position on Libya and that of Trump. “He’s on record extensively supporting intervention in Libya,” she said. Trump, in turn, criticized the Obama administration as pursuing “the dumbest foreign policy” he had ever seen. “The generals have been reduced to rubble,” Trump said, when pressed on the assertion he has made during his campaign that he knows more about Islamic State than military generals. Asked what his own strategy would look like against the militant group, Trump declined to say. “I have a substantial chance of winning – make America great again,” he said. “If I win, I don’t want to broadcast to the enemy what my plan is.” A new NBC News/Survey Monkey poll found Trump holding a sizeable lead over Clinton among military and veteran voters, with 55% to her 36%. Clinton has nonetheless earned endorsements from a wide swath of national security experts, including former aides to Republican presidents George W Bush and Ronald Reagan, who have deemed Trump as too dangerous to assume the role of commander-in-chief. A number of surveys have also found voters more inclined to trust Clinton’s handling of terrorism over that of Trump’s.

“I have a lot of experience dealing with classified material,” Hillary Clinton told NBC's Matt Lauer. | Getty Clinton, Trump on defensive in national security forum Neither candidate did much to advance their case to voters, while Democrats raged at the event's tight format. NEW YORK — Hillary Clinton spent a third of the time fending off questions about her emails. Donald Trump struggled to explain his secret plan to defeat the Islamic State. Both presidential candidates walked into Wednesday night’s national security forum seeking to prove themselves ready to serve as commander in chief. Clinton sought to showcase her superior experience, and Trump to indict her foreign policy along with President Barack Obama’s. Instead, both almost immediately found themselves on the defensive. Story Continued Below The Democratic nominee, appearing first at the “Commander-in-Chief Forum” held by NBC News and a veteran’s group, was hit with a barrage of detailed questions about her use of a private email system as secretary of state, as well as her initial support of the Iraq war — both of which she has called mistakes. Her Republican counterpart, meanwhile, faced broader queries about his qualifications for the Oval Office, his many past controversial statements about the state of the military, and even his apparent admiration for Russian President Vladimir Putin. The result was a scattered hour at the Intrepid Sea, Air, and Space Museum that set the stage for a particularly bitter lead-up to the first presidential debate in under three weeks — where the candidates will not be constrained by the guidelines put in place on Wednesday by moderator Matt Lauer that they should refrain from attacking each other. The evening began on shaky footing for Clinton almost immediately, as she carefully weighed her answer to Lauer’s opening question about the FBI’s conclusion that she was “extremely careless” in handling classified information. “I have a lot of experience dealing with classified material,” Clinton said defensively, her jaw setting. “Classified material has a header which has ’top secret,’ ’secret,’ ‘confidential.’ Nothing — and I will repeat this, and this is verified in the report by the Department of Justice, none of the emails sent or received by me had such a header,” she explained. It took Clinton nearly one-third of her allotted half-hour to work in a shot at her GOP rival, but her opening answer — that a commander-in-chief must have “absolute rock steadiness” — was also a potential dig at a man her campaign has portrayed as erratic and untested. Clinton noted that Trump, who appeared at the forum separately, supported invading Iraq, but found herself forced to answer yet again why she had voted to give President George W. Bush the green light. "I think the decision to go to war in Iraq, and I have said that my voting to give President Bush that authority was, from my perspective, my mistake,” she said, promising yet again not to commit ground troops to Iraq “ever again” and ruling out additional ground troops in Syria. “I also believe that it is imperative that we learn from the mistakes like after-action reports are supposed to do, so we must learn what led us down that path so it never happens again,” she said. Trump, appearing after Clinton, insisted to Lauer that he had opposed the war — despite telling radio host Howard Stern in 2002 that he supported it — quickly turning to criticize the current state of American foreign policy under Obama. Presented with his past statement that he knows more about ISIS than “the generals,” Trump responded that those same generals he would have to work with “have been reduced to rubble” under Obama’s leadership. And he repeated his lament that the United States failed to “take the oil” in Iraq, an idea that national security experts across the political spectrum have panned as ludicrous. Thought Lauer missed an opportunity to corner Trump on his support for the war, he did press the Republican nominee on an apparent contradiction between his declaration that he would have top generals present him with a blueprint to defeat ISIS with his longstanding intimation that he already has a secret plan to do so. “I have a substantial chance of winning, but if I win I don’t want to broadcast [the plan],” Trump explained. “The fact is, we have had the worst — and you could say the dumbest — foreign policy." Trump was perhaps most emphatic in defending his past comments about Putin, despite the fodder those remarks have provided for attacks from Clinton and her allies. “I think when he calls me brilliant I’ll take the compliment, but it’s not going to get him anywhere,” Trump told Lauer, who had just listed a roster of controversial Russian moves, including Moscow’s reported culpability for the hack of the Democratic National Committee earlier this year. Trump responded that Putin has an 82 percent approval rating in Russia: “He’s been a leader far more than our president has been a leader.” And, as part of his answer to a question posed by a father who said his daughter was concerned about rising incidents of sexual assault in the military, Trump also stood by a past tweet suggesting that allowing women to serve — rather than lax enforcement of military discipline — was to blame. If Clinton appeared defensive and cautious in answering the specific, pointed questions posed to her, Trump also stumbled with the broader queries Lauer offered him. At one point, he corrected a veteran who had lost two friends to suicide about veteran suicide statistics — when she in fact had the right number, shaking her head at his retort. His defense of Putin was politically dodgy, too — the Russian leader’s American approval rating is in the single digits, according to an NBC News survey conducted even before the DNC hack that his government is widely believed to be behind. And his insistence that the generals currently serving had “been reduced to rubble” neared crossing a line in military circles because of the implication that he would replace officers who are not political appointees. Tensions boiled over between Democrats and NBC News, as well, as they questioned Lauer's questioning: "@NBCNews it is irresponsible to allow Trump to go on a 60 second rant about a military intervention that he supported w/o clarifying," tweeted Clinton's traveling press secretary, Nick Merrill. The forum landed in the wake of new NBC News/Survey Monkey polling showing that Trump holds a substantial lead over Clinton when it comes to military and veteran voters — a crucial voting bloc in states like Virginia -- 55 percent to 36 percent overall. Even so, as the two campaigns spar over the numbers of military endorsers each has, Trump’s poll numbers in the military community are lower than either Mitt Romney's in 2012 or John McCain’s in 2008 relative to Obama’s. After a summer of Clinton bombarding Trump’s fitness for the office and a stretch in which he saw his support waver after clashing with the parents of a deceased soldier, however, this week’s pitches are not just targeted at the significant military community: They are each campaign’s latest attempt to appeal to middle-class voters concerned about national security and their own safety. While Trump has gone after Clinton aggressively over her emails, she has responded by pointing out some of his policy inconsistencies on the trail and specifically identifying his ties to Russia as particularly troubling. Prior to the event, members of the Trump team spent pieces of the morning playing defense after Clinton’s super PAC, Priorities USA Action, promoted an ad that prominently featured the candidate declaring his love for war, the latest in a series of spots from both the campaign and super PAC making the case that Trump is far too inconsistent and unpredictable to sit in the Oval Office or have control of the nuclear codes. But on Wednesday, the man who once promised to “bomb the hell out of ISIS" spit the accusation right back at his opponent. “The current strategy of toppling regimes, with no plan for what to do the day after, only produces power vacuums that are filled by terrorists,” Trump said earlier in the day at a speech in Philadelphia. “Immediately after taking office, I will ask my generals to present me with a plan within 30 days to defeat and destroy ISIS." Even as he called for a massive increase in the military budget, Trump suggested he would more judicious in using force -- upending the traditional partisan battles lines on national security. “Unlike my opponent, my foreign policy will emphasize diplomacy, not destruction. Hillary Clinton’s legacy in Iraq, Libya, and Syria has produced only turmoil and suffering,” he said in Pennsylvania, skipping over his own one-time support for intervention in some of those conflicts. “Sometimes it has seemed like there wasn’t a country in the Middle East that Hillary Clinton didn’t want to invade, intervene or topple. She is trigger-happy and unstable when it comes to war,” he said.

During a "commander-in-chief forum" on NBC News on September 7th, Democratic presidential nominee Hillary Clinton and her Republican opponent Donald Trump addressed critical national security and foreign policy issues. Each candidate participated in a 30-minute segment, fielding questions on these vital topics from moderator Matt Lauer and audience members. This document provides a comprehensive review of the questionable claims made by both candidates during the event. As per our established practice, we refrain from awarding "Pinocchios" during live broadcasts.

**Hillary Clinton's Statements:**

*   **Regarding her private email server:** Clinton stated, "It was a mistake to have a personal [email] account." The central issue revolved around her use of a private server and her failure to utilize her designated State Department email account, which would have ensured the preservation of emails subject to Freedom of Information Act requests. She asserted, "Classified material has a header which says ‘top secret,’ ‘secret,’ ‘confidential.’ Nothing — and I will repeat this, and this is verified in the report by the Department of Justice — none of the emails sent or received by me had such a header." Initially, Clinton claimed, "I did not email any classified material to anyone on my email. There is no classified material." As the State Department began retroactively classifying some emails, her statement evolved to, "I’m confident that this process will prove that I never sent nor received any email that was marked classified." She has since become more specific, referencing headers indicating "top secret" and similar designations. This specificity arises because FBI Director James Comey disclosed that three emails sent to Clinton's server contained the marking "(c)" for "confidential" but lacked a formal header. The State Department indicated that two of these emails were erroneously marked. It is important to note that classification markings do not definitively render information classified, nor does their absence render it unclassified. Comey further revealed that 110 emails, separate from the 2,000 retroactively classified, were found to "contain classified information at the time they were sent or received." He also stated, "there is evidence to support a conclusion that any reasonable person in Secretary Clinton’s position, or in the position of those government employees with whom she was corresponding about these matters, should have known that an unclassified system was no place for that conversation." Therefore, Clinton's emphasis on the presence or absence of a "header" appears to be a misdirection.

*   **On her system being hacked:** Clinton claimed, "There is no evidence my system was hacked." While the FBI did not uncover evidence of Clinton's emails being compromised, their report did highlight numerous attempts to access her private server and noted its lack of expected safeguards for sensitive communications. Furthermore, an aide to Bill Clinton, utilizing the Clinton private server, was indeed hacked. The report also indicated that "hostile foreign actors" successfully infiltrated the email systems of close personal aides to Hillary Clinton, acquiring emails.

*   **On veteran suicides:** In response to a question about veteran suicide rates, Clinton stated, "Twenty — twenty suicides a day." This aligns with the updated, accurate statistic. Trump, however, reverted to the outdated and misleading figure of 22 veteran suicides per day. The discrepancy between these figures is significant. The updated statistic is derived from the most comprehensive, data-driven study on veteran suicides to date, whereas the older figure was a rough estimate based on incomplete data, a figure even its researchers cautioned against using. The Department of Veterans Affairs, in collaboration with the Department of Defense and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, conducted a study of veteran mortality records, compiling 55 million records over 35 years using military, health, and mortality data. This study found that in 2014, an average of 20 veterans died by suicide daily, representing an actual count from all 50 states. Of these suicides in 2014, 65 percent involved veterans aged 50 and older. The study also revealed that from 2001 to 2014, the suicide rate among veterans utilizing VA services increased by 8.8 percent, compared to a 38.6 percent increase among veterans who did not use VA services. It is crucial to provide context to the statistic of 20 veteran suicides per day: veterans generally exhibit higher suicide rates than the civilian population, though the degree of this difference varies across specific demographics. Clinton's presentation of the statistic lacked this essential context, rendering it less informative.

*   **On VA privatization:** Clinton asserted, "I will not let the V.A. be privatized. And I do think there is an agenda out there, supported by my opponent, to do just that. I think that would be very disastrous for our military veterans." This is a misleading assertion often made by Clinton regarding veterans' issues. She has previously referenced proposals by Concerned Veterans for America, a conservative veterans advocacy group, to reform the Department of Veterans Affairs. These proposals, developed by a bipartisan task force, would offer veterans the option of subsidized private care and establish a new, nonprofit government corporation to manage VA medical facilities. While these plans incorporate elements that open veteran healthcare to the private sector, the term "privatization" typically implies a complete transfer of government services to private industry, which does not accurately describe the group's proposal. Furthermore, Trump has stated he does not support such a privatization of the VA, as he clarified during the forum. In a July 2016 speech outlining his veterans' policy, he declared, "veterans should be guaranteed the right to choose their doctor and clinics, whether at a VA facility or at a private medical center." Trump's position is to provide veterans with the option of private healthcare if they reside far from a VA facility or face lengthy appointment wait times. This mirrors a provision in the 2014 Veterans Choice Act, a bipartisan bill enacted under President Obama in response to the VA scandal.

*   **On gun purchases by individuals on terror watch lists:** Clinton stated, "Here at home, for goodness’s sakes, we have to finally pass a law prohibiting people on the terrorist watch list from being able to buy a gun in the United States of America." While Democrats frequently highlight that individuals on the terror watch list can acquire firearms, the legislative proposals they have put forth do not automatically ban such purchases. Senator Dianne Feinstein (D-Calif.) has introduced legislation granting the attorney general the authority to determine if a suspected terrorist can purchase a firearm. Individuals subjected to a federal terrorism investigation within five years of attempting to buy a gun would be flagged in the background check system, allowing the Justice Department to review these cases. The government employs a "reasonable suspicion" standard for inclusion on the Terrorist Watchlist, which encompasses the "no fly list." Membership in a terrorist organization or inclusion on a watch list does not inherently prevent an individual from purchasing a gun. An additional disqualifying factor under federal or state law, such as a felony conviction or illegal immigration status, must be present.

*   **On the Iraq War:** Clinton correctly stated, "Now, my opponent was for the war in Iraq. He says he wasn’t. You can go back and look at the record. He supported it. He told Howard Stern he supported it." This has been extensively documented. There is no evidence that Trump opposed the Iraq War at its outset. He reiterated this false claim during the forum.

*   **On intervention in Libya:** Clinton accurately noted, "With respect to Libya, again, there’s no difference between my opponent and myself. He’s on record extensively supporting intervention in Libya, when Gadhafi was threatening to massacre his population." This is also correct, with video evidence of Trump advocating for intervention, initially highlighted by BuzzFeed.

*   **On ground troops in Iraq and Syria:** Clinton remarked, "They are not going to get ground troops. We are not putting ground troops into Iraq ever again. And we’re not putting ground troops into Syria. We’re going to defeat ISIS without committing American ground troops." This statement is perplexing given the presence of approximately 3,000 U.S. troops in Iraq at the time, serving in advisory and training capacities or on special assignments. Clinton's comment drew widespread mockery on Twitter. While she appeared to be referring to combat troops, her phrasing was imprecise. Prior to this remark, Clinton seemed to acknowledge the existing troops by stating that Arab forces engaged against the Islamic State would receive support from the United States, including "special forces, as you know, they have enablers, they have surveillance, intelligence, reconnaissance help."

*   **On troop deployments when she became Secretary of State:** Clinton stated, "When I became secretary of state, we had 200,000 troops deployed in Iraq and Afghanistan." This figure is close to accurate. Troop levels in Iraq were declining when President Obama took office, while he initiated a surge in Afghanistan, increasing troop numbers from approximately 50,000 to 100,000 by August 2010. In June 2010, there were still 88,000 troops in Iraq, down from 150,000 prior to Obama's presidency. The Congressional Research Service reported an average of 186,300 troops across both theaters in fiscal year 2009.

**Donald Trump's Statements:**

*   **On the Iraq War:** Trump asserted, "I happened to hear Hillary Clinton say that I was not against the war in Iraq. I was totally against the war in Iraq. You can look at Esquire magazine from ’04. You can look at before that." This claim is difficult to reconcile with the facts. Trump did not express opposition to the Iraq War prior to the

query: What specific technology does Gillette claim Dollar Shave Club's products infringe upon in their lawsuit?

documents.text:
**Gillette Sues Dollar Shave Club Over Razor Patent**

Gillette, a leading razor brand owned by Procter & Gamble (P&G), has filed a lawsuit against online razor retailer Dollar Shave Club. The legal action, initiated in federal court in Delaware, alleges that Dollar Shave Club's products **infringe on a 2004 Gillette patent**. This patent specifically covers a razor blade featuring a **"chromium-containing overcoat layer."** Gillette is seeking **monetary damages** and a **court order to halt the sale of these allegedly infringing razors**.

The lawsuit **does not name Dorco**, the South Korean manufacturer of Dollar Shave Club's razors, despite Dorco being an equity stakeholder in the startup. A P&G spokesman, Damon Jones, clarified that Dollar Shave Club was named because it is **"actually selling the product and bringing it to market."** Jones explained that P&G regularly conducts **"market surveillance,"** which involves testing competitor products. During a recent testing phase of Dollar Shave Club blades, P&G identified what it believed to be patent-infringing razors. This testing was prompted by press reports indicating Dollar Shave Club was **"investing new money in product development."**

A representative for Dollar Shave Club has **declined to comment** on the lawsuit. The online retailer has secured nearly **$150 million in equity investments** and was recently valued by investors at approximately **$600 million**. Dollar Shave Club gained significant traction after its **2012 launch**, which featured a viral promotional video starring CEO Michael Dubin. Since then, the company has employed a strategy of **irreverent online videos, TV commercials, and social media campaigns** to build a substantial customer base. Earlier this year, Dubin stated that the company had **over two million subscribers** receiving monthly or bi-monthly razor blade shipments. He also projected **at least $140 million in revenue for 2015**.

In response to the competitive landscape, Gillette launched its own service, **Gillette Shave Club, last year**. The company enlisted celebrities, such as Giants quarterback Eli Manning, to promote it in the lead-up to Father's Day in June.

*Correction: The patent in question pertains to a razor blade that includes an overcoat layer, not the overcoat layer itself.*

Gillette is taking its battle with Dollar Shave Club to court, filing a patent infringement lawsuit. This legal action comes as Dollar Shave Club, an online subscription service, has rapidly gained market share in the U.S. razor and blade industry. In just three years, the company has captured 8% of the $3 billion market, challenging Gillette's long-standing dominance.

The lawsuit, filed by Procter & Gamble's (P&G) Gillette unit in Delaware, asks the U.S. District Court to halt Dollar Shave Club's sales of razors that allegedly infringe upon one of Gillette's numerous patents. Specifically, the seven-page complaint details how Dollar Shave Club's blades are believed to violate a patent issued in 2004. This patent covers two coatings designed to enhance blade sharpness and durability. Gillette currently employs these coatings on popular products like its Mach 3, Venus, and Fusion lines.

A P&G spokesman, Damon Jones, stated that the company regularly examines competitor products and had "reason to believe" Dollar Shave Club was modifying its offerings. Following an evaluation of Dollar Shave Club's blades, P&G claims to have discovered the alleged patent infringement. A representative for Dollar Shave Club declined to comment on the lawsuit.

This legal challenge arises at a time when Gillette, a company with over a century of history, has been slower to adapt to the emergence of online competitors. While Gillette commands over 60% of the U.S. retail market for razors and blades, its share of the online shaving market is only 20%. In response, Gillette launched its own subscription service, Gillette Shave Club, last year. P&G has not disclosed subscriber numbers for its service, but reported a four-percentage-point increase in its e-commerce share for blades and razors since the service's relaunch in June.

P&G has a history of using legal means to address competitive challenges. In 2014, the company sued Hello Products LLC, a toothpaste manufacturer, over claims that their product was "99% natural." P&G has also defended its Crest Whitestrips teeth-whitening product against a private-label manufacturer. "We take every violation of our intellectual property seriously, and when necessary, we take legal action to defend our business as we have in the past and will continue to do in the future," Mr. Jones added.

Dollar Shave Club gained significant attention in 2012 with a viral video featuring founder Michael Dubin. The company's model of delivering razor cartridges directly to consumers for as little as $1 per month resonated with shoppers seeking alternatives to the increasing cost of shaving. The company now reports having 10 million customers who purchase blades at least every other month, with prices ranging from $1 to $9. In June, Mr. Dubin projected that Dollar Shave Club would generate $140 million in sales this year, a figure that includes other personal care items like shaving cream and flushable wipes. The company has secured over $90 million in venture funding, with its latest valuation reaching $630 million. In contrast, P&G's market value stands at $220 billion.

Gillette's subscription service adopts a different strategy than Dollar Shave Club's approach, which encourages weekly blade changes. P&G suggests that its premium razor blades can cost men approximately $5 per month, based on the durability of Gillette blades and the assumption of shaving three to four times per week. The lawsuit alleges that Dollar Shave Club's razors infringe on Gillette's patent by using chromium to adhere an outer layer of polytetrafluoroethylene on their blades. Dollar Shave Club sources its blades from Dorco, the U.S. division of a South Korean manufacturer that also produces private-label razors for retailers such as Walgreens and Dollar General Corp. Gillette's lawsuit does not name Dorco as a defendant. Representatives for Dorco were not immediately available for comment. While razor manufacturers have historically focused on the number of blades per cartridge, Gillette's latest cartridge, the Fusion ProShield, will feature a new lubricating strip.
