Here's a revised version of the open letter, aiming to meet your quality targets:

**An Open Letter From The Thousands Of Men And Women At Gillette**

Trust in our word is as important as trust in our product – they go hand in hand. Recently, there's been a lot of discussion about Gillette: our claims, our technology, our pricing, and our marketing. Essentially, people are talking about us, the individuals who work here, and what we stand for. We want to speak to you directly about a few things that are incredibly important to us.

First and foremost, **shaving is our passion**. We don't just mean the daily act of shaving; we mean shaving as a **science**. For us, **every single detail matters**. We conduct over 60 precise quality checks, from the raw materials to the final cartridge. Each one **must pass every time**, or it will never leave our facilities. We are confident that **no other razor manufacturer globally adheres to this rigorous standard**. Our team works tirelessly every day to ensure our razors are **superior to any other product available**. Our goal is to provide you with the **absolute best shave possible**, consistently exceeding your expectations. Why is this crucial? Because we want you to start every day with the **confidence that you're using the optimal razor for the job**, without needing to worry about the correct shaving technique or experiencing irritation from inferior razors that are merely rebranded with a fancy name and website.

Secondly, we understand that being the best isn't always enough, and you've told us that **our blades can be too expensive**. We have heard you. **Effective April 1st, the majority of Gillette blade refills in the United States will be available at lower prices** at most retailers, both online and in-store. This is a **genuine price reduction**, not a temporary promotion. You will receive the **exact same high-quality products** you've come to expect – with **zero compromise on quality, period**. Please note that actual retail prices are determined solely by individual retailers, as is standard in the industry. You can find an overview of our products and pricing on our website.

Finally, we are thrilled that **over 200,000 of you have returned to Gillette** in just the past few weeks. Many of you have explored other products, and we are proud that you have come back to the **Gillette quality you know and trust**. It's a significant fact that between November 2013 and October 2015, **more than half of individuals who tried a competitor's product did not make a repeat purchase**. This trend was evident even before we launched our "Welcome Back" offer last year. While some competitors may not welcome this news, ultimately, **the quality of the shave should be the deciding factor**. We are a company with a legacy of over 115 years, built on the core values of **quality, innovation, and trust**. These are principles we have no intention of changing.

Thank you for entrusting us with your faces, every single day.

– The Faces Behind the Blades, the Men and Women of Gillette

The invention relates to improvements to razors and razor blades. A razor blade is typically formed of a suitable substrate material such as stainless steel, and a cutting edge is formed with a wedge-shaped configuration with an ultimate tip having a radius less than about 1000 angstroms, e.g., about 200-300 angstroms. Hard coatings such as diamond, amorphous diamond, diamond-like carbon-(DLC) material, nitrides, carbides, oxides or ceramics are often used to improve strength, corrosion resistance and shaving ability, maintaining needed strength while permitting thinner edges with lower cutting forces to be used. Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) outer layer can be used to provide friction reduction. Interlayers of niobium or chromium containing materials can aid in improving the binding between the substrate, typically stainless steel, and hard carbon coatings, such as DLC. Examples of razor blade cutting edge structures and processes of manufacture are described in U.S. Pat. Nos. 5,295,305; 5,232,568; 4,933,058; 5,032,243; 5,497,550; 5,940,975; 5,669,144; EP 0591339; and PCT 92/03330, which are hereby incorporated by reference. In use, the ultimate tip of the edges having hard coatings and polytetrafluoroethylene outer layers can become more rounded after repeated shaves such that there is an increase in the tip radius and a generally perceived decrease in shaving performance. SUMMARY OF THE INVENTION In one aspect, the invention features, in general, a razor blade including a substrate with a cutting edge defined by a sharpened tip and adjacent facets, a layer of hard coating on the cutting edge, an overcoat layer of a chromium containing material on the layer of hard coating, and an outer layer of polytetrafluoroethylene coating on the overcoat layer. In another aspect the invention features, in general, a shaving razor including a handle and a razor head with a blade having a substrate with a cutting edge defined by a sharpened tip and adjacent facets, a layer of hard coating on the cutting edge, an overcoat layer of a chromium containing material on the layer of hard coating, and an outer layer of polytetrafluoroethylene coating on the overcoat layer. Particular embodiments of the invention may include one or more of the following features. In particular embodiments, the hard coating material can be made of carbon containing materials (e.g., diamond, amorphous diamond or DLC), nitrides, carbides, oxides or other ceramics. The hard coating layer can have a thickness less than 2,000 angstroms. The overcoat layer can be made of chromium or a chromium containing alloy compatible with polytetrafluoroethylene such as a chromium platinum alloy. The overcoat layer can be between 100 and 500 angstroms thick. The blade can include an interlayer between the substrate and the layer of hard coating. The interlayer can include niobium or a chromium containing material. The polytetrafluoroethylene can be Krytox LW1200 available from DuPont. The PTFE outer layer can be between 100 and 5000 angstroms thick. In another aspect, the invention features, in general, making a razor blade by providing a substrate with a cutting edge defined by a sharpened tip and adjacent facets, adding a layer of hard coating on the cutting edge, adding an overcoat layer of a chromium containing material on the layer of hard coating, and adding an outer layer of polytetrafluoroethylene coating over the overcoat layer. Particular embodiments of the invention may include one or more of the following features. In particular embodiments the layers can be added by physical vapor deposition (i.e., sputtering) or by chemical vapor deposition. The chromium containing layer, preferably chromium, can be sputter deposited under conditions that result in a compressively stressed coating. The sputter deposition of chromium containing materials can include applying a DC bias to the target that is more negative than −50 volts, preferably more negative than −200 volts. Alternatively an appropriate RF bias scheme can be used to achieve an equivalent chromium layer. Embodiments of the invention may include one or more of the following advantages. The use of a chromium containing overcoat layer provides improved adhesion of the polytetrafluorethylene outer layer to the hard coating layer. The razor blade has improved edge strength provided by hard coating and has reduced tip rounding with repeated shaves. Reduced tip rounding minimizes the increase in cutting force thereby maintaining excellent shaving performance. The razor blade has excellent shaving characteristics from the first shave onwards. Other features and advantages of the invention will be apparent from the following description of a particular embodiment and from the claims. DESCRIPTION OF THE DRAWINGS FIG. 1 is a vertical sectional view of a cutting edge portion of a razor blade. FIG. 2 is a perspective view of a shaving razor including the FIG. 1 razor blade. DESCRIPTION OF A PARTICULAR EMBODIMENT Referring to FIG. 1, there is shown razor blade 10 including substrate 12, interlayer 14, hard coating layer 16, overcoat layer 18, and outer layer 20. The substrate 12 is typically made of stainless steel (though other substrates can be employed) and has an ultimate edge sharpened to a tip radius of less than 1,000 angstroms, preferably 200 to 300 angstroms, and has a profile with side facets 22 at an included angle of between 15 and 30 degrees, preferably about 19 degrees, measured at 40 microns from the tip. Interlayer 14 is used to facilitate bonding of the hard coating layer to the substrate. Examples of suitable interlayer material are niobium and chromium containing material. A particular interlayer is made of niobium greater than 100 angstroms and preferably less than 500 angstroms thick. PCT 92/03330 describes use of a niobium interlayer. Hard coating layer 16 provides improved strength, corrosion resistance and shaving ability and can be made from carbon containing materials (e.g., diamond, amorphous diamond or DLC), nitrides (e.g., boron nitride, niobium nitride or titanium nitride), carbides (e.g., silicon carbide), oxides (e.g., alumina, zirconia) or other ceramic materials. The carbon containing materials can be doped with other elements, such as tungsten, titanium or chromium by including these additives, for example in the target during application by sputtering. The materials can also incorporate hydrogen, e.g., hydrogenated DLC. Preferably coating layer 16 is made of diamond, amorphous diamond or DLC. A particular embodiment includes DLC less than 2,000 angstroms, preferably less than 1,000 angstroms. DLC layers and methods of deposition are described in U.S. Pat. No. 5,232,568. As described in the “Handbook of Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) Processing,” DLC is an amorphous carbon material that exhibits many of the desirable properties of diamond but does not have the crystalline structure of diamond. Overcoat layer 18 is used to reduce the tip rounding of the hard coated edge and to facilitate bonding of the outer layer to the hard coating while still maintaining the benefits of both. Overcoat layer 18 is preferably made of chromium containing material, e.g., chromium or chromium alloys that are compatible with polytetrafluoroethylene, e.g., CrPt. A particular overcoat layer is chromium about 100-200 angstroms thick. Blade 10 has a cutting edge that has less rounding with repeated shaves than it would have without the overcoat layer. Outer layer 20 is used to provide reduced friction and includes polytetrafluoroethylene and is sometimes referred to as a telomer. A particular polytetrafluoroethylene material is Krytox LW 1200 available from DuPont. This material is a nonflammable and stable dry lubricant that consists of small particles that yield stable dispersions. It is furnished as an aqueous dispersion of 20% solids by weight and can be applied by dipping, spraying, or brushing, and can thereafter be air dried or melt coated. The layer is preferably less than 5,000 angstroms and could typically be 1,500 angstroms to 4,000 angstroms, and can be as thin as 100 angstroms, provided that a continuous coating is maintained. Provided that a continuous coating is achieved, reduced telomer coating thickness can provide improved first shave results. U.S. Pat. Nos. 5,263,256 and 5,985,459, which are hereby incorporated by reference, describe techniques which can be used to reduce the thickness of an applied telomer layer. Razor blade 10 is made generally according to the processes described in the above referenced patents. A particular embodiment includes a niobium interlayer 14, DLC hard coating layer 16, chromium overcoat layer 18, and Krytox LW1200 polytetrafluoroethylene outer coat layer 20. Chromium overcoat layer 18 is deposited to a minimum of 100 angstroms and a maximum of 500 angstroms. It is deposited by sputtering using a DC bias (more negative than −50 volts and preferably more negative than −200 volts) and pressure of about 2 millitorr argon. The increased negative bias is believed to promote a compressive stress (as opposed to a tensile stress), in the chromium overcoat layer which is believed to promote improved resistance to tip rounding while maintaining good shaving performance. Blade 10 preferably has a tip radius of about 200-400 angstroms, measured by SEM after application of overcoat layer 18 and before adding outer layer 20. Referring to FIG. 2, blade 10 can be used in shaving razor 110, which includes handle 112 and replaceable shaving cartridge 114. Cartridge 14 includes housing 116, which carries three blades 10, guard 120 and cap 122. Blades 10 are movably mounted, as described, e.g., in U.S. Pat. No. 5,918,369, which is incorporated by reference. Cartridge 114 also includes an interconnect member on which housing 116 is pivotally mounted at two arms 128. The interconnect member includes a base 127 which is replaceably connected to handle 112. Alternatively, blade 10 can be used in other razors having one, two or more than three blades, double-sided blades, and razors that do not have movable blades or pivoting heads where the cartridge is either replaceable or permanently attached to a razor handle. In use, razor blade 10 has excellent shaving characteristics from the first shave onwards. Blade 10 has improved edge strength provided by hard coating and has reduced tip rounding with repeated shaves provided by the overlayer coating while maintaining excellent shave characteristics. Other embodiments of the invention are within the scope of the appended claims.

Procter & Gamble's Gillette subsidiary has filed a patent infringement suit against Dollar Shave Club. The complaint, lodged in the U.S. District Court for Delaware, alleges that Dollar Shave Club's offerings infringe upon Gillette's proprietary blade coating technology. This specific coating is a key component in Gillette's prominent razor lines, such as the Mach3, Venus, and Fusion. Gillette, with its headquarters in Boston, asserts full ownership of the patent rights in question. Dollar Shave Club, located in Venice, California, has confirmed receipt of the legal filing and is undertaking a review of the allegations. A spokesperson for Dollar Shave Club stated that no additional statements would be made at this time. Gillette has pinpointed several Dollar Shave Club products, including The Humble Twin, The 4X, and The Executive, as the specific items allegedly in violation of their patent.

**Dollar Shave Club Claims No. 2 Spot in Razor Cartridge Sales, Challenging Industry Giants**

Dollar Shave Club (DSC) has announced a significant milestone, asserting it has **surpassed Schick to capture the No. 2 position in razor cartridge volume sales** as of July. This remarkable achievement comes just over three years after the online disruptor's market debut. While this claim awaits independent verification, it generally aligns with available third-party data. If confirmed, this positions DSC considerably behind Procter & Gamble Co.'s dominant Gillette brand in dollar sales, and also behind Schick in that metric.

### **Aggressive Marketing Fuels Explosive Growth**

The online shaving brand has experienced **phenomenal subscriber growth, doubling its base to 2.2 million in under ten months**. This surge is largely attributed to a **major national television advertising campaign launched in November**. According to founder and CEO Michael Dubin, DSC has been the **most significant spender on television advertising within the razor category since November**, outspending even Gillette. This aggressive push is further supported by a substantial **$75 million venture capital round closed in June**, earmarked to continue fueling its TV ad initiatives, following a $50 million round nine months prior.

### **Strategic Focus: Fresh Blades and Unbeatable Value**

"TV has been a winner for us and a space we're going to stay in," stated Mr. Dubin, underscoring the brand's commitment to the medium. A **new advertising campaign, rolling out this month, will highlight the distinct benefits of using fresh razor blades**. "It feels great to shave with a fresh blade," Mr. Dubin remarked. "And we feel there are millions more guys out there that have that perception and would enjoy that experience. This campaign is about finding those guys."

### **The Highly Lucrative Razor Cartridge Market**

Razor cartridges represent the **largest and most profitable segment of the shaving market**, a high-margin category within packaged goods. Dollar Shave Club reports a **16% volume share of the cartridge market for July**, based on its internal sales data. This figure is closely watched against Nielsen's annual scanner data, which indicates a 15% volume share for Schick. However, Nielsen data compiled by Deutsche Bank suggests Schick's cartridge volume share for the four weeks ending August 8th was approximately one point higher than its annual share, potentially placing the two brands in a **near tie**.

### **Competitor Perspectives and Market Dynamics**

Chris Gough, VP-investor relations at Edgewell, Schick's parent company, declined to comment on Mr. Dubin's share claim. He cited Dollar Shave Club's private status, lack of publicly audited financials, and operation within an online marketplace with limited third-party sales data. He also noted that Nielsen's coverage does not encompass the entire shaving market.

### **Third-Party Data Points to Continued Momentum**

Conversely, third-party data from Slice Intelligence, which analyzes online purchase receipts from a panel of 2 million consumers, indicates that Dollar Shave Club is **either already the No. 2 U.S. razor cartridge brand or will be imminently, even by dollar sales**. Slice data corroborates Dollar Shave Club's impressive growth trajectory, showing online sales of **$16.3 million in July**. This figure more than doubles the $7.7 million recorded in October, prior to the launch of its in-house TV campaign, and nearly triples the brand's sales rate from the previous year.

### **Schick's Performance and Market Position**

In contrast, Schick's offline unit sales declined by **6.8% year-over-year** for the four weeks ending August 8th, according to Nielsen, with dollar sales falling by 5.5%. While these figures are slightly better than the full-year results, Dollar Shave Club, which focuses on more affordable blades intended for weekly replacement, still trails Schick in cartridge dollar share by a **9% to 16% margin**, according to DSC's own Nielsen-aided estimates. Schick also maintains a lead in both volume and dollar sales when considering broader razor product categories, including disposables and handles.

### **A Remarkable Rise for a Young Upstart**

Despite these differences, Dollar Shave Club's ascent is a **notable achievement for a three-year-old company challenging an 89-year-old incumbent**. If Dollar Shave Club sustains its current monthly sales pace, it is projected to achieve **nearly $200 million in sales over the next twelve months**, according to Slice data. Even if its current annual growth rate were halved, DSC could **overtake Schick in cartridge dollar sales within a year**. A hypothetical tripling of its sales, though ambitious, could see the club surpass Schick in total dollar sales. Schick reported $222 million in cartridge sales over the past year, per Nielsen, with an additional estimated $13 million from online sales according to Slice. Schick also generated $115 million in razor handle sales and $239 million in disposable razor sales, a segment where Dollar Shave Club does not compete.

### **The Shifting Landscape of the Offline Market**

The offline razor market, while larger, is experiencing a **decline**. Nielsen estimates offline razor cartridge sales at $1.3 billion, razor handles at $427 million, and disposable razors at $1.2 billion, totaling nearly $3 billion. While disposable razor sales saw a modest 1.8% growth over the past year, sales of blades and handles each decreased by over 4%.

### **Competitive Responses and Future Challenges**

Mr. Dubin's success has not gone unnoticed by competitors. Gillette launched its "Gillette Shave Club" online sales effort in June, advertising its blades as 50% cheaper than "the other shave club." Nielsen data indicates a continued dip in Gillette's offline sales and market share in recent months, with Mr. Dubin reporting no discernible impact on his business from these ads. However, Slice data does show a **rebound in Gillette's online share**, increasing by over 4 percentage points to 18.6% from June to July, while Dollar Shave Club's share has seen a decline of over 4 percentage points since March from a peak of nearly 54%.

Coincidentally, the new Dollar Shave Club advertisements humorously address the practice of shaving with old, dirty blades to save money. Gillette is also preparing to introduce an improved men's cartridge early next year, along with new theft-resistant packaging designed to make blades more accessible from behind locked store cases – a practice that has been a recurring theme in Dollar Shave Club's advertising.

### **Emerging Competition in the Value Segment**

Dollar Shave Club also faces increasing competition in the affordable blade market, potentially from unexpected sources. Dorco, the South Korean manufacturer widely believed by industry insiders to produce DSC's blades, has been more aggressively marketing its own brand online. According to Slice, Dorco captured an **8.5% share of U.S. online sales last month** and also launched value-priced Swipe blades at Costco earlier this year.

The Internet Archive undertakes extensive web crawling initiatives with the primary objective of meticulously archiving digital content as it emerges and preserving the interconnected pages it references. This proactive approach ensures that even as referenced web pages undergo modifications or are subsequently removed from the internet, a persistent link to the specific version that was active at the time of the original page's creation remains intact.

Subsequently, the Internet Archive endeavors to facilitate the integration of these archived page references into the web's fabric. The aspiration is that these preserved links will serve as replacements for currently broken hyperlinks or function as supplementary connections, thereby enabling users to ascertain the original intent and context envisioned by the authors of a given webpage. Ultimately, this ambitious undertaking aims to systematically rectify all instances of broken links across the World Wide Web, with ongoing crawls specifically targeting sites that support the "No More 404" initiative.

### Kalamazoo Shootings: A Night of Unspeakable Terror and Witness Accounts

The Kalamazoo shootings, a series of horrific events that unfolded on a Saturday night, left a community in shock and mourning. **Twelve-year-old Joi Coleman** recounted a **terrifying encounter** in the parking lot of the Meadows Townhomes. She described holding a woman's hand when a man, later identified as **Jason Dalton**, approached. Dalton allegedly asked, "Have you seen Mazie?" According to Coleman, the woman offered help, but Dalton **immediately opened fire**.

Coleman vividly described the unfolding horror: "He shot her once and she was still running and then he shot her again and she fell. She was asking 'Joi, help me. Joi, help me.'" The young witness expressed her **overwhelming fear**, stating, "I knew I was going to get shot too, he didn't stop shooting." Despite the terror, Coleman and the children she was supervising managed to reach safety inside. The victim's daughter witnessed the event from a window. Police reported Dalton fired **10 rounds**, with **three bullets striking her mother**.

Another child present, Meghan Coleman, shared the **chilling words** of a young girl who ran to the window, crying, "My mom is dead! My mom is dead!" The shooter, described as "the guy," reportedly **backed up and resumed shooting**, narrowly missing. Joi's mother, who was caring for 11 children, stated that Dalton **returned to the scene four times**, with Joi recalling him pointing a silver gun directly at their house.

Neighbors Travis Gettys and Devin Fletter, who live adjacent to the Colemans, reported seeing Dalton interacting with children in the neighborhood earlier that day, about an hour before the shooting. Fletter commented, "Makes me feel like he was looking for a specific person." Gettys described the surreal experience, noting, "It's such an adrenaline rush, we were just playing video games and everything happened." Joi Coleman also remembered Dalton **initially observing them and other children** before firing. The woman who was shot was expected to recover after sustaining injuries to her legs and arm.

### Escalating Violence and Devastating Impact

Travis Gettys emphasized the **direct link between the various incidents**, stating, "At this point the connection between the three cases is the defendant. Is Mister Dalton." The violence escalated just hours after the initial shooting. Shortly after 10 p.m., **17-year-old Tyler Smith and his father, Richard Smith, were fatally shot** while looking at cars at Seelye Kia on Stadium Drive. Minutes later, **close friends Mary L. Nye, Mary Jo Nye, Judy Brown, and Barbara Hawthorne were killed** in their vehicles at the Cracker Barrel parking lot. A 14-year-old who was also in one of the cars remained on life support at Bronson Hospital.

Gettys expressed the **profound shock and grief** gripping West Michigan and the nation, stating, "This is just the most awful thing I can think of having happened." As victims and their families grapple with the senseless violence attributed to one man in the Kalamazoo community, Gettys reflected, "I never thought it would happen in my lifetime." When asked about her feelings on Sunday, Joi Coleman simply responded, **"Scared."**

### A Plea for Privacy and Healing

On Wednesday, the Kalamazoo County Sheriff's Office released a statement regarding the victim from The Meadows Apartments. The family has requested that her name and any related information **not be released**, as they are not in a condition to speak with the media. They have indicated they will inform Sheriff Richard Fuller when they are ready. The Sheriff's Office urged the media and concerned parties to **allow the family the necessary time and space** to cope with the situation and focus on recovery. The victim's condition remains serious.

**Uber Faces Scrutiny Over Driver Safety Following Kalamazoo Shootings**

In the wake of a tragic mass shooting in Kalamazoo, Michigan, ride-hailing giant Uber is once again under intense scrutiny regarding its driver safety protocols. A driver, Jason Dalton, has been charged with murder in connection with the deaths of six people. Uber, a rapidly expanding San Francisco-based company, **defended its driver screening process**, asserting that it could not have foreseen Dalton's alleged actions.

Uber maintains that its **safety procedures are robust and do not require modification**. However, some experts suggest the company needs to implement more stringent vetting for potential drivers to reassure passengers of their safety. These experts acknowledge that Dalton, who had no prior criminal record, would likely not have raised any red flags during a standard background check.

Dalton, who had been driving for Uber for less than a month, is accused of carrying out the shootings over a nearly five-hour period on Saturday night. The Kalamazoo County prosecutor indicated that Dalton **picked up Uber passengers both before and after the initial shootings**.

Uber's Chief Security Officer, Joe Sullivan, stated that Dalton passed a background check and was approved as a driver on January 25th. He had completed over 100 rides and maintained an impressive rating of 4.73 stars out of five. Sullivan emphasized that, prior to Saturday's events, Uber had **no indication of any issues with Dalton's behavior**. He further commented, "No background check would have flagged and anticipated this situation."

Despite Uber's assurances, this incident has reignited concerns about the company's security measures. Earlier this month, Uber agreed to a **$28.5 million settlement** to resolve lawsuits alleging deceptive practices regarding safety procedures and fees. Additionally, the company is facing a separate lawsuit from district attorneys in San Francisco and Los Angeles, who claim Uber's vetting process failed to prevent the hiring of several individuals with felony convictions.

Past incidents have also cast a shadow over Uber's safety record. In Massachusetts, an Uber driver was **sentenced to prison for raping a passenger**. Around the same time, a teacher working as an Uber driver in South Carolina was accused of raping a passenger. In India, an Uber driver received a life sentence for raping a passenger, leading to Uber's temporary ban in New Delhi.

In response to these legal challenges, Uber has **ceased using marketing phrases** such as "safer than a taxi" and "industry-leading background checks." Nevertheless, the company insists that riders are protected and highlights its unique feature of allowing passengers to rate drivers. Ed Davis, a former police commissioner and member of Uber's Safety Advisory Board, stated, "The system that Uber has is extremely safe."

However, critics like Matthew Daus, a former New York City Taxi Commissioner and transportation lawyer, argue that Uber's name-based background checks are **less effective than fingerprint checks** that access the FBI criminal history database. Daus points out that name checks can miss records if individuals use aliases or if their names differ slightly from official records. Uber disputes this, arguing that FBI records can be misleading if charges were dropped. Sullivan stated that Uber reviews court records for up to seven years and conducts in-person checks for non-online records. Both sides agree, however, that Dalton would not have been flagged due to his lack of a criminal record.

Daus also expressed concern that Uber controls the background check information, whereas fingerprint-based checks conducted by government agencies are public records.

Sullivan indicated that Uber became aware of issues with Dalton's driving on Saturday, receiving **complaints about dangerous and erratic driving** from several passengers. He noted that Uber typically suspends drivers accused of violent acts but usually contacts drivers first in cases of erratic driving to hear their side. It remains unclear if Uber contacted Dalton on Saturday night.

Uber passenger Matt Mellen reported that he called the police to report Dalton's erratic driving over an hour before the shootings began and also attempted to report him to Uber.

Allen Adamson, founder of Brand Simple Consulting, believes that driver behavior is a critical issue Uber must address as it continues to grow. With millions of rides occurring daily worldwide, Adamson stressed the importance of **demonstrating rigorous screening procedures** to assure the public that drivers are safe and trustworthy. He warned that while isolated incidents may be overlooked, repeated issues could significantly damage the brand.

Despite these concerns, some customers remain confident in Uber's safety measures. Ian Leavitt, a medical research assistant, stated he will continue using Uber, believing the company is doing all it can as long as drivers are screened for criminal records or links to terrorism. He acknowledged that **background checks cannot predict future behavior**, especially concerning individuals with mental health issues who have not previously acted out.

### Uber's Handling of Driver Complaint Before Kalamazoo Shootings

Uber is under intense scrutiny after it was revealed that a **complaint about driver Jason Dalton's "dangerous driving" was received and not acted upon** before his shooting spree that killed six people in Michigan. The rider, Matt Mellen, reported Dalton for **erratic behavior**, even calling 911 after exiting the vehicle. Uber confirmed this feedback, received about five hours before the first victim was killed, had **not been reviewed**.

#### Rider's Account of Dangerous Driving

Mellen described the terrifying incident to CNN affiliate WWMT. He recounted how Dalton began driving erratically after a phone call. "We were kind of driving through medians, driving through the lawn speeding along and then finally, once he came to a stop, I jumped out of the car and ran away," Mellen stated. This **explicit report of dangerous driving** was sent to Uber.

#### Uber's Safety Feedback Prioritization Challenges

An Uber spokesperson acknowledged the complaint but emphasized the company's difficulty in prioritizing feedback from **millions of daily rides**. "Remember we’re doing 3 million rides a day. How do you prioritize that feedback and how do you think about it?" the spokesperson asked. Uber explained that complaints involving **direct threats of violence**, such as "the driver assaulted me," lead to **immediate driver suspension**. However, feedback concerning general "bad driving" is handled differently.

#### Differentiating Types of Driving Complaints

The spokesperson clarified that complaints about **erratic driving are not automatically prioritized for immediate action**. "Because we get a lot of people saying bad driving and there’s two sides to that and sometimes, it’s not fair to deactivate them or not give them a warning," they explained. This particular complaint "fell into that back bucket." Uber's policy is **not to immediately deactivate drivers for erratic driving** due to the high volume of such complaints and a desire to avoid unfair consequences.

#### Process for Handling Driving Complaints

"The reason we do that, just to be clear, is because with something like bad driving, we’re not going to take away someone’s ability to earn a living without talking to them first," the spokesperson stated. When asked if Uber had reached out to Dalton after receiving the complaint, the spokesperson confirmed, "**We had not gotten around to doing that, no.**"

#### Driver Background and Uber's Safety Measures

Jason Dalton had passed a background check and had been an Uber driver since January 25th, completing approximately 100 rides. Joe Sullivan, Uber’s chief security officer, stated that the driver's rampage could not have been predicted, noting Dalton's generally good rating of 4.73.

#### Law Enforcement Cooperation and Investigation

Kalamazoo County sheriff Richard Fuller confirmed that Uber is cooperating with law enforcement and is expected to assist in filling in timeline gaps. Investigators are particularly interested in any communication between Dalton and Uber, as well as with his past customers. The motive for the attacks remains unclear, and the victims had no apparent connection to the gunman or to each other.

#### Timeline of the Kalamazoo Shootings

The attacks began Saturday evening outside the Meadows apartment complex, where a woman was shot. Approximately four hours later and 15 miles away, a father and his 17-year-old son were shot while at a car dealership. Fifteen minutes after that, five people were shot in the parking lot of a Cracker Barrel restaurant, with four of them succumbing to their injuries.

#### Uber's Gun Policy

Uber's policy **prohibits both passengers and drivers from possessing any type of firearm within a vehicle**. Violators of this policy may face prohibition from using or driving for the service.

In Kalamazoo, Michigan, a chilling series of events unfolded on February 20, 2016, when Jason Dalton, an Uber driver, allegedly carried out a spree of shootings that left six people dead and several others injured. The attacks, which spanned several hours and multiple locations, have left the community and authorities grappling with the profound mystery of Dalton's motive. Despite his admission to investigators that he "took people's lives," the reasons behind his seemingly random acts of violence remain elusive, deepening the tragedy for the victims and their families.

The timeline of the attacks is particularly disturbing, as Dalton reportedly continued to ferry passengers for Uber between the shootings. This detail has raised serious questions about the ride-sharing company's awareness of his erratic behavior and their response to customer complaints. While Uber acknowledged receiving reports of Dalton's unsafe driving on the day of the attacks, the company has remained tight-lipped about whether they directly communicated with him about these concerns. This lack of transparency has fueled public scrutiny and added another layer of complexity to an already devastating situation.

Jason Dalton, a 45-year-old former insurance adjuster, appeared in court via video link and was formally charged with six counts of murder, offenses that carry a mandatory life sentence. A judge denied bail, underscoring the severity of the alleged crimes. The fact that Dalton admitted to the killings, yet the motive remains unknown, presents a significant challenge for law enforcement and prosecutors. Kalamazoo County Prosecutor Jeff Getting acknowledged the profound impact of the violence on the community, stating, "We've lost six people, we have a 14-year-old girl clinging to life, we have another victim who is recovering, thankfully, ... we have a high school that's lost a classmate. And no one understands why it happened."

One of the earliest accounts of Dalton's erratic behavior came from a passenger, Matt Mellen, who hailed an Uber ride around 4:30 p.m. on the day of the shootings. Mellen recounted that Dalton, who introduced himself as "Me-Me" and had a dog in the backseat, began driving recklessly about a mile into their trip after receiving a phone call. Mellen described Dalton blowing through stop signs, sideswiping cars, driving through medians and lawns, and speeding. Fearing for his safety, Mellen jumped out of the moving vehicle and immediately called the police. He later learned that his fiancée had posted a warning on Facebook about the incident.

Following Dalton's arrest, several individuals have come forward claiming to have been passengers of his Uber rides in the hours after the first attack. While the Associated Press has not been able to independently confirm these accounts, Kalamazoo County Sheriff Richard Fuller stated that Uber is cooperating with law enforcement and is expected to help fill in crucial timeline gaps. Investigators are particularly interested in any communication records between Dalton and Uber, as well as details about his passengers. Uber confirmed that Dalton passed a background check and began driving for the company on January 25, having completed approximately 100 rides prior to the attacks.

The first shooting occurred outside the Meadows apartment complex on the eastern edge of Kalamazoo County, where 25-year-old Tiana Carruthers was shot multiple times but survived. Sheriff Fuller noted that Carruthers displayed remarkable bravery, sensing danger when Dalton's car pulled up and placing herself between his vehicle and several children she was supervising, urging them to run to safety. Prosecutor Getting has dismissed the notion that Dalton was targeting a specific individual at the complex, explaining that Dalton used a different name to "get her attention" before opening fire.

Approximately four hours later and 15 miles away, a father and his 17-year-old son were fatally shot while browsing vehicles at a car dealership. Tragically, just fifteen minutes after that, five people were gunned down in the parking lot of a Cracker Barrel restaurant, resulting in four fatalities. Initial reports indicated that 14-year-old Abigail Kopf was among the deceased after sustaining a gunshot wound to the head. However, in a glimmer of hope, she later squeezed her mother's hand and remained hospitalized in critical condition.

The question of motive and Dalton's state of mind continues to be the most challenging aspect of this investigation. Sheriff Fuller articulated the community's struggle to comprehend the senseless violence, stating, "In the end, I ask people, because I keep hearing this question of why, 'What would be the answer that would be an acceptable answer for you?' They have to think about it for a moment, and they say, 'Probably nothing.' I have to say, 'You are probably correct.' I can't imagine what the answer would be that would let us go, 'OK, we understand now.'" The ongoing investigation aims to piece together the events of that tragic night, hoping to provide some answers, however insufficient, to a community shattered by inexplicable violence.

This report was compiled with contributions from Associated Press writers Tom Krisher in Saline, Michigan; Dee-Ann Durbin in Ann Arbor, Michigan; Ed White in Detroit; and Don Babwin in Chicago.

### Passenger Recounts Terrifying Encounter with Suspected Kalamazoo Gunman

Hours before the alleged shooting spree that rocked Kalamazoo County, a local man, Matt Mellen, found himself in a terrifying situation as a passenger in the car of suspected gunman Jason Dalton. Dalton, who was reportedly working as an Uber driver before, during, and after the alleged attacks, picked up Mellen on Saturday night. Mellen recounts that from the moment he entered the vehicle, he sensed something was amiss.

#### A Disturbing Ride

Dalton, who introduced himself as "Me-Me" instead of his Uber-listed name, had his dog in the back seat. The ride quickly escalated from unsettling to dangerous as Dalton began driving erratically, reaching speeds of 80 miles per hour down West Main Avenue and sideswiping other vehicles. Mellen describes Dalton driving through medians and lawns, running stop signs, and exhibiting increasingly alarming behavior.

"We got a mile from my house and then he got a telephone call and after he got that telephone call he started driving really erratically," Mellen stated. "We were running stop signs."

#### Escape and Warning

Fearing for his life, Mellen managed to escape the vehicle when Dalton eventually stopped. He immediately called the police and later informed his fiancée about the harrowing experience. His fiancée then took to Facebook, posting a warning with Dalton's picture to alert friends who might be using Uber that night. Mellen believes his encounter occurred around 4:30 PM, significantly before the first reported shooting by police, which they estimate took place around 6:00 PM.

"I'm upset because I tried contacting Uber after I had talked to the police, saying that we needed to get this guy off the road," Mellen expressed.

#### Others Narrowly Avoid Danger

Tragically, Mellen's warning may not have reached everyone in time. Another Newschannel 3 viewer reported being scheduled for a pickup by Dalton at 11:19 PM, by which time six people had already been shot.

Mark Dunton, Scott Dixon, and Kevin Leonard also recounted their unnerving experience of having Dalton pull up to pick them up for an Uber ride on Saturday night. Having heard about the shooting spree and the suspect's vehicle, they were understandably apprehensive when a car matching the description arrived. Dunton recalled a friend questioning Dalton, "this isn't the HHR, you aren't the guy are ya?" to which Dalton reportedly denied it.

"It's crazy to think that someone could have gone out and just done all these horrible things and came to work straight-faced -- like he's just coming to work doing his job and just didn't say much at all," Dunton commented.

An Indiana couple also shared their story of requesting an Uber that Saturday night and ending up in Dalton's car, even making a lighthearted, yet now chilling, joke to him, "'You're not the shooter are you?'"

#### Uber's Response

Uber released a statement expressing their horror and heartbreak over the "senseless violence" and offered their full cooperation with the police investigation. Dalton was apprehended by police in downtown Kalamazoo around 12:30 AM, shortly after reportedly driving three more passengers to a downtown bar. Mellen emphasized the need for a more rapid response system when Uber drivers exhibit dangerous behavior.

## Kalamazoo Shootings: Suspect Jason Dalton Appears in Court Amidst Community Shock

Kalamazoo County is reeling from a **devastating series of shootings**, with the first court appearance of **Jason Brian Dalton**, the man accused of the crimes, sending shockwaves through the community. Dalton faces **grave charges**, including multiple counts of murder and assault with intent to commit murder. Law enforcement described the situation as a **"worst nightmare"** due to the **sheer randomness and brutality** of the violence.

### The Alleged Pattern of **Deliberate Killings**

Jason Brian Dalton, a husband and father of two, is accused of **systematically taking the lives of eight individuals** across three separate locations on a single Saturday evening. The prosecuting attorney, Jeffrey Getting, characterized these acts not as impulsive outbursts, but as **"very deliberate killings,"** highlighting the **calculated nature** of the alleged crimes. Disturbingly, evidence suggests Dalton continued to operate as an **Uber driver between the horrific incidents**, accepting fares. Getting emphasized the **cold, calculated intent**, stating, **"He chose people and he shot them simply because he could."**

The formal indictment includes **six counts of murder, two counts of assault with the intent to commit murder, and eight firearms-related charges**. Detectives testified that Dalton, after being apprehended, admitted to **"taking people's lives."** Appearing remotely from jail, Dalton showed **little outward emotion** as the charges were read. **Bail was unequivocally denied**, reflecting the severity of the accusations. The only discernible connection between the victims was Dalton himself and a **semi-automatic pistol**, whose ballistics match evidence from the crime scenes. This lack of any other apparent link amplified the sense of **random terror**.

### Family's Plea and Law Enforcement's **Bewilderment**

The Dalton family released a statement expressing **deepest condolences** to the victims' families and pledging **complete cooperation** with the investigation, aiming to uncover the motive. Sheriff Richard Fuller corroborated their commitment but acknowledged the **significant void of clear answers** regarding the precipitating factors. "My best sense is that it was somebody who was having an issue at the time and for whatever reason they decided to do what they did," Fuller commented to CNN, reflecting law enforcement's **bewilderment**.

### A **Timeline of Terror**

The tragic sequence began around 3 p.m. on Saturday. Dalton visited a local firearms retailer, where he was a known customer. The owner described Dalton's demeanor as **unremarkable**, noting he purchased a jacket suitable for concealing a compact firearm. His departure was **without incident**.

#### First Attack: Apartment Complex

The first confirmed shooting occurred around 5:42 p.m. at an apartment complex. Law enforcement believes Dalton fired multiple times at Tiana Carruthers in the parking lot. Her children witnessed the attack, but **Carruthers is expected to recover**.

#### Escalation at the Dealership

Approximately four hours later, violence escalated at a car dealership. **Richard Smith, 53, and his son Tyler, 17, were fatally shot**. Tyler's girlfriend witnessed the brutal attack. Public Safety Director Jeff Hadley described this incident as **"cold blooded."**

#### Final Attack: Cracker Barrel

Dalton then proceeded to a Cracker Barrel restaurant, unleashing gunfire in the parking lot. This attack resulted in the deaths of **Dorothy Brown, 74; Barbara Hawthorne, 68; Mary Lou Nye, 62; and Mary Jo Nye, 60**. A 14-year-old girl, Abigail Kopf, sustained **critical injuries** but is showing responsiveness.

### Apprehension and **Discovery of Arsenal**

About two hours after the final shooting, Dalton was apprehended without incident in downtown Kalamazoo. A subsequent search of his residence uncovered **eleven rifles**, underscoring the arsenal available to the accused.

### Witness Accounts and **Uber's Troubling Role**

Matt Mellen, a passenger in Dalton's vehicle shortly before the shootings, recounted a disturbing experience. Mellen described Dalton receiving a phone call and operating the vehicle erratically, including **side-swiping another car and disregarding traffic signals**. Mellen implored Dalton to stop so he could exit, noting Dalton's **surprising composure**. Mellen successfully escaped and contacted 911. In another chilling account, an individual named Derek informed WOOD-TV that Dalton was his Uber driver. Derek nervously inquired if Dalton was the shooter, to which Dalton responded with a dismissive "no" and a head shake, attributing his fatigue to driving.

Uber confirmed Dalton had passed their background checks, and law enforcement stated he had **no prior criminal record**. "For all intents and purposes, he was your average Joe," Hadley remarked, emphasizing the suspect's seemingly ordinary nature. **"This was random,"** he reiterated, a statement that encapsulates the profound shock and lack of discernible motive leaving the community reeling. The investigation continues to delve into the complex circumstances that may have led to this **horrific tragedy**.

query: What circumstances led to James Harrison's 2008 arrest, and what were the outcomes of the charges against him?

documents.text: James Harrison believes he has grown since being arrested in 2008, according to Scott Brown of ESPN. Harrison, who recently signed a one-year deal with the Pittsburgh Steelers, was arrested for hitting his girlfriend and charged with simple assault and criminal mischief. Both charges were misdemeanors and were eventually dropped. "It was something that happened, an unfortunate situation, and right or wrong everybody makes mistakes," Harrison said following his first practice since re-signing with the Steelers on Tuesday. "I learned from it and grew from it and I'm where I'm at now." Before the Steelers brought Harrison back into the fold, head coach Mike Tomlin sat down and talked with Harrison on the current state of the league. The NFL has recently been rocked by scandals involving domestic violence, most notably cases including Ray Rice, Greg Hardy and Ray McDonald. Rice is under indefinite suspension and Hardy was placed on the exempt list, allowing him to be paid but not to participate with his team. McDonald is still playing for the San Francisco 49ers, who are waiting for the legal system to play out. Harrison could be active this Sunday against the Tampa Bay Buccaneers, although the amount of playing time he might receive is uncertain. Harrison won two Super Bowls in 2005 and 2008 with the Steelers, along with a Defensive Player of the Year award.

James Harrison, the veteran linebacker for the Pittsburgh Steelers, is making headlines not for his on-field performance, but for his parenting philosophy. Harrison recently took to Instagram to announce his decision to return two participation trophies his sons, James III and Henry, received from a local athletics event organized by former Steelers quarterback Charlie Batch.

### A Stance Against Participation Trophies

Harrison, a former NFL Defensive Player of the Year, expressed his pride in his elementary school-aged sons but firmly stated that these trophies would be returned. He believes that his sons should only receive accolades they have genuinely earned. Harrison articulated his reasoning, emphasizing that he doesn't want his children to develop a sense of entitlement. He explained, "cause sometimes your best is not enough, and that should drive you to want to do better." He shared his sentiment on social media, stating, "I came home to find out that my boys received two trophies for nothing, participation trophies!"

### Teammates Show Support

The linebacker's stance resonated with his Pittsburgh Steelers teammates. Several players voiced their approval of Harrison's decision. Linebacker Jordan Zumwalt told Channel 11’s Bill Phillips that the entire team room applauded the post, echoing the sentiment that "You don’t get trophies for participating." Zumwalt found Harrison's action unsurprising, remarking, "I laughed and thought it was a very James thing to do." Wide receiver Darius Heyward-Bey also expressed his support, telling Phillips, "He can raise his kids the way he wants to. I kind of agree with him. You know, in this world, you’ve got to earn things."

Best of the Batch Foundation is a nonprofit organization devoted to improving the lives of children and families in distressed communities by building character, self-esteem, and appreciation for education. Currently serving southwestern Pennsylvania, Best of the Batch Foundation does good works, such as scholarships to college-bound students and a yearly back-to-school BatchPack book bag distribution to kids and teachers. Best of the Batch also offers programming that couples sports with learning—such as the seven week summer basketball camp where participating boys and girls attend study halls where they read and discuss pre-selected books. Founded by NFL quarterback Charlie Batch in 1999, Best of the Batch Foundation embodies Charlie's values of dedication, leadership, and trust. Over the years, Charlie has leveraged his status as a Pittsburgh Steeler to attract and engage young people. Every program at Best of the Batch affirms the value of his personal motto--the 5 P's--in all aspects of life: proper preparation prevents poor performance. His passion is to unlock the potential of children and their families by providing resources to educate and empower them to give their best efforts in all they do...to be the best of the batch in life. CLICK HERE to download Best of the Batch of the Batch Foundation Fact Sheet pdf Let me say something about Charlie Batch. …The great thing about Charlie is that he’s not just a football player but really cares about this community. He established Best of the Batch Foundation to assist local youth and hosts Project C.H.U.C.K, an annual youth basketball league in Homestead. I can vouch for the fact that Charlie loves kids.—Dan Rooney

LATROBE, Pa. -- James Harrison has sacked quarterbacks 71.5 times in his career. Never has he struck something quite like this. What started as an Instagram post over the weekend has mushroomed into a parenting discussion about whether sports participation deserves a trophy. When CNN gets involved, the story has transcended normal NFL fandom. Does sports participation deserve a trophy? Let the parental debate begin! @kellywallacetv http://t.co/DXhDMQG41y pic.twitter.com/jo6Juqum3Y — CNN Health (@cnnhealth) August 18, 2015 Like most things, there’s more to the Harrison trophy story. His sons were recipients of student-athlete awards for good grades as part of the Best of the Batch Foundation, run by Harrison’s former teammate, now-retired quarterback Charlie Batch. Upholding a certain standard in the classroom is part of the program. That’s not what drew interest. Harrison’s words on the topic were pointed, and they came at a time when the everyone-gets-a-trophy notion had bubbled to the mainstream. If Harrison hits send on this message two years ago, the reaction might have been tame. Not now. Harrison told ESPN on Sunday he's successfully returned the trophies but didn't answer follow-up questions as he entered a loud haze of autograph-seekers at training camp. Harrison is taking a stand on something to which on some level every parent can relate. There doesn’t seem to be mass outrage against Harrison’s stance, or at least I haven’t seen that. After following the story on social media, many take the stand of indifference while appreciating why Harrison might feel this way. After all, he was a former Kent State walk-on has been cut twice and played a season in NFL Europe early in his career. One fan outside Steelers’ practice Sunday yelled to Harrison, “I appreciate what you’re teaching your sons.” ESPN’s Mike Greenberg from Mike and Mike used the story line to draw attention to the need for kids’ participation in activities, whether they receive trophies or not. By snapping a picture and posting the trophies on Instagram with his own message, Harrison has stirred a debate about the concept of rewarding children in athletics and whether benchmarks should be appropriate for those rewards. There doesn’t’ seem to a right or wrong answer. This story is all about timing and resonance. It's also emblematic of how sports coverage has shifted. A story like this can draw more interest than player injuries and coaching changes because it affects people on a human level. It also elicits the sort of he-did-what reaction that social media is made for. Who knows if Harrison will go anywhere else with this story, though holding up a Lombardi in six months would be an entertaining response.

**Returning participation trophies is a deliberate choice rooted in a strong belief that recognition should be earned through genuine achievement.** The author, while incredibly proud of their sons' efforts and dedicated to their ongoing encouragement, feels that participation trophies devalue true accomplishment. This decision is driven by the conviction that **all areas of life require demonstrable success to warrant reward.** The author is committed to avoiding the cultivation of entitlement in their children.

The author argues that simply giving one's best effort is **not always enough to justify an award.** Instead, this effort should be seen as a **springboard for improvement**, not a reason to expect unearned praise or rewards. This philosophy is a core part of the author's family values.

Steelers linebacker James Harrison wrote on his Instagram account that he is taking away participation trophies awarded to his two sons — ages 8 and 6 — until they 'EARN a real trophy.' (Photo: Charles LeClaire, USA TODAY Sports) Life isn't always fair. You can work your hardest, try your best, expend every ounce of energy you have and sometimes things just don't work out the way you hoped or imagined. That's just the way things go. Yet somewhere along the way, someone had the misguided notion that kids should live in a la-la land where everything is perfect, there are no hardships or heartbreaks, and you get a shiny trophy or a pretty blue ribbon just for being you. There's time enough to get acquainted with reality, the thinking goes. In the meantime, children should be praised and encouraged, reminded at every turn how wonderful they are. No wonder study after study has shown that millennials, the first of the trophy generations, are stressed out and depressed. They were sold a bill of goods when they were kids, and discovering that the harsh realities of life apply to them, too, had to have been like a punch to the gut. Pittsburgh Steelers linebacker James Harrison may be the last person you want to take life lessons from, given his history of violence on and off the field. But his announcement Sunday that he was giving back his 8- and 6-year-old son's "participation" trophies because they hadn't earned them was dead on, and that message shouldn't be discounted simply because he was the one delivering it. "While I am very proud of my boys for everything they do and will encourage them till the day I die, these trophies will be given back until they EARN a real trophy," Harrison said in a post on Instagram. "I'm sorry I'm not sorry for believing that everything in life should be earned and I'm not about to raise two boys to be men by making them believe that they are entitled to something just because they tried their best." Amen. Everybody-gets-a-trophy proponents say children should be rewarded for their efforts, that the prizes give kids incentive to always try their best and persevere. But isn't that what the orange slices and cookies are for? By handing out trophies and medals at every turn, it actually sends the opposite message, essentially telling kids it's enough just to show up. Why should a kid strive to improve or put in the extra effort when he or she is treated no differently than the kid who sits in the outfield picking dandelions? Or, as NFL MVP Kurt Warner said on Twitter on Monday, "They don't let kids pass classes 4 just showing up!" "The whole idea is to protect that kid and, ultimately, it’s a huge disservice. What kids need is skill-building. Help them do what they’re doing and do it better," said Ashley Merryman, co-author of Top Dog: The Science of Winning and Losing. "The benefit of competition isn’t actually winning. The benefit is improving," Merryman added. "When you're constantly giving a kid a trophy for everything they’re doing, you're saying, 'I don’t care about improvement. I don't care that you're learning from your mistakes. All we expect is that you’re always a winner.' " And if you've taken a peek at any 9-year-old's room recently, you'll see how much those precious trophies and ribbons really mean. Most are either coated in dust or buried in the back of a closet. If we're honest with ourselves, the trophies, ribbons and medals we hand out so willingly are more about us than the children getting them. It's affirmation that our kids are as wonderful as we think they are. It's also a way to fool ourselves into thinking that we're sheltering them, at least temporarily, from the cold, cruel world. But real life is hard, and no amount of trophies can shield kids from the disappointments and challenges they'll eventually face. "I like kids. I want them to be happy and do well," said Merryman, who has mentored Olympic athletes. "But I'd much rather have a 6-year-old cry because he didn’t get a medal than have a 26-year-old lose it because they realized they weren’t as special as they thought they were." Learning the true values of hard work, perseverance and resilience, that's the real reward. All other trophies pale in comparison. VIDEO: Examining the youth sports trophy culture

**James Harrison's Trophy Return: A Closer Look at "Best of the Batch" Awards**

Pittsburgh Steelers linebacker **James Harrison** recently returned participation trophies given to his sons, sparking a debate about modern "everyone's a winner" culture. While this action might seem to criticize all participation awards, the situation is more nuanced. Harrison was aware of the trophies' nature and **consciously chose** to return them. This is key, as the meaning of a participation trophy can differ. What some see as a simple token for showing up, others might view as **significant recognition of effort and achievement**.

Initially, Harrison's social media post resonated. As a parent, I often tell my children that in competition, there's only one winner. This aligns with Harrison's view on the **harmful societal issue of entitlement**. Children need to learn to value both true achievement and consistent effort. The idea that good results will come without hard work is **unrealistic and potentially damaging**.

However, a closer look at the trophies revealed an inscription: "**Best of the Batch**." This detail immediately brought to mind **Charlie Batch's foundation**. While I agreed with Harrison's core principle, I knew about Charlie Batch's foundation and its **strict requirements**. Children often must attend mandatory tutoring to join their activities. Opportunities for enjoyable competition are actually **dependent on demonstrated achievement and sustained effort** – qualities Harrison himself would likely support.

While Harrison's children are well-off, many student-athletes benefiting from Charlie Batch's foundation face **food insecurity**. This is a stark reality. The foundation provides essential after-school snacks, helping children focus on sports, studies, or activities without hunger as a distraction.

For many in the "Best of the Batch" program, **basic survival requires effort**. They actively work to get necessities like clothing, shelter, and food. It's in this context that Batch's sports programs, including the Next Level Athletics Track Club where Harrison's children participated, operate. The program aims to boost academic development, self-esteem, and confidence. However, Charlie Batch originally founded the program simply "to get the kids off the streets and give them something to do." Though the program has grown, its **fundamental values remain strong**.

Achieving good grades and participating in sports can be a major accomplishment for a young person facing challenges like hunger and an unstable home. Therefore, these trophies were **not given for just showing up**. The trophies James Harrison returned are powerful tools for valuable life lessons. They represent the **opposite of entitlement**, showing that **hard work leads to real rewards** and that dedication to both sports and school is highly valuable. These trophies can inspire motivation and encourage these children to start their own lifelong journeys of effort and achievement, much like a Super Bowl ring signifies to James Harrison.

query: What did President Donald Trump say about the suspected chemical attack in Syria and whom did he blame for the situation?

documents.text:
**Trump Denounces Syrian Chemical Attack, Criticizes Obama's Past Response**

Following a suspected chemical weapons attack in Syria, President Donald Trump strongly condemned the actions of the Bashar al Assad regime, calling them "heinous" and unacceptable globally. Trump directly linked the current events to the policies of the previous administration, stating, "These heinous actions by the Bashar al Assad regime are a consequence of the past administration's weakness and irresolution." He specifically recalled a 2012 declaration by then-President Barack Obama, which set a "red line" against the use of chemical agents, and noted the absence of subsequent enforcement.

**Allegations and the Syrian Civil War**

The Syrian government faces accusations of orchestrating the attack in a rebel-held territory, an incident that claimed the lives of at least 58 individuals, according to the Syrian Observatory for Human Rights. This event stands as one of the most severe assaults during the ongoing six-year Syrian civil war. In 2013, President Obama had previously warned Assad against employing chemical weapons against his own populace. However, a decision was ultimately made to refrain from intervention, partly due to concerns that removing Assad could potentially empower more extremist factions.

**Trump's Evolving Position**

President Trump's statement underscored the severity of the situation: "Today's chemical attack in Syria against innocent people including women and children is reprehensible and cannot be ignored by the civilized world." While the White House has not yet outlined specific retaliatory measures, Trump's current stance marks a distinct shift from his 2013 counsel to President Obama, when he advised against military engagement, suggesting it was prudent to "save your 'powder' for another day."

**Political Responses and Demands for Action**

This developing situation has elicited responses from notable political figures. Senator John McCain, a strong proponent of arming Syrian opposition groups, declared that the Trump administration would be held responsible if the United States failed to act. "I want to hear him say we’re going to arm the Free Syrian Army, we’re going to dedicate ourselves to the removal of Bashar al Assad," McCain commented in a CNN interview. He further emphasized the moral obligation, stating, "We will not sit by and watch chemical weapons being used to slaughter innocent women and children." The international community now anticipates further developments and potential responses to this critical humanitarian crisis.

President Trump is flanked by Secretary of State Rex Tillerson to his left and Defense Secretary Jim Mattis, right, at the White House on Thursday. (Olivier Douliery/European Pressphoto Agency) President Trump released a statement Tuesday on the apparent use of chemical weapons that have killed dozens of people in Syria. It is full of sound and fury — at the Obama administration — and it signifies almost nothing. Here's what Trump said: Today’s chemical attack in Syria against innocent people, including women and children, is reprehensible and cannot be ignored by the civilized world. These heinous actions by the Bashar al-Assad regime are a consequence of the past administration’s weakness and irresolution. President Obama said in 2012 that he would establish a “red line” against the use of chemical weapons and then did nothing. The United States stands with our allies across the globe to condemn this intolerable attack. First off, the statement reads like something that you would put out in the heat of the campaign. Half of it is devoted to what the past administration did and didn't do. Certainly the Obama administration took heat — and most would say deservedly so — for not holding to its “red line” policy on Syrian President Bashar al-Assad using chemical weapons. But two full sentences — out of four? Second is the fact that Trump himself in 2013 urged Obama not to enforce that red line. To wit: AGAIN, TO OUR VERY FOOLISH LEADER, DO NOT ATTACK SYRIA - IF YOU DO MANY VERY BAD THINGS WILL HAPPEN & FROM THAT FIGHT THE U.S. GETS NOTHING! — Donald J. Trump (@realDonaldTrump) September 5, 2013 President Obama, do not attack Syria. There is no upside and tremendous downside. Save your "powder" for another (and more important) day! — Donald J. Trump (@realDonaldTrump) September 7, 2013 But even more conspicuous than that, the statement takes a harsh tone toward the Obama administration without saying what the Trump administration will do differently. The applicable Trump policy here, in fact, appears even less stringent than Obama's was: It's leaving Assad in power in the name of fighting the Islamic State (ISIS) first. As recently as last week, both Secretary of State Rex Tillerson and U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations Nikki Haley both signaled that Assad would be left alone. “Are we going to sit there and focus on getting him out? No,” Haley said. Tillerson said during a trip to Turkey, meanwhile, that he thought the “longer-term status of President Assad will be decided by the Syrian people.” Doctors and activists in rebel-held areas have blamed the Syrian government for a sharp increase in chemical attacks since the end of last year. (The Washington Post) Obama set a red line and then didn't live up to it; the Trump administration's policy is apparently to talk tough without dictating any specific policies — except that Obama's policies were wrong (even though Trump agreed with them in the end). The White House, of course, explains that it doesn't want to tip off the enemy before any action is taken. “I think the president has made it clear in the past, and will reiterate that today, that he is not here to telegraph what we're going to do,” press secretary Sean Spicer said earlier Tuesday. That's a great excuse for not having a real strategy, which the Trump administration doesn't seem to have.

Survivors of a **devastating airstrike in Syria** have provided harrowing accounts of **chemical bombs being dropped from planes**, directly contradicting the Assad regime's narrative of the dawn attack. This strike, one of the deadliest in the six-year Syrian war, claimed the lives of at least **70 people, including children**.

While Syria has **denied using chemical weapons**, and Russia asserted the deaths resulted from a gas leak after a regime airstrike hit a "terrorist" chemical weapons factory, survivors treated in a Turkish hospital near the border told CNN they **witnessed chemical bombs descending from the sky**. The World Health Organization stated that victims displayed signs consistent with **exposure to nerve agents**, and Amnesty International concluded that evidence pointed to an **"air-launched chemical attack."** International agencies are actively investigating the origin of the agents used.

**Key Developments:**

*   A chemical weapons expert deemed Russia's explanation "highly implausible."
*   US President Donald Trump stated the attack **"changed his views on Syria and Assad."**
*   Medical experts indicated the attack was likely caused by a **nerve agent, such as sarin gas.**
*   The UN Security Council convened an emergency meeting but took no immediate action.

**A War of Words and Witness Accounts:**

A child receives treatment in an Idlib province hospital following the suspected chemical attack. President Trump condemned the attack on innocent civilians as an **"affront to humanity,"** emphasizing that it had significantly altered his perspective on Syria and its leader. "These heinous actions by the Assad regime cannot be tolerated," Trump declared. "It crossed a lot of lines for me. When you kill innocent children, innocent babies... with a chemical gas that is so lethal that people were shocked to hear what gas it was, that crosses many, many lines -- beyond a red line."

The Russian Defense Ministry claimed a Syrian airstrike hit "workshops, which produced chemical warfare munitions" on the outskirts of Khan Sheikhoun, alleging that "terrorists" were transporting these munitions. However, **Kaszeta, managing director of Strongpoint Security**, stated, "Nerve agents are the result of a very expensive, exotic, industrial chemical process -- these are not something you just whip up." He added, **"It's much more plausible that Assad, who's used nerve agents in the past, is using them again."**

Hours after the initial attack, another airstrike hit near a hospital in the same town, where survivors were being treated. The hospital was subsequently rendered out of service, according to the Syrian Civil Defense rescue group, known as the White Helmets.

**Victim's Testimony: 'I don't know if my family is dead or alive'**

Mazin Yusif, a 13-year-old boy, recounted his harrowing experience from Reyhanli Hospital in southern Turkey. He described seeing a plane drop a bomb and then running to his roof, witnessing the strike near his grandfather's house. He found his grandfather slumped over and, while trying to call for help, became dizzy and fainted, only to wake up in the hospital.

Aisha al-Tilawi, 55, who lost three family members, described seeing **blue and yellow colors** after a chemical-laden bomb was dropped. "We started choking, felt dizzy, then fainted. Mazin was trying to wake up his grandfather. Three of my family died," she said, receiving oxygen.

Ahmed Abdel Rahim, 31, expressed his uncertainty about his family's fate. He stated he was hit by a poisonous substance from three rockets, experiencing breathing difficulties and vomiting. "I don't know if my family is dead or alive. I don't know anything," he said.

**Worldwide Condemnation and International Scrutiny:**

The WHO indicated that some victims exhibited symptoms consistent with exposure to **nerve agents**, a conclusion echoed by Amnesty International, which stated victims were "very likely" exposed to compounds like sarin.

Photos displayed at a UN Security Council meeting showed children foaming at the mouth, presented as evidence by US Ambassador Nikki Haley. She urged Russia to use its influence to end these "horrific acts" and hinted at potential US military action if the UN failed to act.

Russia, a key ally of Syria, blocked a resolution condemning the use of chemical weapons and demanding flight logs. Russia has previously used its veto power on Syrian resolutions multiple times. The UN Security Council concluded its emergency meeting without a vote on the draft resolution.

Syria's deputy ambassador to the UN, Mounzer Mounzer, **categorically denied responsibility**, blaming "terrorist groups" and reaffirming that the Syrian Arab Army possesses no chemical weapons.

The Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons is gathering evidence. UK Foreign Secretary Boris Johnson stated, **"All the evidence I have seen suggests that it was the Assad regime who did it, in full knowledge they were using illegal weapons in a barbaric attack on their own people."**

Assad's military has consistently denied using chemical weapons, attributing reported attacks to "terrorist" groups. However, the capacity for aerial bombardments is believed to be beyond rebel or terrorist groups. A 2016 UN investigation found that chemical weapons had been used by both the national air force and ISIS.

The Syrian Coalition drew parallels between this attack and a 2013 incident in eastern Ghouta, where sarin was reportedly used, killing approximately 1,400 people.

President Donald Trump strongly condemned a chemical weapons attack in Syria that caused many deaths, directly blaming Syrian President Bashar al-Assad. Despite international calls for stronger U.S. action, including from France, Trump's statement on Tuesday did not specify any retaliatory steps.

Trump described the assault in Syria's Idlib province as "reprehensible and cannot be ignored by the civilized world." He also criticized his predecessor, Barack Obama, stating, "These heinous actions by the Bashar al-Assad regime are a consequence of the last administration's weakness and irresolution." Trump elaborated, "President Obama said in 2012 that he would establish a 'red line' against the use of chemical weapons and then did nothing." The Syrian military has denied any involvement, asserting it would never employ chemical weapons.

This chemical weapons incident, which claimed the lives of many, including children, occurred just one week after Secretary of State Rex Tillerson and U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations Nikki Haley indicated that the administration's primary focus in Syria was combating Islamic State militants, not compelling Assad's departure. A senior Trump administration official confirmed on Tuesday that policy options were under review following the Idlib attack, but acknowledged limitations and reiterated the existing strategic priorities. "Nothing has changed in terms of that dynamic. Our ability to get rid of him doesn’t rise because he does something terrible," the official stated.

France's foreign minister suggested the suspected chemical attack was a test of the Trump administration's resolve and urged Washington to clarify its stance on Assad. "The question is to know, yes or no, whether the Americans support a political transition in Syria, which means organizing this transition, elections and that at the end of the process the question of Assad's departure is asked," Jean-Marc Ayrault told RTL radio. A representative of the Syrian opposition viewed the attack as a "direct consequence" of recent U.S. pronouncements regarding Assad.

Another senior U.S. official indicated that key allies Saudi Arabia and Turkey had signaled that the militant group represented a higher priority than Assad's removal, and that his ouster could exacerbate regional instability.

**'CLEARLY A WAR CRIME'**

Nevertheless, the nature of the attack significantly impacted the new Trump administration. "If it’s what it looks like, it is clearly a war crime," remarked a senior State Department official, noting that information gathering was ongoing.
