His strategy appears to be consolidating support on Super Tuesday and seeking a resurgence later in states like Maine and in the Rust Belt.

WELLINGTON, New Zealand (AP) — In a unique twist on the U.S. presidential election cycle, Democratic Party supporters in Wellington, New Zealand, were the first in the world to cast their ballots for the multi-state primaries known as "Super Tuesday." These overseas voters, registered Democrats residing in Wellington, cast their votes just after midnight on Tuesday, nearly a full day before their counterparts in the United States.

The voting took place in a decidedly informal setting: the Public Bar and Eatery. A total of 28 votes were cast. Senator Bernie Sanders secured a decisive victory in New Zealand, garnering 21 votes, while former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton received six. One ballot was spoiled. The official confirmation of these results will occur later this month at the global voter tally center located in Germany.

Wellington holds the distinction of being the inaugural city among 111 locations in 41 countries outside the U.S. where Democrats abroad can participate in the primary process. Notably, Republican voters overseas do not have a comparable mechanism to cast primary ballots.

As of this report, four states have already conducted their primaries or caucuses to determine each party's nominee for the November presidential election. On Super Tuesday itself, Democrats will be voting across 11 states and American Samoa, with 865 delegates up for grabs. Securing the party's nomination requires 2,383 delegates, and Democrats abroad are allocated 17 of these. For Republicans, Super Tuesday involves voting in 11 states, with 595 delegates available toward the 1,237 needed to clinch their party's nomination.

Kat Allikian, chair of Democrats Abroad New Zealand, explained that the expatriate American community in New Zealand comprises students, individuals in specialized professions, and retirees. She highlighted a significant motivator for these local voters: preventing a Republican victory in the White House, with particular concern directed at leading candidate Donald Trump. "The overwhelming concern of all Democrats overseas is this Trump phenomenon that is happening on the Republican side," Allikian stated. "It's mindboggling that he's running away with the nomination. Although it's not set yet."

Allikian expressed pride in leading the initial overseas vote. "People are really tickled that we're the very first in the world," she added.

As the calendar flips, March brings a whirlwind of states voting on the same days and in quick succession. By the middle of the month, 58 percent of the total delegates will have been awarded, and Mr. Trump could be unstoppable in getting the 1,237 needed to clinch the nomination. With the exception of Texas, the home state of Senator Ted Cruz, recent polls show Mr. Trump leading in the so-called Super Tuesday states that vote this week, including Alabama, Georgia, Massachusetts and Virginia. Though Texas has the most delegates of states voting on Tuesday, 155, they all award delegates proportionally, so that Mr. Cruz will most likely have to share the haul. The Southern states, with many evangelical Christians, have been the linchpin of Mr. Cruz’s strategy. But after Mr. Trump trounced him in South Carolina on Feb. 20, winning all 50 delegates, Mr. Cruz’s prospects are not as bright. Mr. Trump carries “enormous momentum,” Mr. Cruz admitted on Friday, and if he sweeps Super Tuesday, when more delegates are awarded than on any other day, “he could easily be unstoppable.” For Senator Marco Rubio of Florida, who ferociously challenged Mr. Trump last week in a debate and on the stump, mocking the real estate mogul’s “spray tan” and calling him a “con man,” the path forward is narrowing.

Donald Trump is poised for significant victories on Super Tuesday, strengthening his lead as the Republican front-runner and increasing pressure on his rivals. Top Republicans, including governors who held an emergency conference call to discuss Trump, anticipate he will win up to 10 states. This outcome would severely impact Texas Sen. Ted Cruz, who has focused on Southern states, and Florida Sen. Marco Rubio, who has yet to win a primary.

"If Trump wins 8 or more states on Tuesday, it would take a massive collapse for him not to be the nominee," stated Tony Fabrizio, a GOP pollster. Trump's path to the nomination has caused anxiety within the Republican establishment, with officials debating whether to endorse or oppose him.

Reflecting this concern, New Mexico Gov. Susana Martinez and Wisconsin Gov. Scott Walker, leaders of the Republican Governors Association, convened a call with fellow governors to discuss the primary's trajectory, with Trump as a central focus. During the call, Kentucky Gov. Matt Bevin asked New Jersey Gov. Chris Christie to explain his decision to endorse Trump. Christie defended his endorsement, citing his decade-long acquaintance with Trump and his confidence in Trump's leadership abilities.

The stakes are particularly high for Cruz, who had hoped to dominate Super Tuesday by appealing to conservative voters in states like Tennessee and Oklahoma. He had called March 1 the most crucial day of the primary calendar. However, with Trump's strong performance in the South, Cruz may only win his home state of Texas, a contest he has likened to the Battle of the Alamo. Trump has successfully appealed to Cruz's evangelical base, presenting himself as a devout Christian while labeling Cruz a "liar." Jerry Falwell Jr., an influential evangelical leader and Trump supporter, has recorded a robocall for the Trump campaign, criticizing Cruz for using "dirty tricks" and urging voters to choose Trump based on character over ambition.

The Cruz campaign has not yet revealed its post-Super Tuesday plans, but Cruz is expected to focus on deeply conservative states like Idaho, Louisiana, and Kansas in the following week. Rubio will concentrate on his home state of Florida, where a crucial winner-take-all primary on March 15 could determine his fate. Despite trailing Trump in polls, Rubio's team is committed to winning Florida, with top aides vowing not to lose the state. Rubio has been preparing for the Florida primary, deploying former RNC political director Clint Reed to assist his campaign there. Rubio's aides are also organizing donor meetings and a fundraising tour in South Florida.

Regardless of Tuesday's results, Cruz and Rubio's allies emphasize that Trump will still be far from securing the 1,237 delegates needed for the nomination. The 595 delegates available on Super Tuesday will be awarded proportionally, allowing Cruz and Rubio to accumulate delegates even without winning states. Trump currently holds 82 of the 133 delegates awarded so far. This suggests the primary race could extend for months.

"Donald Trump is the clear front-runner, and he’s in a very strong position," said Steve Munisteri, former Texas Republican chairman. "But it’s not over." The primary's structure favors Trump, who stands to gain from a delegate lead and a divided opposition if he wins most Super Tuesday states.

Rubio's allies are exploring a scenario where he could prevent Trump from securing a majority of delegates and then win the nomination at the convention in Cleveland. Mel Martinez, former RNC chairman and senator, acknowledged this "narrow way forward," believing that a majority of the party finds Trump "unpalatable" and would resist his nomination, potentially leading to a convention showdown. Cruz, however, dismissed this idea as an attempt by the establishment to anoint a preferred candidate.

Anti-Trump groups, like Our Principles PAC, are actively fundraising and planning ad campaigns in key March states. The PAC, led by former Mitt Romney aide Katie Packer, has secured donations from prominent Republicans, including Marlene Ricketts and Stan Hubbard. "The opposition to Trump is not going quietly into the night anytime soon," said Josh Holmes, former chief of staff to Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell. "The vast majority of Republicans are horribly dissatisfied with the current state of affairs and there is no sign whatsoever that fundraising or energy is dissipating for an alternative."

Some in Rubio's campaign are frustrated by the lack of endorsements from prominent party figures. While Mitt Romney and Jeb Bush have criticized Trump, neither has endorsed Rubio, which could unlock significant fundraising and consolidate party support. Many of Bush's former backers have shifted to Rubio, but some remain undecided. One Bush donor, Mel Sembler, stated he was focused on other issues.

However, some Bush supporters are backing Trump. Doug Ose, a former Republican congressman who fundraised for Bush, announced his endorsement of Trump, viewing him as a problem-solver and expressing dissatisfaction with Rubio's campaign tone. Ose believes Rubio could be a future leader but hopes he doesn't harm his prospects.

Super Tuesday is a **crucial day** in the U.S. presidential nominating contests, with a **large number of Americans** heading to the polls. This **pivotal event** gives voters a **significant chance** to shape the direction of both the Democratic and Republican parties. The leading candidates, **Hillary Clinton** for the Democrats and **Donald Trump** for the Republicans, are aiming for **decisive wins** that could **cement their paths** to their party nominations. Meanwhile, Clinton's challenger, **Bernie Sanders**, and Trump's rivals, **Marco Rubio** and **Ted Cruz**, are working to **keep the electoral competition alive** and their campaigns viable. This guide highlights the **key elements to watch** as voters across a dozen states cast their ballots on this **momentous Tuesday**.

### Super Tuesday Results Timeline and Voting Methods

The **Super Tuesday results** will unfold throughout the evening. **Early projections** are expected as polls close in various states. Georgia, Virginia, and Vermont are set to release their initial results at 7 p.m. EST. This will be followed by Alabama, Tennessee, Oklahoma, and Massachusetts at 8 p.m. Arkansas will announce its outcomes at 8:30 p.m., with Texas, Minnesota, and Colorado reporting at 9 p.m. Alaska's results are anticipated around midnight. States with **significant early voting**, such as Arkansas, Georgia, Tennessee, and Texas, may see **quicker calls**. Conversely, Vermont, Bernie Sanders' home state, is known for a **more deliberate reporting process**. Furthermore, Minnesota, Colorado, and Alaska, which use **caucuses**, might face **more logistical challenges** compared to states holding traditional primaries. Caucuses involve **local gatherings** of registered party members organized by the state party, while primaries are **state-administered elections** using secret ballots at polling stations, similar to the general election.

### Key Democratic Contenders and Their Strategies

On the Democratic side, a **primary focus** will be on Hillary Clinton's performance among **minority voters**, particularly in the Southern states. If she can **replicate the strong support** she received from Black voters in South Carolina, where she secured approximately 90% of their vote, her **trajectory toward the nomination will be significantly boosted**. For Bernie Sanders, success depends on his appeal to **younger voters**, not just the youngest demographic. In South Carolina, Sanders only carried voters under 30, a segment that may not be **large enough to guarantee him a decisive victory** at the Democratic Convention.

### Republican Candidates and Their Crucial Stakes

For Ted Cruz, Texas is considered his "**crown jewel**." After three consecutive third-place finishes in New Hampshire, South Carolina, and Nevada, a **disappointing Super Tuesday showing** in his home state could **severely damage his campaign**. A **substantial portion of the Texas vote** has already been cast through early balloting, with over 1.7 million voters participating, representing 12% of registered voters from both parties and approximately 7% of registered Republicans. Even with a victory in Texas, Cruz is **not guaranteed all 155 Republican delegates**, as the state awards them proportionally. Amidst Donald Trump's **expected strong delegate accumulation** on the Republican side, his rivals will be closely watching every result for **positive indicators** that might justify their continued participation. The question remains whether Marco Rubio can **demonstrate increased margins of support**, even without securing wins. Will Ted Cruz see **enhanced backing from evangelical voters**, a demographic from which Trump is already drawing significant support? And can the trailing candidates, John Kasich and Ben Carson, find any **encouraging takeaways** from the evening's results?

### The Southern Firewall and Beyond

With seven Southern states participating in Super Tuesday, a **key question** is whether Hillary Clinton can solidify the much-discussed "**Southern firewall**." If Bernie Sanders fails to show strength beyond his Northeastern base in Vermont and neighboring Massachusetts, it would signal **significant challenges** for his campaign.

### Signals for the General Election

A **significant indicator** of the evolving race will be the election-night speeches delivered by both Hillary Clinton and Donald Trump. Observers will be eager to see if they begin to **shift their focus** from primary rivals to a potential general-election matchup. **Exit poll data** will offer valuable insights into the candidates' **potential weaknesses**. Will Clinton continue to face challenges with voters who prioritize honesty? Will Trump continue to **underperform among Republican voters** seeking a candidate with strong general election prospects, as suggested by his performance against Rubio in this demographic?

### Delegate Count: The Ultimate Decider

Ultimately, the **Super Tuesday outcomes** will be determined by the **delegate count**. Approximately a quarter of all delegates are awarded on this day, with most states using a **proportional system**. Heading into Super Tuesday, Donald Trump had accumulated 82 delegates, followed by Ted Cruz with 18, Marco Rubio with 16, John Kasich with 6, and Ben Carson with 4. A total of **1,237 delegates are required** to secure the Republican nomination. On the Democratic side, Hillary Clinton began Super Tuesday with 546 delegates, which includes superdelegates—party leaders and members of Congress who can endorse any candidate. Bernie Sanders had 87 delegates. A total of **2,383 delegates are needed** to win the Democratic nomination.

This report was compiled with contributions from AP writers Stephen Ohlemacher in Washington and Will Weissert in Houston.

### How 9/11 Reshaped "New York Values" and American Conservatism

The **September 11th terrorist attacks** fundamentally changed how the nation viewed "New York values." While New Yorkers generally lean more liberal than the rest of America, the shared experience of 9/11 **profoundly shifted the national perspective**. The country witnessed the **inherent humanity and resilience of New York City** in a way that left a lasting impression.

#### A Moment of National Unity

In the wake of 9/11, typical political divisions seemed to disappear. For weeks, Americans were united, watching the news, mourning victims, and celebrating the **bravery of first responders and the strength of survivors**. This **shared national trauma** made it harder for voters, even in places like Iowa, to see New Yorkers as having fundamentally different values.

#### Trump's Strategic Use of 9/11

Donald Trump masterfully leveraged this shift. By referencing New York's spirit after 9/11, he subtly reminded conservatives that **all parts of America are valuable**. As David French of National Review noted, Trump's success against Ted Cruz highlighted a crucial point: **conservatives cannot afford to dismiss any segment of the country**. French observed that Trump, by "hearkening back to New York’s heart after 9/11, for a moment transcended politics. How easily we forget, but for weeks after the terror attacks, New York was America."

#### The "New York" Persona's Appeal

It's debatable whether Trump's "New York values" attacks would have been as effective without his skillful invocation of 9/11. Many qualities his supporters admire in him are **quintessentially New York City**: his larger-than-life personality, his **boldness**, his comfort with **self-promotion**, and his **direct, often confrontational style**. He is unafraid to be assertive, embraces conflict, and prioritizes **ruthlessness and victory**. These traits often resonate more strongly with his base than any perceived deviation from conservative ideology.

#### The Rise of the "Breitbartian" Movement

This phenomenon mirrors the admiration many on the right had for Andrew Breitbart, despite his own complexities. While the Tea Party movement fostered a libertarian wing, a new, ascendant faction on the right seems less focused on traditional conservatism or libertarianism and more on what can be called **"Breitbartian" principles**. This group is often more engaged in **cultural warfare** than traditional political battles, characterized by opposition to the left, Islamism, the establishment, and political correctness.

#### The Deciding Factor for the GOP?

The critical question for the Republican nomination may be whether this **"Breitbartian" faction is larger than the traditional evangelical and social conservative base**. Without the powerful reminder of 9/11, these latter groups might have been more critical of Trump's "New York values." The ultimate size and influence of these competing factions could **determine the outcome of Republican primaries**.

This is what Ted Cruz, who wasn’t just born out of town but out of the country, said about Donald Trump the other day: “Donald comes from New York and he embodies New York values.” And made it sound as if this was some variation of insulting Trump about his hair. DE BLASIO: CRUZ OWES AN APOLOGY FOR 'NEW YORK VALUES' INSULT Later Cruz kept digging, telling Megyn Kelly of “Fox News” that New York values weren’t Iowa values or New Hampshire values, as if he is suddenly as big an expert on values as he says he is on the Constitution. Finally on Thursday night he brought his cockeyed theories about the city to the Republican debate in South Carolina. The next time Cruz shows up here and treats the city like an ATM machine, he should make a side trip to the Bronx and explain himself there on New York values. (MARK KAUZLARICH/REUTERS) “Everybody understands that the values in New York City are socially liberal and pro-abortion and pro-gay marriage,” he said. “And focus on money and the media.” This was right before Trump turned and calmly took him apart again for insulting the city the way he has. TRUMP DEFENDS 'NEW YORK VALUES' AS CRUZ USES TERM AS INSULT Ted Cruz, who only comes here with his hand out, has decided that the most diverse city the world has ever known is filled with people who all think alike. He sounds in these moments like as slow a thinker as we have ever had run for President. If you are dumb enough to think that New York values are some sort of handicap in this presidential season, then you are as dumb and tone deaf as Jesse Jackson was calling the city “Hymietown,” as dumb as Gerald Ford was when he gave this paper the most famous front page in its history, the day he effectively told New York to drop dead. CUOMO CALLS CRUZ'S ATTACKS ON NEW YORK VALUES "OFFENSIVE" The next time Cruz shows up here he should tear himself away from his friends at Goldman Sachs and make a side trip to the Bronx and explain himself there on New York values; or to the Lower East Side, or Williamsburg, Brooklyn, or Jamaica, Queens, or 125th St. in Harlem. He should go talk to people in the Rockaways or Staten Island, the hardened survivors who bound together after Hurricane Sandy, about New York values. Or finally he should go all the way downtown, and back to September 2001. New York City became the capital of heroes on Sept. 11, 2001. (Shawn Baldwin/AP) Here is what New York values are: New York values are a young guy, a paralegal, literally giving somebody he doesn’t know the shirt off his back on a subway because winter has finally come to the city and brought freezing temperatures with it. New York values are the New York taxi driver who traveled three boroughs across four days to find the guy who had left $1,400 in his cab, so he could return the money to him. You know what that really was? It was the real life of a city that Ted Cruz knows nothing about. He is simply another tourist here, one constantly on the make. HATERS IN CHIEF:l FIND YOUR CANDIDATE BY WHAT THEY HATE The best of this city is the best of this country, and always has been. When it was hit in a way that no American city had ever been hit, it came together and rose up together in a moment as thrilling as any America had ever seen. "Well, look, he may shift in his new rallies to play 'New York, New York' because Donald comes from New York and he embodies New York values,’” Ted Cruz said of the bombastic billionaire GOP front-runner, who was born in Queens. (CHRIS KEANE/REUTERS) This wasn’t the civil rights movement, it was a different kind of movement, to lower Manhattan. That was where the city was stronger and better than it ever had been on Sept. 12, when in the words of the great Pete Hamill, the city first got to one knee, and then began to get up. We will never know how it would have gone in another American city. We just know how it went here. New York values? New York values are the ironworkers who carried their tools in backpacks and gym bags and, by God, walked over the Brooklyn Bridge on the night of Sept. 11, 2001, or all the way downtown from all the way uptown. HARRY SIEGEL: HE-MAN, REV. BACON AND NYC'S SILLY SEASON One of them I know simply said to a cop who didn’t want to let him get close to where the towers had been. And the guy said, “They need me. I cut steel.” Cruz didn't make things better for himself at the debate. Somehow he sounded even dumber about New York City as he talked about abortion and gay marriage and the rest of it, even as he applauded after Trump spoke to the greatness of the city. Cruz means what he says about New York values, clearly believes they are in opposition to the values on which he has based his campaign, whatever those actually are. Earlier on Thursday Rep. Pete King came right at Cruz like some guy he’d grown up with in Sunnyside, Queens, who needed a good slap. “Memo to Ted Cruz,” King said in a statement on Thursday. “New York values are the heroes of 9/11; the cops who fight terror; and the people you ask for campaign donations. Go back under a rock.” Cruz is a lightweight. He always has been, however well he is doing in caucuses in the heartland. He may do well there. He is just out of his class here. It is the other party that has a donkey as its mascot. But Cruz is the one who’s a career jackass. City to him: Get lost.

The **Republican presidential candidates** engaged in their most **heated and personal clashes** of the campaign season during a Thursday debate in North Charleston, South Carolina. The **"bromance" between Donald Trump and Ted Cruz appeared to be over**, as Trump told CNN's Dana Bash, "I guess the bromance is over." This **2.5-hour event**, sponsored by Fox Business Network, featured **intense exchanges** among the seven candidates on stage.

Cruz and Trump are locked in a **tight race for first place in Iowa**, with less than three weeks until the state's caucuses. Trump, however, holds a **significant national lead**. Amidst growing pressure for an **establishment alternative** to Trump and Cruz, **sparks also flew between Marco Rubio and Chris Christie**.

The highly anticipated **showdown between Trump and Cruz** took a few minutes to ignite, but when it did, it was **impactful**. Cruz **forcefully countered Trump's accusations** regarding his eligibility to be president, stemming from his birth in Canada – a controversy Trump has recently amplified. "Back in September, my friend Donald said he had his lawyers look at this in every which way," Cruz stated. "There was nothing to this birther issue." He added, **"Since September, the Constitution hasn't changed. But the poll numbers have."**

While Trump has shown animosity towards most rivals, his relationship with Cruz had been largely amicable for much of the campaign. This dynamic shifted as **Iowa's polls tightened** before the February 1 caucuses. Trump, aiming to erode Cruz's support in Iowa, has repeatedly questioned Cruz's status as a natural-born citizen, given his mother's U.S. citizenship. However, Cruz **turned the tables on Trump**, pointing out that some extreme interpretations of the citizenship requirement would deem Trump ineligible, as his mother was born in Scotland. **"On the issue of citizenship, Donald, I'm not going to use your mother's birth against you,"** Cruz declared. Trump retorted, **"Because it wouldn't work."**

Cruz wasn't the only candidate put on the defensive. In the debate's closing moments, **Marco Rubio sharply criticized Ted Cruz**, accusing him of hiding behind conservative values. Rubio highlighted a series of issues where he claimed Cruz had **"flip-flopped,"** including immigration and ethanol subsidies, stating, **"That is not consistent conservatism. That is political calculation."** When Cruz suggested Rubio was merely presenting "oppo research," Rubio retorted, **"It's your record."**

**'New York Values' Debate**

Trump appeared more dominant than in previous debates. In a memorable exchange, he responded to Cruz's recent accusation that Trump **"embodies New York values."** When asked to define this, Cruz described New Yorkers as tending towards "socially liberal" views and being focused on "money and media," suggesting **"not a lot of conservatives come out of Manhattan."** Trump delivered an **emotional rebuttal**, recalling the **resilience of New Yorkers** after the September 11, 2001, attacks. **"I saw something that no place on Earth could have handled more beautifully, more humanely than New York,"** Trump stated, emphasizing the city's strength and recovery. He concluded, **"We rebuilt downtown Manhattan and everybody in the world watched and everybody in the world loved New York and loved New Yorkers. And I have to tell you, that was a very insulting statement that Ted made."**

Cruz also faced criticism from **The New York Times** regarding alleged undisclosed loans during his Senate campaign. Cruz dismissed the report as a **"hit piece,"** acknowledging "paperwork errors" but asserting that if that was the best the Times had, they "better go back to the well."

**Alternatives to Trump and Cruz**

**Chris Christie and Marco Rubio** also clashed, each promoting their conservative credentials. Rubio accused Christie of aligning with President Barack Obama on issues like gun control, Common Core, and Planned Parenthood. Christie, in turn, **criticized Rubio's past statements**, suggesting he was being influenced by others, and highlighted his own executive experience as governor. **"This is the difference between being a governor and being a senator... when you're a senator, what you get to do is just talk and talk and talk,"** Christie remarked.

**Muslim Ban and Gun Control**

Candidates also debated Trump's controversial proposal to **temporarily ban Muslims from entering the U.S.** Trump defended the idea as necessary to combat ISIS and avoid "political correctness." However, **Jeb Bush urged Trump to reconsider**, warning that the ban would hinder efforts to build a coalition with Arab nations. **"All Muslims? Seriously? What kind of signal does that send to the rest of the world?"** Bush questioned. Other candidates, like Kasich and Christie, expressed support for **temporarily halting Syrian refugees**.

In response to Obama's executive actions on gun control, several candidates **passionately defended Second Amendment rights**. Rubio stated, **"I am convinced that if this president could confiscate every gun in America, he would."** Christie condemned the actions as undemocratic, calling it **"not a democracy, that's a dictatorship."**

**Undercard Debate**

Earlier in the day, **Carly Fiorina** stood out in a smaller debate, addressing national security, technology, and Hillary Clinton. Fiorina, whose candidacy has been marked by strong debate performances, delivered a sharp critique of Clinton, stating, **"I'm not a political insider. I haven't spent my lifetime running for office. And unlike another woman in this race, I actually love spending time with my husband."** She also commented on Trump's relationship with Vladimir Putin, asserting, **"Despite Donald Trump's bromance with Vladimir Putin... Russia is our adversary."**

Senator Ted Cruz is employing a new strategy in Iowa: **criticizing Donald Trump by invoking "New York values."** However, Cruz has **not clearly defined what these "New York values" actually are**, despite claiming Iowans understand them. This mirrors his past use of vague terms like "Washington cartel," making it **essential for Cruz to explain his exact meaning.**

### What Are "New York Values"?

What does Cruz mean by "New York values"? Could it refer to an **elite education**, something Cruz himself possesses with degrees from Princeton and Harvard? Or perhaps it points to New Yorkers' **embrace of diversity and tolerance**, making their city a welcoming place for immigrants. This is especially relevant as Trump's views on immigration have shifted from supporting legal immigration to opposing it, even for skilled workers vital to the economy.

Another interpretation is that Cruz is alluding to **crass materialism**. While this might be a factor, it's worth noting that Cruz also seeks donations from New York, where he might adopt a different stance on issues like abortion compared to his Iowa audiences. This suggests **inconsistent messaging.**

### Personal Attacks and Political Tactics

Is Cruz hinting at Trump's **multiple marriages**? While this attack might not be effective in Iowa, Cruz should be direct if he intends to use it. Even though divorce is less stigmatized today, Cruz needs to clearly state if he believes Trump's marital history reflects a deeper issue with his relationships with women.

Cruz seems to be attempting a **two-pronged approach: criticizing Trump without specific accusations or direct confrontation.** Many voters might not see this as a sign of strength. Beyond these indirect criticisms, some Republicans tend to dismiss large cities and entire states as not representing "real America." Cruz's claim that New York values differ from Iowa values fits this pattern, suggesting an **inability to connect with people outside their political base.**

### The Dangers of Divisive Language

This approach is **problematic**, as Cruz will likely need to seek votes in major cities like Cleveland and Philadelphia. Alienating voters in these areas, whether in the primary or general election, is **unwise.** The idea of dismissing cities as foreign or lacking in values, and as opposed to "real" America, will likely **backfire with many residents.** Such language reinforces negative stereotypes of the GOP as **provincial, bigoted, and exclusionary.** For Cruz, it appears to be an **easy way out** for someone struggling to connect with ordinary people. His **difficulty in social interactions within politics is noticeable.** Cruz's habit of promoting himself by **disparaging others**, presenting himself as ideologically pure, and questioning others' motives **hinders his ability to build political alliances.** The nation already has a condescending and divisive president; **another is not needed.**

Governor Andrew Cuomo launched a strong media response Friday, directly challenging Senator Ted Cruz's recent disparaging remarks about New York. Cruz, a 2016 presidential candidate, had referenced "New York values" during a debate, characterizing the state as "socially liberal, are pro-abortion, are pro-gay marriage, focused around money and the media."

### Cuomo's Vigorous Rebuttal

Governor Cuomo articulated his strong opposition in a series of interviews. "What he said is not merely anti-New York; it is fundamentally anti-American," Cuomo stated on CNN's "New Day." He elaborated, "I find it lamentable and disturbing for all Americans, not exclusively New Yorkers. This represents the politics of division, an attempt to fracture people and indeed, this nation. Such tactics are ineffective and antithetical to our national identity."

### The Context of "New York Values"

Cruz's invocation of "New York values" arose amidst Donald Trump's questioning of Cruz's eligibility for the presidency due to his Canadian birth, despite his mother's American citizenship. Trump, a native of Queens, countered by referencing New York's resilience and response to the September 11, 2001, terrorist attacks as a testament to its values.

Governor Cuomo supported Trump's assertion on MSNBC's "Morning Joe." "That is a salient point," Cuomo remarked. "While Trump's timing may have been politically calculated, it resonated with the public. As Cruz propagates anti-New York sentiments and attempts to leverage offensive stereotypes, it's crucial to remember that 9/11 stands as a profound symbol of America's noblest qualities."

### Hypocrisy and Broad Offense

Cuomo also highlighted the apparent hypocrisy of Cruz's stance, noting the senator's history of soliciting campaign contributions in Manhattan. "This is profoundly troubling on multiple fronts and demonstrably hypocritical," he asserted on MSNBC. "To criticize New York's financial influence while simultaneously benefiting from it is disingenuous. One can only assume he will now refuse any donations from New Yorkers."

During a third interview with NY1, Cuomo further elaborated on the breadth of Cruz's perceived offenses. "He has offended the LGBTQ+ community," Cuomo stated. "He has been offensive to women. He has offended 18 million New Yorkers, and a significant congressional delegation, which is not a viable approach to governance."

### Wider Condemnation

Beyond the Governor's office, other New York entities also condemned Cruz's rhetoric. Representative Pete King (R-New York) advised the senator to "retreat." Brooklyn Borough President Eric Adams publicly denounced Cruz on Twitter. The New York Daily News featured a provocative image of the Statue of Liberty extending a middle finger towards Senator Cruz.

The author, a lifelong New Yorker with deep roots in both upstate and downstate, offers a personal and nuanced exploration of what "New York values" truly signify, aiming to dismantle the simplistic and often inaccurate caricatures frequently propagated by political figures like Ted Cruz. Having been born in Rochester and now residing in New York City for nearly a decade, the author possesses a comprehensive understanding of the state's diverse character and its iconic metropolis. This intimate connection provides a unique vantage point from which to dissect the frequently misunderstood identity of New Yorkers.

Ted Cruz, whose upbringing was shaped by Canada and Texas, and whose academic career led him through Princeton and Harvard, has never personally experienced life in New York. Consequently, his pronouncements on "New York values" are, as the author suggests, based on a superficial acquaintance, akin to forming an opinion of a novel solely from its dust jacket or a film from its promotional trailer. The author posits that Cruz, much like many transient tourists, has only glimpsed a minuscule fraction of the multifaceted reality that constitutes New York.

The author also addresses the public perception of former Mayor Michael Bloomberg, whose policies, in the collective imagination, became synonymous with liberal "nanny-statism." This portrayal, the author argues, is a mischaracterization, as New York City possesses a distinct character that sets it apart from places like Berkeley. The author highlights that a proposed regulation concerning the sale of large sugary beverages, which significantly fueled this perception, ultimately failed to be enacted.

When Ted Cruz alluded to Donald Trump's use of "Born in the USA" at rallies, suggesting Trump would instead champion "New York, New York" and embody "New York values," he was, in the author's view, fundamentally misrepresenting the city. Cruz’s assertion that these values were distinct from "Iowa values" or "New Hampshire values" is met with a counterargument from the author. The author contends that a substantial portion of New York state shares considerable commonalities in topography, culture, and prevailing values with New Hampshire. For instance, the author points to upstate New York, where conservative Republicans often prioritize concerns such as gun ownership and exhibit less engagement with issues like same-sex marriage.

The author posits that Cruz's conception of "New York values" is likely informed by a narrow, stereotypical image of New York City, heavily influenced by media portrayals such as the procedural drama "Law and Order" or a fleeting taxi ride through the bustling thoroughfares of Times Square. This stereotypical view often encompasses a perception of Democratic political leanings, an emphasis on political correctness, and the presence of regulations like the aforementioned soda ban, alongside the often-vocal concerns voiced by publications like the New York Post. These are values, the author notes, that tend to be most prominent in the affluent neighborhoods of Manhattan, areas frequently utilized by politicians for fundraising.

Furthermore, this stereotypical perception does not accurately reflect Donald Trump's own background. While Trump is undeniably wealthy and resides in a prominent skyscraper, his formative experiences are rooted in the outer boroughs, particularly Queens. His father provided him with a substantial business and financial foundation, but Trump's defining characteristic, according to the author, is an outsider's drive for success. This contrasts sharply with the "old money" sensibilities prevalent on the Upper East Side, where Trump's more ostentatious displays are often viewed with disdain.

The author asserts that the majority of New Yorkers, much like the general populace in Iowa and New Hampshire, are not consumed by an obsessive interest in politics. Their primary focus, the author suggests, remains on their personal lives and immediate concerns, unless an exceptionally widespread or unusual topic captures the public's attention. Many New Yorkers, the author recalls, also found Bloomberg's proposed limit on sugary beverage sizes to be an ill-conceived notion. Bloomberg himself, a quintessential New Yorker, was reportedly unconcerned with such matters, a trait attributed partly to his considerable wealth and, more significantly, to his inherent New York identity.

The author directly challenges the simplistic notion that New Yorkers are exclusively characterized by toughness, rudeness, and an inclination to impose their values upon others. It is illogical, the author argues, to simultaneously embody gruffness and a "namby-pamby bleeding heart" disposition. To illustrate this point, the author extends a humorous invitation to Mr. Cruz: to reside in a modest apartment within an ordinary New York neighborhood for a full month. This immersive experience would necessitate navigating the intricacies of daily life, from discovering a preferred local bodega to mastering the complexities of the subway system and engaging with local healthcare facilities. After such an immersion, if Cruz still maintains that New York's values are demonstrably more liberal than those found in Iowa City, the author playfully concedes they will acknowledge his critique, though ultimately, they profess they will not be overly concerned.

An update from a pro-Cruz Super PAC, identified as @KeepPromise1, has suggested that "NY values" are synonymous with an overemphasis on financial gain at the expense of morality and a prioritization of self-aggrandizement over humility. The author likens this critique to an individual who, after visiting Times Square on New Year's Eve, proceeds to comment on the perceived scarcity of homeless shelter volunteers. This analogy serves to highlight the author's view that such critiques are often based on isolated, unrepresentative observations rather than a comprehensive understanding of the subject.

New York City is taking no guff from Sen. Ted Cruz. At least that is the case for the New York Daily News, which used the front page of its Friday issue to slam the conservative Texas senator, who in turn blasted wealthy businessman and Republican front-runner Donald Trump over his so-called New York values. The paper’s cover delivers a graphic of the Statue of Liberty making an obscene gesture with one finger and this headline: “Drop Dead Ted.” The cover, a riff on the paper’s famous “Ford to City: Drop Dead” headline from 1975, came after Republican contenders locked horns in a debate in South Carolina Thursday night—likely the last debate before the Iowa caucuses kick off the nominating process on Feb. 1. Epic @NYDailyNews cover "drop dead Ted. go back to Canada" after Cruz questions "NYC values" pic.twitter.com/JRY2Ojksor — Sara Eisen (@SaraEisen) January 15, 2016 During one heated clash, Cruz tried to raise doubts about Trump’s legitimacy as a Republican candidate. “I think most people know exactly what New York values are,” Cruz said. “Everyone understands that the values in New York City are socially liberal.” In response, Daily News columnist Mike Lupica pointed to a man who gave the shirt off his back to another man in need on a Manhattan subway. Here’s Lupica’s summation of his views: The best of this city is the best of this country, and always has been. When it was hit in a way that no American city had ever been hit, it came together and rose up together in a moment as thrilling as any America had ever seen. Trump offered his own sharp rebuttal during the debate: “We rebuilt downtown Manhattan and everybody in the world watched and everybody in the world loved New York and loved New Yorkers. And I have to tell you, that was a very insulting statement that Ted made.” To be sure, the Daily News is no Trump booster. In a recent cover, the paper took Trump to task for suggesting a ban of all Muslims attempting to enter the U.S. According to the results from a new NBC News/Wall Street Journal poll, Trump continues to lead among Republican candidates with the real-estate mogul appearing as the first choice for 33% of would-be Republican primary voters compared with 20% for Cruz.

Ted Cruz suggested on Tuesday that Donald Trump "embodies New York values," interpreting Trump's persistent criticisms as a sign of concern over his front-runner position in the presidential race. Cruz made these remarks on The Howie Carr Show, responding to a question about Trump's use of Bruce Springsteen's "Born in the USA." This song choice by Trump was linked to allegations that Cruz might be ineligible to be president due to his Canadian birth.

### "New York Values" and Shifting Strategies

"Well, look, I think he may shift in his new rallies to play 'New York, New York' because Donald comes from New York and he embodies New York values," Cruz stated, referencing the iconic Frank Sinatra tune. The Texas senator further elaborated that Trump appeared "rattled" by Cruz's increasing prominence in the campaign. He characterized Trump's actions as "unloading at every stop with direct attacks."

### Cruz's Stance on Engagement

Cruz initially indicated he did not "intend to respond or reciprocate" these attacks. However, on Tuesday, he appeared to subtly counter Trump on two occasions. Earlier in the day, he told reporters that supporters of Democratic candidate Hillary Clinton were "eager to support Donald Trump and the attacks that are being tossed my direction."

### Echoes of Democratic Attacks

During the radio interview, Cruz reiterated this point. In response to a clip of Clinton remarking that Trump was "a Democrat before he was a Republican," Cruz commented, "Well, I would say, Hillary would know well how to identify Democrats. She has been a partisan Democrat herself obviously and, uh, she and Donald know each other well and I do think it’s interesting that Hillary Clinton’s key supporters are doing everything they can to echo Donald’s attacks on me."

### Polling Data and Campaign Concerns

Cruz posited that Democratic attacks against him might stem from current polling data. "The polling right now shows Donald loses to Hillary and loses by a pretty big margin but I beat Hillary and I think that’s got the Hillary folks a little bit concerned and so they’re doing everything they can to amplify Donald’s attacks," he explained.

### Addressing Faith-Based Criticisms

Cruz also addressed Trump's comment that "not a lot of evangelicals come out of Cuba." He responded by inviting the reality television personality to attend church with him. "Any time someone is attacking your faith that starts to suggest they’re getting really nervous about what’s happening in the race," Cruz remarked. "I would be happy to invite Donald to come with me to church any time he would like. He would certainly be welcome there. And, uh, you know, at the end of the day, my focus is not to engage in the mudslinging."

query: What prompted the backlash against Matthew and Terces Engelhart, the owners of Cafe Gratitude and Gracias Madre?

## Vegan Restaurant Founders Under Fire for Animal Consumption

The founders of the popular vegan restaurant group Cafe Gratitude and Gracias Madre are facing intense backlash, including death threats, after photos emerged showing Matthew and Terces Engelhart raising, slaughtering, and consuming animals on their farm.

### A Turn Towards "Regenerative Agriculture"

Matthew and Terces Engelhart, who opened the first Cafe Gratitude in 2004 and have served celebrity clients like Gwyneth Paltrow and Beyoncé, are defending their actions at Be Love Farm in Vacaville, California. They describe this shift as a personal choice rooted in a commitment to "regenerative agriculture."

The controversy intensified when activists shared blog posts from 2015 that featured images of a freezer filled with beef and Matthew Engelhart eating a hamburger. These posts detailed their "transition" to eating meat after nearly four decades of vegetarianism. Matthew Engelhart explained to the Hollywood Reporter, "We started to observe nature and what we saw is that nature doesn’t exist without animals." He views animals as making an "extreme sacrifice for humanity" and believes humans can partake in this "sacrament."

### Outrage from the Vegan Community

While the Engelharts' farm supplies produce to their restaurants, the revelation has deeply angered strict vegans. Online criticism has been severe, with accusations of hypocrisy and ill will directed at the couple. Critics argue that the Engelharts have misled vegans who supported their businesses, unknowingly contributing to practices involving animal slaughter.

The couple built their brand on a message of peace and love, a philosophy extended to their sister restaurant, Gracias Madre, with locations in San Francisco and Los Angeles. They have also authored books, including "Sacred Commerce: Business as a Path of Awakening." Their personal website, Eternal Presence, also mentions a board game they created in 2004, "The Abounding River Board Game," designed to encourage players to adopt an "unfamiliar view of Being Abundant" and develop a "spiritual foundation" for financial perspectives.

A February blog post from Be Love Farm described the week they began incorporating meat products, calling the transition a "necessary and important part of our growth as well as the sustainability of our farm." This post included images of their new beef products alongside pastoral scenes of their cows.

### Past Controversies and Personal Backgrounds

This is not the first time the Engelharts have faced public scrutiny. In 2011, they cited "aggressive lawsuits" from former employees as the reason for closing or selling eight Cafe Gratitude restaurants in the Bay Area. These lawsuits challenged tip-pooling practices and alleged unpaid overtime, with the couple stating that defending themselves in court would be too costly. Another lawsuit involved an employee who claimed wrongful termination for refusing to attend a controversial self-improvement course. Ultimately, the Engelharts settled these disputes, selling or transferring some restaurants to family members.

Terces Engelhart reportedly met Matthew at a Landmark Forum seminar. She had previously left a conventional life with her husband and children, selling their possessions and traveling in an RV. Her daughter from that marriage now co-owns a vegan beer garden and a vegan ice cream parlor in Los Angeles.

Activist Mary Cummins has suggested that the Engelharts may have been influenced by Allan Savory, an ecologist who advocates for using livestock in grasslands to combat climate change. This theory has garnered both support and opposition.

### Uncertain Future for the Engelhart Empire

The long-term impact of this controversy on the Engelhart empire, which serves a substantial number of meals daily and hosts various events at their farm, remains uncertain. It is yet to be seen whether patrons will continue to engage with the farm, particularly its animal interactions, after learning about their meat production practices.

What a wonderful week it was on the farm. Our great friends who own Ca Momi in Napa gifted us with some of their truly delicious pizzas and profiteroles to make my recovery from a surgical procedure easier on me. I was so grateful for the added rest and kindness. I then got inspired to make my own profiteroles since we have this amazing farm cream and voila, see them in the picture above. Here I am piping them onto the parchment paper getting ready to bake them. Matthew continued his pruning of all the trees while the boys, Jaxon and Jack put mulch around the base of the trees. You can see that the almonds are in full bloom and the nectarine blossoms are coming on in their beautiful pinks! It was a big week as we begin to make the transition into our own meat products after nearly 40 years of vegetarianism. While I would clearly say we are in transition and that transition is happening deep within our beings, we know it is a necessary and important part of our own growth as well as the sustainability of our farm. Certainly a part of us wants to either deny the inevitability of death or simply not let ourselves get present to the reality of it, for our animals as well as ourselves. But we know that while we die a little bit each day as we open our hearts further to the presence of love, and as we are the caretakers of our farm animals the responsibility for their health and well being lies with us. This is clearly a longer discussion and we invite you to one of our upcoming farm dinners over the next 9 months, where we share more about this process. Here Matthew is straining off beef broth and our freezer full of pastured beef, our pastured beef. Here are the girls full of Spring clover sitting down chewing their cud in the warm Spring breeze of the midday. and introducing Jack (blonde) and Jaxon, our apprentices on the farm and a welcome youthful pair of energetic young men. With no rain in sight we have started to irrigate already, which brings me to my question for the week. How do you nourish your soul? Think about it. Have a great week, Love, Terces

The husband-and-wife owners of a trendy chain of California vegan restaurants are under attack from an angry pack of animal rights activists who discovered the restaurateurs were slaughtering animals on their farm and eating meat. Matthew and Terces Engelhart, owners of the famous celebrity-patroned Cafe Gratitude and Gracias Madre restaurants, have been receving death threats from activists after they started circulating blog posts from the couple’s Be Love Farm in northern California that show the couple raising cattle, according to what they told the Hollywood Reporter. “They are duping vegans to support their animal killings,” activist Chase Avior, who organized protests at the southern California restaurants, told the Hollywood Reporter. The Engleharts started the first Cafe Gratitude in San Francisco in 2004 before opening more vegan restaurants in southern California around 2011, riding the wave of consumer demand for healthy, sustainable food. The restaurants are known for being celebrity hotspots — Emma Stone, Andrew Garfield, Kerry Washington, Jay Z, and Beyonce have all been spotted enjoying their vegan dishes. Cafe Gratitude owners Matthew and Terces Engelhart are under fire after the California based vegan restaurateurs were found to have returned to eating meat. (Ashley Dentino/via YouTube) The firestorm over the owners’ carnivorism started when vigilant vegans got a hold of a 14-month old blog post stating the couple’s decision to end their 40 years of vegetarianism and begin what they call “regenerative agriculture.” The post included photos showing freezers full of beef at their farm, jars of beef stock and of Matthew Engelhart enjoying a hamburger. “We started to observe nature and what we saw is that nature doesn’t exist without animals,” Matthew Engelhart told the Hollywood Reporter last week, as an explanation for their transition. Outraged animal rights activists weren’t so forgiving of their change in diet calling it “regenerative bulls--t!” on a Facebook page dedicated to boycotting the restaurant and organizing protests. Cafe Gratitude in Venice, Calif. is a known celebrity hotspot. (Rick Loomis/LA Times via Getty Images) Protesters also left a slew of one-star Yelp reviews on the popular eatery’s site. “I’m GRATEFUL that I know they’re s--theads!” wrote one commenter. "Here's a new dish for Cafe Gratitude - I Am MURDERER!!!" another person wrote. Numerous protests have taken place outside their restaurants — with demonstrators carrying signs that called the restaurateurs “hypocrites” as leaders in the vegan community and others that said “no animal is grateful to die,” as a play on the restaurant's name. “People have taken up the mob mentality,” Matthew Engelhart bemoaned. Sign up for BREAKING NEWS Emails privacy policy Thanks for subscribing!

The owners of the most prominent vegan restaurant chain in Los Angeles were found to be raising and "harvesting" animals at their Northern California farm. Now Hollywood's vegan community is angry, and death threats have been made. "People have taken up the mob mentality," says Cafe Gratitude owner Matthew Engelhart. L.A.'s vegan vortex has angrily turned on the most prominent vegan restaurant group in town this week as word has spread that its owners are not just eating meat but raising and slaughtering animals at the working farm where they live in Northern California. Matthew and Terces Engelhart, the husband-and-wife proprietors of favored entertainment industry haunt Cafe Gratitude, tell The Hollywood Reporter they've been receiving death threats as part of a quickly growing, internet-bred campaign against them. It has also spawned a deluge of one-star reviews on their local outposts' respective Yelp pages, a boycott group on Facebook that tallies 571 members at press time and plans now underway for a protest at the Larchmont Village location on Friday at 7 p.m. "People have taken up the mob mentality," says Matthew. "It saddens me that the choices we made in the privacy of our home would lead people to feel so betrayed that it's elevated to threats on our lives. I'm very discouraged." The trouble began last week when animal rights activists discovered and then widely circulated a 14-month-old blog post written by Terces on the Engelharts' Be Love Farm website, which mixed an announcement of their transition back into a meat diet again after nearly 40 years of vegetarianism (they had been vegan since 2003) with posted pictures of strained beef broth and a freezer full of pastured beef from their own dairy cows. Matthew tells THR they have kept chickens on the farm for seven years "for eggs only," along with the cows for five years for milk, cheese and butter that's for sale. (He claims they've "harvested," or slaughtered, several cows in total and never sold the meat, only shared it with "our friends, neighbors and community.") The news has come as a shock to many vegans, who have been regular customers of the restaurants and claim the Engelharts have built their brand on not just serving vegan food but clearly wrapping themselves in the righteousness of the vegan cause — which they argue has now been undermined. "The reason we're so upset is that veganism is a belief system," says Carrie Christianson, who started the Facebook boycott group. "You are patronizing a restaurant that you think has that philosophy, and it turns out it doesn't. Vegans should know that this restaurant has a farm that slaughters animals." The Engelharts launched the first Cafe Gratitude in San Francisco in 2004. The mini-chain began colonizing Southern California in 2011 with the on-the-ground help of their manager sons from different marriages, Ryland Engelhart and Cary Mosier, beginning with a location in Larchmont Village, followed by outposts in Venice and downtown L.A.'s Arts District (as well as locations in San Diego and Newport Beach). Investors such as musician Jason Mraz have backed them. (A newer sister concept, Gracias Madre, which serves "meatless Mexican" fare, opened in 2014 in West Hollywood; their daughter, Mollie, co-owns Culver City's separate Sage Organic Vegan Bistro — which also purchases from Be Love Farm — with Woody Harrelson.) Cafe Gratitude is known for giving dishes names that are self-affirmations. The most popular include the "I Am Whole" macrobiotic sea-vegetable rice and a curried lentil assemblage called "I Am Humble." The locations have become a solid entertainment industry draw for business lunches, as well as a reliable celebrity nexus. Customers range from Beyonce Knowles and Sacha Baron Cohen to Ariana Grande and Jake Gyllenhaal. The Engelharts frame their return to "sustainable, regenerative" animal consumption as the mindful culmination of years spent laboring on their upstate farm. "We started to observe nature, and what we saw is that nature doesn't exist without animals," says Matthew. "Neither does natural farming. You know, you can't buy organic vegetables that aren't fertilized with animal residue. So that was our discovery. We aren't on a soapbox." Yet while the Engelharts are at pains to paint their decision as a private and personal rural journey separate from their public work at their urban restaurants, their critics find the reasoning specious, believing the enterprises, the figureheads and their choices are inextricably bound. "This is a vegan restaurant company that's been a leader in the vegan community," says Chase Avior, a screenwriter and director who is organizing the Friday protest. "If they're going to go around and say it's not about animal rights, it's not about speaking about the animals horrifically abused, it's just about better nurturing a plant-based lifestyle, that's something different. A lot of vegans have a right to feel betrayed by this." Still, the couple's vegan supporters think the fury is misplaced. "Boycott factory farming — something that will make a real difference to the cause," says Rainbeau Mars, author of The 21-Day SuperStar Cleanse and, along with Ryland Engelhart, a board member at Venice-based healthy-soil nonprofit Kiss the Ground. "And as for their own personal diets, would we stop supporting the Dalai Lama because he eats meat? Or stop supporting the organic farmer who grows your tomatoes because she eats meat?" After that interview, Mars wrote a long follow-up email to THR to clarify her position ("despite needing to go teach a yoga class on the helicopter pad of the Four Seasons this morning and head out of town this evening"). In it, Mars explains how she has worked in "service to greater humanity" and preaches a sort of compassion. "I feel we should support the leaders in our community that are sharing charity and compassion, health and community activism such as the founders of Cafe Gratitude and Gracias Madre," she says. "I invite us all to not point the finger at others but look at ourselves." Such thinking doesn't sit well with others who have spoken out about the Engelharts' choices at Be Love Farm, which according to the couple these days supplies only a limited amount of fruits and vegetables to the restaurants. One of them is the musician Moby, who opened his own vegan restaurant five months ago in Silver Lake called Little Pine. "I sincerely hope that they discontinue their practice of raising and killing animals for food," he wrote on Facebook. Meanwhile, Cooking Channel personality and raw food chef Jason Wrobel (his clients include Robin Wright and Jeremy Piven) penned a long open letter on his website, echoing many online sentiments: "People feel misled, deliberately lied to and that a business they've so lovingly supported for many years has lost its way. I feel that my hard-earned money has been used for purposes that are unethical, cruel and out-of-alignment with my values. And I strongly feel that a more public announcement from the company should have been made about the practices at the farm, not tucked away in some obscure blog post." For their part, the Engelharts profess to be perplexed by the dissent. "I don't think there's any organization on the planet that's done more to promote a plant-based diet than us," says Matthew. "We've moved it from a dogma to a genre. We serve 28,000 meals a week in all of our enterprises. We've done nothing but a plant-based diet at our restaurants and we're being attacked. It doesn't make sense to me." Their critics aren't satisfied, despite their record. "I eat at non-vegan restaurants all the time," says Elana Lavine, who runs the L.A. healthy lifestyle blog Klean Slate. "Just don't claim to be something you're not. They're slaughtering animals! And there's been a lack of total transparency about it on the restaurant side. It looks kind of shady, like they are hiding things. And there was always that cloud around them when it came to the cult thing." The "cult thing" has left customers leery in the past. The couple, who like to say they practice "sacred commerce," had to close their Northern California locations in 2010 in the midst of lawsuit battles, one related to a policy encouraging allegedly illegal tip-pooling and another stemming from a mandate that their employee get involved with the controversial personal-betterment organization Landmark Forum, a successor to Werner Erhard's 1970s self-help program EST, which centered on a unique style of session therapy that was recently dramatized on FX's period spy show The Americans. Both iterations have been criticized for their aggressive recruitment tactics. The Engelharts, who met while attending Landmark Forum, believe they're being held up to a dogmatic purity standard — one that, in the scheme of their contributions to veganism, is entirely unfair. "Somehow we got made mom and dad of some vegan movement," says Matthew. "We never signed up for that. It's crazy. Do [the protestors] check in on the diets of every other vegan restaurant owner? Do they check in on the diets of the owners of Whole Foods? We are baffled. And believe me there are lots of vegan enterprises where the principals are not vegan, or they have other enterprises that are not vegan." He goes on, "I am allowed to change my mind. And by the way, I never even told them what my mind was. All I told them was it's a vegan restaurant. And it still is! And it always will be!"

**Chef Chandra Gilbert** is bringing her **bold flavors** and **seasonally-conscious ingredients** to Los Angeles with the opening of **Gracias Madre**. With **33 years of experience** in the restaurant industry, including time at renowned establishments like Alice Waters' Café Fanny, Cowgirl Creamery, and Greens Restaurant, Gilbert has been a key part of the Café Gratitude family since **2004**.

Her journey with the esteemed vegan restaurant group began as Director of Operations in the Bay Area. It was during this period that the concept for Gracias Madre first emerged, in discussions with Café Gratitude Founders Matthew and Terces Engelhart. Together, they **co-designed the restaurant's look and feel** and **co-created the menu**. This menu prominently featured produce from the Engelhart's Be Love Farm in Vacaville.

Gracias Madre first opened its doors in San Francisco in **2009**, realizing Gilbert's vision. The restaurant, situated in San Francisco's Mission District, rapidly earned **critical acclaim** for its **vibrant take on Mexican cuisine**. Now, with the **January 2014 opening of Gracias Madre in West Hollywood**, Gilbert is extending this culinary passion to Los Angeles. The Los Angeles menu continues to showcase produce from Be Love Farm, **complemented by ingredients sourced from local farmers markets**.

## Café Gratitude: A Plant-Based Culinary Journey Rooted in Gratitude

Café Gratitude invites you to experience a distinctive plant-based culinary adventure, where gourmet cuisine nourishes both your well-being and the planet. Our core philosophy is to cultivate an environment and a menu that actively champions health and sustainability for our community and the broader world. We operate under a unique business model we term “Sacred Commerce,” which prioritizes inspired service, clear and honest communication, and a profound appreciation for life's abundance.

### Our Vision: Cultivating a World of Plenty

At Café Gratitude, our mission is to embody and manifest a world of abundance. We celebrate life through our vibrant food and our dedicated people. To honor the earth and ourselves, recognizing our deep interconnectedness, we meticulously source the finest organic ingredients. Our commitment extends to supporting local farmers, advocating for sustainable agricultural practices, and employing eco-friendly products. Each dish is prepared with intention, encouraging you to embrace our spaces and adopt a mindset of loving your life, cherishing yourself, accepting the world, practicing generosity and gratitude daily, and experiencing the profound joy of being provided for. We encourage you to savor the experience, have fun, and feel truly revitalized. Welcome to Café Gratitude!

### Culinary Excellence with Chef Dreux Ellis

Chef Dreux Ellis, our Executive Chef, spearheads the development and execution of the organic, plant-based menu across all Café Gratitude locations in Southern California. Since joining the Venice, California team in April 2012, Chef Ellis has been instrumental in bringing health-conscious, flavor-rich cuisine to a wider audience. He now directs the culinary vision for all regional restaurants, including those in the Arts District, Larchmont Village, Venice, and San Diego. This includes the refined new interpretation of the iconic Gratitude restaurant, now open in Newport Beach and Beverly Hills.

This week was a **period of significant growth and learning**, highlighted by the **successful launch of a new Cafe Gratitude** in downtown Los Angeles' Arts District. The training of **112 new employees** presented a substantial challenge, yet it also showcased the **remarkable individuals** who have been instrumental in shaping Cafe Gratitude. Their commitment to cultivating open hearts, expanding love, and promoting ethical livelihoods fuels our ongoing mission.

### Welcoming New Team Members and Family

The atmosphere during the new employee training was **exceptionally vibrant**. A surprise visit from a special guest during a lunch break brought widespread smiles and laughter. Beyond the professional excitement, my visits to Los Angeles always include **cherished moments with my two youngest grandsons**, Valentine (6 months) and Rio (2 months). The role of grandmother is truly a profound joy.

### A Farm's Bounty and a Dietary Shift

On the farm, we celebrated an extraordinary harvest of **enormous lettuce heads**, a clear indication of the land's fertility. This week also marked a significant personal turning point: we prepared and consumed our **first hamburgers in over four decades**. This dietary adjustment, though demanding, has been profoundly illuminating, and we hold deep gratitude for the **sacrifices our cattle make**.

### Rethinking Agriculture and Non-Violence

Matthew's decision to reintroduce meat into his diet after forty years of vegetarianism is rooted in a **complex understanding of non-violence**, encompassing the entire ecological web. He contends that conventional agricultural practices, with their reliance on plowing and monocultures, represent one of the **most damaging forces on the planet**, contributing to species extinction and habitat loss.

The production of items like Earth Balance margarine, derived from Canadian canola, necessitates plowing that **devastates prairie ecosystems**, harming numerous species. Matthew proposes that **well-managed grasslands, supported by herds of ruminants** such as cattle and buffalo, are essential for preserving biodiversity and preventing land degradation.

### The Vital Role of Ruminants and Restoration

Grasslands, which cover 30% of the Earth's surface, depend on the presence of **ruminants to flourish**. Their absence leads to land deterioration and desertification, a process with extensive repercussions, even contributing to conflicts in regions like Syria and Iraq due to poor land management.

Cattle, in this context, are not merely livestock but **critical partners in ecological restoration**. They aid in maintaining soil fertility, ensure the ground remains covered, and play a crucial role in **reversing desertification and mitigating climate change**. Matthew believes that integrating cattle into our food system, when raised ethically, offers a way to participate in a **natural sacrament**, recognizing their divinely appointed role in God's plan as sustenance for predators.

### Sacrifice and Reverence

This perspective on sacrifice is powerfully articulated by Wendell Berry: "To live we must daily break the body and shed the blood of Creation. When we do it knowingly, lovingly, skillfully, reverently, it is a sacrament. When we do it ignorantly, greedily, clumsily, destructively it is a desecration."

This philosophy was further solidified by attending an Allan Savory workshop. A memorable dinner conversation with Mr. Savory himself provided insight into the **authentic South African method of preparing "biltong,"** a type of cured dried beef.

### Bridging the Gap: Knowing Our Food

While acknowledging that veganism is a significant response to animal cruelty within industrial food systems, we also recognize the **nutritional benefits of animals raised in healthy and humane environments**. It is crucial to **bridge the divide between consumers and their food sources**, embracing the complete cycle of life and death rather than remaining detached from it.

### A Look Back and a Look Forward

This week has served as a profound reminder of divine grace and the importance of embracing it. As we breathe and love, we also reflect on our journey. A look back to the opening of our first Cafe Gratitude in San Francisco 11 years ago reveals a time when cold-brewed coffee and almond milk were considered novelties, met with questions like, "How do you get milk out of an almond?" The progress from then to now has been **truly extraordinary**.
