House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi has declared that House Democrats campaigning in 2014 will prominently feature the Affordable Care Act (ACA) in their re-election strategies. Speaking on NBC’s “Meet the Press” on Sunday, the California Democrat affirmed that while she would not speculate on the ACA's impact on securing a House majority, the party would not shy away from this critical healthcare legislation as a campaign cornerstone. "I don't believe one can definitively predict future electoral outcomes," Pelosi stated, "but I can assure you that Democrats will unequivocally champion the Affordable Care Act." She highlighted that the party's candidates are deeply invested in addressing economic concerns and preventing government shutdowns. Pelosi emphasized that the economy would constitute the principal theme of the upcoming campaign. Regarding healthcare, she articulated, "This is a matter that necessitates resolution, but it does not imply that we will evade it simply because it presents a political challenge. Its value to the American populace is too substantial. The paramount consideration is ensuring the American people are adequately served, rather than focusing on individual re-election prospects."

President Barack Obama’s mishandling of the Affordable Care Act rollout has been incompetent, Washington Post journalist Bob Woodward said, but it doesn’t rise to past president-dooming scandals. “What this is, it’s a mess, clearly, but what it isn’t, and I think you have to look at the question of motive. And the president’s motive here, even though there were deep problems with the implementation, he wants to do something good for 30 million people and get them health insurance,” Woodward said on “Fox News Sunday.” “So this isn’t Watergate, this isn’t [Bill] Clinton and Monica Lewinsky.” Asked by Fox host Chris Wallace if the Obamacare implementation revealed “rank incompetence,” Woodward agreed. (Understanding Obamacare: POLITICO’s guide to the ACA) “There’s no question about that,” he said. “But you see all of these stories and this frenzy out there, the game over, the presidency is over some people are saying, and I think that’s not the case.” But Woodward did say the ACA rollout is a harbinger of bad things to come for Obama’s White House. (VIDEO: Sunday shows in 90 seconds) “When you go down the road, it’s going to get worse,” he said. “It’s going to blow a hole in the budget when you go two or three months from now… All of a sudden this is going to come on the table and people are going to say, ‘My God it’s going to cost much more money than we thought before. How you disentangle this is now on Obama’s head.” Read more about: Bob Woodward, Monica Lewinsky, Obamacare, Watergate

query: What was the estimated wealth of Mr. Trump’s senior staff and cabinet, according to Bloomberg?

documents.text: Mr. Trump’s administration is considered the most wealthy in American history, with members of his senior staff and cabinet worth an estimated $12 billion, according to a tally by Bloomberg. The Friday filings will add voluminous detail to that top-line figure. The White house chief of staff, Reince Priebus, for example, earned at least $1.18 million — nearly half of which came from the Republican National Committee, which he formerly led. His assets totaled between $604,008 and at least $1.26 million. “I think one of the really interesting things that people are going to see today — and I think it’s something that should be celebrated — is that the president has brought a lot of people into this administration, and this White House in particular, who have been very blessed and very successful,” said Sean Spicer, the White House press secretary. The officials “have given up a lot to come into government by setting aside a lot of assets,” he said. Until January, Mr. Kushner was the chief executive of Kushner Companies, a family-run real estate investment firm with holdings across the country. It is a growing business that has taken part in at least $7 billion of acquisitions over the past decade. Late Friday, the White House released details of the plan devised by his advisers to avoid conflicts of interest between Mr. Kushner’s government role and the wide-ranging business empire he ran with his father. That business depends on foreign investment from undisclosed sources, as well as billions of dollars in loans from the world’s biggest financial services firms. Although Mr. Kushner has stepped down from his management positions at the more than 200 entities that operated aspects of the family real estate business, he will remain a beneficiary of a vast majority of the business he ran for the past decade, through a series of trusts that already owned the various real estate companies. The plan laid out on Friday “is not sufficient,” said Larry Noble, a former general counsel and chief ethics officer for the Federal Election Commission. “While removing himself from the management of the businesses is an important step, he is still financially benefiting from how the businesses do. This presents potential for a conflict of interest. Given his level in the White House and broad portfolio, it’s hard to see how he will recuse himself from everything that may impact his financial interest.” While the filing discloses Mr. Kushner’s personal lenders, it does not provide information on his business partners or lenders to his projects.

The Trump Administration's Financial Disclosures: A Deep Dive into the 45th President's Inner Circle

In a notable Friday night release, the Trump administration disclosed financial information for numerous White House staffers. However, this release was characterized by a deliberate lack of transparency. These disclosures are crucial, offering insight into the financial dealings of individuals who advise the President and influence national policy. They detail assets such as stock ownership, real estate holdings, and business interests, thereby illuminating potential conflicts of interest.

Despite the significance of these documents, the White House mandated a separate request for each staffer's disclosure and withheld the names of the individuals involved, creating a challenge in identifying who had filed. As the White House declined to make these documents publicly accessible, we collaborated with The New York Times and The Associated Press to obtain and compile them. These filings, representing nearly all known staffers, are now available in a public Google Drive folder. Our ongoing review of these documents is ongoing, and we invite public assistance. Any findings that warrant further scrutiny can be shared via comments on this thread or through our Google Form.

Initial findings reveal significant financial entanglements. Steve Bannon, President Trump's chief strategist, generated over $500,000 in the past year from entities linked to Republican donors Robert and Rebekah Mercer. These include the conservative news outlet Breitbart News Network, the data analytics firm Cambridge Analytica, the nonprofit Government Accountability Institute, and the production company Glittering Steel. Per an agreement with White House ethics counsel, Bannon is divesting his interests in Cambridge Analytica and Glittering Steel. His total earnings for the year, according to the filings, ranged from $1.3 million to $2.3 million.

Jared Kushner, the President's son-in-law and a senior White House adviser, relinquished his roles in 266 business entities to adhere to federal ethics regulations. His and his wife Ivanka's financial disclosures highlight their substantial wealth, estimated at up to $741 million, primarily derived from the family's real estate and investment firm, Kushner Companies. Kushner retains over 100 real estate assets, including a Trump-branded rental property in Jersey City, New Jersey, which received millions in funding from Chinese investors through an EB-5 visa program.

Ivanka Trump, who recently assumed an official White House position, was required to disclose her assets as part of Kushner's filing. Her branded companies, encompassing fashion and jewelry lines, reported over $5 million in revenue in 2016 and are valued in excess of $50 million. Her investment in the Trump International Hotel in Washington, D.C., which commenced operations in September, yielded an income between $1 million and $5 million. She is placing her companies into a trust that she will not manage during her father's presidency.

Additional disclosures include Gary Cohn, former Goldman Sachs investment banker and current Director of the National Economic Council, whose assets are valued at a minimum of $253 million, including substantial stakes in private companies. Omarosa Manigault, a reality television personality serving as a White House communications staffer, holds a 33% stake in a trust valued between $1 million and $5 million, established by her late fiancé, actor Michael Clarke Duncan. Reed Cordish, a family friend of the Trumps and a Maryland real estate developer now leading White House technology initiatives, reported assets of at least $197 million, including interests in Baltimore casinos.

We have received less than half of the approximately 180 financial disclosures that White House officials indicated they have processed. These documents will be made available to the public as they are received.

The White House has released financial disclosure forms for over 100 senior officials appointed to President Donald Trump's administration. These January filings provide a **detailed view of the substantial personal wealth** these individuals bring to public service, wealth that is now shaping national policy. While the disclosures outline considerable assets and income, they **do not always fully clarify divestment from business interests** that could potentially create conflicts of interest. It is important to note that President Trump and Vice President Pence are not required to submit new financial disclosures until next year.

A comprehensive review of these filings by the Associated Press has uncovered several key insights into the financial backgrounds of prominent administration figures.

### Navigating Wealth and Business Interests

**Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump: A Real Estate Empire**
Senior Adviser Jared Kushner and his wife, Ivanka Trump, have taken **significant steps to distance themselves from their extensive business holdings**. They have formally resigned from all business entities and divested from 58 distinct assets. Despite these considerable efforts, their financial portfolio remains anchored by substantial holdings within their global real estate empire, with reported assets valued at a minimum of **$240 million**. Kushner began divesting from potentially problematic assets shortly after the presidential election, with some transactions occurring even before official disclosure requirements. He sold his stake in a prominent Manhattan skyscraper to a trust overseen by his mother and has strategically organized many remaining assets into trusts where he is the sole beneficiary.

**Gary Cohn: From Wall Street to Economic Advising**
Gary Cohn, formerly an executive at Goldman Sachs, joined the Trump administration as chief economic adviser. His financial disclosure reveals an impressive income of at least **$40 million** from his tenure at Goldman Sachs, encompassing dividends, salary, and bonuses, with approximately half from stock compensation. Cohn's investment portfolio is diverse, spanning venture capital funds like Andreessen Horowitz to tangible assets such as self-storage units. Notably, he also reported receiving over **$1 million in income from the Industrial and Commercial Bank of China**, an interest the White House indicated he is actively divesting from.

**Steve Bannon: A Multifaceted Financial Profile**
Chief Strategist Steve Bannon's financial disclosures indicate assets ranging between **$13 million and $56 million**. A significant portion, up to **$25 million**, is attributed to his political consultancy, Bannon Strategic Advisors Inc. Prior to his prominent role in the Trump campaign, Bannon earned less than **$200,000** as executive director of Breitbart News Network LLC. His professional history also includes a two-year tenure as vice president of the data firm Cambridge Analytica, from which he resigned in August 2016. Bannon's consulting firm received over **$125,000 from Cambridge Analytica** in the preceding year. He also holds a stake in Cambridge Analytica, valued between **$1 million and $5 million**, with an agreement in principle to sell this holding. Additionally, Bannon maintains an investment worth up to **$5 million in Bannon Film Industries Inc.**, an entertainment company known for producing political documentaries.

**Kellyanne Conway: A Strategic Financial Approach**
Counselor to the President Kellyanne Conway possesses assets estimated to be worth up to **$40 million**, primarily derived from investments and salary from her political consulting firm, "the polling company/WomanTrend." Her firm generated over **$800,000 in business income** during 2016 and is valued between **$1 million and $5 million**. A significant portion of Conway's assets, exceeding **$31 million**, is held in cash or money-market accounts, likely a result of her decision to sell most investments prior to joining the White House. She continues to hold minor stock holdings, valued at less than **$200,000**, in companies such as Pfizer, Kraft Heinz, Mondelez, Altria, and Philip Morris. Conway has also supplemented her income through paid speeches and consulting services to various conservative organizations and Steven Cohen's firm, Point 72 Asset Management.

### Legal Expertise and Policy Roles

**Don McGahn: Legal Expertise and Political Ties**
White House Counsel Don McGahn commanded a substantial income of over **$2.4 million** in the past year from his work at Jones Day, a prominent law firm with strong Republican affiliations. His disclosures list legal services rendered to more than 22 conservative and GOP-affiliated entities, including the NRA and the Republican National Committee. McGahn also reported earning **$4,900 from a music booking firm**.

**Boris Epshteyn: Investment Banking and Media Engagement**
Boris Epshteyn's primary financial asset is a significant stake, valued between **$1 million and $5 million**, in TGP Securities Inc., an investment banking firm where he previously served as a managing director. His income in the past year included **$30,000 earned as a Trump media surrogate** and **$226,000 from TGP**. Epshteyn also received **$240,000 in consulting fees from a health-care firm**. While he joined the White House press office, reports have indicated his departure from that role.

**Reince Priebus: Leadership and Legal Practice**
White House Chief of Staff Reince Priebus earned a considerable income exceeding **$500,000** in salary and bonuses from his leadership role within the Republican Party. Additionally, he garnered at least **$750,000 from his former law firm**, Michael Best & Friedrich.

**Julia Hahn: From Journalism to Policy Aide**
Julia Hahn, a former employee of Breitbart News and now a White House aide, possesses assets that could potentially be worth up to **$2.3 million**. Her professional earnings include **$117,217 from her role as a senior investigative reporter at Breitbart News** and **$74,082 as the executive producer of the Laura Ingraham show**. Hahn also holds a stake valued between **$250,001 and $500,000 in Cherrywood Partnership**, a real estate investment company.

**Peter Navarro: Economic Expertise and Diverse Investments**
Peter Navarro, who directs the National Trade Council, previously earned a salary of **$240,000** as an economics professor at the University of California-Irvine. His disclosed assets, including stocks, bonds, and investment property, exceed **$1 million**. A significant portion of these assets is tied to his pension and mortgaged properties, reflecting a diversified investment strategy.

**Jason Greenblatt: Real Estate Law and Financial Markets**
Jason Greenblatt, a former lawyer for the Trump Organization who now advises on Middle East policy, reported assets exceeding **$1.4 million**, primarily concentrated in stock mutual funds. His earnings in the past year from the Trump Organization amounted to **$1.02 million**.

**Keith Kellogg: National Security and Corporate Investments**
National Security Council Chief of Staff Keith Kellogg reported earning **$96,000** in salary and severance from Cubic Corp. His financial disclosures also indicate investments worth at least **$366,000**. Furthermore, he has loans totaling at least **$600,000**, including a mortgage.

**Makan Delrahim: Legal Practice and Entertainment Investment**
Deputy White House Counsel Makan Delrahim earned over **$1 million** in the past year through a combination of salary, stock payouts, and a buyout from his law firm, Brownstein Hyatt Farber Schreck. His financial interests extend to being a passive investor in the movie "Trash Fire."

This reporting was conducted by Associated Press writers Julie Bykowicz, Chad Day, Stephen Braun, Jack Gillum, Michael Biesecker, Ted Bridis, Bernard Condon, and Ken Sweet.

Bannon reported an ownership stake of $1 million or more in a data-crunching firm that worked for Sen. Ted Cruz (R-Texas) during the presidential race. He also took in $191,000 in consulting fees in 2016 as executive chairman of the conservative media website Breitbart News, which sided with Trump during the Republican primary. Story Continued Below The disclosure highlighted Bannon's ties to a pair of conservative political donors, Robert and Rebekah Mercer. The couple, which funded the upstart data company Cambridge Analytica, backed Cruz early on but eventually swung their allegiance to Trump. Cambridge Analytica later worked for Trump, and now it is seeking government contracts. Bannon’s disclosure form said that he has “an agreement in principle” to sell his stake in Cambridge. Bannon also reported income from the Government Accountability Institute and the film production company Glittering Steel, both of which are linked to the Mercers, who also are part owners of Breitbart. The documents released Friday night also put on public view new details about two Trump family members who have taken official White House posts: Trump's daughter Ivanka and son-in-law, Jared Kushner. Ivanka Trump’s self-branded businesses, including her clothing line, brought in more than $5 million since the beginning of 2016 and are valued at over $50 million, the documents showed. Ivanka recently assumed an official assistant-to-the-president post, so no disclosure has been released for her yet, but her husband's form also includes her assets. She is putting the Ivanka brands in a trust she will no longer manage directly, her legal advisers say. Meanwhile, Kushner resigned from positions with 266 different entities in order to begin work as a senior adviser to Trump, a senior administration official told reporters Friday. The disclosures showed the wealth of an administration filled with real estate and finance experts alongside government hands. They show former National Security Adviser Michael Flynn got paid at least $5,000 for a speaking engagement with the Kremlin-funded English language network RT -- a disclosure Flynn left out of his initial January disclosures but which he revealed in an amended document that he signed Friday. The forms also offer a small window into how much people are paid at the privately-held Trump Organization. Jason Greenblatt listed a little more than $1 million in pay from the president’s company for his work as its executive vice president and chief legal officer. Greenblatt spent a decade working in Trump Tower before joining the White House in January as Trump’s special representative for international negotiations. The forms being made public cover White House staff making more than $161,755 a year, as well as anyone who's a commissioned officer — people who hold a title like assistant or special assistant to the president. Trump's own form was not among those released Friday. He filed the disclosure twice as a candidate, in July 2015 and May 2016. He's not required by law to file again until May 2018, but aides said they have not decided whether he will file an update this year. Here are highlights from the newly-disclosed financial reports: Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump Ivanka Trump’s investment in the Trump International Hotel in Washington is valued at between $5 million and $25 million and brought her between $1 million and $5 million in income since its opening in September, the disclosure says. Meanwhile, Kushner made $4.5 million in 2016 from the company that owns the New York Observer. He's sold his stake in the firm. Kushner has sold or is selling several other operating businesses, including an insurance brokerage, a broadband firm and a window and door company. But he’s holding on to well over 100 other real estate assets, primarily buildings in the New York City area. Critics say his investments could pose a conflict of interest as he tackles a broad, almost unbounded portfolio of issues for his father-in-law. Omarosa Manigault The reality television star-turned-Trump White House communications staffer accepted a wedding dress, custom veil and other accessories -- worth an estimated $25,000 -- to appear on an episode of TLC’s “Say Yes to the Dress.” She also listed on her financial disclosures between $7,100 and $18,000 for rents or royalties surrounding her role in several television shows, including syndication of Trump’s ‘The Apprentice.’ She is also a one-third beneficiary of a trust worth between $1 million and $5 million that was established by her late fiancée Michael Clarke Duncan, an Oscar-nominated actor who died in 2012. Gary Cohn The former Goldman Sachs chief operating officer now heading the National Economic Council director reported assets worth at least $200 million and perhaps $1 billion or more, since the forms generally use broad ranges and don't even require a range for assets worth over $50 million. His income in the past year was between about $48 million and $76 million. Cohn did not include details about selling off his investment stakes in the financial disclosure, as other senior White House staff did, though it has previously been reported that he sold his shares in Goldman for approximately $285 million. Earlier this month the New York Times also reported that Cohn planned to sell a stake in one of China’s largest banks. The total stock is listed as worth over $1 million in his disclosure though the Times reported it was worth approximately $16 million. In addition to his Goldman holdings, Cohn reported stock worth as much as $250,000 in Bank of America, which could pose a conflict should the administration rewrite the financial regulatory regime put in place by Dodd-Frank. He also reported stock in Facebook and the president’s favorite social media, Twitter. Cohn also reported ownership stakes in several commercial real estate properties, technology patents, and a helicopter. Don McGahn The chief White House counsel left behind $2.4 million in pay as a Jones Day partner. At the firm, McGahn’s clients included Trump, then-Sen. Mark Kirk (R-Ill.), the National Rifle Association, the GOP data firm i360 and Aaron Schock, the former Illinois Republican congressman under indictment on federal criminal corruption charges. Stephen Miller Trump’s top policy adviser on the campaign made $124,885 in 2016, according to his financial disclosure. At the same time, he listed as an employer “Insperity PEO Services, L.P.,” which he wrote on his form is the name of his parents' real estate company, where he had an advisory, unpaid position while working on the campaign. Reed Cordish One of the Trump family’s closest friends is now serving in the White House. His financial disclosure forms show he maintains a stake in companies and holdings worth tens of millions of dollars, including casinos, real estate and restaurants in Baltimore, Kansas City, St. Louis, Richmond, Louisville, Kentucky, and Arlington, Texas. He’s also a beneficiary of a family trust worth between $5 million and $25 million. Breaking News Alerts Get breaking news when it happens — in your inbox. Email Sign Up By signing up you agree to receive email newsletters or alerts from POLITICO. You can unsubscribe at any time. Katie Walsh The White House deputy chief of staff who departed Thursday in a staff-shake up made more than $600,000 in the last year. About $320,000 of that was from the Republican National Committee and about $283,000 came from her Missouri-based political consulting firm, the Laymont Group. Sean Spicer Press secretary Sean Spicer made $260,000 in his former post as spokesman for the Republican National Committee. But he shares another passion with the man he represents in the briefing room: real estate investment. The combative press aide owns commercial and rental properties in Rhode Island, Alabama and Virginia worth between $2.6 million and $5.2 million, according to his financial disclosure. He also has a family trust worth between $500,000 and $1 million, and between $45,000 and $135,000 in income last year from stock funds. Peter Navarro Peter Navarro, director of the newly-created National Trade Council, made $240,000 in his previous job as a professor of economics and public policy at the University of California-Irvine. He also earned extra income of $10,500 for delivering a keynote speech to the Casket & Funeral Supply Association of America on Nov. 15, 2016. Shane Goldmacher, Colin Wilhelm, Matthew Nussbaum, Tara Palmeri, Annie Karni, Isaac Arnsdorf and Kenneth Vogel contributed to this report.

## Trump Administration Officials Reveal Extensive Financial Holdings

The White House has released extensive financial disclosure forms for dozens of its top administration officials, revealing significant real estate investments and business entanglements among President Donald Trump's inner circle. These disclosures offer a snapshot of the wealth and financial interests held by individuals serving in key government roles.

### Kushner and Ivanka Trump's Substantial Portfolio

President Trump's son-in-law, Jared Kushner, and daughter, Ivanka Trump, are holding onto a substantial portfolio of at least $240 million in assets, including numerous real estate investments, while serving in White House positions. Kushner, a senior adviser, has resigned from approximately 260 entities and divested from 58 businesses or investments identified by lawyers as potential conflicts of interest. However, legal counsel, in consultation with the Office of Government Ethics, has determined that Kushner's real estate holdings, particularly in New York City, are unlikely to necessitate further divestment. Jamie Gorelick, an attorney involved in their ethics agreements, stated that the remaining conflicts are "pretty narrow and very manageable." Kushner began divesting from problematic assets shortly after the election, with some transactions predating the disclosure requirements. For instance, he sold his stake in a Manhattan skyscraper to a trust managed by his mother, with neither he nor Ivanka Trump holding any financial interest in it. The Kushner Companies, now managed by his relatives, are reportedly seeking investment partners for a major redevelopment of this building.

### Bannon's Financial Landscape

Steve Bannon, President Trump's chief strategist, disclosed assets ranging from $13 million to $56 million. This includes his influential political consultancy, Bannon Strategic Advisors Inc., valued at up to $25 million. Bannon also reported earning slightly less than $200,000 in the previous year as executive director of Breitbart News Network LLC before resigning to join the Trump campaign. Further disclosures show he served as vice president of the data firm Cambridge Analytica for over two years, resigning in August 2016 to manage Trump's campaign. Cambridge Analytica was a primary data provider for Senator Ted Cruz during his presidential bid. Bannon's consulting firm received over $125,000 from Cambridge last year, and he holds a stake in the firm valued between $1 million and $5 million, with an "agreement in principle" to sell this investment.

### Other High-Profile Officials' Disclosures

White House Chief of Staff Reince Priebus reported receiving over $500,000 in salary and bonuses from the Republican National Committee, which he led during the presidential primaries. He also earned at least $750,000 from equity buyout and partner-distribution income from the law firm Michael Best & Friedrich. Gary Cohn, who left a prominent role at Goldman Sachs to serve as Trump's chief economic adviser, is among the wealthiest officials. His financial disclosure indicates at least $40 million in income from Goldman Sachs-related dividends, interest, salary, and bonuses, with roughly half in stock compensation. Cohn also reported over $1 million in income from the Industrial and Commercial Bank of China; the White House has stated he is in the process of divesting from both this bank and his Goldman holdings. White House Press Secretary Sean Spicer characterized the business professionals joining the administration as "very blessed and very successful," asserting that the disclosures demonstrate their commitment to public service.

### Conflict of Interest and Divestment

These financial disclosures, mandated by law, provide a snapshot of officials' financial standing upon entering the White House. However, the specific ethics agreements detailing how potential conflicts of interest will be managed are not being publicly released, according to White House lawyers. While "certificates of divestiture" for employees seeking capital gains tax deferrals on sold assets will eventually be accessible, these detailed agreements remain private. Jared Kushner, for example, received such certificates for financial interests in several assets, including funds linked to his brother Joshua Kushner's investment firm, Thrive Capital. Kushner and Ivanka Trump have stepped away from businesses valued at least $50 million each, though they retain financial interests in many of them, similar to the President. Ivanka Trump is set to become a federal employee and will file her own disclosure later.

### Debt and International Dealings

Jared Kushner's disclosure also reveals tens of millions of dollars in bank debt incurred in 2015 and 2016, including liabilities with several international banks whose interests could intersect with the Trump administration's agenda. Kushner has as much as $25 million in liabilities to Deutsche Bank, a significant lender to Kushner family real estate ventures and a major creditor for Trump Organization-branded projects. Additionally, Kushner and his father, Charles, have liabilities up to $5 million with the U.S. unit of Israel Discount Bank. The Kushner Companies have also acknowledged "long-standing" relationships with two other Israeli banks. Financial information for Cabinet members requiring Senate confirmation has largely been available for weeks through the Office of Government Ethics. President Trump is required to file periodic financial disclosures, with his next due next year.

The Trump administration's **financial disclosures** present a stark contrast to its "drain the swamp" promise. These documents reveal that **top officials, including Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner, possess substantial wealth**. Combined, Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner may hold financial assets exceeding **$740 million**. White House Chief Strategist Steve Bannon's assets are estimated at **$56 million**, while White House Chief of Staff Reince Priebus reported earning **$1.4 million in 2016**, nearly half of which came from the Republican National Committee.

These disclosures **underscore the significant financial holdings of President Trump's closest advisors** and raise **ethical questions** about these high-ranking administration members. While the White House plans to release more disclosures, those belonging to President Trump and Vice President Mike Pence will **not be included**.

White House press secretary Sean Spicer defended the release, calling the disclosures **"more accessible, more available than in history."** He explained President Trump's decision not to release his tax returns, stating it's **"apples and oranges"** as the financial disclosures are **"required by law."** Spicer emphasized the administration is **"going above and beyond what has been done in the past"** to ensure public access.

The administration's wealth is viewed as a **point of pride** by some. Spicer suggested it should be **"celebrated,"** believing it demonstrates Trump's ability to surround himself with **"very blessed and very successful"** individuals. He noted that their willingness to list assets and undergo public scrutiny, **"set aside a lot,"** speaks volumes about their commitment to the president's vision and agenda.

According to USA Today, the White House is set to release up to **180 financial disclosures** from top staffers. These initial releases highlight the **extraordinary wealth** within the administration. Prominent figures include Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner, alongside Kellyanne Conway with her **multimillion-dollar portfolio**, Gary Cohn, former CEO of Goldman Sachs now leading Trump's National Economic Council, and Dina Habib Powell, another former Goldman executive.

Notably, the disclosures reveal that Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner **continue to profit from businesses they left** to join the White House. Kushner still benefits from his family's real estate empire, and Ivanka Trump retains an ownership stake valued between **$5 million and $25 million** in the Trump International Hotel in Washington, D.C.

Further details include payments to Kellyanne Conway for consulting work with the Center for Medical Progress, an anti-abortion organization known for its undercover videos of Planned Parenthood. Reince Priebus earned **three-quarters of a million dollars** from a Milwaukee law firm where he has been on leave since 2011.

The financial disclosures also cast doubt on the timeline of Steve Bannon's departure from Breitbart. While the disclosures suggest he resigned in August, coinciding with his joining Trump's campaign, Breitbart CEO Larry Solov stated Bannon resigned **"on or about Nov. 13,"** after the election.

The financial disclosures of Trump administration appointees offer a compelling glimpse into the substantial wealth and diverse income streams of those serving in high-level government positions. The White House's release of approximately 180 financial disclosure forms for its officials has painted a vivid picture, leading many to characterize this as the wealthiest administration in American history. This influx of significant personal wealth into public service raises important questions about potential conflicts of interest and the intricate dance between private financial interests and public duty.

Among the most prominent figures are Jared Kushner, President Trump's son-in-law and a key White House adviser, and his wife, Ivanka Trump. Their extensive financial disclosure, spanning an impressive 54 pages, meticulously details a vast array of assets and liabilities held by both individuals. As influential advisors, they have chosen to serve in unpaid roles, a decision made to navigate the complexities of anti-nepotism regulations. Kushner received clearance for his advisory role in January, while Ivanka Trump officially announced her White House position more recently. Together, their disclosed assets are valued at over $200 million. The New York Times highlighted that despite their new government responsibilities, they "will remain the beneficiaries of a sprawling real estate and investment business still worth as much as $741 million." To proactively address potential conflicts of interest, Kushner undertook a significant divestment, stepping down from dozens of businesses and resigning from over 260 positions. His legal counsel, in close consultation with the Office of Government Ethics, concluded that his substantial real estate holdings, many concentrated in New York City, were unlikely to necessitate further divestment due to conflict concerns.

Ivanka Trump's disclosure also reveals a significant stake in the Trump International Hotel in Washington, D.C., an asset valued between $5 million and $25 million. For the period of 2016 and part of 2017, she reported earnings from this stake ranging from $1 million to $5 million. As she assumes her official White House role, her comprehensive financial disclosures and ethics agreements are anticipated. Notably, The Times reported that she will retain her ownership stake in the Washington hotel while serving in her governmental capacity.

The financial landscape of other appointees also commands attention. Steve Bannon, formerly the executive chairman of Breitbart News and President Trump's chief strategist, reported substantial earnings and assets. The Wall Street Journal indicated that Bannon earned at least $1.4 million in the year preceding his administration appointment and possessed assets valued between $10.7 million and $48.6 million. His disclosed income sources included Breitbart News ($191,000), Cambridge Analytica ($125,333), and the Government Accountability Institute ($61,539). These organizations have notable connections to hedge fund manager Robert Mercer and his daughter Rebekah Mercer, who are prominent figures in funding conservative political initiatives. Bannon's largest reported income stream, exceeding $493,000, originated from Bannon Strategic Advisors, Inc. The New York Times suggested this company likely functioned as an umbrella entity for fees derived from various interests, including Breitbart News, Cambridge Analytica, and Glittering Steel, a company associated with the documentary "Clinton Cash."

Gary Cohn, who served as the director of the National Economic Council and previously as the president of Goldman Sachs, stands out as one of the wealthiest Trump appointees. Bloomberg reported that Cohn held assets totaling at least $254 million and garnered income of at least $48.3 million between 2016 and early 2017. His compensation from Goldman Sachs in 2016 included a base salary of $1.85 million and a $5.74 million cash bonus from 2015. According to reports from AP and CNBC, when factoring in dividends and interest from Goldman-affiliated assets, Cohn's earnings from the organization reached at least $40 million. He also received over $1 million in dividends from the Industrial and Commercial Bank of China.

The financial disclosures for former national security adviser Michael Flynn also provide insight into his financial dealings. An initial form, signed on February 11, did not list any payments from Russia-linked organizations. Flynn's resignation as national security adviser occurred just two days later, following reports of his meeting with Russia's ambassador and subsequent misrepresentations to Vice President Mike Pence. A subsequent disclosure, signed on March 31, did include "speaking engagements" with RT TV, Kaspersky Government Security Solutions, Inc., and Volga-Dnepr Airlines. These payments were itemized in the section designated for "sources of compensation exceeding $5,000 in a year." NPR reported, citing separate documents released by congressional Democrats, that Flynn's income from these three entities collectively surpassed $50,000. Flynn's more recent disclosure indicates at least $1.37 million in income, with $827,055 attributed to his consulting firm, Flynn Intel Group LLC, which had been inactive since December.

These publicly released financial disclosure documents offer a valuable snapshot of the holdings of these appointees at the commencement of their government service. Information regarding subsequent divestitures or resignations is slated for later release. It is important to note that some appointees are still in the process of divesting assets, a step typically completed prior to assuming public office. However, the Trump administration announced many of its appointees before these complex financial negotiations had reached their conclusion. Data compiled by the Office of Government Ethics suggests that the Trump White House has proceeded at a slower pace than the Obama administration in submitting nominees' financial arrangements for official review. The White House has attributed this delay to the intricate and substantial financial portfolios of its numerous wealthy appointees. Media estimations place the cumulative wealth of the Trump Cabinet within a broad range of $6 billion to $14 billion.

The release of these financial disclosure forms is a mandatory component of federal ethics law. This legislation requires high-ranking executive branch appointees to transparently disclose their financial holdings and to establish formal agreements with ethics officials. These agreements are designed to meticulously prevent any conflicts that might arise between an appointee's personal financial interests and their official governmental duties. Such agreements often necessitate the sale of assets, resignation from certain positions, or recusal from specific matters where a conflict of interest could emerge. The President and Vice President, as elected officials, file their financial disclosures at a later juncture and are exempt from many of the ethics regulations that apply to their staff.

New financial disclosures provide a granular view of the personal finances of President Trump's senior White House staff. These filings detail the wealth of approximately 180 individuals, revealing an administration populated by numerous millionaires whose collective net worth surpasses $12 billion. The disclosures also highlight specific financial details, including significant gifts received by a prominent aide and substantial compensation earned by former media professionals.

Here are seven key insights from the financial disclosures:

Omarosa Manigault, a former "Apprentice" contestant and current White House aide, received an estimated $25,000 in merchandise from Kleinfeld Bridal. This was a gift acknowledging her appearance on the TLC program "Say Yes to the Dress."

White House Chief Strategist Stephen Bannon received up to $100,000 in television show royalties. This income derived from a long-standing arrangement involving the production entity behind the 1990s series "Seinfeld" and Bannon's investment firm.

Bannon also garnered over $125,000 in consulting fees from Cambridge Analytica. He previously held the positions of vice president and secretary for the firm, which receives substantial funding from GOP benefactor Robert Mercer.

Kellyanne Conway, Counselor to the President, was compensated more than $5,000 for each of 66 distinct consulting engagements prior to her White House tenure.

Julia Hahn, a key aide to Bannon, earned over $117,000 as a reporter for Breitbart News. Additionally, she generated $74,000 as a producer for conservative radio personality Laura Ingraham. In total, Hahn reported investments valued up to $2.5 million.

KT McFarland, now serving as Trump's deputy national security adviser and formerly a Fox News analyst, reported over $125,000 in income from speaking engagements in the preceding year. These payments contribute to the millions of dollars in income and assets listed by her and her spouse on their filings.

The disclosures further illuminated the considerable wealth of Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner. They are beneficiaries of the Kushner family's real estate enterprise, valued at up to $740 million. Ivanka Trump's personal business trust, encompassing her fashion brand, is valued at over $50 million, according to Kushner's financial disclosure.

This document details the financial disclosures of several individuals associated with the Trump administration, highlighting their reported earnings, assets, and debts. These disclosures, made public as part of government ethics requirements, offer a glimpse into the financial backgrounds of key personnel.

Sebastian Gorka, who previously served as a deputy assistant to President Trump and was an editor at Breitbart News, reported receiving $38,200 in consulting fees from Breitbart. He also disclosed royalties ranging from $50,000 to $100,000 for his book, "Defeating Jihad: The Winnable War." Furthermore, Gorka indicated that he had signed a contract for a second book.

Boris Epshteyn, a prominent figure during the presidential campaign known for his role as a television spokesperson for Mr. Trump, still has outstanding college loans exceeding $50,000. These loans were taken out over a decade ago, according to his financial filing. Epshteyn recently departed his White House position under unspecified circumstances.

Jason Greenblatt, an attorney with the Trump Organization, was tasked with efforts to achieve peace in the Middle East. His financial disclosure shows he earned $1,025,000 in compensation from Mr. Trump's company in the past year.

White House Press Secretary Sean Spicer addressed the financial disclosures at a daily briefing. He characterized the wealth of individuals joining the administration as something to be celebrated. Spicer stated that the President has brought many successful individuals into his administration, particularly the White House. He emphasized that these individuals have made significant sacrifices by setting aside many of their assets to serve in government. Spicer suggested that their willingness to disclose all assets, endure public scrutiny, and relinquish substantial holdings demonstrates their commitment to the President's vision and agenda.

The release of these financial disclosures on a Friday evening aligns with a common practice in Washington D.C. of releasing large volumes of documents late in the week. This timing often occurs when the public is less likely to be actively reviewing such information, drawing a parallel to a quote from the fictional character Josh Lyman, Deputy Chief of Staff on "The West Wing," who remarked, "No one reads the paper on Saturday."

The document also lists several other individuals who have submitted disclosure forms. These include:

*   Stephen K. Bannon, Counselor to the President.
*   Thomas Bossert, Assistant to the President for Homeland Security and Counterterrorism and Deputy National Security Adviser.
*   James W. Carroll, Senior Counsel.
*   Justin Clark, Deputy Assistant to the President and Director of Intergovernmental Affairs.

query: What were Donald Trump's initial ambitions regarding the health care deal, and how did they ultimately end in failure?

documents.text: Donald Trump crowed for months he would strike a "terrific" deal on health care. His ambitions ended in a brief phone call Friday afternoon, in which Speaker Paul Ryan told him the truth: He was nowhere close. The businessman president, who sold himself to tens of millions of disillusioned voters last year as the only outsider who could tame a broken capital, ended his first confrontation with lawmakers overmatched, outmaneuvered and ultimately empty-handed. Story Continued Below “We learned a lot about some very arcane rules,” Trump said in the Oval Office soon after the defeat of his effort to undo President Obama’s health care law. It was actually the most basic fact of Congress that set Trump back: the majority rules. And despite a 22-seat margin for error in the House, Trump had proved unable to corral support for a plan to repeal the law, one of Trump’s key campaign pledges. His failure to advance legislation through a single chamber of Congress controlled by members of his own party — despite it being a cornerstone of the Republican agenda for more than half-decade — casts doubt both on Trump’s much-bragged-about dealmaking skills and the GOP’s path forward. “It's a black eye for the speaker and the president,” said Scott Reed, the top strategist of the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, which supported the measure. The setback, described in interviews with multiple senior administration and congressional officials, was especially humiliating because Trump was sunk not by Democrats but by his inability to ride herd over the same rebellious element of the Republican conference that previously bucked Speaker Paul Ryan’s predecessor John Boehner out of his job. Most House Republicans have never served in the majority under a Republican president and it’s unclear how in the future they will cobble together a governing coalition. “The Republican Party is still operating as an opposition party,” said Josh Holmes, a former chief of staff to Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell, who added, “If they can't break the fever…it says an enormous amount about the prospects of tax reform, infrastructure and some sort of immigration proposal.” Trump himself seemed almost relieved to move on from the health care fight, even as other White House officials were fretting about the long-term implications of what one senior White House official called a "clear embarrassment for us." The president himself wasn’t nearly as upset about the health care defeat as he was about the size of his inauguration crowds, Attorney General Jeff Sessions’ decision to recuse himself from the investigation into Russia’s meddling in the 2016 election or the repeated legal setbacks on his travel-ban executive order, which has been blocked by multiple courts. “No bullshit, I think he's actually pretty comfortable with the outcome. He wants to move ahead and do taxes,” another senior White House official said. But tax reform — which no president or Congress has been able to tackle successfully for more than three decades — is no easy task, especially as Republicans had banked on deficit-cutting from any health package to give them a financial cushion in that endeavor. “Yes,” Ryan conceded Friday, “this does make tax reform more difficult.” For weeks Trump had seemed disinterested and disengaged from the specifics of the health care fight, both behind closed doors with his aides and at public rallies. Trump “just wanted to get something he could sign,” said one adviser who talks to him frequently. “He was over it.” He would often interrupt conversations on the law to talk about other issues, advisers and aides said. In one phone call with Ryan earlier this month, Trump told the House speaker that he had a problem with the bill. It wasn’t over Medicaid expansion, maternity coverage, deductibles or insurance premiums. Rather, it was that he didn’t like the word “buckets”—which Ryan had been using to describe the parts of their plan. “I don't like that word buckets. You throw trash in buckets. I don't like that word,” Trump said, according to two people familiar with the call. Trump preferred “phases.” Ryan agreed and adopted the term. It was the kind of messaging detail Trump focused on during the campaign, when he described his plan as “repeal and replace — with something terrific.” But by February, Trump confided to a group of the nation’s governors visiting Washington D.C. that undoing Obamacare would be more involved. “Nobody knew healthcare could be so complicated,” he said. In the closing days of negotiations, Trump found himself stuck in the middle of an ideological tug-of-war between the two factions at the extremes of a deeply fissured House Republican conference — with every concession offered to the hardline House Freedom Caucus driving away votes from the moderate Tuesday Group, and vice versa. The arguments for the legislation from the White House were political. “This is your chance,” press secretary Sean Spicer urged lawmakers on Friday hours before the bill died. The arguments against it were based in policy, where Trump was less comfortable. When Trump trekked to Capitol Hill to make his case in person on Tuesday, lawmakers were mostly nonplussed. “Not a whole lot about health care, except to vote for it,” Rep. Walter Jones, R-N.C., said afterward. He remained opposed. Other lawmakers said Trump wasn’t at all conversant in the specifics and mainly wanted to talk about his popularity in their districts – or how voting against the proposed bill could hurt them politically. But the proposed law ended up polling below 20 percent – and the president's approval rating dropped below 40 percent. Short the votes, Trump trotted out one of his favorite deal-making tactics late Thursday to seal the deal: the ultimatum. All House Republicans were told by Trump’s top lieutenants that the bill was take-it-or-leave-it proposition, and their only chance to get rid of Obamacare. In the days and hours leading up to a vote that looked bleak, the White House did little to tamp down expectations. “He is the closer,” Spicer labeled Trump. Trump pushed hard for a floor vote up until the final minute. Senior administration officials maintained deep into Friday afternoon that they wanted the roll call "to make them vote against the president on live TV,” in the words of a third senior White House official. “We are voting,” this person said, 45 minutes before it was called off. It was straight out of Trump’s The Art of the Deal playbook, where he wrote simultaneously about boldness and maintaining a willingness to retreat. “I never get too attached to one deal or one approach,” Trump wrote. “For starters, I keep a lot of balls in the air because most deals fall out.” That might work in the real estate business, where there’s always another property to develop, or another contractor to hire. But it backfired in Congress, where time and political capital are not unlimited, where the last deal impacts the next and where there is no alternative set of lawmakers to whom Trump can pitch his next demands. The setback could also shake up the internal dynamics of a White House, where infighting among rival factions has become the norm. Some pointedly noted that Trump’s son-in-law and senior adviser, Jared Kushner, spent the run-up to the vote in Aspen. Some pinned blame on chief of staff Reince Priebus, who is close to Ryan and had been expected to help Trump navigate the complexities of Capitol Hill. Others faulted chief strategist Steve Bannon for failing to mobilize his former company, Breitbart News, on behalf of the bill, or to corral hardliners on the Hill. Breaking News Alerts Get breaking news when it happens — in your inbox. Email Sign Up By signing up you agree to receive email newsletters or alerts from POLITICO. You can unsubscribe at any time. Indeed, the blame game began even before the vote was called off. “This is 100 percent a Ryan failure,” said the third White House official. While Trump wasn’t upset with Ryan, multiple senior officials said, a number of his advisers and aides were trashing the speaker before the vote – and Ryan told others he expected to get the brunt of the blame. By midday Friday, the health care bill was hemorrhaging support, as the powerful chairman of the House Appropriations Committee came out against the bill. Ryan came to the White House to brief Trump on the bleak outlook. But even as Trump and his top advisers wanted to forge ahead, they were showing sign of worry. Spicer no longer embraced the term “the closer.” GOP leadership pushed to drop what was now seen as a kamikaze mission. And a little after 3 p.m., Trump talked to Ryan again — the two had a 45-minute conversation late Thursday night about the law. “He talked to Paul Ryan for a few minutes, who said he was at least 10 and 15 votes short,” one of the senior White House officials said. Ryan said he planned to pull the vote unless Trump objected, and Trump said he was OK with that. Ryan explained soon after what it meant to a national television audience: “We’re going to be living with Obamacare for the foreseeable future.” Trump got off the phone, scribbled down some notes and dialed up reporters to give his side before the full White House staff was even briefed. The president was most focused on the news coverage and how it reflected on him, as he had been throughout, telling advisers how much the criticism of the law on TV bothered him. In the Capitol basement after the vote was cancelled, Republican lawmakers were gathered to hear what came next. The Rolling Stones refrain Trump often played at his rallies was playing in the background: “You can’t always get what you want.” Tara Palmeri and Jake Sherman contributed reporting.

## Republican Healthcare Overhaul Fails: AHCA Pulled Amidst Party Divisions

Republicans suffered a significant legislative defeat on Friday when they were forced to withdraw their proposed legislation to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act (ACA) from the House floor. After weeks of intense negotiations surrounding the American Health Care Act (AHCA), the party conceded failure, unable to secure enough support from within their own ranks for the ambitious overhaul of the U.S. health insurance system. This dramatic turn of events highlighted deep divisions within the Republican party and the formidable challenge of legislating complex issues like healthcare.

### Blame Game and Shifting Priorities

President Donald Trump, addressing the situation from the Oval Office, pointed fingers at Democrats for the bill's collapse, characterizing the ACA as "the signature achievement of Barack Obama." He expressed a willingness to collaborate, stating, "If [Democrats] got together with us, and got us a real healthcare bill, I’d be totally OK with that." Trump then squarely placed blame on Democratic leaders, declaring, "The losers are Nancy Pelosi and Chuck Schumer, because they own Obamacare. They 100% own it." While refraining from criticizing House Speaker Paul Ryan, the President sidestepped questions regarding specific policy adjustments for health reform, instead pivoting to the administration's next legislative priority: tax reform. "We’re probably going to start going very strongly on big tax cuts. Tax reform that will be next," he announced, adding a reflective note, "We all learned a lot. We learned a lot about loyalty."

### The Road to Withdrawal

Earlier that day, as opposition within the Republican party solidified, Speaker Paul Ryan personally informed President Trump that the bill lacked the necessary votes for passage. White House Press Secretary Sean Spicer had, until that point, maintained that the vote would proceed as scheduled. He told reporters, "Has the team put everything out there, have we left everything on the field? Absolutely. But at the end of the day this isn’t a dictatorship and we’ve got to expect members to ultimately vote how they will according to what they think." However, the 3:30 PM ET deadline passed without a vote being called. Subsequently, reports emerged indicating that President Trump had requested the bill be pulled. Minutes later, House Republicans officially announced the withdrawal of the legislation.

### Acknowledging Growing Pains

At a subsequent press conference, Speaker Ryan candidly acknowledged the inherent difficulties of transitioning from an opposition party to a governing party. "Moving from an opposition party to a governing party comes with growing pains and, well, we’re feeling those growing pains today. I will not sugarcoat this: this is a disappointing day for us," he stated. He admitted that after nearly a decade of campaigning against the ACA, Republicans had failed to coalesce around a viable alternative. "We are going to be living with Obamacare for the foreseeable future," Ryan conceded. He explained that he had recommended the bill's withdrawal upon realizing the vote count was insufficient, while also commending President Trump's efforts, describing him as "fantastic." When pressed on how Republicans would address constituents after failing to deliver on their central campaign promise, Ryan responded with a candid, "That’s a really good question. I wish I had a better answer for you."

### Shifting Focus to Other Priorities

A reporter from The Washington Post later recounted a conversation with President Trump, during which the President indicated that the healthcare bill would not be revisited in the immediate future. Speaker Ryan also confirmed that Republicans would be shifting their focus to other key priorities, including securing the border, rebuilding the military, and enacting tax reform. "Now we’re going on to move on with the rest of our agenda because we have big, ambitious plans to improve people’s lives in this country," Ryan stated.

### Surprise and Internal Disagreement

Despite considerable speculation, the bill's withdrawal came as a surprise to many Republican members. An emergency House Republican Caucus meeting had been convened shortly before the scheduled vote. As this meeting commenced, the House entered a recess, with Democratic members audibly chanting "Vote, vote, vote" in an effort to pressure Republicans. Speaker Ryan then emerged to announce the bill's withdrawal in a brief statement to the press.

### Key Obstacles to Passage

A significant factor contributing to the bill's demise was the hesitation of many moderate Republicans representing swing districts. These members expressed concern over the substantial cuts to Medicaid proposed in the AHCA. The non-partisan Congressional Budget Office had estimated that these cuts would lead to approximately 24 million fewer Americans having health insurance over a ten-year period. Conversely, conservative Republicans objected to the bill for what they perceived as its retention of too much of the ACA's existing structure. Despite a concession made on Thursday to remove the federal mandate for essential health benefits, such as maternity and mental healthcare, this adjustment failed to sway enough conservative votes. Nevada Republican Mark Amodei highlighted a critical deficiency, noting a lack of discussion on a "unified Republican vision for what we should do with healthcare in the House." Paul Gosar, a member of the hard-right Freedom Caucus, attributed the setback to issues with White House staff.

### Political Ramifications and Reactions

The failure to pass the AHCA represented a major political blow to Speaker Paul Ryan, who had spearheaded the legislative effort. It also placed President Trump in a vulnerable position, having made the repeal of the "disaster" of Obamacare a cornerstone of his campaign. Despite his public persona as a skilled dealmaker, Trump's first significant legislative negotiation on Capitol Hill concluded in defeat. House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi hailed the outcome as "a great day for our country" and "a victory for the American people." Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer attributed the bill's failure to "incompetence and broken promises," delivering a sharp critique: "In my life, I have never seen an administration as incompetent as the one occupying the White House today. They can’t write policy that actually makes sense, they can’t implement the policies they do manage to write, they can’t get their stories straight, and today we’ve learned that they can’t close a deal, and they can’t count votes. So much for The Art of the Deal."

### Varied Interpretations and Future Uncertainty

Republican members offered varied interpretations of the events. Louie Gohmert of Texas, who had opposed the bill, blamed House leadership for cornering members and the President. "The president didn’t really get involved until after they created this bill and he was fighting for it," Gohmert remarked. Bradley Byrne of Alabama expressed continued willingness to support the AHCA, lamenting that House Republicans "fell just short" and praising the efforts of both Ryan and Trump. Some Republicans voiced uncertainty about the future of healthcare reform. Gohmert suggested that while he would not prioritize healthcare if he were president, as a legislator, the issue still required a correct resolution. Mark Walker, chair of the Republican Study Committee, acknowledged the setback, stating, "I can’t pretend that this is a win for us. I’m sure our friends on the left, this is a good moment for them."

President Donald Trump about the health care overhaul bill, Friday, March 24, 2017, in the Oval Office of the White House in Washington. rump says he would be willing to reopen negotiations for a health... (Associated Press) President Donald Trump about the health care overhaul bill, Friday, March 24, 2017, in the Oval Office of the White House in Washington. rump says he would be willing to reopen negotiations for a health... (Associated Press) WASHINGTON (AP) — Just two months in, Donald Trump's presidency is perilously adrift. His first major foray into legislating imploded Friday when House Republicans abandoned a White House-backed health care bill, resisting days of cajoling and arm-twisting from Trump himself. Aides who had confidently touted Trump as the deal's "closer" were left bemoaning the limits of the presidency. "At the end of the day, you can't force somebody to do something," White House spokesman Sean Spicer said. On its own, the health care bill's collapse was a stunning rejection of a new president by his own party. And for Trump, the defeat comes with an especially strong sting. The president who campaigned by promising "so much winning," has so far been beset by a steady parade of the opposite. With each setback and sidetrack, comes more concern about whether Trump, the outsider turned president, is capable of governing. "You can't just come in and steamroll everybody," said Bruce Miroff, a professor of American politics and the presidency at the State University of New York at Albany. "Most people have a modest understanding of how complicated the presidency is. They think leadership is giving orders and being bold. But the federal government is much more complicated, above all because the Constitution set it up that way." The ambitious agenda Trump vowed to quickly muscle through has now been blocked by both Congress and the courts. Whole weeks of his presidency have been consumed by crises that are often self-inflicted, including his explosive and unverified claim that President Barack Obama wiretapped his New York skyscraper. Earlier this week, the FBI director confirmed that Trump's campaign is being investigated for possible coordination with Russia during the election, an investigation that could hang over the White House for years. Trump's advisers say some of the churn is to be expected from a president with an unconventional style and little regard for Washington convention. They counter the notion of a White House in crisis by pointing to Trump's well-received nomination of Neil Gorsuch to the Supreme Court. They appeal for patience, noting that the administration is indeed in its early days. But early missteps can be difficult to overcome, particularly for a president like Trump, who took office with historically low favorability ratings and has continued to lose support since his inauguration. According to Friday's Gallup daily tracking poll, 54 percent of Americans disapprove of his performance on the job. James Thurber, who founded the Center for Congressional and Presidential Studies at American University, blamed Trump for an apparent "misunderstanding or ignorance of how the separation of powers works" that is hurting him at a time "where he should have much more success." Trump is hardly the first president to stumble through his early days in arguably one of the hardest jobs in the world. Bill Clinton's presidency got off to a chaotic start and was quickly shrouded by an ethics controversy involving the firing of employees at the White House travel office. Jimmy Carter, another Washington outsider, clashed with his own party. Richard Nixon struggled to unite a deeply divided nation. "There have been moments like this in history in terms of where the country is, in terms of the president kind of having a chaotic couple of months," said David Greenberg, a historian at Rutgers University. Still, he said Trump's challenges are exacerbated by his "complete inexperience in the political arena, his personality and style." Indeed, many of Trump's early struggles have been clearly avoidable. His courting of chaos undercuts his campaign promises to bring business efficiency to Washington. Infighting and gamesmanship among top White House aides, sometimes egged on by the president himself, consume a striking amount of attention in the West Wing. His wiretapping allegations against Obama put his advisers in the untenable position of trying to justify an allegation for which there is no evidence. His first, hastily written ban on travel to the U.S. from several countries was blocked by the courts, as was a rewrite, and judges cited his own campaign rhetoric in their rulings. The president's advisers had hoped the health care measure would give the White House a much-needed burst of momentum and prove to wary Republicans that it was worth sticking with Trump in order to accomplish something the party had sought for seven years. Hardly a policy wonk, Trump embraced the plan put forth by House Speaker Paul Ryan and promised Republican leaders he would invest his own political capital into rounding up votes. He did, spending hours working the phones with lawmakers, often early in the morning and late at night. His advisers held bowling and pizza nights for GOP lawmakers at the White House. But Ryan's decision to pull the bill Friday underscored Trump's limitations. His powers of persuasion couldn't overcome the ideological concerns of conservatives who are more popular in their home districts than Trump or the political fears of moderates worried about attaching themselves to an unpopular president. Trump, who has privately lashed out at his staff and publicly berated the media during other rough patches in his young presidency, was surprisingly sanguine in defeat. "We learned a lot about the vote-getting processes," Trump said. "For me, it's been a very interesting experience." ___ EDITOR'S NOTE: Julie Pace has covered the White House and politics for The Associated Press since 2007. Follow her at http://twitter.com/jpaceDC ___ This story has been corrected to change Wednesday to Friday in 2nd paragraph.

The Republican effort to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act (ACA) suffered a major legislative defeat this week. The bill, a central promise of President Donald Trump and Speaker Paul Ryan, was withdrawn from House consideration Friday, avoiding a guaranteed loss. Speaker Ryan acknowledged that "ObamaCare" would likely remain law "for the foreseeable future," signaling no immediate plans for another attempt. This outcome marks a significant moment for key players, with distinct winners and losers emerging from the political fallout.

**LOSERS**

**President Donald Trump:** This legislative failure is a substantial blow to President Trump, who built his campaign on promises of decisive action and deal-making. His first major legislative initiative faltered, despite his direct efforts to persuade wavering Republican members. The impact on his broader agenda and the potential for Republican frustration directed at him remain unclear. While Trump projected resilience, his comment about the ACA's persistence – "there’s not much you can do about it" – from a president with congressional majorities, highlighted a sense of powerlessness. The full extent of this setback will unfold over time.

**Speaker Paul Ryan (R-Wis.):** The failed healthcare bill is as damaging to Speaker Ryan as it is to the President. His inability to pass the American Health Care Act through the House is a profound embarrassment. Ryan was deeply committed to the legislation, which faced strong opposition from conservative members of his own party and influential interest groups. Some Trump supporters suggest Ryan prioritized healthcare over tax reform, a strategic error. Conservative media figures are questioning his leadership. Despite Trump's public support, Ryan's defeat reveals a struggle to command sufficient party discipline.

**Vice President Pence, Health and Human Services Secretary Tom Price, and OMB Director Mick Mulvaney:** These former House members were expected to use their insider knowledge to influence the ultra-conservative House Freedom Caucus and other lawmakers. Mulvaney, a founding member of the caucus, was particularly positioned to sway votes. However, their combined efforts failed to secure the necessary support, resulting in a notable political setback for each.

**MIXED**

**The House Freedom Caucus:** This conservative group successfully blocked the Republican healthcare bill, but the long-term effects of this victory are still uncertain. Led by Rep. Mark Meadows (R-N.C.), the caucus resisted presidential pressure. Their united stance was crucial to the bill's failure, demonstrating their significant influence. However, they now face the challenge of managing the consequences of opposing a president from their own party, who has strong support among many grassroots Republicans. Furthermore, their strategy of forcing a more accommodating proposal from leadership has not yet yielded results.

**WINNERS**

**Former President Barack Obama:** The survival of his signature domestic policy, the ACA, is a major victory. This occurred despite unified control of the White House and Congress by those who vowed to dismantle it. While Trump suggested the ACA would fail on its own, he did not commit to further repeal efforts, instead indicating openness to incremental reforms with Democratic help. The ACA's endurance strengthens Obama's presidential legacy.

**House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi (D-Calif.):** Pelosi showcased remarkable party unity, a stark contrast to Republican division. No Democrats broke ranks to support the Republican plan, creating a significant legislative hurdle. Pelosi, a skilled political strategist, appeared to enjoy the Republicans' internal struggles, famously calling it a "Rookie’s error, Donald Trump" after the initial vote delay.

**Jared Kushner:** Trump's son-in-law and a key advisor, Kushner reportedly opposed the immediate focus on healthcare. His absence from Washington for much of the week, attending a family ski trip, kept him physically and politically removed from the difficult negotiations. As a result, he emerged from this legislative failure relatively unharmed.

**Governors:** Several Republican governors strongly criticized the House replacement plan, citing concerns about its impact on Medicaid. Governors like John Kasich (Ohio), Brian Sandoval (Nevada), Asa Hutchinson (Arkansas), and Rick Snyder (Michigan) co-signed a letter to congressional leaders opposing the bill. Their stance provided political cover for lawmakers in their states who were hesitant to back the legislation.

**The AARP:** This organization representing older Americans actively campaigned against the ACA repeal bill. They labeled a proposed change an "age tax," highlighted the projected decrease in health insurance coverage, and framed the vote as an "accountability vote," signaling their intent to oppose lawmakers who voted against their interests. The AARP's successful advocacy demonstrated its considerable influence.

The dramatic collapse of the American Health Care Act unfolded over a tense 24-hour period, a political crucible that White House and congressional officials described as a significant learning experience for President Trump and his senior advisors. This account draws on insights from government officials who participated in the final negotiations and spoke on the condition of anonymity.

### A Political Education

"We all learned a lot," President Trump remarked to reporters on Friday afternoon. He elaborated, "We learned a lot about loyalty, and we learned a lot about the vote-getting process." The President acknowledged, "Certainly for me, it was a very interesting experience." While initially having limited involvement in the bill's creation, Mr. Trump became increasingly invested in recent weeks. He actively promoted the legislation at rallies outside Washington and convened meetings in the West Wing with both conservative and moderate factions, whose support was deemed essential for its passage.

### Shifting Priorities and Falling Short

Despite his efforts, Mr. Trump expressed a degree of ambivalence about prioritizing healthcare reform, stating in Nashville the previous week, "I would have loved to have put it first, I’ll be honest," referring to tax reform. Nevertheless, he conveyed to his aides his belief that the healthcare measure could not succeed without his direct advocacy. By Thursday afternoon, mere hours before a scheduled vote, it became evident that the combined efforts of the President, Vice President Mike Pence, and other senior White House officials had not secured sufficient support.

### The Freedom Caucus Standoff

A pivotal meeting in Speaker Paul Ryan's Capitol office with members of the resistant Freedom Caucus highlighted the deepening divisions. Top White House officials presented the concessions made at the group's request, including the removal of federal standards for essential health benefits such as maternity and wellness care. However, Freedom Caucus members proceeded to articulate further demands, sparking frustration among Speaker Ryan and President Trump's aides.

Stephen K. Bannon, the President's chief strategist, and Mick Mulvaney, his budget director, informed the caucus that negotiations had concluded and that the President required a definitive stance – yes or no. When called upon to declare their positions, several members declined. As the tension escalated, Representative Mark Meadows of North Carolina stated he was speaking for the group and that they were not yet prepared to commit, signaling that the necessary votes would not materialize.

President Trump addressed his plan to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act, saying he will let Obamacare "explode," before taking questions from the media on March 24 at the White House. (The Washington Post) Donald Trump was elected in large part on one, loud promise: I know how to make deals these normal politicians don't. Part of that mystique — as outlined in his best-selling book “The Art of the Deal” — is the willingness to call his rival's bluff, to put his cards on the table and ask everyone else to do the same. That's what Trump did Thursday night after a postponement of the planned vote to begin the process of reforming the Affordable Care Act. House Republicans needed to put up or shut up, Trump insisted. Despite being told the votes simply weren't there, Trump pushed forward — arguing that it was now or never. It was vintage Trump, taking a gamble no other typical politician would take: Force a vote on a massive part of your legislative agenda with an uncertain outcome. Then Trump blinked. Hours after Trump huddled with Speaker Paul D. Ryan at the White House, he told The Post's Bob Costa that he had decided congressional Republicans should pull the legislation. The votes simply weren't there, and the possibility of real embarrassment on the House floor existed. Sure. But, Trump was elected as an outsider — someone who ran against the system. Why not force that system to go on the record with either their support or opposition? I regret that @POTUS @realDonaldTrump and @SpeakerRyan agreed to pull the bill.
