The music video, filmed over the course of two days in the Holloway Sanatorium outside London, is also a cult classic for its otherworldly themes and non-sequitur scenes. “It was snowing on the ground, and I had to run barefoot,” Tyler remembered of making the clip. The Total Eclipse Cruise experience will be more straightforward for viewers — although certainly unique in its own way. While the cruise will continue on to visit islands in the Caribbean, Tyler will be ducking out to continue touring. Her most recent album, a Nashville-recorded collection is called Rocks and Honey. Write to Raisa Bruner at raisa.bruner@time.com.

**Oregon Man's Permanent Vision Loss from 1962 Solar Eclipse: A Crucial Warning for the Upcoming Celestial Event**

As a significant solar eclipse approaches, an Oregon man is sharing his personal story of permanent vision damage sustained during a 1962 partial solar eclipse. Lou Tomososki, who was a high school student at the time, looked directly at the sun without proper eye protection, a decision that led to lasting consequences. His experience highlights the serious dangers of viewing solar eclipses without certified protective eyewear.

In 1962, Tomososki and a friend were watching a partial solar eclipse near Marshall High School. Tomososki recalled, "The sun at that time, at 3:30 p.m., was in the one o’clock position." He mistakenly believed, "If you stare at it long enough the brightness goes away." This impulse resulted in a life-altering mistake. By that evening, both Tomososki and his friend started experiencing significant vision problems.

"It doesn’t get any worse and it doesn’t get any better," Tomososki said, describing the unchanging nature of his vision deficit. Both men, now 70 years old, are still affected by the incident. Tomososki explained his condition: "You know how the news people blur a license plate out. That’s what I have on the right eye, about the size of a pea, I can’t see around that."

Doctors later diagnosed Tomososki with retinal burns from the partial eclipse. Dr. Brandon Lujan of the Casey Eye Institute emphasizes the critical need for appropriate eye protection during any solar eclipse, even total ones. "I think anytime is too much," Lujan told KGW-TV. "Anytime looking can do damage." Tomososki's ongoing vision impairment serves as a powerful example of this medical advice.

While Tomososki understands the excitement for the upcoming August 21st eclipse, he plans to watch it with extreme caution. "I’m going to go out and enjoy it," he stated. "But I’ll stand and watch it get dark." His account is a strong plea for others to prioritize eye safety and avoid the permanent damage he has experienced.

Read or Share this story: https://usat.ly/2w6owYH

## From Omens to Algorithms: The Evolving Science of Eclipse Prediction

**Ancient civilizations** viewed solar and lunar eclipses with awe and trepidation, often interpreting these celestial events as divine pronouncements. The earliest surviving record of a solar eclipse, from a Babylonian clay tablet dated March 5, 1223 BCE, ominously states, "The sun went down, its gatekeeper was Mars." Similarly, Chinese records from October 22, 2134 BCE, recount Emperor Chung K’ang observing that "the Sun and Moon did not meet harmoniously." His court astronomers, Hsi and Ho, were famously executed for failing to forecast this event. Early cultures frequently attributed eclipses to supernatural forces. Chinese astronomers believed a celestial dragon was consuming the sun, while Babylonians perceived the moon god as being besieged by seven demons. The Babylonian city of Ugarit yielded a tablet that also recorded, "Two livers were examined: danger." This alludes to the practice of diviners interpreting animal entrails as divine pronouncements, signaling impending misfortune.

The scientific pursuit of astronomical prediction has undergone a **profound transformation**, evolving from superstitious interpretations to **sophisticated mathematical models**. As former NASA astronomer Fred Espenak notes, "It has been centuries of gradual improvement that just piled up over time." Definitive ground maps for eclipse paths did not emerge until the 18th century, and their computational demands remained substantial until the 1970s. "The floodgates really opened when **digital computers came online**," Espenak added. The earliest Babylonian records of lunar eclipse prediction date back to 745 BCE. By 600 BCE, lunar eclipse prediction had become a routine practice, with astronomers dispatching cautionary missives to ruling elites. An analysis of 61 solar eclipse predictions spanning from 357 BCE to 6 CE demonstrated a **100% accuracy** in forecasting an eclipse within an eight-hour temporal window anywhere on Earth, although only 28 were observable from Babylon. This precision was a direct consequence of rigorous observational data collection, which facilitated the identification and formalization of the **Saros cycle** – a period of 18 years, 11 days, and 8 hours, during which eclipses recur at predictable intervals.

By the Middle Ages, astronomers compiled "ephemerides," comprehensive tables detailing the positions of celestial bodies, often derived from the methodologies of the Greek astronomer Ptolemy (circa 150 CE). However, the accuracy of these predictions gradually diminished due to the Earth's rotational deceleration, a phenomenon causing its day to lengthen by approximately 40 seconds per century, primarily driven by the moon's tidal gravitational influence. The precise, dynamic distance between the Earth and moon was only accurately quantified after Apollo astronauts deployed retroreflectors on the lunar surface. The modern era of eclipse path prediction commenced with **Isaac Newton's seminal 1687 theory of universal gravitation**. Edmond Halley, celebrated for his discovery of comets, leveraged Newton's principles to compute eclipse trajectories, publishing the first public "broadsheet" forecasting an eclipse's path across England in 1715. Although his prediction deviated by approximately 20 miles, historian Alice Waters notes that Halley played a crucial role in dispelling public and royal anxieties surrounding eclipses as portents of ill fortune.

A watershed moment in eclipse observation occurred in 1919 during a total solar eclipse observed on the island of Príncipe. English astronomer Arthur Eddington utilized this event to empirically test **Albert Einstein's nascent theory of general relativity**. Eddington meticulously measured the deflection of starlight by the sun's gravitational field, finding it to be nearly twice the magnitude predicted by Newtonian physics. This experimental validation propelled Einstein to international acclaim. Subsequent empirical investigations have consistently corroborated Einstein's theoretical framework. With the advent of digital computing in the 1970s, the intricate calculations required for predicting eclipse paths became substantially more tractable, as elucidated by Espenak. Nevertheless, long-term predictions continue to present challenges owing to the moon's gradual orbital recession from Earth. The moon's orbital plane is inclined at 5 degrees relative to Earth's equatorial plane and is subject to the gravitational perturbations of Earth, the sun, and even Jupiter. "They pull it up and down — that causes all kinds of problems," Espenak stated, rendering precise eclipse mapping "devilishly difficult to predict." Furthermore, anthropogenic climate change, with its attendant impacts on sea levels and glacial melt, may potentially alter tidal dynamics, thereby introducing further complexities into lunar eclipse predictions. "We can’t be certain how that will go in the coming centuries," Espenak concluded, "But we are certainly a lot better at predicting than they were all those centuries ago."

The upcoming total solar eclipse on August 21st is a momentous celestial event, marking the first time in 99 years that the moon's umbra will traverse the entire United States from coast to coast. This astronomical alignment ensures that for the first time in generations, a significant portion of the contiguous 48 states will be within a manageable distance of the path of totality. During this phenomenon, the moon will completely obscure the sun, plunging the daytime landscape into an ephemeral twilight. The profound and singular experience of witnessing totality has prompted both amateur enthusiasts and seasoned astronomers to meticulously plan travel and accommodations years in advance. BuzzFeed News engaged with dedicated eclipse chasers, challenging them to articulate the transformative magic of this rare spectacle. Their profound insights reveal the deeply personal and universally awe-inspiring nature of a total solar eclipse.

"Experiencing a total solar eclipse is the closest most of us will ever get to space travel," shares David Baron, author of *American Eclipse*. "During totality, the Earth's blue sky recedes like a drawn curtain, unveiling the inner reaches of our solar system, a vista normally obscured. You witness a sun unlike its everyday appearance—a frilly, shimmering corona flanked by planets and brilliant stars. It evokes the sensation of standing on a distant world, observing creation itself. As a normally staid, logical, and scientifically minded individual, a total solar eclipse accesses an entirely different facet of my being. I feel in the presence of a deity, profoundly understanding my infinitesimal place within the vast cosmos. This terrifying yet humbling realization paradoxically bestows immense peace."

Rick Fienberg, press officer for the American Astronomical Society, elaborates on the visual splendor: "Inside the path of totality, you will witness nature's most spectacularly beautiful sight: the sun completely obscured by the moon, revealing its outer atmosphere, the solar corona. The moon's silhouette appears an absolute black, stark against the pearly white, diaphanous glow of the corona, adorned with loops and streamers extending solar diameters, delineating the sun's magnetic field. At the ingress and egress of totality, brilliant red arcs of the sun's chromosphere, its lower atmosphere, become visible just beyond the moon's limb. You may also observe prominences—jets, arcs, or loops of red chromospheric gas—erupting from the sun's edge. The sky transforms into a lovely twilight blue, punctuated by a handful of bright stars and planets. The horizon displays pastel sunset colors emanating from areas still illuminated by the sun beyond the moon's shadow. A noticeable temperature drop occurs, and wind patterns may shift in speed and direction. This extraordinary event, lasting mere minutes, often feels as though it concludes in mere seconds."

Doug Duncan, an astronomer at the University of Colorado, likens the visual distortion to an underwater experience: "When you experience a total eclipse, it can appear apocalyptic. On a clear day, the sun is high, and its sudden absence, replaced by a black hole in the sky adorned with pink flames and silver streamers, is disorienting. The darkness is not absolute but akin to a deep dusk. The light emanating from the sun's silvery corona alters colors, creating an effect comparable to viewing objects underwater while snorkeling. This chromatic shift, coupled with the surreal visual transformation, triggers a profound cognitive dissonance, making one question reality. This phenomenon, occurring minutes before totality, invariably causes the hairs on my neck to stand on end due to its sheer strangeness. I have now chased approximately ten to twelve total solar eclipses since 1970."

Theoretical astrophysicist Katie Mack recounts her experience in southern England in 1999: "Despite England's notoriously unpredictable skies, the occasion was deeply surreal and affecting. As the eclipse progressed, intermittent glimpses of the crescent sun appeared through cloud breaks. By the time totality arrived, complete cloud cover persisted, yet the darkness that enveloped the hills and bay was profound. Intermittent camera flashes from unseen boats punctuated the gloom. The air grew colder, streetlights illuminated the road, and seabirds shrieked, disoriented. A 360-degree pale glow, resembling a perpetual sunset, stretched across the horizon, where distant land remained bathed in daylight. While the solar corona was obscured, the experience of being in a momentary island of complete darkness surrounded by daylight was extraordinary, viscerally connecting us to the cosmos' predictable motions. Clear skies are preferable, but even under cloud cover, standing on Earth amidst a total solar eclipse is a phenomenal event."

Nathalia Alzate, a postdoctoral research associate in Space Weather, describes the meticulous preparation and emotional crescendo: "Eclipse day arrives after years of preparation, months of construction, weeks of testing, and days of assembly. Despite exhaustion, anxiety and excitement permeate the atmosphere. We stand in the cold, awaiting the pivotal moment. The cry of 'First contact!' signals the moon's slow waltz across the sun's face. Spectators capture images and perform jumping jacks to ward off the chill as totality approaches, anticipating a further temperature drop. Finally, totality is achieved. The moon positions itself between us and our star, initiating an unparalleled phenomenon. Darkness descends, and the sun's atmosphere, the corona, is revealed. Words fail to capture the feeling of witnessing this event; the surrounding world vanishes, leaving only us and the eclipse. The realization of our insignificance in the face of such cosmic grandeur is humbling, yet profoundly beautiful. As the moon continues its celestial dance, the sun reappears, bathing the world in light once more. Cheers erupt, smiles abound, and final photographs are taken. The moon departs, and the sun shines, the silent protagonist of our collective awe."

Amateur astronomer and astrophotographer Mohamad Soltanolkotabi views eclipses as catalysts for altered perception: "For me, a total solar eclipse transcends the mere passage of the moon before the sun. It serves as a pretext to perceive the world differently, demonstrating that daylight can indeed yield to midday darkness. Chasing an eclipse is an endeavor to seek unusual events that disrupt routine and foster discovery. These celestial occurrences are profoundly inspiring and educational, potentially initiating new life paths, as they did for me. The mesmerizing spectacle of a total solar eclipse ignited my passion for astronomy and its spectacular, science-based phenomena. Eclipse chasing encompasses exciting activities like travel, adventure, photography, education, and observation. It is a privilege to join the global community of eclipse chasers."

Mike Kentrianakis, solar eclipse project manager at the American Astronomical Society, recalls the visual intensity of the 1998 Aruba eclipse: "Aruba in 1998 was my second total solar eclipse, and I was ecstatic. The scene—a beautiful beach, turquoise water—was juxtaposed with the celestial event. A redness encircled the horizon, a violet sky emerged, and silvery rays of the corona streamed outwards. This was the true sun, revealed in its full glory by the moon's intervention. The colors and the palpable 'electricity' of the phenomenon were overwhelming, appearing almost electrified, a purely magical effect. The experience concluded within seconds, immediately igniting a desire to witness another. The wait, however, is not measured in days or months, but years. Time itself seems to suspend during totality."

Evan Zucker, an eclipse chaser, emphasizes the profound emotional impact: "Total solar eclipses are unequivocally awe-inspiring. While other natural phenomena like the aurora borealis or a stunning sunset are spectacular, none possess the power to shake one to their core so fundamentally. The sun's centrality to life on Earth makes its gradual disappearance and sudden replacement by a jet-black circle surrounded by the wispy white corona a nearly incomprehensible event for the primal brain. Furthermore, no other phenomenon elicits such universal reactions of joy and excitement. While photographs and videos fall short, audio recordings come closest to conveying the experience. Despite not being a particularly emotional person, listening to my past eclipse recordings often brings me to tears."

Michael Zeiler, co-owner of Great American Eclipse, describes the transformative power of totality: "A total solar eclipse of the sun is an unparalleled natural experience. The moment the moon's shadow envelops you is a full sensory immersion: the sky darkens abruptly, planets and brighter stars become visible, and the sun's corona, normally unseen, stuns with its beauty. One might gasp, scream, or weep at this astonishing vision. Upon the conclusion of totality, you will be irrevocably changed, feeling a profound connection to the universe and a spiritual upliftment. The emotions experienced are bittersweet—a giddy euphoria from totality mingled with sadness at its end. Your immediate thought will invariably be: 'When and where is the next one?' You will then resolve to witness the next great American eclipse, traversing from Texas to Maine on April 8, 2024."

Jackie Beucher, vice president of the Astronomical Society of Kansas City, recounts a deeply moving experience in Hawaii: "We were on a beach on the northwest corner of the Big Island of Hawaii. The wind and waves subsided completely by the time of totality. Birds had roosted for the 'night,' and tropical frogs chirped audibly. Clouds broke over the mountain, revealing the advancing partial eclipse. Shortly thereafter, the black moon obscured the sun, creating a 'black hole in the sky.' Simultaneously, the corona erupted around it, overwhelming me with magnificent beauty. It was an explosion of colors within my heart and mind, accompanied by waves of goosebumps. Even 26 years later, reliving it brings tears to my eyes. Each subsequent total eclipse allows me to relax and surrender to the experience. I eagerly anticipate August 21st."

Psychologist and author Kate Russo describes the intense emotional spectrum of a total solar eclipse: "For me, a total solar eclipse is an intensely emotional moment, encompassing a wide range of human feelings in a short span. There's an initial rush of emotion, a sense of wrongness as the light dims

## The Eclipse Experience: Shared Awe in the Path of Totality

Witnessing a total solar eclipse is a powerful draw, prompting many to seek out unique vantage points. While some were drawn to quirky locales like Carhenge in Alliance, Nebraska, or sought unobstructed views in Glendo, Wyoming, the shared anticipation has transformed these spots into bustling hubs. The sheer number of enthusiasts has led to humorous comparisons of the scene to a "zombie apocalypse," as towns grapple with crowds far exceeding their usual capacity, even surpassing the busiest local pancake breakfast.

Despite the logistical challenges, such as strained facilities and general crowd-related disorder, researchers studying human behavior suggest that experiencing the eclipse within a group might indeed be the optimal choice. Their findings provide a fascinating perspective on how collective wonder can amplify such a rare celestial event.

Make Your Own Eclipse Viewer NPR's Skunk Bear YouTube Hundreds of years before solar viewing glasses were readily available, scientists and casual spectators could still enjoy these rare celestial events without frying their eyeballs. They'd use a combination of pinholes and mirrors to redirect the sun's rays onto a screen. Enlarge this image toggle caption Bettmann Archive/Getty Images Bettmann Archive/Getty Images It took a while to figure out how to build the so-called camera obscura. Ancient Chinese and Greek scholars puzzled over pinholes for centuries before an Arab mathematician and scientist came up with a design. You can rig up your own version with simple household items. It's easy. Skunk Bear's latest video shows you how. And remember, never look directly at the sun without appropriate eye protection. ____ Eclipses are beautiful, and they're also really important to scientists. Skunk Bear's latest video explores the many discoveries sparked by solar eclipses. Check it out over on Skunk Bear's YouTube channel.

The highly anticipated "U.S. eclipse of the century" is poised to attract an unprecedented number of spectators, with estimates suggesting that as many as 7 million individuals may converge on the narrow band where the sun will be completely obscured. This celestial spectacle's path of totality, a diagonal swathe stretching from Oregon all the way to South Carolina, is a region of immense interest. GreatAmericanEclipse.com further highlights that over 12.25 million people already reside within this 65-mile-wide corridor of complete solar blockage. The strategic location of this path, coupled with the extensive reach of the United States' highway system, means that a significant portion of the American population lives within a convenient driving distance.

Experts from GreatAmericanEclipse.com emphasize that the path of totality carves a diagonal route across the nation, originating in Oregon and concluding in South Carolina. Crucially, a substantial majority of Americans reside within a day's drive of this unique astronomical phenomenon. The nation's well-developed highway infrastructure, combined with the financial accessibility of short driving vacations for many American families, further facilitates potential travel. August presents an ideal vacation window; the weather is typically warm, and the likelihood of disruptive summer storms diminishes across much of the country. Furthermore, the timing of the eclipse, August 21st, often precedes the commencement of the fall academic session for most schools, with some institutions situated near the path of totality even strategically scheduling a delayed start to accommodate this extraordinary event.

The pervasive influence of social media is expected to play a pivotal role in galvanizing eclipse enthusiasts and motivating potential visitors. Events of this magnitude are precisely the kind that are guaranteed to achieve viral status across platforms like Twitter, Facebook, and Instagram. It is anticipated that a considerable number of individuals will finalize their travel plans in the week leading up to eclipse day, underscoring the dynamic and responsive nature of modern event participation. The simple yet compelling call to action, "Take a vacation, see the eclipse," resonates with the allure of experiencing something truly extraordinary.

The organization undertook a series of estimations, admittedly employing very broad calculations, to project the number of people who would actively travel to witness this celestial event. Their analysis considered individuals situated at varying distances from the centerline of the eclipse path. For someone residing 200 miles away from the centerline, it is highly probable they would have encountered news of the eclipse through television or social media within the week preceding the event. This average individual would likely form the impression that the total solar eclipse is an exceptionally special sight, worthy of consideration.

However, the decision to travel is not universally straightforward. Not everyone possesses the flexibility to embark on a journey. Monday, the day of the eclipse, is a standard workday for many and a school day for students, presenting a tangible obstacle. Some individuals might also be dissuaded by lingering myths surrounding safe eclipse viewing or by cautionary tales of potential traffic congestion, which can be a significant deterrent for those seeking a stress-free experience. Despite the widespread media coverage highlighting the spectacle of the eclipse, a segment of the population may remain entirely uninterested in the phenomenon.

Considering this profile of an average person living 200 miles from the path of totality, the organization's analysis suggests a high probability of approximately 2% of this population choosing to drive into the path of totality, with a lower probability of 0.5%. These percentages are subsequently halved for individuals residing 400 miles away and halved again for those living 800 miles away, reflecting the diminishing likelihood of travel with increasing distance. This methodology was systematically applied to all populated areas across the United States, utilizing the sophisticated geospatial analysis capabilities of ArcGIS software by Esri. The cumulative estimate derived from this comprehensive analysis indicates that the number of visitors expected to enter the path of totality on eclipse day will range between a conservative 1.85 million and an expansive 7.4 million people. The consideration of "school days" is acknowledged as a genuine impediment for individuals eager to witness an event that is truly a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity, potentially impacting the higher end of these projections. The sheer scale of this anticipated influx underscores the profound impact of this astronomical event on American travel and public engagement.

query: What reasons did Sen. Lindsey Graham give for his change in opinion about Newt Gingrich?

documents.text: Sen. Lindsey Graham is changing his tune about Newt Gingrich. The South Carolina Republican, who helped organize a failed attempt to oust Gingrich from his post as speaker of the House in 1997, said Sunday that Gingrich has "matured" and that he would "certainly support him as president if he won the nomination." Graham stopped short of endorsing Gingrich on NBC's "Meet the Press," but he said he and other former critics of Gingrich are coming around. "He's been out of government for a long time; he's matured as a person. He's reattached himself to his faith," Graham said. He also said he thinks Gingrich has the ability to beat Obama in the general election. Graham added that he hopes Texas Rep. Ron Paul won't run as a third-party presidential candidate if he doesn't win the GOP nomination. "We're better together," Graham said. "We have a common political desire and that is to end Obama's policies." Read more about: Lindsey Graham, Newt Gingrich

John H. Sununu, former governor of New Hampshire, defended Mitt Romney's controversial $10,000 wager during a Sunday appearance on CNN's "State of the Union." The remark, made during Saturday's Republican presidential debate against Rick Perry, concerned a bet over Romney's alleged support for a national health insurance mandate. Sununu characterized Romney's statement as a "figure of speech," suggesting that the incident would likely be overshadowed by other campaign narratives. He humorously alluded to Newt Gingrich's substantial credit line at Tiffany's, implying that such financial details are not appropriate metrics for presidential qualifications. Critics, however, have seized upon Romney's comment, interpreting it as evidence of his detachment from ordinary Americans. Bob Walker, an advisor to Gingrich, countered Sununu's defense, asserting that Gingrich's Tiffany's credit line is "not at all" comparable to Romney's debate quip, which he believes "fits a matter of perception."

## Bachmann Challenges Establishment Candidates, Declares Herself the True Conservative

Michele Bachmann, a notable Republican politician, has sharply criticized presidential front-runners Newt Gingrich and Mitt Romney, labeling them collectively as "Newt Romney" and highlighting their perceived ties to the Washington establishment. Bachmann asserted that these candidates do not represent the desires of the Republican party, stating, "That’s not what we want in our nominee. It doesn’t survive the falling-off-the-chair laughing test."

### Targeting "King of K Street"

During an appearance on CBS's "Face the Nation," Bachmann specifically focused her critique on Newt Gingrich. She declared, "His offices are on the Rodeo Drive of Washington called K Street. He’s the king of K Street." Bachmann further elaborated on this point, arguing, "For a person who’s been influence peddling for over 30 years in Washington D.C., to think that Newt Gingrich is somehow an outsider when he is the consummate establishment insider, he’s the big government candidate just like Mitt Romney is the big government candidate."

### Advocating for a Constitutional Conservative

Bachmann emphasized the necessity of a nominee who genuinely champions core Republican issues and values. She positioned herself as a "true proven constitutional conservative," suggesting she is the candidate who best embodies these principles.

### Declining Support and Addressing Debate Absence

Bachmann made it clear that she would not commit to supporting either Romney or Gingrich, reiterating her determination to win the party's nomination. Additionally, she addressed her absence from Donald Trump's December 27th debate. Bachmann remarked, "Donald Trump is a big boy, these things happen," and pointed out the limited participation of other candidates. She advocated for debates that include all candidates, positioning herself as a serious contender in the race.

## Ron Paul's 2012 Presidential Ambitions: Exploring Third-Party Possibilities

Former Republican Congressman Ron Paul has kept the door ajar regarding a potential 2012 presidential run as a third-party candidate. When pressed on his intentions during an appearance on NBC's "Meet the Press," Paul offered a characteristically open-ended response, stating, "I'm not going to rule anything out or anything in 2012."

### No Immediate Plans for Independent Bid

While Paul has not definitively closed the door on a third-party candidacy, he clarified that he currently has "no plans" to pursue an independent bid should his efforts to secure the Republican presidential nomination fall short. This measured approach echoes sentiments he has expressed in recent weeks, where he has consistently provided similarly non-committal answers when questioned about a potential independent campaign.

### Current Focus Remains on Republican Nomination

Despite the ongoing speculation about his future political endeavors, Paul stressed that his immediate focus is not on a third-party run. He emphasized, "I am not even thinking about it" at this juncture. His primary objective, he explained, is to concentrate his efforts on his campaign for the Republican nomination, a significant undertaking that currently occupies his full attention.

Rick Perry said Sunday he was surprised by Mitt Romney's offer of a $10,000 bet about Romney's support for an individual mandate in his book "No Apologies" during Saturday night's debate. "I was taken a little aback," the Texas governor told Chris Wallace on "Fox News Sunday." "Driving out to the station this morning I'm pretty sure I didn’t drive by a house that anyone in Iowa would think a $10,000 bet was possible. It was a little out of touch with the normal Iowa citizen. But the issue of the individual mandate is still at the center here and Mitt can deny this as many times as he wants, but in his first book ... he clearly stated that the individual mandate should be the model for this country." Romney's opponents are already jumping on his $10,000 bet comment as evidence that he's out of touch.

Iowa Governor Terry Branstad has expressed that Mitt Romney's campaign strategy in the Hawkeye State is exhibiting excessive prudence. "I believe he is beginning to comprehend the necessity of adopting a more assertive approach; he will need to dedicate increased attention to this region," the Republican governor articulated on Sunday during an appearance on NBC's "Meet the Press." Branstad characterized the contest in Iowa as "wide open," noting that "the lead has shifted hands on numerous occasions." He further elaborated, "The outcome remains uncertain, and constituents are actively seeking the most formidable candidate capable of vanquishing Barack Obama."

Ron Paul asserts that Newt Gingrich bears a moral imperative to reimburse the substantial consulting fees he acquired from the mortgage institution Freddie Mac. When questioned on Sunday about whether Gingrich should offer an apology and return the funds earned before the federal intervention in the struggling mortgage enterprise, Paul stated, "Well legally he doesn't have to, but I would think morally, him having received this money, yes." The Texas congressman elaborated on NBC's "Meet the Press," declaring, "I wouldn't have taken their money, just for the fact that I think it was an immoral thing to take money." Paul had previously criticized Gingrich during the Republican presidential debate on Saturday for accepting between $1.6 million and $1.8 million in consulting fees from Freddie Mac, a figure reported by Bloomberg News. Gingrich, meanwhile, has maintained his defense of the consulting services he provided during his tenure in the private sector.

Texas Gov. Rick Perry said Sunday he doesn't have all nine of the Supreme Court justices memorized. “I don’t have memorized all of the Supreme Court judges,” Perry said on "Fox News Sunday," responding to a question about a Des Moines Register interview he did last week when he referred to the eight justices of the high court. After the interview, the campaign said Perry was referring to a specific case that went 8-to-1 in a direction Perry didn't agree with. The American people "aren’t looking for a robot that can spit out the name of every Supreme Court justice or someone that's going to be perfect in every way. They're looking for somebody who's got values," Perry said. Perry also defended his new ad referring to President Barack Obama's "war on religion," citing a Supreme Court case in which the administration argued that churches shouldn't be able to discriminate based on religious belief and "Catholic charities that aren't allowed to make money because they've made decisions that they're not going to allow abortions in their facilities." He said he would "support a constitutional amendment that allows children to pray in school anytime that they'd like," calling out "those activist judges like Sotomayor and Kagan that [Obama] put on the Supreme Court, they would continue to say that that is a decision the Supreme Court should make. I happen to believe that that would be a local decision and that's not the Supreme Court's business to be telling Americans how and when to pray." Perry said he would "go across this country as I’m promoting a balanced budget amendment to the United States Constitution and I’ll work on a balanced, or I should say an amendment to let our children pray in school I think Americans are greatly supportive of both of those issues." The governor dismissed criticism that he was making a blatant grab for Iowa's evangelical caucus goers. "Faith is a part of who I am," he said. "I can’t change that any more than I can change that I’m the son of two tenant farmers." Read more about: Rick Perry, Presidency

query: What is the primary goal of the Internet Archive's 'No More 404' initiative as discussed in the article?

documents.text: These crawls are part of an effort to archive pages as they are created and archive the pages that they refer to. That way, as the pages that are referenced are changed or taken from the web, a link to the version that was live when the page was written will be preserved.Then the Internet Archive hopes that references to these archived pages will be put in place of a link that would be otherwise be broken, or a companion link to allow people to see what was originally intended by a page's authors.The goal is to fix all broken links on the web . Crawls of supported "No More 404" sites.

The company developing Elon Musk's 700mph maglev train has announced new details about the levitation system it plans to use. Hyperloop Transportation Technologies said on Monday that it will use a passive magnetic levitation system developed by Lawrence Livermore National Labs in the US. The Los Angeles-headquartered transportation company wants to build a system that involves firing a pod full of people through a low-pressure tube at speeds of up to 760 mph. It has been described by inventor Musk as a mix between Concorde, a rail gun, and an air hockey table that could take you from Los Angeles to San Francisco in under 30 minutes. Hyperloop Technologies said that magnetic fields in the tunnel will lift the pod before a "thrust force" is applied, accelerating the pod to speeds just shy of the speed of sound (767 mph). The same thrust force is simply reversed to decelerate the pod. As the pod slows down, the system's battery is recharged through what Hyperloop describes as regenerative breaking. "Utilising a passive levitation system will eliminate the need for power stations along the Hyperloop track, which makes this system the most suitable for the application and will keep construction costs low," said Bibop Gresta, COO of Hyperloop Transportation Technologies, in a statement. "From a safety aspect, the system has huge advantages, levitation occurs purely through movement, therefore if any type of power failure occurs, Hyperloop pods would continue to levitate and only after reaching minimal speeds touch the ground." Critics have questioned whether Hyperloop will ever come to fruition, targeting cost and safety as potential major setbacks.

## Hyperloop Propulsion Test: A **Big Step** for **Super-Fast Travel**

Elon Musk's **Hyperloop idea**, aiming to **change how we travel fast**, has moved **closer to reality** with its **first public test**. This **important trial**, held in the Nevada desert, gave a **real look** at a future where passenger pods could reach **amazing speeds** inside special vacuum tubes. While not the final version, this test **showed a key part** of this **big transport plan**.

### The Idea: **Faster Than Planes**

Musk first shared the Hyperloop concept in 2013. It suggests pods moving through **almost-empty tubes** at speeds that could **greatly cut travel times**. For instance, a trip from San Francisco to Los Angeles, about 400 miles, could take as little as **30 minutes**. Since Musk's first paper, **several separate companies** have started working to make this **bold concept happen**.

### Hyperloop One's **Key Speed Test**

**Hyperloop One**, previously Hyperloop Technologies, ran today's **propulsion system test**. The Wall Street Journal reported the **main goal** was to **prove the propulsion tech could move** a test sled. The test track was short, and **air drag**, something to fix later, was a factor.

### **New Partners** and **Smart Design**

The company also announced **major progress**, including **new money** and **important partnerships** with leaders in transport and engineering. One new partner is famous architect **Bjarke Ingels**. Gizmodo noted Ingels' **deep experience** with big public projects, suggesting he will help **guide the design** for fitting Hyperloop One's systems into cities. Ingels said, "With hyperloop we are not only designing a futuristic station or a very fast train, we are dealing with an entirely novel technology with the potential to completely transform how our existing cities will grow and evolve, and how new cities will be conceived and constructed."

Hyperloop One CEO Rob Lloyd was positive about the new teams, saying the company will work with them "to **shape the future of travel**, offering a quicker, safer, better, and greener high-speed network for moving people and goods."

### **Technical and Rule Hurdles**

The **practicality** of such a system has been **much discussed** since Musk's first idea. Electrical engineer Marc Thompson told NPR's Elise Hu that while it has potential, there are **tough engineering problems**. "The devil's in the details in terms of testing, safety, passenger safety, egress, vibration, all that engineering stuff," Thompson explained.

Former U.S. Department of Transportation Assistant Secretary Emil Frankel also shared concerns with Elise Hu. He noted the difficulty of pursuing "leapfrogging technology" when **fixing current roads and bridges is already hard**, questioning its **immediate public policy use**.

### **Ongoing Effort** and **Industry Race**

Despite these points, **Hyperloop One** and its rival, **Hyperloop Transportation Technologies (HTT)**, are **still dedicated**. HTT is also moving ahead, with Wired reporting they expect to "demonstrate full Kitty Hawk capabilities by the end of this year."

The race to build the future of high-speed transportation is heating up, with multiple companies unveiling their hyperloop prototypes. This innovative technology, first conceptualized by Elon Musk's SpaceX, aims to transport people and cargo at speeds approaching 700 mph within vacuum-sealed tubes.

Hyperloop Transportation Technologies (HTT), a venture comprised of NASA and Boeing veterans, recently announced its acquisition of passive magnetic levitation technology. This advancement will power their prototype system, designed to propel passengers and goods at speeds up to 750 mph. To illustrate the potential, a journey from Los Angeles to San Francisco, which currently takes six hours by car or an entire day by train, could be reduced to a mere 30 minutes. HTT plans to showcase its prototype in the Nevada desert, north of Las Vegas, this week.

This announcement precedes a similar showcase by rival Hyperloop Technologies Inc. (HTI), scheduled just two days later for investors and media in the same desert location. Both HTI and HTT, based in Los Angeles, are developing systems featuring large tubes designed to house pods for either passengers or freight.

Another contender in this burgeoning field is skyTran, located at NASA Research Park. This company has demonstrated its two- and four-person vehicle technology and is targeting a 30-mile track in Lagos, Nigeria. skyTran has secured $30 million in funding and operates in partnership with NASA.

Hyperloop technology promises rapid transit without reliance on fossil fuels. However, significant challenges remain, including ensuring system reliability and mitigating risks associated with supersonic speeds. The concept, while futuristic, draws upon established principles like magnetic levitation (maglev), already employed in high-speed trains in Europe and Japan. Nevertheless, hyperloop faces substantial infrastructure and regulatory hurdles, akin to those confronting high-speed rail and autonomous vehicle development. The estimated cost for a hyperloop system connecting Los Angeles and San Francisco is projected to exceed $6 billion.

HTT's passive magnetic levitation system is designed to address safety concerns by eliminating the need for power stations along the track, thereby reducing construction costs. Bibop Gresta, COO of HTT, explained, "From a safety aspect, the system has huge advantages, levitation occurs purely through movement, therefore if any type of power failure occurs, Hyperloop pods would continue to levitate and only after reaching minimal speeds touch the ground."

SpaceX, founded by Elon Musk, who popularized the hyperloop concept in 2013, is also a key player. A team from MIT, sponsored by SpaceX, won an inaugural hyperloop competition by proposing a design similar to HTT's, utilizing magnets on pods that interact with passive coils in the tubes for levitation. Philippe Kirschen, the MIT team captain, highlighted the system's passive nature as a key to its scalability. Musk has indicated that SpaceX might construct a test track for its hyperloop technology, likely in Texas.

While SpaceX benefits from Musk's considerable resources and skyTran has government backing, HTT and HTI have adopted distinct funding strategies. HTT relies on financial contributions and the expertise of space technology veterans. In contrast, HTI, co-founded by early Uber investor Shervin Pishevar and former SpaceX engineer Brogan BamBrogan, has raised $37 million.

HTT's passive magnetic levitation approach is rooted in the work of physicist Richard Post. By integrating magnets into the hyperloop pods that interact with coils in the tubes, the system aims to achieve levitation more cost-effectively than current maglev trains, which require expensive tracks embedded with copper coils. In 2015, HTT announced plans to integrate its technology into a proposed solar-powered city in Quay Valley, California. More recently, HTT CEO Dirk Ahlborn revealed discussions with the Slovakian government regarding a potential hyperloop route in the country.

One small push for Hyperloop, one giant thrust for the future of transportation. In the arid northern deserts of Nevada, far from the bright lights and towering edifices of Las Vegas, the Los Angeles-based Hyperloop One took a gamble and won, successfully testing its linear induction propulsion system. A grandstand full of journalists and Hyperloop One supporters watched as a series of stators (basically electromagnetic blades) quickly powered up under the sled, which then flew by at about the speed of a fast car (115 mph or so). Image: Hyperloop One/Mashable The test, while fast, was, to be honest, somewhat underwhelming. After the initial countdown, the sled took off from the start position and whizzed by on a track at a measured 2.4 Gs of acceleration. Special grabbers underneath the sled then slammed into a sandpit, which effectively slowed the sled down to zero and ended the test. Future tests on this track at the 39.9 acre facility will go much, much faster. The Hyperloop One speed-by. Image: Hyperloop One While the final Hyperloop One system will use permanent magnets to levitate the pods off the track, this sled actually sat on the metal rails, which probably future slowed it down. The beginning Image: Hyperloop One "This is the first step in a long journey," said Hyperloop One co-founder and CTO Brogan BamBrogan He's right. The electromagnet-based propulsion system is just one small part of the full transportation system that Hyperloop One (formerly Hyperloop Technologies) hopes to launch in the not-too-distant future. Hyperloop One Co-Founder and Executive Chairman Shervin Pishevar makes a statement moments before the big test. Image: Lance Ulanoff/Mashable Originally the brainchild of billionaire entrepreneur Elon Musk, Hyperloop transportation consists of sealed pods traveling at subsonic speeds in a low-pressure tube that's elevated above the ground on pylons. It's intended to be a low-energy (solar) and low-emission system. It could also be a viable mass transportation alternative for traffic-snarled corridors like those between New York and Washington, D.C., and Los Angeles and San Francisco, completing the latter trip in as little as 35 minutes. Musk produced a lengthy Hyperloop white paper and then open-sourced the concept in 2014. As a result, the Hyperloop race is on, and, with Wednesday's test, Hyperloop One may have just inched ahead of rival HTT. The Hyperloop One propulsion system test sled. Image: lance ulanoff/mashable Hyperloop One CEO Rob Lloyd seemed elated by the short test's results and predicts that, with enough cooperation from local and state government bodies (the kind they've found in Nevada and Las Vegas), Hyperloop One will be moving cargo by 2019 and people as early as 2021. As for what Hyperloop One has in store for the short-term, BamBrogan said, "a lot more of this testing." Have something to add to this story? Share it in the comments.

The dream of Hyperloop, a revolutionary mode of transportation promising speeds that dwarf current high-speed rail, is captivating the public imagination. While companies like Hyperloop Transportation Technologies are actively pursuing this vision, a critical aspect often overlooked in the initial excitement is the human experience within such a system. The primary expense in building a Hyperloop, as one executive noted, might indeed be the "tube" itself for a mere proof of concept. However, for a functional system that people will actually use for daily commutes or long-distance travel, the true cost might lie in ensuring passenger comfort. The current public perception of Hyperloop, often painted as a 750-mile-per-hour pneumatic tube whisking passengers from San Francisco to Los Angeles in a mere 35 minutes, largely ignores a fundamental challenge: human physiology and the potential for severe motion sickness.

Elon Musk's vision of hyperfast hypertransport, while technologically ambitious, faces a significant hurdle that isn't frequently discussed: the likelihood of widespread passenger discomfort and nausea. The allure of incredible speed is undeniable, but the reality of human comfort is equally important. People choose their modes of transport based on a complex interplay of factors, and while speed is a consideration, so too are space, comfort, and the absence of unpleasant physical sensations. Consider the common trade-off people make between a crowded subway and a traffic-jammed car; the latter, despite its inefficiencies, offers a degree of personal space and control that can be preferable to a physically uncomfortable public transit experience.

In a perfectly controlled environment, such as a vacuum tube, humans can tolerate constant velocity without significant issue, regardless of whether that speed is leisurely or extreme. The problem arises not from sustained speed, but from the dynamic changes in motion that a practical Hyperloop system would inevitably involve. A functional Hyperloop, especially if it's envisioned as a successor to our existing, aging railroad infrastructure, will necessitate frequent stops and starts. This means pods will be accelerating and decelerating far more often than most passengers would find comfortable. Furthermore, the United States is not a uniformly flat landscape. The proposed Hyperloop routes will encounter varied terrain, requiring the system to navigate inclines, declines, and potentially even elevated sections to circumvent geographical obstacles.

Current high-speed trains, which are already a marvel of engineering and widely adopted in countries like China and Japan, typically operate at speeds around 200 miles per hour. While this sustained velocity generally produces minimal physical symptoms for passengers, the forces experienced during acceleration, deceleration, and turns become increasingly problematic as speeds increase. The human body has a limited tolerance for G-forces, generally capping out around 0.2 Gs in both vertical and lateral directions. A Hyperloop operating at speeds envisioned by its proponents could easily generate accelerations that far exceed these limits, potentially reaching levels seven times greater than those experienced on Japan's Shinkansen bullet train.

These G-forces, whether positive (pushing you back into your seat) or negative (pulling you forward against your restraints), are a direct consequence of motion. When you introduce the possibility of movement in three distinct planes – forward, backward, up, and down – the complexity of these forces escalates dramatically. The human gastrointestinal system, for instance, can be subjected to forces that are not aligned with the spinal column. This misalignment is a primary trigger for nausea. If the journey continues without respite or effective countermeasures, the unpleasant experience can escalate to the point of vomiting, turning a futuristic travel experience into a deeply regrettable one.

The most straightforward solution to mitigate these physiological challenges is, naturally, to reduce speed. The Hyperloop Pod Competition, initiated by Elon Musk and SpaceX, mandates that competing teams design pods capable of reaching at least 240 miles per hour. While teams like MIT are developing prototypes that far exceed this benchmark, it's crucial to consider that this speed might become the standard for many routes, particularly outside of flatter regions like the Great Plains. If excessive speed leads to widespread discomfort, the Hyperloop will inevitably be forced to slow down.

It is vital to acknowledge that human physiology is not uniform; what causes one individual to feel unwell might be perfectly tolerable for another. Some people possess a greater tolerance for motion and turbulence than others. However, the fundamental challenge with the Hyperloop, even in its most optimized design, lies in the inherent discomfort associated with turns. Any deviation from a straight path, even a gentle curve, will introduce lateral G-forces that are likely to be universally unsettling.

As a technological concept, the Hyperloop remains one of the most captivating and forward-thinking projects currently under development. Its potential to revolutionize travel is immense. Nevertheless, it is imperative that we approach its realization with a healthy dose of scientific skepticism. Before we fully embrace the idea of passengers being transported at speeds approaching nine-tenths the speed of sound, a thorough understanding and rigorous testing of how the human body reacts to such conditions are absolutely essential. The success of Hyperloop hinges not just on engineering prowess, but on its ability to deliver a safe, comfortable, and ultimately enjoyable experience for its passengers. The "tube" might be the most visible component, but the unseen, internal experience of the rider will ultimately determine its viability.

## Experience the Thrill of Hyperloop: A New Browser Game Challenges Your Survival Skills

Ever dreamt of zipping through airless tubes at incredible speeds, just like in Elon Musk's visionary Hyperloop concept? Now, you can get a taste of that high-speed future with a new 8-bit styled browser game. "Break-a-Pod" puts you in control of a pod, tasked with navigating the challenging Hyperloop environment and keeping your vehicle intact.

### Gameplay Mechanics: Simple Controls, Extreme Difficulty

Much like the notoriously difficult "Flappy Bird," "Break-a-Pod" offers a deceptively simple premise with a steep learning curve. Your objective is to maintain the pod's speed and stability, aiming to travel as fast as possible without succumbing to crashes or power depletion. The game utilizes only the four arrow keys: up to accelerate, down to brake, and left/right to manage the pod's pitch and stability. Even minor missteps, such as hitting the tube walls too frequently or losing control of the pod's orientation, will result in an instant, explosive failure.

### The Minds Behind the Game: rLoop's Vision

The innovative game was developed by rLoop, a team that originated on Reddit and is actively working on building a physical Hyperloop pod prototype with aspirations of bringing it to SpaceX. Their goal with "Break-a-Pod" extends beyond mere entertainment. The team hopes the game will serve as an engaging and accessible introduction to the Hyperloop concept, fostering broader interest and encouraging further research and development.

### Supporting the Future of Hyperloop

"Break-a-Pod" also serves as a promotional tool for rLoop's IndieGoGo campaign. The team is seeking to raise $66,000 to complete the construction of their prototype pod. Successful funding could also pave the way for a mobile version of the game on iOS and Android, creating an additional avenue for financial support for their Hyperloop endeavors.

### rLoop's Journey with SpaceX

The rLoop team has already made significant strides in the Hyperloop initiative. SpaceX, the company spearheading the Hyperloop project, previously hosted an open competition for pod designs. From hundreds of submissions, rLoop was recognized as one of 18 finalists and has advanced to the next stage of the competition. This upcoming event, scheduled to take place in California, will feature prototype demonstrations. However, the exact date remains contingent on SpaceX's completion of a test track section.

The Hyperloop, a proposed high-speed transit system, faces substantial obstacles that extend beyond its technological viability. While Hyperloop Transportation Technologies secured a permit in 2015 for a 5-mile prototype in Quay Valley, a planned eco-community in Central California, this project underscores the core difficulties. The main challenge for realizing a Hyperloop system, like the one envisioned between San Francisco and Los Angeles, is not the underlying technology itself, but rather the intricate land acquisition and bureaucratic processes inherent in any large-scale infrastructure undertaking.

Elon Musk's 2013 unveiling of the Hyperloop concept sparked excitement about the possibility of travel at speeds up to 600 mph between major cities. Although the technical aspects of the proposal were widely discussed, a critical factor often overlooked is the necessity of obtaining land rights for the extensive corridors needed to link distant urban centers. Beyond land acquisition, other significant considerations include environmental impact studies and liability frameworks.

In 2016, Dirk Ahlborn, CEO of Hyperloop Transportation Technologies, stated, "After over two and a half years of research and development, our team has reached another important milestone. This will be the world’s first passenger-ready Hyperloop system." He also noted that the company's work would be visible from California's I-5 freeway in 2016. However, a consistent focus on the technological advancements of companies like Hyperloop Transportation Technologies, separate from competitors such as Hyperloop Technologies, often distracts from the fundamental challenges of land rights and navigating regulations.

The ambition to build a Hyperloop track between major cities, even with a prototype permit, remains a techno-utopian dream, perhaps as idealistic as the Quay Valley concept itself. As Josh Stephens pointed out, enthusiasm and innovation alone cannot secure farmland, bypass eminent domain, overcome geological barriers, relocate utilities, finalize station plans, or complete environmental impact reports and legal challenges like those under the California Environmental Quality Act. Terrestrial transit systems, regardless of their propulsion, encounter the same geographical and societal complexities that complicate projects like California's High Speed Rail, including mountains, urban areas, agricultural land, and natural habitats.

The ongoing emphasis on rapid transit capabilities without adequately addressing the immense public policy, governmental cooperation, and financial demands risks positioning the Hyperloop as an impractical fantasy rather than a workable transportation solution. This situation mirrors concerns raised before the official 2013 announcement, questioning the feasibility of a San Francisco-Los Angeles Hyperloop without extensive underground or sub-oceanic construction, which would still require significant land rights and governmental involvement. While the development of a working prototype in Quay Valley is met with optimism, skepticism persists about the realization of a Hyperloop system within our lifetime until companies show a commitment to legal and lobbying efforts comparable to their dedication to technological development.

query: What are the key components of President Trump's first budget proposal, and how does it prioritize military spending versus social programs?

documents.text:
## Trump's "America First" Budget: A Radical Shift in Federal Priorities

President Trump's initial budget blueprint, titled "America First: A Budget Blueprint to Make America Great Again," proposes a sweeping overhaul of federal spending. The plan prioritizes a significant boost in military funding while mandating steep reductions across numerous other government programs. This budget seeks to dismantle many established federal initiatives that offer crucial support to low-income citizens, advance scientific discovery, and cultivate global partnerships.

### Defense Expansion and Agency Cuts

Central to the proposal is a $54 billion increase for defense. This substantial investment is to be funded by cuts affecting over 18 other federal agencies. Departments like Agriculture, Labor, and State could see reductions exceeding 20 percent, while the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) might face cuts of over 30 percent. Furthermore, future federal support for esteemed institutions such as the National Endowment for the Arts, the National Endowment for the Humanities, and the Corporation for Public Broadcasting would be entirely eliminated. Within the EPA alone, the proposal targets the elimination of 50 programs and 3,200 positions.

### Workforce Reduction and "Draining the Swamp"

These proposed cuts are poised to represent the most extensive reduction in federal programs since the post-World War II drawdown. White House officials have explicitly stated that a significant curtailment of the federal non-military workforce is a key objective. OMB Director Mick Mulvaney articulated this sentiment, stating, "You can’t drain the swamp and leave all the people in it."

### Congressional Hurdles and Budgetary Authority

Many of Trump's budget proposals are anticipated to face considerable opposition from lawmakers on Capitol Hill, including some within his own Republican party. Congress ultimately holds the power of appropriation. For instance, Republicans have voiced reservations regarding the substantial proposed cuts to foreign aid and diplomatic efforts. The budget specifically targets foreign aid programs administered by the Education, State, and Treasury Departments. Senator Marco Rubio (R-Fla.) emphasized Congress's role, remarking, "The administration’s budget isn’t going to be the budget. We do the budget here. The administration makes recommendations, but Congress does budgets."

### Fiscal Deadlines and Shutdown Risks

While the Trump budget would not take effect until the new fiscal year on October 1st, a separate agreement with Congress is required by the end of April to avert a lapse in temporary funding. Disagreements over this budget could heighten the probability of a partial government shutdown commencing on April 29th. Additionally, the president and Congress must confront the recurring political challenge of the debt ceiling. Although the ceiling was extended until March 15th, experts suggest the government can continue borrowing through August or September.

### Negotiating Stance and Voter Mandate

White House budget proposals typically undergo revisions by lawmakers but serve as a crucial indicator of the president's governing agenda and initial negotiation stance. Mulvaney affirmed the White House's openness to negotiation while defending the scope of the proposed reductions. He asserted, "This budget represents a president who is beholden to nobody but the voters. He is following through on his promises." He further noted that the budget was developed by the president's team without input from special interests or lobbyists.

### Unforeseen Contradictions and Programmatic Impacts

The 53-page budget plan offers a clear snapshot of Trump's priorities, though it is notably more concise and less detailed than typical White House budget documents. Specifics regarding job eliminations across the federal government are not yet provided.

Intriguingly, some aspects of the budget appear to diverge from Trump's stated goals. For example, while Trump has expressed a desire to eradicate disease, the budget proposes a $5.8 billion cut to the National Institutes of Health, representing nearly 20 percent of its funding. Similarly, despite advocating for a $1 trillion infrastructure program, the proposal would eliminate a Transportation Department program that funds nearly $500 million in road projects. The budget lacks details on funding and tax mechanisms for the infrastructure program, deferring these critical decisions.

The administration also proposed eliminating programs designed to assist low-income Americans and minority communities, questioning their efficacy. This includes the Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program, which dispenses over $3 billion annually for home heating assistance, and the Community Development Block Grant program, which allocates roughly $3 billion for affordable housing, community development, and homelessness initiatives.

Further proposed cuts include privatizing the Federal Aviation Administration's air traffic control function, eliminating funding for long-distance Amtrak services, and ceasing EPA funding for Chesapeake Bay restoration. Job training programs would also be scaled back, shifting greater responsibility to states and employers. While many Republicans have criticized these programs as inefficient, proponents argue they are indispensable for many communities.

### Climate Change and International Commitments

The budget extensively targets Obama-era programs related to climate change. It seeks to terminate payments to the United Nations’ Green Climate Fund, a cornerstone of the U.S. commitment to the Paris climate agreement. Research funding for climate, ocean, and earth sciences at agencies such as NASA and NOAA would also face significant reductions. Clean-energy research, a priority under the Obama administration, would be heavily impacted by the elimination of the ARPA-E program and unspecified cuts to the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy. Senator Patrick J. Leahy (D-Vt.) commented on the strategic intent, suggesting, "I think one of the reasons they’re proposing them [big spending cuts] is that they know they won’t ever get through Congress. They know they’d be a disaster for their own party if they did. It makes for a great talking point. It actually fits on a tweet."

### Targeted Spending Increases and Border Security

Conversely, Trump proposed increased spending in several key areas. This includes $168 million for charter school programs and $250 million for a new private-school choice program, likely intended to offer tuition assistance for private school enrollment. The most substantial spending increase is directed towards the Pentagon, with an additional $54 billion allocated, though the specific distribution remains unclear. Funding would rise for the Army, Navy, Marine Corps, and Air Force, supporting initiatives such as acquiring new F-35 Joint Strike Fighters and replenishing munitions. However, the budget does not detail how these funds will bolster new strategies against the Islamic State.

This significant defense spending increase stands in stark contrast to proposed cuts to diplomatic and international programs. Combined spending for the State Department and the U.S. Agency for International Development would be reduced by $10.1 billion, a reduction of nearly 29 percent. Funding for U.N. peacekeeping efforts would also be curtailed, as would $803 million (35 percent) from Treasury International Programs, foreign assistance initiatives supported by previous administrations.

In alignment with his campaign pledges, Trump proposed new funding for border security agents and immigration judges. He also requested $1.7 billion in new funding for the current year and an additional $2.6 billion in 2018 to initiate the construction of a wall along the U.S.-Mexico border. Trump had championed this wall during his campaign, asserting Mexico would finance it, though some congressional Republicans are reportedly reconsidering the concept.

### Fiscal Outlook and Future Negotiations

The federal government is projected to spend over $4 trillion in the upcoming fiscal year. Trump's budget proposal addresses slightly over 25 percent of this total. With an anticipated deficit of $487 billion, the proposed budget cuts are intended to offset the increased defense spending and prevent the deficit from widening. A more comprehensive budget plan, potentially encompassing changes to programs like Medicaid and revised economic forecasts, is expected from Trump in May, after the deadline for avoiding a shutdown. Consequently, Thursday's budget proposal will play a pivotal role in ongoing negotiations.

Big Bird, the beloved icon of public broadcasting, once again finds himself facing the threat of reduced federal funding. This recurring challenge to the financial stability of public television and radio stations raises a crucial question: will this time be different? As President Trump prepares to present his first federal budget, the prospect of eliminating the government's subsidy for noncommercial broadcasting looms large. This proposed cut would eliminate the $445 million currently allocated to the Corporation for Public Broadcasting (CPB), the entity responsible for distributing funds to nearly 1,500 stations affiliated with NPR, PBS, and other public media organizations.

For many public broadcasters, particularly those serving smaller and more remote communities, the loss of federal funding would be devastating. These stations often depend on federal dollars for more than half of their annual operating budgets. Paula Kerger, president of PBS, has voiced grave concerns, stating that such a cut would be an "existential question for many of them," potentially forcing numerous stations off the air entirely. Roger LaMay, chairman of NPR's board and general manager of WXPN-FM in Philadelphia, echoed these sentiments, explaining that surviving stations would need to intensify fundraising efforts, such as pledge drives and sponsorships, to compensate for the lost federal revenue. He warned that this would inevitably "weaken the entire public radio system."

Despite these dire predictions, the history of public broadcasting funding suggests a resilient past. Time and again, despite political rhetoric and budgetary crises, public media has managed to secure the necessary funds to continue broadcasting cherished programs like "Sesame Street" and "All Things Considered." In fact, the CPB has frequently seen its federal funding slightly increase each year, even amidst discussions or proposals for zero funding from various administrations. Presidents dating back to Richard Nixon and Ronald Reagan have explored or suggested eliminating CPB funding, only to see congressional allies step in to ensure its survival. Even during President George W. Bush's eight years in office, a period marked by proposals for zero funding, the CPB's appropriation actually grew by 14 percent. This enduring resilience makes Big Bird seem almost like a cultural phoenix, repeatedly rising from the ashes of budget battles.

The CPB's portion of the vast federal budget is remarkably small, typically hovering around 0.01 percent of the $4 trillion total. However, it has consistently been a target for conservatives who view it as a symbol of government overreach and criticize what they perceive as a liberal bias in public broadcasting's programming. One notable instance occurred in 2005 when then-Secretary of Education Margaret Spellings criticized an episode of the children's program "Postcards From Buster" for featuring two families with lesbian parents. She demanded the return of federal funds used to subsidize the program, leading PBS to withdraw the episode and avert a funding crisis.

While public broadcasters anticipate further challenges with President Trump's budget proposal, there is a prevailing optimism that history will indeed repeat itself. Patrick Butler, president of America's Public Television Stations (APTS), which represents the majority of the nation's 171 public TV licensees, expressed hope for the best while acknowledging their preparedness to advocate for their cause. "Until we hear from the president, I’m hoping for the best," Butler stated, "But we’re prepared to defend ourselves."

The broad appeal of public broadcasting across diverse communities, spanning both "red" and "blue" states, contributes to its widespread political support. Over the years, public media advocates have emphasized the vital role these stations play in providing essential educational content and local news programming, particularly in areas that might otherwise lack such resources. They have also demonstrated their ability to mobilize public support, as seen in a 2011 lobbying campaign that famously brought Big Bird to Capitol Hill. It's worth noting that the landscape of "Sesame Street" production has shifted, with a 2015 agreement between Sesame Workshop and HBO leading to first-run episodes being produced for the premium cable network, while PBS now airs reruns.

The coalition supporting public broadcasting funding typically includes strong Democratic backing, but it also relies on influential Republican allies. Key figures include Senate Appropriations Committee Chairman Thad Cochran (R-Miss.), whose father was instrumental in establishing Mississippi Public Broadcasting, and Senator Roy Blunt (R-Mo.), who chairs the Senate subcommittee overseeing CPB funding. In the House, Appropriations Committee Chairman Rodney Frelinghuysen (R-N.J.) and Subcommittee Chairman Tom Cole (R-Okla.) have also historically supported CPB funding. Even figures like former House Speaker Paul D. Ryan (R-Wis.), who proposed eliminating funding when he chaired the House Budget Committee, highlight the complex political dynamics at play. As Butler optimistically noted, "We only need a few Republicans to win the day on either side of the Capitol, and we have more than a few of those." He concluded with a hopeful plea for the President to consider the proven value of public broadcasting: "I hope President Trump will review the record . . . and realize our funding is a tremendous bargain for the American people. I feel optimistic, but I don’t want to get ahead of things."

President Donald Trump's inaugural budget proposal, unveiled this week, is characterized by its theatrical presentation and appeal to his core supporters, rather than a detailed policy roadmap. This blueprint is anticipated to mandate substantial reductions across federal programs and agencies frequently targeted by Republicans, including the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), Department of Energy, Department of the Interior, Department of State, Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD), and Department of Commerce. It also proposes cuts to foreign aid, the federal workforce, and education and labor training initiatives. Concurrently, defense spending is slated for an approximate $54 billion increase, a figure that pertains solely to discretionary spending, which constitutes only one-third of the federal budget.

Agencies slated for increased funding include the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) and the Department of Homeland Security, encompassing Customs and Border Protection. These entities are deemed critical for implementing the administration's travel ban policy and constructing the yet-to-be-realized wall along the U.S.-Mexico border. According to a policy expert involved with the transition team, other agencies could face reductions ranging from 15% to 20%, with the EPA specifically targeted for a proposed cut exceeding 25%. A source from Trump's presidential transition team, who worked on policy matters, stated, "I am looking to see who is being protected. Other than that, I don’t expect to learn much more from the budget."

However, these fiscal details may hold little sway with Trump's base, who elected him on a platform of disrupting Washington and dismantling federal institutions. Voters often prioritize decisive action over granular policy specifics. Trump's budget is expected to deliver this by broadly delineating federal agencies into categories of beneficiaries and those facing reductions, as indicated by transition sources. Most proposed agency cuts are expected to lack precise specifications, requiring each Cabinet secretary to identify savings through program elimination, workforce reduction, or a combination thereof. Notable exceptions may include significant portions of the EPA, Commerce, State, and Energy departments, where the budget is projected to advocate for the wholesale termination of programs related to solar energy, biofuels, or any initiative perceived as government subsidization of specific industries, a concept anathema to conservative fiscal principles.

Stephen Moore, a senior economist at the Heritage Foundation who contributed to the budget draft during Trump's presidential campaign, asserted, "Foreign aid is a complete waste of money." Moore emphasized that he had not reviewed the final blueprint, which is being kept under strict wraps, with even political appointees at various agencies lacking access. The White House intends to release a more comprehensive version of the budget in May. Moore further commented, "The big question is whether the political system can absorb these cutbacks. If this was a business, they could cut by 10 to 15 percent. Some of the agencies should be cut 100 percent. The argument that President Trump needs to make is that businesses downsize. They suck in their gut. The government has not done that."

Despite these pronouncements, veteran Republican budget leaders and staffers express skepticism regarding Trump's initial budgetary approach due to its conspicuous lack of critical details. The "skinny budget," as the administration terms this proposal, deliberately omits mandatory spending categories such as Medicare and Social Security—entitlement programs that represent the primary drivers of governmental expenditure over the next decade and which Republicans have long sought to reform. Furthermore, the plan is not expected to incorporate tax proposals, a common component of budget documents. The proposed increase in defense spending, amounting to billions of dollars, also fails to account for existing sequester-imposed budget caps. To achieve the desired spending levels, the Senate would require 60 votes to amend the relevant legislation, a threshold that is far from assured. Douglas Holtz-Eakin, former Republican director of the Congressional Budget Office, characterized this approach as "anorexic, not skinny," predicting, "It will be politics as usual. Trump’s base will like it. Other Republicans won’t. He’ll divide the GOP the same way he always does."

The implications for policy formulation are equally significant. This deficiency in detailed financial accounting within Trump's proposal will likely compel Congress to either undertake the arduous task of making critical decisions within its appropriations bills or postpone legislative action entirely, awaiting further directives from the White House—a scenario reminiscent of the poorly executed rollout of the House Republican healthcare bill. G. William Hoagland, former Republican staff director of the Senate Budget Committee, observed, "The budget is not going to be that revealing. We’ll have to wait until the full budget comes at end of April and in May. What the appropriators need are the account-level details."

The prospect of wholesale departmental eliminations instills considerable apprehension among federal employees and their representative unions. Tim Kauffman of the American Federation of Government Employees, representing approximately 700,000 federal workers, stated, "If budgets cut require reductions in force, then federal workers will be concerned about their job and taking care of their family, but they’re also concerned about being able to care for everyone that their jobs are meant to support."

In the preceding weeks, the White House Office of Management and Budget (OMB) has been engaged in confidential negotiations with agency heads regarding proposed budget reductions.
