For instance, at the Department of State, Secretary Rex Tillerson reportedly secured less severe cuts than the 37% initially outlined in earlier documentation, as reported by POLITICO. A State Department employee, speaking anonymously due to fear of reprisal, indicated that personnel were advised to refrain from mentioning climate change in discussions with OMB officials as the department sought to preserve programs. This employee also noted that State Department staff attempted to reframe foreign aid and education programs as initiatives to foster American or female entrepreneurship, aligning with the president's emphasis on business growth.

This negotiation process has been ongoing across various agencies as the administration finalizes its inaugural budget. Sandy Davis, former associate director for legislative affairs at the Congressional Budget Office, described the current timeline as compressed, which is typical for a new administration. Normally, agencies receive the initial draft of the budget proposal around Thanksgiving, allowing for weeks of back-and-forth before its winter release. The delay in OMB Director Mick Mulvaney's confirmation until mid-February, and the prior management of budget policy by Paul Winfree, a former Heritage Foundation and Senate Budget Committee staffer now serving as the White House's Director of Budget Policy, contributed to this expedited process. Winfree's prior work on Heritage's 2016 budget blueprint has significantly influenced the administration's fiscal thinking, although Trump diverged by opting not to address entitlement reform, with the exception of Medicaid, which faces substantial cuts in the House Republican healthcare bill.

President Donald Trump's Fiscal Year 2018 budget proposal, released by the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) on March 16, 2017, outlines significant shifts in federal spending priorities. The plan, presented to Congress, emphasizes increased investment in national defense, border security, and immigration enforcement, while proposing substantial reductions to numerous other federal programs. This budget marks the initial step in what is anticipated to be a contentious legislative debate over federal expenditures.

The proposal targets agencies such as the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the State Department for significant funding cuts. Nineteen independent organizations reliant on federal funding for public broadcasting, arts initiatives, and regional development projects across the nation would see their funding eliminated entirely. This budget outline focuses specifically on "discretionary" spending for the fiscal year beginning October 1, 2017.

While the Republican-controlled Congress may not approve all proposed cuts, some of these measures have long been advocated by conservative Republicans. Moderate Republicans have already voiced concerns regarding potential reductions to popular domestic programs, including home-heating subsidies, clean-water initiatives, and job training programs.

White House Budget Director Mick Mulvaney stated that the President aims to increase spending on defense, border security, law enforcement, and school choice without expanding the national deficit. He indicated a willingness to consider alternative approaches to achieving these goals.

The budget calls for an additional $54 billion for defense, a down payment on the border wall, and funding for other campaign promises. Notably, the initial outline does not include the proposed $1 trillion investment in infrastructure projects, which the White House stated is forthcoming. The defense spending increase is balanced by cuts elsewhere to maintain the federal deficit at $488 billion. Mulvaney also acknowledged that the proposal would likely lead to a reduction in the federal workforce, citing the need to "drain the swamp."

The budget's priorities were shaped by President Trump's campaign speeches and "America First" platform. This translated into increased funding for the U.S. nuclear arsenal, veterans' healthcare, the FBI, and the Justice Department's efforts to combat drug trafficking and violent crime. The Department of Homeland Security is slated for a 6.8 percent increase, with additional resources allocated for personnel to manage the apprehension, detention, and deportation of undocumented immigrants.

President Trump is requesting $1.5 billion for the border wall in the current fiscal year to fund pilot projects and an additional $2.6 billion in fiscal year 2018. The full projected cost of the wall will be detailed in the comprehensive budget, expected in mid-May, which will also include a 10-year projection of spending and revenues. While President Trump has stated Mexico will fund the wall, the Mexican government has rejected this proposition, and the White House has indicated that U.S. funding will initiate the project. The full budget document will also address "mandatory entitlements" like Social Security and Medicare, programs President Trump pledged to protect during his campaign.

Key areas facing significant reductions include the EPA, with a proposed cut of over 31 percent ($2.6 billion), and the State Department, facing a reduction of over 28 percent ($10.9 billion). Mulvaney assured that the "core functions" of these agencies would be maintained. Foreign aid, contributions to multilateral development banks, and climate change programs at the United Nations are also targeted for cuts. The EPA would see over 50 programs eliminated, including funding for the Clean Power Plan and renewable energy research at the Department of Energy. Regional environmental initiatives, such as those for the Great Lakes and Chesapeake Bay, are also on the chopping block.

Community development grants from the Department of Housing and Urban Development, established in 1974, are reduced, as are over 20 programs within the Department of Education, including some for before- and after-school activities. Anti-poverty grants and programs assisting low-income individuals with energy bills would be significantly reduced. A Labor Department program aiding low-income seniors in finding employment is also affected.

Even President Trump's rural base is not exempt from proposed cuts. The Agriculture Department faces a 21 percent reduction, impacting loans and grants for wastewater projects, reducing staff in county offices, and ending a program that facilitates U.S. farmer donations of crops for overseas food aid.

Military spending would get the biggest boost... (Associated Press) President Donald Trump's first proposed budget, released by the Office of Management and Budget, is photographed in Washington, Wednesday, March 15, 2017. Military spending would get the biggest boost... (Associated Press) WASHINGTON (AP) — Military spending would get the biggest boost in President Donald Trump's proposed budget. Environmental programs, medical research, Amtrak and an array of international and cultural programs — from Africa to Appalachia — would take big hits, among the many parts of the government he'd put on a crash diet. The budget proposal out Thursday is a White House wish list; it'll be up to Congress to decide where money goes. If Trump gets his way, there will be more losers than winners among government departments and programs. Some programs would tread water: WIC grants — money to states for health care and nutrition for low-income women, infants and children — are one example. Money for states grants for water infrastructure projects would be held level as well. Some others would lose everything: Trump proposes to eliminate money for the Corporation for Public Broadcasting, the national endowments for arts and humanities and more than a dozen other independent agencies financed by the government. A sampling: WINNERS —The Pentagon. Trump proposes a 10 percent increase in the massive defense budget, adding $52 billion in military spending in one year top expand personnel, equipment and capability. Another $2 billion would go to nuclear weapons. —Veterans Affairs. Up 5.9 percent. That's an additional $4.4 billion, driven by ever-growing health care costs. —Homeland Security. Up 6.8 percent. That's $2.8 billion more. Most of the increase, $2.6 billion, would be to help kick-start Trump's promised border wall. The president has repeatedly said Mexico would pay for the wall; Mexican officials are adamant that they won't. Trump also wants an extra $1.5 billion for more immigration jails and deportations, and $314 million to hire 1,500 immigration enforcement and border patrol agents. —The National Nuclear Security Administration, which oversees the maintenance and safety of the nuclear arsenal and its research labs. The agency would grow by 11.3 percent, or $1.4 billion, so that it takes up more than half the Energy Department's budget, which would shrink overall. —Opioid prevention and treatment: a proposed $500 million increase in the Health and Human Services Department to counter the epidemic and more money for the Justice Department to combat the problem. —School choice: $1.4 billion more to expand school choice programs, bringing spending in that area to $20 billion, even as the Education Department's overall budget would be cut by $9 billion, or 13 percent. LOSERS: —EPA, facing a 31.4 percent cut, or $2.6 billion. The plan would cut 3,200 jobs at the agency, eliminate a new plan for tighter regulations on power plants, and "zero out" programs to clean up the Great Lakes and the Chesapeake Bay. —Health and Human Services, facing the largest cut in dollar terms: $12.6 billion, or 16.2 percent. The plan would cut $5.8 billion from the nearly $32 billion National Institutes of Health, the nation's premier medical research agency, bringing its total to $25.9 billion. It's not clear what research on diseases or disorders would lose the most money, although the budget plan specifically calls for elimination of a division that focuses on global health. Already, the NIH's budget hasn't kept pace with inflation over the last decade, making it dramatically harder for scientists around the country to win money for research projects into potential new treatments or better understanding of disease. —State Department and U.S. Agency for International Development. Down 28 percent, or $10 billion. Foreign aid would be reduced, as would money to the U.N. and to multilateral development banks including the World Bank. Some foreign military grants would be shifted to loans. —Labor Department. A more than 20 percent cut, or $2.5 billion. To be eliminated: a $434 million program that has helped more than 1 million people 55 and older find jobs, according to the department. The blueprint says the Senior Community Service Employment Program is inefficient and unproven. —Agriculture Department. A nearly 21 percent cut, or $4.7 billion, achieved in part by cutting land acquisition in the National Forest System, rural water infrastructure and statistical capabilities at the department. Trump also proposes reduced staff in county USDA offices, an idea that fell flat in Congress when President Barack Obama proposed a similar reduction. —Transportation Department. Trump proposes a cut of nearly 13 percent, or $2.4 billion. Amtrak, local transit agencies, and rural communities that depend on federal subsidies to obtain scheduled airline service would take the brunt. Trump would eliminate subsidies for Amtrak long-distance train routes, which would most likely mean the end of those routes since they are generally not profitable. Money for the Federal Transit Administration grant program for new light rail and subway construction would be eliminated except for multi-year projects the government has already committed to help fund. —Internal Revenue Service: After years of cuts, the IRS budget would be cut again — by $239 million from this year's spending levels. The IRS budget is down about $1 billion from its height in 2010. Since then, the agency has lost more than 17,000 employees. As a result, the chances of getting audited have rarely been so low. —Commerce Department. A 16 percent or $1.5 billion cut. The plan would eliminate more than $250 million in National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration grants, including a program that helps coastal communities adapt to climate change, deal with invasive species and maintain healthy water and fisheries. Also on the chopping block: the Economic Development Administration, which provides federal dollars to foster job creation and attract private investment; and the Minority Business Development Agency, which is dedicated to helping minority-owned business get off the ground and grow. The Trump administration says the two agencies duplicate work done elsewhere. —School programs: The plan would eliminate a $1.2 billion initiative that supports before- and after-school programs as well as summer programs. —Independent agencies supported by tax dollars. If Trump prevails, a hefty contingent of entities would lose all federal money and be shut. Among them, the Public Broadcasting Corporation, the Appalachian Regional Commission, the Chemical Safety Board, the United States Institute of Peace, the National Endowment for the Arts, the National Endowment for the Humanities, the Corporation for National Community Service and the African Development Foundation. That foundation was established by Congress and provides seed money and other support to enterprises in some 20 countries on that continent. ___ Associated Press writers Andrew Taylor, Lauran Neergaard, Ricardo Alonso-Zaldivar, Stephen Ohlemacher, Joan Lowy, Laurie Kellman, Mary Clare Jalonick, Kevin Freking, Alicia A. Caldwell and Evan Berland contributed to this report.

## Trump Administration Proposes Steep EPA Budget Cuts

The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) confronts significant proposed budget reductions under the Trump administration, igniting widespread debate. The administration's plan targets a nearly one-third decrease in the EPA's budget, amounting to a $2.6 billion cut from its current $8.2 billion. This would reduce the agency's funding to roughly $5.7 billion, a level not seen in approximately forty years when accounting for inflation.

### Internal Discussions and Final Budgetary Plan

Early White House considerations involved a $2 billion reduction, which would have left the agency with just over $6 billion. However, EPA Administrator Scott Pruitt reportedly advocated for a less drastic cut, suggesting a budget closer to $7 billion. Despite these internal deliberations, the White House ultimately decided on a more substantial reduction, proposing the $5.7 billion figure.

### Congressional Pushback and State-Level Initiatives

These proposed EPA cuts are notably deeper than those considered by many congressional Republicans. While some lawmakers have expressed reservations about the agency's regulatory actions, the sheer scale of the proposed reductions has still prompted concern. For perspective, the preceding year saw the House subcommittee responsible for the EPA's budget suggest funding at $8 billion, a mere $291 million decrease from the Obama administration's request. Representative Ken Calvert, the subcommittee's chairman, had previously signaled a commitment to preserving funding for vital state grant programs, essential for projects such as Great Lakes restoration and brownfield site cleanup.

### Wider Budgetary Context

The proposed cuts to the EPA are part of a broader budgetary trend. Similar opposition has surfaced regarding proposed reductions to the State Department budget, even from within the Republican party. Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell has publicly indicated that Senate Republicans will not endorse the deep cuts initially proposed by the Office of Management and Budget for the State Department and the U.S. Agency for International Development, which collectively manage a $50 billion budget.

During his remarks, Trump repeated a claim that the U.S. has spent $6 trillion on wars in the Middle East in the last sixteen years. That figure appears to combine a high estimate of money spent (about $4.79 trillion, including money set aside for 2017) and projected future spending, with a difference of $1 trillion or more, according to PolitiFact . Donald Trump, speaking to the nation's governors at the White House on Feb. 27, said he plans to increase defense spending by finding savings and efficiencies in the government. “We are going to do more with less,” Trump said. President Donald Trump unveiled a $1.15 trillion budget on Thursday, a far-reaching overhaul of federal government spending that cuts the federal workforce and slashes many domestic programs to finance a significant increase in the military and make a down payment on a U.S.-Mexico border wall. Trump's proposal seeks to upend Washington with cuts to long-promised campaign targets like foreign aid and the Environmental Protection Agency as well as strong congressional favorites such as medical research, help for homeless veterans and community development grants. "A budget that puts America first must make the safety of our people its number one priority — because without safety, there can be no prosperity," Trump said in a message accompanying his proposed budget that was titled "America First: A Budget Blueprint to Make America Great Again." The cuts could have devastating effects in the D.C. area, where the federal government is the largest employer. Environmentalists and Environmental Protection Agency employees protested against the proposed cuts Wednesday outside the agency's Washington headquarters. Workers are concerned about their jobs and their ability to serve the country, said the president of the largest union representing EPA employees, AFGE Local 331. "People are definitely worried. They're definitely worried about their jobs, and they're worried about the mission of the agency. We do need clean air and water," Nathaniel James said. The $54 billion boost for the military is the largest since President Ronald Reagan's Pentagon buildup in the 1980s, promising immediate money for troop readiness, the fight against Islamic State militants and procurement of new ships, fighter jets and other weapons. The 10 percent Pentagon boost is financed by $54 billion in cuts to foreign aid and domestic agencies that had been protected by former President Barack Obama. The budget goes after the frequent targets of the party's staunchest conservatives, eliminating the National Endowment for the Arts, legal aid for the poor, low-income heating assistance and the AmeriCorps national service program established by former President Bill Clinton. Spicer Stands By Trump's Wiretapping Claim White House press secretary Sean Spicer said President Donald Trump stands by his claim of being under surveillance by the Obama administration, despite a joint statement from the top two members of the Senate Intelligence Committee. Spicer alleged the House and Senate were not fully briefed, despite statements to the contrary. (Published Thursday, March 16, 2017) Such programs were the focus of lengthy battles dating to the GOP takeover of Congress in 1995 and have survived prior attempts to eliminate them. Lawmakers will have the final say on Trump's proposal in the arduous budget process, and many of the cuts will be deemed dead on arrival. Mulvaney acknowledged to reporters that passing the cuts could be an uphill struggle and said the administration would negotiate over replacement cuts. Mulvaney also went after GOP favorites, including aid to rural schools and health research, while eliminating subsidies for rural air service and the federal flood insurance program that's a linchpin for the real estate market, especially in coastal southern states and the Northeast. Trump's GOP allies Capitol Hill gave it only grudging praise, if any. "Congress has the power of the purse," reminded House Appropriations Committee Chairman Rodney Frelinghuysen, R-N.J. "I look forward to reviewing this," said House Speaker Paul Ryan, R-Wis. "This is not a take-it-or-leave-it budget," Mulvaney admitted. Key Takeaways From Trump's 'America First' Budget Proposal President Donald Trump released his first budget proposal on Thursday. Here are some of the biggest budget cuts and increases that he’d wants made in the federal government. (Published Thursday, March 16, 2017) Law enforcement agencies like the FBI would be spared, while the border wall would receive an immediate $1.4 billion infusion in the ongoing fiscal year, with another $2.6 billion planned for the 2018 budget year starting Oct. 1. Trump repeatedly claimed during the campaign that Mexico would pay for the wall when, in fact, U.S. taxpayers will foot the bill. Twelve of the government's 15 Cabinet agencies would absorb cuts under the president's proposal. The biggest losers are Agriculture, Labor, State, and the Cabinet-level EPA. The Defense Department, Department of Homeland Security and Department of Veterans Affairs are the winners. More than 3,000 EPA workers would lose their jobs and programs such as Obama's Clean Power Plan, which would tighten regulations on emissions from power plants seen as contributing to global warming, would be eliminated. Popular EPA grants for state and local drinking and wastewater projects would be preserved, however, even as research into climate change would be eliminated. 'Lock Her Up': Crowd Chants as Trump Rips Clinton Months after his victory over Hillary Clinton, President Donald Trump prompted chants of "Lock Her Up" from the crowd during a joke made at her expense at a Wednesday night Nashville, Tennessee, appearance. (Published Thursday, March 16, 2017) Trump's proposal covers only roughly one-fourth of the approximately $4 trillion federal budget, the discretionary portion that Congress passes each year. It doesn't address taxes, Social Security, Medicare and Medicaid, or make predictions about deficits and the economy. Those big-picture details are due in mid-May, and are sure to show large — probably permanent — budget deficits. Trump has vowed not to cut Social Security and Medicare and is dead set against raising taxes. "The president's going to keep his promises" to leave Social Security and Medicare alone, Mulvaney said. But the budget increases user fees, boosting the airline ticket tax by $1 per one-way trip. The so-called "skinny budget" is indeed skimpy, glossing over cuts to many sensitive programs such as community health centers, national parks, offering only a vague, two-page summary of most agencies, including the Pentagon, where allocating its additional billions is still a work in progress. Trump Lashes Out at Report of Leaked Tax Returns President Donald Trump lashed out on Twitter Wednesday morning after Rachel Maddow and David Cay Johnston released leaked copies of Trump's 2005 tax returns. The returns revealed Trump paid $38 million on a $152 million income. (Published Wednesday, March 15, 2017) Trump's proposal is sure to land with a thud on Capitol Hill, and not just with opposition Democrats outraged over cuts to pet programs such as renewable energy, climate change research and rehabilitation of housing projects. Republicans like Sen. Rob Portman of Ohio are irate over planned elimination of a program to restore the Great Lakes. Top Republicans like Majority Leader Mitch McConnell of Kentucky and Senate Foreign Relations Committee Chairman Bob Corker of Tennessee are opposed to drastic cuts to foreign aid. And even GOP defense hawks like Armed Services Committee Chairman Mac Thornberry of Texas aren't satisfied with the $54 billion increase for the military. Before the two sides go to war over Trump's 2018 plan, they need to clean up more than $1.1 trillion in unfinished agency budgets for the current year. A temporary catchall spending bill expires April 28; negotiations have barely started and could get hung up over Trump's request for the wall and additional border patrol and immigration enforcement agents, just for starters. Some of the most politically sensitive domestic programs would be spared, including food aid for pregnant women and their children, housing vouchers for the poor, aid for special education and school districts for the poor, and federal aid to historically black colleges and universities. Russian Intelligence Members Charged in Massive Yahoo Hack The Department of Justice charged two Russian intelligence officers and two criminal hackers in the 2014 hacking of Yahoo that affected at least 500 million accounts and millions of user contracts. (Published Wednesday, March 15, 2017) But the National Institutes of Health would absorb a $5.8 billion cut despite Trump's talk in a recent address to Congress of finding "cures to the illnesses that have always plagued us." Subsidies for airlines serving rural airports in Trump strongholds would be eliminated. It would also shut down Amtrak's money-losing long-distance routes and kill off a popular $500 million per-year "TIGER Grant" program for highway projects created by Obama. Copyright Associated Press / NBC Bay Area

President Donald Trump's initial budget blueprint, unveiled by the Office of Management and Budget, proposes a significant recalibration of federal expenditures totaling $1.15 trillion. This fiscal plan prioritizes a substantial augmentation of defense appropriations and earmarks funds for a U.S.-Mexico border barrier, while simultaneously instituting reductions across numerous government departments. The proposal, officially disseminated on March 16, 2017, signifies a divergence from prior administrations by targeting programs previously considered areas of bipartisan consensus.

A foundational principle articulated by Trump is that "A budget that puts America first must make the safety of our people its number one priority — because without safety, there can be no prosperity." The accompanying document bore the title "America First: A Budget Blueprint to Make America Great Again."

The proposed $54 billion increment for the military represents the most substantial increase since the 1980s under President Ronald Reagan. This allocation is designated for immediate enhancements in troop readiness, intensified operations against the Islamic State, and the procurement of new naval vessels, advanced fighter aircraft, and other materiel. This 10 percent expansion of the Pentagon's budget is offset by commensurate reductions in foreign assistance and domestic programs that had previously received protection under former President Barack Obama.

The budget also targets initiatives frequently opposed by conservative elements within the Republican party. These include the proposed dissolution of the National Endowment for the Arts, legal aid services for indigent populations, subsidies for heating costs for low-income households, and the AmeriCorps national service program, established by former President Bill Clinton. These programs have historically been subjects of considerable debate, dating back to the Republican congressional majority in 1995, and have previously withstood elimination attempts. Ultimately, Congress retains the prerogative to approve or reject the president's budget proposal through a complex legislative process.

Although the budget was officially scheduled for release on March 16, 2017, news organizations, including The Associated Press, acquired the document prior to its public dissemination. Law enforcement entities such as the FBI are anticipated to experience minimal budgetary alterations. The border wall initiative would receive an initial $1.5 billion in funding for the current fiscal year, with an additional $2.6 billion projected for Fiscal Year 2018, commencing October 1. This contrasts with Trump's campaign declarations that Mexico would finance the wall; the current proposal indicates that U.S. taxpayers will bear the financial responsibility.

Under this proposal, twelve of the government's fifteen Cabinet-level agencies are slated for budget reductions. The Departments of Agriculture, Labor, State, and the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) are identified as the most significant recipients of these cuts. Conversely, the Defense Department, Department of Homeland Security, and Department of Veterans Affairs are designated for increased funding. The EPA could face the elimination of over 3,000 positions, and initiatives such as Obama's Clean Power Plan, which aimed to regulate power plant emissions contributing to global warming, would be discontinued. However, established EPA grants supporting state and local drinking and wastewater infrastructure projects are slated for preservation.

It is crucial to note that Trump's proposal encompasses approximately one-fourth of the total federal budget, specifically the discretionary spending that Congress appropriates annually. It does not address tax policies, Social Security, Medicare, or Medicaid, nor does it provide projections for deficits or economic performance. These broader fiscal details are anticipated in mid-May and are expected to reveal substantial, likely persistent, budget deficits. Trump has publicly committed to not reducing Social Security and Medicare benefits and has expressed opposition to tax increases. White House Budget Director Mick Mulvaney affirmed, "The president's going to keep his promises" regarding these programs.

The budget's reception on Capitol Hill is anticipated to be contentious, not only from Democrats who are reportedly dismayed by proposed cuts to renewable energy, climate change research, and housing rehabilitation programs. Certain Republicans are also voicing strong opposition. For instance, Senator Rob Portman of Ohio is reportedly displeased with the proposed elimination of a program dedicated to restoring the Great Lakes. Prominent Republican leaders, including Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell and Senate Foreign Relations Committee Chairman Bob Corker, have expressed concerns regarding drastic reductions in foreign aid. Even defense proponents within the Republican party, such as Armed Services Committee Chairman Mac Thornberry, have indicated that the $54 billion military increase may not be sufficient.

Before addressing the Fiscal Year 2018 budget, lawmakers must first resolve over $1.1 trillion in outstanding agency budgets for the current year. Negotiations for a continuing resolution, set to expire on April 28, have seen limited progress and could be complicated by Trump's requests for funding for the border wall and additional border patrol and immigration enforcement personnel.

Certain politically sensitive domestic programs are expected to be spared, including nutritional assistance for pregnant women and children, housing vouchers for low-income families, support for special education and schools in underserved areas, and federal aid to historically black colleges and universities. However, the National Institutes of Health are projected to face a $5.8 billion reduction, despite Trump's recent congressional address emphasizing the pursuit of "cures to the illnesses that have always plagued us."

Additionally, subsidies for airlines serving rural airports, including those in areas that supported Trump, are to be eliminated. Funding for Amtrak's long-distance routes, which are not profitable, will also be curtailed. The "TIGER Grant" program, established by Obama to fund multimodal transportation projects, which provides $500 million annually, will be discontinued. In a move that could impact Republican Senator Dean Heller of Nevada, the budget proposes $120 million to restart the Yucca Mountain nuclear waste repository. This project is highly unpopular in Nevada and was largely halted by the efforts of former Democratic Senator Harry Reid. Senator Heller faces re-election in 2018 in a state that voted for Democrat Hillary Clinton in the previous presidential election.

query: What was the reported death toll from the attack on Tunisia's National Bardo Museum, and how many of the deceased were foreign tourists?

documents.text:
**Tunisia's Bardo Museum Attack: A Grim Toll and Ongoing Investigation**

The **deadly assault** on Tunisia's National Bardo Museum on March 18, 2015, has resulted in a tragic death toll of **23 people**. Among the victims, a significant number were **foreign nationals**, with **18 international visitors** losing their lives. The attack, which involved **gunmen opening fire inside the museum**, also claimed the lives of **five Tunisians**, including the **two attackers themselves**.

Authorities are currently **intensively investigating** and are pursuing leads on **two to three more individuals** suspected of being involved in the **horrific incident**.

At the Charles Nicolle hospital, where many of the injured and deceased were taken, officials stated that as of Thursday, **seven victims remained unidentified**. The **swift response** from emergency services was evident, with rescue workers seen **evacuating individuals of all ages** from the museum.

In the wake of the violence, Spanish officials confirmed that **two Spanish tourists were found safe**. They had **sheltered within the museum** throughout the night following the attack. The **full scope of those affected** by this violent event highlights the **gravity of the assault** at this renowned Tunisian landmark.

The Bardo Museum attack in Tunis, a tragic event that claimed the lives of 20 tourists and several others, has brought Tunisia's vulnerability to the forefront. While the nation has largely avoided the widespread conflicts that have destabilized other Arab states, this incident underscores the persistent threat of terrorism. The revelation that one of the assailants, Yassine Laabidi, was known to authorities, though without specific intelligence of his intentions or affiliations with known militant groups, raises critical questions about security protocols and information sharing. This attack, which also resulted in the death of a Tunisian police officer and the assailants themselves, has prompted an ongoing manhunt for accomplices, with authorities indicating that two to three individuals may still be at large.

The victims of this horrific assault represented a diverse group of nationalities, highlighting the global reach of such violence. Among those tragically killed were visitors from Japan, Italy, Colombia, Australia, France, the United Kingdom, Poland, and Spain. The attack's impact was deeply felt across these nations, with varying official death tolls reported, adding to the somber complexity of the aftermath. In a testament to the resilience of the human spirit, three individuals—two Spanish tourists and a Tunisian museum worker—were discovered to have survived by hiding within the museum overnight, a harrowing experience that underscores the terror they endured.

In the wake of the attack, Tunisia's commitment to overcoming this challenge was powerfully articulated by President Beji Caid Essebsi. His address to the nation conveyed a resolute stance against terrorism, emphasizing that such acts of violence would not deter the country's progress or instill fear. He declared, "These monstrous minorities do not frighten us. We will resist them until the deepest end without mercy." This unwavering determination was echoed by the UN Security Council, which issued a statement condemning the act and affirming that no terrorist action could derail Tunisia's democratic trajectory. The council extended condolences to the victims and their families and called for the perpetrators to be brought to justice.

The Bardo Museum, a significant cultural and tourist hub, was the target of this brutal assault. Its planned reopening early the following week signals a determination to reclaim normalcy and demonstrate defiance against those who seek to disrupt the nation's life. The attack's timing was particularly poignant, occurring as deputies within the adjacent parliament building were engaged in discussions regarding anti-terrorism legislation. This proximity to a legislative body, coupled with reports that the gunmen may have initially intended to target parliament, suggests a calculated and audacious plan. A statement from a jihadist media outlet corroborated this, indicating that the shift to targeting tourists occurred after the gunmen encountered resistance from police at the parliament. While the statement did not explicitly name the group responsible, it provided a chilling account of the events.

The economic implications of this attack are substantial, particularly for Tunisia's vital tourism industry. This sector is a cornerstone of the nation's economy, drawing significant numbers of European visitors to its renowned resorts. The government, which had only recently solidified after a lengthy political transition following the Arab Spring, faces the immense challenge of reassuring international visitors and safeguarding its economic stability. The BBC's Arab affairs editor, Sebastian Usher, noted that Islamist militants have actively sought to undermine this fragile democratic transition, which remains one of the most hopeful outcomes of the Arab Spring in the Middle East.

This incident is not the first time Tunisia has faced such threats. In 2002, a bomb blast at a synagogue in Djerba, attributed to Al-Qaeda, resulted in the deaths of 19 people, including 11 German tourists. The increasing instability in neighboring Libya has also heightened security concerns in recent months, creating a more volatile regional environment. Furthermore, the departure of a significant number of Tunisians to fight in conflict zones like Syria and Iraq has raised anxieties about potential attacks by returning militants on home soil. These converging factors underscore the complex security landscape Tunisia navigates.

The Bardo National Museum, a treasure trove of Tunisian history and art, stands as a symbol of the nation's rich cultural heritage. The attack on this iconic institution was an assault on Tunisia's identity and its aspirations for a peaceful and democratic future. The outpouring of solidarity from Tunisians, who took to the streets to protest the violence, wave their flags, and light candles outside the museum, demonstrated a collective resolve to stand against terror. World leaders also voiced their condemnation and offered support for Tunisia's counter-terrorism initiatives, recognizing the broader implications of this event for regional stability and the global fight against extremism. The international community's unified response underscores the shared commitment to upholding democratic values and ensuring that such acts of violence do not succeed in their aim of sowing discord and fear. The resilience shown by the Tunisian people in the face of this tragedy is a powerful testament to their enduring spirit and their determination to protect their hard-won freedoms. The ongoing efforts to rebuild and reinforce security measures are crucial steps in ensuring that Tunisia can continue its path towards a stable and prosperous future, free from the shadow of terrorism. The international community's continued support will be vital in this endeavor, reinforcing the message that Tunisia is not alone in its struggle against extremism.

Tunisia is grappling with the aftermath of a deadly attack at the Bardo National Museum, which claimed the lives of 18 tourists and three Tunisians. In response, four arrests have been made, and civil society groups are organizing a silent demonstration. The nation is in shock following the incident, which marks the deadliest attack on civilians in 13 years.

Prime Minister Habib Essid stated that one of the two gunmen was known to security services. He revealed in an interview with France's RTL radio that Tunisia is collaborating with other nations to gather more information about the attackers, identified as Yassine Laabidi and Hatem Khachnaoui. Essid noted that Laabidi had been flagged to intelligence, though not for any particularly serious reasons.

President Beji Caid Essebsi addressed the nation, declaring, "We are in a war with terror, and these savage minority groups will not frighten us. The fight against them will continue until they are exterminated." Authorities are actively searching for two to three suspected accomplices.

Following the attack, thousands of Tunisians gathered in the capital's main thoroughfare, Avenue Habib Bourguiba, waving flags and singing songs from the 2011 Arab Spring revolution. Young activist Mohammed Nasri expressed dismay, stating, "After the last election, we thought we made a big step forward to real democracy, but what happened today was like a KO to our future."

Tunisia's main trade union confederation and other civil society groups have called for a silent demonstration outside the Bardo museum at 3 pm GMT to show national unity against terrorism.

The health minister, Said Aidi, confirmed that 23 people died, including 18 foreign tourists and five Tunisians, with nearly 50 people injured. The claim of responsibility by the so-called Islamic State for the Tunis attack warrants contextualization. Tunisia embarked on a democratic transition in 2011 following the overthrow of Zine al-Abidine Ben Ali. The attack specifically targeted the Bardo Museum, a site of immense cultural significance. Among the victims were 17 tourists and two Tunisians. Fabienne, a French tourist, described seeking refuge in a museum gallery with fellow tourists and their guide. "We couldn't see anything, but there must have been a lot of them. We were afraid that, at any moment, they would come and kill us," she recounted, her voice trembling.

The Bardo Museum in Tunis, Tunisia, became the scene of a devastating attack on Wednesday, where gunmen disguised as military personnel opened fire. The Bardo Museum is renowned for its exceptional collection of ancient Tunisian artifacts, including mosaics from the Roman and Greek eras. Hundreds of Tunisians gathered in downtown Tunis, waving their national flag and chanting anti-terrorism slogans, demonstrating resolve. Tunisia's 2011 revolution inspired similar movements in neighboring countries. The Bardo Museum attack occurred during a sensitive period for Tunisia, as the government pursued economic reforms and public spending cuts. Affiliates of the Islamic State, active in Iraq and Syria, were increasing their presence in North Africa, particularly in unstable Libya. In 2013, a militant detonated a bomb at the Sousse beach resort with no other casualties. TUNIS, Tunisia (AP) — Spanish tourist Juan Carlos Sanchez was waiting in Tunisia's leading museum to go have lunch with his tour guide when a man came streaking past, fleeing gunshots. Policemen guard the entrance of the Bardo museum in Tunis, Tunisia, Thursday, March 19, 2015, a day after gunmen opened fire killing over 20 people, mainly tourists. One of the two gunmen who killed... (Associated Press) As gunmen fired on tourists, Sanchez and Cristina Rubio, who is four months pregnant, hid in fear — and stayed in hiding all night, hours after police killed the two gunmen and the museum's other visitors were escorted to safety. "We saw a man come running in chased by the shots of a terrorist. We just saw the man who hit the floor and realized that someone was shooting and went to hide," Sanchez told The Associated Press in the maternity ward of a Tunis hospital Thursday. "We hid in a small room and that's where we stayed until the police saw us today and then we left." Sanchez's voice was steady but his eyes were still wide as he recounted the attack and ensuing all-night vigil at the National Bardo Museum in Tunis. query: What predictions did the author have about the tech companies Intel, HP, Cisco, IBM, and Oracle in December, and how were they proven correct?

documents.text: Hewlett-Packard's Mark Hurd didn't sit down for an interview with me, but he's begun doing phoners post-earnings and I'm still holding out hope that 2010 will be the year for a two-camera, in-depth production. Come on, Mr. Hurd. Now's a good time! I predicted that Google would come up with new revenue streams, and with YouTube turning a profit and Android looking good, that prediction came true too, and its stock price reflects a new found euphoria. Microsoft's redesign of the Zune came true, but it didn't include the phone I expected. Steve Ballmer tells me no way, but I still see the company entering the smart phone arena with a handset of its own. I predicted that Intel , HP, Cisco , IBM and Oracle were all oversold and that such lows presented good opportunities for investors. All of them would have been good bets last December. I asked whether Palm would survive 2009. Despite the Pre and the Pixi, I think the jury's still out. Dell is entering the smart phone market with hardware of its own. Maybe that means Nokia is the best (last? Only?) hope for Palm in 2010. I predicted that Advanced Micro Devices would be acquired in 2009. Didn't happen. With that $1 billion from Intel as part of that new settlement, 2010 could be a much better year for AMD. But "much better" is relative when you're scraping for Intel scraps merely to survive. I said Apple posed the biggest opportunity for tech investors in 2009 and I was right. I'd say the same holds true for 2010. Look for the tablet Mac, but not anywhere close to the $2,000 price tag being thrown around. Apple will re-invent mobile computing once again with this device, but beyond hardware and software, it'll do so with price as well. If 2009 was a big year for 3D movie-making, 3D TV will blossom big in 2010. New TVs from Sony , Samsung, LG and others will grab lots of attention, and new 3D TV production from upstarts like 3Ality will show viewers enormous possibilities. Some other predictions. This is cool stuff. Digital books and eReaders from Amazon , Sony, Barnes & Noble and 17 other manufacturers will enjoy their true break-out year. Microsoft will enjoy a big-time renaissance. Cisco continues to be one of this region's most exciting growth stories. Netbooks start to fade as a fad as consumers realize that their iPhones, Research In Motion's Blackberrys and Nokia smart phones get the job done at a fraction of the size (and cost.) And Twitter, as we know it, will fade away. Either acquired or shut down. $155 million in venture capital so far, and still no meaningful revenue stream to speak of. And certainly no profits. At some point, someone will step up and yell "Tulips!" Meantime, I'm sure of a few key predictions in 2010: Innovation and the entrepreneurial spirit will continue to thrive here in Silicon Valley. It's what makes my job so fun and America so great.

My personal Top 10 technology predictions for 2010 One of my responsibilities as the Director of IT Innovations at PwC is to spend a good deal of time researching and developing insights on the impact of emerging technologies. This year, for the first time, I thought it might be fun and, frankly, quite useful to share with you my thoughts on what I believe may be the big IT trends in 2010. While I was somewhat tempted to be bold and creative in my forecast, I decided to ground the Top 10 in areas that have some real momentum. If you agree with the predictions, what might that mean for your work and your industry? In what area do you think I got it completely wrong? I’d love to know what you think. 1. Software as a Service 2010 will be a big year for providers of software as a service (SaaS). The obvious big names in this space will release new offerings to compete with popular desktop applications. New and existing operating systems that are built primarily to support the SaaS model will begin to be more widely accepted and adopted. 2. Netbooks This popular form-factor will have outstanding sales and may even surpass laptop sales by year-end. Given its remarkable low-cost, we will likely see more offerings that make available free Netbooks. In addition, the ubiquity of embedded Web-cams will drive further use of personal video in both non-work and work environments. 3. Cloud Services An obvious growth area in 2010; we will see new and expanded services from all the usual suspects. Expect major announcements from large businesses and government agencies choosing to move some of their core applications and data to the cloud. 4. Mobile Money By late 2010, paying for products and services via a mobile device such as a cellphone will begin to emerge in the mainstream US. Multiple flavors will be available including custom applications and text messaging. More likely in 2011-12 will be the emergence of banking services from the big Telco’s. Rather than simply being a middleman, the telecommunication companies may announce banking divisions. 5. Free Software If current trends continue, it’s quite possible that all software will be available in a form of free, but 2010 will be the first year that this trend reaches a point of inflection. A combination of enterprise-class open source, freemium, freeware, ad-supported, and alternate revenue-model software will have lasting and destructive impact on the notion of license-paid software. 6. Harvesting the Social Graph and Web-Squared 2010 will see the introduction of the first widely available and easily usable products for better understanding the mass of unstructured data being accumulated across public and private clouds. The emergence of intelligent solutions to interpret massive related and un-related data in order to create forecasts and identify trends will help people make more sense of the world and see previously hidden signals. 7. More Video Continued investment in video infrastructures will see greater use in work and non-work environments. It will be more common (but still not ubiquitous) to have video conversations with colleagues and external parties such as customers and suppliers. Rigorous competition in this space between the major players and many start-ups will continue to push the price down for high-quality video. Greater use of PCs and Netbooks with Web-cams will continue towards critical mass. In addition, content creation will continue its profound migration from text to video, further consuming bandwidth and forcing more enterprise investment in network infrastructure. 8. Green IT This may be a inconsistent area of investment as continued tight budgets and more immediate costs (e.g. migration to updated operating system) distract from major green initiatives. However, going into 2011 and beyond, broad adoption of virtualization and further movement towards hosting in the cloud may help organizations lower their data center carbon footprint. 9. Mobile Location-based Services (LBS) and Augmented Reality Expect to see an extraordinary number of start-ups and existing technology companies offering mobile LBS-related services. Proximity-based solutions will become more common. Mobile devices will begin to offer compelling overlay data for the real world that help people with existing and new activities. Lots of noise and confusion will ensue as both consumers and providers try to figure out acceptable services. For example: how will people respond when they stroll through a mall and are bombarded with text messages from different retail stores? 10. Social Spaghetti Integration More social features will begin to show up in ERP apps. New and increased support for ERP solutions that, for example: integrate social networking will see a further blurring of the lines between work and non-work applications and activities. Do you agree or disagree with any of my predictions? I’d love to know what you think.

But real-time is more than just a horde of new Twitter-like services hitting the Web in 2010 (although that's inevitable -- cargo cults abound). It's a combination of factors, from the always-connected nature of modern smartphones to the instant gratification provided by a Google search. Why wait until you get home to post a restaurant review, asks consumer trends tracker Trendwatching, when scores of iPhone apps let you post feedback as soon as you finish dessert? Why wonder about the name of that song, when humming into your phone handset will garner an instant answer from Midomi? Look out, too, for real-time collaboration: Google Wave launched earlier this year, resulting in both excitement and confusion. A crossover between instant messaging, e-mail and a wiki, Wave is a platform for getting things done together. Web users, however, remain baffled. In 2010, Wave's utility will become more apparent. Location, location, location Fueled by the ubiquity of GPS in modern smartphones, location-sharing services like Foursquare, Gowalla, Brightkite and Google Latitude are suddenly in vogue. As I ruminated in this column two weeks ago, Foursquare and its ilk may become the breakout services of the year ... provided they're not crushed by the addition of location-based features to Twitter and Facebook. What's clear is that location is not about any singular service; rather, it's a new layer of the Web. Soon, our whereabouts may optionally be appended to every Tweet, blog comment, photo or video we post. Augmented reality It's yet to become part of the consumer consciousness, but augmented reality has attracted early-adopter buzz in the latter part of 2009.

Tablet PCs suck. They're underpowered, only marginally portable, and awkward to use in anything but a traditional seated position, with a desk to support them. Microsoft and its cadre of hardware partners have been trying for years to create a compelling tablet computing experience -- and consistently failed. Yet this year, persistent rumors of an Apple tablet -- an "iTablet" -- created a real buzz. But to believe that Apple can somehow succeed where all others have failed is to ignore some fundamental realities of tablet computing. Reality No. 1: The lap doesn't work as a desk Have you ever tried filling out a paper questionnaire on a moving train? Even with a clipboard, the mechanics are anything but graceful. With each lurch or bump you risk missing the mark. At best you end up with illegible chicken scratch; at worst, you put the pen right through the paper. It's a real mess. [ Stay up on tech news and reviews from your smartphone at infoworldmobile.com. | Get the best iPhone apps for pros with our business iPhone apps finder. | See which smartphone is right for you in our mobile "deathmatch" calculator. ] Now imagine this same scenario with the iTablet. You're trying to enter an e-mail address or a URL using a nifty onscreen keypad. Each time you aim for the "R" key you end up hitting "T" instead. Then, just as you go to touch the Send button, you slip and hit Cancel -- or worse. Contrast this with a typical small laptop or netbook, where the combination of your lap and palms acts as stabilizing influences. Add the surety of a traditional keyboard -- for typing, navigating, and so on -- and this "lapdesk" modus operandi gives the clamshell form factor a distinct advantage when operating in raucous mobile environments.

## The Digital Age: How We Consume Media Today

The way we consume media is **radically changing**. Technology and how people behave are the main drivers. With more **smartphones** and the expected rise of **NetBooks**, plus widespread **WiFi** and better **broadband**, getting high-definition content on mobile devices will become much easier. This means people will want to watch and read all sorts of media **anywhere, anytime**.

### New Ways to Get Content and the Rise of Premium

Movie studios are likely to **shorten the time** between a movie's release in theaters and when it's available to rent or buy at home. This is called "collapsing windows." The goal is to bring the movie experience home faster. A big question is: will we have to **pay to access** these new ways of getting movies? While some leaders, like Rupert Murdoch, suggest charging for content on their sites, **free options will still exist**. People will likely **pay for special, high-quality content**, like what you get from HBO or Showtime, seeing it as a worthwhile expense.

### Social Media: Where We Find and Promote Content

**Social media's power is growing fast**. Social networks and messaging apps will become even more popular and influential. Platforms like **Twitter and Facebook** help spread recommendations quickly, and this will only get bigger as more people use smartphones and social media. For businesses, social media will be **essential for talking directly to customers** and for people to **discover new content**. When people can easily share their opinions on Twitter and Facebook, it changes how traditional advertising works. Because opinions spread so fast, movie marketers can't just rely on a big opening weekend anymore. Now, it's **crucial to get people excited** and encourage them to **spread the word naturally**.

Marketers everywhere can learn from how Paramount successfully promoted "Paranormal Activity." They let people **"request" the movie** in their local areas. Social media's impact on what we watch and read is clear, with people often sharing articles or videos their friends recommend on Facebook. **Twitter, especially, offers a personalized news feed**, pulling together content from many different websites, bloggers, and journalists. Even MySpace is becoming a place for content and for people to connect over shared interests.

### Content Gets Fragmented, Niche Content Thrives

We're going to see a **huge increase in the amount of content being made**. This means media companies will need to **stand out** from a growing number of less professional options. The amount of content available is expected to **skyrocket**. Because it's so easy to create and share content on platforms like YouTube, blogs with ads, and Twitter, people will find **niche content perfectly suited to their interests**. This doesn't just apply to what users create; the cable TV industry will continue to do well because people want **specialized channels**. Oprah Winfrey moving from regular TV to her own cable network, OWN, shows this trend towards more viewer choice. People are moving away from regular broadcast TV to **niche cable channels**, and advertisers are following. Traditional TV will likely still have an edge over platforms like YouTube because of its **better production quality and value**, areas where user-generated content often falls short.

### Challenges for Old Media and Studio Plans

**Print media faces tough competition** in a world full of citizen journalists and user-created blogs. Local newspapers may close as they lose readers and advertising to free online sources. Even sites like The Huffington Post, which pays its bloggers very little, can significantly impact the online readership of national newspapers. To deal with so many entertainment choices, movie studios are focusing on reaching the **broadest audience possible**. Making movies is expensive, so focusing on niche audiences isn't financially smart. As a result, studios will **make fewer films**, cutting out specialty or art-film departments. Instead, they will focus on **"sure-thing" investments**. They will buy independent films that seem like they could appeal to a wide audience, like Paramount did with "Paranormal Activity," but they **won't invest in creating or distributing niche products**.

Paul Sancya, AP Paul Sakuma, AP Sponsored Links If 2010 taught us anything, it's that few people are able to accurately predict even the simplest events in the near-term future, let alone the big ones. From the price of gold, to unemployment, to Google Wave, prognosticators got more than a few major items wrong in their predictions lists.Here, in no particular order, are "10 for 10" -- 10 of the worst predictions of 2010."Unemployment Drops Below 9 Percent," Newsweek "business predictions for 2010," December 2009.Unfortunately, despite increasing efforts to contain our nation's unemployment epidemic, the unemployment rate never dipped below 9.5 percent, and climbed to 9.8 percent in November. Let's hope that a dip in the unemployment rate below 9.5 percent is something we will see in the coming year."The GOP will make good, though not great, gains in the midterms," John Berbyshire, National Review , Dec. 30, 2009Republicans took an incredible 63 seats in the House and came within just a few seats of taking the Senate. But the next part of Derbyshire's prediction -- "They will immediately embark on a strategy guaranteed to snatch defeat from the jaws of victory" -- may still come to pass."A new social network will rise to join the big ones." Marshall Kirkpatrick, ReadWriteWeb , Dec. 24, 2009.Not quite. In fact, 2010 was the year of Facebook. It topped 500 million users, it was the subject of constant IPO speculation, Time named its founder Person of the Year, and it spawned a hugely successful feature film. Meantime, its competitors struggled to stay afloat (looking at you, MySpace and Bebo). In short, no one is even attempting to challenge Facebook for supremacy. Perhaps someday a new player will rise to make the king of all social networks a second-rate choice, but that day doesn't look to be coming any time soon."Twitter, as we know it, will fade away. Either acquired or shut down." CNBC Tech Check Predictions 2010 , Dec. 1, 2009Many predicted the downfall and/or complete demise of Twitter in 2010, and many were completely wrong. Twitter has instead silenced its critics by gaining more than 100 million users, increasing staff from 130 to 350 people and raising more than $200 million in financing, raising its value to $3.7 billion, all during the past 12 months "To believe that Apple can somehow succeed where all others have failed is to ignore some fundamental realities of tablet computing." Randall C. Kennedy, Infoworld.com , Dec. 22, 2009Apple still hasn't lost its golden touch: More than 4 million iPad buyers in a single quarter can attest to that."In 2010 ... netbooks will take off," Julia Boorstin, CNBC , Dec. 1, 2009Between the announcement and introduction of the iPad and its many tablet-based competitors, and the lack of performance and high prices, netbook sales spiraled downward in 2010. Expect a continued free-fall in sales unless prices are slashed drastically, or the iPad ceases to exist."In 2010, Wave's utility will become more apparent," Pete Cashmore, CNN Tech , Dec. 3, 2009Massively over-hyped during its introduction in late 2009, Google Wave was to be the central hub of our lives, merging our e-mail, social networking, instant messaging, wiki and every other form of digital media in one "easy to use" package. "Easy to use" it was not, however. So while some were optimistic that Google Wave's utility would shine through eventually, the rest of us decided to simply ignore its clunky, scatterbrained approach to digital life.Google promptly flushed Wave down the toilet in August, less than a year after its introduction."A serious correction could send the precious metal toward $870," Saxo Bank analysts, cited by CNBC , Dec. 17, 2009.Not even close to coming true. Gold prices have steadily rallied upward in the past year, rising past the $1,400 mark per troy ounce late in the year."By late 2010, paying for products and services via a mobile device such as a cell phone will begin to emerge in the mainstream U.S.," Jonathan Reichental, reichental.com , Jan. 1, 2010.Another prediction that may come to pass someday but that wasn't meant to be in 2010. Simply put, people are not yet ready to give up their credit cards in favor of a "credit phone" to pay for purchases on- or offline."We project that at least half the cast will, of their own volition, opt to stay," Sarah Ball, Newsweek , December 2009While the cast of ABC's monumental hit would probably have liked to stay on the island and continue to collect their handsome paychecks, writers instead opted to kill everyone off in the series' finale.Here's one prediction you can count on coming true: Most 2011 predictions will also be spectacularly wrong, and I'll be watching.J. Conboy is a highly opinionated freelance writer with many specialties. You can find more of his work on AOL News, AOL Travel Luxist and various other AOL sites.

Here are predictions for the upcoming year from National Review Online's esteemed contributors, offering insights into potential events and trends.

**John Derbyshire's Prognostications:**

Reflecting on last year's forecasts, my predictive accuracy fell slightly short of a .500 batting average, a humbling but not entirely unexpected outcome. Consequently, my predictions for the coming year are more circumspect, though I offer a few nonetheless.

*   **Astronomy:** We anticipate the observation of an exoplanet exhibiting characteristics analogous to Earth, orbiting a star with spectral similarities to our Sun.
*   **Geopolitics (North Korea):** Kim Jong Il's military regime will likely precipitate his deposition. This prediction, carried over from the previous year, remains a strong possibility unless the leader's demise precedes it.
*   **Literary Market:** "We Are Doomed: Reclaiming Conservative Pessimism," after an initial period of low visibility, is projected to ascend to the top of the bestseller lists. Its success will coincide with the release of Sarah Palin's memoir, leading to a speculative literary phenomenon where the two titles mutually neutralize each other's impact.
*   **Cultural Milestones:** Virginia Woolf's assertion that "on or about December 1910 human character changed" is expected to gain widespread citation.

John Derbyshire is a columnist for National Review Online and the author of "We Are Doomed: Reclaiming Conservative Pessimism."

**Jonah Goldberg's Forecasts:**

*   **Domestic Policy:** The Obama administration will likely defer action on gay rights and immigration to regain centrist appeal in anticipation of the 2010 elections.
*   **Electoral Landscape:** Marco Rubio is predicted to defeat Charlie Crist in a senatorial race that will evolve into a nationally significant contest. The progressive commentary, exemplified by Frank Rich's circle, will likely interpret Rubio's victory as a definitive blow to the Republican Party's mainstream viability.
*   **Senate Races:** Chris Dodd is expected to lose his re-election bid, with a colorful depiction of his departure from office. Harry Reid's re-election campaign will also falter, with him characterizing the results as a fraudulent outcome orchestrated by the insurance industry.
*   **Judicial Appointments:** Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg's retirement due to health concerns will present a complex challenge for President Obama. The progressive base will advocate for a nominee more liberal than Sonia Sotomayor, while prevailing political exigencies may necessitate a more moderate selection. Obama's inclination is predicted to lead him to appoint a highly liberal candidate.
*   **Congressional Control:** While the Republican Party is not anticipated to regain control of the House of Representatives, the midterm elections are expected to be exceptionally close.
*   **National Security:** Following an initially stable period, challenges related to the Guantanamo Bay detention facility will escalate. The terrorism trial scheduled for New York is predicted to be fraught with procedural difficulties from its inception.
*   **Media Landscape:** National Review Online will experience a significant expansion of its blog content, potentially exceeding the number of its editorial staff. A new reality television program focusing on the production of another reality show is expected to trigger a self-referential crisis within the cultural commentary sphere.
*   **Media Personalities:** Keith Olbermann's perceived seriousness will manifest in an extreme, symbolic gesture.
*   **International Affairs:** A binding global treaty addressing climate change is unlikely to materialize in 2010. Concurrently, Iran is projected to achieve nuclear weapons capability, even as its internal pro-democracy movement gains momentum.
*   **Publishing Success:** "The Goldberg File," transitioning to a newsletter format, is anticipated to achieve substantial success, securing its continued publication for another year.

Jonah Goldberg is the editor-at-large of National Review.

**Additional Predictions:**

*   **Global Initiatives:** A worldwide eugenics program will be advocated for.
*   **Immigration Policy:** Barack Obama's aunt, Zeituni Onyango, who is an undocumented immigrant, will not face deportation. This prediction is a repeat from the previous year's forecast.
*   **Economic Trends:** The economy will continue its sluggish performance, punctuated by occasional downward fluctuations.
*   **Republican Strategy:** The Republican Party is expected to achieve moderate gains in the midterm elections but will subsequently implement strategies that undermine their electoral advantages.
*   **Public Discourse:** Michelle Obama may inadvertently make an unscripted, controversial statement that the mainstream media will not report. Individuals who reference such a statement will face accusations of racism.

### Navigating the New Economic Landscape: Unemployment Outlook

As we adjust to a **new normal** following recent economic challenges, achieving **sub-9 percent unemployment** within the next year represents a **significant success** and a realistic objective. This perspective, shared by NEWSWEEK's Rana Foroohar, acknowledges that a complete return to pre-recession conditions may take time.

### Recession's Toll and Emerging Positive Signs

The recent economic downturn resulted in the loss of **7.2 million jobs**, causing the unemployment rate to climb from **4.9 percent** in December 2007 to its current **10 percent**. This substantial increase occurred over approximately two years. Consequently, a **one percentage point reduction** in unemployment over 12 months, a **10 percent decrease**, is a manageable target. Encouragingly, the trend is already improving, with the rate dropping from **10.2 percent to 10 percent** between October and November.

### Early 2010 Forecast: A Positive Path Forward

Historically, December often sees job gains, even if temporary, due to seasonal hiring. This suggests that by early 2010, the unemployment rate is likely to stabilize in the **high 9 percent range**. It is important to recognize that unemployment acts as a **lagging economic indicator**. The economic growth that began last summer, and which most experts anticipate will continue, should provide sufficient impetus to drive unemployment **below 9 percent**. This progress will be further supported by the recently passed **job-creation package** from Congress.

### Future Challenges: State Finances and Public Sector Cuts

Despite these positive trends, a return to **over 10 percent unemployment in 2011** remains a possibility. State budgets are still under considerable strain. If stimulus funds are insufficient to address future deficits, a wave of layoffs could impact **public sector workers**, including police, teachers, and firefighters. Given that unemployment lags economic shifts, such an increase, if it occurs, would likely not manifest until **2011**, potentially aligning with the president's re-election efforts.

query: What was Carrie Fisher best known for in her acting career?

documents.text:
The entertainment community is deeply saddened by the passing of iconic actress Carrie Fisher, a loss that has created a significant void in Hollywood and across the globe. Despite hopes for her recovery after a recent cardiac event, the news of her death has been met with widespread shock and profound grief. Fisher was widely admired for her sharp intellect, remarkable talent, and vibrant spirit, leaving an enduring legacy on both fans and fellow artists.

This collection presents heartfelt tributes from her friends and collaborators, reflecting on her extensive career. Particular attention is paid to those who shared the *Star Wars* universe with her, from the original films to newer chapters. As further tributes emerge, this compilation is regularly updated, highlighting the profound and widespread impact of her absence.

**Carrie Fisher, the iconic Princess Leia from the Star Wars saga, has passed away.** The beloved actress, known for her sharp wit and candid insights, died after suffering a heart attack on a flight from London to Los Angeles on December 23rd. She was hospitalized in Los Angeles and passed away four days later.

While **Carrie Fisher is most famous for her unforgettable portrayal of Princess Leia**, her talents extended far beyond the galaxy far, far away. The daughter of Hollywood legends Debbie Reynolds and Eddie Fisher, she was also a **highly acclaimed author**, celebrated for her **trenchant wit and fearless honesty**.

Fisher's life and work were marked by a profound ability to find humor and profound truth in even the most challenging circumstances. Here is a selection of her **memorable and insightful quotes**:

**From Twitter:**

*   "If my life wasn't funny, it would just be true, and that is unacceptable." (2011)
*   "There's no room for demons when you're self-possessed." (2014)

**From her 1987 semi-autobiographical novel, *Postcards From the Edge*:**

*   "Instant gratification takes too long."
*   “I shot through my twenties like a luminous thread through a dark needle, blazing toward my destination: Nowhere.”
*   “Actually, I am a failed anorexic. I have anorexic thinking, but I can't seem to muster the behavior.”
*   “Life is a cruel, horrible joke and I am the punch line.”
*   “You know how I always seem to be struggling, even when the situation doesn't call for it?”
*   “Sometimes I feel like I've got my nose pressed up against the window of a bakery, only I'm the bread.”
*   “My inner world seems largely to consist of three rotating emotions: embarrassment, rage, and tension. Sometimes I feel excited, but I think that's just positive tension.”

**From her 2008 memoir, *Wishful Drinking*:**

*   “Sometimes you can only find Heaven by slowly backing away from Hell.”
*   “I feel I'm very sane about how crazy I am.”
*   “No motive is pure. No one is good or bad — but a hearty mix of both. And sometimes life actually gives to you by taking away.”
*   “I quote fictional characters, because I'm a fictional character myself!”
*   “Having waited my entire life to get an award for something, anything … I now get awards all the time for being mentally ill. It’s better than being bad at being insane, right? How tragic would it be to be runner-up for Bipolar Woman of the Year?”

**From her new memoir, *The Princess Diarist*:**

*   “Vultures are difficult to charm unless you’re off somewhere rotting in the noonday sun. Casually rotting … a glib cadaver.”
*   “Someone has to stand still for you to love them. My choices are always on the run.”
*   “I act like someone in a bomb shelter trying to raise everyone’s spirits.”
*   “Movies were meant to stay on the screen, flat and large and colorful, gathering you up into their sweep of story, carrying you rollicking along to the end, then releasing you back into your unchanged life. But this movie (Star Wars) misbehaved. It leaked out of the theater, poured off the screen, affected a lot of people so deeply that they required endless talismans and artifacts to stay connected to it.”

Carrie Fisher's **legacy as Princess Leia and as a brilliant writer and thinker will undoubtedly endure.**

Sources: Goodreads.com, USA TODAY research

Carrie Fisher, forever remembered as **Princess Leia**, was also a **powerful voice for destigmatizing mental illness**. Her **brave openness** about her own struggles with **depression and bipolar disorder** profoundly changed how people viewed these conditions. Fisher, who died after a cardiac arrest, openly shared her history of **substance abuse in the 1970s and 80s**. She brilliantly turned these experiences into the celebrated novel, ***Postcards From the Edge***. By the 1990s, Fisher had revealed her **bipolar disorder diagnosis** and her ongoing battles with **depressive episodes**. In a 1995 interview, she told Diane Sawyer, "I used to think I was a drug addict, pure and simple — just someone who could not stop taking drugs willfully. And I was that. But it turns out that I am severely manic depressive." Her **honesty was crucial** in breaking down the **stigma surrounding mental health**, a topic that deeply connected with her fans, especially after her death.

LISTEN: Carrie Fisher, Terry Gross — And Gary The Dog toggle caption Anne-Christine Poujoulat/AFP/Getty Images Actress Carrie Fisher, beloved for her iconic role as Princess Leia, died on Tuesday at the age of 60. She leaves behind her daughter, Billie Lourd, her brother, Todd Fisher, her mother, Debbie Reynolds — and her French bulldog, Gary. Gary Fisher is a celebrity in his own right — he traveled widely with Fisher and was a star on Instagram and Twitter. And when Carrie Fisher visited NPR's studios in New York City, to talk to Fresh Air host Terry Gross, Gary came along, too. Gross, however, was in Philadelphia. She didn't know there was a dog in our studios. She didn't even know that was allowed. In the conversation that followed, it's impossible to miss the buoyant personalities of both Fishers — Carrie and Gary. (And a quick look at the photos will reveal, Carrie Fisher wasn't lying about that tongue.) LISTEN: 'Oh, my God, I hear him licking your hand.' GROSS: So I have to ask you about your dog Gary who is now a kind of famous dog, and you use him as your profile picture on Twitter. And he's - what kind of dog is he? FISHER: He is a French bulldog. GROSS: Oh, OK. FISHER: And he's right here in the studio with me. At it again this morning 🐶📖#garyworkswithhismom #theprincessdiarist #npr #garyinnewyork2016 #garyloveshismom #garyfisher A photo posted by Gary Fisher (@garyfisher) on Nov 22, 2016 at 9:23am PST GROSS: Yeah. You get to take him everywhere. It's like — I don't know that they usually let dogs in the NPR studio. You're in the NPR bureau in New York. Is he officially a therapy dog? FISHER: Yeah. I — you know, I didn't get him for that, but he's very soothing to have around. He's licking my hand right now. He's just very nice to have around. GROSS: Oh, my God, I hear him licking your hand (laughter). FISHER: Can you hear him? Oh, my God that is such a loud lick. GROSS: Yes. FISHER: (Laughter). GROSS: Let's listen. (SOUNDBITE OF DOG LICKING) FISHER: (Laughter). GROSS: Oh, my God that is such a loud lick (laughter). FISHER: Well, he has a very big tongue. GROSS: (Laughter). toggle caption Barry Brecheisen/Invision/AP FISHER: Well, at least it's not that wet, but it's a very, very long tongue. GROSS: (Laughter) So did you have him certified as a therapy dog, so you could, like, take him onto planes and things like that? FISHER: Yes. Yes. So he sits with me on the plane. Frequently, he sits in the chair, and I sit on the ground. GROSS: So how did you find him? FISHER: I got him here in New York in the village at a very tragic pet store. GROSS: And ... FISHER: So he looks like he was from like a puppy mill. He's not — everything is sort of wrong with him. Enlarge this image toggle caption Robin Marchant/Getty Images for Tribeca Film Festival Robin Marchant/Getty Images for Tribeca Film Festival GROSS: (Laughter). So what attracted you to him? FISHER: The tongue. No — I don't even know if the tongue was like that when I first got him. It just gradually got longer and longer and never went into his mouth.
