This approach, involving the designation of significant share blocks for major institutional entities like mutual funds or corporate bodies, is a prevalent tactic to underpin the success of a prominent stock launch. In exchange for a commitment to purchase, these investors typically consent to a period during which they are restricted from selling their acquired shares.

### Lockup Provisions and Possible Exemptions

While the objective is to cultivate a base of enduring shareholders, Snap's filing also acknowledges the company's prerogative to unilaterally suspend these lockup stipulations. This provision affords adaptability should prevailing conditions necessitate it. The IPO has already garnered considerable enthusiasm, with reports suggesting it was oversubscribed by at least $6.8 billion prior to the weekend.

### Schedule and Valuation

As of Monday's official submission, Snap had not yet formalized any binding commitments concerning these lockup arrangements. The company is projected to finalize its share pricing on March 1, with trading slated to commence on March 2. It remains a possibility that Snap may revise its share valuation or augment the quantity of shares available before trading officially commences.

### Extract from Snap's Regulatory Submission

The company's filing explicitly states: "We expect approximately 50 million shares of our Class A common stock purchased by investors in this offering will be subject to a separate lock up agreement with us providing for a restricted period of one year following the date of this prospectus. These agreements will reduce the number of our shares available for sale in the public market during their term. We may, in our sole discretion, waive any of these lock up agreements before the restricted period expires." A spokesperson for Snap declined to offer additional commentary on this subject.

Mark Zuckerberg says he created Facebook Inc. FB 1.17% to make the world more open and connected. Twitter Inc. TWTR 1.28% says it wants to give everyone the power to share ideas instantly. Snap Inc., which this week could become the biggest technology public offering in years, is the unsocial social-media company. Not only does its app feature messages that disappear, the company defiantly operates unlike most Silicon Valley outfits, where collaboration and wide-open office spaces are prized. When Snap Inc. goes public, Snapchat co-founders Evan Spiegel and Bobby Murphy will retain control of more than 90% of the company's voting rights. WSJ's Shelby Holliday looks at how Snap's rare share structure stacks up against other tech companies. Photo: Evan Agostini/Invision/AP Former employees say often the only way they knew co-founder and CEO Evan Spiegel was at work was by seeing his chauffeured SUV. He avoids holding companywide meetings and prefers to dispense information to individuals or small groups, they say. In contrast to the big, open campuses of Facebook and Alphabet Inc.’s GOOGL 0.74% Google, designed for employee collaboration, Snap doesn’t have a headquarters. Its main offices are scattered around Venice, Calif., keeping employees siloed and making communication difficult, the former employees say. The company in its IPO filing listed the lack of a headquarters as a risk factor that could hurt morale, prevent adequate oversight and cost talent. So far, Snapchat has won legions of teenage users, who like that what they share now won’t define them permanently—and that it keeps their parents out of their business. And it has attracted advertisers who want to reach a young audience, setting the stage for Snap’s initial public offering. The listing on the New York Stock Exchange is expected to take place this week and could value the company at as much as $22 billion, which would make it the biggest U.S. tech IPO in years. A Snap spokeswoman declined to comment or make Mr. Spiegel available for this article, citing the company’s quiet period ahead of the public offering. The question is whether this management style and focus on privacy will help the company compete with television networks and challenge the Facebook juggernaut. Mr. Spiegel’s approach at times has left staff in the dark about important initiatives, the former employees say. And it has made the company resist giving advertisers the ability to narrowly target users based on their behavior and preferences, a strategy that has enabled Facebook, Google and others to mint enormous profits. ‘Doesn’t talk much’ “Evan doesn’t talk much,” says Hemant Taneja, managing director at venture-capital firm General Catalyst, an early Snap investor, saying confusion can stem from the fact that Mr. Spiegel doesn’t always feel compelled to explain his concepts to the public. In September, he surprised potential investors when he began publicly calling Snap “a camera company” instead of a social-media company. Some investors wondered if Snap was suddenly becoming a hardware company, but Mr. Taneja says the camera concept wasn’t new. Snapchat Evan Spiegel chief executive speaks onstage at the American Magazine Media Conference at Grand Hyatt New York in February last year. Photo: Larry Busacca/Getty Images Rapid growth and increasingly intense competition are putting Mr. Spiegel’s management style to the test. Snap’s full-time workforce tripled during last year to 1,859 as it expanded internationally. It is competing head-to-head with the global social-media giants, especially Facebook, whose Instagram unit already has emulated Snap’s features with some success, such as the Stories feature it rolled out over the summer. Snap in its public filing attributed part of its slowing user growth to increased competition. Part of the competition is about the race to hook users first. Instagram is more popular than Snap internationally, and people could be inclined to stick with Instagram if they are already using Facebook, or with Snap if they landed there first. “You aren’t going to switch if you are satisfied with what you are using,” says Wedbush Securities analyst Michael Pachter. Snap is also vying with traditional television networks to woo young viewers and advertising. Young people have been drifting away from television to their smartphone screen, where hours of videos from friends can be played. Meanwhile, Snap must continue to generate unusual and captivating content—much of which has been meticulously managed by Mr. Spiegel, former employees and business partners say. The listing is expected to take place this week and could value the company at as much as $22 billion. Photo: Brendan McDermid//Reuters Like many tech executives, Mr. Spiegel attended Stanford University. But that is about where the comparisons end. He was more social than secretive when he arrived at college in 2008, friends recall, even though his high-profile lawyer-parents had had a rancorous and public divorce when he was in high school in Los Angeles. Far from geek, he was the life of the party, they say, and his style was more hipster than hoodie: skinny jeans, V-neck T-shirts, flip flops. These days, Mr. Spiegel, who is more of a product designer than a computer scientist, eschews many of the tech industry’s habits, preferring to be away from what he has described as the bubblelike culture of Silicon Valley. As he got to college, Facebook was taking off. In just four years since its launch in 2004, the social network had gained 58 million active users (it now has 1.9 billion monthly active users). In February 2009—during Mr. Spiegel’s freshman year—it added the Like button. From the start, Mr. Spiegel wasn’t a fan, friends recall. He would come to see it as a form of social pressure, where people create falsely perfect worlds in the quest to rack up likes. Mr. Spiegel said Snapchat was much more, a place for spontaneous interaction that evaporates in the same way a real conversation would. It was also about creativity and fun: Photos and video could be animated in whimsical and ridiculous ways using its functions to make selfies that vomit rainbows, sport puppy and bunny ears, and wear banana faces. Ukrainian tennis player Elina Svitolina used Snapchat at the French Open in Paris last May. Photo: Christopher Levy /Zuma Press In most cases, photos and videos that users send in messages disappear after they are viewed, and other content disappears after 24 hours, although some items can be saved. In the recent company video, created for its IPO, Mr. Spiegel says the ephemerality is “why people love creating Snaps. Because there isn’t pressure to feel pretty or perfect. Self-expression isn’t a contest, it’s not about how well you can express yourself, it’s about being able to communicate how you feel, and doing that in the moment.” Conceived for mobile Also key to Snap’s success, and unlike incumbents such as Facebook and Twitter: Its design and concept is mobile-only. Content is presented vertically, to fill a smartphone screen; location-based tags and filters are popular; and bite-size content is swipeable. As Facebook was trying to transition to mobile in 2012, Snapchat sped out of the gate, catching the bigger company by surprise. Mr. Spiegel saw the smartphone as the new movie screen. When Mr. Zuckerberg offered $3 billion to buy Snapchat in 2013, Mr. Spiegel turned him down. Mr. Spiegel’s talent combining a Hollywood approach to content with a keen business sense is admired by many who know him. Related Video Snap's initial public offering will enable the social media platform's founders and two Silicon Valley venture-capital firms to rake in a huge fortune. WSJ's Lee Hawkins explains. Photo: Zuma Press “For someone of his age, he operates with much more wisdom than anyone else I have seen. I find him to be a very, very clear thinker,” Eric Schmidt, executive chairman of Alphabet, said in an email. When faced with critical decisions on issues like funding and partnering and selling, Mr. Schmidt says, “he gets it right every time.” He says Mr. Spiegel, a former student of his at Stanford, is now a friend and customer. Snap has a five-year, $2 billion contract for Google to provide cloud-data storage. Other people note Mr. Spiegel’s timing on the dominance of mobile, getting ahead of Facebook and the recognition that social media was getting boring. Revenue is generated by brands placing short video advertisements and simple location-linked overlays called “geofilters,” plus more elaborate “Lenses”—Taco Bell made a Lens that turned faces into tacos being doused with hot sauce. More than two dozen media and entertainment outlets, including The Wall Street Journal, also provide news, sports, fashion and features. Advertisements are sold to place in the content the media companies produce. Snap’s revenue last year jumped more than 500% to $404.5 million. Its net loss widened to $514.6 million, and its user growth slowed somewhat, rising 48% to 158 million in the fourth quarter from the same period a year earlier. That revenue is a fraction of the $27.6 billion last year at Facebook, which also booked $10.2 billion in profit. Mr. Spiegel has been wary of advertising from the start, worrying that it would feel intrusive. As his business marketing team was crafting presentations for prospective advertisers and business partners, Mr. Spiegel didn’t want to explain the app to them, said one of the former employees. He preferred that CEOs learn it—not from a presentation but from their children. It was “difficult for a salesperson to run that one up the flagpole,” the former employee recalls. A product demonstration is now part of the meetings. When Snapchat’s first ads made their debut in October 2014—Facebook was already generating $12.5 billion in annual revenue by that year—Mr. Spiegel’s ambivalence was evident in a company blog post. It told users if they didn’t want to watch the ads, which were in a different section, “No Biggie.” The company wouldn’t place ads in personal communiqués because that would be “totally rude.” It wanted Snapchat advertising to be “the way ads used to be, before they got creepy and targeted.” In the past, he has resisted efforts to collect and share information that would enable advertisers to target the app’s individual users. Lately, he has made concessions. In January, for example, Snap signed a deal with Oracle Corp. to help marketers use data from offline purchases, such as supermarket loyalty cards, to target Snapchat users with more relevant ads. Ad targeting Snap is still far away from the more aggressive approaches of Facebook and Google, which have used precise ad targeting to make billions in profits. As a result, the giants can outspend Snap on talent and fresh content, and bankroll development of potentially expensive new products, such as hardware that taps into augmented reality, or tech that blends computer images onto a user’s real view of the world. Mr. Spiegel with his fiancée, model Miranda Kerr, at a White House state dinner last May. Photo: Andrew Harnik/Associated Press Because of Snap’s vision of ads as less intrusive than most digital advertising—more like old-fashioned television spots made for a broader audience—Snap ads must have a wider appeal, with high production values, and be spliced into the rest of the app in an interesting way. During the Super Bowl, for example, users could choose to adorn their selfies with either Falcons’ or Patriots’ football helmets or cascade gushers of colorful Gatorade over their heads. Snap’s requirements set a high bar for its partners. The small group of media and entertainment outlets that appear on Snapchat have tough targets to meet, according to a person familiar with the process. If Snap isn’t happy, it suggests changes to the content, and if the material doesn’t get enough traffic, the providers fear they could be booted off the app, according to a current and a former editor of content for Snapchat Discover, the section of the app where publishers post content. Until lately, a lot of Snap’s advertising has come directly from brands like Coca-Cola Co. and Yum Brands Inc.’s Taco Bell. It has been slow to woo Madison Avenue’s big ad agencies, which have bigger budgets and can commit to longer contracts, and to form partnerships that would enable advertisers to measure the effectiveness of their campaigns. Last summer, Snap hired Viacom ’s Jeff Lucas to help court big deals. As the IPO has neared, Snap has signed deals with Oracle Data Cloud, Nielsen and others to ramp up its use of metrics that will dispel some of the mystery that has enshrouded the effectiveness of advertising on the app. The stock-exchange listing will force more transparency about the business, including regular updates on user and engagement data. Even so, Mr. Spiegel will continue to keep a tight grip on the company after it goes public. Snap is selling to the public only shares that have no voting power. Afterward, Mr. Spiegel and co-founder and chief technologist Bobby Murphy will retain more than 90% of the voting shares. Write to Betsy Morris at betsy.morris@wsj.com and Georgia Wells at Georgia.Wells@wsj.com

Snapchat's remarkable revenue surge of 589 percent last year, despite remaining unprofitable, highlights a complex financial picture. While its daily active user base expanded to 158 million, a notable increase from 107 million a year prior, this growth has exhibited a deceleration in recent quarters. This slowdown in user acquisition, as noted by Cramer, could present a significant challenge. The introduction of Instagram Stories by Facebook last summer, perceived as a direct imitation of Snapchat's core functionality, is cited as a primary contributor to Snap's diminished user growth rate. Specifically, Snap's daily active user base saw a mere 10 percent increase in the latter half of 2016 compared to the preceding six months, a stark contrast to the 33 percent growth in the prior period. Performance issues on Android devices also played a role in this deceleration.

Furthermore, Cramer identified substantial cloud-hosting expenditures as another potential concern. Snap anticipates annual spending of $400 million on Google Cloud and an additional $200 million on Amazon Web Services over the next five years. This considerable outlay contrasts with its projected $1 billion in ad revenue for the current year, meaning at least 60 percent of this revenue will be absorbed by hosting costs.

Despite these challenges, Cramer maintains an optimistic outlook, stating, "the good absolutely outweighs the bad here, at least for the near- and intermediate-term future." He believes Snap's impressive user growth still presents ample opportunities for international expansion. Cramer posits that any company experiencing such significant revenue growth must be executing its strategy effectively. Adding to this positive sentiment, Goldman Sachs, an underwriter for Snap's upcoming Initial Public Offering (IPO), forecasts that the company could achieve approximately $2 billion in revenue by 2018. The potential for Snap to become a compelling investment is further amplified if it strategically deploys the substantial capital raised through its IPO to develop and successfully launch new business ventures.

For the immediate future, Cramer anticipates Snap will be a "phenomenal trade," with its stock poised for a significant surge upon its public debut. The company's decision to go public approximately a year earlier in its growth cycle than Twitter did offers investors the opportunity to capitalize on its early-stage expansion. Cramer advises caution for investors, suggesting, "If Snap spikes really hard right out of the gate, I suggest waiting for a pullback before you buy or accepting that you missed it if the stock is more than doubled at the opening."

Snap is expected to price its IPO on Wednesday within a range of $17 to $18 per share, valuing the company at $20 billion. At this anticipated IPO price, Snap would trade at 20.3 times its projected sales for the year. While this valuation initially appeared steep to Cramer, he notes it is comparable to Facebook's 19.4 times sales at its own IPO, indicating it is not an unprecedented valuation. Cramer suggests that the stock could easily double, driven by large institutional investors who acquired significant stakes during the IPO and will likely increase their holdings in the open market, thereby benefiting from a superior cost basis relative to the closing price.

Snap Inc. has made a significant splash in the financial world, successfully pricing its Initial Public Offering (IPO) above its initial projections and raising an impressive $3.4 billion. This substantial capital infusion comes at a time when investors, despite lingering questions about profitability and the absence of voting rights for new shareholders, have shown immense enthusiasm for what is widely regarded as the most anticipated tech IPO in recent memory. At $17 per share, Snap, the parent company behind the immensely popular disappearing-messaging application Snapchat, now boasts a market valuation of approximately $24 billion. This valuation places Snap at more than double the size of its rival Twitter and marks the most robust valuation for a U.S. tech IPO since Facebook's landmark debut in 2012.

The company had initially anticipated a valuation ranging between $19.5 billion and $22.3 billion, a projection that was clearly surpassed by the overwhelming investor demand. Reports indicate that the offering was oversubscribed by more than ten times, suggesting that Snap could have potentially priced its shares as high as $19. However, the company made a strategic decision to prioritize securing mutual funds as long-term investors, rather than hedge funds that are often more inclined towards short-term trading strategies. This IPO represents a crucial test of investor confidence in a social media platform that has profoundly captivated younger demographics, particularly teenagers and young adults. These users are drawn to Snapchat's unique features, such as the ability to apply playful and creative filters to their selfies, fostering a sense of fun and self-expression. The fundamental challenge for Snap now lies in its ability to effectively translate this widespread user appeal into sustainable and tangible financial success.

Despite experiencing a nearly seven-fold increase in revenue, the Los Angeles-based company has reported a significant escalation in its net loss, which saw a substantial jump of 38 percent over the past year. Furthermore, Snap is contending with formidable competition from established tech giants like Facebook, alongside the persistent challenge of decelerating user growth. The pricing of 200 million shares at $17 on Wednesday night, exceeding its stated range of $14 to $16, was significantly bolstered by opportune market timing. The technology IPO market had experienced a considerable slowdown in 2016, marking the quietest period for such launches since 2008, which consequently left investors actively seeking fresh and promising investment opportunities. This successful debut by Snap could potentially serve as a catalyst, paving the way for other highly valued tech startups, often referred to as "unicorns" due to their private valuations exceeding $1 billion, to confidently enter the public market.

Investors demonstrated a remarkable willingness to acquire Snap shares, even though these shares carry no voting power, a notable departure from typical IPO structures. This is particularly significant given the increasing concerns among fund managers regarding corporate governance and their desire for greater influence over company executives. Snap is scheduled to commence trading on Thursday on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol SNAP. A visual representation of this significant milestone, a banner for Snap Inc., was prominently displayed on the facade of the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) on the eve of the company's IPO in New York City on March 1, 2017, a moment captured by Reuters photographer Brendan McDermid.

While Snap is embarking on its public journey at a considerably earlier stage of its corporate development compared to industry giants like Twitter or Facebook, its current valuation of nearly 60 times its revenue is more than double the 27 times revenue Facebook commanded at its IPO in 2012. To substantiate its ambitious valuation and effectively address concerns about its user growth trajectory, Snap has strategically highlighted the profound importance of Snapchat in the daily lives of its users. The company has emphasized the significant amount of time users dedicate to engaging with the app and has pointed to the burgeoning trend of young people favoring visual communication over traditional text-based interactions as a key avenue for future revenue generation.

Snap has remained somewhat guarded about its specific strategies for leading and monetizing image-driven conversations. However, it has actively encouraged investors to place their trust in the visionary leadership of its co-founder, Evan Spiegel. During the investor roadshow, Spiegel was presented as a "once-in-a-generation founder." This 26-year-old entrepreneur is poised to benefit significantly from the IPO, retaining a stake valued at approximately $4.05 billion, representing roughly 17 percent of the company. Spiegel and his co-founder, Bobby Murphy, are each selling 16 million shares in the IPO, a transaction that is expected to net them a substantial $272 million apiece. This event is also poised to create numerous "overnight millionaires" among other Snap investors and early employees.

Spiegel and Murphy will maintain substantial control over Snap's stock through a distinctive three-tier share class structure. This innovative arrangement grants Spiegel and Murphy ten votes for every share they hold, effectively consolidating their decision-making power. Existing investors will receive one vote per share, while new investors purchasing shares in the IPO will be allocated no voting rights whatsoever. This carefully crafted structure ensures that the founders retain significant control over the company's strategic direction and long-term vision, a common strategy employed by tech companies seeking to maintain their core identity post-IPO. The company's strong emphasis on user engagement and the inherent potential of visual communication, coupled with the founders' robust control mechanisms, paints a compelling picture of a company betting heavily on its unique appeal and forward-thinking vision to drive future success in the intensely competitive digital landscape. The resounding success of this IPO could indeed signal the dawn of a new wave of tech companies going public, emboldened by Snap's remarkable ability to capture significant investor attention and capital, even in the face of its current financial standing.

## Snap's Hardware Ambitions and IPO: A Glimpse into the Future

Snap's recent ventures into hardware, notably with its Spectacles drone, offer a compelling preview of potential future product directions that could significantly shape the company's trajectory. As Snap embarks on its initial public offering this week, investor attention is sharply focused on its growth strategies. The company finalized its IPO pricing on Wednesday, with shares scheduled to begin trading on the New York Stock Exchange on Thursday. This event represents the most significant technology IPO since Alibaba's debut in 2014. Snap set its IPO valuation at $17 per share, surpassing the anticipated $14 to $16 range, thus valuing the enterprise at $24 billion.

### The Centrality of Visual Data

The acquisition of augmented visual data, including photographs and video, is fundamental to Snap's operational model, as it directly boosts user engagement within the Snapchat ecosystem. Communication on Snapchat is inherently a visually driven medium, designed to foster deep user immersion. In its public offering prospectus, Snap revealed that the average Snapchat user accesses the application more than 18 times daily, with over 2.5 billion messages and images exchanged each day.

### Strategic Vision for Camera Technology

Evan Spiegel, Snap's Chief Executive Officer, has consistently articulated his company's strategic priorities regarding camera technology. In a recent investor presentation concerning Snap's public offering, Spiegel suggested that cameras serve to enhance human communication rather than simply preserve memories. "We are at the early stages of exploring the full potential of camera capabilities," Spiegel stated during the presentation. The introduction of Spectacles drew scrutiny from critics regarding Snap's strategic shift into hardware manufacturing. This product is carefully designed to integrate video capture seamlessly into daily activities, aligning with the company's broader goal of cultivating a continuous stream of visual content from its user base into Snapchat.

Snap, the company behind Snapchat, is expected to price its initial public offering today and start trading on the New York Stock Exchange tomorrow. Ahead of Snap’s IPO, Recode Senior Editor Kurt Wagner answered some of what are no doubt the questions you’re dying to ask. What does an IPO actually mean? IPO stands for Initial Public Offering, and it's the first time that a private company starts selling its stock on the public stock market. That means you (yes, you!) are now able to buy a piece of Snap. Does this mean we’re all getting rich? Sorry, you're probably not gonna get rich, but you never know. People invested in Facebook at $20-$30 a share. It's now worth over $100, so you can make some money. Someone is getting rich off this IPO. Who? Snapchat's co-founders are going to get very rich. Evan Spiegel and Bobby Murphy are both going to be worth billions of dollars by the time this whole thing is done. Why does Snap want to go public? Companies go public for a number of reasons: First, to pay back their investors that have given them money along the way to keep the business going. It's a good way for founders and executives to actually cash out some of the stock that they've earned during their time working there. It's also a way for the company to raise money from people like you who may invest. They can use that money to do things like acquisitions, international expansion and new product growth, things that Snap would love to do. Does Snap even make money? Believe it or not, Snapchat is a real business. The company brought in over $400 million in revenue last year, the vast majority of which came from advertising. So, even though the app is free, the company is still making money by putting ads alongside all the content that you're creating inside the app. Doesn’t Snap make money in other ways? Snap also makes hardware, called Spectacles. These are video-recording sunglasses that, until recently, you had to buy out of a vending machine. Snap says those sunglasses don't create meaningful revenue for the company. So while they may be cool, they're not actually benefitting the company's bottom line. What could possibly go wrong with Snap going public? When you're a publicly traded company, you have to share a lot of information and you have to do it regularly. When you're private, you can keep things like revenue or user growth to yourself, but now, as a publicly traded entity, Snap's going to have to report that number publicly to investors. That means if things aren't going well, all of us are going to know about it.

query: What was CNN's coverage of the Steubenville Rape Case verdict criticized for focusing on?

documents.text:
CNN's reporting on the Steubenville rape case verdict has ignited significant controversy due to its perceived misdirection. Rather than prioritizing the victim's experience and the profound ramifications of the sexual assault, outlets including CNN, with contributions from anchors Candy Crowley and Poppy Harlow, and legal analyst Paul Callan, disproportionately centered the narrative on the consequences for the two teenage offenders.

This coverage frequently spotlighted the "bright futures" of convicted rapists Trent Mays and Ma'lik Richmond, lamenting the perceived derailment of their lives by what was characterized as a "one little indiscretion." Such framing often eclipsed the brutal reality faced by the sixteen-year-old survivor. The narrative presented by CNN appeared to exhibit sympathy for the young men's distress upon learning their sentences, detailing their emotional responses and the prospect of juvenile detention.

For instance, during a broadcast, Poppy Harlow described the courtroom atmosphere as "incredibly emotional" and "incredibly difficult" to observe, focusing on how the two young men, identified as "star football players" and "very good students," witnessed their lives seemingly unravel. She recounted Ma'lik Richmond's despair, quoting him as stating, "My life is over. No one is going to want me now." This was presented alongside the established fact that both were found guilty of raping a sixteen-year-old girl during parties where alcohol was a significant factor.

Following the verdict, the apologies offered by Mays and Richmond were featured. Mays stated, "I would truly like to apologize to [the girl], her family, my family, and community. Those pictures shouldn't have been sent around, let alone taken." Richmond added, "I had no intentions of doing anything like that, and I'm sorry I put you guys through it. I'm sorry." The reporting noted Richmond receiving comfort from his attorney.

Further intensifying the criticism, Harlow highlighted a detail from Ma'lik Richmond's defense counsel, suggesting it was the first time Richmond's father had expressed love for him, implying this perceived deficit in paternal affection as a contributing factor to his actions.

Legal commentator Paul Callan also contributed, discussing the enduring impact of being designated as registered sex offenders. He explained how this classification, mandated by Ohio statutes, would affect their future employability and societal integration, suggesting it would "haunt them for the rest of their lives."

The original analysis posits that this media approach constituted a disservice to the victim, advocating for a more appropriate focus on the severity of the sexual assault and the enduring psychological trauma experienced by the survivor. It suggests that CNN should have framed the narrative around the perpetrators as "rapists" from the outset, emphasizing their criminal actions rather than their ostensibly ruined futures. The revised perspective contends that the focus should have been on the gravity of their culpability and the comparatively lenient sentences they received, with one sentenced to two years and the other to one year in juvenile detention. The document questions the efficacy of rehabilitation in such cases, implying that the offenders might acquire more knowledge about evading detection than about the inherent wrongfulness of their conduct.

The piece also underscores the inadequacy of the sentences, questioning their potential as deterrents and the likelihood of recidivism. It criticizes the societal and legal systems for failing to instill fundamental respect for consent, implying that the adults responsible for their upbringing have not adequately addressed this critical issue. While acknowledging that their lives should indeed be challenging, the document suggests they should also receive support and educational interventions, which it implies will not be provided in a constructive manner.

The author expresses dismay at the legal consequences for the perpetrators, highlighting the irony of their potential future difficulties in securing housing while the victim confronts a lifetime of trauma.

The document concludes by noting that the legal proceedings are not entirely concluded, with the Ohio attorney general announcing a grand jury investigation into potential additional charges. The attorney general acknowledged the profound hardship for the victim and her family, emphasizing that rape is a tragedy, particularly when the victim is "continually re-victimized in the social media." The author concurs that both mainstream and social media platforms bear responsibility for improving their coverage and preventing further harm to victims.

An update indicates that CNN, mirroring Fox News, failed to redact the victim's name from footage of Trent Mays' courtroom apology, leading to the subsequent removal of the video.

## Steubenville Trial: Media Coverage Sparks Outrage and Debate

The Steubenville rape trial verdict triggered a firestorm of online reactions, many of which were deeply disturbing. CNN's reporting, in particular, drew significant criticism for its perceived tone and focus.

### Sympathy for the Accused?

During a report from the Steubenville courtroom, anchor Candy Crowley and correspondent Poppy Harlow discussed the guilty verdicts for Trent Mays and Ma'lik Richmond. Harlow's commentary, expressing a complex emotional response, stated, "Incredibly difficult, even for an outsider like me, to watch what happened as these two young men that had such promising futures, star football players, very good students, literally watched as they believed their lives fell apart." Legal expert Paul Callan further elaborated on the consequences, noting that the conviction would result in the Steubenville rapists becoming registered sex offenders, a status that "will haunt them for the rest of their lives."

While these statements were factually accurate, their delivery and overall tone were perceived as problematic. The segment, in its attempt to acknowledge the defendants' backgrounds, inadvertently seemed to elicit sympathy, which was poorly received, especially when juxtaposed with overtly offensive online comments from individuals defending the perpetrators. For instance, one sarcastic tweet read, "The Steubenville story is all too familiar. Be responsible for your actions ladies before your drunken decisions ruin innocent lives." Another, more disturbingly sincere tweet, stated, "So you got drunk at a party and two people take advantage of you, that's not rape you're just a loose drunk slut."

### Critiques of Media Framing

Cases like the Steubenville rape trial often highlight the complexities and sometimes distorted perceptions of justice. The blogosphere reacted strongly to CNN's coverage. Gawker's Mallory Ortberg criticized Crowley and her colleagues for focusing on the impact on the rapists' "promising futures." Ortberg argued, "Their dreams and hopes were not crushed by an impersonal, inexorable legal system; Mays and Richardson raped a girl and have been sentenced accordingly." She further pointed out that such reporting often humanizes the accused while leaving victims as anonymous figures, a valid concern given that the victim's testimony was not prominently featured until late in the trial.

The media's focus on the defendants' ruined lives had been evident even before the official guilty verdicts. For example, a preview piece on Good Morning America, titled "The Steubenville Rape Case: What You Haven't Heard," detailed the backgrounds of Mays, an honors student, and Richmond, who came from a disadvantaged background. This report concluded by lamenting that the two teens "face incarceration in a detention center until their 21st birthdays and the almost-certain demise of their dreams of playing football." While expressing empathy for the defendants might have been more palatable before their conviction, this approach still fell short of objective reporting.

### Ongoing Legal Proceedings and Media Responsibility

The legal proceedings are far from over. The state of Ohio planned to convene a grand jury to investigate potential further charges, suggesting the media attention surrounding the case would continue. Ohio Attorney General Mike DeWine commented after the verdict, "As I said already, any rape is a tragedy. But, it is even more of a tragedy when that victim is continually re-victimized in the social media." An update on Monday revealed that CNN was not the only network to face scrutiny; MSNBC and Fox also allowed the victim's name to be broadcast. The hope is that mainstream media outlets will adopt a more balanced approach moving forward.

CNN on Sunday aggressively covered the breaking news of the verdict in the Steubenville rape case. Star football players Trent Mays, 17, and Ma’lik Richmond, 16, were found guilty of raping a 16-year-old girl. Propelled by social media and mainstream media, the Steubenville case has long held a high profile. Defense attorney Walter Madison, right, holds his client, 16-year-old Ma’lik Richmond, second from right, while defense attorney Adam Nemann, left, sits with his client Trent Mays, foreground, 17, as the judge reads the decision. (Keith Srakocic/Associated Press) The scrutiny that comes with its profile descended on CNN. In repeated segments yesterday, CNN featured reports from reporter Poppy Harlow, who was in Ohio for the verdict. Here’s the transcript of some of the coverage at the 11 a.m. hour: ANNOUNCER: This is CNN breaking news. CANDY CROWLEY, CNN ANCHOR: I’m Candy Crowley in Washington. RELIABLE SOURCES is just ahead. But, first, a breaking story we’re following. Two star football players in Steubenville, Ohio, have been found guilty of raping a West Virginia teenager. The story has attracted national attention. The judge just ruled a few minutes ago. Listen in. (BEGIN VIDEO CLIP) JUDGE THOMAS LIPPS, HAMILTON COUNTY FAMILY COURT: In this case, you know, regarding the charges of rape, both defendants Ma’lik Richmond and Trent Mays are committed to the Department of Youth Services for a minimum of one year and a maximum period until you’re 21. (END VIDEO CLIP) CROWLEY: Again, this case was played out in juvenile court, that is why there was a judge, no jury. He decided on the verdict, as well as, you heard there, talking about the sentence. We want to go now to CNN’s Poppy Harlow. She is in Steubenville, and has been covering this trial. I cannot imagine having just watched this on the feed coming in. How emotional that must have been sitting in the courtroom. POPPY HARLOW, CNN CORRESPONDENT: I’ve never experienced anything like it, Candy. It was incredibly emotional — incredibly difficult even for an outsider like me to watch what happened as these two young men that had such promising futures, star football players, very good students, literally watched as they believe their life fell apart. One of — one of the young men, Ma’lik Richmond, when that sentence came down, he collapsed. He collapsed in the arms of his attorney, Walter Madison. He said to me, “My life is over. No one is going to want me now.” Very serious crime here. Both found guilty of raping this 16- year-old girl at a series of parties back in August, alcohol-fueled parties. Alcohol is a huge part in this. […] I want to bring in Paul Callan, our CNN legal contributor. You know, Paul, a 16-year-old now just sobbing in court, regardless of what big football players they are, still sound like 16 year olds. The other one, 17. A 16-year-old victim. The thing is, when you listen to it and you realize that they could stay until they’re 21, they are going to get credit for time served. What’s the lasting effect, though, on two young men being found guilty in juvenile court of rape, essentially? PAUL CALLAN, CNN LEGAL CONTRIBUTOR: Well, you know, Candy, we’ve seen here a courtroom drenched in tears and tragedy and, you know, Poppy’s description, I think, you know, sums it all up. But across America scenes like this happen all the time. I know as a prosecutor and defense attorney, when that verdict is handed down, usually it’s just the family and families of the defendants and the victims, there’s always that moment of just lives are destroyed. And lives have already been destroyed by the crime. And we got a chance to see that. But in terms of what happens now, yes, the most severe thing with these young men is being labeled as registered sex offenders. That label is now placed on them by Ohio law and, by the way, the laws in most other states now require such a designation in the face of such a serious crime. That will haunt them for the rest of their lives. Employers, when looking up their background, will see they’re registered sex offender. When they move into a new neighborhood and somebody goes on the Internet where these things are posted. Neighbors will know they’re a registered sex offender. It’s really something that will have a lasting impact. Much more of a lasting impact than going to a juvenile facility for one or two years. CROWLEY: Paul, thanks. I want to bring Poppy back in — because, Poppy, there’s — you know, the 16-year-old victim, her life, never the same, again. And I understand you have been talking to some of the families involved. HARLOW: Her life never the same again. Absolutely, Candy. The last thing she wanted to do was sit on that stand and testify. She didn’t want to bring these charges. She said it was up to her parents. But I want to tell our viewers about a statement that her mother just made, just made in the court after the sentencing. Her mother just said that she has pity on the two young boys that did this. She said human compassion is not taught by teachers or coaches. It’s a God-given gift, saying that you displayed a lack of compassion, a lack of moral code, saying that you were your own accuser throughout this for posting about this all over social media. And she said she takes pity on them.

Journalists on Twitter Sunday voiced strong criticism of how the media covered the Steubenville, Ohio, rape case, particularly after two teenage boys were found guilty. Several prominent journalists, including Lauren Wolfe and Xeni Jardin, called out CNN's Poppy Harlow and Paul Callan. They accused the anchors of showing sympathy towards the convicted boys and inappropriately highlighting that the victim was "allegedly drunk." The following day, Fox News, CNN, and MSNBC aired the woman's name, a move Think Progress labeled as "an act of serious journalistic negligence."

Lauren Wolfe, director of the Women's Media Center's Women Under Siege project, expressed her outrage. She stated, "What I’m so furious about, after the act perpetrated on this young woman, is our media’s take. Mainstream media, of course, reflects society — so in this case, they reflect rape culture. But shouldn’t we expect more from the media? Aren’t there such things as news judgment and context and analysis?" Wolfe questioned why so few outlets were investigating the underlying issues of masculinity and the arrogance of those defending a football team over a violated young woman. She also pointed out the media's emphasis on the victim's alleged intoxication, asking, "How could the media possibly be putting the emphasis on [the fact that] the girl drank? Did the boys not drink?"

Wolfe also criticized ABC's Nightline for framing the Steubenville case as a "cautionary tale for teenagers living in today’s digital world." She argued this framing was misleading, stating, "As if the problem was what the boys did online, not that they raped a 16-year-old girl and then bragged about it publicly." CNN's Poppy Harlow had commented on how the charge against the two young men meant they "literally watched as their life fell apart." However, Wolfe noted that Harlow did not discuss the severe traumatic effects that rape can inflict on young women.

This incident is not the first time news organizations have been criticized for omitting crucial context or using language that implies the victim bears responsibility. The New York Times faced similar backlash in 2011 for its coverage of a gang rape in Cleveland, Texas. When the Steubenville case first emerged earlier this year, initial media coverage offered little information about the young woman. Her identity was protected, and as she had not spoken out, the focus remained predominantly on the accused boys. For example, in January, Matt Lauer interviewed the attorney and former guardians of Ma’lik Richmond, one of the convicted teenagers. Richmond's former guardians presented him in a positive light, showing childhood photos and expressing their support. This coverage left many wondering about the well-being of the young woman and whether anyone was advocating for her.

The media also extensively discussed the teenage boys' roles as football players, often referring to them as "glorified athlete suspects." In January, BuzzFeed's Katie Heaney wrote about how an accuser's athletic achievements could be perceived as a "legitimate alibi absolving them of wrongdoing." Salon's Irin Carmon echoed these concerns, critiquing CNN's coverage, which Gawker also found problematic. Carmon explained via email, "Yes, networks are limited in how much footage they can show when it comes to the victim and her family, whereas they can show the boys’ emotional breakdowns, but Harlow was narrating events and not limited to footage. Yes, these boys are young. But the seriousness of their crimes was utterly glossed over in favor of a sideshow about whether a father told his son he loved him." She added, "We rarely see such compassion evinced for young offenders when the crime isn’t rape, or when they lack the social status of football players."

Both Wolfe and Carmon highlighted Yahoo Sports' Dan Wetzel as a journalist who handled the Steubenville news effectively on Sunday. Wolfe praised his column for "going beyond the obvious" and "smashing victim-blaming." Wetzel's reporting delved into the complexities of the town, describing how "a culture of extreme arrogance collapse[d] in two tearful rape convictions." The local football team was placed under scrutiny, with critics suggesting its prominence had allowed players to act with impunity for years. Social media posts, videos, photos, and text messages revealed a disturbing picture of a high school team seemingly out of control. Wetzel also provided essential context on the nature of rape, explaining that experts consider it a crime of power and control rather than solely sexual gratification. He emphasized that arrogance often underlies such acts, and in Steubenville, it reached extreme levels. Reporting on rape is undeniably challenging, requiring significant time and resources to explore the crime's nuances, its prevalence, and all perspectives. However, this in-depth and contextualized reporting is precisely what is most needed.

CNN faced significant backlash for its reporting on the Steubenville rape case, with critics arguing the network **prioritized the convicted rapists and their families over the victim**. A notable segment, hosted by Fredricka Whitfield, focused on the **emotional distress of the parents** of Trent Mays and Ma'lik Richmond, both minors at the time of the crime. The broadcast featured **footage of the defendants' parents weeping** and pleading for forgiveness.

Whitfield introduced reporter Poppy Harlow, stating the families had "tried their hardest to plead for some forgiveness." While Harlow attempted to steer the conversation toward the **severity of the crime**, noting, "this is an incredibly serious crime, it's the crime of rape," the segment's tone repeatedly returned to the defendants' emotional state. Harlow's observation that it was "incredibly emotional, it was difficult for anyone in there to watch those boys break down," followed by "[It was] also difficult, of course, for the victim's family," was widely seen as **downplaying the victim's suffering**.

The coverage consistently highlighted the **distress of the rapists' families**. CNN aired the **full plea of Richmond's father**, who expressed regret for his parenting and urged others to "get involved more in their kids' lives." An interview with Harlow also touched on a personal interaction between Richmond and Mays. Although the victim's mother's statement was presented, it appeared **after extensive coverage of the defendants' families' reactions**.

This was not an isolated incident. Earlier that day, anchor Candy Crowley expressed sympathy for Mays and Richmond, describing them as "star football players, very good students" whose "life fell apart." Harlow characterized the courtroom scene as "incredibly difficult" to witness. Crowley then posed the question, "What's the lasting effect though on two young men being found guilty juvenile court of rape essentially?" This framing **shifted the focus from the crime to the perpetrators' perceived consequences**.

CNN's coverage exhibited a clear slant for two main reasons. First, the network seemed to favor **visually compelling emotional displays**. With the victim's identity protected and her family not shown in distress, the **weeping of the defendants' families provided the most accessible emotional content**, a common media tactic to boost viewer engagement. Second, the **persistence of this tone across multiple segments**, despite online criticism, suggested a deliberate editorial choice.

Whitfield concluded the initial segment by calling it "a heart-breaking case to watch, no matter how you look at it." Critics, however, argued that CNN's focus on perspectives that seemed to **sympathize with the perpetrators** resulted in "embarrassing and damaging coverage," failing to uphold journalistic impartiality and sensitivity.

The network's reporting sparked a **significant online backlash**. Many users decried CNN's **pronounced focus on the defendants' futures and their families**, contrasting it with the **scant attention given to the victim**. Outrage grew, with accusations that CNN was **promoting rape culture** by framing the narrative around the ruined "promising" lives of the convicted rapists. Hashtags like #SteubenvilleRapists, #CNNFail, and #RapeCulture trended, questioning the network's editorial priorities and integrity.

One widely shared sentiment highlighted perceived hypocrisy: "CNN a month ago: OMG SOMETHING NEEDS TO BE DONE FOR THOSE POOR INDIAN WOMEN! CNN today: poor defendants #Steubenville! what abt their future!" Another user directly challenged Candy Crowley's earlier comments, questioning her continued employment. This indicated **frustration with a perceived double standard** in the network's coverage.

Criticism also targeted the fundamental narrative framing. Commentators stressed that the focus should have been on the **crime of rape and its impact on the victim**, not the perpetrators' consequences. Comments like "Not the #rape but guilty verdict ruined ‘promising’ lives of #SteubenvilleRapists? #RealMenDoNotRape" and "Hey @CNN, glad you care so much about the rapists. What about what happens to the rape VICTIM?" encapsulated public disappointment.

The collective outcry suggested CNN's coverage **failed to adequately address the crime's severity and the victim's trauma**. The emphasis on the defendants' emotions and their families' distress was seen as a **misstep that trivialized rape and perpetuated victim-blaming**. This intense criticism underscored broader concerns about how media reports on sexual assault, and the responsibility media outlets have in shaping public understanding. The overwhelming negative reaction revealed a **disconnect between CNN's reporting and public expectations for sensitive, victim-centered coverage**. The debate served as a stark reminder of the challenges in reporting on sexual violence with the **appropriate gravity, nuance, and unwavering focus on the survivor's experience**.

Reporting on rape trials demands a delicate balance: conveying the legal outcomes while honoring the victim's experience. However, a recent CNN broadcast by Poppy Harlow and Candy Crowley took a concerning turn. Instead of focusing on the victim or the gravity of the crime, the report emphasized the diminished "promising futures" of the convicted perpetrators, a direct consequence of their actions.

### Shifting the Focus from Victim to Perpetrator

During the broadcast, General Correspondent Poppy Harlow described the verdict as "incredibly difficult... to watch what happened as these two young men that had such promising futures, star football players, very good students, literally watched as they believed their lives fell apart." She recounted Ma'lik Richmond's despair, quoting him saying, "My life is over. No one is going to want me now." This framing, while capturing a moment of distress, immediately shifted the focus from the rape of a sixteen-year-old girl to the perceived tragedy of the defendants' downfall. CNN further humanized the convicted rapists by airing footage of them expressing remorse in court.

### The Legal Ramifications and the Sex Offender Registry

The report then explored an emotional interaction between Ma'lik Richmond and his estranged father. Anchor Candy Crowley invited legal expert Paul Callan to discuss the future prospects of the two young men, highlighting their youth and perceived emotional vulnerability. Callan noted, "Sixteen-year-olds just sobbing in court, regardless of what big football players they are, they still sound like sixteen-year-olds...what's the lasting effect, though, on two young men being found guilty in juvenile court of rape, essentially?" He explained that the most severe consequence is being designated as a registered sex offender, a designation mandated by Ohio law that would permanently impact their lives, affecting potential employers and even neighbors who can access this information online.

### Understanding the Sex Offender Registry

The sex offender registry functions precisely as Callan described. Individuals convicted of sexual offenses, such as rape, are legally required to register on the national sex offender public registry. This public access allows potential employers and neighbors to be aware of their status. The most effective preventative measure against such a designation and its far-reaching consequences is to abstain from acts of sexual violence. Regardless of academic achievements or athletic prowess, engaging in rape carries a significant risk of conviction for a sex crime. This outcome is a direct result of the decision to commit a criminal act rather than pursuing lawful activities. A person's proficiency in calculus or their skill on the football field is generally not considered a mitigating factor in legal proceedings when weighed against the gravity of sexual assault.

### The Inappropriateness of Framing Perpetrators' Downfall as Tragic

To be recognized as a "good student and excellent football player" rather than a "convicted sex offender" requires prioritizing lawful conduct and refraining from committing sexual offenses. While reporting on the severity and length of a sentence is a necessary aspect of covering a trial, framing the termination of convicted rapists' athletic and academic careers as an event divorced from their culpability is unjustifiable. Their aspirations were not extinguished by an impersonal legal system; Mays and Richmond committed rape, and they have been sentenced accordingly. Had they not perpetrated this crime, they would not be serving a juvenile detention sentence, nor would they face the lifelong stigma of being a registered sex offender.

### Misplaced Sympathy and the Victim's Experience

While it's improbable that Candy Crowley and Poppy Harlow are proponents of rape apologia, their chosen narrative likely stemmed from a desire for a sensational and emotionally resonant conclusion to a sensationalized trial. Given the victim's protected identity and the defendants' public displays of emotion, the chosen narrative regrettably focused on the tragic downfall of exceptionally talented young men, attributing it to unspecified circumstances rather than their deliberate actions. This approach fundamentally misrepresents the realities of rape trials and the justice system's response to such heinous crimes.

Trent Mays and Ma'lik Richmond are not the central figures in the Steubenville rape trial, nor are they the sole characters in this legal drama. However, CNN's portrayal presented viewers with complex, sympathetic male protagonists, effectively relegating the anonymous victim to an afterthought. This narrative choice ignored the profound and lasting effects of her graphic sexual assault, leaving the audience to grapple with a skewed perspective. Consequently, it is understandable how some viewers, influenced by the emphasis on the young men's lauded academic and athletic abilities, might inadvertently sympathize with them, while overlooking their commission of rape and the devastating impact on their victim. The focus on the perpetrators' lost futures, while a human element, distracts from the core issue: the violation of another person's autonomy and the pursuit of justice for that violation.

The nation's eyes will be focused this week on what happens inside a tiny Steubenville, Ohio, courthouse. The juvenile trial set to begin there is every parent's nightmare and a cautionary tale for teenagers living in today's digital world. Steubenville is a town used to having media attention lavished on a much different building. In the middle of this city of 18,000 nestled on the Eastern border of Ohio stands Harding Stadium, the crown jewel of this former steel town. Nicknamed Death Valley, the 10,000-seat structure is home to the Big Red football team, one of Ohio's most storied high school programs. Steubenville is a place where football is more than just a past time; it's a religion. And residents here worship on Friday nights. Every time Big Red scores, a sculpture of a stallion named Man O' War breathes a 6-foot stream of fire into the night sky over Harding Stadium. But this past season, the team's second-round playoff defeat was overshadowed by a very different firestorm that engulfed the team and the entire town. Steubenville Launches Website on Alleged Teen Rape Just as the season was gearing up late last summer, two Big Red football players were accused of participating in the rape of a 16-year-old intoxicated girl with friends documenting the alleged crime through cellphone pictures and video. The social media frenzy took on a life of its own, with reports going as far as calling the incident a "gang-rape" of an unconscious girl. In reality, prosecutors contend that Trent Mays, 17, and Ma'lik Richmond, 16, used their hands to penetrate her while she was too drunk to consent, By Ohio law, such a crime constitutes rape, as it does in many places. At least three other Steubenville students say they witnessed the alleged encounters, and still others heard about them and posted messages, photographs and videos about the incident on social media sites. See the full story on ABC's "20/20" Friday, March 22, at 10 p.m. The news soon spread beyond Steubenville, leading both hacker-activists and women's advocacy groups to blow the lid off the story nationally, questioning why people who knew about the allegations weren't also charged under an Ohio law requiring people to report crimes of which they're aware. The uproar surrounding the case soon split the town into two furious camps; one that firmly believes there's a conspiracy to cover up a "rape culture" among the football team, and the other believing that the town's once-stellar reputation is being unfairly tarnished by outsiders who don't know all the facts. Now, documents and photographs obtained exclusively by "20/20," along with never-before-seen taped police interviews with many of the teenage party goers, are shedding light on many of the facts of the case for the first time. On the night of Aug. 11, 2012, Big Red ran a scrimmage to show off the team's newest talent. Trent Mays was a quarterback and honors student from a town 15 minutes outside of Steubenville. With a football coach for a father, Trent had the sport in his DNA. Ever since he could remember, he shared a dream that so many boys in this corner of the Ohio Valley do; to one day hear the roar of Big Red fans from the field. A favorite target for Trent that night was wide receiver Ma'lik Richmond. Ma'lik came from the rougher side of Steubenville. His earliest memories involve dodging stray bullets in his living room and watching most of his male role models being killed or incarcerated. He had turned to sports early in life as an escape from the realities around him. That night, Trent and Ma'lik helped propel Big Red to victory. For the faithful who filled the stands, it was tempting to fantasize about winning a 10th state championship. For the players, it was an excuse to party. Hours after the game, Trent, still relishing his role in Big Red's win, was receiving text messages from a girl he had been flirting with over social media, according to his lawyer. She was from just over the Ohio River in Weirton, W.Va., and, his lawyer says, persuaded him to come to a party where she was with several girlfriends. Party No. 1: When Trent and Ma'lik arrived, the narrow street outside the house of the party was crammed with cars. By some estimates, there were as many as 40 to 50 teenagers there and no adults. What was in abundance was alcohol, according to Ma'lik and several of the attendees. Witnesses said the girl who invited Trent was one of the more tipsy teens there. "She had her arm wrapped around me and one hand on my chest. It just felt like she was coming on to me," Ma'lik told ABC News' Elizabeth Vargas in an exclusive interview for "20/20." After midnight, the party was breaking up. The intoxicated girl, who would soon be at the center of a rape investigation, made it clear she wanted to leave with Trent, according to the police interviews with several of her friends. They also said she resisted their pleas for her not to leave with a car full of boys. Nevertheless, the girl got into a car with Trent, Ma'lik and two other boys and drove off. In her interview with police exclusively obtained by ABC News, the alleged victim says there is little she remembers from the time between the first party and waking up the next morning. "I remember everything that happened at the girl's house I was at but I don't remember anything past the point of me walking off the porch with him," she told them. Party No. 2: When the five teenagers arrived at the next house, the group was much smaller. There are contradictory accounts about whether the girl was able to walk into the house on her own or needed help from Ma'lik and Trent. Feeling ill, the girl was taken to the bathroom where she threw up. When she emerged, a photo of her was taken that would become a flashpoint in the case. The photo shows Trent and Ma'lik's holding the girl by her arms and legs with her head hanging back. It is unclear from the picture whether her eyes are open and witness accounts conflict on the exact context of this photo. The boy who took it, and ultimately uploaded it to his Instagram account, was another football player for Big Red and an ex-boyfriend of the intoxicated girl in the picture. "She was just like laughing, we were all talking, just clowning around and that's when her ex-boyfriend was like, 'Let me get a picture of this drunk B. And that's when we took the picture," Ma'lik told ABC News. The picture, Ma'lik maintains, was intended as a joke; he says the girl was conscious, was playing along and was not carried out of the house that way. The girl's civil attorney, Bob Fitzsimmons, calls this characterization "bizarre." "It's common sense as to what's going on in that picture," he said. Adds Fitzsimmons: "My client was unconscious that night. She doesn't have any memory of what happened." ABC News does not name the victims of alleged sexual assaults. Several witnesses said that once outside, the girl needed to stop in the street because she was sick again. "She throws up on her blouse and takes her blouse off," Ma'lik said. "And then she asked for something to drink and I gave her my jacket to cover her up." After several minutes, the girl got back into the car with those same four boys. It is during this ride that prosecutors contend Trent raped the alleged victim. One of Trent's teammates, who was seated in the backseat, told police that he used his phone to videotape Trent exposing the girl's breasts and penetrating her vaginally with his fingers. The girl was talking but he could not decipher her slurred speech, he told police. But Ma'lik, who was seated in the front passenger seat, told ABC News that she was participating. "I turned around and I can see the flash on his phone. Trent was rubbing on her breasts and she was kissing his neck. And then he was trying to unbutton her pants," Ma'lik said. Police would never see the video because, by the next morning, he had deleted it from his phone. Party No. 3 That same boy who videotaped the alleged rape in the car, and who is now a key prosecution witness, testified that when the car arrived at his home, the alleged victim was again taken to the bathroom to throw up. When the girl emerged, prosecutors say, a second alleged rape occurred. The eyewitness told police that he saw Trent trying to get the girl to perform oral sex on him while she was lying on the floor. Next, he says he saw both Trent and Ma'lik's lying beside her, sexually touching the girl's groin area with their hands. At least one other witness claims to have seen the alleged rape. "I wouldn't say she was completely passed out but she wasn't in any state to make a decision for herself," one of the eyewitnesses told police. A defense attorney for Ma'lik told Vargas of "20/20" that the alleged victim was conscious enough to provide the pass codes for her cellphone at some point after the second alleged assault. "That doesn't sound like a person that's incapacitated to the point where they cannot answer a question, let alone consent," defense attorney Walter Madison said. The girl's civil attorney challenges such an assessment, saying, "The mere fact that someone presents an argument doesn't make it true." The Steubenville rumor mill was already beginning to churn with speculation about what happened to the intoxicated girl. Naked photos of the girl that were circulated that night fueled a series of tweets and also one YouTube video of an 18-year-old former Steubenville baseball player named Michael Nodianos. In the rambling 12-minute rant, Nodianos, who wasn't present during the alleged rapes, made jokes about the incident, repeatedly referring to the victim as "dead." When the sun finally rose over Steubenville the next morning, the 16-year-old alleged victim woke up naked in a home she had never been to before. Her girlfriends, who spent much of the previous night trying to contact her and anxiously reading tweets posted about her, soon were summoned to pick her up. ABC News has learned that one of the girls who picked up the alleged victim told police, "She and Trent were just lying on the couch together as if nothing happened. She looked hung over but then she got up and was completely fine." By the next day, so much had been written and uploaded to social networking sites that the town was abuzz with rumors and innuendo. Even the girl's parents found out by word of mouth. They brought her to the hospital Aug. 13, more than 24 hours after the incident. By then, she had already showered and her clothes from that night had been washed. No physical evidence of a rape was recovered. Nevertheless, 10 days after the alleged assault, on the strength of the witness accounts, Ma'lik Richmond and Trent Mays were arrested in the middle of the night and charged with rape and kidnapping (the kidnapping charge was later dropped.) Trent was also charged with disseminating child pornography for texting naked photos of the underage alleged victim. "They sent three or four police cars," Trent's mom, Linda Mays, told ABC News. "They surrounded the house and it was surreal." By this time, many of the social media posts and pictures had been deleted. But not all were lost. ABC News has learned that, in addition to the picture of the defendants' carrying the alleged victim, they also recovered two additional photos from Trent's phone. One of the photos shows the alleged victim lying naked and face down on the floor and the other shows her naked on the couch seemingly asleep. The intersection of idolized athletes, social media over-sharing and reckless teen behavior proved an explosive combination and the story soon went national. In December, the Nodianos video was re-posted by an offshoot of the Internet hacking group Anonymous called Knight Sec. The video quickly went viral and appeared to be proof to online activist groups and even the National Organization for Women that other athletes either witnessed or knew of the alleged assault and were never charged with a crime. Such sentiments have fueled much speculation of a cover up in Steubenville. Nodianos, who until this winter was attending Ohio State University on an academic scholarship, told police he only saw the alleged victim in passing that night as she left the second location. The details he talked about in the video came from viewing one photo of the alleged victim and talking to the other boys who were with her that night, he said. His lawyer has since issued an apology on his behalf for the shameful comments he made on the video posted on YouTube. Prosecutors have not commented on the specifics of the case but at the probable cause hearing in October, prosecutor Marianne Hemmeter said, "She was a toy to them that night and the bottom line is we don't have to prove that she said no. All we have to prove is when she's being penetrated that she was unresponsive and not in a position to consent and they knew it." Attorneys for Trent and Ma'lik insist that their clients are not guilty of any crime, claiming that she was sober enough throughout the night to consent. "What we believe we will be able to support is that she voluntarily proceeded throughout the night with our client," Trent's attorney, Brian Duncan, told ABC News. "There is no indication that she was somehow so intoxicated that she could not have consented to any of the contact that occurred." Ma'lik's attorney, Walter Madison, is equally confident in his client's innocence. He questions the prosecution's dependence on testimony from the three teenage witnesses. "They all have immunity and have been granted deals not to be prosecuted for their involvement," he said. "When you give a child an option to have a seat at the trial table or tell us what we need to know and in exchange we won't prosecute you, they're probably going to tell you what you want to hear." Attorney General DeWine denied that any deals have been made and won't rule out future charges for those witnesses. The alleged victim is slated to take the stand, but because she says she has little memory of the night in question, her testimony is not expected to clarify the events of Aug. 11-12. Defense attorneys say the intense scrutiny the case has garnered is creating another challenge for them. "We have found it very difficult to find people willing to talk to us," Duncan, Trent's attorney, said. "People have either not returned calls or they have lawyers that are involved. We have material subpoenas that have been issued." A West Virginia judge Friday refused to enforce those subpoenas for three juveniles who reside just outside of Ohio. The judge cited a lack of legal precedence for compelling an underage witness to testify in a juvenile proceeding out of state. When the trial commences Wednesday, there will be no jury involved. Instead, a juvenile judge will decide the fates of Trent Mays and Ma'lik Richmond, who face incarceration in a detention center until their 21st birthdays and the almost-certain demise of their dreams of playing football. See the full story on ABC's "20/20" Friday, March 22, at 10 p.m.
